improvedDescription: A low-caste Indian monarch who embraced Buddhism for self-protection and became one of history's most remarkable civil rulers, training and sending over seventeen thousand Buddhist missionaries throughout the known world during his twenty-five year reign.

name: assassination
originalDescription: it is only fair to record that many an ancient rich man distributed much of his fortune because of the fear of being killed by those who coveted his treasures. (69:5.14) the most despotic tyrants were subject to some restrictions; they were at least restrained by the ever-present fear of assassination. (70:6.6) Cain slew Abel, one the the most famous assasinations (76:2.5) primitive man did not recognize accidental death, there was always a culprit even in case of natural death (70:10.3) primitive men could kill their wives if their full price was paid; many towns simply accepted compensation money when someone was killed (70:10.11-12) The blood guilt ceases to be a crime when participated in by all, by society. (89:5.9) commandments against assassination: * “The Father’s Way” of Hap: 4. You shall not kill men, women, or children. (66:7.12) * The moral law of Eden: “whoso sheds man’s blood by man shall his blood be shed, for in the image of God made he man.” (74:7.20) * The seven commandments promulgated by Melchizedek: 4. You shall not kill. (93:4.10) * The five moral commandments of Gautama: 1. You shall not kill. (94:8.10) * The Hebrew commandments: you shall not kill (70:11.2) (131:2.12) (162:2.1) (163:2.4) indwelling Adjuster was additional reason against assassination in Eden times (74:7.20) on a planet close to ours, in its main continental nation, recently they have gone so far as to attempt the prevention of crime by sentencing those who are believed to be potential murderers and major criminals to life service in the detention colonies. (72:10.2)
improvedDescription: The deliberate killing of another person, prohibited by moral and religious codes throughout history including the commandments of Dalamatia, Eden, Melchizedek, Gautama, and the Hebrew law, with primitive societies often seeking compensation rather than punishment for such acts.

name: assemblies of Paradise
originalDescription: After graduation from world number seventy, ascending mortals take up residence on Edentia. Ascenders now, for the first time, attend the “assemblies of Paradise” and hear the story of their far-flung career as it is depicted by the Faithful of Days, the first of the Supreme Trinity-origin Personalities they have met. (43:9.1)
improvedDescription: Gatherings held on Edentia where ascending mortals first attend after graduation from world seventy, hearing the story of their career as depicted by the Faithful of Days.

name: Assigned Sentinel of Satania
originalDescription: the Assigned Sentinel of Satania is the ex officio head of the council of 24 councilors, the direct and personal representative of the Associate Inspector on Salvington and of the Supreme Executive of Orvonton. (24:5.3) (114:2.3) he counseled Gabriel in Lucifer rebellion (54:5.10)
improvedDescription: The direct and personal representative of the Associate Inspector on Salvington and the Supreme Executive of Orvonton, serving as ex officio head of the council of 24 councilors on Jerusem.

name: Assigned Sentinels
originalDescription: The Assigned Sentinels are co-ordinating personalities and liaison representatives of the Seven Supreme Executives. They were personalized on Paradise by the Infinite Spirit and were created for the specific purposes of their assignment. They are of stationary numbers, and there are exactly seven billion in existence. (24:5.1) they are the representatives of superuniverse Supreme Executives to local systems (24:0.10) (24:5.1-2) (37:8.5) (55:8.2) sirven in rotation (24:5.3) They are responsible solely to the Supreme Executive of the superuniverse concerned; their activities are distinct from the administration of the Ancients of Days. (24:5.4) they are the advisers to local systems in light and life (55:8.2) (55:10.3) In the local systems they serve as the ex officio heads of the four and twenty administrators hailing from the evolutionary worlds, but otherwise, ascending mortals have little contact with them. (24:5.3) they appear in personality registers (30:1.76) (30:2.72) omniaphim are the personal representative of Assigned Sentinels in planets in light and life (55:4.16) The Associate Inspectors receive reports and recommendations only from their subordinates, the Assigned Sentinels (24:4.3) (24:5.2)
improvedDescription: Coordinating personalities and liaison representatives of the Seven Supreme Executives, personalized on Paradise by the Infinite Spirit with exactly seven billion in existence. They serve as representatives of superuniverse Supreme Executives to local systems, rotating assignments every millennium and serving as ex officio heads of the twenty-four administrators from evolutionary worlds.

name: assistant teachers
originalDescription: The assistant teachers are the helpers and associates of their fellow seraphim, the teaching counselors. (39:2.7) A marvelous corps of this order of seraphim functions on Urantia for the purpose of fostering and furthering the cause of truth and righteousness. (39:2.7)
improvedDescription: Seraphic helpers and associates of teaching counselors who are individually connected with educational enterprises of the local universe, especially the sevenfold training scheme on mansion worlds.

name: Associate Circuit Supervisors
originalDescription: they are part of order of Universe Circuit Supervisors; they direct superuniverse circuits (24:1.2-8)
improvedDescription: One of four orders of Universe Circuit Supervisors created by the Infinite Spirit, responsible for directing and supervising the circuits of the seven superuniverses from their headquarters on the Paradise spheres of the Infinite Spirit.

name: Associate Inspectors
originalDescription: The Associate Inspectors are the personal embodiment of the authority of the Supreme Executives to the local universes of time and space. These high observers of the affairs of the local creations are the joint offspring of the Infinite Spirit and the Seven Master Spirits of Paradise. In the near times of eternity seven hundred thousand were personalized, and their reserve corps abides on Paradise. (24:4.1) they direct Assigned Sentinels in local universe light and life (55:10.3) one of them embodies authority of Supreme Executives to each local universe (24:0.10) (24:4.1-2) (24:5.2) (37:8.5) they receive reports from Assigned Sentinels (24:4.3) (24:5.2) they appear in personality registers (30:1.75) (30:2.71)
improvedDescription: Personal embodiments of the authority of the Supreme Executives to local universes, being joint offspring of the Infinite Spirit and Seven Master Spirits of Paradise. Seven hundred thousand were personalized in near times of eternity, with one stationed on each local universe headquarters as a close associate of the resident Union of Days.

name: associate power directors
originalDescription: These marvelously efficient beings are intrusted with the assignment and dispatch of all orders of the Master Physical Controllers in accordance with the ever-shifting needs of the constantly changing energy status of the realms. (29:4.4,15) At least one acting inspector is always present in each local system, maintaining headquarters on its capital sphere. (29:4.17) there are 3 billion of them in each superuniverse (29:4.16) they serve as instructors of all who study the sciences of the techniques of intelligent energy control and transmutation on minor sectors (29:4.16) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.101)
improvedDescription: Marvelously efficient beings entrusted with assignment and dispatch of all orders of Master Physical Controllers according to the ever-shifting energy needs of the realms. Three million are assigned to each minor sector of Orvonton, serving alternating periods of executive service and inspection.

name: associate registrars
originalDescription: they are part of Morontia Power Supervisors; they are the recorders for morontial world (48:2.25) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.115)
improvedDescription: Morontia world recorders who serve with spirit recorders in supervising and maintaining custody of records and data indigenous to morontia creations.

name: Associate Transcendental Master Force Organizers
improvedDescription: A classification of Master Force Organizers operating at transcendental levels of reality organization and energy manipulation.

name: Assuntia
originalDescription: Assuntia is a local system immediately neighboring Satania (41:2.1)
improvedDescription: A local system immediately neighboring Satania, part of the administrative organization of the constellation Norlatiadek.

name: assurance
originalDescription: Jesus portrayed the profound surety of the God-knowing mortal when he said: “To a God-knowing kingdom believer, what does it matter if all things earthly crash? (100:2.7) Of God, the most inescapable of all presences, the most real of all facts, the most living of all truths, the most loving of all friends, and the most divine of all values, we have the right to be the most certain of all universe experiences. (102:7) Jesus gave a great speech about security in the Jerusalem temple during Passover week of the year 27 (142:5) mortal man assurance of Deity kinship must be faith realized (117:6.8) The reason of science is based on the observable facts of time; the faith of religion argues from the spirit program of eternity. The more of science we know, the less sure we can be; the more of religion we have, the more certain we are. (102:1) the assurance of spirit communion develops gradually (34:6.12) Spiritual assurance is the equivalent of your personal religious experience in the eternal realities of divine truth and is otherwise equal to your intelligent understanding of truth realities plus your spiritual faith and minus your honest doubts. (146:3.4) Uncertainty with security is the essence of the Paradise adventure—uncertainty in time and in mind, uncertainty as to the events of the unfolding Paradise ascent; security in spirit and in eternity, security in the unqualified trust of the creature son in the divine compassion and infinite love of the Universal Father; uncertainty as an inexperienced citizen of the universe; security as an ascending son in the universe mansions of an all-powerful, all-wise, and all-loving Father. (111:7.1) security in experience of cosmic citizenship (118:6.8) To recognize Deity omnipotence is to enjoy security in your experience of cosmic citizenship, to possess assurance of safety in the long journey to Paradise. (194:0.1) temporal securities are vulnerable; spiritual securities are impregnable (100:2.7) temporal security is society’s prime gift to man (70:9.1) One of the characteristic peculiarities of genuine religious assurance is that, notwithstanding the absoluteness of its affirmations and the stanchness of its attitude, the spirit of its expression is so poised and tempered that it never conveys the slightest impression of self-assertion or egoistic exaltation. (102:2.2)
improvedDescription: A profound spiritual confidence and certainty experienced by God-knowing believers, characterized by unwavering trust in divine love and protection despite temporal uncertainties. This religious assurance represents the soul's recognition of its eternal kinship with God, manifesting as inner security that remains unassailable even amid earthly trials and adversities.

name: Assyria
originalDescription: Assyrians constantly raided tribes of Palestine (93:5.6) The fall of Assyria and the ascendancy of Egypt brought deliverance to Judah for a time. (97:9.23) Israel conspired with Egypt to refuse tribute to Assyria, what caused teh vanish of the kingdom of Israel (97:9.21-22) the western or Syrian Nodites were the Vanite ancestors of Assyrians (77:4.3,11)
improvedDescription: An ancient Middle Eastern empire that frequently raided Palestinian tribes and eventually conquered the northern kingdom of Israel after it conspired with Egypt to refuse tribute payments. The Assyrians descended from western Nodite stock that settled northwest of Mesopotamia, with their ancestors receiving moral teachings from Van on Mount Ararat.

name: Astarte
originalDescription: The cult of Ishtar among the northern tribes became the cult of the goddess Astarte (95:1.7)
improvedDescription: A goddess worshipped by northern tribes as part of the widespread Ishtar fertility cult, which manifested under different names across various cultures including Ashtoreth in Palestine, Isis in Egypt, and Aphrodite in Greece.

name: asteroids
originalDescription: 5th Monmatia planet fragmentized into (57:6.5) asteroids are made from solar matter (57:5.8)
improvedDescription: Fragments of celestial bodies created when larger space objects are disrupted by tidal gravity forces from nearby massive bodies. The present-day asteroid cluster in our solar system formed when the fifth planet was fragmentized after making increasingly closer approaches to Jupiter until it entered the critical zone of gravity-tidal disruption.

name: astrology
originalDescription: Belief in astrology led to the development of astronomy (81:2.9) (88:6.5) In connection with a revival of the worship of Ishtar in Mesopotamia, the Babylonian priests again turned to stargazing and astrology experienced its last great revival. (95:1.7) spirit messages were interpreted by astrology (87:5.9) Primitive astrology was a world-wide belief and practice. (90:2.5) astrology is a pseudo science of Babylon developed into a religion throughout the Greco-Roman Empire; a mass of superstitious error which has no place in the gospel of the kingdom. (121:5.5) (150:3.2-3) the stars were consulted by astrologers before any marriage (83:4.4) many supposedly intelligent people still believe in good luck, the evil eye, and astrology. (88:6.7) (90:2.7)
improvedDescription: A primitive pseudo-science based on the superstitious belief that celestial bodies influence human affairs and terrestrial events. While astrology led to the development of legitimate astronomy, it represents a mass of superstitious error that has no place in spiritual truth, despite its continued practice throughout history by various civilizations.

name: astronomy
originalDescription: astronomy has its origin in astrology (81:2.9) (88:6.5) in Jerusem there is the vast astronomic observatory (46:2.9) (46:5.30) astronomical objects named in The Urantia Book are: * Andromeda | Andromeda * Andronover | Andronover * Angona | Angona * Antares | Antares * Crab nebula | Crab nebula * Jupiter | Jupiter (planet) * Magellanic Cloud | Magellanic Cloud * Mars | Mars (planet) * Mercury | Mercury * Milky Way galaxy | Milky Way galaxy * Monmatia | Monmatia * the moon (Urantia’s)| moon (Urantia’s) * Pole Star | Pole Star * Sagittarius | Sagittarius * Saturn | Saturn * the sun | sun (Urantia’s) * Umajor the fifth | Umajor the fifth * Uminor the third | Uminor the third * Urantia | Urantia * Uranus | Uranus * Venus | Venus (planet) the errors in astronomic measurement caused by: | red shift * multiple revolutionary movements in Orvonton (15:3.7-15) * opposite clockwise procession of first outer space level (12:4.15) * space respiration (12:4.12-14)
improvedDescription: The scientific study of celestial objects and phenomena that evolved from the primitive practice of astrology. Modern astronomy faces measurement challenges due to multiple revolutionary movements in space, including planetary rotations, stellar circuits, and the expansion of the universe, which create various observational distortions and illusions for Urantian observers.

name: Athanasius
originalDescription: He was a presbyter who very passionately defended the idea of the identical nature of Father and Son at the Council of Nicaea, against the ideas of Arius (195:0.18)
improvedDescription: A Greek presbyter from Egypt who courageously defended the divine nature of Jesus at the Council of Nicaea, challenging the assembly with his eloquence and logic to prevent the obscuration of Christ's true nature. His passionate advocacy against the teachings of Arius helped preserve essential truths about Jesus' divinity.

name: atheism
originalDescription: many cults in India were frankly atheistic and believed that salvation could only be achieved through the efforts of man without outside help (94:2.5) materialism, atheism, is the maximation of ugliness, the climax of the finite antithesis of the beautiful. (56:10.4)
improvedDescription: A philosophical position rejecting belief in God or divine beings, representing the extreme antithesis of spiritual beauty and truth. Some Indian cults were frankly atheistic, teaching that salvation could be achieved only through human effort without divine assistance, while materialistic atheism is described as the climax of finite opposition to the beautiful.

name: Athens
originalDescription: Atenas was the cultural center of Alexander’s empire (133:5.1,12) Jesus held a talk on science with a Greek philosopher in Athens (133:5) The Hellenization of Christianity started in earnest on that eventful day when the Apostle Paul stood before the council of the Areopagus in Athens and told the Athenians about “the Unknown God”. (195:1.1,5)
improvedDescription: The ancient cultural center of Greek learning and science, and former heart of Alexander's empire that extended to India. Athens became historically significant for Christianity when the Apostle Paul preached before the Areopagus about "the Unknown God," marking the beginning of Christianity's Hellenization and the merger of Greek philosophical ideals with Jesus' spiritual teachings.

name: Atlantic Ocean
originalDescription: Atlantic Ocean made its appearance about 380,000,000 years ago (59:1.8) (60:3.4)
improvedDescription: A major ocean that first appeared approximately 380 million years ago, making extensive inroads on adjacent coastlines as Asia subsided. By 100 million years ago, the southern Atlantic had reached South America's present shore line, with both the Atlantic and Indian Oceans achieving roughly their current configurations.

name: atman
originalDescription: In the conception of the atman the Hindu teachers really approximated an appreciation of the nature and presence of the Adjuster, but they failed to distinguish the copresence of the evolving and potentially immortal soul. (111:0.4)
improvedDescription: A Hindu concept representing the soul or self, which approximated an understanding of the nature and presence of the Thought Adjuster but failed to distinguish the copresence of the evolving and potentially immortal soul.

name: atmosphere
originalDescription: terrestrial atmospehere atmospehere in other planets atmospehere in morontia spheres
improvedDescription: The gaseous envelope surrounding planets that filters sunlight, supports life, and contains multiple layers including the troposphere, stratosphere, and ionosphere. Planetary atmospheres vary greatly and determine the types of life that can exist on different worlds.

name: atmospheric mortals
originalDescription: there are four types of mortal beings according to the atmospehere of their planet: nonbreathers, subbreathers, mid-breathers, and superbreathers (49:2.10-14)
improvedDescription: Mortal beings classified into four types according to their planetary atmospheric requirements: nonbreathers, subbreathers, mid-breathers like those on Urantia, and superbreathers. These classifications represent different evolutionary adaptations to varying atmospheric conditions across inhabited worlds.

name: atoms
originalDescription: the formation of all matter is on the same order: atoms with a nuclear portion and electrons (42:7) Each atom is a trifle over 1/100,000,000th of an inch in diameter (42:6.7) the atom is formed like solar system. Surrounding this energy center there whirl, in endless profusion but in fluctuating circuits, the energy units which are faintly comparable to the planets encircling a sun. (42:7.1-2) When electrons pass from higher to lower energy levels of orbital revolution, quanta are always given off. (42:5.6) Calcium easily loses its outermost electron and moves the previous one thousands of times between its orbit and that of the lost electron. With these juggling with electrons the atom is able to partially defy gravity and get fired with the emanations of sunlight (41:6.3-4) Space is not empty; the spheres of all space whirl and plunge on through a vast ocean of outspread force-energy; neither is the space content of an atom empty. (42:5.16) (42:8.1-2) one drop of ordinary water contains over one billion trillions of atoms. This is the energy of more than one hundred horsepower exerted continuously for two years. (41:7.13) atomic cohesion atom construction and destruction indeterminacy science limits
improvedDescription: The basic building blocks of matter, structured like miniature solar systems with a central nucleus containing protons and neutrons, surrounded by orbiting electrons. Each atom is approximately one hundred-millionth of an inch in diameter and contains vast amounts of energy within its structure.

name: Aton
originalDescription: God of the sun; Ikhnaton based his monotheism on Aten (95:5.6-7,11)
improvedDescription: The sun-god of ancient Egypt whose worship was promoted by Pharaoh Ikhnaton as part of his monotheistic religious reforms. Ikhnaton used Aton as a means to approach worship of the Universal Father while maintaining the outward appearance of traditional Egyptian religion.

name: atonement, feast of
improvedDescription: A Jewish religious observance and feast day related to the concept of atonement for sin.

name: atonement doctrine
originalDescription: all atonement doctrine is an erroneous teaching (20:6.6-7) (148:4.7-10) (188:4-5) Jesus did not bestow himself to reconcile an angry God (2:5.2) (4:5.6) (98:7.1) (188:4.7) God certainly would never be satisfied with the childish scheme of substituting an innocent sufferer for a guilty offender (188:4.11) Father had nothing to do with Jesus’ torture (183:1.1-2) (186:5.2) Father’s justice not influenced by acts of his creatures (2:3.1) Father’s love not dependent on bestowals of Avonal and Michael Sons (20:5.2) (186:5.5-9) The heavenly Father is never torn by conflicting attitudes towards his universe children; God is never a victim of attitudinal antagonisms. (2:4.3) never necessary to influence Father to be merciful (2:4.2) (6:3.4) All this concept of atonement and sacrificial salvation is rooted and grounded in selfishness. The believer’s chief concern should not be the selfish desire for personal salvation but rather the unselfish urge to love and, therefore, serve one’s fellows (188:4.9) The erroneous supposition that the righteousness of God was irreconcilable with the selfless love of the heavenly Father, presupposed absence of unity in the nature of Deity and led directly to the elaboration of the atonement doctrine, which is a philosophic assault upon both the unity and the free-willness of God. (2:6.5-6) In the more advanced epochs of planetary evolution the seraphim are instrumental in supplanting the atonement idea by the concept of divine attunement as a philosophy of mortal survival. (39:5.6) history affront to God
improvedDescription: An erroneous religious teaching that Jesus died as a sacrifice to appease an angry God or satisfy divine justice. The Urantia Book teaches this doctrine is fundamentally flawed, as it misrepresents both God's loving nature and the true purpose of Jesus' bestowal mission.

name: Attis
originalDescription: son of Cybele in the Phrygian mystery cult (98:4.2-8) (98:5.5)
improvedDescription: The divine son of the goddess Cybele in the Phrygian mystery cult, believed to have died and been resurrected. His worship involved bloody festivals and ceremonies commemorating his death and resurrection, which followers believed would grant them immortality.

name: Augustus
originalDescription: Emperor of Rome during a part of the life of Jesus and co-emperor with Tiberius Caesar; he died in AD 14. (136:2.8) decreed a census in 8 B.C. (122:7.1) founded Nicopolis (133:2.5) a temple was dedicated to him at Caesarea (130:2.1) tried to destroy mysteries and revive the oldest political religion (98:3.6-8) created a new Roman state religion and proclaiming himself god, did not hesitate to encourage the worship of his person (98:3.7-8)
improvedDescription: Roman Emperor who ruled during part of Jesus' life and decreed the census that brought Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem. He attempted to revive Roman state religion, proclaimed himself a god, and established emperor worship throughout the Roman Empire except in Palestine.

name: auroral displays
originalDescription: The height of the earth’s atmosphere is indicated by the highest auroral streamers—about four hundred miles. Auroral phenomena are directly related to sunspots (58:2.6-8)
improvedDescription: Atmospheric light phenomena that occur at heights of up to four hundred miles above Earth's surface, directly related to sunspot activity. These spectacular displays result from charged particles ejected from solar magnetic fields ionizing the upper atmosphere.

name: Australia
originalDescription: geology fauna aborigin customs
improvedDescription: A continental landmass that separated from the supercontinent early in Earth's geological history and drifted southward, developing unique fauna including ancestors of kangaroos and remaining isolated during major animal migrations, leading to its distinctive biological evolution.

name: authority
originalDescription: Caligastia had tendency to be restless under authority, to mildly resent all forms of supervision (66:8.1) A Divine Counselor speaks with unchallengeable authority. He has enjoyed many times the supreme pleasure of a sojourn in the immediate personal presence of the Universal Father, and he knows whereof he speaks (1:7.9) (6:8.8) Jesus spoke with undoubted confidence and taught with absolute authority. (100:7.5) There is no division of authority; never is there a cross working of divine power and purpose. (32:4.3) We must not forget, to understand many events of Jesus life, that authority was the watchword of all Jewry. (173:2.3)
improvedDescription: The power and right to command, govern, or make decisions, particularly emphasized in Jewish culture where proper rabbinical ordination and sanction by the Sanhedrin were required for teaching, and where divine authority flows without division from the Universal Father through the Creator Sons.

name: Avalon
originalDescription: Avalon is one of Nebadon's neighboring local universes, along with Henselon, Sanselon, Portalon, Wolvering, Fanoving, and Alvoring. (32:2.12) Following the Lucifer rebellion the affairs of Urantia have long been administered by a council of planetary receivers and an advisory council which included a Brilliant Evening Star from Avalon who came periodically (67:6.5) The first created group of Nebadon seraphim were trained by a corps of one thousand seraphim from Avalon; subsequently our angels have been taught by their own seniors. (38:1.2) (38:5.1) it was Avalon surgeons who transferred life plasma from the body of the Andonite cooperators to that of the members of the corporeal staff of the Planetary Prince (66:2.7) (77:2.6) In Urantia popular culture Avalon is the name of a legendary mythological island
improvedDescription: One of Nebadon's seven neighboring local universes, whose experienced seraphim helped train the first created group of Nebadon seraphim and whose skilled surgeons assisted in transferring life plasm during the establishment of the Planetary Prince's corporeal staff on Urantia.

name: avoidance
originalDescription: avoidance had a place in evolution of religious observances (90:0.1) avoidance rituals merged with exorcism, coercion, and propitiation (89:0.2)
improvedDescription: A primitive religious practice involving the deliberate shunning of certain actions, objects, or situations believed to bring spiritual harm, which evolved as part of early religious observances along with placation, exorcism, and propitiation rituals.

name: Avonals, order of
improvedDescription: An order of Paradise Sons, also known as Magisterial Sons, who serve judicial and magisterial functions in the local universes.

name: axes
originalDescription: Neanderthalers made axes with flints (64:4.3) In a apocaliptic sentence from John the Baptist: “And even now is the ax laid to the very roots of the trees. Every tree that brings not forth good fruit is destined to be cut down and cast into the fire. ” (135:6.7)
improvedDescription: Stone tools made by attaching large flints to wooden handles, used by Neanderthal peoples as cutting implements and referenced metaphorically by John the Baptist when warning that unfruitful trees would be cut down.

name: Azariah
originalDescription: son of King Amaziah; also known as Uzziah (97:9.22)
improvedDescription: Son of King Amaziah who became king of Judah at age sixteen after his father was assassinated, also known as Uzziah, whose reign preceded the time when Isaiah prophesied that Jerusalem would never fall.

name: ba and ka
originalDescription: Egyptian and African teaching of soul and spirit (111:0.4-6)
improvedDescription: Egyptian concepts representing the dual nature of human beings, where the ka was believed to be a protecting guardian spirit bestowed at birth that guided the mortal throughout life and into the afterlife, while the ba represented another aspect of the soul.

name: Baal
originalDescription: word Baal means owner (97:3.3) the cult was founded on the right to buy and sell land; Baal sent rain, he was a god of fertility of the soil (97:3.3) (97:9.24) ritual prostitution took place in temples. Each Baal had a sacred place, a priesthood, and the “holy women,” the ritual prostitutes. (97:3.4) Samuel mocked priests of Baal and overthrow Baal sites (97:1.3) there was a temple dedicated to Baal in Jerusalem alongside the temple of Yahweh (97:9.21) there was a temple, “high place of Baal,” on hill in Nazareth one hundred years earlier from Jesus times (126:1.2)
improvedDescription: A Canaanite fertility deity whose name means 'owner,' worshiped as the god who sent rain and ensured soil fertility, with a cult centered on land ownership rights and featuring temples with sacred prostitution practices.

name: Baal-Perazim
originalDescription: It is the name of a city of King David, who gave it that name because a good part of his army were Canaanites, Baalites (97:9.12)
improvedDescription: A place named by King David to commemorate a military victory, so called because eighty percent of David's army consisted of Baalite soldiers rather than Hebrews.

name: Baalim
improvedDescription: The political and economic faction of Baalites who controlled courts and priesthood, dealing with property rights and land ownership in ancient Palestine.

name: Baalites
originalDescription: In general, the Baalites owned houses, lands, and slaves. They were the aristocratic landlords and lived in the cities. (97:3.4) Elijah, one of the greatest of the prophets, began his teaching as a defender of the old land mores as against the land-selling attitude of the Baalim, against the attempt of the cities to dominate the country. (97:9.19) The northern and more settled Canaanites, the Baalites, freely bought, sold, and mortgaged their lands. (97:3.3) When the Baal political party returned to power in Jerusalem then began the real Egyptian bondage. (97:9.24)
improvedDescription: The aristocratic landlord class of northern Canaanites who owned houses, lands, and slaves, living in cities and freely engaging in land commerce. They followed the Baal cult which validated property exchange and promoted land fertility through religious practices.

name: Babel
originalDescription: city also known as Bablod, Bablot (77:3.3,6) tower of Babel (77:3)
improvedDescription: An ancient city that developed from the original settlement of Bablot, named after its architect and builder. It became the site of multiple attempts to build great towers, including the famous Tower of Babel.

name: babies
improvedDescription: Young human infants in their earliest stage of development after birth.

name: Bablod
improvedDescription: An intermediate name for the city that was originally called Bablot and eventually became known as Babel.

name: Bablot (architect)
originalDescription: Bablot was the architect and builder of first tower of Babel (77:3.1-3)
improvedDescription: A descendant of Nod who designed and built the first tower of Babel as a monument to Nodite racial unity and greatness. His plan was endorsed by the tribal council after much deliberation.

name: Bablot (city)
improvedDescription: The original name of the city planned by the architect Bablot to become the nucleus of future Nodite culture and civilization, later known as Bablod and eventually Babel.

name: baboons
originalDescription: baboons are descendants of the branch of intermediate mammals that gave rise to the less advanced apes, as are monkeys, chimpanzees and gorillas, unlike the human species that descends from a higher branch of intermediate mammals (62:3.12)
improvedDescription: Primates that descended from the inferior branch of intermediate mammals, along with monkeys, chimpanzees, and gorillas, unlike humans who descended from a superior branch of the same ancestral group.

name: baby talk
originalDescription: The origin of dialects is illustrated by the indulgence in “baby talk” in a family group. (81:6.17)
improvedDescription: A form of playful language modification within family groups that illustrates how dialects originate through the tendency to play with language and create new words.

name: Babylon
originalDescription: baptism was religious ceremonial in (85:4.1) due to a drought Andites from south and east of the Caspian Sea migrated to the south, and their cavalry from Babylon pushed into Mesopotamia (79:1.5) Jewish theology did survive as it was interpreted and practiced at Jerusalem, notwithstanding that it was several times saved from oblivion by the timely intervention of certain Babylonian teachers. (121:6.8) In Babylon the god Marduk was a perpetuation of the Adam legend, the son-of-God idea, the connecting link between man and God. (92:5.6) Peter the apostle traveled extensively, visiting all the churches from Babylon to Corinth (139:2.11)
improvedDescription: An ancient Mesopotamian city that served as a major center of civilization and religious practice, where baptism became a religious ceremonial and the god Marduk perpetuated Adam legends. It was visited by Jesus during his travels and later by the apostle Peter.

name: Babylonian captivity
originalDescription: Jews were five hundred years under the overlordship of babylonian rulers (97:8.3) the end of Judah came suddenly. The city was destroyed, and the people were carried away into Babylon. The Yahweh-Baal struggle ended with the captivity. And the captivity shocked the remnant of Israel into monotheism. (97:9.26) Sargon, king of Assyria, in subduing a revolt in central Palestine, carried away and into captivity over twenty-five thousand Jews of the northern kingdom of Israel and installed in their place an almost equal number of the descendants of the Cuthites, Sepharvites, and the Hamathites. (143:4.1-2) The national ego of the Jews was tremendously depressed by the Babylonian captivity. (93:9.9) Hebrews carried back Sabbath taboos from Babilonia (95:1.2) During the captivity the Jews were much influenced by Babylonian traditions and legends, although it should be noted that they unfailingly improved the moral tone and spiritual significance of the Chaldean stories which they adopted, notwithstanding that they invariably distorted these legends to reflect honor and glory upon the ancestry and history of Israel. (97:7.1-3) The later captivity that enslaved the Jews in Babylon finally liberated the evolving concept of Yahweh to assume the monotheistic role of the God of all nations. (96:4.8) Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem and carried people away into Babylon (97:9.24-28) Persia freed Jews from Babylonian captivity (97:10.2) Jew scriptures
improvedDescription: The period when the Jews were exiled to Babylon after Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem and carried the people away. This captivity, lasting several centuries, shocked the remnant of Israel into monotheism, ended the Yahweh-Baal struggle, and significantly influenced Jewish traditions and religious development through exposure to Babylonian legends and customs.

name: Babylonians
originalDescription: The Babylonians, because of immediate contact with the remnants of the civilization of the Adamites, enlarged and embellished the story of man’s creation; they taught that he had descended directly from the gods. (74:8.6) The Jews were a part of the older Semitic race, which also included the Babylonians, the Phoenicians, and the more recent enemies of Rome, the Carthaginians. (121:2.1) Babylonians, as many other races, had triad gods, but these were still not true trinities. (104:0.3)
improvedDescription: A Semitic people who, due to immediate contact with remnants of Adamite civilization, developed elaborate creation stories teaching that humans descended directly from the gods. They were part of the older Semitic race along with the Jews, Phoenicians, and Carthaginians, and like many ancient peoples, worshipped triad gods.

name: bachelors
originalDescription: The Urantia Book mentions as a curiosity that on a nearby planet, in the main nation of that world, the state police force is recruited solely from unmarried men between 25 and 50 years of age, and unlike what is usually happen, these single cops are exempt from paying taxes (72:7.5)
improvedDescription: Unmarried men who, on a nearby planet, constitute the entire state police force between ages 25-50 and are exempt from the bachelor tax that other unmarried men must pay.

name: back to nature
originalDescription: The modern phrase, “back to nature,” is a delusion of ignorance, a belief in the reality of the onetime fictitious “golden age”. (68:1.7)
improvedDescription: A modern phrase representing a delusion of ignorance and belief in a fictitious golden age. The only historical basis for such legends lies in the improved societies of Dalamatia and Eden, which were far from utopian dreams.

name: backsliding
originalDescription: Andon and Fonta, our first parents, succumbed to their fears for a time (63:2.1) Mortal man may draw near God and may repeatedly forsake the divine will so long as the power of choice remains. (5:1.11) Jesus said: “ No man, having put his hand to the plough, if he turns back, is worthy to become a messenger of the kingdom”. (163:2.3)
improvedDescription: The act of turning away from divine will or spiritual progress after having made commitments to follow God. Mortals may repeatedly forsake the divine will as long as they retain the power of choice, but those who permanently lose the desire to do God's will face eternal consequences.

name: bacteria
originalDescription: bacterias destroyed many small ferns in Devonian (59:4.13) Life Carriers greatly disappointed by reversion of certain primitive plant life to the prechlorophyll levels of parasitic bacteria on such an extensive and unexpected scale that caused many distressful diseases in human species (65:5.2) The bacteria, simple vegetable organisms of a very primitive nature, are very little changed from the early dawn of life; they even exhibit a degree of retrogression in their parasitic behavior. (65:2.3) mortals contend with diseases by bacteria on all worlds (49:4.6) The majority of disease-causing bacteria and their auxiliary virus bodies really belong to a group of renegade parasitic fungi that retrograde evolutively. (65:2.3)
improvedDescription: Simple, primitive vegetable organisms that have changed little since the early dawn of life and exhibit some degree of evolutionary retrogression in their parasitic behavior. Many disease-causing bacteria belong to a group of renegade parasitic fungi, and mortals on all worlds must contend with these microscopic adversaries.

name: Badonan
originalDescription: Badonan was a great-great-grandson of Andon and Fonta, the first parents of human race (64:3.1) he founded center of culture in what today is Afghanistan (67:6.1) (79:0.1)
improvedDescription: A great-great-grandson of Andon and Fonta who founded a primitive center of culture in the foothills of what is now Afghanistan. This eastern focus of human civilization persisted for over half a million years and became an important cultural center.

name: Badonan tribes
originalDescription: The children of Badonan developed a belief in two souls, the breath and the shadow. (86:5.17) they were a people fearful of ice and water (64:3.3) they never practiced human sacrifices, but the other Andonites did it (64:3.1) During a long period of cultural decadence the Foxhall peoples of England and the Badonan tribes northwest of India continued to hold on to some of the traditions of Andon and certain remnants of the culture of Onagar. (64:2.4) (64:3) 850,000 years ago, the superior tribes of Badonán began a war of extermination against their inferior neighbors. This slightly improved the Badonite race and its descendants is what is now known as the Neanderthal race. (64:3.5) 500,000 years ago a relentless warfare in northwestern highlands of India left only most intelligent alive (64:5.1) Sangik races born into existence thanks to Badonites (64:5.2-3)
improvedDescription: Descendants of Badonan who inhabited the northwestern Indian highlands and were the only Andonite descendants who never practiced human sacrifice. They believed in two souls (breath and shadow), feared ice and water, and through warfare and evolution eventually gave rise to the Sangik races and contributed to the Neanderthal peoples.

name: Baganda tribes
originalDescription: The Baganda tribes of Africa have only recently emerged from the mana level of prayer. (91:0.5)
improvedDescription: African tribes that have only recently emerged from the mana level of prayer, representing an early evolutionary stage of religious development.

name: balance
originalDescription: In Palestine, human thought was so priest-controlled and scripture-directed that philosophy and aesthetics were entirely submerged in religion and morality. (97:10.5) (98:2.7-9) But Greek morals, ethics, and philosophy presently advanced far beyond the god concept, and this imbalance between intellectual and spiritual growth was as hazardous to Greece as it had proved to be in India. (98:1.6) the religionin Greece as a personal experience failed to keep pace with the intellectual probings into the nature and reality of the cosmos. (98:2.7-9) the ideal human estate is that in which philosophy, religion, and science are welded into a meaningful unity by the conjoined action of wisdom, faith, and experience. (98:2.12) A sense of proportion is also concerned in the exercise of virtue because evil may be perpetrated when the lesser is chosen in the place of the greater as a result of distortion or deception. (16:7.7) there is a greatest spiritual growth when physical, mental, and spiritual powers are in balance (110:6.4) mathematic logic, mind-reason, and spirit-faith may be unified and co-ordinated in personality development (16:6.10)
improvedDescription: The ideal state of harmonious development where physical, mental, and spiritual powers work together in unified coordination. True balance allows philosophy, religion, and science to be welded into meaningful unity, promotes maximum spiritual growth, and enables the correlation of factual science, moral philosophy, and genuine religious experience.

name: balances of power
originalDescription: World peace cannot be maintained by treaties, diplomacy, foreign policies, alliances, balances of power, or any other type of makeshift juggling with the sovereignties of nationalism. World law must come into being and must be enforced by world government—the sovereignty of all mankind. (134:6.9)
improvedDescription: A concept referring to diplomatic and military arrangements between nations that are inadequate for maintaining world peace, according to The Urantia Book's teachings on world government.

name: Balkans
originalDescription: Andonites entered Europe via Balkans (80:9.6)
improvedDescription: A region in southeastern Europe that served as one of the invasion routes for Andonites entering Europe around 3000 B.C., along with Ukraine, during their southward migration from central Asia.

name: Baltic
originalDescription: The Baltic was an invasion route for Andonites who penetrated Europe between the third and fourth Andite invasions (80:4.1-2)
improvedDescription: A region that served as an invasion route for Andonites who entered Europe from the north between the third and fourth Andite invasions, having traveled from Siberia via Russian rivers.

name: Baluchistan
originalDescription: Baluchistan was an Andite invasion route to India (79:1.1) (79:2.4)
improvedDescription: A region that served as a major invasion route for Andite peoples migrating from Iranian grazing lands into India, particularly during extensive movements around 15,000 B.C.

name: bank (moral)
originalDescription: Scientists have unintentionally precipitated mankind into a materialistic panic; they have started an unthinking run on the moral bank of the ages, but this bank of human experience has vast spiritual resources; it can stand the demands being made upon it. (195:6.1)
improvedDescription: A metaphorical concept representing humanity's accumulated spiritual and moral resources, which according to The Urantia Book has vast reserves capable of withstanding materialistic challenges to traditional values.

name: banking
originalDescription: ancient interest rate was 100% a year, which was another way of enslavement (69:5.8) early creditors controlled body after death (69:5.8) The early banker was the valorous man of the tribe. (69:5.2) It was among these enlightened groups educated in the Dalamatia schools that the first commercial credit was practiced. (66:5.12) in a nearby planet courts determine interest rates in main continental nation (72:5.5) Moses and lending Jesus and lending
improvedDescription: An ancient financial practice that evolved from tribal treasure-keeping, with early bankers serving as guardians of group wealth and later developing commercial credit systems, loan practices, and money-changing services.

name: banner of Michael
improvedDescription: A symbolic banner associated with Michael, likely featuring the three concentric circles symbol representing the Trinity and universal government.

name: banquets
originalDescription: in ancient times in Jesus times
improvedDescription: Ceremonial feasts and social gatherings that served important cultural functions in ancient times, with Jesus teaching about proper conduct at such events and encouraging hospitality toward the poor and disadvantaged.

name: banshees
originalDescription: The monotheists keep their subordinate gods as spirits, demons, fates, Nereids, fairies, brownies, dwarfs, banshees, and the evil eye. (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Supernatural spirits from folklore that monotheistic religions relegated to subordinate status alongside other mythical beings like demons, fairies, and brownies as belief systems evolved toward single deity worship.

name: baptism
originalDescription: baptism in antiquity baptism of John Jesus baptism baptism of the church
improvedDescription: A religious ceremony involving water that evolved from ancient ghost-protection rituals into a formal rite of admission to religious communities, practiced by John the Baptist and later adopted by early Christian followers as an outward sign of spiritual commitment.

name: Barabbas
originalDescription: Barabbas was a political agitator, murderous robber, and son of a priest (185:5.3-11) Jesus was crucified instead of Barabbas and the crowd cheered this decision (185:8.2) (187:0.2) the thief on the cross had regarded Barabbas as a hero (187:4.5)
improvedDescription: Barabbas was a political agitator, murderous robber, and son of a priest who had been apprehended for robbery and murder on the Jericho road. He was released by Pilate instead of Jesus during the Passover pardon, and Jesus was crucified in his place.

name: Barak
originalDescription: Barak was an Israelite general who, under the leadership of the prophetess Deborah, defeated the Canaanite leader Sisera at Taanaj (126:1.2)
improvedDescription: Barak was an Israelite general who, under the leadership of the prophetess Deborah, defeated the Canaanite leader Sisera at Taanach.

name: barbers
originalDescription: first matchmakers were barbers; later, the priests (83:2.1)
improvedDescription: Barbers were the first professional matchmakers in primitive societies, serving as marriage brokers before priests later took over this role.

name: bark
originalDescription: For writing material early peoples utilized tree barks, clay tablets, stone slabs, a form of parchment made of hammered hides, and a crude form of paperlike material made from wasps’ nests. (66:5.9)
improvedDescription: Tree bark was one of the early writing materials used by ancient peoples, along with clay tablets, stone slabs, hammered hide parchment, and crude paper made from wasps' nests.

name: barley
originalDescription: For thousands of years the descendants of Adam grew wheat and barley, as improved in the Garden, throughout the highlands of the upper border of Mesopotamia. (81:1.7)
improvedDescription: Barley was one of the improved grains cultivated by Adam's descendants in the Garden of Eden, which they continued to grow for thousands of years throughout the highlands of upper Mesopotamia.

name: Barnabas
originalDescription: Barnabas was a very clever Greek believer, a disciple of Rodan of Alexandria, and a friend of Stephen, who both went to Jerusalem to preach; there Esteban would suffer being stoned (194:4.11)
improvedDescription: Barnabas was a clever Greek believer and disciple of Rodan of Alexandria who came to Jerusalem with Stephen to preach. Their Greek perspective and teaching style brought them into conflict with Jewish authorities, leading to Stephen's martyrdom by stoning.

name: barnacles
originalDescription: During the Silurian period, barnacles appeared for the first time, marking an important development in marine life as species began to diversify and intermingle. (59:4.3)
improvedDescription: Barnacles first appeared during the Silurian period, marking an important development in marine life as species began to diversify and intermingle with other arthropods.

name: barns (parable)
originalDescription: the parable of the rich fool whose barns were pulled down was the story Jesus told to exemplify that greed was a serious impediment to entering the kingdom (165:4.2-7)
improvedDescription: The parable of the rich fool and his barns was told by Jesus to illustrate how greed and materialism can become serious impediments to entering the kingdom of heaven. The story depicts a wealthy man who built larger barns to store his riches but died before he could enjoy them.

name: barrenness
originalDescription: among ancients, barrenness attributed to spirit machinations (83:4.4) ancients always blamed barrenness on wife (83:7.2) The doll was first employed as a magic talisman by the barren wife. (88:6.4)
improvedDescription: Barrenness was greatly feared among ancient peoples and was always blamed on the wife rather than the husband. It was attributed to spirit machinations and led to various magical practices and religious ceremonies to ensure fertility.

name: barter
originalDescription: trade by barter followed seizure by raids. But a long period of piracy intervened between the early practices of silent barter and the later trade by modern exchange methods. A fetish was used to stand guard over the deposits of goods for silent barter. Such market places were secure against theft; nothing would be removed except by barter or purchase; with a fetish on guard the goods were always safe. (69:4.1-4) never did the barter in sex slaves advance society; such traffic was and is a racial disgrace, for at one and the same time it hindered the development of family life and polluted the biologic fitness of superior peoples. (69:5.10)
improvedDescription: Barter was an early form of trade that followed seizure by raids, involving the exchange of goods without money. It began with silent barter conducted at neutral market places guarded by fetishes, where women served as the first traders.

name: Bartholomew
originalDescription: Bartholomew is the father of Nathaniel the apostle, and he lived with his wife in Cana of Galilee. They were both elderly and suffering from some illness. He passed away shortly after Pentecost. Surely his death caused Nathaniel to prepare to travel abroad to preach, because until that moment he had been the only support for his parents. (139:6.2,9)
improvedDescription: Bartholomew was the father of the apostle Nathaniel and lived with his wife in Cana of Galilee. Both were elderly and infirm, and Nathaniel served as their only support until Bartholomew's death shortly after Pentecost, which enabled Nathaniel to begin his missionary travels to Mesopotamia and India.

name: Bartimeus
originalDescription: Bartimeus is the blind man to whom Jesus restored sight (171:5)
improvedDescription: Bartimeus was an elderly blind beggar in Jericho who had been blind from his youth and had heard much about Jesus' healing miracles. When Jesus passed through Jericho, Bartimeus appealed to him for the restoration of his sight.

name: basalt
originalDescription: earth’s crust rested directly upon a molten sea of basalt (58:5.3-4) seafloor basalt is a form of lava heavier than granite, and that makes the seafloor heavier than land masses, and thus sunk (58:5.6) The Palisades of the Hudson River were formed by the extrusion of basalt lava between these Triassic strata. (60:1.3)
improvedDescription: Basalt is a form of molten lava that forms a protective layer beneath Earth's outer crust and comprises the ocean floors. This dense volcanic rock is heavier than granite, which helps keep the continents floating above sea level and provides stability against severe earthquakes.

name: Bashan
originalDescription: The raid of Hebrews upon Og, the king of Bashan, was brutal and effective. The Hebrews “utterly destroyed” their enemies, taking all their property as spoils. (69:8.3)
improvedDescription: Bashan was an ancient kingdom whose ruler, King Og, was defeated in a brutal Hebrew raid. The Hebrews utterly destroyed their enemies and took all their property as spoils, exemplifying the barbaric warfare practices of supposedly civilized peoples.

name: basket weaving
originalDescription: It was from early huts that the subsequent idea of all sorts of basket weaving independently originated. (81:2.17-18)
improvedDescription: Basket weaving originated from the construction techniques used in early huts, where river races created circular pole frameworks interlaced with transverse reeds resembling large inverted baskets. This ancient craft developed independently from these practical building methods.

name: Basques
originalDescription: The Basques and the Berbers represent the survival of two branches of the blue race, but even these peoples have been thoroughly admixed with the Saharans. (80:9.11)
improvedDescription: The Basques represent one of two surviving branches of the blue race in southern Europe, along with the Berbers. Even these peoples have been thoroughly mixed with the Saharans through intermarriage and conquest.

name: Batanea
originalDescription: In the time of Jesus, Batanea was a region frequently traversed by him and his followers during their travels. Matthew Levi, a customs collector in Capernaum, had his tax office near the borders of Batanea, just east of the city. This area became significant during one of Jesus’ escapes from the Pharisees, who were searching for him in the vicinity of Capernaum after he had departed. While the Pharisees spent almost a week unsuccessfully searching for Jesus, he and his group journeyed northward through Batanea. (138:2.4) (154:7.4) On one memorable Sunday, Jesus and his twenty-four disciples landed near Kheresa and proceeded north to camp overnight in a familiar park south of Bethsaida-Julias. This spot, often used by the group in previous travels, provided a peaceful retreat. That evening, Jesus gathered his followers to outline plans for their upcoming tour, which would take them through Batanea and northern Galilee before reaching the Phoenician coast. (155:0.1)
improvedDescription: Batanea was a region east of Galilee frequently traversed by Jesus and his followers during their travels. Matthew Levi had his customs office near its borders, and Jesus used this area as a route for escaping the Pharisees and for planning missionary tours to northern territories.

name: bathing
originalDescription: Deformed or premature babies were regarded as the young of animals which had found their way into a woman’s body as a result of careless bathing or through malevolent spirit activity. (84:1.4) Before the Prince’s arrival, bathing had been an exclusively religious ceremonial. (66:5.21) (87:6.12) Greeks practiced the annual ritual bath (85:4.1) Jesus refused to go to public baths because of the sex promiscuity which there prevailed. (132:4.5) (133:3.6)
improvedDescription: Bathing was originally an exclusively religious ceremonial practice before becoming a health measure. Ancient peoples associated water with protection against ghosts and spirits, leading to ritual baths, baptism ceremonies, and various purification practices across different cultures.

name: Bathsheba
originalDescription: Bathsheba was the wife of Uriah the Hittite. King David fell in love with her and after having Uriah killed he married her. Bathsheba was the mother of Solomon, David's successor (97:9.10,15) Mary, the mother of Jesus, was a descendant of Bathsheba (122:1.2)
improvedDescription: Bathsheba was the wife of Uriah the Hittite whom King David married after having Uriah killed. She became the mother of Solomon, David's successor, and was an ancestor of Mary, the mother of Jesus.

name: bats
originalDescription: it is the only one diminutive mammals that are air navigators (49:2.17) (65:2.12)
improvedDescription: Bats are the only diminutive mammals that are air navigators, having evolved from agile reptilian dinosaurs. They represent one of the diverse evolutionary developments of placental mammals, alongside marine mammals like whales and seals.

name: Bautan
originalDescription: Bautan was a disciple of Gautama who imparted the traditions of Salem to Gautama (94:7.5)
improvedDescription: Bautan was a student of Gautama Buddha who during the second year of Gautama's school at Benares imparted the traditions of the Salem missionaries about the Melchizedek covenant with Abraham to his teacher.

name: Bay of Bengal
originalDescription: Dravidian shipping was pushing coastwise across the Arabian Sea to the Sumerian cities of the Persian Gulf and was venturing on the waters of the Bay of Bengal as far as the East Indies. An alphabet, together with the art of writing, was imported from Sumeria by these seafarers and merchants. (79:3.7)
improvedDescription: A body of water in the Indian Ocean where Dravidian seafarers ventured as far as the East Indies around 7000 B.C., establishing important maritime trading routes.

name: beads
originalDescription: The first fetishes were peculiarly marked pebbles, and “sacred stones” have ever since been sought by man; a string of beads was once a collection of sacred stones, a battery of charms. (88:1.2)
improvedDescription: Originally a string of sacred stones used as fetishes and charms, beads represented a collection of peculiarly marked pebbles believed to possess spiritual power.

name: beam
originalDescription: one of the unknown parables from Jesus was that of a foolish carpenter that spent time squaring a rotting timber (156:5.2) Jesus also mention wood and beams saying:  Make not the mistake of trying to pluck a mote out of your brother’s eye when there is a beam in your own eye. (140:3.17)
improvedDescription: Featured in Jesus' parable about a foolish carpenter who wastes time working on rotting timber, and in his teaching about removing the beam from one's own eye before trying to remove a mote from another's eye.

name: bears
originalDescription: 10 million years ago, during the last great global migration, bears from Asia entered North America via land bridges, while other species moved between continents. Later, during the glacial period 100,000 years ago, bears demonstrated remarkable adaptability by following the advancing and retreating glaciers. Alongside species like reindeer and mammoths, they endured extreme conditions, solidifying their status as one of the most resilient mammals of the era. (61:4.3) (61:7.13) (62:5.2)
improvedDescription: Large mammals that migrated between continents during various geological periods and demonstrated remarkable adaptability by following advancing and retreating glaciers during the ice age, alongside reindeer and mammoths.

name: beatitudes
originalDescription: The faith and the love of these beatitudes strengthen moral character and create happiness. The Urantia Book gives a detailed description and analysis of the beatitudes (140:5.6-24)
improvedDescription: Jesus' teachings from the Sermon on the Mount that describe eight blessed states of spiritual attitude, based on faith and love rather than law and duty, strengthening moral character and creating happiness.

name: beautiful gate (Jerusalem)
originalDescription: one of the gates of the inner enclosure of the temple in Jerusalem, which opened upon the court of the Gentiles (162:4.3)
improvedDescription: A gate in the inner enclosure of the Jerusalem temple that opened from the court of the women onto the court of the gentiles, used during ceremonial processions.

name: beauty
originalDescription: Beauty is always triumphant over ugliness in the hearts of all who are illuminated by the love of truth. (156:5.5) Beauty aroused the envy of spirits; it betokened sinful human pride. (87:5.7) We can consciously augment Adjuster harmony by sincerely basing the human life on the highest consciousness of truth, beauty, and goodness, and then co-ordinating these qualities of divinity through wisdom, worship, faith, and love. (110:3.7) The concepts of truth, beauty, and goodness are not inherent in either physics or chemistry. (195:6.11) The discernment of supreme beauty is the discovery and integration of reality: The discernment of the divine goodness in the eternal truth, that is ultimate beauty. (2:7.8) Jesus impressed upon his apostles the great value of beauty as an influence leading to the urge to worship, especially with children (167:6.5-6) The understandings of truth, beauty, and goodness, morality, ethics, duty, love, divinity, origin, existence, purpose, destiny, time, space, even Deity, are only relatively true. (115:1.2) Beauty is most religious when it is most simple and naturelike. (167:6.6) the mortal attempt to depict the human recognition of divine beauty can never be truly satisfying if ununified (44:7.3) pleasing surroundings contribute to happiness (140:4.10) service of beauty is not a substitute for genuine religious experience, spiritual reality (196:3.23) The sincere pursuit of goodness, beauty, and truth leads to God. (195:6.17) Truth, fact, and relationship are intellectually inseparable and associated with the philosophic concepts of beauty. (44:7.2) Variety is essential to the concept of beauty. (56:10.3-4) defined as
improvedDescription: One of the supreme values along with truth and goodness that represents the harmonious recognition of divine reality, essential for spiritual growth and worship, and always triumphant over ugliness in hearts illuminated by truth.

name: beavers
originalDescription: Certain North American Indian tribes believed they originated from beavers and coyotes. (74:8.5)
improvedDescription: Large rodents that evolved during the Cenozoic era and were among the animal totems revered by certain North American Indian tribes who believed they originated from beavers and coyotes.

name: Bedouins
originalDescription: The ancient Bedouins believed that a nature spirit produced the sand whirls (85:4.2)
improvedDescription: Ancient nomadic Arab peoples who believed that nature spirits produced sand whirls and maintained strong beliefs in nature spirits that influenced Hebrew theology.

name: beeches
originalDescription: 65 million years ago, during a period of significant plant evolution, beeches (trees that form beech forests) first emerged as part of the dominance of angiosperms. These trees, along with birch, oak, and others, marked a transformative era in plant history. Seed-bearing trees like the beech were crucial to the plant world, comparable in importance to the evolutionary role of early human ancestors in the animal kingdom. The beech, as part of this global spread of flowering plants, became one of the defining species of modern forests. (60:3.19)
improvedDescription: Trees that first appeared 65 million years ago during a period of significant plant evolution when angiosperms predominated. Beeches, along with birch, oak, walnut, and other seed-bearing trees, were crucial to the plant world and comparable in evolutionary importance to early human ancestors in the animal kingdom.

name: Beelzebub
originalDescription: Beelzebub was the leader of the apostate Secondary Midwayers (53:1.5) (77:7.4) Jesus was accused of performing miracles by the power of Beelzebub (153:4.2-3) (169:0.7) Jesus warned the apostles that they would be called associates of Beelzebub (150:4.2)
improvedDescription: The leader of the disloyal secondary midway creatures who allied themselves with the rebellious Caligastia. Jesus was accused by the Pharisees of performing miracles by the power of Beelzebub, whom they called the prince of devils.

name: Beeroth
originalDescription: Population through which Jesus passed during his journey through Palestine in the year 25 (134:7.5)
improvedDescription: A town through which Jesus passed during his final tour as a private individual through the heart of Palestine in the year 25, traveling from Beersheba in the south to Dan in the north.

name: Beersheba
originalDescription: Population through which Jesus passed during his journey through Palestine in the year 25 (134:7.4) (190:1.8)
improvedDescription: A city in southern Palestine where Jesus remained for a week during his journey from Gaza, and later served as the southern boundary point for dispatching believers to spread the gospel message.

name: beggar of Naples
originalDescription: When Jesus was in Naples he give a coin to a beggar that he said was not of normal mind and so he lacked the ability to respond to spirit leading (130:8.4)
improvedDescription: A street beggar in Naples to whom Jesus gave a coin but refused to speak comfortingly, explaining that the man was not of normal mind and lacked the ability to respond to spirit leading or perceive spiritual teaching.

name: begging
originalDescription: begging was almost unknown among ancient tribes; they practiced an automatic commmunism (69:9.1)
improvedDescription: A practice that was almost unknown among ancient primitive tribes because they practiced an automatic communalism that prevented pauperism and want.

name: beginning of reality
improvedDescription: The initial emergence or genesis of reality as a fundamental concept in cosmology and philosophy.

name: behavior
improvedDescription: The manner of acting or conducting oneself, particularly in relation to moral and spiritual development.

name: being
originalDescription: Jesus talked trying to show the twelve what they must be, not what they must do. They knew only a religion that imposed the doing of certain things as the means of attaining righteousness—salvation. But Jesus would reiterate, “In the kingdom you must be righteous in order to do the work”. (140:10.1)
improvedDescription: The state of existence or spiritual condition that Jesus emphasized to his apostles as fundamental to righteousness, teaching that in the kingdom one must be righteous in order to do the work, rather than doing certain things to attain righteousness.

name: Beirut
originalDescription: Town through which Jesus passed with the twelve during their flight north on May 22 and August 4, 29 (156:3.1)
improvedDescription: A coast city where Jesus visited a Syrian believer named Malach for three or four days while his apostles and evangelists taught in nearby Porphyreon and Heldua during their ministry in the region.

name: Beit Adis
originalDescription: Beit Adis is a village near the rocky cavern where Jesus lived during his forty-day retreat before the preaching (136:4.14)
improvedDescription: Beit Adis is a village near the rocky cavern where Jesus lived during his forty-day retreat before beginning his public preaching ministry.

name: Beit Jenn
originalDescription: Beit Jenn is a town near which Jesus set up camp while on Mount Hermon in the summer of 25 (134:8.1)
improvedDescription: Beit Jenn is a village in the foothills of Mount Hermon where Jesus established his headquarters in August of A.D. 25 before ascending the mountain for his solitary sojourn.

name: Bel
originalDescription: Babylonian god, also called Bel; Mesopotamian god of the earth; equivalent to Semitic Baal; formed a triad with Ea and Anu (95:1.4) (97:6.2)
improvedDescription: Bel is the Babylonian god of the earth, equivalent to the Semitic Baal, who formed a supreme triad with Ea and Anu representing earth, sea, and sky in Mesopotamian religion.

name: Bel-Marduk
originalDescription: The Mesopotamians reduced their gods to the concept of Belo-Marduc, a god who fused several (96:0.1) there were temples to Belo-Marduc in Babylon (95:1.10)
improvedDescription: Bel-Marduk is the centralized Mesopotamian deity concept that resulted from reducing their multitude of gods into a more unified monotheistic conception, with temples dedicated to this god in Babylon.

name: Belgium
originalDescription: The most northerly settlement of the Danubians was at Liege in Belgium. (80:8.4)
improvedDescription: Belgium is a European country that was partially under water 10 million years ago and later became the location of Liege, the most northerly settlement of the Danubian peoples.

name: belief
originalDescription: Belief has attained the level of faith when it motivates life and shapes the mode of living. The acceptance of a teaching as true is not faith; that is mere belief. (101:8.1) Belief is always limiting and binding; faith is expanding and releasing. Belief fixates, faith liberates. (101:8.2) Convictions about God may be arrived at through wise reasoning, but the individual becomes God-knowing only by faith, through personal experience. (102:6) After all, it is what one believes rather than what one knows that determines conduct and dominates personal performances. (99:4.5) The higher a creature’s education, the more respect he has for the knowledge, experience, and opinions of others. (25:3.12) beliefs, when they once become a part of one’s religion, are hard to get away from. (153:3.6) Belief may not be able to resist doubt and withstand fear, but faith is always triumphant over doubting, for faith is both positive and living. (102:6.7) The religions of authority require of men uniformity in belief, but this is impossible of realization in the present state of the world. (155:6.9) It requires time for men and women to effect radical and extensive changes in their basic and fundamental concepts of social conduct, philosophic attitudes, and religious convictions. (152:6.1)
improvedDescription: Belief is the acceptance of a teaching as true, which becomes faith when it motivates life and shapes one's mode of living. Belief is limiting and binding, while faith is expanding and releasing, and it is what one believes rather than what one knows that determines conduct and dominates personal performance.

name: believers
improvedDescription: Believers are individuals who accept religious teachings as true and demonstrate their faith through their conduct and personal performances.

name: believer’s prayer
improvedDescription: The believer's prayer refers to the personal prayers offered by those who have faith in religious teachings and seek communion with the divine.

name: belligerence
improvedDescription: Belligerence is an aggressive, hostile attitude or behavior that manifests as combativeness and readiness for conflict or war.

name: bells
originalDescription: When the Chinese made ready to cast a bell, custom decreed the sacrifice of at least one maiden for the purpose of improving the tone of the bell; the girl chosen was thrown alive into the molten metal. (89:6.4)
improvedDescription: Bells were metal instruments cast by the Chinese, who traditionally practiced human sacrifice by throwing a maiden alive into the molten metal to improve the bell's tone.

name: Benares
originalDescription: Gautama founded school at Benares (94:7.5)
improvedDescription: The city in India where Gautama Buddha founded his school and began sending students out in groups of sixty to proclaim the teachings of salvation through faith.

name: benediction
originalDescription: Immanuel’s benediction for Michael Jesus’ benediction to apostles
improvedDescription: A blessing or farewell prayer, including Immanuel's benediction to Michael before his bestowal on Urantia and Jesus' final blessing to his apostles before his ascension.

name: Bengal
originalDescription: In Bengal many worship a meteor which fell to earth in A. (85:1.2)
improvedDescription: A region in India where many people worship a meteor that fell to earth in A.D. 1880, representing early humanity's reverence for meteoric stones and celestial phenomena.

name: Berbers
originalDescription: The current Berbers are a people very mixed with the Saharans, but they descend from one of the purest remnants of the blue race (80:9.11)
improvedDescription: A North African people who represent one of the survival branches of the blue race, though they have been thoroughly mixed with the Saharans over time.

name: Bering Strait land bridge
originalDescription: lemurs and turtles migrated North America over the Bering land bridge (62:1.2) (60:2.13) red race migrated North America over land bridge between 100,000 – 85,000 B.C. (64:6.5) (79:5.6) Bering Strait land bridge emerge and submerge several times (60:1.6,13) (60:3.8) (61:0.2) (61:1.12) (61:3.4) (61:4.6)
improvedDescription: A land connection between Asia and North America that emerged and submerged multiple times throughout geological history, serving as a migration route for early lemurs, the red race, and various animal species.

name: Bernice
originalDescription: Berenice was the mother of Justa, in whose house Jesus stayed when he was in Sidon (156:2.2)
improvedDescription: The mother of Justa, in whose house north of Sidon Jesus stayed while teaching the twenty-four apostles during their mission work in that city.

name: bestowal
improvedDescription: A mission undertaken by Paradise Sons of God, particularly Creator Sons and Avonal Sons, involving incarnation in mortal form to serve and understand the creatures of their domains.

name: bestowal attendants
originalDescription: Paradise Avonals, but not Creator Sons, when on a bestowal mission are always accompanied by a corps of 144 supreme seraphim who attend then in Avonal missions (39:1.4-6)
improvedDescription: A corps of 144 completion seraphim who accompany Paradise Avonal Sons during their bestowal missions, serving as chiefs of all other Son-Spirit ministers and coordinating all seraphic activities during the bestowal.

name: Beth-Marion
originalDescription: Beth-Marion was a fanatical Phoenician woman in her faith in Jesus who nearly drowned trying to walk on water (148:8.4)
improvedDescription: A fanatical Phoenician woman who became so overwhelmed by her faith in Jesus that she lost her mental balance and nearly drowned attempting to walk on water.

name: Beth-Meon
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the cities in Perea where Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers conducted their final three-month ministry mission before Jesus went to Jerusalem.

name: Beth-Nimrah
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during the Perean mission, which lasted almost three months from January 3, A.D. 30 until Jesus's death.

name: Beth-Peor
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during the Perean mission, which lasted almost three months from January 3, A.D. 30 until Jesus's death.

name: Beth-shean
originalDescription: Beth-shean is an ancient Hebrew city that was later part of the Greek cities of Scythopolis. Jesus was amazed at the beauty of this city when he visited it with his father Joseph for the first time when he was ten years old, on 5 AD. (124:3.6-9)
improvedDescription: An ancient Hebrew city that later became the Greek city of Scythopolis in the Decapolis. Jesus visited here with his father Joseph at age ten and was amazed by its beauty, marble temples, and amphitheater, though his enthusiasm for the Greek athletic games troubled his father.

name: Beth-zur
originalDescription: Beth-sur was the home of Ezda, an orphan boy adopted by John the Baptist (135:3.1)
improvedDescription: The home of Ezda, an orphan boy who was adopted by John the Baptist and helped care for John's herds of sheep and goats in the wilderness.

name: Bethabara
improvedDescription: An alternative name for Bethany beyond Jordan, a ford crossing of the Jordan River where John the Baptist preached and baptized.

name: Bethany beyond Jordan
originalDescription: Called Bethany ford of river Jordan, Bethany in Perea, and also Bethabara, it was a ford in Jordan river (171:2.1) John preached four months at Bethany ford before starting north up the Jordan. He returned a year later to preach there again. (135:6.5) (135:10.2) Jesus and the apostles preached in this place for the first time in February 27, in the same place where John preached (141:7.1-2) The disciples of John remained at Bethany beyond the Jordan, teaching and baptizing the multitudes. (141:9.2)
improvedDescription: A ford crossing of the Jordan River, also called Bethabara, where John the Baptist preached for four months and later returned to preach again.
