Jesus and his apostles also taught and preached here for four weeks in February A.D. 27, and John's disciples remained here teaching and baptizing after Jesus departed.

name: Bethany (near Jerusalem)
originalDescription: Bethany was about two miles from the temple of Jerusalem (172:3.1) Jesus met Mary, Martha, and Lazarus of Bethany when he and his parents happened to stop near the three brothers' home on their first trip to Jerusalem in April of the year 7 (124:6.9) Lazarus and his sisters were the children of Simon, a well-to-do and honorable Jew, one who had been the leading resident of the little village of Bethany. (168:0.4) almost every person from Bethany became believer in Jesus (172:3.1) Jesus appeared in Bethany to his brother James, his third appearance, and then to twenty people including family and friends, his fourth appearance (190:2.5,6)
improvedDescription: A small village about two miles from Jerusalem's temple, home to Lazarus, Martha, and Mary. Jesus first met the family during a childhood visit to Jerusalem and maintained a lifelong friendship with them. Nearly every resident became a believer in Jesus, and it was here that he performed the resurrection of Lazarus and made several post-resurrection appearances.

name: Bethel
originalDescription: Jesus passed through here when he took his brother James to Jerusalem for Passover in the year 12 (127:3.3) Jesus and the apostles passed through here during their preaching through Samaria in the year 27 (143:0.1)
improvedDescription: A town in Samaria where Jesus passed through with his brother James on their way to Jerusalem for Passover in A.D. 12, and later where Jesus and the twelve apostles preached for several days during their Samaritan ministry in A.D. 27.

name: Bethesda (pool)
originalDescription: Site of a supposed miracle of Jesus, in which in reality Jesus simply preached to a group of sick people who were waiting for a miracle to be healed (147:3.1,6)
improvedDescription: A hot spring pool in Jerusalem surrounded by five porches where sick people gathered hoping for healing. The pool's periodic bubbling due to underground gas was believed to have supernatural healing powers, and Jesus preached to the afflicted gathered there on a Sabbath afternoon.

name: Bethlehem (Judea)
originalDescription: Bethlehem is the city of birth of king David (122:4.3) (135:7.1) Upon the basis of a remarkable astronomic conjunction of planets the appealing legend of the star of Bethlehem was constructed (122:8.7) Jesus was born in Bethlehem on August 21, 7 BC, at noon (122:7.7-8) (122:8) True, Joseph did go to the City of David, Bethlehem, to be registered for the Roman census, but that was because, six generations previously, Joseph’s paternal ancestor of that generation, being an orphan, was adopted by one Zadoc, who was a direct descendant of David; hence was Joseph also accounted as of the “house of David. (122:4.3) (122:8.4) Herod killed sixteen baby boys at Bethlehem in his attempt to kill the infant Jesus (122:10.3) Mary had never fully given up the idea that Jesus ought to grow up in Bethlehem, the City of David. (123:0.5) The people living in Galilee did not know that Jesus had been born in Bethlehem.  They always supposed the Master had been born at Nazareth, as did the vast majority of his disciples, but the twelve knew the facts. (142:8.2) it is in Bethlehem that Abner, the leader of John's disciples, established his headquarters about September 29; also there was the headquarters of the body of messengers led by David Zebedee (159:6.4) (162:9.2)
improvedDescription: The City of David and birthplace of Jesus on August 21, 7 B.C. Joseph brought Mary here for the Roman census registration since his ancestor had been adopted into David's lineage. Later, Herod killed sixteen baby boys in Bethlehem trying to eliminate the infant Jesus, and the city later served as headquarters for Abner and the messengers after Jesus's ministry began.

name: Bethlehem (Galilee)
originalDescription: Joseph father of Jesus worked at a village called Bethlehem, in Galilee (123:3.8)
improvedDescription: A village in Galilee where Joseph, father of Jesus, worked as a carpenter and did considerable building projects. The apostles also carried on their personal evangelical work in this Bethlehem of Galilee during their ministry.

name: Bethphage
originalDescription: Bethphage is a hamlet short distance northwest of Bethany, a little off the main road (168:3.7) (172:3.6) Jesus’ Palm Sunday donkey has obtained from Bethphage (172:3.6)
improvedDescription: A small hamlet located northwest of Bethany, slightly off the main road. This is where Jesus obtained the donkey for his Palm Sunday entry into Jerusalem, and where he and his disciples stayed during their final visits to the Jerusalem area.

name: Bethsaida
originalDescription: Bethsaida was the fishing harbor of Capernaum (139:1.1) here took place on January 17, 28, Saturday, an extraordinary event for an entire universe: first the impressive sermon of Jesus in the synagogue of Capernaum on “The will of the Father who is in heaven”; and then Jesus' healing of 683 people at sunset in Bethsaida (145:3) Bethsaida is also where Jesus healed the paralytic who was lowered from the roof (148:9) here Jesus made his thirteenth morontia appearance, to ten of the apostles, near the landing (192:1-2) John Zebedee, and presumably the entire Zebedee family, were from Bethsaida (139:4.1) Mary the mother of Jesus died in Bethsaida, less than a year after Jesus' death, in the year 31 (187:4.7) Philip the fifth apostle lived at Bethsaida (137:2.3) (139:5.1) Zebedee’s home was at fishing headquarters of Bethsaida (129:1.4) the encampment at Bethsaida (148) archeology
improvedDescription: The fishing harbor of Capernaum and home to several apostles including Andrew, Peter, Philip, and John Zebedee. This was the site of Jesus' extraordinary healing of 683 people at sunset, his thirteenth morontia appearance to the apostles, and where Mary the mother of Jesus spent her final years.

name: Bethsaida-Julias
originalDescription: there was a beautiful park called Magadan south of Bethsaida-Julias (155:0.1) (157:2.1) south of the city of Bethsaida-Julias, in Magadan Park, is where the miraculous feeding of some five thousand people took place (152:2.1) Jesus had very harsh words towards the inhabitants of this city: woe upon the light-rejecting inhabitants of Chorazin, Bethsaida-Julias, and Capernaum, the cities which did not well receive these messengers (163:6.5)
improvedDescription: A city near the beautiful Magadan Park where Jesus performed the miraculous feeding of five thousand people. Jesus pronounced woe upon this city along with Chorazin and Capernaum for rejecting his message and messengers.

name: betrayal
originalDescription: Treason—the “selling out” or betrayal of one’s tribal associates—was the first capital crime. (70:10.15) Of all the sorrows of a trusting man, none are so terrible as to be ‘wounded in the house of a trusted friend. (149:6.12) Jewish law forbade testimony of traitors (184:3.6) Solemnities of Trust reflect individual’s possibilities of trustworthiness and betrayal (28:6.14) betrayal to Jesus betrayal on a neighboring planet
improvedDescription: The act of treacherously betraying one's associates or trust, considered among the first capital crimes in human society. Jesus experienced the ultimate betrayal when Judas delivered him to his enemies, fulfilling the sorrowful truth that nothing wounds a trusting person more than being betrayed by a trusted friend.

name: betrayer
improvedDescription: One who betrays trust or commits treachery against another, particularly referring to Judas Iscariot who betrayed Jesus to the religious authorities.

name: betrothal
improvedDescription: The formal engagement or promise of marriage between two individuals, a preliminary stage before the actual marriage ceremony.

name: bewitching
originalDescription: Jesus denounced belief in bewitching (150:3.12)
improvedDescription: A superstitious practice involving the casting of spells or magical enchantments. Jesus exposed and denounced belief in bewitching along with other forms of ignorant superstition.

name: Bible
improvedDescription: The collection of sacred scriptures containing the Old and New Testaments, regarded as holy writings by Christians and referenced throughout religious teachings.

name: bigamy
improvedDescription: The practice of being married to more than one spouse simultaneously, which was sometimes practiced among primitive peoples in various marriage arrangements.

name: Bildad
originalDescription: Bildad was one of Job's friends who, faced with Job's misfortune, offered him a series of depressing advice for Job (148:6.5,9)
improvedDescription: Bildad was one of Job's three friends who offered counsel during Job's suffering. He provided depressing theological advice, arguing that God cannot be unjust and that Job's afflictions must be punishment for sin, either his own or his children's.

name: bipeds
originalDescription: There are great differences between the mortals of the different worlds, even among those belonging to the same intellectual and physical types, but all mortals of will dignity are erect animals, bipeds. (49:4.1)
improvedDescription: All mortals of will dignity are erect animals walking on two legs. Despite great differences between mortals of different worlds, even among those of the same intellectual and physical types, this bipedal characteristic is universal among creatures capable of moral choice.

name: birches
originalDescription: 65 million years ago, during a period of significant plant evolution, birches (trees that form birch forests) first emerged as part of the rise of angiosperms. These trees, along with beeches, oaks, and others, ushered in a transformative era in plant history. Seed-bearing trees like the birch played a vital role in the plant world, comparable in importance to the evolutionary impact of early human ancestors in the animal kingdom. The birch, as part of the widespread dominance of flowering plants, became one of the key species shaping modern forests. (60:3.19)
improvedDescription: Birch trees first appeared 65 million years ago during a period of great plant evolution when angiosperms predominated. Along with beech, oak, walnut, and other modern trees, birches were part of the flowering plant revolution that transformed Earth's flora.

name: birds
originalDescription: birds' evolution man and birds birds in heavens types named
improvedDescription: Birds evolved from reptiles in one of the greatest leaps of prehuman evolution. The first true bird appeared 55 million years ago as a small pigeonlike creature that became the ancestor of all bird life, marking the beginning of the age of birds.

name: birthday text
improvedDescription: A reference to a text from Isaiah that was associated with Jesus's birthday.

name: birthmarks
originalDescription: The evil eye gave origin to the first superstitions respecting prenatal marking of children, maternal impressions, and the cult was at one time well-nigh universal. (87:5.5)
improvedDescription: Prenatal markings on children that ancient peoples believed were caused by the evil eye and maternal impressions during pregnancy. This superstition was nearly universal and gave rise to early beliefs about supernatural influences on unborn children.

name: bison
originalDescription: Bison, an iconic species of North America, played a pivotal role in the continent's ecosystem during the ice age. As ruminants spread across the land, bison thrived alongside deer, oxen, camels, and other species. However, the advancing and retreating glaciers of the ice age challenged their survival, forcing bison to migrate repeatedly. These animals adapted to the harsh conditions, following the ice back and forth across the land. While many species, including mammoths and mastodons, succumbed to extinction, bison endured, eventually becoming integral to the lives of indigenous peoples. Early humans hunted bison by surrounding herds, a practice that foreshadowed the domestication of animals. (61:3.5) (61:5.7) (61:7.13-14) (69:7.2)
improvedDescription: Large ruminant animals that thrived in North America during the ice age alongside deer, oxen, and camels. Bison survived the glacial periods by migrating back and forth with the advancing and retreating ice, and were later hunted by early humans who surrounded entire herds.

name: Bithynia
originalDescription: Matthew the apostle preached in Bithynia (139:7.10)
improvedDescription: An ancient region in Asia Minor where the apostle Matthew preached the gospel and baptized believers during his missionary journeys after the persecution of Christians in Jerusalem.

name: Black Forest mountains
originalDescription: 210 million years ago, the Black Forest mountains began forming, alongside the Vosges and Ural ranges. Remnants of older mountains can be found across Great Britain and Europe. (59:5.12)
improvedDescription: A mountain range in Europe that began forming 210 million years ago, along with the Vosges and Ural mountains, during a period of significant geological activity.

name: Black Friday
originalDescription: The most holy day was Black Friday, the “day of blood,” commemorating the self-inflicted death of Attis. (98:4.7)
improvedDescription: The most sacred day in Phrygian mystery religions, known as the "day of blood" that commemorated the self-inflicted death of the god Attis. This bloody festival was followed three days later by celebrations of Attis's resurrection.

name: black race
improvedDescription: One of the six colored races that evolved from the original Sangik peoples on Urantia, genetically related to the indigo race.

name: Black Sea
originalDescription: Andonites entered Europe via region of Black Sea (80:9.6) the Mesopotamian Andites worked the iron ores extracted in the Black Sea regions (80:6.3) the Black Sea once was an extension of Mediterranean Sea (64:4.10)
improvedDescription: A body of water between Europe and Asia that was once an extension of the Mediterranean Sea and served as a route for Andonite migrations into Europe around 3000 B.C., while its regions provided iron ore for Mesopotamian Andites.

name: black smiths
originalDescription: black smiths were the early metal workers and magicians (69:3.5) they were the early specialists; were the first nonreligious group to enjoy special privileges. But through gross abuse of these privileges the smiths became universally hated (69:3.6,9)
improvedDescription: Early metalworkers who competed with medicine men as magicians and became the first nonreligious group to enjoy special privileges, but through abuse of these privileges became universally hated and were eventually driven from villages.

name: Blackfoot Indians
originalDescription: Blackfoot Indians are descendants of Onamonalonton (64:6.7)
improvedDescription: A Native American tribe descended from Onamonalonton, the spiritual leader who brought temporary peace among the American red men about sixty-five thousand years ago.

name: blame
originalDescription: Judas never acquired a philosophic technique for meeting disappointment. Instead of accepting disappointments as a regular and commonplace feature of human existence, he unfailingly resorted to the practice of blaming someone in particular, or his associates as a group, for all his personal difficulties and disappointments. (193:4.7) How prone is man, when he is confronted with the failures of his own making, to put the blame upon others, oftentimes upon those who least deserve it! (176:3.9) Jesus asked to refrain from condemnation: will you call upon the Lord, and he shall answer; you will cry out, and he shall say—Here am I. And all this he will do if you refrain from oppression, condemnation, and vanity (147:8.4)
improvedDescription: The tendency to hold others responsible for one's failures and disappointments rather than accepting them as normal human experiences, a practice Jesus warned against and that Judas habitually employed.

name: blasphemy
originalDescription: Jesus was charged with blasphemy (148:9.3) (153:3.6) (164:5.3) (169:0.5) (184:5.7) (186:2.7) Jesus said this about it: “Say what you will about the Son of Man, and it shall be forgiven you; but he who presumes to blaspheme against God shall hardly find forgiveness. When men go so far as knowingly to ascribe the doings of God to the forces of evil, such deliberate rebels will hardly seek forgiveness for their sins. (153:4.3) (165:3.6)
improvedDescription: The charge leveled against Jesus by religious authorities for claiming divine authority and equality with God, though Jesus taught that knowingly ascribing God's works to evil forces was the true unforgivable blasphemy.

name: blemishes
originalDescription: The sale of animals in the temple of Jerusalem prospered because, when the worshiper purchased such an animal, although the price might be somewhat high, no more fees had to be paid, and he could be sure the intended sacrifice would not be rejected on the ground of possessing real or technical blemishes. (173:1.1-2)
improvedDescription: Physical defects that disqualified animals from being acceptable temple sacrifices according to Levitical law, leading to a lucrative business of selling certified unblemished animals directly at the temple.

name: blind
originalDescription: Jesus said: You have heard it said: ‘If the blind lead the blind, they both shall fall into the pit. (140:3.18) (153:3.5) (175:1.15) Jesus restored sight to Bartimeus, a blind elderly man from Jericho (171:5) Jesus restored sight to Josiah, a blind beggar that solicit alm by the temple, and did this on the Sabbath (164:3) The rabbis taught that all such cases of blindness from birth were caused by sin. (164:3.3) Jesus said: When you give a banquet, sometimes bid the poor, the maimed, and the blind. In this way you shall be blessed in your heart, for you well know that the lame and the halt cannot repay you for your loving ministry (167:1.5)
improvedDescription: Those lacking physical sight, whom Jesus healed on multiple occasions including Bartimeus and Josiah, teaching that spiritual blindness was more serious than physical blindness and encouraging care for those who cannot repay such kindness.

name: blood
originalDescription: ancient beliefs salvation by shedding blood scientific aspects of blood
improvedDescription: Ancient peoples considered blood the seat of the soul and creator of life, leading to blood-bond concepts of relationships, primitive salvation beliefs involving blood sacrifice, and various medicinal uses, though it also serves essential biological functions in oxygen transport and chemical reactions.

name: blood drinking
originalDescription: The ancient social brotherhoods were based on the rite of blood drinking; the early Jewish fraternity was a sacrificial blood affair. (89:9.3) belief blood was seat of soul gave origin to the practice of blood drinking (84:2.1) The embryonic state was made possible by the decline of the blood bond in favor of the territorial, and such tribal federations were usually firmly cemented by conquest. (71:1.23) The first treaties of peace were the “blood bonds”. (70:3.10) The adherents of the cult of Mithra worshiped in caves and other secret places, chanting hymns, mumbling magic, eating the flesh of the sacrificial animals, and drinking the blood. (98:5.4) Blood drinking became common, and it was customary to mix the “edible” parts of the body with medicines. (89:5.11) blood drinking as a seal of personal friendship and as a token of group loyalty (66:5.22) (70:3.7)
improvedDescription: An ancient ritual practice used to seal friendships, form brotherhoods, and create peace treaties between tribes, stemming from the belief that blood contained the soul and could transfer spiritual qualities between individuals.

name: bloody way
originalDescription: Name given to way from Jerusalen to Jericho (182:2.11)
improvedDescription: The name given to the dangerous road from Jerusalem to Jericho, known for its hazardous conditions, especially when traveled at night.

name: blue race
originalDescription: blue race is what today is called Cro-Magnoids (80:3) The early blue men were responsive to the persuasions of the teachers of Prince Caligastia’s staff and were thrown into great confusion by the subsequent perverted teachings of those traitorous leaders. (64:6.22) The blue man most of all profited by the Dalamatia teachings, the red man to some extent, and the black man least of all. (68:0.3) The blue men were scattered all over Europe, but their better centers of culture were situated in the then fertile valleys of the Mediterranean basin and in northwestern Europe. (78:1.8) In Mexico, Central America, and in the mountains of South America the later and more enduring civilizations were founded by a race predominantly red but containing a considerable admixture of the yellow, orange, and blue. (79:5.8) The blue races, when mixed with the Andonite stock, were decidedly broad-headed. (81:4.2-3) usually in all planets the blue race subdues indigo race (51:4.6) achievements blend with Andonites blend with Andites the white race nature Orlandof
improvedDescription: One of the six colored evolutionary races on Urantia, also known as the blue men. They were great inventors who developed the spear and many arts of civilization, combining the brain power of the red man with the soul and sentiment of the yellow man. The blue race was scattered throughout Europe and became the ancestors of the Cro-Magnons, eventually contributing significantly to the white races through admixture with Andite and Adamic stock.

name: board of animal domestication
improvedDescription: A council of the Caligastia one hundred dedicated to selecting and breeding animals best adapted to help humans with transportation, food supply, and agricultural cultivation.

name: board of planetary supervisors
improvedDescription: A governing body that provides oversight and supervision of planetary affairs.

name: boats
originalDescription: mariners people Jesus the apostles
improvedDescription: Watercraft used for transportation and fishing. The blue men were expert boatbuilders, and Jesus himself was an experienced boatman who created new boat designs and improved boatmaking methods while working with Zebedee, revolutionizing craft construction on the Sea of Galilee.

name: body, human
originalDescription: body is the living electrochemical mechanism of animal nature and origin. (0:5.7) physical characteristics spiritual characteristics after death the morontial body fabricated bodies
improvedDescription: The material or physical organism of humans, described as the living electrochemical mechanism of animal nature and origin. The human body serves as the earthly temple for the indwelling spirit and provides the physical foundation for personality expression and spiritual development.

name: Bon
originalDescription: Bon was a member of the hundred attendees of Caligastia on Urantia; he presided over the council for the domestication and utilization of animals (66:5.4) he and his entire ten-member council were lost in Lucifer's rebellion, joining his cause (67:4.1)
improvedDescription: A member of the Caligastia one hundred who directed the board of animal domestication and utilization on Urantia. He and his entire council of ten members were lost in the Lucifer rebellion, joining Caligastia's cause.

name: bond servants
originalDescription: Bond servants were among the earliest form of property to be accumulated, and in olden days debt slavery extended even to the control of the body after death. (69:5.8)
improvedDescription: An early form of property and debt slavery where individuals became bound servants to their creditors. In ancient times, debt slavery was so extensive that it could even extend control over the debtor's body after death.

name: bones
originalDescription: Primates, the parents of this first human couple, made use of sharp spicules of stone, flint, and bone. (63:1.2) The ancients always revered the bones of their leaders, and the skeletal remains of saints and heroes are still regarded with superstitious awe by many. Even today, pilgrimages are made to the tombs of great men. (88:2.1) The bones of the dead were believed to be very magical. (88:5.2) Latins preserved the bones of heroes and later on those of the Christian saints. (98:3.4)
improvedDescription: Skeletal remains that were considered magical and sacred by ancient peoples. The bones of leaders, heroes, and saints were revered and believed to possess supernatural powers, leading to their preservation and veneration in tombs and shrines.

name: books
originalDescription: Books mentioned in The Urantia Book: * Genesis (74:8) | Genesis, Book of * Deuteronomy (97:8.3) (150:8.8) (153:2.1) | Deuteronomy * Isaiah (97:7.4) | Isaiah (first); Isaiah (second) * Book of Hebrew Psalms (95:2.10) (95:4.5) (95:5.7) (96:7.3) (97:7.9) (123:5.3) (165:4.8) (187:5.2) | Psalms * Ecclesiastes (97:8.2) | Ecclesiastes * The Book of Wisdom (95:4.3,5) | Ecclesiastes * The Book of Proverbs (95:4.3,5) | Proverbs * The Book of Job (96:7.1) | Job (book) * Acts of the Apostles (121:8.8) | Acts, Book of * The Book of Enoch (126:3.6,8) (136:1.6) | Enoch:Book of * The Book of Hebrews (93:9.11) (196:2.1) | Hebrews, Book of * Revelations (139:4.14) | Revelations, Book of * The Book of the Dead (95:2.5) | Dead, Book of the * The Book of Life. The Custodians of knowledge are that living books (27:5.1) the best books
improvedDescription: Written works and collections of religious and historical texts mentioned throughout The Urantia Book, including Hebrew scriptures like Genesis, Deuteronomy, Psalms, and Proverbs, as well as other works such as the Book of Enoch, Acts of the Apostles, and various wisdom literature that influenced spiritual and cultural development.

name: bookkeeper
originalDescription: God is a Father, not a great and supreme bookkeeper who is chiefly engaged in making damaging entries against his erring children on earth, recordings of sin and evil to be used against them when he subsequently sits in judgment upon them as the just Judge of all creation (141:4.1)
improvedDescription: A metaphor used by Jesus to contrast with the true nature of God as a loving Father. Jesus taught that God is not a supreme bookkeeper who keeps damaging records of human errors to use against people in judgment, but rather a loving Father to each individual.

name: boredom
originalDescription: Monotony is indicative of immaturity of the creative imagination and inactivity of intellectual co-ordination with the spiritual endowment. (14:5.8)
improvedDescription: Monotony that indicates immaturity of the creative imagination and inactivity of intellectual coordination with spiritual endowment.

name: Borneans
originalDescription: The Borneans still provide a courier companion; a slave is speared to death to make the ghost journey with his deceased master. (87:2.8)
improvedDescription: A people who practiced the ritual of killing a slave to accompany their deceased master on the ghost journey, providing a courier companion for the afterlife.

name: bosom of the Father
originalDescription: Divinington. This world is, in a unique sense, the “bosom of the Father,” the personal-communion sphere of the Universal Father (13:1.4) (13:2.7) (20:4.3) (39:2.13) God lives in every one of his spirit-born sons. All of his creature personalities have access to the “bosom of the Father. ” This refers to the personality circuit, whenever, wherever, and however contacted, or otherwise entails personal, self-conscious contact and communion with the Universal Father (5:2.2) there are those who hold that this soul-identity of Jesus now reposes in the “bosom of the Father,” to be subsequently released for leadership of the Nebadon Corps of the Finality in their undisclosed destiny in connection with the uncreated universes of the unorganized realms of outer space. (188:3.8)
improvedDescription: A spiritual concept referring to Divinington, the personal-communion sphere of the Universal Father, and more broadly to the personality circuit that provides all creature personalities access to personal contact and communion with the Universal Father.

name: bosom of the Son
originalDescription: Sonarington. This sphere is the “bosom of the Son,” the personal receiving world of the Eternal Son. It is the Paradise headquarters of the descending and ascending Sons of God when, and after, they are fully accredited and finally approved. (13:1.7)
improvedDescription: Sonarington, the personal receiving world and Paradise headquarters of the Eternal Son, serving as the home for all Sons of the Eternal Son when they are fully accredited and finally approved.

name: bosom of the Spirit
originalDescription: Spiritington. This world is the “bosom of the Spirit,” the Paradise home of the high beings that exclusively represent the Infinite Spirit. (13:1.9)
improvedDescription: Spiritington, the Paradise home of high beings that exclusively represent the Infinite Spirit, where the Seven Master Spirits and their offspring from all universes gather.

name: Bosora
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the cities in Perea visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during their final three-month mission before Jesus went to Jerusalem.

name: boulders
originalDescription: boulders are a glacial product (61:7.1)
improvedDescription: Distinctive rocks that are a characteristic glacial product, serving as telltale evidence of ice activity along with surface cleavages, potholes, and lakes.

name: bow and arrow
originalDescription: the bow and arrow were invented by the red race at an early date (64:6.4)
improvedDescription: A weapon invented early by the red race, which aided them in their conflicts but could not overcome their tendency to fight among themselves.

name: brachiopods
originalDescription: The brachiopods, marine organisms with protective shells, flourished during the Silurian period, with 3,000 varieties emerging, though only 200 survive today. They lived much as they do now, with hinged and notched shells for defense. Brachiopods coexisted with gastropods, mollusks, and coral reef builders in ancient seas, contributing significantly to limestone formations from their calcified remains. (59:1.18) (59:2.12) As the Silurian seas advanced and retreated, marine ecosystems evolved. Fossil evidence shows brachiopods and other marine life thrived even near the poles, where warm seas bathed the shorelines. Toward the end of the epoch, brachiopods declined, succeeded by arthropods and cephalopods, signaling a shift in oceanic dominance. (59:3) (59:4.3)
improvedDescription: Marine organisms with hinged, protective shells that flourished during ancient seas. Three thousand varieties appeared in the Silurian period, though only 200 survive today, and they contributed significantly to limestone formations through their calcified remains.

name: Brahma
originalDescription: Brahma is the first member of the Hindu Trimurti trinity; created from infinity, the Brahman; he is identified with destiny (94:4.4) (104:1.5) he is considered the Vedic principle of the father-deity (94:1.4)
improvedDescription: Brahma is the first member of the Hindu Trimurti trinity, conceived as being self-created from the Brahman (infinity). He is identified with destiny and fate, and represents the Vedic principle of the father-deity. In Hindu theology, Brahma could have formed the foundation for a concept of the Universal Father were it not for his close identification with the pantheistic Infinite One.

name: Brahman
originalDescription: Brahman-Narayana was conceived as the Absolute, the infinite IT IS, the primordial creative potency of the potential cosmos, the Universal Self existing static and potential throughout all eternity. (94:3.3-4) it was a distant idea of the all-encompassing Absolute but of an impersonal kind (94:2.6-7) was considered the divinity-energy principle that activates the Vedic pantheon (94:1.4) it was thought to be creative energy, cosmic reaction; beyond all definition (94:3.2) India was left helpless by the impersonal hypothesis of a Brahmin (94:2.4,7) the Infinite One, IT IS; no personality attributes (94:3.2) (94:4.3) the Vedic oversoul of all creation (94:2.4)
improvedDescription: Brahman-Narayana was conceived in Hindu philosophy as the Absolute, the infinite IT IS, representing the primordial creative potency of the potential cosmos and the Universal Self existing static and potential throughout eternity. This impersonal concept was thought to be the energy-divinity principle activating the entire Vedic pantheon, beyond all definition and capable of being comprehended only through successive negation of all finite qualities. Despite approaching the idea of an all-pervading Absolute, this concept lacked personality attributes and left India's spiritual life helpless due to its impersonal nature.

name: Brahmanas
originalDescription: the Brahmanas and the Upanishads are the later scriptures of Hindu faith (94:2.6)
improvedDescription: The Brahmanas are later scriptures of the Hindu faith, compiled along with the Upanishads during a period when the Brahmanic priesthood sought to find true reality by deanthropomorphizing Indian concepts of deity, though this effort led to the depersonalization of God rather than spiritual advancement.

name: Brahmanism
originalDescription: origin in Aryan Sethites philosophy priesthood
improvedDescription: Brahmanism is a Hindu religious and philosophical system that originated from Aryan-Sethite influences and developed under the Brahman priest caste. While representing one of the most noble reaches of the mortal mind into philosophy and metaphysics, Brahmanism failed to grasp the essential dual concept of religion: the existence of the Universal Father and the ascending experience of creatures seeking to attain him. The system maintained religious hegemony in India through the caste system but ultimately became spiritually debilitating through its impersonal concept of deity.

name: brains
originalDescription: the brain is the material mechanism for intelligent response to internal and external stimuli (65:0.1) (65:6.10) Mind, while not a physical evolution, is wholly dependent on the brain capacity afforded by purely physical and evolutionary developments. (58:6.7) The stoppage of life destroys the physical brain patterns for mind endowment, and the disruption of mind terminates mortal consciousness. (112:5.14) Adjusters human brainedness | mortals:planetary types animal brainedness
improvedDescription: The brain is the material mechanism that provides capacity for intelligent response to internal and external stimuli. Mind, while not a physical evolution, is wholly dependent on the brain capacity afforded by purely physical and evolutionary developments. Mortal types are classified by brain organization into one-brained, two-brained, and three-brained categories, with Urantians being of the two-brained type. The stoppage of life destroys the physical brain patterns for mind endowment, and the disruption of mind terminates mortal consciousness.

name: branches
originalDescription: the branch will die if it is separated from vine. Every branch coming out of me which bears no fruit, the Father will take away. Every branch which bears fruit, the Father will cleanse that it may bear more fruit. (180:2.1)
improvedDescription: In Jesus's parable of the true vine, branches represent his followers who must remain connected to him to bear spiritual fruit. Every branch that bears no fruit will be removed by the Father, while fruit-bearing branches will be cleansed to increase their productivity. The branch will die if separated from the vine, symbolizing that disciples cannot yield the fruits of loving service except by abiding in Jesus.

name: bravery
improvedDescription: A quality of courage and fearlessness in the face of danger or difficulty, often demonstrated in moral or physical challenges.

name: bread
originalDescription: use of bread by Maquiventa use of bread by Jesus bread of life bread of remembrance
improvedDescription: Bread serves multiple symbolic and literal purposes in spiritual contexts. Melchizedek introduced bread and wine as a sacrament to replace blood sacrifices. Jesus used bread in the feeding of the five thousand, his only consciously preplanned nature miracle. Most significantly, Jesus declared himself to be the "bread of life" and established the bread of remembrance during the Last Supper, representing the united life of the Father and the Son as spiritual nourishment for believers.

name: breadfruit trees
originalDescription: Around 90 million years ago, breadfruit trees emerged alongside angiosperms during the early Cretaceous period. These trees, along with fig trees and palms, rapidly spread across Europe and the western plains of North America, becoming prominent in the evolving plant landscape. Their arrival marked a significant expansion of land flora, though no new land animals appeared during this time. (60:3.7)
improvedDescription: Breadfruit trees emerged around 90 million years ago during the early Cretaceous period alongside other angiosperms. These trees, along with fig trees and palms, rapidly spread across Europe and the western plains of North America, becoming prominent in the evolving plant landscape of that era.

name: breast-feeding
improvedDescription: The natural process by which mothers nourish their infants through milk production, representing an important aspect of early child care and development.

name: breath
originalDescription: primitive men thought soul’s qualities imparted by the breath (86:5.13)
improvedDescription: In primitive belief systems, breath was considered to be associated with the soul, whose qualities could be imparted or transferred through breathing.

name: Breath Giver
originalDescription: Onagar directed Andonites to worship “The Breath Giver”, “Breath Giver to men and animals”. (45:4.3) (63:6.1) (64:1.7)
improvedDescription: The deity concept introduced by Onagar, who directed the Andonites to worship "The Breath Giver" or "Breath Giver to men and animals" as their supreme being.

name: breath of life
originalDescription: for early man the breath of life was regarded as the one phenomenon which differentiated the living and the dead. (86:4.3) local universe Mother Spirits contribute “breath of life”. (34:2.5) (36:6.4)
improvedDescription: The vital spark contributed by the Universe Spirit that animates physical forms created by the Life Carriers, distinguishing living beings from dead matter. Early humans regarded breath as the fundamental phenomenon separating the living from the dead.

name: Brenner Pass
originalDescription: Nordics traded with Danubians via the Brenner Pass (80:9.4)
improvedDescription: A mountain pass through which the Nordics conducted extensive trade in amber with the Danubians, leading to cultural exchanges including the adoption of mother worship.

name: brethren
originalDescription: As individuals, contend not among yourselves as to who shall be greatest. Be you all as brethren. (179:5.7) disciples must be willing to forsake father, mother, wife, children, brothers, and sisters for gospel (163:3.4) (171:2.2) forgive 70 times 7 (159:1.4) Jesus’ walk with the 2 brothers (190:5) practice of marrying deceased brother’s wife (139:4.12) (174:3.2) whosoever does Father’s will is Jesus’ brother (154:6.5)
improvedDescription: A term referring to spiritual brotherhood among believers, emphasizing equality and unity rather than competition for greatness, as taught by Jesus to his disciples.

name: Bretons
originalDescription: Bretons are the survivors of the original Andonite inhabitants of western Europe, mixed with the Mediterranean stock. (80:9.14)
improvedDescription: The descendants of original Andonite inhabitants of western Europe mixed with Mediterranean stock, who retained many ancient superstitions and practices despite centuries of Christianity.

name: bribery
originalDescription: Roman soldiers and guards were bribed to say that the disciples had taken the body of Jesus (189:2.5)
improvedDescription: The corrupt practice employed by Jewish leaders who paid Roman soldiers and guards to falsely claim that Jesus' disciples had stolen his body from the tomb.

name: brick
originalDescription: Dalamatia was built of brick (66:3.6) Eden Garden had a 27 mile wall across neck of the peninsula of Eden that was built of brick (73:4.2-3) buildings and drains in Eden were also built of brick (73:5.2,4) brick was an early building material (81:2.15-16)
improvedDescription: A manufactured building material used extensively in early civilizations, notably in constructing the city of Dalamatia, the walls and buildings of the Garden of Eden, and various ancient settlements.

name: bridegroom
originalDescription: the bridegroom of Cana wedding was named Johab (137:4) Jesus used bridegroom in his teachings: “Do the sons of the bridechamber fast while the bridegroom is with them? As long as the bridegroom remains with them, they can hardly fast”. (147:7.2)
improvedDescription: The male participant in a wedding ceremony, used by Jesus in his teachings about fasting to illustrate that his disciples need not fast while he remained with them, just as wedding guests do not fast while the bridegroom is present.

name: brides
originalDescription: The wearing of the bridal veil is a relic of the times when it was considered necessary to disguise the bride so that ghosts might not recognize her and also to hide her beauty from the gaze of the otherwise jealous and envious spirits. (83:4.7) The carrying of the bride over the threshold is reminiscent of a number of ancient practices, among others, of the days of wife stealing. (83:2.3) bridal shows
improvedDescription: Women entering marriage, whose wedding customs and traditions preserve ancient practices designed to protect them from jealous spirits and commemorate historical marriage practices including wife capture and bridal shows.

name: bridges
originalDescription: In his devotion to the cause of the kingdom, Jesus burned all bridges behind him; he sacrificed all hindrances to the doing of his Father’s will. (196:2.7) As they walked over this bridge, Jesus, among other things, said: “This world is only a bridge; you may pass over it, but you should not think to build a dwelling place upon it”. (156:2.1)
improvedDescription: Metaphorical structures representing commitment and spiritual passage. Jesus used bridges as a teaching metaphor, saying "This world is only a bridge; you may pass over it, but you should not think to build a dwelling place upon it." In his devotion to doing his Father's will, Jesus burned all bridges behind him, sacrificing all hindrances to his spiritual mission.

name: brigands
improvedDescription: Bandits or robbers who engage in violent theft and criminal activity.

name: Bright and Morning Stars
originalDescription: The Bright and Morning Star is not a creator, but he is a marvelous administrator, being the personal administrative representative of the Creator Son. (33:4.4) Ancients of Days delegate mass judgment and dispensational resurrections to Bright and Morning Stars (33:4.6) they are beings of unprecedented versatility and unimagined brilliance (33:4.3) they are the chief executives of local universes when a Michael Son is absent (21:2.11) (32:2.6) (33:0.1) they are the creators of Spironga with Father Melchizedeks (37:10.2) they were created fully endowed for his work, but he has gained experience with the growth and evolution of our local creation. (33:4.5) they are the first-born of local universes, personalized by Creator Sons and Mother Spirits (21:2.11) (28:1.3) (32:2.6) (33:4.1) (34:2.5) they are catalogued inside the group Universe Aids (37:1.2) in personality registers appear as a descending order inside dual-origin beings (30:1.33) (30:2.141) intelligence corps seraphim report directly to Bright and Morning Stars (39:2.3) These chief executives of the universe, the personal associates of the Creator Sons, are like them in all aspects of character, though markedly limited in the attributes of divinity and their creative prerogatives. (32:2.6) (33:4.2) Son-Spirit minister supreme seraphim are closely affiliated with intelligence service of Bright and Morning Star (39:1.2) they would become chief local universe administrators if Creator Sons left for new universes in outer space (55:10.10)
improvedDescription: The first-born beings of each local universe, created by Creator Sons and Mother Spirits as their personal administrative representatives. These unique dual-origin personalities serve as chief executives when Creator Sons are absent, possess unprecedented versatility and brilliance, and function as supreme commanders of the celestial hosts. Gabriel of Salvington is the Bright and Morning Star of Nebadon.

name: Brilliant Evening Stars
originalDescription: Brilliant Evening Stars are also known as superangels (55:4.14) They are easily visible to morontia personalities (37:2.5) They appear mentioned in personality registers of descending order of dual-origin beings and as Universal Aids of Composite Personality Groups (30:1.38) (30:2.142) They serve in many capacities but chiefly as liaison officers of Gabriel, the local universe chief executive. (37:2.1-3) They always work in pairs—one a created being, the other an ascendant Evening Star. (37:2.7) origin headquarters numbers functions and actions nature
improvedDescription: Superangels who serve primarily as liaison officers of Gabriel, the local universe chief executive. They are a unique twofold order comprising both created beings and ascendant spirits, always working in pairs on assignments. These versatile beings are easily visible to morontia personalities and frequently represent Gabriel at constellation and system capitals throughout the local universe.

name: Brilliant Evening Stars (chief)
originalDescription: the Brilliant Evening Stars is intrusted with the custody and dispatch of all personal communications (39:2.17) Gavalia, the first born of this order, is chief of ascendant Brilliant Evening Stars in Nebadon (37:2.6) (46:5.25) (119:0.1) Gavalia is who wrote Paper 119 (119:8.9)
improvedDescription: The head of the Brilliant Evening Stars order, responsible for custody and dispatch of all personal communications in the local universe. Gavalia, the first-born of this order in Nebadon, serves as chief of ascendant Brilliant Evening Stars and was assigned to reveal the story of Michael's seven bestowals.

name: British Columbia
originalDescription: Around 70 million years ago, significant geological activity occurred in British Columbia during the peak elevation of the Rocky Mountains. A massive rock segment was displaced, thrusting Cambrian rocks fifteen miles over Cretaceous layers. On the eastern slopes near the Canadian border, another notable overthrust pushed prelife stone layers over more recent Cretaceous deposits. (60:3.14)
improvedDescription: A Canadian province where significant geological upheaval occurred 70 million years ago during the Rocky Mountains' peak elevation. A massive rock segment was displaced, thrusting Cambrian rocks fifteen miles over Cretaceous layers, demonstrating the dramatic crustal distortions of that era.

name: British Isles
originalDescription: Geology first settlers british people modern age
improvedDescription: A group of islands in northwestern Europe including Great Britain and Ireland. The region experienced extensive volcanic activity, glacial coverage during ice ages, and served as home to early Andonite peoples including the Foxhall peoples who preserved ancient cultural traditions.

name: Brittany
originalDescription: Only in Lapland (and to a certain extent in Brittany) did the older Andonites retain even a semblance of identity. (80:4.6) The superstitions of this comparatively recent sun-worshiping era even now persist in the folkways of Brittany. (80:9.14)
improvedDescription: A region in northwestern France where older Andonite peoples retained much of their ancient identity and cultural practices. The area preserves many superstitions from the sun-worshiping era, including New Stone Age charms and protective customs that persist despite centuries of Christianity.

name: broad-heads
originalDescription: The Adamites and Nodites were long-headed; the Andonites were broad-headed. (81:4.2) The blue races, when mixed with the Andonite stock, were decidedly broad-headed. (81:4.2) The Nordics continued the trade in amber from the Baltic coast, building up a great commerce with the broadheads of the Danube valley via the Brenner Pass. (80:9.4) mountain survivors of earlier Andonite tribes were broad-headed. The ancient Hittites and Abraham’s ancestry stemmed directly from the Andonite stock; their pale skins and broad heads were typical of that race. (80:8.1,2) Syrian Nodite-Andonites were broad-headed (80:1.2) The Sangik races were medium-headed, with the yellow and blue men tending to broad-headedness. (81:4.2)
improvedDescription: A physical characteristic describing skull shape, particularly associated with Andonite peoples and their descendants. The Andonites were distinctly broad-headed, contrasting with the long-headed Adamites and Nodites, while various racial mixtures produced different degrees of broad-headedness throughout human populations.

name: broadcast directors
originalDescription: The broadcast directors are thought conservers, and part of celestial artisans (44:4.9)
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans who serve as thought conservers responsible for supervising broadcasts from Paradise, superuniverses, and local universes. They function as censors, editors, and coordinators of broadcast material, adapting communications for different universe levels and translating them into local languages.

name: broadcast receivers
originalDescription: energy transmitters are invaluable to broadcast receivers (29:4.29) Broadcast receivers are primary seconaphim, and serve Ancients of Days with a special class of broadcast messages (28:4.12)
improvedDescription: Primary seconaphim who serve the Ancients of Days by receiving and processing special broadcast messages, working in liaison with energy transmitters to detect and amplify weak energy currents carrying intelligence communications across the universe.

name: broadcasters (superior seraphim)
originalDescription: Broadcasters, inside superior seraphim, are a specialized subdivision of the seraphic recorders, being concerned with the dispatch of records and with the dissemination of essential information. They can simultaneously transmit 144,000 messages (39:2.15) maintain reciprocal contact with both the intelligence co-ordinators of the tertiary supernaphim and the glorified intelligence co-ordinators of the Seraphic Corps of Completion (39:2.15) they use superaphic ideographic techniques (39:2.15)
improvedDescription: A specialized subdivision of seraphic recorders among the superior seraphim, responsible for dispatching records and disseminating essential information. They can simultaneously transmit 144,000 messages using superaphic ideographic techniques and maintain contact with intelligence coordinators throughout the universe.

name: broadcasters (tertiary supernaphim)
originalDescription: Broadcasters, inside tertiary supernaphim, are space report directors of all Deity phenomena on Paradise (26:3.6)
improvedDescription: Tertiary supernaphim who function as master technicians serving as broadcast receivers and senders of the central creation, directing space reports of all Deity phenomena on Paradise and operating all basic circuits of space.

name: broadcasts (celestial)
originalDescription: Adjusters are able to receive the continuous stream of cosmic intelligence coming in over the master circuits of time and space; they are in full touch with the spirit intelligence and energy of the universes. (110:4.1) (110:7.6) The transmittal of space reports is carefully supervised, and there is always a back registry to insure the proper reception of every report on every world in a given circuit. (44:4.10) Havona pilgrims may consult the broadcasts to ascertain who of their fellow pilgrims have departed for Havona since the time of their leaving Uversa. (30:4.28) Spirit melodies can be broadcast to an entire universe (44:1.1-2) The broadcasts are received by a technique involving the polar crystal, the sea of glass. (46:3) The broadcasts of Paradise, the superuniverses, and the local universes are under the general supervision of celestial artisans (44:4.9) All higher orders of celestial beings are able to utilize broadcasts for communication with their fellows scattered throughout the universe. (33:6.5) on Salvington on constellations on Jerusem on a planet space reports
improvedDescription: Universe-wide communication system that transmits intelligence, space reports, and spiritual messages across all levels of creation, from Paradise and Havona down to individual planets. These broadcasts include cosmic intelligence streams, spirit melodies, legislative enactments, and space reports that keep all celestial beings informed of universe affairs.

name: brokenhearted
originalDescription: The Lord is near to those who are brokenhearted; he saves all who have a childlike spirit. Many are the afflictions of the righteous man, but the Lord delivers him out of them all. (131:2.10)
improvedDescription: Those who experience deep spiritual or emotional distress, whom the Lord draws near to comfort and save, especially those who maintain a childlike spirit despite their afflictions.

name: bronze
originalDescription: The discovery of mixing copper and tin to make bronze was made by one of the Adamsonites of Turkestan whose highland copper mine happened to be located alongside a tin deposit. (81:3.5)
improvedDescription: A metal alloy created by mixing copper and tin, first discovered by one of the Adamsonites of Turkestan whose highland copper mine was located alongside a tin deposit.

name: Bronze Age
originalDescription: Bronze Age took place at ca. 3000 B.C., overlapping New Stone Age at times, a period associated with mother worship in some places and sun worship in others (80:9.12-13) it was not a distinct period. There was a bronze culture in Turkestan dating before 9000 B.C., and the Andites early learned to work in iron, gold, and copper, as well. But conditions were very different away from the more advanced centers of civilization. There were no distinct periods, such as the Stone, Bronze, and Iron Ages; all three existed at the same time in different localities. (81:3.4)
improvedDescription: A cultural period around 3000 B.C. characterized by the widespread use of bronze tools and weapons, overlapping with the New Stone Age in some regions. This era was associated with mother worship in Scandinavia and sun worship in southern Europe, though distinct periods like Stone, Bronze, and Iron Ages existed simultaneously in different localities.

name: brooding
originalDescription: the measure of our human strength of character is our ability to resist the holding of grudges and our capacity to withstand brooding in the face of deep sorrow. (156:5.17)
improvedDescription: A destructive mental habit of dwelling on sorrows and grievances that tests human strength of character, which can be overcome through spiritual capacity and resistance to holding grudges.

name: brothel keeper in Jerusalem
originalDescription: the anonymous woman who wet Jesus' feet with tears and wiped them with her hair was a former Jerusalem brothel keeper who had remade her life by accepting Jesus' teachings (147:5.3-4)
improvedDescription: An unnamed woman who formerly operated a high-class brothel near the temple court but became a believer in Jesus' teachings, closed her establishment, and helped the women associated with her accept the gospel and change their lives.

name: brothels
improvedDescription: Establishments for prostitution, including high-class ones that operated near the temple court of the gentiles in Jerusalem during Jesus' time.

name: brotherhood (attitude)
originalDescription: The appearance of genuine brotherhood signifies that a social order has arrived in which all men delight in bearing one another’s burdens; they actually desire to practice the golden rule. (71:4.16) brotherhood is one of the most powerful and most tenuous factor of civilization (118:8.10) how to achieve it barriers to brotherhood
improvedDescription: The attitude of genuine brotherhood signifies a social order where all people delight in bearing one another's burdens and actually desire to practice the golden rule. Brotherhood is one of the most powerful yet most tenuous factors of civilization, requiring voluntary self-restraint and spiritual insight to overcome natural human selfishness and achieve true unity.

name: brotherhood of man
originalDescription: Brotherhood constitutes a fact of relationship between every personality in universal existence.  (12:7.10-11) The ultimate goal of human progress is the reverent recognition of the fatherhood of God and the loving materialization of the brotherhood of man. (71:4.15) (143:1.4) While religion is exclusively a personal spiritual experience—knowing God as a Father—the corollary of this experience—knowing man as a brother—entails the adjustment of the self to other selves, and that involves the social or group aspect of religious life. (99:5.1) No person can escape the benefits or the penalties that may come as a result of relationship to other persons. (12:7.11) origin how to achieve it Jesus versus Lucifer
improvedDescription: The universal relationship between all personalities based on their common divine origin and spiritual kinship. Brotherhood constitutes a fact of relationship between every personality in universal existence, where each person's actions affect the whole, and the ultimate goal of human progress is the reverent recognition of God's fatherhood and the loving materialization of human brotherhood.

name: Brotherhood of United Midwayers
improvedDescription: An organization of midway creatures who serve on Urantia, combining both primary and secondary midwayers into a unified order of celestial beings.

name: brotherhoods
improvedDescription: Organizations or associations formed around shared beliefs, purposes, or activities, often involving bonds of fellowship and mutual support among members.

name: brown race
originalDescription: present-day brown men descend from mix of yellow and red races in Asia. They held islands off the Asiatic coast (64:7.6,16) at some point in time they begun a civilization on the near-by islands of the Pacific (78:3.8) Serapatatia was of brown race, a brown-tinted man, a brilliant descendant of the onetime chief of the Dalamatia commission on health (75:3.1)
improvedDescription: A racial group descended from the mixture of red and yellow races in Asia, who held islands off the Asiatic coast and eventually began developing civilization on Pacific islands. Serapatatia, a prominent figure in Urantia's history, was described as a brown-tinted man and brilliant descendant of a former Dalamatia commission chief.

name: brownies
originalDescription: evolving monotheists keep subordinate gods as brownies, among others ficticious beings (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Mythical creatures that evolving monotheists retain as subordinate spiritual beings alongside other fictitious entities like fairies, demons, and spirits, representing remnants of earlier polytheistic beliefs.

name: brutality
improvedDescription: Savage cruelty or violence in behavior, representing one of the lower aspects of human nature that must be overcome through moral and spiritual development.

name: Buddha
originalDescription: Buddha was the name that Gautama master received, revered as enlightened one (92:5.12) (94:8.2) (94:9.1) Buddha became a reflective devotee of leisure (69:2.5) Jesus had a talk with Gonod regarding Buddha (132:7.3-5) the return of Buddha is looked for in Tibet, China, and India (92:5.6)
improvedDescription: The title meaning "enlightened one" given to Gautama Siddhartha by his followers, who revered him as a master teacher. Buddha became a reflective devotee and is looked for to return in Tibet, China, and India, though Jesus noted that Buddha was better than Buddhism itself, describing him as a great man and prophet who unfortunately lost sight of his spiritual Father.

name: Buddha, Sakyamuni
originalDescription: Gautama’s followers called him also Sakyamuni Buddha (94:8.2)
improvedDescription: An additional title given to Gautama Buddha by his followers, meaning the sage of the Sakya clan, used alongside his other titles of master, teacher, and the enlightened one.

name: Buddhism
originalDescription: Buddhism is one of the four great international and interracial faiths (92:6.5,16) Buddhism has shown an adaptability to the mores of many peoples that has been equaled only by Christianity. (92:6.16) The great weakness in the original gospel of Buddhism was that it did not produce a religion of unselfish social service. (94:8.19) Buddhism is a living, growing religion today because it succeeds in conserving many of the highest moral values of its adherents. It promotes calmness and self-control, augments serenity and happiness, and does much to prevent sorrow and mourning. (94:9.6) doctrines history Jesus and Buddhism types
improvedDescription: One of the four great international faiths that took origin from the historic teachings of Gautama Buddha. Buddhism offers salvation through belief in Buddha and has shown remarkable adaptability to different cultures, though its original weakness was failing to produce a religion of unselfish social service and lacking a clear concept of a personal God.

name: buildings
originalDescription: in the neighbor planet with a main continental nation all buildings are fireproof (72:7.4) In olden times, when a new building of any importance was started, it was customary to slay a human being as a “foundation sacrifice”. (89:6.4)
improvedDescription: Structures for human habitation or use. In advanced civilizations, all buildings are fireproof, while in ancient times new buildings of importance were often consecrated with human foundation sacrifices.

name: bulls
originalDescription: Some Egyptian tribes worshiped bulls (95:2.3) The Mithras myth tells that he slew the sacred bull, and in recognition, Mithras was made immortal, being exalted to the station of intercessor for the human race among the gods on high. (98:5.3)
improvedDescription: Bovine animals that were objects of worship in some Egyptian tribes and featured prominently in the Mithraic religious mythology, where the slaying of a sacred bull granted Mithras immortality and the role of divine intercessor.

name: bully near Tarentum
originalDescription: At Tarentum Jesus restrained this rough and bullying youth brutally attacking a smaller lad (133:1.1)
improvedDescription: A rough youth whom Jesus encountered near Tarentum who was brutally attacking a smaller boy. Jesus intervened to rescue the victim and restrained the bully, demonstrating mercy over retribution.

name: burdens
originalDescription: The appearance of genuine brotherhood signifies that a social order has arrived in which all men delight in bearing one another’s burdens; they actually desire to practice the golden rule. (71:4.16)
improvedDescription: Hardships or difficulties carried by individuals. In an ideal society marked by genuine brotherhood, all people delight in bearing one another's burdens and practicing the golden rule.

name: Burma
originalDescription: Andites drove southern and eastern inferiors into Burma (79:2.4) Asoka established Buddhism in Burma, among toher countries (94:9.1) In Burma and the peninsula of Indo-China the cultures of India and China mixed and blended to produce the successive civilizations of those regions. (79:6.2) In Burma the vanished green race persisted in larger proportion than anywhere else in the world. (79:6.2) Hinayana Buddhism persisted in Ceylon, Burma, and the Indo-China peninsula. (94:9.4)
improvedDescription: A Southeast Asian region where Indian and Chinese cultures mixed to produce successive civilizations. It became a stronghold for Hinayana Buddhism and retained the largest proportion of the vanished green race anywhere in the world.

name: bushel
originalDescription: Jesus said: “Neither do men light a candle and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it gives light to all who are in the house. Let your light so shine before men that they may see your good works and be led to glorify your Father who is in heaven”. (140:3.13)
improvedDescription: A container used to conceal or hide something, referenced by Jesus in his teaching that disciples should be like lights placed on candlesticks rather than hidden under bushels, shining their good works before others.

name: Bushmen
originalDescription: African Bushmen are mentioned as a crude culture in present days (66:5.30) (70:3.2) they are called also an inferior race (68:1.6) (103:3.1) they do not have individual names (88:5.5) they still employ food-gathering lines of primitive nomadic stage (68:5.3)
improvedDescription: An African people representing one of the most primitive surviving cultures, characterized by nomadic food-gathering lifestyle, lack of individual names, absence of formal leadership, and retention of early tribal social conditions.

name: business
improvedDescription: Commercial or economic activities involving the exchange of goods, services, or labor for profit or livelihood.

name: butter
originalDescription: butter became common articles in days of Prince’s staff (66:5.5)
improvedDescription: A dairy product that became a common article of human diet during the time of the Prince's staff, produced from improved cattle through careful breeding programs.

name: butterflies
originalDescription: The philosophy of the universe cannot be predicated on the observations of so-called science. If such a metamorphosis could not be seen, a scientist would be inclined to deny the possibility of developing a butterfly out of a caterpillar. (42:9.4)
improvedDescription: Insects that undergo metamorphosis from caterpillars, used as an example of natural transformation that demonstrates the limitations of materialistic science in understanding life's possibilities.

name: buzzards
originalDescription: About 30 million years ago, bird life continued to evolve with little significant change. Most modern species already existed, including vultures, along with gulls, herons, flamingos, falcons, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Large birds of prey that existed about 30 million years ago alongside most other modern bird species, including vultures, gulls, herons, flamingos, falcons, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches during a period when bird life continued to develop with few important evolutionary changes.

name: Caesarea
originalDescription: Herod the Idumean built the harbor of Caesarea, which further aided in making Palestine the crossroads of the civilized world. (121:2.11) It was the Roman capital of Judea (130:2.1) (184:3.17) Jesus stopped at Caesarea on his way to Rome for a few days, helping to repair one of the ship's oars and meeting and influencing several people, especially Anaxandus, a leading member of the later Christian church. (130:2) Caesarea was the home of Nathan, author of Gospel of John (121:8.10) Paul was imprisoned at Caesarea (130:2.5) there were slaughtered 20,000 Jews (130:2.5)
improvedDescription: A major coastal city built by Herod the Idumean with an impressive harbor that served as the Roman capital of Palestine and residence of the Roman procurator. Jesus visited Caesarea during his journey to Rome, where he helped repair a ship's steering paddle and influenced several people including Anaxand, who later became a prominent Christian leader.

name: Caesarea Philippi
originalDescription: Caesarea Philippi, also known as Dan (134:7.5) it was the capital of Tetrarch Philip’s domain (157:3.1) Celsus, a Jesus follower, was from Caesarea Philippi (157:3.7) (157:4.1) (157:6.1) (158:6.1) Veronica, the faithfully woman that was healed when she touched Jesus, was from Caesarea Philippi (152:0.3)
improvedDescription: The capital of Tetrarch Philip's domain, also known as Dan, situated in a region of wondrous beauty in a charming valley where the Jordan poured forth from an underground cave. This was where Jesus made his great confession as the Son of God and where several of his followers, including Celsus and Veronica, resided.

name: Caiaphas
originalDescription: Caiaphas was the aggressive son-in-law of Annas, the former high priest (183:5.1) (184:0.2) (184:1.1) at the time of Jesus' capture, Caiaphas was acting high priest (175:4.13) (184:0.2) stated that it is better for one man to die than for the whole community to perish (168:3.6) Caiaphas was hard-hearted and vainglorious (177:4.7-8) accused Jesus before Pilate (185:6.7) Judas met with Jesus' enemies at the house of Caiaphas (177:4.1) The temple guards had orders to bring Jesus to Caiaphas (183:5.1) The Sanhedrin met in the palace of Caiaphas (175:4.13) (184:0.2) (188:2.2) in the trial of Jesus Caiaphas behaved more as a prosecutor than as an impartial judge (184:3.4) during the trial he shook an accusing finger provocatively and hit the Master in the face (184:3.14,18) he waved a vengeful finger in Pilate's face when he saw that the procurator was willing to release Jesus (185:7.5)
improvedDescription: The aggressive son-in-law of Annas who served as acting high priest during Jesus' time. Known for being hard-hearted and vainglorious, he famously declared that it was better for one man to die than for the whole community to perish, and during Jesus' trial he behaved more as a prosecutor than an impartial judge, even striking Jesus in the face.

name: Cain
originalDescription: Cain was the son of Eva and Cano, a man of the Nodite race; he had a natural bellicose inheritance (76:2.4) he was born before the Adamic caravan reached the second garden (76:0.2) he was two years older than Abel, who was the first son of Adam and Eve in the second garden. Cain chose to dedicate himself to agriculture (76:2.1,5) he had always defied family discipline and despised Adam's religion (76:2.8) he slew Abel (76:2.4-5) because of that act he finally repented and was inhabited by an Adjuster (76:2.8) Adam and Eve encouraged him to leave the second garden (76:2.7) he went to the land of Nod for a wife (74:8.8) (148:4.7) he married Remona; his first son was Enoch (76:2.9) the expression avenged seven times was applied to Cain, because his tribal mark was considered to be that of the tribe that would take revenge up to seven times on whoever killed him (159:1.7) the tribal mark was vital in the days of Cain, as it ensured that someone could survive (68:1.2)
improvedDescription: The son of Eve and Cano, a Nodite man, making him Adam's stepson rather than biological son. Born before the Adamic caravan reached the second garden, Cain was two years older than Abel and chose agriculture as his profession. After slaying Abel in a fit of rage, he eventually repented and received an Adjuster, then departed to the land of Nod where he married Remona and became a leader among the Nodites.

name: calamities
improvedDescription: Major disasters or catastrophic events that can befall individuals or communities, often viewed in the context of divine providence and human understanding of suffering.

name: calcium
originalDescription: Calcium is, in fact, the chief element of the matter-permeation of space throughout Orvonton. (41:6.2) By tossing its 19th electron back and forth between two orbits more than 25,000 times a second, a calcium atom is able partially to defy gravity and thus successfully to ride the light (41:6.4-5) they are wanderers derelicts of space, together with sodium (41:6)
improvedDescription: The chief element of matter-permeation throughout the superuniverse of Orvonton, literally the basic building matter for planets and spheres of space. Calcium atoms possess remarkable properties, with their nineteenth electron able to move between orbits over 25,000 times per second, allowing them to partially defy gravity and ride light beams, making calcium the most expert solar-prison escaper.

name: Caleb
originalDescription: Caleb is one of Moses' scouts who lived to enter the promised land (166:3.2)
improvedDescription: One of Moses' scouts who, along with Joshua, was among the few from the hosts that went out of Egypt to actually live to enter the promised land.

name: Calebites
originalDescription: Calebites were a non-Hebrew nomadic Palestinians tribe. They joined David and his coalition of non-Hebrew tribes (97:9.7)
improvedDescription: A non-Hebrew nomadic Palestinian tribe that joined David's coalition when he established his kingdom at Hebron. As nomadic herders, they were devoted to the Hebrew idea of land ownership and held the ideologies of the desert clans.

name: Caledonian mountains
originalDescription: About 290 million years ago, the early formation of mountain ranges began across all continents. Among the most significant were the Caledonian Mountains, extending from Ireland through Scotland to Spitzbergen. (59:3.3)
improvedDescription: A great mountain range that formed about 290 million years ago during the early mountain-building movements across all continents, extending from Ireland through Scotland to Spitzbergen and representing one of the most significant crustal upheavals of that era alongside the Himalayas of Asia.

name: calendar
originalDescription: When the Egyptians undertook to reform the calendar, about 7,000 years ago, they did it with great accuracy, introducing the year of 365 days. (77:2.11-12) calendar used in Urantia Papers is the present leap-year calendar of 365¼ days to the year (57:0.1) (126:3.1)
improvedDescription: A system for organizing time into years and days. The Egyptians reformed the calendar about 7,000 years ago, introducing a year of 365 days. The Urantia Papers use the present leap-year calendar of 365¼ days to the year.

name: California
originalDescription: The oldest Amerindian culture was the Onamonalonton center in California, but this had long since vanished by 35,000 B. C. (79:5.8) Onamonalonton lived to be ninety-six years of age and maintained his headquarters among the great redwood trees of California. Many of his later descendants have come down to modern times among the Blackfoot Indians. (64:6.7) geology
improvedDescription: A region on the western coast of North America. California was home to the oldest Amerindian culture, the Onamonalonton center, though this had vanished by 35,000 B.C. Onamonalonton lived among the great redwood trees of California and maintained his headquarters there.

name: Caligastia
originalDescription: Caligastia is the titular Planetary Prince of Urantia until he was deposed at the time of Jesus (75:2.2) Caligastia advocated the cause of Lucifer on Urantia (49:5.23) (53:7.2) he is now the deposed Planetary Prince of Urantia. He receives various names such as the devil, the evil one (53:1.4) (75:2.3) (179:2.3) (189:3.3) Abaddon was Caligastia's chief of staff (53:1.5) before rebelling rebellion personal characteristics power of Caligastia sin of Caligastia Adam and Eve arrival Jesus arrival
improvedDescription: The deposed Planetary Prince of Urantia, a Lanonandek Son who joined the Lucifer rebellion. Caligastia was the titular Planetary Prince until the time of Jesus and is referred to as the devil or the evil one. He arrived on Urantia 500,000 years ago with a staff of one hundred to help advance human civilization.

name: Caligastia one hundred
originalDescription: they were a group of 100 ex-mortals chosen by Caligastia from among 785,000 citizens of Jerusem to accompany and assist him in his mission as prince all volunteered (66:2.4) 50 were men and 50 women; they served in pairs and the pairs lived together (66:7.4-5) (77:1.3,5) were brought to Urantia by seraphic transportation (66:2.5) they lived in dwellings provided with abundance of land (50:4.3) they were the parents of 50,000 primary intermediates; each pair produced 1,000 of them (66:4.10) (77:1.2-5) 40 of 100 refused to join the rebellion (67:3.2) They constituted 10 autonomous councils presided over by Daligastia, with 10 members each (66:5) # The council on food and material welfare. It was led by Ang and 3 members remained loyal (66:5.2-3) (67:4.1) # The board of animal domestication. Led by Bon; none remained loyal (66:5.4-6) (67:4.1) # The advisers regarding the conquest of predatory animals. Led by Dan; none remained loyal(66:5.7-8) (67:4.1) # The faculty on dissemination and conservation of knowledge. Directed by Fad; 5 members remained loyal (66:5.9-10) (67:4.1) # The commission on industry and trade. Hosted by Nod; none remained loyal (66:5.11-12) # The college of revealed religion. Hosted by Hap; all remained loyal (66:5.13-16) # The council of health and life. Directed by Lut; none remained loyal (66:5.17-22) (67:4.1) # The council of art and science. Led by Mek; all remained loyal (66:5.23-27) (67:4.1) # The governors of advanced tribal relations. Directed by Tut; none remained loyal (66:5.28-30) (67:4.1) # The supreme court of tribal co-ordination and racial co-operation. Driven by Van. Formed by members previously approved by the Most Highs; all remained loyal (66:5.31) (67:2.2) (67:4.1) Andonite counterparts physical aspects loyalists rebels work of the Caligastia one hundred (66:5-7)
improvedDescription: A group of 100 ex-mortals chosen by Caligastia from 785,000 citizens of Jerusem to accompany him as Planetary Prince staff. Consisting of 50 men and 50 women, they were organized into ten autonomous councils of ten members each. When the rebellion occurred, 40 of the 100 remained loyal to the universe government.

name: Caligula
originalDescription: Roman emperor after the death of Jesus. Selta, the writer of a Jewish apocalypse, was attached to Caligula's court (176:2.8)
improvedDescription: Roman emperor who ruled after the death of Jesus. Selta, the writer of a Jewish apocalypse, was attached to Caligula's court.

name: Callirrhoe
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during the Perea mission between January 3, A.D. 30 and Jesus' final journey to Jerusalem.

name: Cambrian
originalDescription: Cambrian is the marine-life era from 400 to 350 million years ago (59:1)
improvedDescription: A geological period representing the marine-life era from 400 to 350 million years ago, during which primitive marine animals were well established and prepared for evolutionary development.

name: camels
originalDescription: appearance domestication Jesus sayings
improvedDescription: Large mammals that originated in North America during the middle Tertiary period and later migrated to Europe and Asia.
