They were among the animals domesticated by ancient peoples and were used as beasts of burden. Jesus used camels metaphorically in his teachings about spiritual priorities.

name: Cana
originalDescription: Joseph father of Jesus worked at CAna (123:3.8) Nathaniel the apostle was from Cana (137:2.3) (139:6.2) the wedding of Naomi and Johab in which water was converted to wine was at Cana (137:4)
improvedDescription: A town in Galilee where Joseph worked, Nathaniel the apostle lived, and Jesus performed his first miracle by turning water into wine at a wedding feast. Jesus was well known and welcomed in Cana.

name: Canaanites
originalDescription: Hebrews became loosely established in Palestine, but they soon drifted back into the benighted beliefs of the desert and became contaminated with the less advanced Canaanite religious practices. (96:7.1) In keeping with the policy of compromise with the Canaanites, David turned seven of Saul’s descendants over to the Gibeonites to be hanged. (97:9.13) were a tribe of non-Hebrew nomadic Palestinians, who joined the confederation of tribes of David (97:9.7)
improvedDescription: A tribe of non-Hebrew nomadic Palestinians who joined David's confederation of tribes. The Hebrews became contaminated with Canaanite religious practices after settling in Palestine, adopting their less advanced beliefs and customs.

name: Canada
originalDescription: geology In North America an ancient and primitive fossil-bearing stone layer comes to the surface over the eastern, central, and northern regions of Canada. (58:7.4) 280,000,000 years ago the continents had largely emerged from the second Silurian inundation. The rock deposits of this submergence are known in North America as Niagara limestone. Several layers extend over Canada. (59:3.9) 260,000,000 years ago North America was partially overspread by seas that in Canada and other areas leave  coral deposits (59:4.6) In Canada there are seventy-five layers of coal in the various beds, which indicate the number of times the land fell and rose. (59:5.17) 150,000 years ago the sixth and last glacier reached its farthest points of southern extension, the western ice sheet crossing just over the Canadian border (61:7.9)
improvedDescription: A North American country where ancient and primitive fossil-bearing stone layers come to the surface over eastern, central, and northern regions. Canada contains seventy-five coal layers indicating numerous land elevation changes, various limestone deposits from ancient sea inundations, and was partially covered by glacial ice during the last ice age.

name: candles
originalDescription: Neither do men light a candle and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it gives light to all who are in the house. (140:3.13) In the twentieth century, candles are still burned in death chambers, and men still sit up with the dead. (87:1.4) in the twentieth century candles still burn as a part of the ritual of many religious services, remnant of ancient fire worship (85:4.4)
improvedDescription: Wax or tallow objects burned for light, still used in twentieth-century religious ceremonies as remnants of ancient fire worship and burned in death chambers where people sit with the deceased, continuing primitive practices of providing light for corpses.

name: canines
improvedDescription: A taxonomic family of carnivorous mammals including dogs, wolves, and related species.

name: cannibalism
originalDescription: cannibalism was an ancient social, economic, religious, military custom (89:5.1) There was considerable commerce in women and children who had been fattened for slaughter. (89:5.7) Human sacrifice sounded the death knell of cannibalism. Human flesh having become the food of superior men, the chiefs, it was eventually reserved for the still more superior spirits (89:5.15) Cannibalism was once well-nigh universal among the evolving races. (89:5.3) Eating human flesh became part of a solemn ceremony of revenge (89:5.5-6) The taste for human flesh grows. Having been started through hunger, friendship, revenge, or religious ritual, the eating of human flesh goes on to habitual cannibalism. (89:5.4) Not long since, one cannibalistic tribe ate every fifth child born. (68:6.10) Cannibalism has been gradually disappearing because of the following influences: (89:5.8-16) # It sometimes became a communal ceremony # It very early became a religious ritual # progressed to the point where only certain parts or organs of the body were eaten # It became limited to men # It was next limited to the chiefs # Then it became taboo # Human sacrifice sounded the death knell of cannibalism.
improvedDescription: The practice of eating human flesh that was once nearly universal among evolving races and served as a social, economic, religious, and military custom in primitive society. It gradually disappeared through various influences including religious ritualization, social taboos, and the substitution of animal sacrifices, though it persisted among some backward tribes into recent times.

name: Cano
originalDescription: brilliant and active Nodite leader (75:3.7) seducer of Eve; father of Cain (75:4.7) (75:5.3) assured Eve people with good motives could do no evil (75:4.5) influenced Eve by flattery, enthusiasm, personal persuasion (75:3.9) slain by Garden dwellers (75:5.3)
improvedDescription: A brilliant and active Nodite leader who seduced Eve through flattery and personal persuasion, assuring her that people with good motives could do no evil. He became the father of Cain and was later killed by the infuriated inhabitants of the Garden of Eden along with his entire settlement.

name: Cape Horn
originalDescription: Around 75 million years ago, the process of continental drift concluded, leading to the formation of the vast north-south mountain range along the Pacific coast. This range extended from Alaska to Cape Horn, marking the completion of these coastal mountains, though significant peaks had not yet emerged. (60:3.2) (60:3.12)
improvedDescription: The southern tip of South America, marking the end of the vast north-south mountain range that extends from Alaska along the Pacific coast. This mountain range was completed around 75 million years ago when continental drift concluded, though significant peaks had not yet emerged at that time.

name: Capernaum
originalDescription: Capernaum was a fishing town located in the north of the Sea of Galilee Bethsaida was fishing harbor of Capernaum (139:1.1) Capernaum was a strong Roman military post on route from Damascus, and the garrison’s commanding officer was a gentile believer in Yahweh (129:1.7) Zebedee was a moderately well-to-do man; his boatbuilding shops were on the lake to the south of Capernaum, and his home was situated down the lake shore near the fishing headquarters of Bethsaida. (129:1.4) Jesus Well-known inhabitants
improvedDescription: A fishing town located on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee that served as Jesus' adopted home and headquarters during his public ministry. It was a strong Roman military post on the route from Damascus with a beautiful synagogue built by a Roman centurion, and home to several of Jesus' apostles including Andrew, Peter, James, and John.

name: Capetown
originalDescription: The societal advancements around Dalamatia, driven by Tut and his associates, were compared to the stark contrast between modern Cape Town's twentieth-century society and the primitive culture of the Bushmen to the north. This highlighted the disparity between centers of progress and surrounding regions. (66:5.30)
improvedDescription: A South African city used as an example of twentieth-century advanced civilization that contrasts sharply with the primitive culture of the Bushmen to the north, illustrating how centers of progress can differ dramatically from surrounding regions.

name: capital
originalDescription: Pastoral living afforded further relief from food slavery; man learned to live on the interest of his capital, the increase in his flocks; and this provided more leisure for culture and progress. (68:5.7) economic freedom has been secured through capital and invention (81:5.5) Capital-accumulation and property-inheritance mores were a distinct social advance, but subsequently the misuse of capital has been accompanied by gross abuses. (69:9.3) Capital is labor applied as a renunciation of the present in favor of the future. (69:5.1,4-5) the accumulation of individual capital and group wealth immediately led to military organization. (69:5.2) Though capital has tended to liberate man, it has greatly complicated his social and industrial organization. Through capital and invention the present generation enjoys a higher degree of freedom, but that dos not mean any justification of the many misuses of capital by thoughtless and selfish custodians. (69:5.15) The abuse of capital by unfair capitalists does not destroy the fact that it is the basis of modern industrial society. (69:5.15) The basic urges which led to the accumulation of capital were: (69:5.3-11) # Hunger—associated with foresight. # Love of family—desire to provide for their wants. # Vanity—longing to display one’s property accumulations. # Position—eagerness to buy social and political prestige. # Power—the craving to be master. # Fear of the ghosts of the dead—priest fees for protection. # Sex urge—the desire to buy one or more wives. # Numerous forms of self-gratification.
improvedDescription: Labor applied as a renunciation of the present in favor of the future, representing accumulated wealth and property that provides economic freedom and leisure for cultural progress. While capital has liberated humanity and enabled modern industrial society, its accumulation and misuse have also created complex social problems and inequalities.

name: capital punishment
improvedDescription: The legal penalty of death imposed by the state for serious crimes, also known as the death penalty.

name: capitalists
originalDescription: The herders were the first capitalists; their herds represented capital, and they lived on the interest—the natural increase. (69:8.5) The priesthoods thus became very rich; they were chief among ancient capitalists. (69:5.9) On the neighbor planet that has a main continental nation the workers are increasingly becoming shareholders in all industrial concerns; every intelligent laborer is slowly becoming a small capitalist. (72:5.1)
improvedDescription: Individuals who own capital and live on the returns from their investments. The herders were the first capitalists, with their herds representing capital that generated income through natural increase. Ancient priesthoods also became wealthy capitalists through death presents and fees for spiritual services.

name: Capitolias
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the cities and towns in Perea where Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers worked during their final ministry mission from January to March of A.D. 30.

name: Capitolium
originalDescription: The Capitol was the hill in Rome that contained the Roman temple dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. Jesus went up here on several occasions while he was in Rome (132:0.2)
improvedDescription: The Capitol hill in Rome containing the magnificent temple dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. Jesus often went up to the Capitolium during his stay in Rome to ponder the spiritual bondage of ignorance that held the Romans.

name: Cappadocia
originalDescription: Matthew preached in Cappadocia (139:7.10)
improvedDescription: A region in Asia Minor where the apostle Matthew preached the gospel of the kingdom and baptized believers during his missionary journeys after leaving Jerusalem due to persecutions.

name: captain
originalDescription: Mind is your ship, the Adjuster is your pilot, the human will is captain. (111:1.9)
improvedDescription: In the spiritual metaphor of human guidance, the human will serves as captain of the mortal vessel, while mind is the ship and the Thought Adjuster is the pilot guiding toward eternal survival.

name: Capua
originalDescription: During a journey to Rome, Jesus and his companions stopped for three days in Capua. From this notable city, they traveled along the Appian Way toward Rome, eager to visit the empire's grand capital. (130:0.2) (130:8.6)
improvedDescription: An ancient city in Italy where Jesus and his companions stopped for three days during their journey to Rome. From Capua, they traveled along the famous Appian Way toward the empire's capital.

name: caravans
originalDescription: By 7000 B.C. camel trains were making regular trips to distant Mesopotamia (79:3.7) Jesus Joseph’s caravan repair shop
improvedDescription: Trade expeditions that transported goods and passengers across ancient highways connecting distant regions. By 7000 B.C., camel trains were making regular trips to Mesopotamia, and Palestine became a major crossroads for caravan routes from the Orient to Mediterranean seaports.

name: carbon
originalDescription: Some of the upper layers of rock deposits contain small amounts of shale or slate of dark colors, indicating the presence of organic carbon and testifying to the existence of the ancestors of those forms of plant life which overran the earth during the Carboniferous or coal age. (58:7.10) Much of the carbon of the atmosphere was abstracted to form the carbonates. Later on, much greater quantities of these carbon gases were consumed by the early and prolific plant life. (57:7.8) In those suns which are encircuited in the space-energy channels, solar energy is liberated by various complex nuclear-reaction chains, the most common of which is the hydrogen-carbon-helium reaction. In this metamorphosis, carbon acts as an energy catalyst (41:8.1)
improvedDescription: A chemical element found in rock deposits indicating organic life and serving as an energy catalyst in stellar nuclear reactions. Carbon acts as a catalyst in the hydrogen-carbon-helium reaction that liberates solar energy in encircuited suns.

name: carbon dioxide
originalDescription: the high degree of carbon in the atmosphere afforded the new land varieties of life opportunity for speedy and luxuriant growth. (58:1.8) 950,000,000 years ago the atmosphere was clearing up, but the amount of carbon dioxide continued large. 220,000,000 years ago the land was overrun by luxurious vegetation; this was indeed the age of ferns. Carbon dioxide was still present in the atmosphere but in lessening degree. (57:8.5,17) (59:2.9) (59:5.2) Much of the carbon of the atmosphere was abstracted to form the carbonates of the various metals which abounded in the superficial layers of the planet. (57:7.8)
improvedDescription: A gas that was abundant in Earth's early atmosphere, providing ideal conditions for luxuriant plant growth but preventing animal life from existing on land. Much atmospheric carbon dioxide was later consumed by early plant life and abstracted to form carbonates.

name: carbon monoxide
originalDescription: The primitive planetary atmosphere of a world in the volcanic age presents a queer spectacle: is slowly evolving, now containing some water vapor, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride, but there is little or no free nitrogen or free oxygen. (57:7.6)
improvedDescription: A gas present in the primitive planetary atmosphere during Earth's volcanic age, along with water vapor, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride, while free nitrogen and oxygen remained scarce.

name: Carboniferous
originalDescription: Carboniferous is the age of ferns and frogs, between 220 to 180 million years ago (59:5)
improvedDescription: The Carboniferous period, known as the age of ferns and frogs, occurred between 220 to 180 million years ago and marked a time when the stage was ideally set for the appearance of the first land animals.

name: careers
originalDescription: We must lighten our burdens of soul by speedily acquiring a long-distance view of our destiny, a universe expansion of our career. (156:5.8) When Thought Adjusters indwell human minds, they bring with them the model careers, the ideal lives, as determined and foreordained by themselves (110:2.1) Adjusters cannot stop or even materially alter our career struggle of time; Adjusters cannot lessen the hardships of life as we journey on through this world of toil. (111:7.2) All things are sacred in the lives of those who are spirit led; that is, subordinated to truth, ennobled by love, dominated by mercy, and restrained by fairness—justice. (155:6.11) (192:2.13) bestowal Paradise Sons have worked in every conceivable occupation throughout the bestowal on their planets (20:6.3) a devoted and determined effort to realize eternal destiny is wholly compatible with a lighthearted and joyous life and with a successful and honorable career on earth. (110:3.4) Jesus deplored all tendency toward overspecialization, toward becoming narrow-minded and circumscribed in life’s activities (149:4.3,6) (155:1.4) life not real to one who cannot do one thing expertly (160:4.11)
improvedDescription: Careers represent the lifelong vocational and spiritual journeys of individuals, encompassing both earthly occupations and eternal destiny. The divine spirit brings model careers and ideal lives to guide human development, while devoted effort toward eternal goals remains wholly compatible with successful and honorable earthly work.

name: Caribbean region
originalDescription: 400 million years ago, the Caribbean region experienced significant elevation alongside highlands rising along the Atlantic and Pacific coasts. By 350 million years ago, despite massive continental flooding, the Caribbean remained notably elevated, marking it as a geologically distinct and prominent area. (59:1.5) (59:2.2)
improvedDescription: The Caribbean region was a geologically elevated area that remained prominently above sea level during major continental flooding periods 400 and 350 million years ago, making it a distinct highland feature when much of North America and Europe were submerged.

name: Carpathians
originalDescription: 40 million years ago, during significant land elevation in the Northern Hemisphere, the Carpathians emerged as part of highlands rising above the water. These mountains formed islands in the expansive seas that covered much of Europe, marking a distinct geological feature during this epoch of terrestrial activity and shifting landscapes. (61:1.12)
improvedDescription: The Carpathians were mountain highlands that emerged as islands above the water during the latter part of an epoch 40 million years ago, when most of Europe was submerged under expanded seas and lakes.

name: carpenter
originalDescription: Jesus was an expert carpenter and cabinetmaker. He also was a boatbuilder (126:1.1) (129:1.2-3) Jesus made use of his first and only parable having to do with his own trade, the parable of the foolish: “Do not make the mistake of the foolish carpenter who wastes valuable time squaring, measuring, and smoothing his worm-eaten and inwardly rotting timber and then, when he has thus bestowed all of his labor upon the unsound beam, must reject it as unfit to enter into the foundations of the building which he would construct to withstand the assaults of time and storm” (156:5.2)
improvedDescription: Jesus was an expert carpenter and cabinetmaker who also became skilled as a boatbuilder, creating new styles of boats and superior boatmaking methods during his year working with Zebedee at Capernaum. He used carpentry in his only parable related to his own trade, warning against building on unsound foundations.

name: carpenter shop
originalDescription: Joseph added carpenter shop to Nazareth home (122:6.3)
improvedDescription: Joseph added a carpenter shop to the Nazareth home as a large room that served as a workspace during the day and sleeping quarters at night after Martha's birth.

name: Carrara marble
originalDescription: Around 150 million years ago, during the early reptile period, the Carrara marble formed from limestone deposited during a sea invasion in the southern Alps. This limestone underwent transformations to become the famous marble now quarried in the region. (60:1.4)
improvedDescription: Carrara marble formed from limestone deposited during a sea invasion in the southern Alps around 150 million years ago during the early reptile period, later transforming into the famous marble now quarried in the region.

name: carriage of life
originalDescription: the carriage of life is a metamorphic state in which a transporter seraphim can become to transport immaterial bodies. The seraphim adopts an elongated form surrounded by a queer amber light, and when it takes off it does so at lightninglike speed. (39:5.14)
improvedDescription: The carriage of life is the metamorphic state of a transport seraphim prepared to carry immaterial bodies through universe circuits, appearing as an elongated, torpedo-shaped outline surrounded by amber light that departs at lightning-like speed.

name: carrier pigeons
originalDescription: carrier pigeons announced arrival of Adam and Eve, kept up by believer settlements for just such an occasion. (74:2.3) It was on Prince’s staff times that carrier pigeons were first used, being taken on long journeys for the purpose of sending messages or calls for help. (66:5.6)
improvedDescription: Carrier pigeons were first used during the Prince's staff times for sending messages over long distances and were maintained by believer settlements to announce the arrival of Adam and Eve in Eden.

name: Carthage
originalDescription: A great Andite colony settled on the Mediterranean near the later site of Carthage. (80:7.11) Carthaginians were part of an older race which included the Jews, the Babylonians, and the Phoenicians (121:2.1) Jesus gave a speech on time and space in Carthage (130:7)
improvedDescription: Carthage was a Mediterranean city near the site of a great Andite colony, whose people were part of the older Semitic race that included Jews, Babylonians, and Phoenicians. Jesus visited Carthage during his Mediterranean tour and gave a speech on time and space there.

name: Caspian Sea
originalDescription: Caspian Sea was expanded in ancient times (80:2.5) Jesus led a caravan to Caspian Sea, from April 1, 24 to just one year later (134:2) civilizations
improvedDescription: A large inland sea that was significantly expanded in ancient times and served as an important geographical landmark and migration route. Jesus conducted a caravan to the Caspian Sea region from April 24 to April 25 CE, and the area was central to various ancient civilizations and migration patterns of the Adamites, Andites, and other early peoples.

name: Caspin
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 CE and Jesus' death.

name: caste systems
originalDescription: The original caste system of India was based on color, as was that of early Egypt. (70:8.11) The caste system failed to save the Aryan race, but it did succeed in perpetuating the Brahmans, who, in turn, have maintained their religious hegemony in India to the present time. (94:2.1,5) Gautama made a determined but unavailing fight against the growing caste system. (94:7.2) Said Jesus: “Though human beings differ in many ways, the one from another, before God and in the spiritual world all mortals stand on an equal footing”. (133:0.3) caste systems was instituted by Aryans in India to save racial identity (79:2.6) (79:4.5-9) castes purchased the enhancement of social stability but diminished personal initiative and prevented social cooperation (70:8.13) Caste systems and social levels are vestigial remnants of olden prohibitions. (89:1.6)
improvedDescription: Social stratification systems based on rigid class divisions, originally established by the Aryans in India to preserve racial identity and prevent intermarriage with conquered peoples. While caste systems provided social stability, they diminished personal initiative and prevented social cooperation, becoming obstacles to spiritual and social progress.

name: casting lots
originalDescription: apostles selected Matthias by casting lots (193:6.3,6) Casting lots, while it may be a convenient way of settling many minor difficulties, is not a method designed to disclose the divine will. (150:3.7,12) The Romans bodily took over Greek culture, putting representative government in the place of government by lot. (195:2.1) Modern survivals of this shamanism proclivity for casting lots are illustrated, not only in the many games of chance but also in child games  (90:2.4) soldiers divided Jesus’ clothes by casting lots (187:2.8)
improvedDescription: An ancient practice of making decisions by chance, used by the apostles to select Matthias as Judas's replacement and by Roman soldiers who divided Jesus' garments at the crucifixion. While convenient for settling minor difficulties, it is not a method designed to disclose divine will but rather represents material chance.

name: castor oil
originalDescription: Sumerians used castor oil as medicine (90:4.9)
improvedDescription: A medicinal oil used by the Sumerians as an early remedy, along with opium, representing one of the ancient medical practices that originated in the Euphrates valley.

name: castration
originalDescription: The making of eunuchs was at first a modification of the idea of human sacrifice. (89:8.3) The priests of the mother cult were especially active in teaching the virtue of physical suffering, setting the example by submitting themselves to castration. (89:3.4)
improvedDescription: The practice of making eunuchs, originally developed as a modification of human sacrifice and later adopted by priests of the mother cult as a form of physical suffering and religious devotion.

name: cataclysms
improvedDescription: Major catastrophic events or disasters that can dramatically alter planetary conditions or civilizations.

name: cataleptic fits
originalDescription: Women who were able to throw themselves into a trance or a cataleptic fit became powerful shamanesses; later, such women became prophets and spirit mediums. (90:1.3)
improvedDescription: Trance-like states that certain women could induce in themselves, making them powerful shamanesses and later prophets and spirit mediums who claimed to communicate with the spirits of the dead.

name: catalyst
originalDescription: In those suns which are encircuited in the space-energy channels, solar energy is liberated by various complex nuclear-reaction chains, the most common of which is the hydrogen-carbon-helium reaction. In this metamorphosis, carbon acts as an energy catalyst (41:8.1)
improvedDescription: In stellar nuclear reactions, carbon acts as an energy catalyst in the hydrogen-carbon-helium reaction chain, facilitating the conversion of hydrogen into helium without being consumed in the process.

name: catastrophes
improvedDescription: Major disastrous events that can cause significant destruction, upheaval, or dramatic changes to civilizations, societies, or planetary conditions.

name: caterpillars
originalDescription: The philosophy of the universe cannot be predicated on the observations of so-called science. If such a metamorphosis could not be seen, a scientist would be inclined to deny the possibility of developing a butterfly out of a caterpillar. (42:9.4)
improvedDescription: Larval stage of butterflies and moths used as an example in The Urantia Book to illustrate how dramatic natural transformations might challenge scientific understanding if they could not be observed.

name: cats
originalDescription: evolution superstition
improvedDescription: Feline carnivores that evolved from early mammalian ancestors and diversified into various species including modern domestic cats, panthers, and saber-toothed tigers. The cat family dominated animal life during certain prehistoric periods and were considered magical in some ancient cultures.

name: Catskill Mountains
originalDescription: Around 240 million years ago, during a period of widespread land subsidence, the Catskill Mountains formed as one of North America's largest geological monuments from this era. These mountains, located along the west bank of the Hudson River, bear evidence of the Devonian floods that slowly advanced and retreated. (59:4.15)
improvedDescription: Mountain range along the west bank of the Hudson River that formed around 240 million years ago during widespread land subsidence, representing one of North America's largest geological monuments from the Devonian period.

name: cattle
originalDescription: When animals became fetishes, there ensued the taboos on eating the flesh of the fetish animal. At one time the cow was a fetish, the milk being taboo while the excreta were highly esteemed. (88:1.5) ancient use Jesus
improvedDescription: Domesticated bovines that were among the first animals tamed by humans and became valuable sources of food, milk products, and labor. Cattle were also used as units of barter in early economic systems and sometimes regarded as sacred animals in various cultures.

name: Caucasians
originalDescription: it is Andite inheritance that gives to the polyglot mixture of the so-called white races that generalized homogeneity which has been called Caucasoid. (78:4.4) The Caucasoid race is the Andite blend of the Nodite and Adamic stocks, further modified by primary and some secondary Sangik admixture and by considerable Andonic crossing. The Occidental white races, together with some Indian and Turanian peoples, are included in this group. (81:4.11)
improvedDescription: The Caucasoid racial group representing the Andite blend of Nodite and Adamic stocks, further modified by primary Sangik admixture and Andonic crossing. This classification includes the Occidental white races along with some Indian and Turanian peoples.

name: causation
originalDescription: causation is the cosmic mind response in reality domain of physical senses (16:6.6) The cosmic mind unfailingly responds, recognizes response, on three levels of universe reality. (16:6.5) evolution in thinking about causality antecedent causation First Cause
improvedDescription: The cosmic mind response recognizing the reality domain of physical senses and logical uniformity, representing one of three fundamental levels of universe reality. Causation involves the relationship between effects and their antecedent causes in the material universe.

name: caves
originalDescription: Andon’s family, the first humans, lived in caves (63:3.3-5) During the vogue of a particular animal, crude outlines of it would be drawn on the walls of the caves, and later on, as continued progress was made in the arts, such an animal god was engraved on various ornaments. (63:6.3) The ignorant and superstitious aborigines believed that caves led to the underworld, with its evil spirits and demons, in contrast with the mountains, where good spirits lived (85:1.5) the Jordan poured forth from an underground cave (157:3.1) Mithraics worshiped in caves (98:5.4) (98:6.3) northern white race built dwellings; they never lived in caves (80:9.4) In the caves of western Europe may be found human bones mingled with the remains of both tropic and arctic animals, testifying that man lived in these regions throughout the later epochs of the advancing and retreating glaciers. (61:6.4) The southern Cro-Magnons generally lived in caves and grottoes. (80:3.6) (80:9.4)
improvedDescription: Natural underground formations that served as dwelling places for early humans and various prehistoric peoples. Caves were also significant in religious practices, being associated with underworld spirits in primitive beliefs and used as places of worship by some mystery cults.

name: Cedes
originalDescription: Cedes made a record of Jesus’ life in A.D. 78 (121:8.9)
improvedDescription: A believer in Antioch who made a brief record of Jesus' life in A.D. 78, which later served as one of the source materials that Luke used in compiling his Gospel.

name: celestial artisans
originalDescription: These beings are the master artists and artisans of the morontia and lower spirit realms. (44:0.1) The celestial artisans are not created as such; they are a selected and recruited. The original teaching corps of these artisans was 7000 Havona instructors assigned by Infinite Spirit (44:0.3,13) all Ascending sons of God are eligible to become celestial artisans, but no one may enlist for less than one superuniveral millennium (44:0.4) Such artisans are distributed throughout the grand universe but their chief realm of activity is in the constellations (44:0.1) they co-operate with recorder-teacher transition seraphim (48:6.30) they also co-operate with reversion directors (48:4.8) The celestial artisans direct the native spornagia in the extensive work of botanic decoration and biologic embellishment of system capitals (43:6.7) (46:5.31) univitatia are largest group associated with celestial artisans in the various courtesy colonies (43:7.4-5) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.151) Whereas human artists must resort to inert paint and lifeless marble to portray their concepts, the celestial artisans and the univitatia more frequently utilize living materials to represent their ideas and to capture their ideals. (43:6.7) they work under supervision of angels of progress on Urantia (44:8.1) headquarters types destiny
improvedDescription: Master artists and artisans of the morontia and lower spirit realms who are responsible for spiritual beautification and morontia embellishment throughout the grand universe. These beings are recruited from selected personalities and serve in seven major divisions of artistic activity.

name: celestial beings
improvedDescription: General term referring to spiritual entities and personalities who exist in the celestial realms above the material universe, encompassing various orders of divine and semi-divine creatures.

name: Celestial Guardians
originalDescription: They are touchingly affectionate, superbly loyal, exquisitely intelligent, supremely wise regarding concept they personify (22:10.8) They are the apprehending agents of the Ancients of Days; they go forth from Uversa to bring back beings whose presence is required before the superuniverse judges; they execute the mandates for the detention of any personality in the superuniverse. (22:9.4) They are the personality embodiment of some divine idea, some universal ideal, as it has never before been conceived, expressed, or trinitized. (22:10.2) they are assisted by ascending Son-fused mortals (22:9.3) they are also assisted by Spirit-fused mortals (22:9.4) (40:10.9) they carry out routine work of major and minor sectors (18:4.4-5) (22:9.3) The Celestial Guardians are the officers of the courts of the Ancients of Days, functioning as court messengers and as bearers of the summonses and decisions of the various tribunals of the superuniverse governments. (22:9.4) they deal with individuals, not groups (22:5.6) they are in the group of Trinitized Sons of Perfection, embraced by Paradise Trinity (22:9.7) they appear in Uversa personalities register (30:2.60) they have never been indwelt by Adjusters (22:9.5) there are almost 7 billion (22:9.3) their performance is less distinguished than the one of High Son Assistants (22:9.1) they present Spirit-fused mortals to Ancients of Days, accepting them as wards of the superuniverse (40:10.9) The Trinitized Sons of Perfection. The Celestial Guardians and their co-ordinates, the High Son Assistants, comprise a unique group of twice-trinitized personalities. They are the creature-trinitized sons of Paradise-Havona, and some are retrinitized by the Paradise Trinity (18:4.4) (22:1.12) (22:9.1) Trinity Teacher Sons are who assign Celestial Guardians to superuniverses (22:9.1)
improvedDescription: Celestial Guardians are twice-trinitized beings who serve as officers of the courts of the Ancients of Days in the superuniverse, functioning as court messengers, apprehending agents, and bearers of summonses and decisions. They are the personality embodiment of single divine ideas or universal ideals, possessed of touching affection, supreme loyalty, and exquisite intelligence regarding their one specialized concept. Nearly seven billion strong in Orvonton, they are assisted by ascending Son-fused and Spirit-fused mortals in carrying out superuniverse administrative duties.

name: celestial influences
improvedDescription: Influences originating from or associated with celestial beings and realms that affect spiritual development and cosmic relationships.

name: celestial musicians
originalDescription: celestial musicians are a subtype of celestial artisans (44:1.1-15)
improvedDescription: Celestial musicians are a specialized group of celestial artisans who create celestial harmony through the manipulation of spiritual forces including spiritual sound, light, energy impingements, color symphonies, harmony of associated spirits, melody of thought, and the music of space. They produce melodies that can be broadcast throughout the universe and represent the highest forms of morontia and spirit musical expression.

name: Celestial Overseers
originalDescription: Celestial Overseers are part of Universe Aids group (37:1.7) they appear named in Uversa personality register (30:2.146) Before leaving the universe of Nebadon, most Urantia mortals will be afforded opportunity to serve for a longer or shorter time as members of the Nebadon corps of Celestial Overseers. (37:6.7) There are upward of three million of them in Nebadon, and they are all volunteers who have qualified by experience to serve as educational advisers to the entire realm.  (37:6.1)
improvedDescription: Celestial Overseers are a recruited corps of over three million volunteers in Nebadon who serve as educational advisers and inspectors of the local universe school system. They are qualified by experience to oversee the mind training and spirit education of ascending mortals, operating from headquarters on the Salvington worlds of the Melchizedeks.

name: Celestial Recorders
originalDescription: Celestial Recorders are the recorders who execute all records in duplicate, making an original spirit recording and a semimaterial counterpar (25:6.1) Celestial Recorders are not created as such; they are ascendant seraphim from the local universes. (25:6.1) (37:8.8) Custodians of Records serve as their Chiefs (25:5.4) (25:6.1) the corps of Celestial Recorders is of permanent assignment, they are mustered into these services for the distant future (25:6.5) never has there been discovered a falsification in their records as they are being scrutinized by their exalted fellows from Uversa and by the Mighty Messengers, who certify to the correctness of the duplicates. (25:6.3) none have ever gone astray (25:6.3) (53:7.5) in Nebadon there are 75 senior or supervising recorders; on Uversa there are 8 millions (25:6.4) (37:8.8) seraphim may attain Paradise through service as Celestial Recorders (39:8.3) Celestial Recorders sponsor superuniverse seraphic record-keeping (27:5.4) they are trained by Divine Counselors and Perfectors of Wisdom (25:6.1) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.79)
improvedDescription: Celestial Recorders are ascendant seraphim who execute all superuniverse records in duplicate, creating both original spirit recordings and semimaterial counterparts through their ability to manipulate both spiritual and material energy simultaneously. They are trained by Divine Counselors and Perfectors of Wisdom, with never a falsification discovered in their records nor any defection from their ranks.

name: celibate orders
originalDescription: celibate was created and maintained by individuals lacking normal sex urges (82:3.9) Among the reasons cited in support of a celibate priesthood, the chief was the baseness of woman. (84:4.4)
improvedDescription: Celibate orders are religious or social groups of men and women who abstain from marriage and sexual relations, often started and maintained by individuals lacking normal sexual urges. These orders arose in various cultures as part of religious practice, though they were sometimes influenced by negative attitudes toward women.

name: cells
originalDescription: injured cells begin the secretion of certain substances which facilitate healing processes in the wound (65:4.3-6) human cells are akin to the living disease-producing microscopic and ultramicroscopic organisms (76:4.7) The biologic unit of material life is the protoplasmic cell, the communal association of chemical, electrical, and other basic energies. (49:1.2)
improvedDescription: Cells are the basic biological units of material life, consisting of protoplasmic associations of chemical, electrical, and other basic energies. When injured, cells possess the ability to elaborate chemical substances that stimulate healing processes and activate neighboring cells to facilitate wound repair and cellular reproduction.

name: Celsus
originalDescription: Celsus was a believer of Jesus at Caesarea Philippi (157:3.7) (157:4.1) (157:6.1) the apostles spent the night with Celsus on one occasion (158:6.1)
improvedDescription: Celsus was a believer and host of Jesus at Caesarea Philippi who provided lodging for Jesus and the apostles on multiple occasions during their ministry. The apostles stayed overnight at his home following significant events, including after Jesus' transfiguration on Mount Hermon.

name: Celta
originalDescription: Celta was the daughter of a Roman centurion; she was part of the body of twelve female evangelists (150:1.1) she was present at the sixteenth appearance of Jesus (193:0)
improvedDescription: Celta was the daughter of a Roman centurion who became one of the twelve women commissioned by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom. She served as part of the women's evangelistic corps and was present at Jesus' sixteenth morontia appearance in Jerusalem.

name: cemetery
originalDescription: A crucified person could not be buried in a Jewish cemetery; there was a strict law against such a procedure. (188:1.2) Kheresa lunatic lived in the caves of the cemetery (151:6.2)
improvedDescription: A cemetery is a burial ground where the dead are interred. Jewish law strictly prohibited burying crucified persons in Jewish cemeteries, leading to Jesus being buried in Joseph of Arimathea's private family tomb instead of a public cemetery.

name: Cenozoic
originalDescription: Cenozoic was the mammalian era, from 50 million years ago to present (59:0.6) (61:0.2) (61:7.19)
improvedDescription: The Cenozoic era, also known as the mammalian era or recent-times era, spans the last fifty million years of Earth's geological history and continues to the present day.

name: censorship
originalDescription: hidden information nothing hidden forever
improvedDescription: The practice of controlling or restricting information, with the understanding that ultimately nothing will remain hidden forever, as all things will eventually be revealed.

name: census
originalDescription: the ancients always opposed the taking of a census, “numbering the people. It was held unlucky to count cattle or other possessions. (88:1.6) Caesar Augustus decreed census in 8 B.C. The Jews had always been greatly prejudiced against any attempt to “number the people” (122:7.1) Joseph did go to the City of David, Bethlehem, to be registered for the Roman census, because Joseph’s paternal ancestor, being an orphan, was adopted by a direct descendant of David (122:4.3)
improvedDescription: A counting or enumeration of people or possessions, which ancient peoples considered unlucky and opposed, believing it was dangerous to 'number the people.'

name: Census Directors
originalDescription: One Census Directors is stationed on capital of each local universe (24:2.4) Another one is stationed on each Havona circuit (24:2.3) they are persons with recognizable spirit form (24:2.9) they are personally sensitive and responsive to intelligent will. They can give us the number, nature, birth, death, and whereabouts of all will creatures (24:2.2,7,8) they have the headquarters on superuniverse capitals; their reserves are on Paradise worlds of Spirit (24:0.10) (24:2.3) they are infallible in function; they never default, neither do they falsify. (24:2.9) They are not concerned with the records of your life and its doings; they are not in any sense recording personalities. (24:2.7) They are so created as to be able to maintain perfect synchrony with the reflectivity technique of the superuniverses (24:2.2) possess tremendous endowment of antigravity (24:0.10) Salsatia is Nebadon Census Director and maintains headquarters within the Gabriel sector of Salvington. (24:2.7) (37:8.4) The Census Directors are a special and completed creation of the Infinite Spirit, and they exist in numbers unknown to us. (24:2.2) Usatia is the superuniverse chief of all Orvonton directors (24:2.5) they appear in personality registers (30:1.73) (30:2.69)
improvedDescription: Special beings created by the Infinite Spirit who maintain perfect records of all will creatures throughout the universe, stationed on superuniverse capitals and local universe headquarters to register births and deaths of intelligent beings.

name: centaurs
originalDescription: creatures that were regarded as half human and half animal, such as centaurs, were onetime objects of worship (85:3.2)
improvedDescription: Mythological creatures regarded as half human and half animal that were objects of worship among early peoples who revered animals for their power and cunning.

name: Center of Centers
improvedDescription: A designation referring to the Universal Father as the absolute center of all reality and existence.

name: centipedes
originalDescription: Centipedes emerged early in the evolution of multicellular life, developing alongside other organisms such as starfish, sea urchins, insects, and crustaceans. These creatures represent one of the groups that survived the long evolutionary struggle, although many other species perished. Centipedes, along with other early animals like earthworms and mollusks, are considered stationary branches in the evolutionary tree, reflecting life forms that ceased to progress significantly over time. (65:2.5)
improvedDescription: Multi-legged arthropods that evolved early alongside starfish, sea urchins, and other primitive creatures, representing one of the stationary branches of the evolutionary tree that failed to progress significantly.

name: Central America
originalDescription: an amalgamated orange-blue race migrated to Mexico and Central America (64:7.5) In Mexico, Central America, and in the mountains of South America the more enduring civilizations were founded by a race predominantly red but containing a considerable admixture of the yellow, orange, and blue. (79:5.8)
improvedDescription: A region where amalgamated races migrated and established civilizations, particularly by predominantly red peoples mixed with yellow, orange, and blue racial elements.

name: central universe
improvedDescription: The eternal universe of Havona, the perfect central creation that exists at the heart of all reality, surrounded by the seven superuniverses.

name: centurions
improvedDescription: Roman military officers who commanded units of approximately one hundred soldiers in the Roman army.

name: cephalopods
originalDescription: Cephalopods, ancestors of modern nautiluses, octopuses, squids, and cuttlefish, were well-developed and became dominant marine predators after the decline of trilobites. Some cephalopods grew to impressive sizes, up to fifteen feet long. During this era, over a thousand species of ammonites evolved on the Pacific coast of California, showcasing the diversity and evolutionary advancements of cephalopods. These creatures thrived as masters of the seas during significant transitional periods in Earth's history. (59:2.11) (59:3.5,8) (60:1.13)
improvedDescription: Cephalopods, ancestors of modern nautiluses, octopuses, squids, and cuttlefish, were well-developed marine creatures that became dominant sea predators after the decline of trilobites. Some cephalopods grew to impressive sizes, up to fifteen feet long, and over a thousand species of ammonites evolved on the Pacific coast of California, showcasing their remarkable diversity and evolutionary success as masters of the ancient seas.

name: cereals
originalDescription: cereals were abundant in Cretaceous (60:3.19)
improvedDescription: Cereals were abundant seed-bearing grasses during the Cretaceous period, representing a major evolutionary development in plant life alongside fruits and other flowering plants that would fundamentally transform the plant world.

name: ceremonies
originalDescription: The evolution of religious observances progressed from placation, avoidance, exorcism, coercion, conciliation, and propitiation to sacrifice, atonement, and redemption. (90:0.1) Prayer is the technique whereby, sooner or later, every religion becomes institutionalized. And in time prayer becomes associated with deleterious agencies as ceremonials (91:5.6) ancient is sacred Rome purification ceremonies Jesus
improvedDescription: Ceremonies are ritualistic religious observances that evolved from primitive placation and propitiation practices into complex forms of worship involving sacrifice, atonement, and redemption. These formal religious rituals, while intended to enhance spiritual connection, often became substitutes for genuine religious experience and were frequently dominated by priests who used ancient languages and mystifying practices to maintain their authority.

name: certainty
originalDescription: certainty in dogmatic religion spiritual certainty God's certainty
improvedDescription: Certainty represents different levels of conviction: physical certainty based on scientific logic, moral certainty rooted in philosophical wisdom, and spiritual certainty arising from genuine religious experience. True spiritual certainty, possessed by God-knowing believers, transcends doubt and provides unshakable confidence in divine truth, while remaining tolerant of honest inquiry and criticism.

name: Ceylon
originalDescription: Asoka established Buddhism in Ceylon (94:9.1) Hinayana Buddhism persisted there, division of Buddhism which clings to the early or asocial doctrine (94:9.4) least progressive Saharans emigrated to Arabia and thence through northern Mesopotamia and India to faraway Ceylon (80:2.2)
improvedDescription: Ceylon was an island where Buddhism was established by the missionary efforts of Emperor Asoka and where the fundamentalist Hinayana division of Buddhism persisted, clinging to the early asocial doctrine of Siddhartha. It was also settled by some of the least progressive Saharan groups who migrated through Arabia, Mesopotamia, and India.

name: Chaldeans
originalDescription: The Chaldeans near Ur were among the most advanced of the eastern Semites. (96:2.1) The Chaldeans were among the first to abandon the sacrificing of humans for ordinary occasions, substituting therefor animals (89:6.2) The Chaldean tribes recognized the right of a wife to impose a premarital pledge upon her spouse not to take a second wife or concubine; both the Greeks and the Romans favored monogamous marriage. (83:6.3) they were star cultists and considered themselves to be the children of the sky father and the earth mother. The Chaldeans put the sun in the center of “the seven circles of the universe.” Also looked upon the stars as the cause of suffering (85:5.1-2) (90:3.8)
improvedDescription: The Chaldeans were an advanced eastern Semitic people near Ur who practiced star worship, believing themselves to be children of the sky father and earth mother. They were among the first to abandon human sacrifice for ordinary occasions, recognized women's rights in marriage contracts, and developed astronomical concepts including placing the sun at the center of seven universal circles.

name: chalk
originalDescription: During the Cretaceous period, chalk formations, derived from deposits of foraminifers, predominated and gave the epoch its name. These deposits, which also include greensand marl, shale, sandstone, and small amounts of limestone, reached thicknesses of 200 to 10,000 feet in regions such as the foothills of the Rocky Mountains and Europe. These porous layers store water, making them crucial for supplying arid regions today. Additionally, tectonic movements and associated volcanic activity contributed to significant crustal deformations, impacting Pacific coastal areas and mountain regions like the Andes. (60:3) The Cretaceous period marked the end of major marine invasions, especially in North America, where 24 extensive inundations had shaped the land. Chalk formations from this time, derived from marine deposits, remain prominent today. These include limestone now uplifted to 10,000 feet in the Alps, 16,000 feet in the Himalayas, and 20,000 feet in Tibet. Chalk deposits are also found along the coasts of Africa and Australia, the west coast of South America, and around the West Indies. (60:4) (61:1.13)
improvedDescription: Chalk formations from the Cretaceous period were created by prolific foraminifers and other marine organisms, giving this geological epoch its name. These deposits, along with limestone, reached enormous thicknesses and today can be found elevated to great heights in mountain ranges like the Alps, Himalayas, and around various coastlines worldwide.

name: challenges
originalDescription: we must accept challenges without complaint and to face difficulties and uncertainties without fear (48:6.35) the apostles' five-month ordeal of fishing and preaching turned out to be grueling but it sparked a love and fidelity to Jesus above all else (138:9) mortal life is short and intense test, but there are many other stages of proof in the future existence (14:5.1) Difficulties may challenge mediocrity and defeat the fearful, but they only stimulate the true children of the Most Highs. (48:7.7) the great challenge
improvedDescription: Challenges are difficulties and uncertainties that test human character and faith, which should be accepted without complaint and faced without fear. While challenges may defeat the fearful and mediocre, they serve to stimulate and strengthen the true children of God, promoting spiritual growth and development through adversity.

name: chamber of hewn stone
originalDescription: the chamber of hewn stone was the Sanhedrin meeting place (173:1.5) (184:3.2) (186:1.1)
improvedDescription: The chamber of hewn stone was the regular meeting place of the Sanhedrin in the temple, where this Jewish governing council normally conducted its official proceedings and deliberations.

name: chance
originalDescription: there are 50,000 facts prove evolution incompatible with chance (58:2.3-4) The shamans were great believers in the mission of chance as revelatory of the will of the spirits; they frequently cast lots to arrive at decisions. (90:2.4) The savage strives to personalize everything intangible and abstract, and thus both nature and chance become personalized as ghosts—spirits—and later on as gods. (86:2) to continue to ascribe things difficult of comprehension to supernatural causes is nothing less than a lazy and convenient way of avoiding all forms of intellectual hard work. Luck is merely a term coined to cover the inexplicable (86:2.5)
improvedDescription: Chance is a term used to describe seemingly random or accidental occurrences that primitive peoples attributed to supernatural forces or luck. However, true chance or accident is incompatible with the intelligent design evident in creation, as demonstrated by thousands of scientific facts that reveal purposeful planning rather than random occurrence in the universe.

name: Chang
originalDescription: Chang was a Chinese merchant with whom Jesus had a conversation in Corinth (133:4.9)
improvedDescription: Chang was a Chinese merchant whom Jesus encountered in Corinth during his Mediterranean journey. Jesus had many visits with Chang and, upon their farewell, advised him to worship only God as his true spiritual ancestor and to follow the leadings of the Father's spirit within him.

name: change
originalDescription: do not attempt great social or economic change suddenly (71:1.22) (81:6.40) Human life is an endless change of the factors of life unified by the stability of the unchanging personality. (99:1.1) (112:5.20) the leaders of a civilization must keep pace with scientific advances to avoid taking the risk of being delayed (81:6.39) Modern man is confronted with the task of making more readjustments of human values in one generation than have been made in two thousand years. (92:7.14) Although the survival of a society depends chiefly on the progressive evolution of its mores this does not mean that each separate and isolated change in the composition of human society has been for the better (68:4.7) Values can never be static; reality signifies change, growth. Change without growth, expansion of meaning and exaltation of value, is valueless—is potential evil. (100:3.5)
improvedDescription: Change is a fundamental aspect of reality and growth, but should not be attempted suddenly in social or economic matters. The Urantia Book teaches that while human life consists of endless change unified by unchanging personality, great reforms require time and wisdom. Values can never be static, and change without growth or expansion of meaning is potentially evil.

name: chanting
originalDescription: primitive people disease was treated by chanting, howling, laying on of hands, breathing on the patient, and many other techniques.  (90:4.4)
improvedDescription: Chanting was one of the primitive healing techniques used by early peoples to treat disease, along with howling, laying on of hands, and breathing on patients. These methods were employed before the development of more sophisticated medical practices.

name: character
originalDescription: defined as acquirement effects Jesus character character in the universe
improvedDescription: Character represents the accumulated consequences of personal decisions illuminated by worship, love, and service. It consists of the correlation of factual science, moral philosophy, and genuine religious experience. Character is acquired through enlightened experience and grows through the wise utilization of life's trials and opportunities, forming the foundation for spiritual development.

name: Charax
originalDescription: Jesus left Ganid and Gonod at Charax, heading back to Palestine (133:9.4) (134:1.1)
improvedDescription: Charax was a city on the Persian Gulf where Jesus bid farewell to his Indian friends Gonod and Ganid in December of A.D. 23, marking the end of their Mediterranean journey together. From Charax, Jesus returned to Palestine via Babylon and Damascus.

name: charcoal picture
originalDescription: Jesus made a charcoal drawing on the floor of the synagogue, a portrait of his teacher, causing a huge uproar in Nazareth (124:1.4-5)
improvedDescription: Jesus drew a charcoal portrait of his teacher on the floor of the Nazareth synagogue schoolroom, which caused a significant controversy among the Jewish elders. This incident led to serious accusations against Jesus and ultimately resulted in restrictions on his artistic activities at home.

name: charity
originalDescription: Jews Jesus modern governments
improvedDescription: Charity refers to the giving of alms or assistance to those in need. Jesus taught that good deeds should be done in secret, with the left hand not knowing what the right hand does. The Urantia Book warns that both private and public charity can become pernicious when extended long-term to able-bodied citizens, and emphasizes the importance of maintaining dignity and self-respect in assistance programs.

name: charms
originalDescription: All ancient objects were magical charms. (88:5.4) Since anything connected with the body could become a fetish, the earliest magic had to do with hair and nails. (88:5.1) a world so filled with charms did much to destroy all personal ambition and initiative.  (69:2.7) (88:6.6) The primitives believed that a ceremony of consecration caused the spirit to enter the image; likewise, when certain objects were blessed, they became charms. (88:2.4) charms were employed to insure a happy and fertile marriage (83:4.4) Charms and relics are impotent to heal disease, ward off disaster, or influence evil spirits; the belief in all such material means of influencing the spiritual world is nothing but gross superstition. (150:3.6) Magical charms were concocted from a great variety of things: human flesh, tiger claws, crocodile teeth, poison plant seeds, snake venom, and human hair (88:5.2-4) The ornamental type of personal effects originated in the wearing of charms. (69:9.10) primitives rubbed charms on infections, then discarded them and supposedly experience a cure (90:4.5)
improvedDescription: Charms were magical objects believed by primitive peoples to possess supernatural powers for protection, healing, or influence. Made from diverse materials including human hair, animal parts, and personal effects, charms were thought to ward off evil and bring good fortune, though The Urantia Book identifies such beliefs as gross superstition.

name: chastity
originalDescription: Chastity in girls was a great hindrance to marriage; the bearing of a child before marriage greatly increased a girl’s desirability as a wife since the man was sure of having a fertile companion. (82:3.8,13) the chastity taboo had its origin as a phase of the property mores (82:4.5)
improvedDescription: Chastity originally developed as part of property customs, initially applying only to married women. Paradoxically, in ancient times chastity in unmarried girls was considered a hindrance to marriage, as proof of fertility was preferred. Later, virginity became a commercial asset demanded by fathers and eventually led to extreme practices of confining girls to ensure their purity.

name: chazan of Nazareth synagogue
originalDescription: the chazan or chazan was an officer of the synagogue who was in charge of teaching the children. The Urantia Book repeatedly tells us about the chazan of Nazareth that Jesus knew, but without giving us his name (123:5.3) this chazan is whom Jesus drew in charcoal, an action that caused the young Jesus a serious problem (124:1.4) he was always interested inJesus’ progress (123:5.9) (124:2.2) (124:3.5) (126:5.3) he advised Jesus on the strategy to follow in rejection of the nationalist Zealots (127:2.7-8) inaugurated a young men’s club (127:3.9) he was already deceased at the time of Jesus’ rejection at Nazareth (150:7.4) Susana, the head of the twelve women evangelists, was the daughter of this chazan (150:1.1)
improvedDescription: The chazan was the synagogue officer responsible for teaching children in Nazareth. He served as Jesus' teacher and mentor, taking special interest in his promising student's progress and spending extra time helping him master Hebrew scriptures. The chazan later counseled Jesus during nationalist controversies and inaugurated a young men's philosophical discussion club.

name: Chedorlaomer
originalDescription: Chedorlaomer is a tribal chief against whom Abraham won a victory (93:4.14)
improvedDescription: Chedorlaomer was a tribal chief against whom Abraham won a victory, after which Abraham offered a conventional sacrifice despite Melchizedek's teachings against such practices.

name: cheek
originalDescription: Jesus said he: “When an enemy smites you on one cheek, do not stand there dumb and passive but in positive attitude turn the other; that is, do the best thing possible actively to lead your brother in error away from the evil paths into the better ways of righteous living”. (140:3.14) (159:5.9)
improvedDescription: The cheek represents Jesus' teaching about nonresistance, where he instructed his followers to turn the other cheek when struck, not in passive submission but as an active, positive response to lead others away from evil toward righteousness.

name: cheese
originalDescription: cheese was a common article in days of Prince’s staff (66:5.5) Jesus learned to milk the family cow and make cheese (123:5.15)
improvedDescription: Cheese was a common food item that became part of human diet during early civilization, and Jesus learned to make cheese as a young boy while caring for his family's animals.

name: chemism
improvedDescription: Chemism refers to chemical processes and reactions, being one of the fundamental material realities that, along with light, heat, electricity, magnetism, energy, and matter, are essentially one and the same thing in origin, nature, and destiny.

name: chemistry
originalDescription: chemical facts beyond chemistry
improvedDescription: Chemistry is the science that evolved from ancient alchemy through honest scientific inquiry, studying chemical reactions and the behavior of matter, though it cannot alone explain the emergence of life or consciousness from material processes.

name: Chemosh
originalDescription: Quemos was the god of the Amorites or Amorites, similar to the Hebrew Yahweh (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Chemosh was the god of the Amorites whom the Hebrews acknowledged as real but considered subordinate to Yahweh in their developing monotheistic beliefs.

name: cherubim and sanobim
originalDescription: Cherubim and sanobim are the faithful and efficient aids of the seraphic ministers (38:7.3) celestial artisans teach efficient energy intake to cherubim and sanobim (44:5.10) chief of cherubim and sanobim took part in the council of Melchizedek receivers after rebellion (67:6.5) they are directed by seraphim, of whom they are the faithful and efficient aids (38:7.2-3) (48:5.1) Cherubim and sanobim are continuously engaged in efforts at self-improvement. By study in the schools of the planets they acquire additional tongues. (38:7.5) Cherubim and sanobim are by nature very near the morontia level of existence, and they prove to be most efficient in the borderland work of the physical, morontial, and spiritual domains. (38:7.6-7) They are the lowest order of angels, hence all the nearer of kin to the more progressive types of human beings on the evolutionary worlds. (38:7.1) (48:3.4) cherubim and sanobim may attain a full seraphic standing (38:8.2,6) (48:5.10) they appear in personality registers (30:1.68,86) (30:2.88) origin número headquarters service the rebellion types
improvedDescription: Cherubim and sanobim are the faithful and efficient assistants of seraphic ministers, functioning as energy-positive and energy-negative pairs respectively. They are the lowest order of angels, inherently near the morontia level of existence, and may eventually attain full seraphic status through service and experience.

name: Chicago
originalDescription: Chicago was the city of the visitation of the Urantia Book's revelators (113:2.7) Chicago is mentioned to establish a comparison of the energy provided by the sun. If the city had to pay for sunlight at two cents per kilowatt-hour, the bill would be more than 100 million dollars a day. (58:2.1)
improvedDescription: Chicago is referenced as "the city of this visitation" where the Urantia Book's revelators worked, and is used to illustrate the enormous energy value of sunlight, with a hypothetical daily sunshine bill exceeding 100 million dollars.

name: chickens
originalDescription: Forms are valueless when lessons are learned. No chick may be had without the shell, and no shell is of any worth after the chick is hatched. (48:6.32)
improvedDescription: Chickens are used metaphorically to illustrate that forms serve their purpose in learning but become valueless once lessons are learned, just as no chick can exist without its shell, yet the shell has no worth after the chick hatches.

name: chief of Mystery Monitors
originalDescription: The head of the Mystery Monitors is the being who presides over the College of Personalized Adjusters on Divininton, and is closely associated with Master Spirit number 1 as a counselor. (16:3.2)
improvedDescription: The chief of Mystery Monitors is the being who presides over the College of Personalized Adjusters on Divinington and serves as a close associate and supernal adviser to Master Spirit Number One.

name: chief of Nebadon archangels
originalDescription: This archangel is the one who considered different techniques for the restoration of the deceased body of Jesus, reaching the conclusion that they could do nothing (189:0.1) he is also the one who wrote papers 33 and 35 (33:8.7) (35:10.6)
improvedDescription: The chief of Nebadon archangels is a high-ranking celestial being who led the council of archangels following Jesus' burial, ultimately concluding that they could do nothing to facilitate his resurrection. This archangel is also the author of Papers 33 and 35 in The Urantia Book.

name: chief of Nebadon Voices
originalDescription: The chief of Nebadon Voices is part of the order of primary seconaphim; he is able to reflect Michael's voice to the Ancients of Days without the need for Michael to be present (28:4.6-7)
improvedDescription: The chief of Nebadon Voices is a primary seconaphim who possesses the remarkable ability to reflect Michael's voice and attitude to the Ancients of Days on Uversa without requiring Michael's physical presence, allowing instantaneous communication across space.

name: chief of Urantia cherubim
originalDescription: advised Melchizedek receivers after rebellion (67:6.5)
improvedDescription: The chief of Urantia cherubim served as an advisor on the council of planetary receivers that administered Urantia's affairs following the Lucifer rebellion, working alongside Melchizedek receivers and other celestial beings.

name: chief of Urantia seraphim
originalDescription: chief of Urantia seraphim is who advised Melchizedek receivers after rebellion (67:6.5) not a day goes by that this boss is not aware of a reflective transference, that he is called upon from Uversa for some reason (28:4.11) they take turns periodically; the current head of Urantia is the second of the order of the primary supernaphim who is in this service (27:0.2) wrote Papers 82–84, 113, 114 (82:6.12) (83:8.10) (84:8.7) (113:7.9) (114:7.18)
improvedDescription: The chief of Urantia seraphim is a primary supernaphim who commands the seraphic hosts ministering on Urantia, a position that rotates periodically among qualified beings. This being served on the advisory council to the Melchizedek planetary receivers following the rebellion and authored Papers 82-84, 113, and 114 of The Urantia Book.

name: chief priests
improvedDescription: The chief priests were high-ranking religious officials in ancient Jewish society who held positions of authority in temple worship and religious governance, often serving as members of the Sanhedrin.

name: chief recorders
originalDescription: chief recorders are part of tertiary supernaphim of Havona (26:3.5)
improvedDescription: The chief recorders are tertiary supernaphim of Havona who maintain comprehensive records in triplicate: literal files for Havona, spiritual files for their order, and formal records for Paradise, while automatically transmitting important knowledge to Paradise's living libraries.

name: chief seat
originalDescription: Said Jesus:: “My brethren, when you are bidden to a marriage feast, sit not down in the chief seat, lest, perchance, a more honored man than you has been invited, and the host will have to come to you and request that you give your place to this other and honored guest”. (167:1.5) (179:1.3-5)
improvedDescription: The chief seat refers to the place of honor at a feast or gathering. Jesus taught that one should not presume to take the chief seat but rather choose a lower position, allowing the host to invite them to a place of honor, demonstrating humility over prideful self-promotion.

name: chief supernaphim
originalDescription: this chief is the original pattern angel of Paradise (27:2.1)
improvedDescription: The chief supernaphim is the original pattern angel of Paradise who presides over the organization of all three orders of supernaphim: primary, secondary, and tertiary. This being serves as the supreme leader of all supernaphim throughout the universe.

name: chiefs of assignment
originalDescription: chiefs of assignment are who periodically disperse worshippers on Paradise, that due to his enchantment could remain in the attitude of worship forever (27:7.3) they are primary supernaphim (26:8.5) (27:0.2-9) (27:2.1) they are in charge of remand defeated candidates for the Deity adventure as pilgrims to superuniverses (26:8.5) (26:10.4)
improvedDescription: The chiefs of assignment are primary supernaphim who serve as administrators on Paradise, responsible for periodically dispersing worship assemblies, overseeing the placement of defeated Deity adventure candidates, and designating supernaphim to command seraphic hosts on reclaimed rebellious worlds like Urantia.

name: child-rearing
originalDescription: It is not best for too many years to intervene between the ages of a family of children. (55:3.10) help wisely your childs to choose their heroes and select their lifework. (140:5.14) The child should be introduced to worship in nature’s outdoors and later accompany his parents to public houses of religious assembly which are at least as materially attractive and artistically beautiful as the home in which he is daily domiciled. (167:6.6) Children are permanently impressed only by the loyalties of their adult associates; precept or even example is not lastingly influential. (100:1.4) the entire religious experience of a child is largely dependent on whether fear or love has dominated the parent-child relationship. (92:7.11) (103:2.3) (177:2.2) child education has become so difficult today because of racial mixing, artificial education and the absence of parents in education (84:7.21-24) indispensable requirement discipline historically in other planets Jesus and relatives education Jesus’ practices parents’ attitude warnings
improvedDescription: Child-rearing encompasses the comprehensive process of raising and training children, involving discipline, education, moral guidance, and spiritual development. The Urantia Book emphasizes that effective child-rearing requires both love and wisdom, with parents serving as examples of divine-human relationships while fostering the child's natural positive moral impulses and preparing them for cosmic citizenship.

name: childbirth
originalDescription: Childbearing was once generally looked upon as rendering a woman dangerous and unclean. (84:4.6) A child has been in existence about nine months before it experiences birth. (103:2.1) Among the unmixed tribes, childbirth was comparatively easy, occupying only two or three hours; it is seldom so easy among the mixed races. (76:4.2) (84:4.7) A woman is indeed sorrowful in the hour of her travail, but when she is once delivered of her child, she immediately forgets her anguish in the joy of the knowledge that a man has been born into the world. (180:6.7) Childbirth was not a painful or distressing ordeal to Fonta, the first human woman, and her immediate progeny. (63:4.2) There was a Mosaic ordinance which directed that a mother, after the passing of a certain period of time, should present herself at the temple for purification (122:9.1) (122:10.2)
improvedDescription: The process of bringing forth offspring, which was once viewed as rendering women dangerous and unclean in primitive societies. Among unmixed tribes, childbirth was comparatively easy, occupying only two or three hours, while mixed races experienced more difficulty. Early human women like Fonta did not suffer pain in childbirth, but later mixed races developed the severe pangs associated with delivery.

name: children
originalDescription: for children of Adam the age of choice was the twenty years (75:6.3) A child acquires physical entity at mortal birth, but in the matter of survival all Adjusterless children are reckoned as still attached to their parents. (49:6.12) It is indeed doubtful whether intelligent and affectionate parents are ever called upon to forgive an average and normal child. (174:1.2) children evince a tendency to converse with imaginary companions. In this way a budding ego seeks to hold communion with a fictitious alter ego. (91:3.1) angels care for children. Jesus said: “See that you despise not one of these little ones, for their angels do always behold the faces of the heavenly hosts”. (158:8.1) A child evaluates experience in accordance with the content of pleasure; maturity is proportional to the substitution of higher meanings for personal pleasure. (100:1.1) the very first conflict in the child mind is between the urges of egoism and the impulses of altruism. (103:2.7) among primitives infanticide discipline educative advices There is present that natural affection between every normal child and its father which insures an understanding and loving relationship (140:10.4) The advancing ideals of family life are leading to the concept that bringing a child into the world, instead of conferring certain parental rights, entails the supreme responsibility of human existence. (84:7.25-26) A human being’s entire afterlife is enormously influenced by what happens during the first few years of existence. (177:2.5) The consequences of the follies of misguided parents are so often shared by their innocent children. (75:5.6) Jesus The hunger of the soul cannot be satisfied with physical pleasures; the love of home and children is not augmented by the unwise pursuit of pleasure. (84:8.4) any child can best relate himself to reality by first mastering the relationships of the child-parent situation and then by enlarging this concept to embrace the family as a whole.  (8:1.11) in an unspoiled child the urge to relieve suffering is natural. (140:5.17) Children are naturally trustful, and parents should see to it that they do not lose that simple faith. (140:5.14) Children need fathers as well as mothers, and fathers need this parental experience as much as do mothers. (47:1.6) In the present industrial and urban era marriage is evolving along new economic lines. Family life has become more and more costly, while children, who used to be an asset, have become economic liabilities. (84:7.27) sons of God | faith sons of God; sonship with God parents are copartners with Makers in creating children (167:5.7) Children are permanently impressed only by the loyalties of their adult associates; precept or even example is not lastingly influential.
