(100:1.4) When children are young and unthinking, they must necessarily be admonished to honor their parents (66:7.15) (93:4.13) (131:2.12) (149:6.6) (153:3.3) (163:2.4) to enter kingdom, become as littleto enter the kingdom one must become as small as a child, that is, one must adopt the essential mental attitude and spiritual reaction to access spiritual realities (137:8.8) (140:10.4) (144:4.3) (149:6.11) (155:6.12) (170:2.21) (170:3.2) (181:2.20) (196:0.12) regarding childs who die before age of spiritual choice see probationary nursery | probationary nursery a child is wholly dependent on his parents and the associated home life for all his early concepts of everything intellectual, social, moral, and even spiritual (177:2.5) Woman’s work was derived from the selective presence of the child; women naturally love babies more than men do. (69:3.2)
improvedDescription: Young human beings who are wholly dependent on their parents and home life for all early concepts of intellectual, social, moral, and even spiritual matters. Children who die before the age of spiritual choice are repersonalized on finaliter worlds, while those reaching the age of choice must make their own spiritual decisions. Jesus emphasized that entering the kingdom of heaven requires becoming like a little child in faith and trust.

name: chimpanzees
originalDescription: chimpanzees, as well as monkeys, baboons and gorillas, descend from a common ancestor with humans, the mid-mammals; but while the pair of twins that gave rise to the chimpanzees were a inferior species, the human pair was of a higher type. (62:3.12)
improvedDescription: Primates that, along with monkeys, baboons and gorillas, descend from a common ancestor with humans called the mid-mammals. However, while the pair of twins that gave rise to chimpanzees were of an inferior species, the human pair was of a higher type, representing divergent evolutionary paths from the same ancestral source.

name: Ch’in Shih Huang Ti
originalDescription: he was one of the Chinese masters who unsuccessfully fought Confucianism, proclaiming a brotherhood based on love and not on ethical duty (94:6.11)
improvedDescription: A Chinese ruler who unsuccessfully fought against Confucianism by proclaiming a brotherhood based on love rather than ethical duty. His imperial efforts to combat Confucian doctrines were ultimately defeated by the vigorous opposition of Confucian disciples.

name: China
originalDescription: In North China there is a certain blending of Caucasoid and Mongoloid types (81:4.14) China is favored by geography. It is protected by the mountains to the west and the Pacific to the east. (79:6.12) cultural evolution political evolution religious evolution education in China geology
improvedDescription: A large nation in eastern Asia that is geographically protected by mountains to the west and the Pacific Ocean to the east. China has maintained one of the most continuous civilizations in human history, with the yellow race achieving remarkable cultural stability and social development over nearly forty thousand years.

name: China Seas
originalDescription: Buddhist missionaries braved dangers of China seas (94:9.2)
improvedDescription: Maritime waters around China that Buddhist missionaries bravely crossed as they spread their faith throughout Asia. These seas presented significant dangers to the religious teachers who ventured across them in their mission to bring Buddhism to distant lands.

name: Chinese
originalDescription: racial influences cultural evolution weird mores achievements religious beliefs Jesus contact with Chinese
improvedDescription: The people of China, primarily of the yellow race, who developed one of the most enduring civilizations in human history. The Chinese were among the first to achieve racial solidarity and large-scale cultural, social, and political organization, with their civilization persisting for nearly forty thousand years despite various retrogressions.

name: chlorine
originalDescription: The early Earth's oceans were initially fresh water, as most of the chlorine was bound with metals, leaving only a small amount combined with hydrogen to make the water slightly acidic. (57:8.3)
improvedDescription: A chemical element that was mostly bound with metals in Earth's early oceans, leaving only small amounts combined with hydrogen to make the primitive waters slightly acidic. This early ocean was essentially fresh water rather than the salty seas of today.

name: chlorophyll making
originalDescription: The most important step in plant evolution was the development of chlorophyll-making ability, and the second greatest advance was the evolution of the spore into the complex seed. (65:6.3)
improvedDescription: The evolutionary development of the ability to produce chlorophyll, which was the most important step in plant evolution. This capability allowed plants to harness sunlight for photosynthesis, fundamentally transforming life on the planet.

name: choice
improvedDescription: The capacity for decision-making that represents a fundamental aspect of human experience and spiritual development. The ability to choose between alternatives is essential for moral growth and the development of character, particularly in relation to spiritual values and divine will.

name: Chorazin
originalDescription: In no place where Jesus had taught had he met with such a general rejection of his message as in the city of Chorazim. (146:4.6) (155:2) Due to that the words of Jesus were: “Woe upon the light-rejecting inhabitants of Chorazin, Bethsaida-Julias, and Capernaum, the cities which did not well receive these messengers”. (163:6.5)
improvedDescription: A city in Galilee where Jesus taught but met with general rejection of his message. Jesus pronounced woe upon its light-rejecting inhabitants, along with those of Bethsaida-Julias and Capernaum, saying these cities did not well receive his messengers.

name: chosen people
originalDescription: There is no such thing as a “chosen people. (134:5.4) Jews dangers of the doctrine
improvedDescription: A religious concept claiming divine selection of a particular group. The Urantia Book teaches there is no such thing as a chosen people, as the rule of the Most Highs is designed for the greatest good of all mortals. This doctrine has historically led to racial superiority attitudes and religious intolerance.

name: Christ
originalDescription: The divine Jesus, who was called Christ, became a religion, Christianity (196:2.4) Jesus of Nazareth must no longer be sacrificed, not even for the splendid concept of the glorified Christ, but must be presented as the living Jesus, as the ideal of personal religious life (196:1.2) (196:2.5) Michael of Nebadon is also referred to as Christ Michael (0:5.3) (20:4.4) (21:0.3) (21:6.4) (27:0.2) (30:4.11) (33:1.1) (33:2.3) (37:2.6) (37:5.1) (40:6.5) (40:7.2) (45:4.1) (50:6.5) (51:3.9) (52:5.5) (52:6.1) (53:0.2) (53:5.3) (75:2.2) (77:6.6) (77:7.5) (83:8.3) (92:4.8) (93:9.11) (93:10.5-8) (94:6.1) (98:7.2) (101:6.5) (102:3.14) (119:8.8-9) (120:0.9) (120:4.2-3) (121:1.1) (121:3.1) (136:5.4) (196:1.1) Christ means the anointed one (98:7.2)
improvedDescription: Christ means "the anointed one" and refers to the divine Jesus who became the central figure of Christianity. While the human Jesus had a religion, the divine Christ became a religion. Christ Michael is also the title used for the Creator Son Michael of Nebadon after his bestowal on Urantia.

name: Christian church, early
originalDescription: The results of Peter’s preaching on the day of Pentecost were such as to decide the efforts to proclaim the gospel of the kingdom. Peter was the real founder of the Christian church; Paul carried the Christian message to the gentiles, and the Greek believers carried it to the whole Roman Empire. (195) For some time the believers in Jesus were a sect within Judaism. (194:1.5) (194:4.10) the fast triumph Abner and Babylonian church Paul and Hellenic church Peter and the church of Jerusalem rituals evolution failings influences strengths
improvedDescription: The Christian church founded by Peter after Pentecost and developed by Paul's missionary efforts. It evolved from a Jewish sect into an organized religious institution, spreading throughout the Roman Empire by combining Jesus' teachings with Greek philosophy and pagan elements to appeal to gentile audiences.

name: Christianity
originalDescription: evolution problems of Christianity influences strengths weaknesses the future (195:10)
improvedDescription: A religion that developed around the person of Jesus Christ, building on his life and teachings but incorporating Greek philosophy, Persian mysteries, and pagan elements. While it contains more of Jesus' gospel than any other religion, it also includes much that Jesus did not teach and has undergone extensive evolution, institutionalization, and sectarian division.

name: Christmas
originalDescription: Christians celebrate actually on festival day of Mithras. Mithraists Three times a day they worshiped, with special weekly ceremonials on the day of the sun-god and with the most elaborate observance of all on the annual festival of Mithras, December twenty-fifth. (98:5.4) modern peoples continue reveling in the lavish distribution of Christmas gifts, and rich men endow great institutions of philanthropy and learning. This make tham feel great men. (69:5.13) The Maypole, the Christmas tree, and the superstitious practice of rapping on wood perpetuate certain of the ancient customs of tree worship and the later-day tree cults. (85:2.5)
improvedDescription: A Christian celebration held on December 25th, which was originally the annual festival day of Mithras. Modern Christmas gift-giving practices echo ancient customs where people would distribute wealth to gain social recognition and status.

name: chromosomes
originalDescription: On planets such as ours the highest form of life is reproduced by a life-carrying bundle which possesses twenty-four pattern units. On Urantia there are forty-eight units of pattern control—trait determiners—in the sex cells of human reproduction. (36:2.11,16) the staff of the Planetary Prince had chromosomes reorganized by the life circuits of the Satania system (77:2.2,5)
improvedDescription: Hereditary units that carry genetic patterns in living organisms. On Urantia, human sex cells contain 48 units of pattern control, while the highest life forms on planets like ours are reproduced by life-carrying bundles with 24 pattern units. The Planetary Prince's staff had their chromosomes reorganized by Satania's life circuits.

name: chronoldeks
originalDescription: chronoldeks are frandalanks who also measure time in addition to quantitative and qualitative energy (29:4.37) The time of the systems is broadcast by the master chronoldeks. (46:1.2) they are listed between Master Physical Controllers (29:4.11) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.107)
improvedDescription: A specialized type of frandalank that measures time in addition to quantitative and qualitative energy presence. They serve as living time gauges and broadcast the standard time of systems. Master chronoldeks handle the time broadcasting function for their respective systems.

name: churches
originalDescription: the authority of the church was established because the words of the sacred books were considered to be inspired (88:2.10) The evolution of religious philosophy among the Hellenic and Hebrew peoples affords a contrastive illustration of the function of the church as an institution in the shaping of cultural progress. (98:2.7) secret societies evolved into the forerunners of churches. (70:7.19) The true church—the Jesus brotherhood—is invisible, spiritual, and is characterized by unity, not necessarily by uniformity. (195:10.11) church and state ideal church buildings
improvedDescription: Religious institutions that evolved from secret societies and early religious groups. While the true church is the invisible, spiritual brotherhood of believers characterized by unity, visible churches serve as social organizations that should support but not replace the spiritual kingdom of God.

name: Chuza
originalDescription: Chuza was a steward of Herod Antipas, and husband of Juana, one of the twelve evangelist women (150:1.1) (154:0.2)
improvedDescription: A steward of Herod Antipas and husband of Joanna, one of the twelve women evangelists commissioned by Jesus. Chuza informed Herod that Jesus posed no political threat and was only concerned with establishing a spiritual brotherhood, helping to protect Jesus from official interference.

name: Cicero
originalDescription: The famous Roman writer and orator is held up as an example of the maturity to receive Christianity (195:2.6)
improvedDescription: The famous Roman writer and orator who is held up as an example of the intellectual maturity Rome possessed to receive Christianity.

name: Cincinnati Island
originalDescription: 260 million years ago, the region southeast of the Cincinnati Island stayed elevated above water, while much of western Europe, including the British Isles, was submerged under seas. (59:4.8)
improvedDescription: A geographic region that, 260 million years ago during the Devonian period, remained elevated above water while much of western Europe, including the British Isles, was submerged under seas.

name: circles
originalDescription: on Jerusem the nonnative residential areas are called the circles. Each consists of seven concentric and successively elevated circles. They are as follows: (46:4.2) # the circles of the Sons of God, with walls built of gleaming gems and up to 150,000 pearly gates. Magisterial Sons, Trinity Teacher Sons, Melchizedeks, Vorondadeks, Lanonandeks, and Life Carriers can be found here. In the center stands the Michael memorial, where more than a million personalities work today. (46:5.10-19) # the circles of the angels, which are surrounded by the exhibit panorama of Jerusem, 5,000 standard miles in circumference, or 55,000 km in Earth units (46:5.20-24) # the circles of the Universal Aids, where the headquarters of the Evening Stars are, led by Galantia, a building made of monolithic cast crystal (46:5.25) # the circles of the Master Physical Controllers, surrounding the mysterious and immense temple of power (46:5.26) # the circles of the ascending mortals, whose center is occupied by the 619 planetary memorials , with the immense working model of Edentia, 65 km in diameter (46:5.27-29) # the circles of the courtesy colonies, adorned with three enormous structures: the vast astronomic observatory of Jerusem, the gigantic art gallery of Satania, and the immense assembly hall of the reversion directors (46:5.30-31) # the circles of the finaliters, with the incomparable empty and silent temple with the seal of Michael (46:5.32) # Other additional residential circles (46:5.33)
improvedDescription: The seven concentric and successively elevated residential areas on Jerusem for nonnative beings, including circles for the Sons of God, angels, Universe Aids, Master Physical Controllers, ascending mortals, courtesy colonies, and finaliters, each with distinctive architectural features and inhabitants.

name: circuit regulators
originalDescription: Circuit Regulators are the unique beings who co-ordinate physical and spiritual energy and regulate its flow. It requires millions of these regulators to energize even a system of mansion worlds like that of Satania. (48:2.13) This Morontia Power Supervisors are like dynamos who transform everywhere energies of space (48:2.13-14) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.109)
improvedDescription: Unique morontia beings who coordinate physical and spiritual energy and regulate its flow into the segregated channels of the morontia spheres, functioning like living dynamos to transform space energies into materials used by morontia supervisors.

name: Circuit Supervisors
improvedDescription: Universe Circuit Supervisors who oversee and manage the various energy and communication circuits that connect different levels of cosmic administration.

name: circuits
originalDescription: Tracing any circuit, whether it be personality, material gravity, cosmic force, spirit gravity, or mental circuitry, all lead back to the Father's presence on Paradise, his central abode. (11:1.4) angels are sustained directly by the intake of the spiritual energy of the primary circuits of the universe (26:1.17) stationed in the local universes are one hundred Power Centers that modify the seven circuits of power that emanate from the superuniverse headquarters. The centers provide lanes or lines of intensified energy that are much used by the Creator Sons. (29:2.16) | local systems:life circuits God is only discernibly present on Paradise; outside of it, God limits himself to respect the sovereignty of his coordinates. That is why the divine presence must be obtained through a series of presence circuits that are perfectly equivalent to the presence of the Father. (3:1.6) The universal circuits of Paradise do actually pervade the realms of the seven superuniverses. These presence circuits of Paradise are: (15:9.1) # the personality gravity of the Universal Father # the spiritual gravity of the Eternal Son # the mind gravity of the Conjoint Actor # the material gravity of the eternal Isle the master circuits of the grand universe are derived from the personality presences of the Paradise Deities. (34:4.5) reflectivity phenomena seems to be independent of all subabsolute universe circuits (17:3.10) Paradise and the Havona circuits are the ancestor creations of all universe creation (23:1.1) In addition to the universal Paradise circuits there function two energy-circuit divisions or power segregations: (15:9.2-14) * The Superuniverse Circuits: *# The unifying intelligence circuit of one of the Seven Master Spirits *# The reflective-service circuit of the seven Reflective Spirits *# The secret circuits of the Mystery Monitors *# The circuit of the intercommunion of the Eternal Son with his Paradise Sons *# The flash presence of the Infinite Spirit *# The broadcasts of Paradise *# The energy circuits of the power centers * The Local Universe Circuits: *# The bestowal spirits. The Spirit of Truth, the spirit of Michael on Urantia. *# The circuit of the Divine Ministers *# The circuit of the adjutant mind-spirits
improvedDescription: The universal energy and communication pathways that connect all creation back to the Father's presence on Paradise, including personality gravity, spiritual gravity, mind gravity, and material gravity circuits, as well as superuniverse and local universe circuits.

name: circumcision
originalDescription: Ancient rite At Salem In Jesus times
improvedDescription: An ancient religious rite that evolved from early sacrificial practices involving partial body sacrifice, later practiced as an initiation ceremony and adopted by various cultures including the Hebrew peoples.

name: circumspection
improvedDescription: The quality of careful consideration and prudent judgment in one's actions and decisions, often associated with proper conduct and wisdom.

name: circumstances
originalDescription: “We know that all things work together for good to those who love God”. Catastrophic physical events always work out for the welfare and progress of the universes. It may be that the inexplicable circumstances of existence are all interwoven into a meaningful pattern of high value by the Supreme and the Trinity. (4:1.4,11) (10:7.5-6) (48:4.7) (54:4.7) (67:7.8) (67:8.4) (94:6.4) (118:10.18) (182:2.1) seraphim can and do so manipulate planetary conditions and so associate circumstances as favorably to influence the spheres of human activity to which they are attached. (113:3.5) (113:4.4) (114:6.18) Jesus
improvedDescription: The conditions and situations of existence which, though sometimes difficult or mysterious, work together for good according to divine providence, with seraphic beings able to manipulate planetary conditions to favorably influence human activities.

name: citadel of the spirit
originalDescription: there is one inner bastion, the citadel of the spirit, which is absolutely unassailable; at least this is true of every human being who has dedicated the keeping of his soul to the indwelling spirit of the eternal God. (100:2.7)
improvedDescription: The absolutely unassailable inner bastion of the human soul that remains secure when dedicated to the indwelling spirit of the eternal God, providing spiritual security even when all earthly things may crash.

name: cities
originalDescription: Sayings History Management
improvedDescription: Urban settlements that concentrate populations for commerce, manufacturing, and cultural development. Cities multiply the power of their inhabitants for either good or evil, serving as centers where fine arts, scientific progress, and spiritual culture thrive best when supported by surrounding agricultural populations.

name: citizenship
originalDescription: The religious initiation of Roman youths was the occasion of their solemn consecration to the service of the state. Oaths and admissions to citizenship were in reality religious ceremonies. (98:3.4)
improvedDescription: The formal status of membership in a political community or state. In ancient Rome, the religious initiation of youths included their solemn consecration to state service, with oaths and admissions to citizenship being conducted as religious ceremonies.

name: citizenship registrars
originalDescription: From time to time Jerusem citizens present themseleves to the Melchizedek examiners, Bright Evening Stars, and the four and twenty counselors, who certify their morontia, spiritual, and social achievements, and then go to the citizenship registrars, who grant a mota and suffrage status. (45:7.7)
improvedDescription: Officials on Jerusem who compute mota status and assign suffrage qualifications to citizens based on certifications from Melchizedek examiners, Brilliant Evening Stars, and the twenty-four counselors regarding their morontia, spiritual, and social achievements.

name: citron
originalDescription: Jews considered the paradise apple, the citron, the “forbidden fruit.” (162:4.4)
improvedDescription: A citrus fruit considered by Jews to be the "paradise apple" or "forbidden fruit." Pilgrims carried citron branches in their left hands during the Feast of Tabernacles as part of the ceremonial procession to the temple.

name: City of David
improvedDescription: Another name for Bethlehem in Judea, the birthplace of Jesus and the ancestral city of King David.

name: City of Judah
originalDescription: City of Judah was a small village 4 miles west of Jerusalem. There was the home of Elizabeth and Zacharias, John the Baptist's parents (122:2.6-7) (135:0.2)
improvedDescription: A small village located four miles west of Jerusalem in the hills, where Elizabeth and Zacharias lived and where John the Baptist was born. Mary visited Elizabeth there for three weeks before John's birth.

name: civil servants
originalDescription: If the unbeliever can qualify as a superior civil servant, we should seriously question if the consciousness  of the sonship with God is truly enlivening our life of service. (178:1.13)
improvedDescription: Public officials who serve temporal government. Kingdom believers called to civil service should display enhanced citizenship traits through spiritual enlightenment, with their consciousness of sonship with God quickening their entire life of service.

name: civilization
originalDescription: Society is concerned with self-perpetuation, self-maintenance, and self-gratification, but human self-realization is worthy of becoming the immediate goal of many cultural groups. (68:2.3) Biologic evolution and cultural civilization are not necessarily correlated. But in lengthy periods of history evolution and culture become related as cause and effect. (81:5.1) Jesus avoided all entanglements with the economic structure and the political commitments of his day. (120:3.4) the civilization of Urantia is the joint product of the Urantia mortals and the Urantia midwayers, and this is true despite the present differential between the two levels of culture (77:9.8) We cannot judge religion by the status of its accompanying civilization; we had better estimate the real nature of a civilization by the purity and nobility of its religion. (102:8.2) dangers of civilization dependencies of civilization (68:2) (71:4.2-14) (81:6) development goals primitive society retrogressions en Urantia modern civilization future Status of civilizations
improvedDescription: The advanced stage of human social development characterized by organized government, cultural institutions, and technological progress. Civilization depends on the sagacious correlation of material wealth, intellectual greatness, moral worth, social cleverness, and cosmic insight, and represents society's evolution from primitive conditions toward higher levels of cooperative achievement.

name: clams
originalDescription: 310 million years ago, various shell animals thrived in ancient seas, with less reliance on shells for defense than in later ages. Gastropods, including drills, periwinkles, and snails, were common, while bivalve gastropods like mussels, clams, oysters, and scallops have persisted largely unchanged. Brachiopods, with their hinged and notched valve shells, also flourished, resembling their modern forms. (59:2.12)
improvedDescription: Bivalve gastropod mollusks that were present in ancient seas 310 million years ago and have persisted largely unchanged through the intervening millions of years, along with mussels, oysters, and scallops.

name: clans
originalDescription: Andonites’ loyalties were limited to the inmmediate clan (63:4.3) The clans were blood-tie groups within the tribe, and they owed their existence to certain common interests, such as: (70:3.3) (70:4.2-8) (70:5.2) (70:7.1) The clans served a valuable purpose in local self-government, but they greatly delayed the growth of large and strong nations. (70:4.10) During later times many clans grew out of trades and other industrial associations. (71:1.23) Many primitive clans were virtually exterminated by the practice of both abortion and infanticide. (68:6.9) The old idea of friendship meant adoption into the clan (70:3.6) The clan peace chiefs usually ruled through the mother line; the tribal war chiefs established the father line. (70:4.10) In spite of their belief in spirits, primitive Australians still focus their religion upon the clan. (103:3.1) during feudalism of the European Middle Ages the territorial state collapsed, and there was a reversion to the small castle groups, the reappearance of the clan and tribal stages of development. (71:1.24) primitive man fortified his houses, and the clans solidified by mutual fear and by the inculcation of hate for foreign groups. (50:5.5) The larger territorial states had a long and bitter struggle with the smaller consanguineous clan groups. (71:1.23) (134:5.8)
improvedDescription: Blood-tie groups within tribes formed around common interests such as shared ancestry, religious totems, language, dwelling places, enemies, or military experiences. Clans served valuable purposes in local self-government but delayed the growth of large nations, with clan loyalty often conflicting with broader territorial sovereignty.

name: classes, social
improvedDescription: Social classes refer to hierarchical divisions within society based on economic, social, or cultural status.

name: Claudia Procula
originalDescription: Claudia Procula was the wife of Pontius Pilate; became prominently identified with gospel (185:1.7) (185:2.6) she asked Pilate not to arrest Jesus (183:2.3) she sent a message to Pilate regarding Jesus (185:5.7-8)
improvedDescription: Claudia Procula was the wife of Pontius Pilate who became a partial convert to Judaism and later a believer in Jesus' gospel. She asked Pilate not to arrest Jesus and sent him a message warning against harming this 'innocent and just man,' having suffered dreams about Jesus. After Pilate's death, she became prominently identified with spreading the gospel.

name: Claudius
originalDescription: Claudius was a wealthy Roman slaveholder with whom Jesus spoke and after whose conversation he freed 117 of them (132:4.5)
improvedDescription: Claudius was a wealthy Roman slaveholder with whom Jesus spoke, and after their conversation he freed 117 of his slaves.

name: Claudus
originalDescription: Claudo was a discouraged young man to whom Jesus spoke on the island of Malta (130:8.1)
improvedDescription: Claudus was a discouraged young man contemplating suicide whom Jesus encountered on the island of Malta. After their conversation, he became enthusiastic about life, later became a Cynic preacher, and eventually joined Peter in proclaiming Christianity in Rome and Naples before going to Spain to preach the gospel.

name: claws
originalDescription: Magical charms were concocted from a great variety of things, as tiger claws, (88:5.2)
improvedDescription: Tiger claws were among the various items used to create magical charms in ancient times, along with human flesh, crocodile teeth, and other materials believed to possess supernatural powers.

name: clay
originalDescription: The belief in man’s having been created from clay was well-nigh universal in the Eastern Hemisphere; this tradition can be traced from the Philippine Islands around the world to Africa. (74:8.4,7) For writing material early peoples utilized, among other things, clay tablets (66:5.9) Jesus put clay on Josiah’s eyes, the blind beggar (164:3.8) there were sculptures of clay adorning the gardens of Dalamatia (66:5.26) Some ancient Sumerian clay tablets found tell of Dilmun, this earthly paradise “where the Gods first blessed mankind with the example of civilized and cultured life”. (77:4.8)
improvedDescription: Clay was an important material in ancient times, used for writing tablets, pottery, and sculptures. The belief that humans were created from clay was nearly universal in the Eastern Hemisphere, and ancient Sumerian clay tablets describe earthly paradises and civilized life.

name: cleansing the temple
originalDescription: Jesus’ cleansing of the temple was a decisive act against the commercialization of worship and the exploitation of the poor. The temple had become a center of profit through the sale of sacrificial animals and currency exchange, benefiting the priestly families. Amid the noise and chaos of commerce, Jesus drove out the animals, declaring, “My house shall be called a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers.” His action inspired the admiration of his listeners, who, following his example, overturned the tables of the money-changers, but it deeply alarmed the temple authorities. (173:1)
improvedDescription: Jesus' cleansing of the temple was a decisive act against the commercialization of worship, where he drove out animals and overturned money-changers' tables, declaring the temple should be a house of prayer rather than a den of robbers. This action challenged the profitable business that had grown around temple sacrifices and currency exchange.

name: Clement
originalDescription: Clement was a prominent Christian teacher in Alexandria, a disciple of Pantaenus or Panteno. Clement had Origen as a disciple (195:3.10)
improvedDescription: Clement was a prominent Christian teacher in Alexandria who was taught by Pantaenus and later taught Origen, helping to preserve Jesus' teachings in the early Christian schools.

name: Cleopas of Emmaus
originalDescription: at Jesus’ 7th appearance (190:5)
improvedDescription: Cleopas was a shepherd from Emmaus and partial believer in Jesus who, along with his brother Jacob, encountered the resurrected Jesus during his seventh appearance while walking on the road from Jerusalem.

name: cleverness
originalDescription: Overpowering arguments and mental superiority are not to be employed to coerce men and women into the kingdom. (159:3.2) Cleverness is not a substitute for true character. (48:7.3)
improvedDescription: Cleverness refers to mental skill or ingenuity, but it is not a substitute for true character and should not be used to coerce people into spiritual beliefs through overpowering arguments or mental superiority.

name: climate
originalDescription: The lower five or six miles of the earth’s atmosphere is the troposphere; this is the region of winds and air currents which provide weather phenomena. (58:2.6) changes in climate have caused changes in civilization: * From 2500 to 2000 B.C. climatic changes managed to do what the barbarians failed to do: bring about the ruin of Mesopotamia. (78:8.11) * The Saharans saw their prosperous and peaceful civilization dispersed due to the upthrust of land and the shifting water-laden winds (80:1.4) * The early expansion of the violet race into Europe was cut short by certain rather sudden climatic and geologic changes. Winds from the west shifted to the north, gradually turning the great open pasture regions of Sahara into a barren desert. (80:2.1) * 15,000 years ago, Alpine forests were spreading extensively. These great climatic modifications forced the races of Europe that practiced hunting to become herders. (80:3.8) * By 3000 B.C. increasing aridity was driving Andonites back into Turkestan. (80:9.6) chief factors causing fluctuations are land elevation, cosmic clouds, and oceans (57:8.22) climate is a decisive factor in early civilization (81:1.1-5) (81:6.3-4) the Permian period was characterized by its harsh continental climate (59:6) (60:0.1) Jesus in a parable used the idea of discerning the weather: “How is it that you so well know how to discern the face of the heavens but are so utterly unable to discern the signs of the times?” (157:2.1)
improvedDescription: The long-term patterns of temperature, precipitation, and atmospheric conditions that influence weather phenomena and significantly impact the development and migration of civilizations. Climate is primarily influenced by land elevation, cosmic clouds, and oceanic conditions, and has been a decisive factor in human evolution and cultural advancement throughout history.

name: Cloa
originalDescription: Mary, the mother of Jesus, was a descendant of Cloa (122:1.2)
improvedDescription: One of the notable ancestral women in Mary's lineage, the mother of Jesus, who was descended from a long line of remarkable women including Cloa among others such as Annon, Tamar, Ruth, and Eve.

name: cloak
originalDescription: Jesus wore the cloak, a typical Jewish clothing (187:2.8) When Jesus instructed his apostles that they should, when one unjustly took away the coat, offer the other garment, he referred not so much to a literal second coat as to the idea of doing something positive to save the wrongdoer in the place of the olden advice “an eye for an eye”. (159:5.11)
improvedDescription: An outer garment worn in ancient times, including by Jesus, whose cloak was taken by Roman soldiers during his crucifixion. Jesus used the concept metaphorically when teaching that offering one's cloak when another garment is taken represents doing something positive to help the wrongdoer rather than seeking retaliation.

name: clock
originalDescription: The radium clock is your most reliable timepiece for making scientific estimates of the age of the planet (57:7.3)
improvedDescription: A timepiece, specifically the radium clock which is the most reliable scientific instrument for estimating the age of planets through the study of radioactive elements, though such estimates tend to be conservative since they only measure surface materials.

name: Clopas
originalDescription: Clopas was the husband of Mary, sister of Jesus’ mother (187:3.2)
improvedDescription: The husband of Mary, who was the sister of Jesus' mother and among those who stood near the cross during the crucifixion.

name: close calls
originalDescription: Andon and Fonta nearly lost their lives (62:5.6) our prehuman ancestors were on the brink of extinction several times. The ancestral frog, the mother of the dawn-mammals, and the mother of the Primates twins, who suffered the closest call caused by lightning, almost died several times (62:3.9)
improvedDescription: Near-death experiences that almost resulted in extinction, particularly referring to the numerous occasions when prehuman ancestors of humanity barely escaped death, including incidents involving Andon and Fonta as children and earlier evolutionary ancestors who survived lightning strikes and other dangers by mere margins.

name: clothing
originalDescription: Andon and Fonta use of animal skins for warmth (63:2.5) Caligastia one hundred taught treating skins for use as clothing, and weaving was later introduced by the teachers of art and science. (66:5.2) the seventy evangelists were sent out without extra clothing (163:1.3) Jesus’ clothing was divided by soldiers before crucifixion (187:2.2,8-9) “Be reminded that a wise tailor does not sew a piece of new and unshrunk cloth upon an old garment, lest, when it is wet, it shrink and produce a worse rent”. (147:7.2)
improvedDescription: Garments and coverings for the human body, ranging from early animal skins used by Andon and Fonta for warmth to woven materials taught by the Caligastia staff. Jesus' clothing was divided among Roman soldiers at his crucifixion, and he used clothing metaphorically in teachings about not mixing old and new approaches.

name: clouds
originalDescription: cosmic clouds can affect climate (57:8.22) Clouds, rain, and hail have all been feared and worshiped by numerous primitive tribes and by many of the early nature cults. (85:4.3) Neanderthalers were deathly afraid of clouds, more especially of mists and fogs. (64:4.12)
improvedDescription: Atmospheric formations of water vapor that, along with land elevation and oceanic influences, are major factors in climate fluctuation. Primitive peoples often feared and worshiped clouds, rain, and hail, with the Neanderthalers being particularly afraid of mists and fogs.

name: clubs
originalDescription: Jesus was member of young men’s club for philosophic discussion which met at the homes of different members and often at his own home. (124:1.13) (127:3.9)
improvedDescription: Social organizations for fellowship and learning, including the young men's philosophical discussion club that Jesus joined in Nazareth, which met at members' homes and provided a venue for intellectual discourse and community engagement.

name: co-operation
originalDescription: One of the most important lessons to be learned during your mortal career is teamwork. (28:5.14) learning steps co-operation and civilization
improvedDescription: The ability to work together effectively with others, which is one of the most important lessons learned during mortal existence. Cooperation becomes increasingly valuable as civilizations advance, eventually proving more efficient than competition in promoting social and spiritual progress.

name: Co-ordinate Creator Sons
improvedDescription: An order of divine beings related to Creator Sons who serve in universe creation and administration.

name: Co-ordinate Ministering Daughters
improvedDescription: An order of divine beings related to local universe Mother Spirits who serve in universe ministry and administration.

name: Co-ordinate Trinity-origin Beings
improvedDescription: An order of divine beings who originate from the Paradise Trinity and serve coordinate functions in universe administration.

name: co-ordinators and liaison directors
originalDescription: Co-ordinators and Liaison Directors are Morontia Companions. They are the instructors of social conduct and morontia progress, sponsoring classes and other group activities among the ascending mortals. (48:3.11)
improvedDescription: Morontia Companions who serve as instructors of social conduct and morontia progress, facilitating intercourse among ascending mortals and preventing confusion through classes and group activities.

name: coabsolute level
improvedDescription: A level of reality that exists between the absolute and finite levels in the cosmic hierarchy of existence.

name: coal
originalDescription: Coal layers often hold both gas and oil. (59:5.16) The coal layers alternate with shale, stone, and conglomerate. (59:5.15) In some parts of North America and Europe the coal-bearing strata are 18,000 feet in thickness. (59:5.15) Coal is the water-preserved and pressure-modified remains of the rank vegetation growing in the bogs and on the swamp shores of this faraway age. (59:5.14-17) deposition (60:2.7) (60:3.9) (61:2.3)
improvedDescription: The water-preserved and pressure-modified remains of ancient vegetation that grew in bogs and swamps, often containing gas and oil, with deposits varying greatly in thickness across different regions.

name: coat
improvedDescription: An outer garment worn for warmth or protection.

name: cock
improvedDescription: A male bird, particularly a male chicken or rooster.

name: cockroaches
originalDescription: Around 210 million years ago insects first appeared, including cockroaches. A thousand species of cockroaches developed, some reaching lengths of ten centimeters. (59:5.7)
improvedDescription: Insects that first appeared around 210 million years ago, with a thousand species developing during ancient times, some reaching lengths of four inches.

name: cocoa
originalDescription: among earliest pharmaceutical discoveries is cocoa (90:4.8)
improvedDescription: One of the earliest pharmaceutical discoveries, valued along with quinine for its medicinal properties by ancient peoples.

name: coercion
originalDescription: The domination of the Spirit is never tainted with coercion nor compromised by compulsion. (34:6.11) The evolution of religious observances progressed from placation, avoidance, exorcism, coercion, conciliation, and propitiation to sacrifice, atonement, and redemption. (90:0.1) Primitive man believed that something special must be done to win the favor of the gods. The rituals of avoidance, exorcism, coercion, and propitiation all merge into one another. (89:0.2)
improvedDescription: The use of force or compulsion to achieve compliance, particularly in religious or spiritual matters. In primitive religious evolution, coercion was used as a technique to control spirits and win divine favor, progressing from early rituals of placation and exorcism. True spiritual guidance, however, is never achieved through coercion but through willing cooperation and divine leading.

name: coinage
originalDescription: a well-known ruling of the Sanhedrin, made for the guidance of the Jews dispersed among the gentile nations, that the “right of coinage carried with it the right to levy taxes”.  (174:2.5)
improvedDescription: The right to mint money, which according to a well-known Sanhedrin ruling carried with it the right to levy taxes. This principle was used to guide Jews living among gentile nations and was referenced in Jesus's teaching about rendering unto Caesar what is Caesar's.

name: coins
originalDescription: Even in modern times superstitious persons make holes in coins. (85:1.4)
improvedDescription: Metal currency pieces that have been objects of superstitious veneration throughout history. Even in modern times, superstitious people make holes in coins, similar to ancient practices where porous stones were believed to have healing properties.

name: cold
originalDescription: The cold and hunger of the open lands stimulate action, invention, and resourcefulness. (64:1.3) Gravity and absence of heat (cold) organize and hold matter together; heat and antigravity disrupt matter and dissipate energy. Cold merely signifies absence of heat—comparative energy rest—the status of the universal force-charge of space. Gravity presence and action is what prevents the appearance of the theoretical absolute zero, for interstellar space does not have the temperature of absolute zero.  (15:8.10) (42:4.5-9)
improvedDescription: The absence of heat, representing comparative energy rest and the status of the universal force-charge of space. Cold stimulates human action, invention, and resourcefulness in open lands, while in cosmic terms it works with gravity to organize matter together, contrasting with heat which disrupts matter and dissipates energy.

name: collapse
originalDescription: If God should retire as the present upholder of all creation, there would immediately occur a universal collapse. (4:1.6)
improvedDescription: The complete breakdown or failure of structure or function. If God were to retire as the upholder of all creation, a universal collapse would immediately occur, demonstrating the continuous divine support required for all reality to exist.

name: college of ethics, law, and Supremacy
originalDescription: one of the steps in Technical Advisers training is into the “college of the ethics of law and the technique of Supremacy,” the Paradise training school for the perfecting of Technical Advisers. (25:4.14)
improvedDescription: A Paradise training school for perfecting Technical Advisers, where they learn the ethics of law and the technique of Supremacy. This represents the final stage of education for Technical Advisers after completing training in local universe Melchizedek colleges and superuniverse courts.

name: College of High Ethics
originalDescription: The highest course of training in universe administration is given by the Melchizedeks on their home world. This College of High Ethics is presided over by the original Father Melchizedek. (35:3.22)
improvedDescription: The highest educational institution for universe administration, located on the Melchizedek home world and presided over by the original Father Melchizedek. This college attracts students and observers from throughout the local universes, with Nebadon's schools being particularly renowned for their practical experience with administrative challenges.

name: college of life planning
originalDescription: the college of life planning is located in the World Number One in Jerusem, the world of Life Carriers (36:2.10)
improvedDescription: An educational institution located on World Number One in Jerusem, dedicated to studying universal life in all its known phases. This college functions with teachers and advisers from Uversa, Havona, and Paradise, and houses the seven central emplacements of the adjutant mind-spirits.

name: college of revealed religion
improvedDescription: An educational institution that was part of the planned curriculum for the Caligastia one hundred, designed to teach revealed religious truth and spiritual principles.

name: college of self-evaluation
originalDescription: The Melchizedek Sons conduct upward of thirty different educational centers on Jerusem. These training schools begin with the college of self-evaluation and end with the schools of Jerusem citizenship (45:7.3)
improvedDescription: The first of more than thirty educational centers conducted by Melchizedek Sons on Jerusem. This college begins the comprehensive training program for mortal survivors that culminates in schools of Jerusem citizenship, preparing them for the responsibilities of representative government.

name: College of Wisdom
originalDescription: The Teacher Sons conduct an agelong course of training, ranging from the planetary courses up to the high College of Wisdom located on Salvington. (20:8.3)
improvedDescription: The highest educational institution for Teacher Sons located on Salvington, serving as the pinnacle of an extensive training program that ranges from planetary courses to this advanced college where recognition is granted to all who complete these adventures in wisdom and truth.

name: collisions
originalDescription: In those regions of thicker clustering, collisions are not uncommon. When physical systems become stabilized, become physically settled, and are swung into the balanced and established circuits, no more collisions or other devastating catastrophes will occur (15:5.12) (15:8.6) In the collisional episodes enormous masses of matter are suddenly converted into the rarest form of energy (15:8.6) Collisions among the giants of space are rare indeed, but gravity-tidal explosions of lesser bodies are quite common. (57:6.4)
improvedDescription: Astronomical events where space bodies crash into each other, common in regions of thicker clustering. These collisions can convert enormous masses of matter into rare forms of energy and often provide material for planetary formation, though they become rare once physical systems stabilize into balanced circuits.

name: colonization
originalDescription: The practice of sacrifice of first-born sons was replaced by exile; this was the origin of colonization. The Romans adhered to this custom in their scheme of colonization. (89:7.3)
improvedDescription: A practice that originated from the ancient custom of exiling first-born sons instead of sacrificing them, allowing them to grow up before being sent away to establish new settlements. The Romans incorporated this custom into their systematic approach to colonization.

name: color workers
originalDescription: color workers are the artists among the heavenly reproducers, a group inside celestial artisans (44:2.4)
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans who work with light and shade, serving as sketchers and painters who preserve passing scenes and transient episodes for future morontia enjoyment.

name: Colorado
originalDescription: The Morrison beds of Colorado, Montana, and Wyoming contain abundant fresh-water fossils. (60:2.5) Pikes Peak and Longs Peak are examples of mountains that were re-elevated after erosion. (60:4.4) The Florissant fossil beds of Colorado belong to the later years of these far-distant times. (61:2.4)
improvedDescription: A state in North America known for its Morrison fossil beds containing abundant fresh-water fossils, mountain peaks like Pikes Peak and Longs Peak that exemplify re-elevation after erosion, and the Florissant fossil beds containing ancient insect remains.

name: Colorado River
originalDescription: The fossiliferous rocks around the Grand Canyon of the Colorado River testify to ancient uplifts and surface fluctuations. (58:7.5)
improvedDescription: A major river in North America whose Grand Canyon contains fossiliferous rocks in multiple layers that provide clear evidence of ancient geological upheavals and surface fluctuations from primitive times.

name: colored races
improvedDescription: The six evolutionary races of humanity that emerged following the Sangik mutation, distinguished by their distinctive skin pigmentation and representing different branches of human development on Urantia.

name: colors
originalDescription: there are seven colors in the natural spectrum. (42:9.3) celestial musicians make symphonies of color, that ranks among the highest accomplishments of the celestial musicians. (44:1.6)
improvedDescription: There are seven colors in the natural spectrum, reflecting the sevenfold nature of creation. Celestial musicians create symphonies of color using morontia color tones, which rank among their highest artistic accomplishments.

name: coma
originalDescription: The ancients believed that the soul could leave the body in various ways, as in coma (86:5.4-8) The Jews were in the habit of burying their dead on the day of their demise; this was a necessary practice in such a warm climate. It often happened that they put in the tomb one who was merely comatose, so that on the second or even the third day, such a one would come forth from the tomb (167:4.3) The daughter of Jairus of Capernaum, who was believed to have resurrected Jesus, was actually in a coma after a prolonged fever (152:1.2) the son of the widow of Nain was another case of supposed healing of Jesus in which the child was not really dead, but in a coma (146:6.2-3)
improvedDescription: A state of unconsciousness associated with disease and accidents that ancients believed allowed the soul to leave the body. Several individuals thought to be dead by Jesus' followers were actually in comatose states, including Jairus's daughter and the widow's son at Nain.

name: combined controllers
originalDescription: Combined Controllers are highly mechanical Morontia Power Supervisors; one of them is stationed on each morontia world (48:2.19) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.112)
improvedDescription: Highly mechanical Morontia Power Supervisors stationed at the center of each administrative unit of morontia worlds. These beings are sensitive to physical, spiritual, and morontial energies and work with associated system coordinators, circuit regulators, and other specialized personnel.

name: comets
originalDescription: Volcanoes, but not mountains, became fetishes; comets, but not stars. (88:1.1) Many comets are unestablished wild offspring of the solar mother wheels, which are being gradually brought under control of the central governing sun. (15:6.13) The meteors and other small particles of matter circulating and evolving in space constitute an enormous aggregate of energy and material substance. (15:6.12) Even some of the comets consist of meteor swarms, but as a rule they are disrupted smaller bodies of matter. (49:3.2) A comet’s tail points away from the attracting body or sun because of the electrical reaction of its highly expanded gases and because of the actual pressure of light and other energies emanating from the sun. (15:6.13)
improvedDescription: Celestial bodies that are often unestablished offspring of solar systems being gradually brought under control of central governing suns. Comets have tails that point away from attracting bodies due to electrical reactions of their expanded gases and pressure from light and solar energies. Some consist of meteor swarms, while others are disrupted smaller bodies of matter.

name: Comforter
improvedDescription: A term referring to the Spirit of Truth, the divine presence promised to provide comfort and guidance to believers.

name: commandments
originalDescription: Dalamatia Eden Machiventa Melchisedek Moses Gautama The great commandments Jews Jesus Commandments for angels
improvedDescription: Divine injunctions and moral laws given throughout history, including the seven commandments of Dalamatia and Eden, Melchizedek's seven commands, Moses' ten commandments, and Jesus' supreme commandment to love God and one's neighbor. These represent progressive revelations of divine will, culminating in the universal mandate to be perfect as the Father in heaven is perfect.

name: commendation
improvedDescription: The act of expressing approval or praise, particularly in recognizing worthy achievements or character.

name: commerce
originalDescription: evolution of commerce history Jesus and commerce
improvedDescription: The evolution of trade from early barter systems to organized commercial exchange. Commerce has been a great civilizer, promoting cross-fertilization of cultures and contributing to the advancement of civilization through international trade relationships and the exchange of goods, services, and ideas.

name: commission on industry and trade
improvedDescription: One of the ten councils established by the Caligastia one hundred in Dalamatia, led by Nod, which fostered primitive industry within tribes and promoted trade between peace groups, contributing to elevated standards of living and the development of early commercial credit systems.

name: common sense
originalDescription: all Jesus’ plans were characterized by a sanctified common sense (100:7.3)
improvedDescription: Practical wisdom and sound judgment. Jesus exemplified sanctified common sense in all his plans and ministry, being reasonable, practical, and free from erratic or eccentric tendencies.

name: communication
originalDescription: The higher forms of intelligent intercommunication between human beings are greatly helped by the indwelling Adjusters. (109:4.1-3) Knowledge and wisdom become cumulative because of man’s ability to communicate these possessions to succeeding generations. (81:6.23,25) (160:2.3) heavenly communication planetary communications
improvedDescription: The process of sharing meanings and ideas between intelligent beings. Higher forms of human communication are enhanced by indwelling Thought Adjusters. The ability to communicate enables the accumulation of knowledge and wisdom across generations, forming the foundation of human culture and civilization.

name: communion (mass)
improvedDescription: A religious ceremony or sacrament, related to the Christian practice of communion or mass, commemorating spiritual fellowship with the divine.

name: communion (spiritual)
improvedDescription: The intimate fellowship and communication between the soul and divine spirit, representing the highest form of personal religious experience and connection with God.

name: communism
originalDescription: Modernized co-ordination and fraternal regulation will be productive of longer-lived co-operation than will the older and more primitive methods of communism or dictatorial regulative institutions based on force. (81:6.33) The present social order is not necessarily right but we must not be persuaded to experiment with the discarded formulas of our forefathers. We must go forward, not backward (69:9.18) The nations of Urantia have already entered upon the gigantic struggle between nationalistic militarism and industrialism (70:2.11) primitive communism weaknesses of communism
improvedDescription: A primitive social system characterized by communal property and automatic economic adjustment. While preventing extreme poverty and want in early societies, communism ultimately gave way to higher social orders because it discouraged individual initiative, stifled ambition, and conflicted with human desires for family inheritance, religious expression, personal liberty, and security.

name: comparisons
originalDescription: There is the same relative distance, in comparison with actual size, between the atomic nucleus and the inner electronic circuit as exists between the inner planet, Mercury, and your sun. (42:7.2) If the mass of an electron were 2.8 grams, the volume of that electron would be as large as that of the Earth. If the volume of a proton were like the head of a pin, the head of a pin would have a diameter equal to that of the Earth's orbit around the Sun. (42:6.8) stars have just as much comparative elbow room in space as one dozen oranges would have if they were circulating about throughout the interior of Urantia, and were the planet a hollow globe. (41:3.2) Some of the reddish have acquired a density at their center that one cubic inch of such a star, if on Urantia, would weigh six thousand pounds. (41:3.6)
improvedDescription: Analogies used to illustrate cosmic proportions and relationships. Examples include the comparison of atomic structure to the solar system, where electrons orbit the nucleus with similar relative spacing to planets around the sun, and the vast spaces between stars being likened to oranges circulating within a hollow Earth.

name: compass
originalDescription: The four points of the compass are universal and inherent in the life of Nebadon. (34:4.13) the compass needle is responsive to solar influence since it turns slightly to the east as the sun rises and slightly to the west as the sun nears setting. (58:2.9)
improvedDescription: A directional instrument responsive to magnetic forces and solar influences. The compass needle points north-south due to universal directional currents inherent in Nebadon, and shows slight daily variations toward east at sunrise and west at sunset, with these movements being twice as pronounced during sunspot cycles.

name: compensations
originalDescription: The Adjuster is our compensatory equalization of the enormous universe tension which is created by the distance of man’s removal from God. (107:0.5) there are many compensations for rebellions as Lucifer's: one is enhanced careers of those mortal ascenders, the Jerusem citizens, who, by withstanding the sophistries of sin, placed themselves in line for becoming future Mighty Messengers (50:7.1) (54:6.5) Although the Universal Father is personally present in the residential universe, he does not indwell the minds of the beings originating in that universe (32:3.4) There are, of course, certain compensations for tribulation, such as Michael’s bestowal on Urantia. (65:5.4) gains of higher loyalty offset earlier tribulation (35:9.8) Gods take note of man’s needs at every stage (40:5.11) It will undoubtedly be the function of the evolutionary citizens of the grand universe to compensate the outer-spacers for this deprivation of the growth of Supremacy. (31:10.14) (117:2.6) Nevertheless, there is a certain, inevitable penalty attaching to mortal existence on such advanced evolutionary planets. (55:3.11) In the time-evolving universes of space there is greater divinity attenuation, more difficult problems to be solved, and larger opportunity to acquire experience in their solution. (116:6.7) Nebadon’s troubles made it an educational clinic for other near-by creations (35:3.22) isolation of the spheres affords their races a unique opportunity for the exercise of faith and for the development of a peculiar quality of confidence in cosmic reliability (50:7.1) Ascending mortals, in their long and loving association with the Havona natives, do much to compensate for the spiritual impoverishment of the earlier stages of mortal progression. (19:6.2) Son and Spirit fusion do, superficially, resemble compensations of unexplained failures in some detail of the Paradise-attainment plan, but is not true. All these happenings unfold in obedience to the established laws (40:10.3-4) Spirit-fused mortals attain unsurpassed wisdom regarding local universe (37:5.11) The evolving Supreme will eventually compensate finite creatures for their inability ever to achieve more than limited experience contact with the universe of universes. (117:5.14) those personalities who are farthest from God, and who can therefore experience the greatest degree of spiritual ascent in the universe (116:4.11) universe unfailingly applies equalizing techniques designed to insure that no ascender shall be deprived of aught which is essential to his ascension experience. (55:2.12)
improvedDescription: Divine adjustments and equalizing techniques that ensure no being is deprived of essential experiences or unfairly disadvantaged in their spiritual journey. These include the Thought Adjuster's role in bridging the distance between mortals and God, enhanced opportunities for growth through trials, and the universe's provision of alternative paths and experiences for spiritual development.

name: competition
originalDescription: angels make effective use of competitive spirit to promote a wholesome rivalry and inspire a quality of service (28:5.18) In advanced civilizations co-operation is more efficient than competition. (71:5.3) Improved tools, ingenious and efficient machines, determine the survival of contending groups in the arena of advancing civilization. (81:6.20) Competition is healthfully stimulated by diversification of races. (64:6.33) In the most continental nation of a neighboring planet, there is keen competition, but not only in industry but also in games, skills, science and letters. (72:4.4) (72:5.10) time and circumstance select the fitter for survival (68:4.7) in schools of Planetary Princes exists a wholesome rivalry among the races of men to enter them (50:4.9) benefits and downsides of competition
improvedDescription: A driving force essential for early evolutionary progress but gradually displaced by cooperation as civilizations advance. While competition stimulates development and determines survival of the fittest groups and individuals, it becomes increasingly wasteful and destructive unless regulated, ultimately requiring transformation into service-motivated cooperation for sustained progress.

name: complaining
originalDescription: we must learn to accept challenges without complaint and to face difficulties and uncertainties without fear (48:6.35) man should not bemoan his lowly origin when Creator Sons pass through same experience to be considered worthy and competent to rule over their universe (21:4.6) Man should not blame God for those afflictions which are the natural result of the life which he chooses to live; neither should man complain of those experiences which are a part of life as it is lived on this world. (148:5.3) Prayer has turned many an irritable and complaining invalid into a paragon of patience and made him an inspiration to all other human sufferers. (91:6.2) Jesus and complaints
improvedDescription: An attitude of dissatisfaction that should be overcome through spiritual growth and acceptance of life's challenges. True spiritual development involves accepting difficulties without complaint, recognizing that many afflictions result from personal choices, and finding strength through faith rather than expressing grievances about circumstances.

name: complement of being
originalDescription: the complemnt of being is an angel of opposite type that is associated to each angel (38:4.3) sanobim is the complement of being to cherubim. They can work alone but usually do their tasks in pairs. (38:7.2) When a seraphic pair accept guardian assignment, they serve for the remainder of the life of that human being. The complement of being, one of the two angels, becomes the recorder of the undertaking. (113:2.9)
improvedDescription: The paired angelic associate of opposite energy type that usually works together with another angel. In seraphic pairs, one angel serves as the complement of being to the other, with cherubim and sanobim being inherently associated as positive and negative energy personalities who function most effectively together.

name: complemental relationships
originalDescription: Creator Sons and local universe Mother Spirits work in a complemental way in universe creation and administration (17:6.4) The experiential nature of the evolutionary type of personality is the natural cosmic complement of the ever-perfect natures of the Paradise-Havona creatures. (32:3.12) Complemental relationships of angles may be transient; they are not necessarily of a permanent nature. Seraphim can work singly as discrete and localized personalities, but they are able to encircuit only when polarized as liaison pairs. (26:1.16)
improvedDescription: Functional associations between different types of beings that enhance their effectiveness and provide cosmic balance. These relationships, such as between Creator Sons and Mother Spirits or between evolutionary and perfect creatures, may be transient or permanent and represent the natural pairing of complementary natures in universe administration.

name: complements of rest
originalDescription: the majestic Complements of Rest enter attainment rest with ascending pilgrims (26:11.7) The superaphic complements of rest are not so much concerned with training as with promoting understanding association with diverse groups in Havona. (26:11.1-3)
improvedDescription: Specialized supernaphim who assist ascending mortals during their final transition rest on Havona's inner circuit. These majestic beings focus on promoting understanding and association among diverse groups rather than formal training, preparing pilgrims for their eventual Paradise residence through experiential education.

name: completion seraphim
originalDescription: To climb to the supernal heights of finaliter sonship with God is a masterly achievement for an angel, an accomplishment far transcending your attainment of eternal survival (40:1.2) After attainment of the Father of spirits and admission to the seraphic service of completion, angels are sometimes assigned to the ministry of worlds settled in light and life. Large numbers of the completion seraphim return to their native universes, there to complement the ministry of divine endowment by the ministry of experiential perfection. (39:9.1-2) Many fascinating avenues of ministry are open to the completion seraphim, but just as they all craved assignment as destiny guardians in the pre-Paradise days, so in the post-Paradise experience they most desire to serve as bestowal attendants of the incarnated Paradise Sons. (39:9.3) often direct seraphic organizations (39:2.1,5)
improvedDescription: Angels who have attained Paradise, been admitted to the seraphic service of completion, and achieved experiential perfection. These highly advanced seraphim serve in exalted capacities throughout the universes, often returning to their native realms to complement divine ministry with their experiential wisdom, with many seeking assignment as bestowal attendants to incarnated Paradise Sons.

name: compossibility and omnipotence
originalDescription: The omnipotence of the Deity does not imply that anything, even meaningless to the Deity, exists. Compatibility is innate in divine power. Omnipotence is the origin not only of nature but of the nature of things, of the original pattern which everything created is then destined to follow. (118:5.1-3)
improvedDescription: A philosophical concept explaining that divine omnipotence includes inherent compatibility and logical consistency. God's omnipotence means the power to create things with specific natures and to establish the fundamental nature of all things, but does not include the ability to create logical contradictions or meaningless entities.

name: compound manipulators
originalDescription: Compound Manipulators are energy manipulator that belong to Celestial Artisans. These are the keen personalities who are in reality seeking to discover the universe presence of God the Supreme (44:5.5)
improvedDescription: A specialized group within the Celestial Artisans who work with the functional association of the three original phases of divine energy: physical, mindal, and spiritual energies. These adventurous beings seek to discover the universe presence of God the Supreme through their manipulative work with combined energy forms.

name: compromise
originalDescription: man must become adept in the art of a continuous human temporal compromise while he yields spiritual allegiance to but one master (109:5.4) the survival character of a soul is not fostered by attempting to secure peace of mind at any price, by the surrender of noble aspirations, and by the compromise of spiritual ideals (133:7.12) Jesus the disciples
improvedDescription: The practice of making temporal adjustments between competing loyalties or principles while maintaining spiritual integrity. Jesus taught that while humans must become adept at continuous temporal compromise in earthly matters, they should yield spiritual allegiance to only one master and never compromise spiritual ideals or noble aspirations.

name: compulsion
originalDescription: the purpose of the true believer is always to act but never to coerce. (94:6.6) The chief factor in early civilization was the force exerted by wise social masters (81:6.14) The domination of the Spirit is never tainted with coercion nor compromised by compulsion. (34:6.11) Never should a righteous cause be promoted by force; spiritual victories can be won only by spiritual power. (16:7.8) (152:6.3) (159:3.2) (178:1.2-3,6,12) Gradually the pleasure-seeking weaklings are subjugated by the more strong and truth-loving elements of the advancing civilization. (50:5.6) Jesus
improvedDescription: The use of force or coercion to achieve ends, which is contrary to spiritual methods. Jesus taught that righteous causes should never be promoted by force, as spiritual victories can only be won by spiritual power, and the Spirit's domination is never tainted with coercion or compromised by compulsion.

name: concentration
originalDescription: Jesus teach: The heathen are not without excuse when they rage at us. Because their outlook is small and narrow, they are able to concentrate their energies enthusiastically.  (155:1.3) Jesus possessed the ability effectively to mobilize all his powers of mind, soul, and body on the task immediately in hand. He could concentrate his deep-thinking mind on the one problem which he wished to solve, and this, in connection with his untiring patience, enabled him serenely to endure the trials (127:3.15)
improvedDescription: The ability to focus one's mental, spiritual, and physical energies on a specific task or objective. Jesus possessed exceptional concentration abilities, being able to mobilize all his powers of mind, soul, and body on immediate tasks and maintain deep focus on problems he wished to solve.

name: concentric circles, three
originalDescription: the banner of Michael, the material emblem of the Trinity government of all creation, is the three azure blue concentric circles on a white background. (53:5.4) (93:2.5) the three circles are emblematic of the infinity, eternity, and universality of the Paradise Trinity (93:3.3) Melchizedek wore it on his chest as an emblem (93:2.5) (104:1.3) the triads that grew up in many religions all were reminiscent of the trinity teachings of the Andites and the Sumerians and based on the belief of the Salemites in Melchizedek’s insignia of the three circles. (95:1.4)
improvedDescription: The symbol of three azure blue concentric circles on a white background, representing the material emblem of Trinity government and used as the banner of Michael. These circles are emblematic of the infinity, eternity, and universality of the Paradise Trinity, and were worn by Melchizedek as his insignia during his bestowal mission.

name: Concept recorders
originalDescription: Concept recorders are part of Celestial Artisans and are concerned with the preservation of concept pictures, idea patterns. (44:4.5)
improvedDescription: A specialized group within the Celestial Artisans responsible for preserving concept pictures and idea patterns through permanent recording methods unknown on material realms. These recorders can capture and store conceptual information in ways far more efficient than ordinary written language.

name: concepts
originalDescription: Beyond the Supreme, concepts are increasingly names; less and less are they true designations of reality; more and more do they become the creature’s projection of finite understanding toward the superfinite. (115:3.4) If mind cannot fathom conclusions, if it cannot penetrate to true origins, then will such mind unfailingly postulate conclusions and invent origins that it may have a means of logical thought within the frame of these mind-created postulates. But concepts are only relatively true. God is much, much more (115:1.1-2) All static, dead, concepts are potentially evil. The finite shadow of relative and living truth is continually moving. Static concepts invariably retard science, politics, society, and religion. (130:4.15) when truth becomes linked with fact, then both time and space condition its meanings and correlate its values. Such realities of truth wedded to fact become concepts and are accordingly relegated to the domain of relative cosmic realities. (118:3.3) Mind is unifying of all divergencies, but in the very absence of such divergencies, mind finds no basis upon which to attempt to formulate understanding concepts. (115:3.2) Urantia Book limitations
improvedDescription: Mental frameworks and understanding patterns that serve as scaffolding for creature thought, though they are always relatively true rather than absolute. Static concepts can become potentially evil by retarding progress, while living concepts remain dynamic and adaptable to expanding cosmic comprehension.

name: conciliating commissions
originalDescription: conciliating commissions are the traveling courts for minor misunderstanding (37:8.6) (39:4.5) Conciliators do not pass upon matters of eternal import; the soul, the eternal prospects of a creature of time, is never placed in jeopardy by their acts. (25:2.12) If the contention is honest, if the difficulties arise out of sincere differences of opinion and honest diversity of viewpoints, no matter how few persons may be involved, no matter how apparently trivial the misunderstanding, a conciliating commission can always be had to pass upon the merits of the controversy. (25:3.8) The members of a conciliating commission are eternally associated as the embodiment of the supreme justice of time and space. (25:3.17) conciliating commissions are “fourth creatures” and discern spirit and matter (38:7.6) (44:0.17) they function upon petition of opposing parties (25:3.3) commissioners, from stage to stage they are evolving from arbiters of differences to explainers of mysteries—judges evolving into interpretative teachers. (25:3.12) Conciliators maintain group headquarters on the capital of their superuniverse, as well as each mansion world (25:3.1) (47:0.4) justice guides commonly appear before conciliating commisions (39:4.5) In each superuniverse the Universal Conciliators find themselves strangely and innately segregated into groups of four, associations in which they continue to serve. (25:2.5-9) The conciliators are of great value in keeping the universe of universes running smoothly. (25:2.11) The conciliators traverse space at the seraphic rate of triple velocity (23:3.2) (25:2.11) a conciliating commission and is made up as follows: Judge-Arbiter, Spirit-advocate, Divine Executioner and Recorder (25:2.5-9) The members of a conciliating commission are never separated. (25:3.17) there is absolutely no limit to their authority to call witnesses from anywhere and everywhere (25:3.4) the exact number operating in Nebadon is not of record, but is estimated in the neighborhood of 100 million conciliating commissions. The last report of registry on Uversa gives the number operating in Orvonton as almost 18 trillion commissions — over 70 trillion individuals (25:3.15) (37:8.6) The actual administration of Urantia is indeed difficult to describe. there are no absolutely authoritative judicial powers operative on the planet—only the conciliating commissions. (114:5.1-2) decisions
improvedDescription: Traveling courts composed of four Universal Conciliators that adjudicate minor disputes and misunderstandings throughout the universes. Each commission includes a Judge-Arbiter, Spirit-Advocate, Divine Executioner, and Recorder, functioning with supreme authority to gather evidence and render final, unanimous decisions on matters of temporal importance.

name: conciliation (ceremony)
originalDescription: conciliation ceremony had a place in evolution of religious observances (90:0.1) As religion evolved, the sacrificial rites of conciliation and propitiation replaced the older methods of avoidance, placation, and exorcism. (89:4.4)
improvedDescription: A religious observance that evolved as part of the progression from primitive placation and avoidance rituals to more sophisticated forms of worship. Conciliation ceremonies represented an intermediate stage in religious development, focusing on reconciling relationships between mortals and spiritual forces before advancing to sacrificial and redemptive practices.

name: Conciliators, Universal
improvedDescription: An order of divine beings who serve as mediators and conflict resolvers in the universe administration.

name: conclaves
