The Danubians practiced cremation as part of their mother worship rituals, while the Nodites encouraged it to combat cannibalism, and it was nearly universal in Scandinavia for thousands of years.

name: Cretaceous
originalDescription: Cretaceous was the era of birds and flowering plants, from 100 to 50 million years ago (60:3.1-22) (60:4.1-6)
improvedDescription: The Cretaceous period was a geological era from 100 to 50 million years ago characterized by the dominance of flowering plants, the appearance of true birds, and the decline of reptiles. It marked the end of continental drift and witnessed significant volcanic activity and mountain building.

name: Crete
originalDescription: About 12,000 B.C. a brilliant tribe of Andites migrated to Crete. (80:7.2,7,12) in Crete that the mother cult of the descendants of Cain attained its greatest vogue. (80:7.7) (80:8.5) Philistines were a political refugees from the higher civilization of Crete (74:8.9) Jesus and the apostles
improvedDescription: Crete was an island settled around 12,000 B.C. by a brilliant tribe of Andites who established an advanced civilization skilled in crafts and agriculture. It became the center of the mother cult that glorified Eve and served as a cultural hub before its inhabitants eventually migrated to Greece and other regions.

name: crickets
originalDescription: About 210 million years ago, crickets, along with spiders, scorpions, cockroaches, and locusts, first appeared and quickly spread across the continents. These early insects, including dragonflies with wingspans over seventy-five centimeters, thrived and diversified, with some cockroaches growing up to ten centimeters long. (59:5.7)
improvedDescription: Crickets were among the early insects that first appeared about 210 million years ago, along with spiders, scorpions, cockroaches, and locusts, quickly spreading across the continents during this period of rapid insect evolution.

name: crime
originalDescription: in the most developed continental nation of a neighboring planet they use methods to deal with crime, madness and degeneracy, which although in some ways would please us, in others we would find them appalling. The most dangerous hardened criminals and the incurably insane are condemned by the courts to die in lethal gas chambers. (72:6.9) (72:10) primitive crime
improvedDescription: Crime represents violations of social order and law, originally conceived as assaults upon tribal customs and taboos. Primitive peoples often used supernatural methods like ordeals to detect criminals, while modern civilized worlds have developed more rational systems of justice and rehabilitation.

name: criminals
originalDescription: Jesus’ words for a condemned criminal in Corinth were: “Your fellows have adjudged you guilty; they have determined that you shall die, but there is a Judge to whom you may appeal for forgiveness, and who will judge you by your real motives and better intentions. You need not fear to meet the judgment of God if your repentance is genuine and your faith sincere”. (133:4.12) in continental nation of neighboring planet
improvedDescription: Criminals are individuals who violate the laws and moral codes of society. On advanced worlds, they are often rehabilitated through agricultural colonies or detention centers, while the most dangerous habitual offenders may face capital punishment for serious crimes like murder and treason.

name: crinoids
originalDescription: much of the limestone was made by the crinoids. (59:5.8)
improvedDescription: Crinoids, also known as stone lilies, were ancient marine echinoderms that flourished in prehistoric seas and contributed significantly to limestone formation through their skeletal remains.

name: cripples
originalDescription: Hunchbacked and crippled children were regarded as fetishes; lunatics were believed to be moon-struck. (88:1.9)
improvedDescription: In primitive societies, physically disabled children including those who were hunchbacked and crippled were regarded as fetishes, believed to possess special spiritual significance or powers.

name: crisis
originalDescription: In times of crisis in the affairs of a universe, the enormous influence of the mind as a factor in spiritual choice is demonstrated. Education, training, and experience are important factors, but the Adjuster can empower the consecrated will to perform amazing acts of loyal devotion. (67:3.7) God has foreseen all the emergencies of the universe, and although it seems that emergency decisions or rectifications are made, this is due to our limited vision of God's plan. (3:2.6-7) humans sometimes hear Adjuster’s direct voice in moments of supreme desire, in a supreme situation, and consequent upon a supreme decision. (110:7.9) The men and women of the reserve corps of destiny function in rare social emergencies and spiritual exigencies for the prevention of the breakdown of evolutionary culture or the extinction of the light of living truth. (114:7.9) The ideas, motives, and longings of a lifetime are openly revealed in a crisis. (187:2.4) seraphim do not intrude into human drama except in emergencies and then usually on the direct orders of their superiors (113:5.4-5) spiritual attainment sometimes is secured by a specific crisis (100:2.8) Woman failed to get social recognition during primitive times because she did not function in an emergency; she was not a spectacular or crisis hero. (84:3.3) Jesus and crisis
improvedDescription: A critical moment or emergency situation that tests character and spiritual choosing, during which the mind's influence in spiritual decisions is demonstrated. In times of crisis, individuals may hear the Thought Adjuster's direct voice, and ideas, motives, and longings of a lifetime are openly revealed.

name: Crispus
originalDescription: Crispus was the head of the Corinthian synagogue; he was married with five children; he met Jesus without knowing it, and later supported Paul in his preaching (133:3.1-4)
improvedDescription: The chief ruler of the Corinthian synagogue who was married with five children. He met Jesus during his travels without knowing his identity, and later when Paul preached in Corinth, Crispus embraced Christianity with his entire family and became one of the chief supports of the Christian church.

name: criticism
originalDescription: Caligastia was inclined to take sides with almost every party of protest, and he was usually sympathetic with those who gave mild expression to implied criticism. (66:8.1) both Lucifer and Caligastia were patiently instructed and lovingly warned respecting their critical tendencies and the subtle development of their pride of self and its associated exaggeration of the feeling of self-importance. But all of these attempts to help were misconstrued as unwarranted criticism and as unjustified interference with personal liberties. (66:8.2) conscience is really the criticism of oneself by one’s own value-habits, personal ideals. (100:1.5) It requires no great depth of intellect to pick flaws, ask questions, or raise objections. (102:7.6) Jesus said: Let us give authorities no occasion for offense at our attitude. (157:1.2) If you truly desire to overcome the habit of criticizing some friend, the quickest and surest way of achieving such a change of attitude is to establish the habit of praying for that person every day of your life. (91:5.3-5) religion does not grow unless it is disciplined by constructive criticism (5:5.12) (99:3.7) True and genuine inward certainty does not in the least fear outward analysis, nor does truth resent honest criticism. (146:3.2)
improvedDescription: The practice of fault-finding or expressing disapproval, which can be constructive or destructive. Constructive criticism helps religion grow and develop, while habitual criticism of others can be overcome through establishing the practice of praying for the person being criticized.

name: Cro-Magnons
originalDescription: Cro-Magnon men are the result of the mixing of the Andites of Eden with the higher types of the blue race, combined with the elimination of the inferior strains (80:1.7) The Cro-Magnon peoples, the blue race, were a brave and farseeing race. They had a familiar spirit and they had an effective system of child culture (80:3.5-7) Cro-Magnons were absorbed by Andites from Mesopotamia in a final and en masse invasion swept over Europe (80:3.9) (80:4.6) the Cro-Magnons were wiped out by the superior intelligence and ever-augmenting biologic reserves of the white race of Andites. The decisive struggles between the white man and the blue man were fought out in the valley of the Somme. The strongholds of the blue man which persisted longest were in southern France (80:5.3-6) The Cro-Magnoid blue man constituted the biologic foundation for the modern European races, but they have survived only as absorbed by the later and virile conquerors of their homelands. (80:5.7) there are few evidences of the white man’s early culture, although the preceding Cro-Magnon type is well preserved where it has been securely sealed up in caves and grottoes. (80:9.4) During the previous hunting era the superior tribes had intermarried with the higher types of war captives and had unvaryingly destroyed those whom they deemed inferior. But then they began to save many of the mediocre captives as slaves for agriculture and interbreeding with slaves deteriorated the race (80:3.9)
improvedDescription: A brave and farseeing people resulting from the mixing of Andites from Eden with higher types of the blue race. They were skilled artists, hunters, and warriors who maintained efficient child culture systems, but were eventually absorbed by later Andite invasions and formed the biological foundation for modern European races.

name: crocodiles
originalDescription: The marine crocodilians were also a reversion from the land type of reptile, but unlike the sea serpents, these animals always returned to the land to lay their eggs. (60:2.11)
improvedDescription: Marine reptiles that evolved as a reversion from land-type reptiles. Unlike sea serpents, crocodiles always returned to land to lay their eggs and have survived as one of the remaining groups from early reptilian families.

name: cross
originalDescription: the cross the cross meaning
improvedDescription: The crossbeam that condemned criminals were forced to carry to their crucifixion site. As a symbol, the cross represents the supreme devotion and love of Jesus, demonstrating God's love for humanity and serving as an eternal symbol of the triumph of love over hate and truth over evil.

name: crossbreeding
originalDescription: Present-day prejudice against “half-castes,” “hybrids,” and “mongrels” arises because modern racial crossbreeding is, for the greater part, between the grossly inferior strains of the races concerned. (82:6.3) it would not prove beneficial for the higher strains of Urantia mortals to mate with the lower races; such an unwise procedure would jeopardize all civilization on the world. we must now work out the planetary problem of race improvement by other methods of adaptation (51:5.7)
improvedDescription: The mating between different racial strains, which in modern times often involves inferior strains and produces unsatisfactory offspring. Present-day prejudice against mixed races arises because contemporary crossbreeding typically occurs between the grossly inferior strains of the races concerned.

name: crossing fingers
improvedDescription: A gesture or practice involving the crossing of fingers, though no specific context or significance is provided in the available source material.

name: crossroad
originalDescription: Spirit of Truth will speak at every crossroad saying: “This is the way” (34:7.8)
improvedDescription: A metaphorical point of decision or choice in life's journey where the Spirit of Truth provides guidance, speaking to say 'This is the way' during moments of uncertainty or spiritual struggle.

name: crown of thorns
originalDescription: plaiting a crown of thorns, they placed it upon Jesus brow. (185:6.2,4)
improvedDescription: A crown made of woven thorns placed on Jesus' head during his scourging before Pilate, as part of the Roman soldiers' mockery of him as 'king of the Jews.'

name: crucifixion
originalDescription: Crucifixion was not a Jewish mode of punishment. Even Herod, with all his cruelty, did not resort to crucifixion. The Romans never crucified a Roman citizen; only slaves and subject peoples were subjected to this dishonorable mode of death.  (187:1.5) Crucifixion was resorted to in order to provide a cruel and lingering punishment, the victim sometimes not dying for several days. (187:2.3) (187:5.8) it was strictly against the law to show friendly feelings for one who was being led forth to crucifixion. (187:1.7) A crucified person could not be buried in a Jewish cemetery (188:1.2) main crucified
improvedDescription: A Roman method of execution involving nailing or binding victims to a cross, designed to provide cruel and lingering punishment. It was not used by Jews and was forbidden for Roman citizens, being reserved for slaves and subject peoples. The method was learned from the Phoenicians and could result in death taking several days.

name: cruelty
originalDescription: A doctrinal fetish will lead mortal man to betray himself into the clutches of bigotry, fanaticism, superstition, intolerance, and the most atrocious of barbarous cruelties. (88:2.7) The few denunciations of Jesus were largely directed against pride, cruelty, oppression, and hypocrisy. (140:8.21) In the half-civilized man there still lurks an evil brutality which seeks to vent itself upon those who are superior in wisdom and spiritual attainment. (184:4.5) When the flood tides of human adversity, selfishness, cruelty, hate, malice, and jealousy beat about the mortal soul, you may rest in the assurance that there is one inner bastion, the citadel of the spirit, which is absolutely unassailable; at least this is true of every human being who has dedicated the keeping of his soul to the indwelling spirit of the eternal God. (100:2.7)
improvedDescription: Brutal or barbarous treatment that Jesus opposed, particularly when manifested through pride, oppression, and hypocrisy. It represents an evil tendency in half-civilized humans who seek to harm those superior in wisdom and spiritual attainment.

name: crumbs
originalDescription: Lazarus desired to be fed with Dives’ crumbs which fell from his table (169:3.2) Norana said no man should dare to deprive the dogs of the privilege of obtaining the crumbs which chance to fall from the children’s table. (156:1.5)
improvedDescription: Small pieces of food that fall from a table, referenced in Jesus' parables and teachings about the beggar Lazarus desiring crumbs from the rich man's table, and in the Syrophoenician woman's humble request for healing, comparing herself to a dog seeking crumbs from the children's table.

name: crusader
originalDescription: Religion may even engender the spirit of the crusader, which is more than dangerous if not controlled by spiritual insight and loyal devotion to the commonplace social obligations of human loyalties. (100:6.5)
improvedDescription: A zealous religious warrior spirit that can emerge from true religion but becomes dangerous when not controlled by spiritual insight and loyal devotion to commonplace social obligations.

name: crust (earth’s)
improvedDescription: The solid outer layer of the planet Earth.

name: crustaceans
originalDescription: The arthropods, or crustaceans, were the ancestors of the first vertebrates. The forerunners of the fish family were two modified arthropod ancestors (59:4.10) air-breathing amphibians developed from the arthropods, whose swim bladders had evolved into lungs. (59:5.5) The brachiopods early reached their climax, being succeeded by the arthropods, and barnacles made their first appearance. 110 millions years ago crabs, lobsters, and the modern types of crustaceans matured.  (59:1.18) (59:4.3,10) (60:2.9) (65:2.5) The trilobites existed in tens of thousands of patterns and were the predecessors of modern crustaceans. (59:2.10)
improvedDescription: Marine arthropods including crabs, lobsters, shrimps, and their prehistoric ancestors like trilobites. They were ancestors of the first vertebrates and reached their modern forms around 110 million years ago, with trilobites being particularly prominent in early seas before becoming extinct.

name: crystals
originalDescription: buildings on Jerusem are constructed wholly of crystal (46:5.11,25,31)
improvedDescription: Crystalline materials used extensively in the construction of buildings on Jerusem and other architectural worlds, including walls made of crystal gems, gates of pearly crystals, and entire structures of transparent crystal.

name: cult of taking no life
originalDescription: It was during these dark days that the cult of taking no life arose in India, and it has ever since persisted.  (94:2.5)
improvedDescription: A religious movement that arose in India during dark days of spiritual despair, advocating against the taking of any life, which has persisted ever since its emergence.

name: cults
originalDescription: A race or nation can only assimilate from any advanced religion that which is reasonably consistent and compatible with its current evolutionary status, plus its genius for adaptation. (92:2.4) atheistic cults claim that such salvation as was attainable could come only by man’s own unaided efforts. (94:2.5) destiny of cults depends on leadership (99:5.1) the cults are formed, not to discover truth, but rather to promulgate their creeds. (92:3.1) primitive cult strengths dangers Jesus cult of new revelation rituals (90:5.1-3)
improvedDescription: Organized religious groups with established rituals, symbols, and ceremonies. While they can preserve sentiment and promote loyalty, cults are formed to promulgate creeds rather than discover truth, and their survival depends largely on intelligent leadership. They serve as the skeletal structure around which personal spiritual experience grows.

name: culture
improvedDescription: The development and expression of human social organization, customs, knowledge, and artistic achievement within a society or group of people.

name: cumin
originalDescription: Jesus said: “Woe upon you, scribes and Pharisees and all other hypocrites who make sure that they tithe mint, anise, and cumin and at the same time disregard the weightier matters of the law—faith, mercy, and judgment!” (175:1.17)
improvedDescription: A spice mentioned by Jesus when he criticized religious leaders for meticulously tithing minor herbs like mint, anise, and cumin while neglecting weightier matters of faith, mercy, and judgment.

name: cup
originalDescription: Jesus warned his apostles to return to their homes in peace if they were not willing to drink with him the cup that was being prepared. (171:0.5) (171:2.5) When Jesus prayed he said: “Father, I know it is possible to avoid this cup—all things are possible with you—but I have come to do your will, and while this is a bitter cup, I would drink it if it is your will” (136:9.4) (182:3.2-9) Jesus complained about Pharisees: “Woe upon you, scribes, Pharisees, and hypocrites! for you are scrupulous to cleanse the outside of the cup and the platter, but within there remains the filth of extortion, excesses, and deception”. (166:1.4) (175:1.18)
improvedDescription: A symbolic term frequently used by Jesus to refer to his impending suffering and crucifixion. Jesus spoke of drinking the cup of bitterness that was prepared for him, representing his willingness to accept the Father's will despite the difficulty of his mortal experience.

name: cup of remembrance
originalDescription: the cup of remembrance is a symbol that Jesus instituted that represented the emblem of the bestowal and ministry of the divine Spirit of Truth. (179:5.1-3)
improvedDescription: A symbolic cup that Jesus instituted during the Last Supper, representing the emblem of the bestowal and ministry of the divine Spirit of Truth and marking the beginning of a new dispensation of grace and truth.

name: curiosity
originalDescription: Not until we traverse the last of the Havona circuits and visit the last of the Havona worlds, will the tonic of adventure and the stimulus of curiosity disappear from our career (14:5.7) We should not try to satisfy the curiosity or gratify all the latent adventure surging within the soul in one short life in the flesh. (195:5.10) Curiosity is the hunger for harmony and thirst for beauty. (56:10.6) Curiosity—the spirit of investigation, the urge of discovery, the drive of exploration—is a part of the inborn and divine endowment of evolutionary space creatures. (14:5.10-11) (16:7.1) Jesus never seemed to be curious about people, and he never manifested a desire to direct, manage, or follow them up.  (171:7.6) mind reaches out to explore remote situations (42:12.4) there are no arbitrary secrets in the universe (19:5.11)
improvedDescription: The spirit of investigation, the urge of discovery, and the drive of exploration that is part of the inborn and divine endowment of evolutionary space creatures. Curiosity represents the hunger for harmony and thirst for beauty that motivates beings throughout their eternal career of adventure and discovery.

name: cursing
originalDescription: Primitive cursing was a coercive practice designed to overawe minor spirits. (87:6.13) oaths at the beginning consisted in pronouncing a curse upon oneself. (70:11.3) The habit of swearing, profanity, represents a prostitution of former ritualistic repetition of holy names. (90:5.3) Peter denied all knowledge of Jesus with much cursing and swearing (184:2.6) Modern man no longer attempts openly to coerce the spirits, though he still evinces a disposition to bargain with Deity. And he still swears, knocks on wood, crosses his fingers, and follows expectoration with some trite phrase; once it was a magical formula. (87:6.17)
improvedDescription: Originally a coercive practice designed to overawe minor spirits in primitive times, cursing evolved from ritualistic repetition of holy names. Modern profanity represents a degraded form of this ancient spiritual practice.

name: curvature
originalDescription: Philosophy dare not project its interpretations of reality in the linear fashion of logic; it must never fail to reckon with the elliptic symmetry of reality and with the essential curvature of all relation concepts. (103:6.14)
improvedDescription: A philosophical concept representing the essential nature of all relation concepts, requiring philosophy to account for the elliptic symmetry of reality rather than projecting interpretations in a purely linear fashion of logic.

name: custodians (Lanonandek)
originalDescription: There are one hundred thousand Lanonandek custodians and archivists in Salvington (35:8.13)
improvedDescription: A group of one hundred thousand Lanonandek Sons who serve as custodians and archivists in Salvington, maintaining records and administrative functions for the local universe.

name: custodians (Life Carrier)
originalDescription: The custodians are a subgroup within the Life Carriers, who are classified into three main divisions: the oldest Life Carriers, the assistants, and the custodians. The custodians, along with the assistants, focus on studying and developing methods to protect and conserve life. They are specialists in handling the basic life patterns and are crucial in the life conservation plans for new worlds. On Urantia, there were twenty-four custodians, two for each fundamental life pattern. Their work ensures the proper establishment and maintenance of life on planets. (36:1.3) (36:2.16)
improvedDescription: The third division of Life Carriers who specialize in the conservation and protection of life. They are experts in handling basic life patterns and serve as commissioners for life-conservation plans on new worlds, with twenty-four custodians assigned to Urantia to oversee the fundamental patterns of life organization.

name: custodians of knowledge
originalDescription: custodians of knowledge help to facilitate the consult of the vast store of knowledge of Paradise. The custodians are also the exalted teachers of the central universe. Only by indirection and reflectively are the benefits of Paradise knowledge secured in the local universes. (27:5.5) custodians of knowledge are the final and living summaries of the vast network of the recording angels, ranging from the seraphim and seconaphim of the local and superuniverses to the chief recorders of the tertiary supernaphim in Havona. (27:5.3) The superaphic custodians of knowledge are the higher “living epistles” known and read by all who dwell on Paradise. They are the divine records of truth, the living books of real knowledge. It is also inherently impossible for an untruth to gain lodgment in them (27:5.1) They are in reality living, automatic libraries, sometimes designated the “living library of Paradise”. (25:5.1) (27:5.1) they are part of primary supernaphim (27:0.6) These primary supernaphim who are inherently in possession of universe knowledge are also responsible for its organization and classification. (27:5.5) in Paradise seekers of a knowledge are presented with beings who are exactly what they seek (27:5.2-3) Voices of Wisdom in perpetual liaison with Custodians of Knowledge (28:5.7)
improvedDescription: Primary supernaphim who serve as living repositories of universal knowledge on Paradise. They are perfect beings in whom the facts of all universes are inherently recorded, functioning as living libraries that organize and classify knowledge into seven grand orders and serve as exalted teachers of the central universe.

name: Custodians of Records
originalDescription: Chief Recorders of tertiary supernaphim transmit to Custodians of Knowledge the transactions of true-knowledge import (26:3.5) Custodians of Records are chosen from tertiary supernaphim in Havona (25:5.1,3) keep formal archives of Paradise, those archives which stand in contrast to the living records of registry in the minds of the custodians of knowledge, sometimes designated the “living library of Paradise” (25:5.1) not one has ever been transferred from the permanent roll call of their order. (25:5.4) serve as Chiefs of Records for Celestial Recorders (25:5.4) they appear in Uversa personality register (30:2.78)
improvedDescription: Custodians of Records are senior tertiary supernaphim chosen from the chief recorders of Havona to serve as keepers of the formal archives of Paradise. They maintain the official records that contrast with the living records held in the minds of the custodians of knowledge, and some serve as Chiefs of Records directing the activities of Celestial Recorders in the superuniverses.

name: customs
improvedDescription: Social practices and traditions passed down through generations within cultures and civilizations.

name: Cuthites
originalDescription: Sargon, king of Assyria, in subduing a revolt in central Palestine, carried away and into captivity over twenty-five thousand Jews of the northern kingdom of Israel and installed in their place an almost equal number of the descendants of the Cuthites, Sepharvites, and the Hamathites. (143:4.1)
improvedDescription: A people whose descendants were among those installed by Sargon, king of Assyria, in central Palestine to replace the twenty-five thousand Jews of the northern kingdom of Israel who were carried away into captivity around 700 B.C.

name: Cutites
originalDescription: After the first garden was vacated by Adam, it was occupied variously by the Nodites, Cutites, and the Suntites. (73:7.1)
improvedDescription: One of the groups that occupied the first Garden of Eden after Adam vacated it, along with the Nodites and Suntites.

name: cuttlefish
originalDescription: cuttlefish are descendant of cephalopods (59:2.11) cuttlefish oxygenate blood with copper (65:6.4)
improvedDescription: Marine cephalopods that evolved from earlier ancestors and use copper instead of iron to oxygenate their blood, representing an alternative biological solution to oxygen transport in living organisms.

name: Cybele
originalDescription: Cybele is the mother of Attis in the Phrygian mystery cult (98:4.2-8) (98:5.5)
improvedDescription: The mother goddess in the Phrygian mystery cult, revered as the mother of Attis whose death and resurrection formed the central ritual of their religious observances.

name: Cymboyton
originalDescription: Cymboyton was a wealthy merchant from Urmia who built a temple dedicated to the religion (134:3.2) exercised unchallengeable authority in Urmia (134:4.10) she lived and died without ever revealing his personal beliefs (134:3.3) there was trouble in Urmia after his death (134:6.15-17)
improvedDescription: A wealthy merchant from Urmia who built a temple dedicated to universal religion and exercised unchallengeable authority over the religionists there. He lived and died without ever revealing his personal beliefs, and after his death his sons encountered great difficulties maintaining peace in the religious community.

name: cynicism (attitude)
improvedDescription: A skeptical and distrustful attitude toward human nature and motives, often expressed through mockery or contempt.

name: Cynicism (religion)
originalDescription: Cynics preached simplicity and virtue and urged men to meet death fearlessly. (121:4.4) The residual teachings of the disciples of Melchizedek, excepting those which persisted in the Jewish religion, were best preserved in the doctrines of the Cynics. (98:0.2) (131:1.1) they were characterized by being wandering preachers and they traced their philosophy to Diogenes of Athens (121:4.4,6) Occidental religion thus languished until the days of the Skeptics, Cynics, Epicureans, and Stoics, but most important of all, until the times of the great contest between Mithraism and Paul’s new religion of Christianity. (98:6.2) Ganid made a summary of the teachings of this religion while he was in Alexandria with Jesus (131:1)
improvedDescription: A religio-philosophical movement that preserved remnants of Melchizedek's teachings, characterized by wandering preachers who taught simplicity, virtue, and fearless acceptance of death. They traced their philosophy to Diogenes of Athens and made their doctrine democratic and accessible to common people.

name: Cynics
originalDescription: Claudus, a downhearted and discouraged young man from Malta, became preacher of Cynics (130:8.1) The last stand of the dwindling band of Salem believers was made by the Cynic preachers, who exhorted the Romans to abandon their wild and senseless religious rituals and return to a form of worship embodying Melchizedek’s gospel (98:3.9) Jesus was once mistaken for priest of Cynics (133:2.1) Before the end of the first week in Rome Jesus had sought out, and had made the acquaintance of, the worth-while leaders of the Cynics, the Stoics, and the mystery cults, in particular the Mithraic group. (132:0.4) Cynic preachers of faith and trust in God were still functioning in Roman Europe in the first century after Christ, being later incorporated into the newly forming Christian religion. (98:0.2) Mardus was the acknowledged leader of the Cynics of Rome, and he became a great friend of Jesus, also known as the scribe of Damascus. (132:2.1)
improvedDescription: Preachers of faith and trust in God who maintained Salem teachings in relatively pure form, functioning as wandering teachers throughout the Roman Empire. They were among the last defenders of Melchizedek's gospel and were later incorporated into the newly forming Christian religion.

name: Cyprus
originalDescription: About 5000 B.C. a mighty host of progressive Mesopotamians moved out of the Euphrates valley and settled upon the island of Cyprus; this civilization was wiped out about 3000 B.C. subsequently by the barbarian hordes from the north. (80:7.10) In Cyprus, Jesus had a long conversation with his student Ganid about the mind (133:7) Thomas preached in Cyprus (139:8.13)
improvedDescription: An island in the Mediterranean Sea where Jesus and Ganid had extended conversations about the mind, and later where the apostle Thomas preached. Around 5000 B.C., progressive Mesopotamians settled there, establishing a civilization that was destroyed around 3000 B.C. by barbarian invasions from the north.

name: Cyrene
originalDescription: Jesus and Ganid gave first aid to a lad named Rufus, who had been injured by a breakdown. His father, Simon, little dreamed that the man whose cross he subsequently bore by orders of a Roman soldier was the stranger who once befriended his son. (130:6.6) Simon was the father of Rufus and Alexander from Cyrene. Both sons became very effective teachers of the new gospel in Africa. (187:1.9-10)
improvedDescription: A city in northern Africa where Jesus and Ganid gave first aid to an injured boy named Rufus, whose father Simon later carried Jesus' cross to Golgotha by Roman order. Simon's sons Alexander and Rufus became effective teachers of the gospel in Africa.

name: Cyrus
originalDescription: Cyrus is mentioned as an example of the tradition of the disowned child saved by divine intervention (89:7.3)
improvedDescription: A historical figure mentioned as an example of the ancient tradition of the divinely preserved disowned child, along with Sargon, Moses, and Romulus.

name: Dabaritta
originalDescription: Dabarita is one of the towns that Jesus, the twelve and 117 evangelists visited during their second preaching tour of Galilee from October 3 to December 30, 28 (149:0.1)
improvedDescription: A town in Galilee that Jesus, the twelve apostles, and 117 evangelists visited during their second preaching tour from October 3 to December 30, A.D. 28.

name: Dagon
originalDescription: God who was the father of the gods for the Philistines (97:6.2)
improvedDescription: The chief deity of the Philistines, described as the father of their gods in contrast to Yahweh's claim as God of all nations.

name: Dakota Black Hills
originalDescription: Pacific ocean extended at one time eastward to the Dakota Black Hills region. (60:2.4)
improvedDescription: A region in North America that marked the eastward extent of the Pacific Ocean during an ancient geological period when much of the continent was submerged.

name: Dalamatia
originalDescription: Dalamatia was the city that was built in the distant past to house the headquarters of the Planetary Prince. It was located in the Persian Gulf region, in the area corresponding to later Mesopotamia. (66:3) This world center of culture was named Dalamatia in honor of Daligastia. (66:3.3) it was a very simple but beautiful city, enclosed within a wall 40 feet high. (66:3.3) (66:4.10) The country around the city was quite well settled within a radius of 100 miles. (66:7.18) the residential buildings were models of neatness and cleanliness, everything was very simple and altogether primitive, built of brick (66:3.5-6) (66:7.1-3) The land provision within the city walls was sufficient to provide for pasturage and gardening for the support of a population of about 20,000. (66:7.1) the law of Dalamatia for almost 300,000 years was the code known as “The Father’s Way” and consisted of the following seven commands: (66:7.8-16) # You shall not fear nor serve any God but the Father of all. # You shall not disobey the Father’s Son # You shall not speak a lie # You shall not kill men # You shall not steal # You shall not touch your friend’s wife. # You shall not show disrespect to your parents The definite order of family life and the living of one family together in one residence of comparatively settled location date from these times of Dalamatia (66:7) life in Dalamatia was far from being utopian (68:1.7) Although the races were at war, they lived together in harmony near the seat of the Prince (64:6.28) the rebellion teachings
improvedDescription: The headquarters city of Planetary Prince Caligastia, built in the Persian Gulf region around 500,000 years ago. This beautiful walled city housed the Prince's staff and served as a center of culture and learning for nearly 300,000 years, operating under a moral code called "The Father's Way." The city was destroyed by rebellion and later submerged beneath the waters, with its legacy influencing later Mesopotamian civilizations.

name: Daligastia
originalDescription: Daligastia is a secondary Lanonandek Son, number 319,407 of this order (66:2.2) Dalamatia, the city of the Planetary Prince on Urantia, was named so in honor of Daligastia (66:3.3) he presided over the ten councils of the hundred of Caligastia, the retinue of councilors of Caligastia (66:5.1) before Caligastia declared himself absolute sovereign of Urantia he demanded that the Prince's corporeal staff resign pending redistribution of authority (67:2.1) he proclaimed Caligastia as “God of Urantia” (53:8.5) (67:2.4) he ordered rebel personnel to reproduce sexually to increase their numbers since they had become mortal (67:4.2) he was deprived of most of the power to do evil in Van's time (73:2.5) he tried to resist Adam's work (73:2.5) (74:5.5) he made overtures to the sons of Adam but with little influence (75:2.1) Miguel offered mercy to Daligastia, but he rejected it (53:8.6) (53:9.1) he was never able to oppress or coerce normal individuals against his will (66:8.6) (75:2.1) since Pentecost, he is servile to Adjusters and the Spirit of Truth (53:8.7) still today he is free on Urantia (53:8.6-7)
improvedDescription: A secondary Lanonandek Son who served as associate-assistant to Planetary Prince Caligastia on Urantia. He presided over the ten councils of Caligastia's staff and later joined the Lucifer rebellion, proclaiming Caligastia as "God of Urantia." Though deprived of much power after the rebellion, he remains active on the planet but cannot coerce normal individuals against their will.

name: Damascus
originalDescription: Jesus met a teacher of mathematics from Damascus, and learning some new techniques of numbers, he spent much time on mathematics for several years. (123:6.3) The Apostle Paul experienced just a sudden and spectacular conversion the eventful day on the Damascus road. Christianity is the religion embodying the theologic views of Paul and portraying his own personal experience with the Jesus of the Damascus road. (100:5.3) (196:2.1)
improvedDescription: An ancient Syrian city where Jesus learned mathematical techniques from a teacher and spent considerable time studying numbers. It was on the road to Damascus that the Apostle Paul experienced his dramatic conversion, which significantly influenced the development of Christianity.

name: Damascus gate
originalDescription: Damascus gate was the one that led north out of Jerusalem (187:1.4) the soldiers guarding the tomb of Jesus fled panic-stricken towards the Damascus gate, where they passed by the believing women (189:4.5,7)
improvedDescription: The northern gate of Jerusalem through which Jesus was led on the direct route to Golgotha for crucifixion. It was near this gate that the panicked tomb guards fled after Jesus' resurrection, encountering the women who were coming to anoint his body.

name: Dan (city)
originalDescription: Dan, also known as Caesarea Philippi, where Jesus passed on a personal journey, and he and the twelve passed on their flight north on May 29 (134:7.5) (134:8.10) (155:2.1-3)
improvedDescription: Dan, also known as Caesarea Philippi, was a northern Palestinian city that Jesus visited during his personal journey and where he and the twelve apostles stayed while fleeing north.

name: Dan (Prince’s staff)
originalDescription: Dan was the head of the council in charge of conquering the predatory animals, one of the ten councils of the Planetary Prince on Urantia (66:5.7) Dan and all his council were lost in the rebellion (67:4.1)
improvedDescription: Dan was the head of the council in charge of conquering predatory animals, one of the ten councils of the Planetary Prince on Urantia. Dan and all his council were lost in the Caligastia rebellion.

name: dance
originalDescription: Our ensembles of dancing undoubtedly represent a crude and grotesque attempt of material creatures to approach the celestial harmony of being placement and personality arrangement. (44:1.10) wild dancing can induce a mystic state. But instead of mystical experiences the more healthful attitude of spiritual meditation is to be found in reflective worship and in the prayer of thanksgiving. (100:5.10) among primitive man
improvedDescription: Dance among mortals represents a crude attempt to approach celestial harmony, while among primitives it served religious purposes in worship festivals and spirit communication. Wild dancing can induce mystical states, though healthier spiritual meditation is found in reflective worship.

name: danger
originalDescription: About danger Jesus decided to exercise normal watchcare over his human safety and to take reasonable precaution to prevent the untimely termination of his career in the flesh but to refrain from all superhuman intervention (136:7.1) Jesus warned his apostles not to expose themselves needlessly to peril when he was arrested, explaining that it was imperative that they stay behind to bear witness to the gospel when he was gone. (178:3.3) Pentecost endowed mortal man with the power to forgive personal injuries, to keep sweet in the midst of the gravest injustice, to remain unmoved in the face of appalling danger, and to challenge the evils of hate and anger by the fearless acts of love and forbearance. (194:3.12)
improvedDescription: Jesus decided to exercise normal watchcare over his human safety and take reasonable precaution to prevent untimely termination of his mission, but to refrain from superhuman intervention when facing personal danger. He warned his apostles not to expose themselves needlessly to peril, and Pentecost endowed mortals with power to remain unmoved in the face of appalling danger.

name: Daniel
originalDescription: Daniel was a prophet who presented the drama of an impending crisis and the establishment of the Messianic kingdom (97:8.3) he believed in the resurrection of the dead (137:7.6) he was one of the few prophets not silenced by the Jewish priests of Jesus' time (97:10.3) the apocalyptists leaned heavily on Daniel's predictions (135:5.2) Daniel understood who the Most Highs were and that they only rule in the kingdoms of men (43:3.4) Daniel's idea of Messiah was that of a superhuman man, albeit a man (136:1.6) Daniel described a Son of Man that Jesus rejected as a figure to adopt (126:3.6) John the Baptist had a predilection for the writings of Daniel, although they caused great confusion (135:3.2-3)
improvedDescription: Daniel was a Hebrew prophet who presented the drama of an impending crisis and the establishment of the Messianic kingdom, believed in the resurrection of the dead, and understood that the Most Highs rule in the kingdoms of men. His apocalyptic writings heavily influenced John the Baptist and later apocalyptists, though they caused great confusion about the nature of the Messiah.

name: Danube
originalDescription: Some entered Europe by way of the islands of the Aegean and up the Danube valley, but the majority of the earlier and purer strains migrated to northwestern Europe by the northern route across the grazing lands of the Volga and the Don. (80:4.1) By 5000 B. C. Central Europe was for some time controlled by the blue man and the round-headed Andonites, mainly situated in the Danube valley; they were never entirely displaced by the Andites. (80:5.8) (80:9.4)
improvedDescription: The Danube was a major European river valley that served as a migration route for various peoples. Some Andites entered Europe by way of the Aegean islands and up the Danube valley, while the round-headed Andonites were mainly situated in the Danube valley and were never entirely displaced by later migrations.

name: Danubians
originalDescription: The Danubians were Andonites, farmers and herders who had entered Europe through the Balkan peninsula and were moving slowly northward by way of the Danube valley. They made pottery and tilled the land, preferring to live in the valleys. (80:8.4) Greeks imported Danubians as slaves (80:7.6) The most northerly settlement of the Danubians was at Liege in Belgium. (80:8.4) The Danubians became mother worshipers as the result of the work of the missionaries from Crete. (80:8.5)
improvedDescription: The Danubians were Andonites who entered Europe through the Balkan peninsula and moved northward via the Danube valley as farmers and herders. They made pottery, tilled the land, became mother worshipers through Cretan missionary influence, and were later imported as slaves by the Greeks.

name: Daphne
originalDescription: Daphne was a Greek mythical character, a naiad, daughter of the river god Ladon (133:8.1)
improvedDescription: Daphne was a Greek mythical character, a naiad who was the daughter of the river god Ladon.

name: Daphne, grove of
originalDescription: Daphne's Grove was a notorious shrine full of shame in Antioch. Jesus refused to visit it when he was in town (133:8.1)
improvedDescription: The grove of Daphne was a notorious shrine of shame and immorality in Antioch that Jesus refused to visit, though Gonod and Ganid did visit this temple.

name: dark ages
originalDescription: When culture advances overfast and material achievement outruns wisdom, civilization retrogrades and society recedes from high but premature levels to “dark ages” of the interregnum of wisdom (118:8.6) in China European dark ages golden age in islam
improvedDescription: Dark ages occur when material achievement outruns wisdom and civilization retrogresses from high but premature levels of attainment. They represent periods when the imbalance between self-liberty and self-control leads to cultural and spiritual decline, as seen in medieval Europe and ancient China.

name: dark giants of space
improvedDescription: Enormous dark celestial bodies that function as powerful balance wheels in space, holding large neighboring systems in effective gravitational control.

name: dark gravity bodies
originalDescription: The center and focal point of absolute material gravity is the Isle of Paradise, complemented by the dark gravity bodies encircling Havona and equilibrated by the upper and nether space reservoirs. (11:8.2) The dark gravity bodies encircling Havona are neither triata nor gravita, and their drawing power discloses both forms of physical gravity, linear and absolute. (11:8.7) Havona, the central universe, is surrounded by the enormous dark gravity bodies. Equalize the lines of Havona gravity as to render the central universe a physically balanced and perfectly stabilized creation. (12:1.10) (14:1.14-18) These dark gravity bodies neither reflect nor absorb light (14:1.14) The intervening space which exists between these two circuits of gravity bodies is unique in that nothing like it is to be found elsewhere in all the wide universe. (11:8.7) (14:1.17-18) The dark gravity bodies form two belts located between the seven Havona circuits and the seven superuniverses. Surrounding the gravity bodies on the outside there are two semiquiet zones of space, and in the middle of the two belts of bodies there is a zone of space unique in its kind. (14:1.1,5-7,15-17) Owing to the enormous encircling masses of the dark gravity bodies about the fringe of the central universe, the mass content of Havona is far in excess of the total known mass of all seven sectors of the grand universe. (12:1.10) primary seconaphim take pilgrims past dark gravity bodies to Havona (26:4.11)
improvedDescription: Enormous dark masses that encircle Havona in two elliptical belts, neither reflecting nor absorbing light. These unique bodies possess both linear and absolute gravity, serving to stabilize the central universe and maintain gravitational equilibrium between Paradise and the seven superuniverses.

name: dark islands of space
originalDescription: The Dark Islands of Space are the dead suns and other large aggregations of matter devoid of light and heat, enormous in mass. They function as powerful balance wheels, holding large neighboring systems in effective leash. They are locatable by their gravity effect. (15:6.11) collisions of dead giants of sapce convert enormous masses into the rarest form of energy (15:8.6) dark worlds and all outer space can slow down electronic and ultimatonic activity to the point of converting these energies into matter (42:4.9) organized matter tends to disintegrate under certain conditions found in very hot stars and under certain peculiar conditions in space near highly energized cold bodies of condensed matter. (15:8.5) the near approach of a sun to a dark island of space may give birth to a planetary system (15:5.5) their origin may be due to: (15:5.10-11) * by accretions of transmuting energy * by accumulations of cold matter * from burned-out suns Many of these dark islands are vast dynamos which mobilize and directionize certain space-energies, and are used by the Satania Power Center directing the streams of more materialized power to the Master Physical Controllers on the evolutionary planets of space. (41:1.5) (41:3.1) Known giants
improvedDescription: Dead suns and other large aggregations of matter devoid of light and heat that function as powerful balance wheels, holding large neighboring systems in effective gravitational control. Many serve as dynamos that mobilize and direct space energies, and some house power centers that regulate energy circuits throughout space.

name: darkness
originalDescription: Neanderthalers dreaded darkness; they had a mortal dread of nightfall. As long as the moon shone a little, they managed to get along, but in the dark of the moon they grew panicky and sacrifice men and women (64:4.13) “He who walks in the darkness knows not where he goes; but if you will choose to walk in the light, you shall all indeed become liberated sons of God”. (174:5.13)
improvedDescription: A concept representing both physical absence of light and spiritual ignorance or separation from divine truth, contrasted with walking in the light of God's truth.

name: daughters of Aaron
originalDescription: Elizabeth, mother of John the Baptist, wife of Zacharias, was also of the priesthood, being a descendant of the “daughters of Aaron” (135:0.4)
improvedDescription: Female descendants of Aaron the priest, representing the priestly lineage in Judaism. Elizabeth, mother of John the Baptist, was descended from this priestly line.

name: daughters of God
originalDescription: When speaking of sexual creatures, it is customary to call sons of God the beings that descend more directly from the Father and the Son, and daughters of God when referring to the children of the Spirit. (33:3.4) (38:2.2)
improvedDescription: A designation for spirit beings who descend more directly from the Infinite Spirit, in contrast to sons of God who descend from the Father and Son. Angels are commonly referred to by feminine pronouns and considered daughters of God.

name: David (King)
originalDescription: David's army was a collection of malcontents, social misfits, and fugitives (97:9.5) with 600 men he entered into an alliance with the Philistines, which led to the defeat of Saul (97:9.5) David's cosmopolitan tribe, Judah, was Gentile rather than Jewish (97:9.11) 80% of David's soldiers were Baalites (97:9.12) David handed over seven descendants of Saul to be hanged by the Gibeonites (97:9.13) the duel with Goliath was the typical case of battle that used to be resolved by the combat of only the two best men (70:1.19) David set in motion a corrupt political machine; no wonder a rebellion broke out against him (97:9.15) he gained control of the Philistine caravan tariffs (97:9.15) he laid a heavy tribute on the Edomites, Moabites, Ammonites, and Syrians (97:9.14) he ate showbread when he was hungry (147:6.4) paid Saul a dowry of one hundred Philistine foreskins for the hand of his daughter Michal (70:1.18) he had many wives (97:9.10) assassinated Uriah the Hittite (97:9.15) on Mount Gilboa David's actions were remembered there (124:6.3) stories about david
improvedDescription: The second king of Israel who established a united kingdom with Jerusalem as its capital. Despite his military successes and political achievements, his reign involved questionable alliances, corruption, and moral failures including the murder of Uriah the Hittite.

name: David Zebedee
originalDescription: he was known as the dependable David Zebedee. He was the brother of John and James Zebedee (186:3) He respected Jesus as mechanic, but took little stock in his religious views (129:1.12) Zebedee's father went to live with his son David (138:5.4) He followed Jesus after the supposedly miraculous draught of fishes (145:1.1,2) work as follower of Jesus guarded Jesus death of jesus after the death of Jesus
improvedDescription: Brother of apostles James and John Zebedee who organized and supervised an extensive messenger service for Jesus' ministry. Known as dependable and efficient, he maintained camps for pilgrims and coordinated communications throughout Palestine, continuing his messenger work even after Jesus' death.

name: dawn mammals
originalDescription: A little more than one million years ago the Mesopotamian dawn mammals, the direct descendants of the North American lemur type of placental mammal, suddenly appeared. (61:6.1) (62:2.1) they developed an extraordinary fear which led to those wise precautionary measures that so enormously contributed to survival (62:2.4) they gave rise to mid-mammals after 70 generations (61:6.1) (62:3.1) When the numbers of this new and superior group grew great, war, relentless war, broke out; and when the terrible struggle was over, not a single individual of the pre-existent and ancestral race of dawn mammals remained alive. (62:3.4) The immediate lemurlike mother of the dawn-mammal species escaped death no less than five times by mere hairbreadth margins before she gave birth to the father of the new and higher mammalian order. (62:3.9) These small animals walked mostly on their hind legs, and they possessed large brains in proportion to their size and in comparison with the brains of other animals. (61:6.1) They experienced many of the emotions and shared numerous instincts which later characterized primitive man (62:2.3) They were, indeed, highly gregarious but nevertheless exceedingly pugnacious when in any way disturbed (62:2.5)
improvedDescription: Small, active creatures that appeared over one million years ago in Mesopotamia as direct descendants of North American lemur-type mammals. These flesh-eating, three-foot-tall animals walked mostly on hind legs, possessed large brains for their size, and gave rise to the mid-mammals after seventy generations.

name: day of atonement
improvedDescription: A significant Jewish holy day of fasting and repentance, observed annually as part of the Hebrew religious calendar and ceremonial system.

name: daydreaming
originalDescription: There is great danger associated with the habitual practice of religious daydreaming; mysticism may become a technique of reality avoidance, albeit it has sometimes been a means of genuine spiritual communion. (100:5.8)
improvedDescription: A practice involving religious daydreaming that carries great danger as it may become a technique for avoiding reality, though mysticism has sometimes served as a means of genuine spiritual communion.

name: Daynals, order of
originalDescription: Daynals are the universal educators, being dedicated to the spiritual awakening and moral guidance of all realms. (20:7.3) (20:8.4) (20:9.4) (20:10.4) (46:5.13)
improvedDescription: Trinity Teacher Sons who serve as universal educators dedicated to the spiritual awakening and moral guidance of all realms throughout the universes.

name: days
improvedDescription: Units of time measurement used across various worlds and contexts in the universe.

name: deaconesses
originalDescription: Throughout the early days of the Christian church women teachers and ministers were called deaconesses and were accorded general recognition. (150:1.3)
improvedDescription: Women teachers and ministers in the early Christian church who were accorded general recognition for their religious work and ministry.

name: Dead, Book of the
originalDescription: The Egyptians priests inscribed the coffins with charm texts. Presently a diverse assortment of these magical texts was collected and preserved as The Book of the Dead. (95:2.5)
improvedDescription: An ancient Egyptian collection of magical charm texts that were inscribed on coffins and believed to protect the deceased in the afterlife.

name: dead
originalDescription: Inantiquity the names of the dead were never spoken. (87:2.6) The Jews were in the habit of burying their dead on the day of their demise; this was a necessary practice in such a warm climate. (167:4.3) Jesus said: “If you would be an ordained teacher, you must let others bury the dead while you go forth to publish the good news”. (163:2.2) Nazarite vow forbade contact with the dead (135:2.1)
improvedDescription: Those who have died, with various cultural practices surrounding them including taboos against speaking their names, burial customs, and beliefs about their spiritual state.

name: dead giants of space
improvedDescription: Massive celestial bodies in space that have ceased active functioning.

name: Dead Sea
originalDescription: Jordan river flowed down toward Dead Sea (124:6.5)
improvedDescription: A body of water in Palestine where the Jordan River flows, located near Jerusalem and significant in the religious geography of the region.

name: deadland
originalDescription: It was once thought that the great desire of a ghost was to be quickly “laid” so that it might proceed undisturbed to deadland. (87:2.2) Fasting and other forms of self-denial were thought to be pleasing to the ghosts, who took pleasure in the discomfort of the living during the transition period of lurking about before their actual departure for deadland. (87:1.1) (87:2.4,8) (87:4.2)
improvedDescription: The primitive concept of a destination realm where ghosts were believed to journey after death, representing an early form of afterlife belief.

name: death
originalDescription: among primitive man physical death (49:6) (55:2) spiritual death | extinction; nonsurvivors; roll call:of justice
improvedDescription: The cessation of mortal life, which may be physical, intellectual, or spiritual in nature, serving as a transition point for surviving personalities to continue their eternal journey in morontia form.

name: death penalty
originalDescription: It was at one time not at all uncommon for ten innocent persons to be put to death because of supposed responsibility for one natural death. (88:4.7) Treason—the “selling out” or betrayal of one’s tribal associates—was the first capital crime. Cattle stealing was universally punished by summary death, and even recently horse stealing has been similarly punished (70:10.15) many ancient rulers, including Hammurabi and Moses, recognized that many crimes, particularly those of a grave sex nature, should be punished by burning at the stake. (70:10.14) Moses prescribed death penalty for ghost cultists (92:5.11) Romans denied Jews right to inflict death penalty without the approval of a Roman tribunal. (162:3.3) Jesus declared that the state has the right of self-defense in the manner of its own choosing, even with death penalty (133:4.12) in the most developed continental nation of a neighboring planet numerous crimes aside from murder, including betrayal of governmental trust, also carry the death penalty, and the visitation of justice is sure and swift. (72:10.1)
improvedDescription: Capital punishment, which has been practiced throughout history. In ancient times, it was common for ten innocent people to be executed for one natural death due to beliefs about responsibility. Various civilizations, including those under Hammurabi and Moses, prescribed death for many crimes, particularly grave sexual offenses, often by burning. The practice continues to exist in some advanced civilizations, where it is applied to multiple crimes beyond murder and administered with certainty and swiftness.

name: debating society
originalDescription: It has been hard for mankind to learn that neither peace nor war can be run by a debating society. (70:5.5)
improvedDescription: A governing body or assembly that makes decisions through discussion and debate. Mankind has learned that neither peace nor war can effectively be managed by such deliberative groups, as they lack the decisive leadership necessary for effective action during critical times.

name: Deborah
originalDescription: Deborah was a ruler of the Hebrew people who managed to defeat Sisera at Taanaj (126:1.2) cruelty persisted in Deborah's reign, even though she was a woman, because after a victory she caused all the surrendered to die (70:1.17)
improvedDescription: A Hebrew judge and ruler who led her people to victory against Sisera at Taanach. Despite being a woman, she maintained the cruel practices of her time, ordering the death of all who surrendered after her military victories.

name: debt
originalDescription: Today, men are not social slaves, but thousands allow ambition to enslave them to debt. (69:8.10) early creditors controlled body after death (69:5.8) secret societies were employed by merchants to collect debts (70:7.18) governments may not incur debt in continental nation that is the most developed in a neighboring planet (72:7.3,7) Jesus gave two parables about debt: * the parable of the moneylender, about what is the greatest love (147:5.4) * the parable of the shrewd steward, on how one must show sagacity and friendship with eternal things to prepare for the future (169:2)
improvedDescription: Financial obligation or indebtedness. While modern humans are not social slaves, thousands allow ambition to enslave them to debt. In ancient times, creditors could control debtors even after death, and debt slavery was common. Jesus taught about debt through parables, including the story of the moneylender who forgave his debtors.

name: Decapolis
originalDescription: Amos the Kheresa lunatic did not stop until he had gone into all the cities of the Decapolis, declaring about Jesus (151:6.8) Jesus, his twelve apostles, and the twelve apostles of John, preached on various occasions in the Greco-Roman cities of the Decapolis, notably Scythopolis, Gerasa, Abila, Gadara, Pella, Philadelphia, and Heshbon (128:3.2) (144:7.1) (123:5.12) (124:3.6-9)
improvedDescription: A league of ten Greco-Roman cities located east of the Sea of Galilee and the Jordan River. Jesus and his apostles preached in several of these cities, including Scythopolis, Gerasa, Abila, Gadara, Pella, Philadelphia, and Heshbon. The healed demoniac Amos proclaimed Jesus's works throughout all the cities of the Decapolis.

name: Deccan
originalDescription: About 15,000 B.C. increasing population pressure throughout Turkestan and Iran occasioned the first really extensive Andite movement toward India, slowly moving southward into the Deccan (79:2.4-5) As the Salem missionaries penetrated southward into the Dravidian Deccan, they encountered an increasing caste system, the scheme of the Aryans to prevent loss of racial identity in the face of a rising tide of the secondary Sangik peoples. (94:2.1-2) The Aryans made very little racial impression on India except in the northern provinces. In the Deccan their influence was cultural and religious more than racial. (79:4.3) Hinduism became contaminated with a flood of degrading and debilitating cults and creeds from the Deccan (79:4.7) (94:2.5-6) Dravidian culture has survived in Deccan to modern times (79:3.6,8)
improvedDescription: The southern plateau region of India. Around 15,000 B.C., Andite peoples moved southward into the Deccan under population pressure from the north. The Salem missionaries encountered increasing caste systems as they penetrated into the Dravidian Deccan. Dravidian culture has survived in the Deccan to modern times, and the region became a source of inferior religious influences that contaminated Aryan beliefs.

name: December 25th
originalDescription: December 25th was the annual festival of Mithras celebrated, the most elaborate observance of all in that cult (98:5.4)
improvedDescription: The date of the annual festival of Mithras, representing the most elaborate observance in the Mithraic cult. This winter solstice celebration was central to Mithraic worship and religious practice.

name: decimal planets
originalDescription: the seven adjutant mind-spirits are relatively isolated on life-experiment planets (65:7.3) midwayers always appear in decimal or life-experiment planets (38:9.2) Life Carriers experiment with new patterns on decimal planets, new patterns of mechanical, chemical, and electrical mobilization at the time of their subsequent arrival with life transplantation and implantation mandates. (57:8.7-9) (58:0.1) On life-experiment worlds, life designs vary more than on other worlds. This happens in one world out of ten (36:2.15) small dark planets are most suited to life experiments (23:2.20) (49:5.11) Tabamantia is the inspector for experimental planets in Satania (49:5.12) Urantia is sixtieth decimal planet in Satania. In all Satania there are only sixty-one worlds similar to Urantia. The two outstanding features have been: (36:2.15) (49:5.11) (57:8.7-10) (58:0.1) (65:4.7-9) * the appearance of the Andonic race prior to the evolution of the six colored peoples * the simultaneous appearance of the Sangik mutants in a single family * Another outstanding variation of procedure was the late arrival of the Planetary Prince.
improvedDescription: Special experimental worlds where Life Carriers are permitted to undertake life experiments to modify or improve standard universe life forms. About one world in ten is designated as a decimal planet, including Urantia. These worlds feature unique variations in life patterns, and midwayers always appear on them. Urantia is the sixtieth decimal planet in Satania, distinguished by the early appearance of the Andonic race and the simultaneous emergence of the six colored Sangik races.

name: decisions
originalDescription: When man decides, and when he consummates this decision in action, man experiences (101:7.1) (117:5.13) Effort and decision are an essential part of the acquirement of survival values. (50:6.3) Man’s choosing between good and evil is influenced, not only by the keenness of his moral nature, but also by such influences as ignorance, immaturity, and delusion. (16:7.7) Man’s moral nature would be impotent without the art of measurement, the discrimination embodied in his ability to scrutinize meanings. (16:7.8) the very power of choice is the universe insignia of freewill creaturehood, that constitutes man’s greatest opportunity and his supreme cosmic responsibility. (112:5.5) upon the steadfastness and wisdom of decision-actions the Supreme Being depends for the actuality of experiential evolution. (112:5.5) “How long will you tarry in the valley of decision? the Father never compels any man to enter the kingdom. The invitation ever has been and always will be: Whosoever will, let him come and freely partake of the water of life (165:3.8) Jesús mortals celestial help result of decisions
improvedDescription: The act of choosing between alternatives, representing the universe insignia of freewill creaturehood. Decisions constitute humanity's greatest opportunity and supreme cosmic responsibility. When mortals make and consummate decisions in action, they gain experience that becomes part of their eternal character. Moral decisions, particularly choosing to do God's will, advance spiritual status and represent the keys to the kingdom of heaven.

name: Declaration of Liberty
improvedDescription: A document or manifesto associated with Lucifer's rebellion, likely referring to Lucifer's formal proclamation of his charges against universe government and his call for creature liberty from divine authority.

name: dedication, feast of
improvedDescription: A Jewish religious feast commemorating the dedication of the temple or altar, likely referring to Hanukkah or similar ceremonial observance.

name: dedication, spiritual
improvedDescription: The act of consecrating oneself wholly to God's will and service, representing a fundamental commitment in spiritual growth and religious experience.

name: deduction
improvedDescription: A logical process of reasoning from general principles to specific conclusions, representing one method of intellectual analysis and problem-solving.

name: deer
originalDescription: 800,000 years ago game was abundant. Deers were hunted by Neanderthalers (64:4.2)
improvedDescription: Abundant game animals that appeared during Earth's evolutionary history and were hunted by early humans including the Neanderthalers around 800,000 years ago.

name: default
improvedDescription: A failure to fulfill obligations or expectations, often associated with betrayal or falling short of one's duties and responsibilities.

name: defeat
originalDescription: When a man is faced with his own failures, how much he tends to blame others, very often those who least deserve it! (176:3.9) (193:4.7) If an ascending mortal should reach the central universe alone and, while traversing Havona, should fail in some phase of the Deity adventure, the pilgrim would be remanded to universes with seraphic guardian or Paradise Companion (25:8.9-11) (26:3.9) prudence and discretion, when carried too far, lead to cowardice and failure. (149:4.4) do not be troubled with doubts of failure or by perplexing confusion (34:7.8) Success may generate courage and promote confidence, but wisdom comes only from the experiences of adjustment to the results of one’s failures.  (160:4.14-16) before reaching Havona, ascendant children have learned to feast upon uncertainty, to fatten upon disappointment, to enthuse over apparent defeat, to invigorate in the presence of difficulties, to exhibit indomitable courage in the face of immensity, and to exercise unconquerable faith when confronted with the challenge of the inexplicable. (26:5.3) apparent failures in reality are simply unescapable delays. (26:8.4) friendship lessens sting of defeat. Difficulties, sorrow, disappointment, and defeat are more painful and disheartening when borne alone. (160:2.9) life will become a burden of existence unless we learn how to fail gracefully. There is an art in defeat which noble souls always acquire; we must know how to lose cheerfully (160:4.13) the times of great testing and threatened defeat are always times of great revelation. (195:9.3) Defeat is the true mirror in which you may honestly view your real self. (156:5.17) Faith produces a sublime trust in the goodness of God even in the face of bitter disappointment and crushing defeat. (101:3.6) Rise again Jesus and Judas
improvedDescription: A spiritual testing experience that, when properly understood, serves as an educational tool for acquiring wisdom and developing character. The noble soul learns to fail gracefully, viewing defeat as a mirror for honest self-examination and a stepping stone to higher spiritual achievement.

name: defectives
improvedDescription: Individuals with physical, mental, or developmental impairments, often grouped with other disadvantaged populations in discussions of social welfare and human improvement.

name: defilement
originalDescription: for the Jews one could incur defilement: * by contact with the dead, as in the case of Nazirite vows, which required purification (135:1.1) (135:2.1) * entering a Gentile building before Passover (185:0.3-4) There was also a Mosaic ordinance which directed that a mother, after the passing of a certain period of time, should present herself, or have someone make the proper sacrifice for her, at the temple for purification. (84:4.6) (122:9.1) (122:10.2) Greeks held the menstruating woman as one of the three great causes of defilement, the other two being pork and garlic. (84:4.8) For Jesus man is only defiled by that evil which may originate within the heart, and which finds expression in the words and deeds of such unholy persons. (153:3.5)
improvedDescription: Ceremonial or spiritual contamination according to religious law, particularly in Jewish tradition through contact with the dead, entering Gentile buildings, or other ritual impurities requiring purification ceremonies.

name: degeneracy
originalDescription: The great handicap confronting Urantia in the matter of attaining the high planetary destiny of light and life is embraced in the problems of disease, degeneracy, war, multicolored races, and multilingualism. (55:3.21)
improvedDescription: The deterioration or decline in quality, morality, or physical condition, identified as one of the major obstacles preventing Urantia from achieving its high planetary destiny of light and life.

name: degenerates
improvedDescription: Individuals who have declined from a higher to a lower state of physical, mental, or moral condition, often considered among society's disadvantaged populations.

name: Deity
originalDescription: Deity functions on personal, prepersonal, and superpersonal levels. Total Deity is functional on the following seven levels: (0:1.3-10,14) # Static # Potential # Associative # Creative # Evolutional # Supreme # Ultimate the Seven Master Spirits are hardly expressive of threefold Deity, they are the eternal portrayal of sevenfold Deity, the active and associative functions of the three ever-existent persons of Deity. (16:1.1-2) In considering the genesis of reality, we must bear in mind that all absolute reality is from eternity and is without beginning of existence. By absolute reality we must refer to the three existential persons of Deity, the Isle of Paradise, and the three Absolutes. These seven realities are co-ordinately eternal (105) Deity presence absolute only on upper Paradise (11:3.1) (56:9.7) description existential Deities experiential Deities Direct descendants from the Deities Nature of Deity Personalization of Deity Approach to the Deity
improvedDescription: The divine reality that functions on personal, prepersonal, and superpersonal levels across seven functional levels from static to ultimate. Deity may be existential (as in the Trinity persons), experiential (as in the Supreme Being), or associative (as in God the Sevenfold), and is characterized by unity on all supermaterial levels of reality.

name: Deity Absolute
originalDescription: definition description
improvedDescription: The causational, potentially personal possibilities of universal reality, representing the totality of all Deity potential. The Deity Absolute is the purposive qualifier of unqualified, absolute, and nondeity realities, existing as experiential in actualization but existential in potentiality.

name: Deity-trinitized Sons
originalDescription: Deity-trinitized Sons are a group of Trinitized Sons of God that are unrevealed (22:0.5)
improvedDescription: A group of Trinitized Sons of God that remain unrevealed in The Urantia Book narratives, forming one category of beings who have undergone the experience of trinitization.

name: Deity Ultimate
improvedDescription: A reference to God the Ultimate, the transcendental-experiential Deity functioning on the second level of unifying divinity expression as effective overcontroller of the master universe.

name: delay
originalDescription: Shortsighted and time-bound mortal minds should be slow to criticize the time delays of the farseeing and all-wise administrators of universe affairs. (54:6.8) On Paradise, disappointment is never regarded as defeat; postponement is never looked upon as disgrace; the apparent failures of time are never confused with the significant delays of eternity. (26:8.3-4) The mansonia life early teaches the young morontia pupils that postponement is in no sense avoidance. (48:5.8) wisdom of delay
improvedDescription: The wisdom of postponement in divine administration, where apparent failures of time are never confused with the significant delays of eternity. On Paradise, postponement is never regarded as disgrace, and shortsighted mortals should be slow to criticize the time delays of farseeing administrators.

name: delegation
originalDescription: Father Jesus
improvedDescription: The distribution of divine authority and responsibility by the Universal Father, who delegates everything possible to his Eternal Son and other beings. This reflects God's self-distributive nature and policy of profound self-distribution throughout creation.

name: delinquency
originalDescription: overindulgent parents encompass moral undoing of their thoughtless children (147:5.9) (177:2.2)
improvedDescription: The moral undoing that results when overindulgent parents conspire with foolishness to encompass the corruption of their thoughtless children. Jesus warned that the heavenly Father is not a foolishly indulgent parent who condones sin and forgives recklessness.

name: Deliverer
originalDescription: When a Paradise Son is bestowed upon such a world, completes his mission, ascends to the Universal Father, is accepted, and returns as the accredited deliverer of this isolated world (27:0.2) Warnings from Jesus: After I leave you, take heed that no man deceive you, for many will come as deliverers and will lead many astray. (176:1.1) Personas who acknowledged
improvedDescription: A title used to describe one who brings salvation or deliverance, particularly referring to Christ Michael as the accredited deliverer of Urantia following his bestowal mission. Jesus warned that many false deliverers would come to lead people astray.

name: dematerialization
originalDescription: on Jerusem there is a series of laboratories wherein the transport seraphim transform material beings into a state quite like that of the morontia order of existence. (46:5.26) While there is this dematerializing technique for preparing the Adams for transit from Jerusem to the evolutionary worlds, there is no equivalent method for taking them away from such worlds unless the entire planet is to be emptied, in which event emergency installation of the dematerialization technique is made for the entire salvable population. (51:2.3) Adam and Eve dematerialized on Jerusem, being seen off by their children, falling asleep and waking up when they were rematerialized on Urantia, ten days after their arrival (74:0.1) (74:1.5) the body of Jesus was dematerialized by archangels and other helpers through near-instantaneous dissolution (189:2.1-4) the body of Melchizedek was also dematerialized by his fellows (93:8.1)
improvedDescription: The technique used by transport seraphim to transform material beings into a morontia-like state for transport between worlds. This process was used for Adam and Eve's journey to Urantia and for the immediate dissolution of Jesus' physical body after his resurrection.

name: demigods
originalDescription: demigods were entities in Hindu theology (94:4.7)
improvedDescription: Supernatural beings in Hindu theology, classified among various spirit entities including supermen, semigods, heroes, demons, and other mythological figures worshipped in later-day cults.

name: democracy
