(159:3.8) (165:5.5) do not fear; do not be troubled with doubts of failure or by perplexing confusion, do not falter and question your status and standing, for in every dark hour, at every crossroad in the forward struggle, the Spirit of Truth will always speak, saying, “This is the way” (34:7.8) the messengers who brought the news of the resurrection of Jesus believed what David had told them, and even those who somewhat doubted, carried the message just as certainly and just as swiftly. (190:1.6) Belief may not be able to resist doubt and withstand fear, but faith is always triumphant over doubting, for faith is both positive and living. (101:0.3) (102:6.7) (103:8.4-5) (182:3.11) the first seven years of rebellion were times of heart searching (67:3.7) But honest doubts and sincere questionings are not sin; such attitudes merely spell delay in the progressive journey toward perfection attainment. (102:1.1) doubts inhibits free expression (196:0.11) doubts most stubbornly asserts itself just before crumbling (191:5.4) reference in 23rd Psalm: (48:6.9-19) * Though I go down into the valley of uncertainty or ascend up into the worlds of doubt, * I shall not doubt you nor fear you, whosoever has been born of the spirit has in himself the power to overcome all doubt, and this is the victory that overcomes all uncertainty, even your faith. (142:5.3) temptation to doubt vanishes on 3rd circuit of Havona (26:9.3) Jesus
improvedDescription: Doubt is an intellectual and emotional state that challenges faith and spiritual certainty. While confusional doubts and uncertainty cannot prevent spiritual survival, doubt represents the primary battle believers face against unbelief. Honest doubts and sincere questioning are not sinful but merely delay spiritual progress, while faith consistently triumphs over doubting through its positive and living nature.

name: doubters
improvedDescription: Individuals who question or challenge religious beliefs and spiritual certainties, particularly those who doubt believers' knowledge of God or challenge faith-based convictions with intellectual skepticism.

name: doves
originalDescription: Early in evolutionary religion the lamb became the typical sacrificial animal and the dove the symbol of peace and love. (85:3.4) Jesus said: “Behold I send you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves; be you therefore as wise as serpents and as harmless as doves”. (140:8.13) (140:9.3) (163:1.3) (178:1.7) Mary maintained dovecote on rooftop (123:4.4) In the house of Nazareth they had a small business selling doves that Jesus transferred to his brother Santiago (126:3.12) At one time the greedy priests went so far as to demand the equivalent of the value of a week’s labor for a pair of doves which should have been sold to the poor for a few pennies. (122:10.2) (173:1.2)
improvedDescription: Birds that became symbols of peace and love in evolutionary religion, used both as sacrificial animals and in commerce. Jesus used doves metaphorically when instructing his followers to be "as harmless as doves" while remaining "as wise as serpents." Mary maintained a dovecote in Nazareth, and temple merchants sold doves for religious sacrifices.

name: dowries
originalDescription: Many tribes allowed rulers to have sex relations with a bride just before she was to be given to her husband. The dowry consisted of the presents received in reward for her sex service in the bride’s exhibition hall. (82:3.10) At first, it was the custom for the man to go to his wife’s people, but in later times, after a man had paid or worked out the bride price, he could take his wife and children back to his own people. (84:2.5) Many of the better classes of women collected their dowries by temporary sex service in the temples, and most men preferred to have such women for wives. (89:7.5) As civilization progressed, fathers did not like to appear to sell their daughters and used presents instead of money. And upon the later discontinuance of payment for the bride, these presents became the bride’s dowry. (83:3) The idea of a dowry was to convey the impression of the bride’s independence, to suggest far removal from the times of slave wives and property companions. A man could not divorce a dowered wife without paying back the dowry in full. (83:3.4)
improvedDescription: Property or money brought by a bride to her husband upon marriage, evolving from earlier bride-price customs. The dowry system conveyed the impression of the bride's independence and provided legal protection, as a man could not divorce a dowered wife without paying back the dowry in full.

name: dragon
originalDescription: The Chinese worship of the dragon is a survival of the snake cults. (85:3.3) in Apostle John’s vision he saw a great red dragon. He wrote: “And his tail drew a third part of the stars of heaven and cast them down in darkness” (53:7.7) The dragon became the symbolic representation of all Lucifer and his rebel followers. Upon the triumph of Michael, “Gabriel came down from Salvington and bound the dragon (all the rebel leaders) for an age”. “There was war in heaven; Michael’s commander and his angels fought against the dragon”, Lucifer, Satan, and the apostate princes; “and the dragon and his rebellious angels fought but prevailed not” (53:1.6) (53:5.6)
improvedDescription: A symbolic representation used in religious contexts, particularly in Chinese culture as a survival of snake worship cults. In Apostle John's vision, the dragon symbolically represented Lucifer and his rebel followers, with references to the great red dragon whose tail "drew a third part of the stars of heaven and cast them down in darkness."

name: dragon flies
originalDescription: First dragon flies measured thirty inches across. (59:5.7)
improvedDescription: Large prehistoric insects that appeared early in evolutionary history, with ancient species measuring thirty inches across during the time when many insect species first developed and spread across the continents.

name: drama
originalDescription: Jesus never premeditated anything dramatic (138:6.5) (172:3.11) Religion enmothered much nonreligious culture as drama in the acting for spirit guidance. (92:3.6)
improvedDescription: A form of cultural expression that originated from religious practices, specifically from acting for spirit guidance. Jesus consistently avoided dramatic presentations in his ministry, preferring quiet and undramatic approaches to his Father's business despite the enormous dramatic possibilities of his mission.

name: draught of fishes
originalDescription: This episode in the life of Jesus, known as the miraculous catch of fish, occurred on a Friday morning when Jesus was teaching by the shore. The crowd pressed so close to him that he signaled to some fishermen in a nearby boat to come to his aid. He boarded the boat, which belonged to Simon Peter and had been built by Jesus himself, and continued teaching for over two hours. After finishing, Jesus suggested they go fishing. Despite having caught nothing all night, the fishermen followed Jesus' instructions and caught so many fish that their nets nearly broke. Amazed at the catch, David Zebedee, a man named Simon, and their companions left their nets and followed Jesus. Although not a miracle, Jesus' knowledge of fishing and the habits of the fish in the Sea of Galilee led to this remarkable event, which his followers always regarded as miraculous. (145:1)
improvedDescription: A remarkable fishing event when Jesus, after teaching from Simon Peter's boat, directed the fishermen to a location where they caught so many fish their nets nearly broke. Though regarded as miraculous by his followers, this event resulted from Jesus' superior knowledge of fishing and fish behavior in the Sea of Galilee.

name: Dravidians
originalDescription: the Dravidians are inhabitants of southern India who invaded the peninsula and absorbed the Aryan conquerors from the north (79:4.2) The blending of the Andite conquerors of India with the native stock eventually resulted in that mixed people which has been called Dravidian. These Andites were the so-called Dravidian and later Aryan conquerors of India (78:5.6) (79:3.1) The Dravidians were among the earliest peoples to build cities and to engage in an extensive export and import business, both by land and sea. (79:3.7) The Dravidian centers of culture were located in the river valleys, principally of the Indus and Ganges, and in the Deccan along the three great rivers flowing through the Eastern Ghats to the sea. (79:3.6) they founded a superior civilization 12,000 years ago (79:3.1,8)
improvedDescription: A mixed people of southern India resulting from the blending of Andite conquerors with native stock around 12,000 years ago. The Dravidians built cities, engaged in extensive trade, and founded a superior civilization with cultural centers in river valleys, though their Andite inheritance became progressively attenuated over time.

name: drawings
improvedDescription: Visual representations or artistic depictions created by drawing, though specific context and usage are not detailed in the available source material.

name: dreamers
originalDescription: Man is naturally a dreamer (99:7.5) We should not to be passive mystics or colorless ascetics; we should not become dreamers and drifters, supinely trusting in a fictitious Providence to provide even the necessities of life. (178:1.14)
improvedDescription: Humans who are naturally inclined to dream and imagine possibilities, but who must balance this trait with practical action and avoid becoming passive mystics who rely entirely on divine providence.

name: dreams
originalDescription: The absurdities of dreams not only testify to pressure of unexpressed emotions but also bear witness to the horrible distortion of the spiritual concepts presented by the Adjusters. (110:5.4) celestials seldom use dreams to communicate (86:5.11) human dreams are the disordered, disconnected parade of uncoordinated sleeping mind (44:4.7) (110:5.2-5) While we are asleep, the Adjusters try to register their creations in the higher levels of our material mind, and some of our grotesque dreams indicate their failure to make efficient contact. (110:5.2-5) interpretation of dreams particular dreams primitives’ dreams
improvedDescription: The disordered, disconnected parade of the uncoordinated sleeping mind that occurs during sleep. Dreams represent the Thought Adjuster's attempts to register spiritual concepts in the material mind, though most ordinary dreams are physiological and psychological phenomena rather than divine communications.

name: drifters
originalDescription: we must be a drifters supinely trusting in a fictitious Providence (178:1.14)
improvedDescription: Passive individuals who drift through life supinely trusting in a fictitious Providence to provide life's necessities, rather than taking active responsibility for their spiritual and material well-being.

name: drills
originalDescription: About 310 million years ago, there were many varieties of shelled animals, but their shells were not as necessary for defense as in later ages. Gastropods, including drilling univalves, periwinkles, and snails, were present in the ancient seas. Bivalve gastropods, such as mussels, clams, oysters, and scallops, have remained practically unchanged through the millions of years since that time. Shelled and valved organisms also evolved, with brachiopods living in these ancient seas in much the same form as today, equipped with hinges, teeth, and other protective devices. (59:2.12)
improvedDescription: Single-shelled gastropod mollusks that were present in ancient seas, along with periwinkles and snails, representing early forms of marine life with defensive shells.

name: drives, innate
improvedDescription: Natural, inborn motivational forces that compel behavior and action in living beings.

name: dropsy
originalDescription: Jesus healed man with dropsy on Sabbath (167:1.4-5)
improvedDescription: A medical condition characterized by abnormal accumulation of fluid in body tissues, which Jesus healed in a man on the Sabbath day at a Pharisee's dinner, demonstrating divine compassion over rigid religious law.

name: drought
originalDescription: a ever-increasing drought gradually brought about the great Andite exodus from the lands south and east of the Caspian Sea. (79:1.3-6) an increasing drought caused highland tribes to invade Euphrates valley (78:8.3) a great climatic change destroyed the civilization of the Sahara. Northern ice retreated and water-laden winds from the west shifted northward, turning the great open pasture regions of Sahara into barren desert. (80:1.4) (80:2.1) By 8000 B.C. the slowly increasing aridity of the highland regions of central Asia began to drive the Andites to the river bottoms and the seashores. This increasing drought not only drove them to the valleys of the Nile, Euphrates, Indus, and Yellow rivers, but it produced a new development in Andite civilization.  (79:1.2-3) By 3000 B.C. increasing aridity was driving these Andonites back into Turkestan and Europe (80:9.6) A severe drought meant death to the early agriculturists; weather control was the object of much ancient magic. (90:2.6)
improvedDescription: Periods of prolonged lack of rainfall that significantly influenced human migration patterns, particularly driving the Andites from central Asia to river valleys and causing major population movements throughout ancient civilizations.

name: drudgery
originalDescription: Labor is ennobling but drudgery is benumbing. (70:2.17) Man has most selfishly chosen the more agreeable work, leaving the routine drudgery to woman. (69:3.3) In the spiritual world there is no such thing as menial work; all service is sacred and exhilarating; neither do the higher orders of beings look down upon the lower orders of existence. (25:1.1) religion ennobles the commonplace drudgery of daily living. We should do oour work, therefore, from this time on, as for God.  (100:0.2) (133:4.8) (181:2.19) (192:2.13)
improvedDescription: Monotonous, routine work that can be spiritually benumbing, though religion has the power to ennoble even commonplace daily labor by transforming it into sacred service to God.

name: drugs
improvedDescription: Chemical substances that alter physical or mental states, often associated with intoxication and potentially harmful effects on consciousness and spiritual development.

name: drumlins
originalDescription: Drumlins are smooth mounds or undulations of the terrain created by the movement of glaciers. (61:7.1)
improvedDescription: Smooth mounds or gentle surface undulations of terrain created by the movement and action of glaciers during ice ages.

name: drunkenness
improvedDescription: A state of intoxication from alcohol or other substances that impairs judgment and behavior.

name: dual-origin beings
originalDescription: dual-origin beings are those of origin in any two of the Paradise Deities or otherwise created by any two beings of direct or indirect descent from the Paradise Deities. (30:1.29) creation of Havona Servitals, the joint creative work of the Seven Master Spirits and their associates, the Seven Supreme Power Directors, is a pattern for the long list of reproductions of the dual order in the evolutionary universes (25:1.2) they appear in the second group in Paradise classification of beings (30:1.29-54) orders
improvedDescription: Beings created through the collaboration of any two Paradise Deities or by any two beings of direct or indirect descent from the Paradise Deities. These beings appear in the second group of Paradise's classification system and include both descending orders like Creator Sons and ascending orders like Adjuster-fused mortals.

name: dual spiritism
originalDescription: The notion of two kinds of spirit ghosts made slow but sure progress throughout the world. (87:4.3-7) while this belief did enable man to reconcile the variables of chance with a concept of unchanging supermortal forces, this doctrine has ever since made it difficult for religionists to conceive of cosmic unity. (87:4.6) The concept of dual spiritism, good and bad spirits, gave rise to the later beliefs in white and black magic. (88:6.2) The dual-spiritism concept of good and bad forces offered man ample opportunity to attempt to pit one agency against another, for, if a powerful man could vanquish a weaker one, then certainly a strong spirit could dominate an inferior ghost. (87:6.13) And this belief later became involved in the superstition of good and bad ghosts, good and bad spirits. (69:3.5) young Jesus was long willing to accept the doctrine of good spirits and evil spirits as the possible explanation of mental and spiritual phenomena, but he very early became doubtful that such unseen influences were responsible for the physical happenings of the natural world. (123:3.3) Zoroaster was much affected by the prevalent concept of dual spiritism, the good and the bad, at the same time definitely exalted the idea of one eternal Deity and of the ultimate victory of light over darkness. (92:5.12) (95:6.5)
improvedDescription: A religious concept that evolved from early ghost beliefs, recognizing two kinds of spirits - good and bad - that influence earthly affairs. This belief system enabled humans to account for both good luck and bad luck while maintaining faith in consistent supernatural forces, though it made conceiving cosmic unity more difficult for religionists.

name: duality relationships
originalDescription: Duality relationships are relations existent between the I AM as sevenfold and the Seven Absolutes of Infinity. The dualities eternalize reality foundations. (105:4.5,8)
improvedDescription: Relations that exist between the I AM as sevenfold and the Seven Absolutes of Infinity. These dualities eternalize the foundations of reality in the eternal economy of universe administration.

name: ducks
originalDescription: The bird types of today—eagles, ducks, pigeons, and ostriches—all descended from the enormous reptiles of long, long ago. (65:2.9)
improvedDescription: Modern waterfowl that, along with eagles, pigeons, and ostriches, descended from enormous reptiles of the distant past during one of evolution's greatest leaps when reptiles became birds.

name: due process
originalDescription: Caligastia contested Moses’ passage to mansion worlds (52:5.5) While Lucifer was deprived of all administrative authority in Satania, there then existed no local universe power nor tribunal which could detain or destroy this wicked rebel; at that time Michael was not a sovereign ruler. (53:7.14) no way found to stop archrebels from attending Edentia councils after the bestowal of Michael on Urantia and his subsequent assumption of unlimited sovereignty throughout all Nebadon (43:4.7-9)
improvedDescription: Legal procedures and administrative protocols that can be suspended under extraordinary circumstances, as occurred during the Lucifer rebellion when normal passage of souls to mansion worlds was interrupted and when rebellious system sovereigns were eventually barred from Edentia councils.

name: dueling
originalDescription: Lynching and dueling represent the unwillingness of the individual to surrender private redress to the state. (70:10.16) Early wars were fought between tribes as a whole, but in later times, when two individuals in different tribes had a dispute, instead of both tribes fighting, the two disputants engaged in a duel. (70:1.19) Dueling is a modern survival of the trial by ordeal. (70:10.7)
improvedDescription: A formalized combat between two individuals to settle disputes, representing a modern survival of ancient trials by ordeal. Dueling evolved from early tribal warfare as a way to resolve conflicts between individuals from different tribes without involving entire groups.

name: duplication
originalDescription: In the eternal economy of universe administration, wasteful and needless repetition of function is never encountered; the Deities are not given to useless duplication of universe ministry. (6:4.2)
improvedDescription: Wasteful and needless repetition of function that is never encountered in the eternal economy of universe administration, as the Deities do not engage in useless duplication of universe ministry.

name: dust
originalDescription: when the dust that composes the mortal tabernacle “returns to the earth whence it came”; then, it is revealed, the indwelling “Spirit shall return to God who gave it.”  “Dust we are and to dust shall we return” (1:4.3) (68:6.1)
improvedDescription: The material substance from which mortal bodies are composed and to which they return at death, as referenced in the saying "Dust we are and to dust shall we return." This represents humanity's fundamental connection to the earth and the temporary nature of physical existence.

name: duty
originalDescription: the sum of duty according to Hinduism: Let no man do to another what would be repugnant to himself; cherish no malice, smite not him who smites you, conquer anger with mercy, and vanquish hate by benevolence. (131:4.6) The work of the Thought Adjuster constitutes the explanation of the translation of man’s primitive and evolutionary sense of duty into that higher and more certain faith in the eternal realities of revelation. (102:1.1) an inspired soul is that faithfully discharging the commonplace duties of routine mortal existence (91:7.9) (100:6.5) (126:5.4) (178:1.8) The satisfying joy of high duty is the eclipsing emotion of spiritual beings. (25:1.6) many peoples have failed in the recognition of high and holy obligations of a divine nature while giving meticulous attention to things of trifling importance (185:0.4) Sorrow cannot exist in the face of the consciousness of divine duty faithfully performed. (25:1.6) it is folly of running away from duty for far-off enticements (130:1.2) for ancient ghost believers duty was those things which must be done to keep the spirits favourably disposed, at least neutral. (87:5.11) We have been shown the way and now we must go forth to do our duty and be not weary in well doing. (163:6.7) The idea of duty signifies that you are servant-minded and hence are missing the mighty thrill of doing your service as a friend and for a friend. (180:1.6) in morontia worlds, time no longer available to circumvent disagreeable obligations (48:5.8) man’s understanding of duty is only relatively true (115:1.2) Moral intuition, the realization of duty, is a component of human mind endowment and is associated with the other inalienables of human nature: scientific curiosity and spiritual insight. (16:6.7) (16:7.1) we will learn to suffer less through sorrow and disappointment by making fewer personal plans concerning other personalities, and by accepting our lot when we have faithfully performed our duty. (48:6.36) we can rise above the lowly paths of toil and in human faithfulness performance of the duties of earthly tasks by discerning the lights of eternal life (34:6.13) promises should be kept (157:1.2) If any man wants to follow Jesus must take up responsibilities daily and follow him (158:7.5) The sense of evolutionary duty and the obligations consequent upon the illumination of revelation make such a profound impression upon man’s moral nature that he finally reaches that position of mind and that attitude of soul where he concludes that he has no right not to believe in God. (101:1.7) summary of mortal duty
improvedDescription: The moral obligation to fulfill one's responsibilities faithfully, whether to God, family, or society. True duty transcends mere obligation and becomes joyful service when performed with wholehearted devotion, representing humanity's evolutionary sense of moral responsibility elevated by spiritual revelation.

name: dwarfs
originalDescription: evolving monotheists keep some subordinate gods as dwarfs (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: In the evolution of religious thought, monotheists subordinate their lesser gods to various minor supernatural beings including spirits, demons, fairies, and dwarfs.

name: Dyaks
originalDescription: Dyaks are a modern tribe that only have developed primitive religious practices (92:6.1)
improvedDescription: A modern tribe that has evolved only the most primitive religious practices, representing an early stage of religious development.

name: Dyaus pitar
originalDescription: Vedic lord of heaven (94:1.3)
improvedDescription: The Vedic lord of heaven who served as part of the triune leadership of the Aryan pantheon alongside Indra and Agni.

name: Dyaus-Zeus
improvedDescription: A deity figure representing the connection between the Vedic Dyaus pitar and the Greek Zeus.

name: Ea
originalDescription: Mesopotamian god of the sea (95:1.4)
improvedDescription: A Mesopotamian god of the sea who was part of the Babylonian triad along with Bel and Anu, representing the gods of earth, sea, and sky.

name: eagles
originalDescription: eagles descended from enormous ancient reptiles (65:2.9) Quoted in Salem teachings: “Those who wait upon the Lord shall renew their strength; they shall mount up with wings as eagles” (97:7.8)
improvedDescription: Large birds of prey that descended from enormous ancient reptiles and are mentioned in Salem teachings as symbols of spiritual renewal and strength.

name: earning a living
originalDescription: Economic necessities tie man up with reality, and personal religious experience brings this same man face to face with the eternal realities of an ever-expanding and progressing cosmic citizenship. (99:7.5) men and women become confused in their efforts to understand themselves and to grapple with the manifold difficulties of making a living in a world so largely dominated by selfishness and sin. (133:3.7) personal earnings may be used as one sees fit (132:5.12,24) economic necessity is one of human sacred loyalties (196:0.7) Success requires not only devotion to one’s work but also that one should function as a part of some one of the channels of material wealth. (160:4.10) We are not to be passive mystics or colorless ascetics; we should not become dreamers and drifters, supinely trusting in a fictitious Providence to provide even the necessities of life. (178:1.14) Religion is the exclusively spiritual experience of the evolving immortal soul of the God-knowing man, but moral power and spiritual energy are mighty forces which may be utilized in dealing with difficult social situations and in solving intricate economic problems. (156:5.10) (160:4.1) It is the Father’s will that mortal man should work persistently and consistently toward the betterment of his estate on earth.  (148:5.3) (149:5.3) workers’ salaries in the most developed continental nation of a neighborhood planet depends on enterprose earnings (72:5.8-9) Jesus
improvedDescription: The practical necessity of providing for one's material needs through work and economic activity, which ties humans to reality while they simultaneously pursue spiritual growth and eternal goals.

name: earth
improvedDescription: The planet Earth, also known as Urantia in cosmic terminology.

name: earthquakes
originalDescription: the age of earthquakes happened from 1 billion to 650 million years ago (57:8.1-5,15-25) During the earlier ages of a new world, earthquakes are frequent, and they are all characterized by great physical disturbances; especially is this true of the gas-contraction spheres, the worlds born of the immense nebular rings which are left behind in the wake of the early condensation and contraction of certain individual suns. (41:10.4) the isthmus of Gibraltar, protecting the western basin of the Mediterranean, gave way as the result of an earthquake, quickly raising this inland lake to the level of the Atlantic Ocean.  (80:2.4) Earthquakes are caused by sliding and shifting of the solid outer crust and not by volcanoes. (58:5.4) For millions upon millions of years earthquakes have diminished, but Urantia still has an average of fifteen daily. (57:8.15) Remarkable events
improvedDescription: Seismic disturbances caused by sliding and shifting of the solid outer crust, not by volcanoes, which were frequent during Urantia's early geological periods but have diminished to an average of fifteen daily occurrences.

name: earthworms
originalDescription: Worms and leeches, closely related groups, appeared by evolution along with other primitive animals like starfish, sea urchins, and crustaceans. (65:2.5)
improvedDescription: Primitive animals that evolved along with other early life forms and represent stationary types that failed to progress in the evolutionary tree of life.

name: ease
originalDescription: To accept the guidance of a seraphim rarely means attaining a life of ease. (113:4.3) The animal nature and the lower forms of will creatures do not progress favorably in environmental ease. (154:2.5) One of the purposes of the morontia career is to effect the permanent eradication from the mortal survivors of such animal vestigial traits as procrastination, equivocation, insincerity, problem avoidance, unfairness, and ease seeking. (48:5.8)
improvedDescription: A state of comfort and lack of challenge that is generally counterproductive to spiritual growth and character development. According to The Urantia Book, accepting seraphic guidance rarely leads to a life of ease, and animal nature does not progress favorably in environmental ease. Ease seeking is identified as one of the animal vestigial traits that must be eradicated during the morontia career.

name: East Indies
originalDescription: Many Andites called Dravidians migrated to India and Central Asia reaching the East Indies (78:5.6) By 7,000 B.C. Andite Dravidian shipping was pushing coastwise across the Arabian Sea to the Sumerian cities of the Persian Gulf and was venturing on the waters of the Bay of Bengal as far as the East Indies. (79:3.7)
improvedDescription: A region in Southeast Asia reached by Andite-Dravidian peoples in their migrations and maritime ventures. By 7,000 B.C., Dravidian shipping was venturing across the Bay of Bengal as far as the East Indies, and small groups of Andites also made their way to this region from Central Asia.

name: Easter Island
originalDescription: Easter Island was long a religious and administrative center of one group of Andite-modified Polynesians (78:5.7)
improvedDescription: A Pacific island that served as a religious and administrative center for one group of Andite-modified Polynesians. It was part of a network of flourishing centers of civilization that grew up on now-submerged Pacific lands as a result of Andite penetration.

name: Eastern Ghats
originalDescription: The Eastern Ghats are home to three major rivers that flow through them towards the sea, supporting Dravidian cultural centers in the Deccan. (79:3.6)
improvedDescription: A mountain range in India through which three major rivers flow toward the sea. The Eastern Ghats supported important Dravidian cultural centers in the Deccan region, with settlements located along these river valleys.

name: eating
improvedDescription: The act of consuming food for nourishment and sustenance.

name: Ebal
originalDescription: Mount Ebal is a mount visible from Nazareth (126:1.2)
improvedDescription: Mount Ebal, a mountain visible from Nazareth that Jesus could view from the hill of Simeon. It was one of the geographical landmarks that Jesus would contemplate while recalling the historic and traditional events of his people.

name: Eber
originalDescription: Eber was an official of the Sanhedrin who was sent to arrest Jesus, ultimately refusing to do so (162:2.6-9) he met with Jesus in home of Nicodemus (164:2.1)
improvedDescription: An official of the Sanhedrin who was sent to arrest Jesus but refused to do so after hearing him teach. Eber defended Jesus before the Sanhedrin, saying he had never heard anyone speak like Jesus, and later attended a meeting of Jewish leaders who believed in Jesus' teachings at the home of Nicodemus.

name: eccentricity
originalDescription: Jesus free from (100:7.3-4) Jesus exhorted his hearers to strive for originality while they shunned all tendency toward eccentricity. He pleaded for sympathy without sentimentality, piety without sanctimoniousness. He taught reverence free from fear and superstition. (149:4.4)
improvedDescription: An undesirable tendency toward odd or unconventional behavior that Jesus consistently avoided and counseled against. While Jesus encouraged originality, he warned his followers to shun all tendencies toward eccentricity, maintaining perfect balance and propriety in all his actions and teachings.

name: Ecclesiastes
originalDescription: Ecclesiastes is a book of the Bible within the group called Wisdom Books as other Wisdom Books like Wisdom of Salomon, exemplifies Platonism and Stoicism (121:6.3) The frank pessimism of Ecclesiastes was a worldly wise reaction to these overoptimistic beliefs in Providence. (97:8.2)
improvedDescription: A biblical book that represents a frank pessimistic and worldly wise reaction to overoptimistic beliefs in Providence. As one of the Wisdom Books, Ecclesiastes exemplifies the influence of Platonism and Stoicism on Hebrew thought.

name: ecclesiasticism
originalDescription: Ecclesiasticism is at once and forever incompatible with that living faith, growing spirit, and firsthand experience of the faith-comrades of Jesus in the brotherhood of man in the spiritual association of the kingdom of heaven. (195:10.8)
improvedDescription: An organized religious system that is fundamentally incompatible with living faith and the spiritual brotherhood teachings of Jesus. Ecclesiasticism represents the institutional preservation of traditions that often becomes an obstacle to spiritual progress and the advancement of genuine gospel teachings.

name: echinoderms
originalDescription: Towards the end of the last Silurian submergence, echinoderms, particularly sea lilies, increased significantly, as evidenced by crinoid limestone deposits. Two groups of echinoderms developed particularly well and are considered guide fossils of this era. (59:3.11) (59:5.8)
improvedDescription: Marine animals that increased significantly towards the end of the Silurian period, particularly sea lilies, as evidenced by crinoid limestone deposits. Two groups of echinoderms became especially well developed and serve as guide fossils of this geological era.

name: eclipses
originalDescription: The early Nordics thought that eclipses were caused by a wolf that devoured a portion of the sun or moon. (85:3.4) the vanishing red man had a prophet within the past hundred years, the Shawnee Tenskwatawa, who predicted the eclipse of the sun in 1806 and denounced the vices of the white man. (90:2.9)
improvedDescription: Celestial phenomena where one heavenly body obscures another. Early Nordic peoples believed eclipses were caused by a wolf devouring portions of the sun or moon, while the Shawnee prophet Tenskwatawa accurately predicted a solar eclipse in 1806.

name: ecstasy
originalDescription: Genuine spiritual ecstasy is usually associated with great outward calmness and almost perfect emotional control. (91:7.3) Religious ecstasy is permissible when resulting from sane antecedents, but such experiences are more often the outgrowth of purely emotional influences than a manifestation of deep spiritual character. (91:7.3-4) The practical test of all these strange religious experiences of mysticism, ecstasy, and inspiration is to observe whether these phenomena cause an individual: (91:7.5-12) # To enjoy better and more complete physical health. # To function more efficiently and practically in his mental life. # More fully and joyfully to socialize his religious experience. # More completely to spiritualize his day-by-day living while faithfully discharging the commonplace duties of routine mortal existence. # To enhance his love for, and appreciation of, truth, beauty, and goodness. # To conserve currently recognized social, moral, ethical, and spiritual values. # To increase his spiritual insight—God-consciousness.
improvedDescription: Religious ecstasy is permissible when resulting from sane antecedents, but such experiences are more often emotional rather than spiritual manifestations. Genuine spiritual ecstasy is characterized by outward calmness and emotional control, and should be tested by whether it produces practical improvements in physical health, mental function, social relationships, and spiritual insight.

name: Eden, Garden of
improvedDescription: The Garden of Eden, the headquarters and dwelling place of Adam and Eve on Urantia, established as a center for planetary uplift and biological advancement.

name: Edenic league
originalDescription: Adam made a heroic and determined effort to establish a world government, but he met with stubborn resistance at every turn. Adam put in operation a system of group control throughout Eden and federated all of these companies into a Edenic league. (74:5.5)
improvedDescription: A federation of companies established by Adam throughout Eden, representing his system of group control and his attempt to create organized governance within the Garden.

name: Edenic teachings
improvedDescription: The spiritual and educational instruction provided by Adam and Eve and their followers in the Garden of Eden, aimed at uplifting human civilization.

name: Edenites
improvedDescription: The inhabitants and followers of Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden, including both the Adamic staff and those who lived under their influence.

name: Edentia
originalDescription: Edentia is the headquarters of Norlatiadek, our constellation (15:7.6) (15:14.6) (43:0.1-2) it is the seat of Constellation Fathers, the Most Highs of Norlatiadek (43:0.2) The construction of Salvington was immediately followed by the creation of the one hundred headquarters worlds of the projected constellations, as Edentia. (32:2.3) description education Administration mortal ascenders
improvedDescription: The headquarters world of the constellation Norlatiadek, seat of administration for the Most Highs and center of morontia culture. Edentia is surrounded by 770 architectural spheres and serves as the training ground for ascending mortals learning group ethics and socialization techniques.

name: Edomites
originalDescription: King David imposed a large tribute on neighboring tribes, which included the Edomites (97:9.14) they revolted against king Amaziah and were defeated in a signal victory (97:9.22)
improvedDescription: A neighboring tribe subjected to tribute by King David and later involved in conflicts with the kingdom of Judah. The Edomites revolted against King Amaziah but were defeated in a decisive victory.

name: Edrei
originalDescription: Edrei is one of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, the twelve evangelists and other evangelists during the second tour of the Decapolis, between August 18 and September 16, 29 AD. (159:0.2) At Edrei, where apostle Thomas and his associates preached, Jesus spent a day and a night and, in the course of the evening’s discussion, gave expression to the principles which should guide those who preach truth, and which should activate all who teach the gospel of the kingdom. (159:3.1) One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: A city in the Decapolis region visited by Jesus and his followers during their second tour and later during the Perea mission. At Edrei, Jesus spent time with Thomas and his associates, giving important teachings about principles for preaching truth and the gospel of the kingdom.

name: education
originalDescription: Real educational growth is indicated by enhancement of ideals, increased appreciation of values, new meanings of values, and augmented loyalty to supreme values. (100:1.3) The mortal career, the soul’s evolution, is not so much a probation as an education. (111:3.5) Unless a free people are educated—taught to think intelligently and plan wisely—freedom usually does more harm than good. (71:2.11) It is not so much what we learn in this first life; it is the experience of living this life that is important. (39:4.13) Education of public opinion is the only safe and true method of accelerating civilization (71:2.7) A personality can look before it leaps and can therefore learn from looking as well as from leaping. A nonpersonal animal ordinarily learns only by leaping. (16:7.2-3) Intellectual deficiency or educational poverty unavoidably handicaps higher religious attainment (102:3.1) spiritual progress dependent on mental expansion and unfailingly delayed by intellectual retardation. But this does not mean that spiritual evolution is dependent on education, culture, or wisdom (65:8.4) (101:2.15) (111:6.6) historically Jesus’ education Apostles’ education education in other planets educational method purpose of education universe schools warnings
improvedDescription: A comprehensive system of learning and development designed to foster wisdom, self-realization, and spiritual values. Education involves the enhancement of ideals, increased appreciation of values, and augmented loyalty to supreme principles. It serves as preparation for progressive universe service, with the entire cosmos functioning as one vast school for ascending beings.

name: education builders
originalDescription: education builders are one of the groups inside divine builders, that are part of celestial artisans (44:3.6)
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans who construct the headquarters for morontia training and advanced spirit learning. They ensure that ascending mortals have access to facilities for acquiring knowledge about their present and future work, as well as universal cultural knowledge designed to make them more intelligent and effective citizens of the morontia and spirit worlds.

name: effects
improvedDescription: The results or consequences that follow from causes in the universal system of causation and natural law.

name: efficiency
originalDescription: In the eternal economy of universe administration, wasteful and needless repetition of function is never encountered; the Deities are not given to useless duplication of universe ministry. (6:4.2) Health, mental efficiency, and happiness arise from the unification of physical systems, mind systems, and spirit systems. (100:4.3)
improvedDescription: The optimal functioning that arises from unified systems and purposeful coordination. In universe administration, the Deities maintain perfect efficiency without wasteful duplication of function, while personal efficiency comes from the harmonious integration of physical, mental, and spiritual systems.

name: effigies
originalDescription: Effigies were believed to be effective in magic. They were made and treated well or poorly to affect the real person they represented. (88:5.3)
improvedDescription: Images or representations of people believed to have magical properties in primitive cultures. These figures were made and treated well or poorly with the belief that the same effects would transfer to the actual person they represented.

name: effort
originalDescription: the ascending pilgrims discover what true study and real mental effort mean as they begin to discern the nature of the still-more-taxing and far-more-arduous spiritual exertion in Havona (26:7.1) Effort and decision are an essential part of the acquirement of survival values. (50:6.3) Loyalties are not exercised in behalf of the great, the good, the true, and the noble without a struggle. (100:4.2) Mankind does not ascend effortlessly in the universe, neither does the Supreme evolve without purposeful and intelligent action. (34:7.2) (117:4.7) Effort does not always produce joy, but there is no happiness without intelligent effort. (48:7.10) Father has predicated finite progress upon effort, creature achievement upon perseverance, and personality development upon faith (115:7.2) Recognition indicative of effort and attainment is granted to all, ascending mortal or ambitious cherubim, who complete these adventures in wisdom and truth. (20:8.3) All through the Paradise career, reward follows effort as the result of causes. (50:7.3) Self-importance, not work-importance, exhausts immature creatures; it is the self element that exhausts, not the effort to achieve. (48:6.37)
improvedDescription: The essential exertion and determination required for spiritual growth and universe ascension. While effort does not always produce joy, there is no happiness without intelligent effort, and meaningful progress depends upon purposeful action rather than passive waiting.

name: ego
improvedDescription: The self-centered aspect of personality that focuses on personal importance rather than service or truth-seeking.

name: egocentricity
originalDescription: While your religion is a matter of personal experience, it is most important that you should be exposed to the knowledge of a vast number of other religious experiences to the end that you may prevent your religious life from becoming egocentric—circumscribed, selfish, and unsocial. (103:1.3) Jesus was free of egocentricity. In a religious genius, strong spiritual faith so many times leads directly to disastrous fanaticism, to exaggeration of the religious ego, but it was not so with Jesus. (196:0.6)
improvedDescription: A self-centered orientation that makes religious life circumscribed, selfish, and unsocial. True spiritual growth requires exposure to diverse religious experiences to prevent one's faith from becoming egocentric and narrow.

name: egotism
improvedDescription: Excessive self-importance and self-centeredness that hinders spiritual development and meaningful relationships with others.

name: Egypt
originalDescription: history achievements migrations in Egypt political matters religion famous Egyptians fun facts
improvedDescription: An ancient civilization in the Nile valley that became a major center of culture and religious development after the decline of Mesopotamia. Egypt fostered a thoroughly blended type of religious philosophy, contributed significantly to moral and ethical development, and served as an important waystation for the spread of Melchizedek teachings throughout the ancient world.

name: Eightfold Path
originalDescription: Closely linked to the Buddhism doctrine of suffering and the escape therefrom was the philosophy of the Eightfold Path: right views, aspirations, speech, conduct, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and contemplation.  (94:8.8)
improvedDescription: A Buddhist philosophical concept consisting of eight elements: right views, aspirations, speech, conduct, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and contemplation. This path was closely linked to the Buddhist doctrine of suffering and the escape from it, designed to show mortals the futility of placing all hope in temporal goals while encouraging them to look beyond material associations to eternal realities.

name: El
originalDescription: composite Hebrew Deity que comprises several diffuse gods and other of the Bedouin nature gods (96:1.7)
improvedDescription: A composite Hebrew deity that comprised several diffuse gods and Bedouin nature gods. The term El evolved as devout believers attempted to worship various ideas of divinity, creating a practice of referring to this composite deity that included multiple Bedouin nature gods.

name: El Elyon
originalDescription: El Elyon was the Most High God preached by Melchizedek (93:2.1,3) (93:3.2) (93:4.2) (96:1.4,12) (142:3.4) The Egyptian king Akhenaten is the teacher who kept alive the doctrine of El Elyón, the One God, thus maintaining the philosophic monotheistic channel (95:5.2) Kenites believed in El Elyon (96:1.12) Moses became convinced his people would never fully comprehend El Elyon. Therefore he deliberately determined upon the compromise adoption of their tribal god Yahweh (96:4.3) Moses’ father-in-law was a Kenite worshiper of El Elyon, but the emancipator’s parents were believers in El Shaddai. (96:4.2)
improvedDescription: The Most High God preached by Melchizedek, representing the concept of one universal Deity. El Elyon was worshipped by various groups including the Kenites and was kept alive as a monotheistic doctrine by figures like the Egyptian king Akhenaten, though Moses eventually compromised by adopting the tribal god Yahweh when he realized his people would not fully comprehend El Elyon.

name: El Shaddai
originalDescription: El Shaddai represented divine Providence; the service rewarded with prosperity (96:1.5-6) It represented the Egyptian concept of God of heaven (142:3.5) Moses’ father-in-law was a Kenite worshiper of El Elyon, but the emancipator’s parents were believers in El Shaddai. (96:4.2)
improvedDescription: A concept of God representing divine Providence, where material prosperity was viewed as a reward for faithful service. El Shaddai was a composite idea derived from Egyptian teachings, Ikhnaton's doctrine, and Melchizedek's teachings, becoming thoroughly colored with Yahweh beliefs among the Hebrews and representing the Egyptian concept of the God of heaven.

name: Elam
originalDescription: Elam was the eastern Nodite headquarters (73:1.5) Enoch, the first son of Cain and Remona, was head of Nodites at Elam (76:2.9) The culture and commerce Nodite advocates migrated in large numbers eastward into Elam and there united with the mixed Sangik tribes. (77:4.4) Sethite priests moved eastward through the Elamite highlands to the Iranian plateau and Turkestan (78:6.3)
improvedDescription: The eastern Nodite headquarters located in highland regions just east of the Euphrates valley. Elam served as the center for Elamite Nodites led by Enoch, son of Cain, and later became a region where Nodite culture and commerce advocates united with mixed Sangik tribes.

name: Elamites
originalDescription: Elamites were a Nodite tribe from Elam. Enoc, son of Cain, was a leader. For hundreds of years the Elamites and the Adamites were at peace. (76:2.9)
improvedDescription: A Nodite tribe from Elam led by Enoch, son of Cain and Remona. The Elamites maintained peace with the Adamites for hundreds of years and eventually became largely Sangik in nature while maintaining a civilization superior to surrounding barbarians.

name: Elealah
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers during the Perea mission that lasted from January 3, 30 A.D. until Jesus's death.

name: elections
improvedDescription: The democratic process of selecting representatives or officials through voting, related to concepts of government and suffrage in organized societies.

name: electricity
originalDescription: The electronic charge creates an electric field; movement gives rise to an electric current; the current produces a magnetic field. (42:5.8) light and electricity are not the basic energies of space; they are secondary and subsidiary manifestations. (41:1.2) Light, heat, electricity, magnetism, chemism, energy, and matter are—in origin, nature, and destiny—one and the same thing, together with other material realities as yet undiscovered on Urantia. (42:4.1-2) Scientists may some day measure the energy, or force manifestations, of gravitation, light, and electricity, but these same scientists can never scientifically tell you what these universe phenomena are. (133:5.4)
improvedDescription: A secondary and subsidiary manifestation of energy that, along with light, heat, magnetism, and matter, represents the same fundamental reality in different forms. Electricity arises from electronic charge creating electric fields and electron movement producing electric current, which in turn generates magnetic fields.

name: electrons
originalDescription: Mutual attraction holds one hundred ultimatons together in the constitution of the electron; and there are never more nor less than one hundred ultimatons in a typical electron. (42:3.3) (42:4.6) (42:6.4-5) there are ten modified forms of the electron caused by losses of ultimatons (42:6.5) 35,000,000°F of heat, in association with certain gravity pressures, as the electronic boiling point. (41:7.4,12,13) Chemical behavior is wholly dependent on the activity of the freely revolving electrons. (42:7.6-10) Some of the reddish stars have a density at the center that has resulted in bringing the orbits of the basic material units closer and closer together until they now closely approach the status of electronic condensation (41:3.6) As the ultimatons aggregate into electrons, condensation occurs with a consequent storage of energy. (42:5.4) Orbital shifting of electrons results in the ejection or the absorption of very definite and uniform measurable particles of light-energy, while the individual electron always gives up a particle of light-energy when subjected to collision. (42:5.6) Heat is the measurement of electronic activity (42:4.5) The 30 innermost orbital electrons have individuality, but their energy systems tend to intermingle, extending from electron to electron and well-nigh from orbit to orbit. The last ten electrons are able to escape more or less freely from the control of the mother nucleus. (42:7.9) mesotrons disintegrate into electrons, releasing a lot of unexpected radioactive energy (42:8.5) In Orvonton it has never been possible naturally to assemble over one hundred orbital electrons in one atomic system. (42:7.7) electrons orbit at same relative distance from nucleus as planets around sun (42:7.1) the orbital velocities of electrons are beyond human imagination (42:7.3) Ultimatons, electrons, and other mass aggregations of energy are uniform particles of matter, and in their transit through space they really proceed in direct lines. (42:5.14) When an electron is suddenly stopped, the resultant electromagnetic commotion produces the X ray; the X ray is that disturbance. (42:5.8) When electrons pass from higher to lower energy levels of orbital revolution, quanta are always given off. (42:5.6) Each atom is a trifle over 1/100,000,000th of an inch in diameter, while an electron weighs a little more than 1/2,000th of the smallest atom, hydrogen (42:6.7-8) Physical conditions may be greatly altered because the electron spin is sometimes in the opposite direction from that of the grosser matter behavior, even in the same physical zone. (58:3.3) Atoms and electrons are subject to gravity. (41:9.2) (42:4.3) The power centers and their associates are much concerned in the work of transmuting the ultimaton into the circuits and revolutions of the electron.  (42:4.3) an X-ray-stimulated electron takes 500,000 years to reach sun’s surface (41:5.4) ultimatonic axial revolution determines the negative or positive reactions (42:6.6)
improvedDescription: Fundamental particles of matter composed of exactly one hundred ultimatons held together by mutual attraction. Electrons orbit atomic nuclei at velocities beyond human imagination and are responsible for all chemical behavior through their freely revolving activity, with their orbital shifts producing measurable light-energy particles.

name: elements (chemical)
originalDescription: The material of Havona consists of the organization of exactly 1,000 basic chemical elements and the balanced function of the seven forms of Havona energy. The local universes are of decimal construction. There are just 100 elements in a dual universe; that is the maximum possible organization of matter in Nebadon. These 100 forms of matter consist of a regular series in which from 1 to 100 electrons revolve around a central nucleus. (14:2.3) (42:7.4-7) The first 27 atoms, those containing 1 to 27 orbital electrons, are more predictable. From the 28th onwards we encounter more and more of the unpredictability of the supposed presence of the Unqualified Absolute. (42:7.10) above 90, electrons may escape more or less freely from the control of the mother nucleus (42:7.9) architectural spheres have 100 chemical elements, like evolved planets, plus 100 chemical elements of morontia material (48:1.3) All the architectural worlds abound in crystals and the so-called precious metals. (46:5.31) The heavier atoms are not found on the surface of many worlds. (42:7.5) the atomic world does display a certain periodic characterization which recurs in groups of seven—a birthmark carried by this material world indicative of its far-distant spiritual origin. (42:9.2-3) radioactive elements are brought in by meteors (57:7.3) transmutation of elements is the source of solar energy (41:7.5) (41:8.1) named
improvedDescription: Chemical elements are the basic building blocks of matter throughout the universe. Havona contains exactly 1,000 basic chemical elements, while local universes like Nebadon are limited to 100 distinguishable atomic forms consisting of 1 to 100 electrons orbiting a central nucleus. The first 27 atoms are more predictable, while those with 28 or more electrons show increasing unpredictability. Elements above 90 electrons become unstable as electrons can escape nuclear control. All architectural spheres contain 100 physical elements plus 100 forms of morontia material. The atomic world displays periodic characteristics recurring in groups of seven, reflecting its spiritual origin.

name: elements (natural)
originalDescription: The Andonites early developed a fear of the elements—thunder, lightning, rain, snow, hail, and ice. (63:6.3) The early Semites regarded everything as being indwelt by a spirit. There were spirits of fire, water, and air (96:1.1) early man was so impressed with the elemental disturbances that thunder was regarded as the voice of an angry god. The worship of fire and the fear of lightning were linked together and were widespread among many early groups. (85:4)
improvedDescription: Natural elements refer to the forces of nature—thunder, lightning, rain, snow, hail, ice, fire, water, and air—that early humans feared and often worshiped. The Andonites developed an early fear of these elemental forces, while the Semites regarded everything as being indwelt by spirits, including spirits of fire, water, and air. Primitive races venerated springs, worshiped rivers, and believed that moving waters contained spirit animation and supernatural power.

name: elephants
originalDescription: elephant was domesticated by 5000 B.C. through Mesopotamian, Turkestan, and Chinese farmers (81:2.12) blue man was successful in taming the elephant (66:5.5) It was easy to tame some animals, but like the elephant, many of them would not reproduce in captivity. (69:7.3) straight-tusked elephants entered Europe from Africa, over the Sicilian land bridge (64:4.7) Mice reproduce much more rapidly than elephants, yet elephants evolve more rapidly than mice. (49:1.6) 800,000 years ago, Neanderthals hunted elephants. (64:4.2) The huge elephants of older and subsequent periods possessed large brains as well as large bodies, and they soon overran the entire world except Australia. Elephants have greater emotional control than horses, albeit at the disadvantage of agility. However, of all the animals it is the most intelligent, and its intelligence is only surpassed by man. (61:3.6,10) though long departed, the massiveness of the passing Reptilia found echo in the elephant and mastodon (65:2.10) Miocene was age of horses and elephants (61:3)
improvedDescription: Elephants are among the most intelligent animals, surpassed only by humans, and possess greater emotional control than horses despite lacking agility. They were successfully domesticated by 5000 B.C. in Mesopotamia, Turkestan, and China, and the blue man was also successful in taming them, though they would not reproduce in captivity. The huge elephants of the Miocene period possessed large brains and bodies, enabling them to dominate the entire world except Australia. Straight-tusked elephants entered Europe from Africa via the Sicilian land bridge, and Neanderthals hunted elephants 800,000 years ago.

name: Eleusinian mysteries
originalDescription: The Eleusinian mysteries grew up within the Olympian pantheon, a Greek version of the worship of fertility; Dionysus nature worship flourished; the best of the cults was the Orphic brotherhood, whose moral preachments and promises of salvation made a great appeal to many. (98:2.10)
improvedDescription: The Eleusinian mysteries were Greek fertility worship cults that developed within the Olympian pantheon during the period when Mediterranean peoples sought personal salvation and gods who could hear their prayers. These mystery cults, along with Dionysus nature worship and the Orphic brotherhood, represented a departure from abstract Greek philosophy toward more emotional religious experiences promising salvation.

name: Elihu
originalDescription: Eliju was an extraordinary prophet of Ur and Salemite priest; he preached a hopeful message of salvation such as had not been heard in the Levantine world (96:7.7)
improvedDescription: Elihu was an extraordinary prophet of Ur and a Salemite priest who preached a hopeful message of salvation through divine grace and faith. He proclaimed that God would show mercy to the repentant, saying 'He shall pray to God and shall find favor with him and shall see his face with joy, for God will give to man divine righteousness.' His teachings represented the most ringing and cheering message of human salvation heard in the Levantine world since the times of Melchizedek.

name: Elijah Mark
originalDescription: Elijah Mark was the father of John Mark, the famous follower of Jesus; he was the husband of Mary Mark (182:0.1) his home in Jerusalem was a regular meeting place for believers (187:6.2) (188:3.2) (189:4.1,13) The last supper of Jesus and his apostles took place in an upper room of the house of Elijah Mark (179:1.2) Judas and the temple guards came to Elijah Mark's house looking for him (183:2.1) Some appearances of Jesus took place in the home of Elijah Mark (191:1.1) (193:3.1) he died suddenly on May 3, A.D. 30. (192:4.5-6) in the upper room of the house of Elijah Mark is where 120 believers gathered at Pentecost, after the departure of Jesus (193:6.1) (194:0.1)
improvedDescription: Elijah Mark was the father of John Mark and husband of Mary Mark, whose Jerusalem home served as a regular meeting place for Jesus' followers. The famous Last Supper took place in an upper room of his house, and Jesus made several post-resurrection appearances there. He died suddenly of a brain hemorrhage on May 3, A.D. 30, just hours before John Mark returned home. The upper room of his house later became the gathering place for 120 believers at Pentecost.

name: Elijah (Prophet)
originalDescription: Elijah was the prophet who tried to restore the concept of God in the northern Jewish kingdom (97:2.1) he was the prophet of Mount Carmel (135:1.4) he secluded himself for a time in solitude (136:3.3) denounced Ahab for the murder of the Naboths; he tore down altars of Baal and demolished idols (97:2.1) (97:9.19) (123:5.12) The Father spoke through Elijah and other prophets like him (155:6.2) Elijah turned Yahweh into Elohim; he turned an agrarian problem into a moral one; he was the one who initiated the passage from the polytheism of the many Baals to the monotheism of Yahweh (97:3.6) changed the Yahweh-Baal controversy from land to religion (97:3.6) currently he is one of the 24 Jerusem counselors, number 13 (45:4.15) Peter thought he saw Elijah in the transfiguration of Jesus, although the one he actually saw is Father Melchizedek of Nebadon (158:1.8) the secret writings of Elijah and other prophets are what began the Old Testament (97:9.20) he was a translated soul of brilliant spiritual achievement; that is, that he did not suffer the normal death (45:4.15) (97:2.2) John the Baptist
improvedDescription: Elijah was a fearless Hebrew prophet who restored the concept of God to the northern kingdom of Israel and transformed the Yahweh-Baal controversy from a land issue into a religious and moral matter. He denounced King Ahab for murdering Naboth, tore down altars of Baal, and demolished idols while advancing Hebrew monotheism. Under his influence, Yahweh became Elohim, and polytheism gave way to monotheism. He was a translated soul who never experienced normal death and currently serves as one of the 24 counselors on Jerusem. John the Baptist was greatly influenced by Elijah's example and methods.

name: Eliphaz
originalDescription: Eliphaz is one of Job's friends who exhorted Job to show fortitude with depressing ideas (148:6.4,9)
improvedDescription: Eliphaz was one of Job's three friends who offered misguided comfort during Job's suffering. He falsely taught that the righteous never suffer and the wicked never prosper, insisting that Job must deserve his punishment and should exhibit the same fortitude he had prescribed for others. His stern, accusing philosophy embodied the flawed concept of a God of injustice, providing no real comfort to Job's afflictions.

name: Elisha
originalDescription: Elisha was a faithful collaborator of the prophet Elijah (97:2.2)
improvedDescription: Elisha was Elijah's faithful associate and successor who continued the prophet's work after Elijah was called away. Working with the assistance of the little-known prophet Micaiah, Elisha kept the light of truth alive in Palestine and was known for performing wonderful works, including those at the village of Shunem.

name: Elizabeth (mother of John the Baptist)
originalDescription: Elizabeth was a distant cousin of Mary, the mother of Jesus (122:2.1,6) she was a descendant of Aaron's daughters (135:0.4) | daughters of Aaron Gabriel first appeared to Elizabeth to announce her pregnancy (122:2) Mary visited her while pregnant (122:2.6) Elizabeth believed that Jesus would become the Messiah (122:8.4) she received a visit from Jesus when he and his son were 5 years old (123:3.4) she kept John informed about world affairs (135:3.2) her husband Zacharias died in July of the year 12 (135:2) She died on August 17, 22 AD. (135:4)
improvedDescription: Elizabeth was the mother of John the Baptist and wife of the priest Zacharias. She was a descendant of Aaron's daughters and a distant cousin of Mary, mother of Jesus. Gabriel first appeared to her to announce her pregnancy, and Mary visited her while both were expecting. Elizabeth was well-educated for a woman of her time and helped strengthen Mary's faith in Gabriel's vision. She believed Jesus would become the Messiah and kept John informed about world affairs. She died on August 17, A.D. 22, when John was twenty-eight years old.

name: Elizabeth of Tiberias
originalDescription: Elizabeth of Tiberias was the daughter of a wealthy Jew; she was a member of the body of twelve female evangelists (150:1.1) she was present at the sixteenth appearance of Jesus (193:0)
improvedDescription: Elizabeth of Tiberias was the daughter of a wealthy Jew and one of the twelve women evangelists commissioned by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom. She was present at Jesus' sixteenth morontia appearance to the Jerusalem believers.

name: Ellanora
originalDescription: Elanora is the young mortal who took leadership of Panoptia in Lucifer's rebellion, preventing the Planetary Prince from leading that world into rebellion. (53:7.1)
improvedDescription: Ellanora was a young mortal woman who took leadership of the human races on Panoptia during Lucifer's rebellion, preventing the Planetary Prince from leading that world into rebellion and ensuring not a single soul enlisted under the Lucifer banner.

name: ellipse
originalDescription: Every known form of cosmic reality has the bend of the ages, the trend of the circle, the swing of the great ellipse. (11:8.2) (12:1.1) In form Paradise differs from the inhabited space bodies: it is not spherical. It is definitely ellipsoid (11:2.2) In heavens have long since discovered that the seven superuniverses traverse a great ellipse, a gigantic and elongated circle. (15:1.2) (41:9.2)
improvedDescription: The ellipse is a fundamental geometric pattern in cosmic reality, describing the curved path that all forms of basic energy follow around the space levels of the master universe. Paradise itself is ellipsoid in form, and the seven superuniverses traverse a great ellipse around the central universe.

name: Elman
originalDescription: Elman was the father of Nasanta, one of the twelve women of the body of women evangelists of Jesus (150:1.1) he was a physician; and he supervised the field hospital that the disciples set up in Bethsaida (148:0.1) (148:2)
improvedDescription: Elman was a Syrian physician who supervised the field hospital established at the seaside encampment near Bethsaida, organizing what could be regarded as the kingdom's first hospital. He was the father of Nasanta, one of the twelve women evangelists commissioned by Jesus.

name: Eloah
originalDescription: Eloah is the singular of Elohim, which is a plural meaning the “God of gods” (96:1.8)
improvedDescription: Eloah is the singular form of Elohim, representing the Trinity concept of God that was taught in Egypt by Sumerian-Chaldean groups based on traditions from the days of Adam and Melchizedek.

name: Elohim
originalDescription: Elohim means for the Jews the “God of gods” (169:4.8) Jesus used this term when he wanted to refer to the Deity as God, never Yahweh (169:4.5) the northern Jewish kingdom returned to the worship of Elohim, while the southern one returned to Yahweh (97:9.17) Elohim is the Trinity concept of God based on the Salem teachings (96:1.8) (104:1.8) (142:3.6) (169:4.5,8-10) under Elijah, Yahweh became Elohim (97:3.6)
improvedDescription: Elohim is the Hebrew plural term meaning 'God of gods' that represents the Trinity concept of three Gods in one, based on Salem teachings from the days of Melchizedek. Jesus accepted this concept and used this term when referring to the supreme group of beings as God, rather than using the tribal deity name Yahweh.

name: elopement
originalDescription: As raiding preceded trading, so marriage by capture preceded marriage by contract. Some women would connive at capture. This pseudo elopement was the transition stage between capture by force and subsequent courtship by charming. (83:2.2-4)
improvedDescription: Elopement in primitive societies was a pseudo-capture practice where some women would connive at being captured by younger men from other tribes to escape domination by older men of their own tribe. This practice served as a transition stage between marriage by capture and marriage by contract.

name: embalming
originalDescription: In time the Egyptians observed that dead bodies placed in brickless graves were preserved—embalmed—by the action of the soda-impregnated sand. These observations led the later practice of embalming the dead. (95:2.4-5)
improvedDescription: Embalming was a practice developed by the Egyptians after they observed that dead bodies placed in brickless graves were naturally preserved by the action of soda-impregnated sand. The Egyptians believed that preservation of the body facilitated one's passage through the future life.

name: emergencies
improvedDescription: Emergencies refer to critical situations or crises that require immediate attention and often extraordinary measures or interventions to resolve.

name: emergency council of Edentia
originalDescription: this council is made up of all the personalities that were assigned to Norlatiadek due to the Satania uprising, and is led by the director Most High (43:5.14)
improvedDescription: The emergency council of Edentia is composed of all personalities assigned to Norlatiadek because of the Satania uprising, presided over by a Vorondadek Son of extraordinary experience who serves as the Most High director.

name: emergent energy
improvedDescription: A type of energy that emerges during certain cosmic processes, related to ultimata and fundamental energy manifestations.

name: Emmaus
originalDescription: Emmaus is a town about 7 miles west of Jerusalem. There lived Cleopas and Jacob, two shepherd disciples to whom Jesus appears in his seventh morontia appearance (190:5.1)
improvedDescription: A town about seven miles west of Jerusalem where two brothers, Cleopas and Jacob, lived as shepherds. Jesus appeared to them in his seventh morontia appearance after his resurrection.

name: emotion designers
originalDescription: emotion designers are a group part of designers and embellishers inside of celestial artisans (44:6.5)
improvedDescription: A group of celestial artisans who enhance and conserve feelings, preserving the sentiments of morontia and the emotions of divinity for the study and inspiration of mortals and advancing spirits.

name: emotions
originalDescription: When it is not possible to worship God in the tabernacles of nature, men should do their best to provide houses of beauty, sanctuaries of appealing simplicity and artistic embellishment, so that the highest of human emotions may be aroused in association with the intellectual approach to spiritual communion with God. (167:6.6) While emotion as a factor in human decisions cannot be wholly eliminated, it should not be directly appealed to in the teachings of those who would advance the cause of the kingdom. (152:6.3) (159:3.2) (160:3.1) The absurdities of dream life not only testify to pressure of unexpressed emotions but also bear witness to the horrible distortion of the representations of the spiritual concepts presented by the Adjusters. (110:5.4) Emotional excitement is not the ideal spiritual stimulus. Excitement does not augment energy; it rather exhausts the powers of both mind and body. (160:3.1) Sometime the children of the kingdom will realize that strong feelings of emotion are not equivalent to the leadings of the divine spirit. (159:3.6) Many human reactions are mechanical in nature; much of life is machinelike. (118:8.2) evolution feelings Jesus particular emotions
improvedDescription: Human feelings that should not be the primary basis for spiritual decisions or religious teachings. While emotions cannot be wholly eliminated from human experience, emotional excitement is not the ideal spiritual stimulus and strong feelings are not equivalent to divine spirit leadings.

name: emperor worship
originalDescription: emperor worship, that deification of man as the symbol of the state, was very seriously resented by the Jews and the early Christians and led directly to the bitter persecutions of both churches by the Roman government. (121:5.4) The “national religions” are nothing more than a reversion to the early Roman emperor worship and to Shinto—worship of the state in the imperial family. (92:6.20) Pilate tried to regain this lost prestige an put on the walls the shields of the emperor, such as were commonly used in Caesar worship (185:1.3-4)
improvedDescription: The Roman practice of deifying the emperor as a symbol of the state, which was strongly resented by Jews and early Christians and led to bitter persecutions by the Roman government.

name: employment
improvedDescription: Work or labor activities that provide occupation and livelihood for individuals in society.

name: emptiness
originalDescription: Throughout all organized space there are gravity-responding energy currents, power circuits, and ultimatonic activities, as well as organizing electronic energies. Practically speaking, space is not empty. Emptiest space has 100 ultimatons per cubic inch (42:4.6) Emptiness does have its virtue, for it may become experientially filled. (117:2.8)
improvedDescription: A relative concept in space, as practically speaking space is not empty but contains gravity currents, power circuits, and ultimatonic activities. Even the emptiest space contains about one hundred ultimatons per cubic inch.

name: En-Gannim
originalDescription: A place where Jesus passed on his private tour of Palestine during AD 25. (134:7.5)
improvedDescription: A place in Palestine that Jesus passed through during his final private tour of the region in AD 25, located between Geba and Endor on his journey northward.

name: enchanters
improvedDescription: Practitioners of magical arts or supernatural rituals, often associated with early religious and shamanic practices.

name: encouragement
originalDescription: The divine Spirit is the source of continual ministry and encouragement to the children of men. (34:6.5)
improvedDescription: The divine Spirit serves as the source of continual ministry and encouragement to humanity, providing spiritual renewal and strength for the ascendant experience.

name: end
originalDescription: In the realms of knowledge, omniscience, we cannot distinguish between the First and Second Sources; like the Father, the Son knows all; he is never surprised by any universe event; he comprehends the end from the beginning. (6:4.7) From a circumscribed view there are, indeed, many ends, many terminations of activities, but from a larger viewpoint on a higher universe level, there are no endings, merely transitions from one phase of development to another. (115:3.17-18)
improvedDescription: A concept representing termination or conclusion. From a circumscribed view there are many ends and terminations of activities, but from a larger universal viewpoint, there are no true endings, merely transitions from one phase of development to another.

name: end of the world
originalDescription: taught the Jewish apocalyptists, Israel should take heart; the days of their affliction were almost over; the discipline of God’s chosen people was about finished; God’s patience with the gentile foreigners was about exhausted. The end of Roman rule was synonymous with the end of the age and, in a certain sense, with the end of the world. (135:5.2-5) For two and a half years John the Baptist lived at Engedi, and he persuaded most of the brotherhood that “the end of the age was at hand”; that “the kingdom of heaven was about to appear. (135:4.3) many followers of Jesus interpreted his predictions concerning the destruction of the temple in Jerusalem as the “end of the world”, the reappearing of the Messiah and the establishment of the New Jerusalem (176:1.6) (176:4.2) The downfall of nations, the crash of empires, the destruction of the unbelieving Jews, the end of an age, even the end of the world, what have these things to do with one who believes this gospel? (176:3.2)
improvedDescription: A concept taught by Jewish apocalyptists who believed that the end of Roman rule would be synonymous with the end of the age and the end of the world. Many of Jesus' followers interpreted his predictions about Jerusalem's destruction as referring to the end of the world and the Messiah's return.

name: Endantum
originalDescription: Endantum is the seat of the 5th constellation of Nebadon, just as Edentia is the seat of our constellation, the 70th. This is where Jesus, that is, Michael, made his sixth bestowal to his creatures in the form of a morontia mortal of evolutionary ascension (119:6.3,6)
improvedDescription: The headquarters world of the fifth constellation of Nebadon, where Michael of Nebadon made his sixth bestowal as a morontia mortal of evolutionary ascension status.

name: endogamy
improvedDescription: The practice of marriage within a specific social group or community, as opposed to marriage outside of it.

name: Endor
