(90:4.9) Humor functions to lessen the shock of the unexpected impact of fact or of truth, rigid unyielding fact and flexible ever-living truth. (48:4.18) A human being can find truth in his inner experience, but he needs a clear knowledge of facts to apply his personal discovery of truth to the ruthlessly practical demands of everyday life. (111:6.7) Facts never quarrel with real spiritual faith; theories may. (195:7.1) we should seek truth as well as fact, expand our soul as well as our mind. (48:6.32) One can be technically right as to fact and everlastingly wrong in the truth. (48:6.33) (143:6.1) (189:2.6)
improvedDescription: Facts are immutable realities that court full comprehension and scientific research, distinct from truth in that one can be technically right as to fact yet wrong in truth, and facts never quarrel with real spiritual faith though theories may.

name: faculty on dissemination and conservation of knowledge
improvedDescription: One of the ten councils of the Caligastia staff on Urantia, this faculty organized and directed the educational endeavors of early ages under the leadership of Fad.

name: Fad
originalDescription: Fad was the director of the faculty in charge of disseminating and preserving knowledge, one of the ten councils of the Caligastia staff on Urantia (66:5.9) Fad and 5 members of his council were opposed to the rebellion (67:4.1) Fad formulated the first 25-character alphabet and introduced a writing system (66:5.9) he sponsored a method of teaching by doing (66:7.6)
improvedDescription: Fad was the director of the faculty in charge of disseminating and preserving knowledge, one of the ten councils of the Caligastia staff on Urantia. He formulated the first 25-character alphabet, introduced a writing system, and sponsored a method of teaching by doing. Fad and five members of his council remained loyal during the Caligastia rebellion.

name: failure
improvedDescription: A concept representing unsuccessful outcomes or the inability to achieve desired goals or objectives.

name: fainting
originalDescription: ancients believed soul left body during fainting (86:5.5)
improvedDescription: A temporary loss of consciousness that ancients believed indicated the soul temporarily leaving the body.

name: Fair Havens
originalDescription: One thing happened on a visit to Fair Havens Jesus restrained a drunken degenerate was attacking a slave girl on the public highway.  (130:5.4)
improvedDescription: A location where Jesus intervened to protect a slave girl from assault by a drunken man, an act that left a lasting impression on his traveling companion Ganid.

name: fairies
originalDescription: Oriental and near-Oriental minds delight in fairy stories (122:8.7) The monotheists keep their subordinate gods as spirits, demons, fates, Nereids, fairies, brownies, dwarfs, banshees, and the evil eye. (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Mythical beings that Oriental and near-Oriental minds delighted in including in stories, and which monotheists kept as subordinate supernatural entities alongside spirits, demons, and other lesser deities.

name: fairness
originalDescription: being just is precondition to showing fairness (28:6.8) fairness considers underlying motives and racial tendencies (39:1.8) In all our praying be fair; do not expect God to love you more than others (91:4.3) Evidence is the basis of fairness, justice in harmony with mercy, and is supplied by the personalities of the Third Source and Center (10:6.3) in Havona requirements are fitting and inherently natural (14:5.3) defined as fairness with primitive man Jesus
improvedDescription: Justice in harmony with mercy that considers underlying motives and circumstances. True fairness requires first being just, then patient and kind, before showing genuine mercy.

name: faith
originalDescription: The relation between the creature and the Creator is a living experience, a dynamic religious faith, which is not subject to precise definition. To isolate part of life and call it religion is to disintegrate life and to distort religion. Belief may not be able to resist doubt and withstand fear, but faith is always triumphant over doubting, for faith is both positive and living. (102:6) faith should accept the fact of the presence of the indwelling Adjuster (108:6.8) Adjuster translates sense of duty into that higher and more certain faith in the eternal realities of revelation (102:1.1) faith and love grounded upon an early training makes faith dependable (177:2.2) Few persons live up to the faith which they really have. (48:7.4) the faith from beyond will not fail us (131:3.2) Havoners are strangers to saving by faith (3:5.17) the just shall live by faith (131:2.8) While there is a very definite limit to the province of the petitions of prayer, such limits do not equally apply to the faith of those who pray. (91:6.1) morontia and spirit reason will augment faith (103:7.2) Faith most willingly carries reason along as far as reason can go and then goes on with wisdom to the full philosophic limit; and then it dares to launch out upon the limitless and never-ending universe journey in the sole company of TRUTH. (103:9.7) our faith limits Father’s bestowal of spiritual gifts (166:4.11) planetary isolation affords opportunity to exercise faith (50:7.1) Father has predicated finite progress upon effort, creature achievement upon perseverance, and personality development upon faith (115:7.2) having faith what have faith in defined as faith and facts effects of faith historically Jesus’ faith (196) (100:7.7) parables regarding faith warnings regarding faith
improvedDescription: A living attribute of genuine religious experience that motivates life and shapes one's mode of living. Faith is the act of recognizing spiritual consciousness and represents man's trust in God's goodness and guidance, enabling entrance into the spiritual kingdom.

name: faith sons of God
originalDescription: In the fraternal realities of the kingdom of heaven the faith sons of God find final deliverance from the isolation of the self, both personal and planetary. (184:4.6) present-day mortals would experience less of this apparent warfare between the flesh and the spirit if they would enter the spirit kingdom, wherein the faith sons of God enjoy comparative deliverance from the slave-bondage of the flesh (34:7.6-7)
improvedDescription: Believers who have entered the spirit kingdom and found deliverance from personality isolation through faith. They experience comparative freedom from the bondage of the flesh through wholehearted devotion to doing the Father's will.

name: Faithful of Days of Edentia
originalDescription: The Faithful of Days is the representative of the Trinity of Paradise and Immanuel in Edentia the Mount Assembly of Edentia is the residence of the Faithful of Days (43:4.1) his residence on Edentia is comparatively modest relative to that of the ruling Vorondadeks (18:7.4) (43:4.4) he advised the Most Highs to allow Lucifer's rebels a free course of action (54:5.9) the Faithful of Days presented Michael's plan of salvation to Lucifer, but was rejected (53:2.5) ascending mortals meet on Edentia with the Faithful of Days, the first Trinity-born personality they know (43:9.1)
improvedDescription: The Paradise Trinity representative stationed on Edentia who serves as counselor to the Most Highs. He advised allowing Lucifer's rebels free course and presented Michael's salvation plan to Lucifer, though it was rejected.

name: faithfulness
originalDescription: Faithfulness is the unerring measure of human trustworthiness. faithfulness is the basis of Supreme Judge’s eternal verdict: “Well done, good and faithful servant; you have been faithful over a few essentials; you shall be made ruler over universe realities” (25:1.6) we must be faithful in little and in much (169:2.6) (171:8.11-13) (176:3.4)
improvedDescription: The unerring measure of human trustworthiness. Those who are faithful in little things will also be faithful in much, earning the eternal verdict of 'Well done, good and faithful servant.'

name: Faithfuls of Days
originalDescription: Faithfuls of Days are the Paradise advisers to the rulers of the one hundred constellations in each local universe. There are 70 million in existence (18:7.1) they are in constant communication with their order (18:7.3) (43:4.3) they appear in personality registers under triune-origen beigns in the Stationary Sons of the Trinity group (30:1.14) (30:2.29) they are instructed to retire after Michael’s completion of sovereignty but Michael requested them to remain forever on duty in Nebadon (119:8.1) They are Supreme Trinity Personalities (18:0.8) service
improvedDescription: Paradise Trinity advisors to the rulers of the one hundred constellations in each local universe. These seventy million Trinity-origin personalities serve as counselors to the Vorondadek constellation rulers and represent the final personal presence of Trinity Sons in local universes.

name: falcons
originalDescription: 30 million years ago, birds continued to develop on the planet with few major evolutionary changes. Most modern birds already existed, including gulls, herons, flamingos, vultures, hawks, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Birds of prey that existed among the majority of modern bird species that had developed by 30 million years ago, alongside eagles, hawks, owls, and other contemporary avian forms.

name: fall of man
improvedDescription: A theological concept referring to humanity's original transgression and subsequent spiritual decline, related to the doctrine of original sin.

name: fallacy of abstraction
originalDescription: Philosophers commit their gravest error when they are misled into the fallacy of abstraction, the practice of focusing the attention upon one aspect of reality and then of pronouncing such an isolated aspect to be the whole truth. (2:7.5)
improvedDescription: The philosophical error of focusing on only one aspect of reality and mistakenly proclaiming that isolated aspect to be the complete truth.

name: falsehood
originalDescription: falsehood provides opportunity to love truth (3:5.10) The shadow of a hair’s turning, premeditated for an untrue purpose, the slightest twisting or perversion of that which is principle—these constitute falseness. (48:6.33)
improvedDescription: Deliberate perversion of truth involving the premeditated twisting of principles for untrue purposes. It provides an opportunity to learn to love truth and differs from mere errors in narration technique.

name: familiarity
originalDescription: Familiarity breeds contempt; so, as the element of individual choice began to dominate mating, it became the custom to choose partners from outside the tribe. (82:5.6) If a prophet is not without honor save in his own country, he is not without understanding appreciation save in his own family. (138:0.2) (150:9.1)
improvedDescription: The principle that close acquaintance often leads to diminished respect or appreciation, as expressed in the saying that familiarity breeds contempt and a prophet is not without honor except in his own country.

name: family
originalDescription: The filial devotion and family loyalty exacted by the growing cult of ancestor worship insured the building up of superior family relationships and of enduring family groups (79:8.9-10) Human society would be greatly improved if the civilized races would more generally return to the family-council practices of the Andites. They did not maintain the patriarchal or autocratic form of family government. (84:7.29) The early family embraced a related working group, including the slaves, all living in one dwelling. (84:7.9) The growth of the family idea is incompatible with the roving and unsettled life of the hunter. (52:2.8) loyalty to and watchcare over family is the most sacred human trust (126:3.5) (127:2.8) (127:5.3) (140:8.14) (196:0.7) We are under moral obligation to represent the past generation in the honest transmittal of legitimate wealth to succeeding generations after subtracting a fair toll for the benefit of the present generation. (69:9.3) (132:5.15) The amazing stability and persistence of Chinese culture is a consequence of the paramount position accorded the family, for civilization is directly dependent on the effective functioning of the family; and in China the family attained a social importance, even a religious significance, approached by few other peoples. (79:8.9) The great threat against family life is the menacing rising tide of self-gratification, the modern pleasure mania. (84:8.1) When all of a family receive the gospel of the kingdom, truly peace abides in that house; but when some of the family enter the kingdom and others reject the gospel, such division can produce only sorrow and sadness. (142:2.2) (150:4.3) (165:6.3) Jesus matriarchal families patriarchal families nature of family family size value of families
improvedDescription: The fundamental unit of civilization and human society, serving as the master civilizer where children learn most of life's essentials. It provides the foundation for morality, loyalty, and the understanding of divine relationships through parental love and care.

name: Family Commission of Twelve
originalDescription: selected 3 families for Michael’s incarnation (122:0.2)
improvedDescription: A commission of twelve beings selected from higher orders of universe personalities who investigated Jewish family life and nominated three prospective families suitable for Michael's incarnation as Jesus.

name: family crests
originalDescription: Private property was early marked by family insignia, and this is the early origin of family crests. (69:9.13)
improvedDescription: Insignia used to mark private property in early times, representing the origin of family heraldic symbols and establishing ownership rights over land and possessions.

name: family of God
originalDescription: It is one of the terms that Jesus used as a synonym for the kingdom of heaven. (170:2.24)
improvedDescription: One of the terms Jesus used as a synonym for the kingdom of heaven, emphasizing the intimate relationship between God as Father and believers as his children.

name: famine
originalDescription: Jesus asked his disciples not be perturbed by famines or earthquakes; neither be concerned when they were delivered up to the civil authorities and were persecuted for the sake of the gospel. (176:1.1) hunger brought prodigal son of the parable to senses (169:1.8) Man was taught to provide for the hazards of famine, which periodically decimated the world. (66:5.3)
improvedDescription: Periods of extreme food scarcity that historically decimated populations. Jesus taught his disciples not to be troubled by famines, and the concept appears in parables illustrating spiritual hunger and awakening.

name: fanaticism
originalDescription: the life purpose must be jealously guarded from the temptation to seek for easy and transient attainment; likewise must it be so fostered as to become immune to the disastrous threats of fanaticism. (160:3.5) doctrinal fetish will lead mortal man to betray himself into the clutches of bigotry, fanaticism, superstition, intolerance, and the most atrocious of barbarous cruelties. (88:2.7) overmuch sympathy and pity may degenerate into serious emotional instability; that enthusiasm may drive on into fanaticism (149:4.3) strong spiritual faith so many times leads directly to disastrous fanaticism, to exaggeration of the religious ego (196:0.6-7) we must prevent religious fanaticism by scientific mental attitude (99:3.14) spiritual revelation often so blinds the creature as to precipitate a convulsion of fanaticism or to initiate some other intellectual upheaval which results disastrously. (110:4.5)
improvedDescription: Extreme and often destructive religious or ideological zeal that distorts balanced judgment and leads to bigotry, intolerance, and harmful behavior. It represents a dangerous deviation from healthy spiritual faith and must be prevented through balanced thinking.

name: fandors
improvedDescription: Large passenger birds used for transportation, capable of carrying riders across distances.

name: Fanoving
originalDescription: a neighboring local universe to Nebadon (32:2.12)
improvedDescription: A neighboring local universe to Nebadon, located in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton.

name: Fantad
originalDescription: leader of green race 350,000 years ago (45:4.9) (64:6.17) one of 24 counselors (45:4.9)
improvedDescription: Leader of the green race who delivered them from darkness and led their cultural revival 350,000 years ago, serving as one of the 24 counselors and promoting worship of "The One Source of Life."

name: farewell salutations
originalDescription: Jesus is not given to farewell ceremonies: * at the end of his first incarnation he took his leave suddenly and unceremoniously (119:5.3) * leaving Rome he did not say goodbye to any of his friends (133:0.1) * when he left Cana after the miracle of the wine he did not say goodbye to anyone (137:5.1) Melchizedek merely bid his human companions goodnight before disappearing from his sight forever (93:8.1) (93:10.2)
improvedDescription: Jesus characteristically avoided formal farewell ceremonies, often departing suddenly and unceremoniously without saying goodbye to friends or associates, as demonstrated when he left Rome, Cana, and during his incarnations.

name: farm
originalDescription: Jesus wanted to have a farm but he did not achieve that dream. Even so, they had at home three cows, four sheep, many chickens, a donkey, pigeons, and a dog. (126:5.10-11) Jesus’ mother’s brother had a farm on the Megiddo road near Sarid (123:1.7) (123:6.1) (124:1.2,11) Jude the Jesus brother ran away to his uncle’s farm (128:7.8)
improvedDescription: Jesus dreamed of owning a farm in the country where his family could enjoy greater freedom, but this aspiration was never realized, though they maintained livestock including cows, sheep, chickens, and other animals at their Nazareth home.

name: Faroes
originalDescription: Salem missionaries penetrated all Europe, even to the British Isles. One group went by way of the Faroes to the Andonites of Iceland, (93:7.2)
improvedDescription: Islands used as a route by Salem missionaries traveling to reach the Andonites of Iceland as part of their efforts to spread religious teachings throughout Europe.

name: fashion
originalDescription: fashion is one of human institution of self-gratification, growing out of vanity proclivities and pride emotions (69:1.5) the races never have been wholly liberated from the slavery of custom; fashion still unduly dominates Urantia. (66:6.2)
improvedDescription: One of humanity's institutions of self-gratification, arising from vanity and pride emotions, which continues to unduly dominate human civilization and represents a form of slavery to custom and tradition.

name: fasting
originalDescription: ancient ritual Isaiah Jesus
improvedDescription: An ancient religious practice of abstaining from food, often used as self-denial or purification ritual, though Jesus taught that fasting was not required in the gospel of the kingdom of heaven and could be spiritually dangerous when prolonged.

name: fat
originalDescription: The soul was generally thought of as being identified with the breath, but it was also located by various peoples in the head, hair, heart, liver, blood, and fat. (86:5.15)
improvedDescription: One of various bodily locations where ancient peoples believed the soul resided, along with the breath, head, hair, heart, liver, and blood, with animal fat consumption being taboo among some groups.

name: fatalism
originalDescription: The better Epicureans were not given to sensual excesses. At least this doctrine helped to deliver the Romans from a more deadly form of fatalism; it taught that men could do something to improve their terrestrial status. (121:4.2) Unbelieving materialists and fatalists can hope to enjoy only two kinds of peace and soul comfort: Either they must be stoics, with steadfast resolution determined to face the inevitable and to endure the worst; or they must be optimists, ever indulging that hope which springs eternal in the human breast, vainly longing for a peace which never really comes. (181:1.7) primitive religion prevented fatalism; they believed they could at least do something to influence fate. (86:7.3) Jesus’ teaching to trust in the overcare of the heavenly Father was not a blind and passive fatalism. (140:8.2)
improvedDescription: A philosophical belief in predetermined fate that Jesus' teachings opposed, as his instruction to trust in the heavenly Father's care was not blind or passive resignation but an active faith that empowered believers to influence their spiritual destiny.

name: Fate
originalDescription: the concept of Fate prevented evolution of Zeus in Greece to true monotheism (98:1.3)
improvedDescription: A Greek concept that prevented the evolution of Zeus worship into true monotheism, as the Greeks believed Fate controlled even the gods rather than Zeus being the ultimate arbiter of destiny.

name: fates
originalDescription: The monotheists keep their subordinate gods as spirits, demons, fates, Nereids, fairies, brownies, dwarfs, banshees, and the evil eye. (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Lesser supernatural beings that monotheists retain alongside spirits, demons, and other subordinate entities after progressing beyond polytheism to belief in one supreme deity.

name: Father-Absolute
improvedDescription: A designation for the Universal Father emphasizing his absolute nature and primacy as the First Source and Center.

name: Father-Brahma
originalDescription: Vedic source of all gods, a Vedic concept very similar to the One God preached at Salem (94:1.5)
improvedDescription: A Vedic concept of the source of all gods that was similar to the Salem missionaries' teaching of the one God of Melchizedek, though it remained ritualistic in nature.

name: Father fragments
improvedDescription: Divine fragments of the Universal Father that indwell mortal minds, also known as Thought Adjusters.

name: Father-fused mortals
improvedDescription: Ascending mortals who achieve eternal fusion with their indwelling Thought Adjusters, becoming one with a fragment of the Universal Father.

name: Father guides
originalDescription: secondary supernaphim of 3rd Havona circle. When the pilgrim soul attains the third circle of Havona, he comes under the tutelage of the Father guides, the older, highly skilled, and most experienced of the superaphic ministers.  (26:9)
improvedDescription: Secondary supernaphim who serve as tutors to pilgrim souls on the third circle of Havona, being the older, highly skilled, and most experienced superaphic ministers who maintain schools of wisdom and colleges of technique.

name: Father-Infinite
originalDescription: the Infinite-Father is the concept of the Infinite that denotes the primacy of the First Source-Center, the infinity of the absolute and unfettered free will of the Universal Father (0:3.24)
improvedDescription: A concept denoting the infinity and primacy of the Universal Father as the First Source and Center, representing the actual value-level of the absolute and unfettered free will of God.

name: Father Melchizedek
originalDescription: The Father Melchizedek is the original or firstborn Melchizedek Son, and in Nebadon there is one (33:6.2) (35:1.1) he is Gabriel's executive associate and chief executive of Gabriel Father Melchizedek is the one who assumes the position of Gabriel in his absence (33:6.2) (35:1.2) he never absents from Salvington if Gabriel is not there (35:1.2) he is always free to travel to other local universes in the interest of his order (35:1.4) he created the local universe Melchizedek Sons in a creative act in union with the Creator Son and the Mother Spirit (20:1.10) (35:1.1) he created the Spirongas in a creative act together with Gabriel (37:10.2) he directs the so-called stabilization corps of the local universe when all of it enters the stage of light and life (55:10.3) in the Paradise classification he belongs to beings of dual origin and descending orders (30:1.34) presides over emergency commissions and advisory bodies (35:1.2) represents Michael in the deliberations of the Life Carriers (36:2.12) there is a simple temple at the entrance of Father Melchizedek's house with an inscription recalling Michael's first incarnation as a Melchizedek Son (119:1.4) He was with Jesus and Gabriel on the Mount of Transfiguration, and the apostles who managed to see him mistook him for Elijah (158:1.6)
improvedDescription: The original and firstborn Melchizedek Son of Nebadon, created through the union of the Creator Son and Creative Spirit, who serves as Gabriel's executive associate and assumes his responsibilities during his absence from Salvington.

name: fatherhood of God
originalDescription: The brotherhood of men is founded on the fatherhood of God. (16:9.14) (52:6.7) (134:4.1) (159:5.7) (195:8.11) When all is said and done, the Father idea is still the highest human concept of God. (196:3.35) The Fatherhood of God and the brotherhood of man present the paradox of the part and the whole on the level of personality. (12:7.8) Fatherhood becomes, or may become, a universe reality to all moral creatures because the Father has himself bestowed personality upon all such beings and has encircuited them within the grasp of the universal personality circuit. (16:9.14)
improvedDescription: The fundamental concept that God is the divine Father of all moral creatures, which forms the basis for recognizing the brotherhood of all humanity and represents the highest human concept of deity.

name: Fatherlikeness
originalDescription: We will sometime realize that success in the quest of the Infinite is directly proportional to the achievement of Fatherlikeness, and that in this universe age the realities of the Father are revealed within the qualities of divinity. (106:9.11)
improvedDescription: The achievement of divine character qualities that resemble the Father's nature. Success in seeking the Infinite is directly proportional to achieving Fatherlikeness, which involves embodying the qualities of divinity by living the will of God.

name: fathers
originalDescription: Jesus stated that a true family is founded on the following seven facts: (142:7.5-12) # The fact of existence. The children take origin in the parents # Security and pleasure. True fathers take great pleasure in providing for the needs of their children. # Education and training. Wise fathers carefully plan for the education # Discipline and restraint. Farseeing fathers also make provision for the necessary discipline # Companionship and loyalty. The affectionate father holds intimate and loving intercourse with his children. # Love and mercy. A compassionate father is freely forgiving # Provision for the future. Temporal fathers like to leave an inheritance for their sons. we must call no man father in spiritual sense (175:1.10) Children need fathers as well as mothers, and fathers need this parental experience as much as do mothers. (47:1.6) The child must derive his first impressions of the universe from the mother’s care; he is wholly dependent on the earthly father for his first ideas of the heavenly Father. (177:2.5,7) disciples must love gospel more than his father or mother (150:4.3) (171:2.2) The earliest races gave little credit to the father, looking upon the child as coming altogether from the mother. (84:2.4) become the head of a human family is a highly educational and disciplinary responsibility (126:2.2) It is intended that mortals who start out from an inhabited world have the experience of recognizing seven fathers: (51:6.6-13) # The biologic father — the father in the flesh. # The father of the realm — the Planetary Adam. # The father of the spheres — the System Sovereign. # The Most High Father — the Constellation Father. # The universe Father — the Creator Son # The super-Fathers — the Ancients of Days # The spirit or Havona Father — the Universal Father Fathers are not like judges, enemies, or creditors. Real families are built upon tolerance, patience, and forgiveness. (142:7.11)
improvedDescription: According to Jesus, true fathers provide existence, security and pleasure, education and training, discipline and restraint, companionship and loyalty, love and mercy, and provision for the future. Children need fathers as well as mothers, and fathers need this parental experience equally. A child derives his first ideas of the heavenly Father from his earthly father.

name: Father’s business
originalDescription: Jesus referred on many occasions to his tasks as the Father's business (124:6.15) (126:3.5) (135:8.3)
improvedDescription: Jesus' divine mission and work as the Son of God on earth. The phrase was first spoken by a messenger from Salvington who told Jesus 'The hour has come. It is time that you began to be about your Father's business,' marking the beginning of his public ministry.

name: Father’s house
originalDescription: Jerusalem temple was called by Jesus “his Father’s house.” (125:1.1) (125:6.7) (171:4.7) Paradise is sometimes called “the Father’s House” since it is his eternal residence, and these seven zones are often designated “the Father’s Paradise mansions. (11:3.3) Jesus said that each of the many, many stations in his Father’s house, that is, the universe, is a stopping place, a life designed to prepare us for the next one ahead. (181:1.2)
improvedDescription: A term used by Jesus to refer to the Jerusalem temple and to Paradise as the Universal Father's eternal residence. Paradise's seven zones are called 'the Father's Paradise mansions,' and each station in the Father's universe is a stopping place designed to prepare mortals for the next stage of eternal progression.

name: Father’s prayer
originalDescription: Hap, the Planetary Prince assistant, taught Dalamatians the seven chants of worship and also gave them the daily praise-phrase and eventually taught them “the Father’s prayer,” which was: (66:5.14-15) “Father of all, whose Son we honor, look down upon us with favor. Deliver us from the fear of all save you. Make us a pleasure to our divine teachers and forever put truth on our lips. Deliver us from violence and anger; give us respect for our elders and that which belongs to our neighbors. Give us this season green pastures and fruitful flocks to gladden our hearts. We pray for the hastening of the coming of the promised uplifter, and we would do your will on this world as others do on worlds beyond”
improvedDescription: A prayer taught by Hap to the early Dalamatians that began: 'Father of all, whose Son we honor, look down upon us with favor. Deliver us from the fear of all save you. Make us a pleasure to our divine teachers and forever put truth on our lips.' It was among the first formal religious prayers given to humanity.

name: Father’s Way
originalDescription: The Father’s way is the exemplification of a farseeing ideal of creature life visualized by the high personalities of the Paradise administration of the universe of universes. (136:4.8) Hap presented the early races with a moral law in Dalamatia. This code was known as “The Father’s Way” and consisted of the following seven commands: (66:7.8-16) # No temerás ni servirás a ningún Dios, salvo al Padre de todos. # No desobedecerás al Hijo del Padre, el soberano del mundo, ni mostrarás falta de respeto por sus asociados superhumanos. # No mentirás cuando seas convocado ante los jueces del pueblo. # No matarás a hombres, mujeres o niños. # No robarás los bienes ni el ganado de tu prójimo. # No tocarás a la esposa de tu amigo. # No mostrarás falta de respeto por tus padres ni por los ancianos de la tribu.
improvedDescription: A moral code presented by Hap to the early races in Dalamatia, consisting of seven commands including honoring only the Father of all, obeying the Father's Son, speaking truth, not killing, not stealing, not committing adultery, and respecting parents and elders. This code exemplified an ideal of creature life visualized by Paradise administrators.

name: Father’s world
improvedDescription: A reference to the Universal Father's domain or sphere of influence, though specific details are not provided in the available context.

name: fatigue
originalDescription: fatigue can induce mystic state (100:5.10) Self-importance, not work-importance, exhausts immature creatures; it is the self element that exhausts, not the effort to achieve. (48:6.37) reversion directors in Jerusem are dedicated to giving themselves to the work of assisting their fellows in the achievement of thought change and mind rest, for such attitudes are most helpful in recuperating depleted energies. (48:4.9-10)
improvedDescription: Physical or mental exhaustion that can induce mystical states and affect spiritual progress. Self-importance, not work-importance, exhausts immature creatures. Reversion directors on Jerusem help beings achieve thought change and mind rest to recuperate depleted energies, as variety is restful while monotony wears and exhausts.

name: favoritism
originalDescription: Do not expect favoritism. In all our praying be fair; do not expect God to show partiality, to love you more than his other children, our friends, neighbors, even enemies. (91:4.3) God is no respecter of persons, either spiritual or material. (1:4.6) (2:3.1) (12:7.1,8) (40:10.13) (133:0.3) (137:8.6) (138:8.2) (140:3.16) (143:1.5) (148:6.2) (155:6.2) (156:2.4) (166:4.11) (175:2.1-3) (181:2.14) (192:2.2) Every son of God shares the fatherhood of God, and God loves each of his creature sons alike; he is no more a respecter of ascendant destinies than is he of the creatures who may attain such destinies. (40:10.5,13) Joseph and Mary were sometimes tempted to show some kind of special favoritism for Jesus. But his parents were wise and sagacious. The few times they exhibit any preference for him in any way, even in the slightest degree, Jesus was quick to refuse all such special consideration. (124:3.2) paternalism was one reason for Roman decline (195:3.9)
improvedDescription: Showing partiality or special preference. God is no respecter of persons, showing no favoritism based on spiritual or material status, loving all his children equally. Believers should not expect God to show partiality or love them more than others, and should avoid making distinctions based on race, social status, or other factors in their ministry.

name: fear
originalDescription: we must do not appeal to fear in proclaiming gospel (159:3.2,5) we must accept challenges without complaint and to face difficulties and uncertainties without fear (48:6.35) Second Isaiah proclaimed: “Fear you not, for I am with you; be not dismayed, for I am your God. ” (97:7.6) Fear not those who, although they may be able to kill the body, after that have no more power over us. (165:3.3) it is easy for us to become self-deceived and thereby fall into foolish fears, divers lusts, enslaving pleasures, malice, envy, and even vengeful hatred (143:2.5) fear persists in present-day races because we lack Adamic inheritance (76:4.6) seraphim cannot understand the legacy of animal fear that bulks so large in our mental life  (113:2.5) religion origin the cure for fear effect of fear historically nature of fear
improvedDescription: An emotion that has played a fundamental role in human evolution and religion, though it should be transcended through spiritual growth. While fear of the Lord can be the beginning of wisdom, mature religion replaces fear with love, reverence, and willing worship. Fear persists in present-day races due to the lack of Adamic inheritance and animal legacy, but faith can overcome fear and lead to spiritual freedom.

name: fests
originalDescription: all ancient ceremonies were terminated by feasting (70:3.7) Jesus preached the feast of spiritual goodness, not the sadness of fasting and the affliction of the soul (147:8)
improvedDescription: Ancient ceremonial gatherings that concluded with communal feasting, representing the social and celebratory aspects of religious and cultural rituals, as contrasted with Jesus's teaching about spiritual feasting over physical fasting.

name: feeble-minded
improvedDescription: Individuals with intellectual disabilities or mental deficiencies, often referenced in discussions of human diversity and social care.

name: feeding the 5000
originalDescription: the feeding of the five thousand and the popular movement to make Jesus king was the apex of the miracle-seeking, wonder-working expectance of the people (152:5.6) (152:6.5) At the feeding of the five thousand Jesus challenged the ideas of the material Messiah (153:1.2) this is the first and only nature miracle which Jesus performed as a result of his conscious preplanning. (152:2.10)
improvedDescription: The miraculous multiplication of food to feed five thousand people, which Jesus performed as his first and only consciously preplanned nature miracle, representing the apex of popular miracle-seeking expectations and marking a turning point in his ministry toward more spiritual teachings.

name: feelings
improvedDescription: Human emotional responses and inner experiences that influence behavior, relationships, and spiritual development.

name: feet
originalDescription: Jesus washed apostles’ feet during last supper (179:1.2,8) (179:3)
improvedDescription: The body parts that Jesus ceremonially washed for his apostles during the Last Supper, demonstrating humble service and teaching about spiritual cleansing and leadership through servanthood.

name: felines
improvedDescription: Cat-like animals representing one branch of mammalian evolution, including both domestic cats and wild cat species.

name: fellowship
improvedDescription: The bonds of companionship, mutual support, and shared purpose that unite individuals in friendship and common endeavors.

name: fellowship of believers
originalDescription: The fellowship of believers is the true institution that Jesus came to establish on Urantia, also called the brotherhood of the kingdom of heaven or the spiritual fellowship in eternal realities. Jesus delivered his apostles and his successors the keys of the outward kingdom, that is, the authority over things temporal, the social and economic features of this association of men and women. But not about the whole association. (157:4.5) Jesus declared that he who is not against us is with us. He warned against establishing unlimited administrative and jurisdictional control between groups of believers, which is just what happened with Christianity. (159:2.2) The true church—the Jesus brotherhood—is invisible, spiritual, and is characterized by unity, not necessarily by uniformity. (195:10.11) Jesus pledged full support for social and nonespiritual decissions of his apostles provided all agreed (144:6.3) Jesus used the term fellowship of believers as synonim for kingdom of heaven (170:2.24) this brotherhood of believers is destined to become a living organism in contrast to an institutionalized social organization. It may well utilize such social organizations, but it must not be supplanted by them. (195:10.11) The kingdoms of this world, being material, may often find it necessary to employ physical force in the execution of their laws and for the maintenance of order, but this does not nullify the right of social groups of believers to maintain order in their ranks and administer discipline upon unruly and unworthy members. (159:1.3) (178:1.2) no intellectual, social, and moral distinction should be made in the spiritual brotherhood of men when they come together to worship in the presence of God (133:0.3) there should be no place for sectarian rivalry, assertions of moral superiority, or spiritual infallibility (182:1.8) (195:10.14) Living associations, human families, social groups, or the Paradise Trinity are not augmented by mere arithmetical summation, is far in excess of the simple sum. (10:5.2) (68:1.4) (133:5.1) spirit gravity creates cohesiveness among believing persons, groups (7:1.6)
improvedDescription: The spiritual brotherhood of those who follow Jesus's teachings, characterized by unity rather than uniformity, representing the true invisible church that transcends institutional boundaries and emphasizes living faith over organizational structure.

name: ferns
originalDescription: 230 million years ago, the elevation of the continents proceeded, and the atmosphere was becoming enriched with oxygen. The earth was overspread by vast forests of ferns one hundred feet high and by peculiar trees without leaves. This was indeed the age of ferns, with luxurious vegetation covering the land. (59:4-5) (60:2.7) (60:4.5)
improvedDescription: Ancient leafless plants that dominated Earth's vegetation 230 million years ago, forming vast forests up to one hundred feet tall during the geological age of ferns, later being largely replaced by conifers and other modern trees.

name: fertility
originalDescription: One of the most ancient forms of the wedding ceremony was to have a priest bless the wedding bed to insure the fertility of the union. It was the custom of many Near Eastern peoples to throw grain upon the newlyweds; this was a magical rite which was supposed to insure fecundity. Certain Oriental peoples used rice for this purpose. Childlessness was greatly dreaded, and since barrenness was attributed to spirit machinations, efforts to insure fecundity also led to the association of marriage with certain magical or religious ceremonials. (83:4.4-9) primitive women desiring children embraced sturdy trees (85:2.3)
improvedDescription: The capacity for reproduction and childbearing, highly valued in ancient cultures where childlessness was greatly feared and various magical or religious ceremonies were performed to ensure fecundity in marriage unions.

name: fetishes
originalDescription: Belief in relics is an outgrowth of the ancient fetish cult. (88:2.1-2) Confucius made a new fetish out of order and established a respect for ancestral conduct that is still venerated by the Chinese at present day (94:6.9) the doctrine of spirit possession is nothing more nor less than fetishism. (88:0.1) to protect them water holes, wells, trees, crops, and honey were considered fetishes (69:9.12) Fetishism ran through all the primitive cults from the earliest belief in sacred stones, through idolatry, cannibalism, and nature worship, to totemism. (88:3.1) fetishes made early market places secure against theft (69:4.3) fetishes were many times mere symbols of the real object of worship. (85:1.3) The technique of religious ritual passed from the forms of the primitive cult through fetishes to magic and miracles (90:0.1) A fetish bag, a medicine bag, was a pouch containing a reputable assortment of ghost-impregnated articles, and the medicine man of old never allowed his bag, the symbol of his power, to touch the ground. (88:3.3) When plants and fruits became fetishes, they were taboo as food. Apple was a fetish fruit. (88:1.3) When animals became fetishes, there ensued the taboos on eating the flesh of the fetish animal. Fetish animals were dogs, apes, monkeys, snakes, birds, pigs and cows. (88:1.4-5) examples
improvedDescription: Objects believed to be inhabited by spirits or ghosts, ranging from sacred stones and animals to human body parts and natural phenomena. Fetishism represents one of humanity's earliest religious practices, evolving from simple belief in spirit-indwelt objects to complex systems of taboos and worship that influenced primitive cults, trade practices, and social customs.

name: feudalism
originalDescription: During the dark ages of feudalism on the European Middle Ages the territorial state collapsed, and there was a reversion to the small castle groups, the reappearance of the clan and tribal stages of development. (71:1.24)
improvedDescription: A medieval European governmental system characterized by small castle groups and clan-based organization that emerged during the dark ages when centralized territorial states collapsed, representing a regression to pre-state conditions of development.

name: fields
originalDescription: Said Jesus: Behold these people coming out from a Samaritan city to hear us; I tell you the fields are already white for the harvest. (138:2.1) (143:6.1)
improvedDescription: Jesus' metaphorical reference to people ready to receive spiritual teaching, declaring that "the fields are already white for the harvest" when speaking of the Samaritans who came to hear his message, indicating spiritual readiness for the gospel.

name: fig tree
originalDescription: the parable of the barren fig tree deals with the need to produce the fruits of the spirit (166:4.9) the parable of the tender branches of the fig tree speaks of knowing how to discern the spiritual times once the materialistic times have passed (176:2.6)
improvedDescription: A plant species that appeared during the Cretaceous period and serves as the subject of Jesus' parables about spiritual fruitfulness and recognizing the signs of changing dispensations, including the barren fig tree parable about producing fruits of the spirit.

name: fighting
originalDescription: Too often we engage in a fight merely to convince ourselves that we are not afraid. (160:3.4) antiquity Jesus
improvedDescription: Physical combat or conflict, which Jesus consistently avoided despite societal expectations. He taught principles of non-resistance while acknowledging that organized society has the right to employ force for justice, and emphasized that engaging in fights often stems from a need to prove one's courage rather than defend righteousness.

name: finaliter corps
improvedDescription: The organized body of perfected ascendant mortals and other beings who have completed the Paradise ascension and been mustered into eternal service, functioning as the highest order of evolutionary creatures in universe administration.

name: finaliters
originalDescription: finaliters are the high spirit personalities who have actually completed the Paradise ascension, and who have returned to the very worlds where we are beginning this long journey, as the pledge of assurance that we may and can complete the stupendous undertaking acting planetary chief of finaliters has the third seat in morontia temple, on the left of the Paradise Son seat (55:1.2) One or more companies of the mortal finaliters are constantly in service on Urantia. There is no domain of universe service to which they are not assigned; they function universally and with alternating and equal periods of assigned duty and free service. (31:0.11) Although the finaliters and their structures are not ordinarily perceptible to morontia vision, we will be more than thrilled when the energy transformers and the Morontia Power Supervisors enable us to glimpse them. (45:1.2) (47:1.3) when a local universe is establish in light and life the finaliter corps, for the first time, acknowledges the jurisdiction of an extra-Paradise authority, the supreme council. Heretofore the finaliters have recognized no supervision this side of Paradise. (55:10.7) Subsequent to our passage through the Paradise regime and after our admission to the Corps of the Finality, we are granted clearance for Sonarington since we are sons of God as well as ascenders—and we are even more. (13:2.6) The finaliters are invisible to mortals, as also is the Prince-Sovereign except when in the morontia temple. (55:6.6) (55:7.2) midsoniters are wards of finaliters (36:4.8) midwayers are contact personalities for finaliters in light and life stages up to the fourth; very few of them ever remain beyond the fourth stage of light. (55:4.10) admission destiny nature of finaliters residence service
improvedDescription: Perfected spirit personalities who have completed the Paradise ascension and been inducted into the Corps of the Finality. These glorified beings serve throughout the universes in various capacities, are invisible to mortals except in morontia temples, and represent the ultimate destiny for Adjuster-fused ascending mortals, though their final purpose remains largely unrevealed.

name: finaliters’ world
originalDescription: it is the transitional culture world with number 1 (45:1.2) (47:1) The Finaliter World is the headquarters of the finaliter corps of the local system and is surrounded by the receiving worlds, the seven mansion worlds, dedicated so fully to the scheme of mortal ascension.  (45:1.2) The finaliter world is accessible to the inhabitants of all seven mansion worlds. (45:1.2) The probation nursery of Satania is maintained by certain morontia personalities on the finaliters’ world, one half of the planet being devoted to this work of child rearing. (45:6.6-9) (47:2.1) This world now serves as the headquarters for more than one hundred thousand companies of finaliters, and there are one thousand glorified beings in each of these groups. (45:1.2) (47:0.2)
improvedDescription: The first transitional culture world of Jerusem, serving as headquarters for the finaliter corps of the local system. This sphere houses over one hundred thousand companies of finaliters and includes a probation nursery for children, while providing visualization experiences for mansion world inhabitants to glimpse their ultimate destiny.

name: fine arts
improvedDescription: Creative artistic expressions and cultural achievements that represent refined aesthetic accomplishments in civilized societies.

name: fines
originalDescription: an ancient advance was the imposition of fines for taboo violations, the provision of penalties. These fines constituted the first public revenue.  (70:10.12)
improvedDescription: Monetary or material penalties imposed for taboo violations and crimes, representing an early advancement in justice systems. These constituted the first public revenue and evolved from the practice of paying blood money as compensation for wrongs, eventually establishing standardized punishment through financial restitution.

name: fingernails
originalDescription: earliest magic had to do with parts of the human body that were looked upon as potential fetishes, particularly the hair and nails. The long-growing fingernails of the chiefs were highly prized, and the trimmings thereof were a powerful fetish. (88:1.8) (88:5.1)
improvedDescription: Parts of the human body considered potential fetishes in early magic, particularly valued when belonging to chiefs whose long-growing fingernails and their trimmings were regarded as powerful fetishes.

name: fingers
originalDescription: Modern man no longer attempts openly to coerce the spirits, though he still evinces a disposition to bargain with Deity. And he still swears, knocks on wood, crosses his fingers, and follows expectoration with some trite phrase; once it was a magical formula. (87:6.17) At one time it was really dangerous to point a finger at anyone; it is still regarded as ill-mannered to point. (90:3.7)
improvedDescription: Body parts associated with various superstitious practices, including crossing fingers as a modern vestige of ancient magical formulas and pointing fingers, which was once considered dangerous and is still regarded as ill-mannered.

name: finite reality
improvedDescription: The level of reality characterized by limitations and boundaries, as distinguished from infinite and absolute levels of reality.

name: finites
improvedDescription: Beings or entities characterized by limitations and boundaries, existing within the finite level of reality as contrasted with infinite or absolute beings.

name: Finns
originalDescription: The Finns believed that most trees were occupied by kind spirits. (85:2.4)
improvedDescription: A Nordic people who believed that most trees were occupied by kind spirits, distinguishing them from other cultures that viewed tree spirits as cruel or deceptive.

name: fire
originalDescription: Andon, fire discoverer benefits and dangers ancient mores Jesus Persians
improvedDescription: A fundamental discovery made by Andon that forever separated humans from animals, serving as the first great civilizer by providing protection, light, heat, and enabling social intercourse, while also becoming an object of worship and superstition among many primitive peoples.

name: fire god
improvedDescription: A deity concept associated with fire worship, where fire itself was venerated as a divine being rather than merely as a symbol of spiritual purification or divine presence.

name: first
originalDescription: Of a truth it is written: “I am the first, and I am the last, and beside me there is no God. (10:7.1) (97:7.6-9) But many who are first shall be last, while the last shall often be first. (163:3.4) (166:3.5)
improvedDescription: A divine attribute expressing God's eternal primacy and supremacy, as declared in scripture: 'I am the first, and I am the last, and beside me there is no God,' while also referring to the paradoxical teaching that many who are first shall be last, and the last shall often be first.

name: first-born
originalDescription: in antiquity it was custom sacrificing the first-born. A petty king in Palestine, in building the walls of Jericho, “laid the foundation thereof in Abiram, his first-born, and set up the gates thereof in his youngest son, Segub” (89:6.6,8) Moses attempted to end human sacrifices by inaugurating the ransom as a substitute. Even after most groups had ceased the ritual killing of children, it was the custom to put an infant away by itself, off in the wilderness or in a little boat on the water. (89:7.1,3)
improvedDescription: The eldest child who in ancient times was often subject to ritual sacrifice during foundation ceremonies or times of great stress, a practice that Moses attempted to end by establishing a ransom system as substitute.

name: First Cause
originalDescription: Anaxagoras, the mechanistic philosopher, recognized the existence of a First Cause (98:2.6) assignment of causes leads to confession that ultimate factors are ignored and to a great first cause (195:6.5) true religion turns the Universal First Cause of logic into the personal God of human salvation (196:3.1) there is only one uncaused Cause in the whole universe (118:6.2) reason may lead to a First Cause but religious faith is needed to transform the First Cause into a God of salvation (101:2.3,7)
improvedDescription: The ultimate originating source of all existence, recognized by philosophers like Anaxagoras as an Initial Mind, but requiring religious faith to transform this logical First Cause into the personal God of salvation—the Universal Father of Paradise.

name: First Epistle of Peter
originalDescription: the vigorous style of apostle Peter was better shown in his letter known as the First Epistle of Peter; at least this was true before it was subsequently altered by a disciple of Paul. (139:2.12)
improvedDescription: A letter written by the apostle Peter that demonstrated his vigorous style and teaching, though it was subsequently altered by a disciple of Paul.

name: first experiential Trinity
improvedDescription: The first of the experiential Trinities, related to the Trinity Ultimate in the cosmic evolution of deity relationships.

name: first mansion world
improvedDescription: The initial world in the mansion world progression where mortal survivors begin their morontia career after death.

name: First Person of Deity
improvedDescription: The Universal Father as the first person of the Paradise Trinity and original source of all personality.

name: First Source and Center
improvedDescription: The Universal Father as the absolute source and eternal center of all reality, energy, and personality in the universe of universes.

name: fish
originalDescription: Only fourteen phyla have appeared on Urantia, the fishes being the last, and no new classes have developed since birds and mammals. (65:2.11) the greatest event of all was the sudden appearance of the fish family. (59:4) (59:5.8) (65:2.5-6) 110,000,000 years ago  marked changes occurred in the fish family, a sturgeon type first appearing, but the ferocious sea serpents, descended from the land reptiles, still infested all the seas, and they threatened the destruction of the entire fish family. (60:2.9) the Neanderthalers made holes in the ice covering the northern rivers and thus were able to spear the fish which came up to these vents (64:4.4) early units of barter were fishes or goats (69:4.5) in Jesus’ ministry
improvedDescription: Marine vertebrates that appeared as the last of fourteen phyla on Urantia and represent the first backboned animals, from which evolved frogs, salamanders, and ultimately the evolutionary line leading to humans.

name: fishermen
originalDescription: The blue men became expert hunters and trappers; by fencing the rivers they caught fish in great numbers, drying the surplus for winter use. (68:5.5) Jesus’ uncle in Magdala was a fisherman (123:6.1) (124:3.1) Jesus was expert fisherman (124:1.2) (124:2.7) (138:7.6) (192:1.7) Jude the Jesus' brother ran away to become fisherman (128:7.8) apostles were fishermen most of them  (138:7.5) (139:1.1) (139:3.1) (139:10.2) (192:1) Jesus enjoined apostles to devote themselves to fishing for two weeks, adding, “And then will you go forth to become fishers of men. (138:7.6)
improvedDescription: Those who catch fish for livelihood, including many of Jesus' apostles such as Peter, Andrew, James, and John, who were partners in the fishing business at Bethsaida before becoming 'fishers of men.'

name: flags
originalDescription: Totems were at one and the same time symbols of the group and their god. The totem eventually evolved into the flag, or national symbol, of the various modern peoples. (88:3.2-4)
improvedDescription: National symbols that evolved from ancient totems, serving as emblems of group identity and consciousness, with civilized peoples ensuring they never touch the ground as symbols of respect.

name: flamingoes
originalDescription: 30 million years ago, the bird life of the planet continued to develop, but with few important evolutionary changes. The majority of modern birds were existent, including gulls, herons, flamingoes, buzzards, falcons, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Large wading birds that were among the majority of modern bird species that existed 30 million years ago during a period of continued avian development.

name: flattery
originalDescription: Jesus advised not weary in well doing; and he warned against the slippery paths of flattery and the poisoned darts of ridicule. (174:0.2) Influenced by flattery, enthusiasm, and great personal persuasion of Cano, Eve then and there consented to embark upon the much-discussed enterprise, to add her own little scheme of world saving to the larger and more far-reaching divine plan. (75:3.9)
improvedDescription: Insincere praise that Jesus warned against as a slippery path, exemplified when Eve was influenced by Cano's flattery to participate in the scheme that led to the Adamic default.

name: Flavius
originalDescription: Flavius was a Greek Jew who had his home in Jerusalem extensively adorned with works of art. Jesus was repeatedly in this house (142:0.1) (142:4.1) The sixth appearance of Jesus was in the house of Flavius to about 40 Greek believers (190:4)
improvedDescription: Flavius was a Greek Jew and proselyte of the gate who owned a beautiful home in Jerusalem adorned with priceless art treasures from his world travels. Jesus stayed at his home during visits to Jerusalem, and prominent Jews would come there secretly to interview the Master. It was also the location of Jesus' sixth morontia appearance to about forty Greek believers after his resurrection.

name: flaws
originalDescription: It requires no great depth of intellect to pick flaws, ask questions, or raise objections. (102:7.6)
improvedDescription: It requires no great depth of intellect to pick flaws, ask questions, or raise objections, but it does require brilliance of mind to answer these questions and solve difficulties; faith certainty is the greatest technique for dealing with such superficial contentions.

name: flesh
originalDescription: Amenemope taught that “riches take themselves wings and fly away”—that all things earthly are evanescent. (95:4.3) be not discouraged by discovery you are human (156:5.8) Poverty was a part of the ritual of the mortification of the flesh which, unfortunately, became incorporated into the writings and teachings of many religions, notably Christianity. (89:3.2-3) one of the difficulties of an Adjuster is unending conflict between the high purposes of a great mind antagonized by the urge of a primitive inheritance (111:7.5) The flesh, the inherent nature derived from the animal-origin races, does not naturally bear the fruits of the divine Spirit. (34:7.1) it is the spirit that quickens; the flesh and all that pertains thereto is of little profit (153:5.4) Jesus faith was so real and all-encompassing that it absolutely swept away any spiritual doubts and effectively destroyed every conflicting desire. (196:0.5) do not to put our trust in the uncertainties of the flesh nor in the frailties of man’s defense against the trials and testing which lie ahead of us (171:4.2) (174:0.2) For Jesus the greatest meat was doing the will of God and to accomplish His Work (143:6.1) flesh and spirit Buddhism urges | sex:urge
improvedDescription: The flesh represents the inherent nature derived from animal-origin races that does not naturally bear the fruits of the divine Spirit. It encompasses the physical body, material appetites, and primitive instincts that often conflict with spiritual aspirations in mortal life.

name: flint
originalDescription: Andon and the Andonites made sticks with sharp pieces of flint tied together, which served them well as a defensive weapon. (63:1.3) (68:5.4) Andon conceived idea of making fire at a flint deposit (63:2.4-7) Andonites traveled far and wide in search of flint, much as present-day humans journey to the ends of the earth in quest of gold, platinum, and diamonds. (63:5.6) Cro-Magnons worked deposits of flint (80:3.5) Many of the earlier wars were fought over natural deposits, such as flint, salt, and metals. (69:4.6) The early specialists in industry were the flint flakers and stone masons; next came the smiths. (69:3.9) Even the iron pyrites and flints used in striking fire were held sacred by early mankind. (69:6.4) Primates and Neanderthalers made tools of flint, the latter reaching the levels of Andonites (63:1.2) (64:4.3)
improvedDescription: Flint was a crucial stone material used by early humans for making tools and weapons. Andon discovered fire by striking flint, and the Andonites traveled far and wide searching for flint deposits, fashioning it into axes, spears, and other implements that were essential for hunting and survival.

name: flogging
originalDescription: olden ideas of self-discipline embraced flogging and all sorts of physical torture. (89:3.4) flogging of Jesus was made with knotted whips (185:6.2) Roman law provided that only those condemned to die by crucifixion should be thus subjected to scourging. (185:6.2) (185:7.2) (187:0.1)
improvedDescription: Flogging was a form of physical punishment involving whipping with knotted whips. Jesus was unjustly subjected to scourging by Roman soldiers before his crucifixion, though Roman law only permitted such treatment for those already condemned to die by crucifixion.

name: floods
originalDescription: Adamites controlled floods in Mesopotamia for thousands of years. It was during the floodtimes that Susa so greatly prospered. Unprecedented floods on the Euphrates around 5,000 B.C. caused dozens of cities to be abandoned. (78:2.1) (78:7.2,6) (78:8.2) floods destroyed Mediterranean civilization ca. 15,000 B.C. (78:3.7) the greatest loss of life caused by a flood occurred with the sinking of the Sicily land bridge, which created a single Mediterranean Sea and connected it to the Atlantic Ocean (80:2.4) as the Hebrew priests in Babylonian captivity sought to trace the Jewish people back to Adam, they found great difficulty in piecing the story together; and they let the whole world drown in its wickedness at the time of Noah’s flood, and thus to be in a better position to trace Abraham right back to one of the three surviving sons of Noah. (78:7.3-5) floods made lowland Chinese cities untenable (79:6.4) Mithraic legend included the story of a man escaped from a flood in a specillay built boat (98:5.3) notable floods: * Floods of the Asian continent (59:2.1) * Floods 350 million years ago on all continents except central Asia (59:2.2) * A great flood 340 million years ago except in Asia and Australia (59:2.3) * 320 million years ago, the third major flood of this period occurred. (59:2.6) * 300 million years ago another great period of immersion of the land began, the Silurian floods. (59:3.1,9) since Archeozoic ages there has never been a universal flood (78:7.4) Noah really lived; he was a wine maker of Aram, a river settlement near Erech. He kept a written record of the days of the river’s rise from year to year. He brought much ridicule upon himself by going up and down the river valley advocating that all houses be built of wood, boat fashion, and that the family animals be put on board each night as the flood season approached. He would go to the neighbouring river settlements every year and warn them that in so many days the floods would come. Finally a year came in which the annual floods were greatly augmented by unusually heavy rainfall so that the sudden rise of the waters wiped out the entire village; only Noah and his immediate family were saved in their houseboat. (77:4.12) (78:7.5)
improvedDescription: Floods were significant geological and historical events that shaped civilizations and landscapes throughout Urantia's history. Notable floods include those that disrupted Mesopotamian settlements around 5,000 B.C., the Mediterranean flood that created the greatest loss of life in world history, and various periodic inundations during geological ages.

name: Florissant fossil beds
originalDescription: The Florissant fossil beds in Colorado date back to ancient times and contain fossils of many insect families that are now extinct but were similar to those of the previous era. (61:2.4)
improvedDescription: The Florissant fossil beds in Colorado contain fossils from ancient times, including many extinct insect families that were similar to those of previous geological eras.

name: flute players
originalDescription: in Palestine flute players were used in houses of mourning (152:1.1)
improvedDescription: Flute players were musicians employed in Palestinian houses of mourning to provide music during funeral proceedings and grieving rituals.

name: focus
improvedDescription: The ability to concentrate attention and energy on a particular object, activity, or goal.

name: fog
originalDescription: Neanderthalers were deathly afraid of clouds, more especially of mists and fogs. (64:4.12) When the clouds gather overhead, our faith should accept the fact of the presence of the indwelling Adjuster, and thus we should be able to look beyond the mists of mortal uncertainty into the clear shining of the sun of eternal righteousness on the beckoning heights of the mansion worlds of Satania. (108:6.8)
improvedDescription: Fog represents both a natural atmospheric phenomenon and a metaphor for uncertainty. Neanderthalers were deathly afraid of mists and fogs, while spiritually, fog symbolizes the mortal uncertainty that faith can help us see beyond to divine clarity.

name: Fonta
improvedDescription: The female companion of Andon, together forming the first human pair to evolve on Urantia approximately one million years ago.

name: food
originalDescription: primitive man Adamites Jesus spiritual food | bread:bread of life
improvedDescription: Essential sustenance that played a crucial role in human evolution and social development, from primitive cannibalism to the development of agriculture, food storage, and cooking methods. Food offerings were central to early religious practices, and Jesus taught about spiritual food as nourishment for the soul.

name: foolish rich man
originalDescription: the parable of the foolish rich man teaches us about the importance to lay up treasures in heaven and not on earth (165:4.2-3)
improvedDescription: A character from Jesus' parable who focused solely on accumulating earthly wealth while neglecting spiritual treasures, ultimately losing everything when his soul was required of him that very night.

name: footprints
originalDescription: numerous sandstone footprints of the land reptiles may be observed (60:1.2)
improvedDescription: Fossilized traces of land reptiles preserved in sandstone layers, providing evidence of ancient life during prehistoric periods on Urantia.

name: foraminifers
originalDescription: The Cretaceous period is named after the prolific foraminifers that dominated the seas, producing vast amounts of chalk, and it marks the near end of the long reign of reptiles, the emergence of flowering plants and birds, and significant geological activities including continental drift, crustal deformations, lava flows, and volcanic activities, with large deposits of foraminiferal limestone found in regions such as the Alps, Himalayas, Tibet, and along the coasts of Africa, Australia, South America, and the Antilles. (60:3.1) (61:1.13) (61:2.4)
improvedDescription: Prolific chalk-making marine organisms that dominated the Cretaceous seas, creating extensive limestone deposits found in mountain ranges like the Alps, Himalayas, and other regions worldwide.

name: forbidden fruit
originalDescription: during Jewish feast of atonement at daybreak the pilgrims assembled from all parts of the city, each carrying in the right hand a sheaf of myrtle, willow, and palm branches, while in the left hand each one carried a branch of the paradise apple—the citron, or the “forbidden fruit. (162:4.4) Every primitive tribe had its tree of forbidden fruit, literally the apple but figuratively consisting of a thousand branches hanging heavy with all sorts of taboos. (89:2.1)
improvedDescription: A symbolic representation of taboos and restrictions, literally referring to the apple but figuratively encompassing all manner of prohibitions that primitive tribes imposed on themselves.

name: force
originalDescription: definition circulation material and spiritual force
improvedDescription: A broadly used term encompassing spiritual, mindal, and material energies, particularly referring to the pregravity stages of cosmic energy that circulate throughout the universes and originate from Paradise.

name: force-charge of space
improvedDescription: The primordial energy charge of space, also known as pure energy or segregata, representing the first stage of space potency individuation into pre-energy forms of cosmic force.

name: force-energy
improvedDescription: The combined manifestation of force and energy that originally proceeded from Paradise and will eventually return there, encompassing all physical energies that circulate throughout the universe of universes.

name: foreknowledge
improvedDescription: The divine attribute of knowing future events in advance, related to but distinct from omniscience, representing God's awareness of what will come to pass.

name: foreskins
originalDescription: Saul did not hesitate to require one hundred Philistine foreskins as the dowry David should pay for his daughter Michal. (70:1.18)
improvedDescription: Foreskins are mentioned in the context of ancient mutilation practices, specifically referencing Saul's demand for one hundred Philistine foreskins as David's dowry for his daughter Michal.

name: forests
originalDescription: Andonites avoided the forests in contrast with the habits of their nonhuman relatives. In the forests man has always deteriorated; human evolution has made progress only in the open and in the higher latitudes (64:1.3)
improvedDescription: Forests are vast woodland areas that evolved on early Earth and played a significant role in human development. The Andonites avoided forests in contrast to their nonhuman relatives, as human evolution made progress in open lands rather than in forested environments where humans tend to deteriorate.

name: Foreword
originalDescription: The Foreword to The Urantia Book is not a finished statement within itself; it is only a definitive guide designed to assist those who shall read papers 1 to 31 (0:0.4)
improvedDescription: The Foreword to The Urantia Book is a definitive guide designed to assist readers in understanding the terminology and concepts used in papers 1-31, rather than being a finished statement within itself.

name: forgetfulness
originalDescription: Having started out on the way of life everlasting, having accepted the assignment and received our orders to advance, we must not fear the dangers of human forgetfulness and mortal inconstancy, must not be troubled with doubts of failure or by perplexing confusion, must not falter and question our status and standing, for in every dark hour, at every crossroad in the forward struggle, the Spirit of Truth will always speak, saying, “This is the way. (34:7.8)
improvedDescription: Human forgetfulness refers to the mortal tendency to lose sight of spiritual truths and divine guidance, which believers must not fear as they progress on the way of life everlasting.

name: forgiveness
originalDescription: divine forgiveness iniquiters Michael’s attitude nature of forgiveness practicing forgiveness primitive men
improvedDescription: Forgiveness is the divine act of pardoning sin and wrongdoing, representing God's eternal love and mercy toward his children. It involves both receiving God's forgiveness through faith and extending forgiveness to one's fellow beings, creating a continuous cycle of divine and human mercy.

name: Formosa
originalDescription: Andite migration reached Formosa (78:5.6)
improvedDescription: Formosa is an island in East Asia that was reached by Andite migrations, along with Japan and the East Indies, though very few Andites entered the region by the coastal route.

name: forms
originalDescription: Forms are valueless when lessons are learned. (48:6.32)
improvedDescription: Forms are external structures or methods that become valueless once their underlying lessons have been learned, like a shell that serves no purpose after a chick has hatched.

name: Fortant
originalDescription: Fortant is one of the seven Lanonandeks currently administering Satania, and he is the director of the donation; he is the secondary Lanonandek Son numbered 319,847. He is in charge of all the activities that have been moved to Jerusem as a result of Michael's donation (45:3.7)
improvedDescription: Fortant is a secondary Lanonandek Son numbered 319,847 who serves as the bestowal director among the seven Lanonandeks administering Satania. He has been attached to Lanaforge's staff for nineteen hundred years and directs all universe activities transplanted to Jerusem since Michael's bestowal on Urantia.

name: Fortune
originalDescription: Fortune is a fearful and downcast young man whom Jesus encountered while touring the mountains of Crete (130:6) became the leader of the Christians in Crete (130:6.5)
improvedDescription: Fortune was a fearful and downcast young man whom Jesus encountered in the mountains of Crete, who had grown up feeling helpless and inferior, especially after losing his father at age twelve. He later became the leader of the Christians in Crete and a close associate of Titus.

name: fortunetellers
originalDescription: in connection with the revival of the worship of Ishtar that the Babylonian priests turned anew to stargazing; astrology experienced its last great Mesopotamian revival, fortunetelling became the vogue, and for centuries the priesthood increasingly deteriorated. (95:1.7) Fortunetellers are still patronized by the credulous. (90:2.7)
improvedDescription: Fortunetellers are practitioners who claim to predict the future, often associated with astrology and stargazing. They experienced a revival during the Babylonian worship of Ishtar and continue to be patronized by credulous people despite repeated attempts by authorities to banish such practices.

name: forty days
originalDescription: After his baptism Jesus entered upon forty days of adjusting himself to the changed relationships of the world and the universe occasioned by the personalization of his Adjuster. (136:3)
improvedDescription: A period of retirement following Jesus's baptism during which he formulated plans and determined the technique for proclaiming the new kingdom of God in human hearts.

name: forum
originalDescription: forum was the center of Roman political, legal, and business life (132:0.2)
improvedDescription: The center of Roman political, legal, and business life where Jesus frequently spent time during his stay in Rome.

name: fossils
originalDescription: Since so few of these early organisms had shells, not many have been preserved as fossils. (59:0.8) Study of the rock-embraced fossils of marine life reveals the early adjustment struggles of these primitive organisms. (58:6.6)
improvedDescription: Preserved remains of ancient life forms found in rock formations that provide a record of evolutionary development throughout Earth's geological history.

name: Foundation of Spiritual Progress
originalDescription: In the most developed continental nation of a neighboring planet, the spiritual teachers or pastors who examine the parents in each family are under the direction of the Foundation of Spiritual Progress, a newly created institution supported by voluntary contributions. (72:3.6)
improvedDescription: A newly created institution on a neighboring planet that supervises spiritual teachers and pastors who examine families, supported by voluntary contributions.

name: fountain gate
originalDescription: fountain gate was a gate in Jerusalem near pool of Siloam (162:4.4)
improvedDescription: A gate in Jerusalem located near the pool of Siloam, used during religious processions in the Feast of Tabernacles ceremonies.

name: four and twenty counselors
originalDescription: advisory supervisors of rebellion-isolated worlds (114:2.4-5) all were real leaders on Urantia; all Jerusem citizens, except Machiventa (50:2.2) (114:2.1) also known as Urantia advisory council (45:4.1) (76:6.3) approve morontia progressors’ plans for future course (48:6.5) Assigned Sentinels are ex officio heads of (24:5.3) direct many matters resulting from Michael’s bestowal (114:2.2) established by Michael (114:1.1) function somewhat like a legislative branch (114:5.1) last of group assembled 1900 years ago (45:4.1) meet around throne of presiding archangels (34:4.11) (45:4.1) nominated by cabinet of Lanaforge (114:2.1) one always present on Urantia as resident governor general (45:4.16) (114:1.1) pass upon ascenders’ attainment of socialization (45:7.7) personal agents of Michael and Gabriel on Jerusem (45:4.1) recruited from all Urantia races (45:4.2) similar commissions exist for all quarantined worlds (114:2.5) they are exempt from the ascension regime for the time being (45:4.19) members (45:4)
improvedDescription: A commission of twenty-four former Urantians who serve as Michael's personal agents on Jerusem, functioning as advisory supervisors for rebellion-isolated worlds and representing the Master Son in matters concerning Satania.
