originalDescription: God is not personally present either in nature or in any of the forces of nature (4:2.5) (131:1.5) the creation of energy and life is the prerogative of God; both flow into all creation (42:1.5) all men are children of God (2:5.4) (40:6) (99:5.5) Creator Sons are God in his local universes (33:1.4) | Michael of Nebadon:description he is only perceptibly present on Paradise and in Havona (3:1.6) he is literally present in Paradise (11:1) divine qualities are perfectly unified in God (44:7.4) God, as a superperson, eventuates; God, as a person, creates; God, as a preperson, fragments into Adjusters (30:1.99) The Urantia Book offers a document, the 97th, on the evolution of the concept of God (97) (5:4.9-13) (5:5.12) (142:3) in God's relationship with the local universes it seems that he withdraws from the activity in favor of the created beings and Creators of him, but there is also a real, literal and personal participation on God's part (32:4) God functions not only in parental capacities, but also in non-Father capacities such as God's relationships of force, energy, power, causality, reaction, potentiality, actuality, gravity, tension, the archetype, the principle and the unit (104:2.6) God Fragments are Thought Adjusters | Thought Adjusters term “God” also known as | Deity:also known as; Universal Father:also known as consciousness of God (0:2.2) (5:2) (8:5) | spiritual communion finding God | faith; spiritual growth God’s nature (2) | beauty; goodness; justice:divine justice; mercy; omnipotence; omniscience; truth God and man misconceptions about God (1:2.2) (4:5) (12:7.6) (102:6.3) (188:4.8) (188:5.1,11) (196:3.24)
improvedDescription: The infinite, eternal, and perfect Universal Father who is the First Source and Center of all reality. God is described as pure spirit, absolute love, and the divine personality from whom all creation emanates. While God is not personally present in nature or physical forces, He indwells mortal minds through Thought Adjusters and can be known through spiritual communion. God is characterized by perfect unity of divine attributes including love, mercy, justice, and truth, and serves as both the origin and ultimate destiny of all personality beings in the universe of universes.

name: God of Action
improvedDescription: A designation referring to the Infinite Spirit, the Third Person of Paradise Deity who serves as the executive agent and active administrator of divine plans. As the God of Action, the Infinite Spirit coordinates and executes the joint will of the Universal Father and Eternal Son throughout creation.

name: God the Absolute
originalDescription: Deity Absolute experiences identity with Unqualified Absolute in (0:2.18) possible trinitization of (0:9.4) (0:12.8) defined as
improvedDescription: The third level of experiential Deity, representing the experiential realization of absolute divinity through the unification of the second experiential Trinity, the Absolute Trinity. God the Absolute is the realization-attainment goal of all superabsonite beings and experiences identity with the Unqualified Absolute.

name: God the Father
improvedDescription: The first person of the Paradise Trinity, also known as the Universal Father, who is the eternal source and center of all reality and the supreme object of worship for all created beings.

name: God the Sevenfold
originalDescription: comprised of (56:5.3) (116:2.4-11) # Creator Sons, local universe Mother Spirits # Ancients of Days # Seven Master Spirits # Supreme Being # Infinite Spirit # Eternal Son # Universal Father The Creator Sons are the ultimate focalizations of the power and personality of the mighty time-space attributes of the Sevenfold God (33:1.3) The Sevenfold God is the personality of Deity functioning in any part of time-space (0:2.16) The first three levels, the Mother Spirits, the Ancients of Days, and the Master Spirits, are known as the Supreme Creators of time and space (31:9.14) (36:6.7) (56:6.1-2) (106:3.2) (106:5.2) (115:4.7) (116:2) (116:4.2) (117:1.2) (117:3.7) (118:0.9) (118:4.7) The union of the Supreme Creators, the Supreme Being, and the Master Architects are the members of the first Trinity experiential (31:9.14) (106:3.2) The Sevenfold embraces the controllers as well as the creators of the grand universe (116:5.1-9) The Universal Father has established seven successive levels of approach to Deity (0:8.1-8) The Supreme is not fully real to creatures but is real to God the Sevenfold (117:7.5) function
improvedDescription: A functional association of Deity comprising seven levels of approach to the Universal Father, established to compensate for creature limitations in approaching infinite Deity. The seven levels include Creator Sons and Creative Spirits, Ancients of Days, Seven Master Spirits, the Supreme Being, the Infinite Spirit, the Eternal Son, and the Universal Father.

name: God the Son
improvedDescription: The second person of the Paradise Trinity, also known as the Eternal Son, who is the eternal word of God and the spiritual center of the Paradise Trinity.

name: God the Spirit
improvedDescription: The third person of the Paradise Trinity, also known as the Infinite Spirit, who is the conjoint actor and the source of mind and spirit ministry throughout creation.

name: God the Supreme
improvedDescription: The first experiential level of Deity manifestation, evolving through the grand universe as the finite synthesis of perfect Creator and perfecting creature experience, unifying all finite reality in the Supreme Being.

name: God the Ultimate
originalDescription: 3 Absolutes are co-ordinated in (56:9.1) Adjuster is secret of personal realization of (101:6.2) foreshadows transcendental overcontrol in later stage of universe light and life (56:7.5) had beginning but will not have end (0:9.5) involvement in emergent-energy manifestation (42:2.13) present in Havona in absonite and superpersonal sense (14:6.29) (106:4.3) reactions unpredictable (12:6.6) Supreme indispensable to emergence of (118:0.9) transforms undifferentiated potentials into definite plans (118:4.6) defined as relationship with Trinity and other future
improvedDescription: The second experiential level of Deity, functioning on transcendental levels beyond time and space. God the Ultimate eventuates from the potentials of divinity in the master universe and represents the transcendental unification of absonite realities through the Ultimate Trinity.

name: Godad
originalDescription: Godad was a Salemite hermit whom Gautama should have listened to in order to have made Buddhism a better religion (94:7.4)
improvedDescription: A Salemite hermit descended from a family that preserved the traditions of Melchizedek missionaries. Gautama could have transformed Buddhism by heeding Godad's instruction about the Salem gospel of salvation by faith.

name: Godheads
improvedDescription: The three distinct and separate persons of the Paradise Trinity - the Universal Father, Eternal Son, and Infinite Spirit - who, while functionally one from the universe standpoint, maintain individual personalities for intimate spiritual relations with created beings.

name: Godlikeness
improvedDescription: The progressive attainment of divine qualities and attributes through spiritual growth and moral development, representing the creature's evolutionary approach toward reflecting the character and nature of God.

name: gods and goddesses
originalDescription: evolution in the consideration of the gods names
improvedDescription: Deities worshiped by various cultures throughout human history, evolving from glorified departed humans and ancestral spirits to more complex pantheons. These early gods were often tribal deities that reflected the limited understanding of their worshipers, including municipal gods of city-states and false gods created by human projection, distinct from true spiritual reality.

name: gold
originalDescription: Gold was the first metal to be sought by man; it was easy to work and, at first, was used only as an ornament. (81:3.4-5) They traveled far and wide in search of flint, much as present-day humans journey to the ends of the earth in quest of gold, platinum, and diamonds. (63:5.6) gold was the product of lava flows in Sierras (60:3.3) Every creature child may truly say: “He knows the way I take, and when he has tried me, I shall come forth as gold.” (3:3.2)
improvedDescription: The first metal sought by humans due to its ease of working and initial use as ornamentation. Gold was discovered alongside copper and tin by early civilizations, with gold-bearing quartz strata formed by ancient lava flows in mountain-building periods.

name: golden age
originalDescription: The modern phrase, “back to nature,” is a delusion of ignorance, a belief in the reality of the onetime fictitious “golden age.” The only basis for the legend of the golden age is the historic fact of Dalamatia and Eden. But these improved societies were far from the realization of utopian dreams. But this ideas were discouraging since they were predicated upon a belief in retrogression rather than progression, as well as implying a vengeful Deity (68:1.7) (74:8.13-14) The tradition of Adam and the Garden of Eden also lent substance to the dream of a onetime “golden age” of the dawn of the races. (89:2.3) the truth is that the early experiences of the human race were characterized by terrible antisocial conditions (81:5.4) times of Onagar were, indeed, the golden age for primitive man (63:6.9) ages of light and life that will come to Urantia in a distant future will be truly golden ages (50:5.10) | light and life
improvedDescription: A mythical concept of a perfect past era that, while largely fictitious, has some basis in the historical realities of Dalamatia and Eden. The true golden ages of humanity lie in the future ages of light and life, rather than in any imagined perfect past.

name: golden calves
originalDescription: Moses always was he plagued by the awkward predicament of trying to present his new and higher idea of Deity to these ignorant slaves under the guise of the ancient term Yahweh, which had always been symbolized by the golden calf of the Bedouin tribes. (96:4.3) the silver and golden calves symbolized the Bedouin herders’ concept of the spirit of the Sinai volcano (96:1.12) (96:5.8)
improvedDescription: Symbolic representations of Yahweh used by Bedouin tribes, particularly golden and silver calves that symbolized their concept of the spirit of the Sinai volcano. These became problematic symbols when Moses tried to present higher concepts of Deity while accommodating existing tribal religious traditions.

name: golden rule
originalDescription: The “golden rulers” may establish a progressive society in which they live according to their ideals while maintaining an adequate defense against their benighted fellows who might seek either to exploit their pacific predilections or to destroy their advancing civilization. (71:4.16-17) The Andonites were early taught the golden rule, and, even today, their Eskimo descendants live very much by that code; custom is strong among them, and they are fairly free from violent antagonisms. (70:1.2) the golden rule was taught in Garden of Eden (74:7.5,18) Jesus golden rule was based on infinite worth of finite (196:2.10) The golden rule of human fairness cries out against all such fraud, unfairness, selfishness, and unrighteousness. (54:1.8) there are two levels of the golden rule: the brotherly love and fatherly love (140:5.1) (147:4) The golden rule, when literally interpreted, may become the instrument of great offense to one’s fellows. (180:5.5) The worship of insects and other animals was promoted by a later misinterpretation of the golden rule—doing to others (every form of life) as you would be done by. (85:3.4) Evolutionary religion drives home to the individual the idea of personal duty; revealed religion lays increasing emphasis on loving, the golden rule. (101:5.11) living the golden rule statements of the golden rule
improvedDescription: The fundamental ethical principle taught by Jesus to treat others as you would want to be treated, expressed in its highest form as loving others as Jesus loves us. This rule operates on multiple levels from human fairness to spiritual realization and requires spiritual insight to be properly understood and applied.

name: Golgotha
originalDescription: Golgotha was the official crucifixion site of Jerusalem (187:1.4) Jesus was crufifixed at Golgotha (186:0.2) (187:1)
improvedDescription: The official crucifixion site of Jerusalem located north of the city beyond the Damascus gate, where Jesus was crucified. The site was positioned near villas of the wealthy on one side and tombs of well-to-do Jews on the other.

name: Goliath
originalDescription: Goliath is the giant who had a duel with David (70:1.19)
improvedDescription: The giant warrior who fought in single combat with David, representing an ancient custom where disputes between tribes were settled by duels between chosen representatives rather than full tribal warfare.

name: Gomorrah
originalDescription: The destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah was due to natural causes despite the invented narratives related to their destruction (93:6.7) (93:8.1)
improvedDescription: An ancient city destroyed along with Sodom due to natural causes, though later legends fabricated supernatural narratives about their destruction. The actual destruction occurred shortly before Melchizedek decided to end his emergency bestowal on Urantia.

name: Gonod
originalDescription: Indian businessman with whom Jesus journeyed to Rome (130) (132) (133) (129:2.9) He made 3 trips to China apart from the trip he made with his son and Jesus (130:0.6)
improvedDescription: A wealthy Indian businessman and traveler who employed Jesus as an interpreter and tutor for his son during their journey to Rome. A citizen of India who had made three extensive trips to China, Gonod provided Jesus with valuable insights into Indian and Chinese civilization and culture.

name: good
improvedDescription: A fundamental value and divine attribute representing righteousness, virtue, and moral excellence, often associated with truth and beauty as one of the supreme values that guide spiritual growth and ethical living.

name: good news
improvedDescription: The message of Jesus' gospel, representing the joyful tidings of spiritual salvation and eternal life through faith in God's fatherhood and human sonship.

name: good Samaritan
originalDescription: the parable of the good samaritan of Jesus was to clarify who is the neighbor (164:1)
improvedDescription: A parable told by Jesus to clarify the meaning of 'neighbor' in response to a lawyer's question about inheriting eternal life, illustrating compassionate service regardless of social boundaries.

name: good shepherd
originalDescription: Jesus was the good shepherd (182:1.16) Jesus preached the sermon on the “Good Shepherd” before a group of more than three hundred Jerusalemites, Pharisees and others, his bitter enemies, and before the apostles (165:1.2) (165:2)
improvedDescription: Jesus' self-designation emphasizing his role as the caring protector and guide of believers, featured prominently in his sermon to over three hundred people including Pharisees and apostles.

name: goodness
originalDescription: Divinity is creature comprehensible as truth, beauty, and goodness; correlated in personality as love, mercy, and ministry; disclosed on impersonal levels as justice, power, and sovereignty. (0:1.17) “We know that all things work together for good to those who love God,” “for the eyes of the Lord are over the righteous, and his ears are open to their prayers. (4:1.4,11) (10:7.5-6) (48:4.7) (54:4.7) (67:7.8) (67:8.4) (94:6.4) (118:10.18) (182:2.1) | providence our Father in heaven permits the good and the evil to go along together until the end of life, just as nature allows the wheat and the tares to grow side by side until the harvest. (4:2.6) (130:1.5-6) (132:2) (159:5.10) The idealization and attempted service of truth, beauty, and goodness is not a substitute for genuine religious experience—spiritual reality. (196:3.23) we must base life on highest consciousness of truth, beauty, and goodness (110:3.7) While it is all too true that good cannot come of evil to the one who contemplates and performs evil, it is equally true that all things (including evil, potential and manifest) work together for good to all beings who know God, love to do his will, and are ascending Paradiseward according to his eternal plan and divine purpose. (54:4.7) The concepts of truth, beauty, and goodness are not inherent in either physics or chemistry. (195:6.11) The understandings of truth, beauty, and goodness, morality, ethics, duty, love, divinity, origin, existence, purpose, destiny, time, space, even Deity, are only relatively true. (115:1.2) Until we attain Paradise levels, goodness will always be more of a quest than a possession, more of a goal than an experience of attainment. (132:2.8) defined as goodness of God (2:6) Jesus and goodness nature of goodness
improvedDescription: One of the three divine qualities that make divinity comprehensible to creatures, along with truth and beauty. Goodness represents the recognition of divine values and moral perfection, serving as both a quest for mortals and an eternal attribute of God that leads to repentance and spiritual growth.

name: gophers
originalDescription: 35 million years ago, gophers, along with other gnawing rodents like beavers, squirrels, mice, and rabbits, appeared and quickly became a notable form of life. (61:2.7)
improvedDescription: Small gnawing rodents that appeared alongside beavers, squirrels, mice, and rabbits 35 million years ago, quickly becoming a notable form of life with little evolutionary change since.

name: Gophna
originalDescription: Jesus preached to the people of this place at the end of June 27 (143:0.1)
improvedDescription: A village near Bethel where citizens came to hear Jesus preach at the end of June 27 A.D., along with people from Ephraim and other surrounding areas.

name: gorillas
originalDescription: gorillas descend from inferior mid-mammals, but it is not the same ancestor as man because man descends from superior mid-mammals (62:3.12)
improvedDescription: Large primates that evolved from inferior mid-mammals, sharing a common extinct ancestor with primitive monkeys but not related to the superior mid-mammals from which humans descended.

name: gospel
originalDescription: the acceptance of Jesus’ gospel will result in the immediate improvement of home life. (177:2.6) believers must love gospel more than mother, father, wife, husband, children, or siblings (150:4.3) (163:3.4) (171:2.2) Those who know the gospel only in the mind, and who have not experienced it in the heart, cannot be depended upon for support when real trouble comes. (177:5.2) the gospel of the kingdom belongs to all who believe it; it shall not be committed to the custody of mere priests. (191:6.3) common people hear gladly teh gospel (173:3.2) (174:4.7) (196:1.4) we should not try to fit Jesus’ new gospel into old forms of religious belief (138:5.2) (140:6.2) (147:7.2-3) The old religion was motivated by fear-consciousness; the new gospel of the kingdom is dominated by truth-conviction, the spirit of eternal and universal truth. (180:5.12) evidence of belief in gospel is the love shown to one another (182:1.6) the gospel sets men free from the burdensome traditions of a formal religion of ceremonies which old teachers are determined to uphold at any cost. (162:2.2) (170:2.1-2) he who knowingly denies gospel before men shall be denied by angels of heaven (165:3.5) the gospel was hidden from wise and self-righteous (163:6.3) it is infectious in its transforming power (159:3.10) Jesus dwells in all who believe his gospel (164:5.3) loyalty to gospel dependent on sound early home training (177:2.2) the gospel of the kingdom is a living truth. From generation to generation the gospel must show increasing vitality and exhibit greater depth of spiritual power. (178:1.15) the gospel, explained | religion:explained spread of the gospel Other gospels proclaiming the gospel | government:believers’ relation to; kingdom of heaven:establishment
improvedDescription: The central teaching of Jesus proclaiming the fatherhood of God and brotherhood of man, offering salvation through faith and spiritual rebirth. The gospel emphasizes love, service, and the recognition that all humans are children of God, requiring personal experience rather than mere intellectual acceptance for transformation.

name: Gospel of John
improvedDescription: The New Testament gospel written by John Zebedee, one of the twelve apostles and closest associates of Jesus during his earthly ministry.

name: Gospel of Luke
improvedDescription: The New Testament gospel written by Luke, a physician and companion of the Apostle Paul, presenting Jesus' life and teachings with particular attention to his ministry to all people.

name: Gospel of Mark
improvedDescription: One of the New Testament gospels, traditionally attributed to John Mark, which presents an account of Jesus' life and teachings.

name: Gospel of Matthew
improvedDescription: One of the New Testament gospels, traditionally attributed to Matthew Levi, which presents an account of Jesus' life and teachings.

name: gossip
originalDescription: Jesus recommended: “Speak only that which you have heard from me; speak not hearsay. ” (138:8.4)
improvedDescription: Spreading unverified information or hearsay about others, which Jesus cautioned against, advising his followers to speak only what they had heard from him directly.

name: government
originalDescription: believers’ relation to government celestial government | Urantia:celestial government democracy history of government (70) (71) | civilization other worlds principles of government (70) (71) (72) | competition; courts; human rights; industry; justice; law; military; profit; taxation stages to evolution of government quality of the state warnings self-government world government
improvedDescription: The system of political authority and administration that regulates society, encompassing both earthly civil governance and celestial administrative structures. Jesus taught his followers to render to Caesar what is Caesar's while maintaining spiritual allegiance to God, emphasizing that believers should be good citizens without allowing earthly government to interfere with their spiritual obligations.

name: governor general
improvedDescription: A high-ranking administrative official in governmental systems, typically overseeing territorial or regional governance.

name: governors of advanced tribal relations
improvedDescription: Members of the Caligastia one hundred who were assigned to oversee and advance the social and political organization of early human tribal societies.

name: grace (God’s mercy)
originalDescription: Time is an invariable element in the attainment of knowledge; religion makes its endowments immediately available, albeit there is the important factor of growth in grace, definite advancement in all phases of religious experience. (102:2.4) While the elements of grace are freely admixed, nevertheless, the creature attainments are the result of individual effort and actual living, personality reaction to the existing environment. (32:3.9) sonship is a gift. No child has aught to do with earning the status of son or daughter. The earth child comes into being by the will of its parents. Even so, the child of God comes into grace and the new life of the spirit by the will of the Father in heaven. (144:4.3) (144:5.13) The creature’s need is wholly sufficient to insure the full flow of the Father’s tender mercies and his saving grace. (2:4.2,4) Few mortals ever dare to draw anything like the sum of personality credits established by the combined ministries of nature and grace.grow in grace by living faith (48:7.6) words of Jesus were: “Grow in grace by means of that living faith which grasps the fact that you are the sons of God while at the same time it recognizes every man as a brother.” (147:8.5) a mercy credit is established for the survival of each rational creature, a credit of lavish proportions and one of sufficient grace to insure the survival of every soul who really desires divine citizenship. (28:6.5) survivors enjoy extended opportunity for spiritual attainment interrupted by death (45:7.1) there is a time lag between sin and penalty. A period of time of sufficient length to allow for such an adjudication of such an individual’s universe status as will prove entirely satisfactory (54:3.2) (54:4)
improvedDescription: God's unearned favor and mercy freely given to his creatures, enabling spiritual growth and survival. Grace is a divine gift that establishes sufficient credit for every soul who truly desires citizenship in God's kingdom, requiring no earning but only acceptance through faith.

name: grace (prayer before eating)
originalDescription: Jesus seldom neglected to give thanks—verbally—when he broke bread for their noontide lunch (143:3.4) The primitive method of saying grace was, before eating, to throw a bit of food into the fire for the purpose of appeasing the spirits, while mumbling a magic formula. (87:2.9)
improvedDescription: The practice of giving thanks before meals, which Jesus typically observed by offering verbal gratitude when breaking bread. The primitive form involved throwing food into fire while reciting formulas to appease spirits.

name: graciousness
originalDescription: We can cultivate gracefulness, but graciousness is the aroma of friendliness which emanates from a love-saturated soul. (171:7.1)
improvedDescription: The natural aroma of friendliness that emanates from a love-saturated soul, distinguished from mere gracefulness which can be cultivated through effort.

name: gradant
originalDescription: The standard weight of Jerusem, the “gradant,” is built up through the decimal system from the mature ultimaton and represents almost exactly ten ounces of our weight. (46:1.2)
improvedDescription: The standard unit of weight on Jerusem, built through the decimal system from the mature ultimaton and equivalent to almost exactly ten ounces of Urantia weight.

name: Graduate Guides
originalDescription: Graduate Guides are counted among Higher Personalities of Infinite Spirit (26:6.1) The Graduate Guides, as a group, sponsor and conduct the high university of technical instruction and spiritual training which is so essential for our goal. They are engaged in piloting the pilgrims of time through the seven circuits of Havona worlds.; stay with same pilgrim throughout (24:6.1,3) (26:10.7) The number of Graduate Guides is beyond the power of human minds to grasp. They have not existed from eternity; they mysteriously appear as they are needed.Malvorian was the first, the Guide of the graduated called Grandfanda, and now is the chief of their supreme council (24:6.4-6) Graduate Guides never leave the Havona worlds; they are dedicated to the service of the graduate pilgrims of time and space. (24:0.10) (24:6.9) Though evolution is not the order of the central universe, it is believed that the Graduate Guides are the perfected or more experienced members of another order of central universe creatures, the Havona Servitals. (24:7.1-7) (25:1.7) A servital is long absent from Havona performing superuniverse assignments and may later be privileged to receive a divine embrace on Paradise that may transform him or her into a Graduate Guide. (24:7.2-3) For example, Sudna is a servital returned from superuniverse service, was received on Paradise, met the Father, entered the divine embrace and is no more, and three days later a newborn Graduate Guide appeared “spontaneously” in Havona. (24:7.5) The newly created servitals, together with newly appearing Graduate Guides, all pass through the courses of training which the senior guides continuously conduct on each of the seven Havona circuits. They then go to great lengths to serve on the study worlds surrounding the headquarters spheres of the seven superuniverses. (25:1.5-6) (26:7.4) in personality records they appear as single-origin beings in the family of the Infinite Spirit (30:1.77) (30:2.73) Graduate Guides, with their servital associates, minister on all circuits of Havona to both ascending and descending pilgrims (26:6.1)
improvedDescription: Graduate Guides are Higher Personalities of the Infinite Spirit who sponsor and conduct the high university of technical instruction and spiritual training in Havona. They pilot ascending mortals from time and space through the seven circuits of Havona worlds, with each guide remaining with the same pilgrim throughout their entire Havona career. These beings mysteriously appear as needed and are believed to be evolved Havona Servitals who have received the divine embrace on Paradise. Malvorian was the first Graduate Guide, assigned to Grandfanda, the first mortal to reach Havona.

name: graft
originalDescription: The graft determines the nature of the fruit, notwithstanding that the living sustenance is drawn from the roots of original divine endowment of both mind and spirit. (102:7.4)
improvedDescription: A metaphor describing how the nature of spiritual fruit in human life is determined by what beliefs and practices are grafted onto one's basic spiritual nature, even though spiritual sustenance comes from divine endowments of mind and spirit.

name: grain
originalDescription: the apostles gathered and rubbed grain in their hands on a Sabbath day, which provoked the indignation of a group of spies of the Sanhedrin (147:6.4) It was the custom of many Near Eastern peoples to throw grain upon the newlyweds; this was a magical rite which was supposed to insure fecundity. (83:4.5)
improvedDescription: Ripened wheat that the apostles gathered and rubbed in their hands while traveling on a Sabbath day, which provoked the indignation of Pharisee spies who accused them of Sabbath breaking. Grain was also thrown upon newlyweds in many Near Eastern cultures as a magical rite believed to ensure fertility.

name: Grand Canyon
originalDescription: In the dawn era of life, the rocks around Lake Superior and in the Grand Canyon of the Colorado River, with their primitive fossil-bearing layers, testify to the upheavals and surface fluctuations of those faraway times. (58:7.5)
improvedDescription: A geological formation in the Colorado River region that contains primitive fossil-bearing rock layers from the dawn era of life, testifying to the upheavals and surface fluctuations of ancient times.

name: Grand Lama
originalDescription: Grand Lama is the Tibetan religious leader (94:10.2)
improvedDescription: The highest-ranking religious leader in the hierarchical structure of Tibetan Buddhism, which includes monks, nuns, and abbots.

name: grand universe
originalDescription: The seven evolving superuniverses in association with the central and divine universe of Havona are commonly refered as the grand universe; these are the now organized and inhabited creations. They are all a part of the master universe, which also embraces the uninhabited but mobilizing universes of outer space. The grand universe has an evolutionary potential of around seven trillion inhabited planets, not including the billion eternal spheres of the central creation and the architectural administrative spheres. (0:0.6) (12:1.13) (15:2.9) Ancients of Days and Seven Master Spirits are eternally fixed as administrators of the grand universe (0:8.11) The seven Supreme Spirit groups are the universal co-ordinating directors of the seven-segmented administration of the grand universe. (17:0.1) The Supreme Center Supervisors, the seven co-ordinates and associates of the Supreme Power Directors, are the regulators of the master energy circuits of the grand universe. (29:2.10) Havona and the seven superuniverses need each other to reach the maximum of finite attainment; and someday future universes in outer space will also depend on the grand universe to transcend the finite. (117:6.20) Much as mortals look to solar energy for life maintenance, so does the grand universe depend upon the unfailing energies emanating from nether Paradise to sustain the material activities and cosmic motions of space. (116:7.2) The grand universe contains the possibility of, and ever seeks for, complete unification, and this grows out of the fact that this cosmic existence is a consequence of the creative acts and the power mandates of the Paradise Trinity, which is unqualified unity. (117:1.3) Its current edge is ragged, its periphery uneven and unfinished, and the entire astronomical terrain highly unstable. The seven superuniverses are yet to be completed. (12:1.13) The grand universe is not only a material creation of physical grandeur, spirit sublimity, and intellectual magnitude, it is also a magnificent and responsive living organism. Just as we are traversed by a network of nerves, the physical universe is permeated with energy lanes that activate it. The grand universe responds to the far-flung spirit-gravity grasp of the Eternal Son (116:7) The grand universe number of our world, Urantia, is 5,342,482,337,666. That is the registry number on Uversa and on Paradise, our number in the catalogue of the inhabited worlds. (15:14.8) the current grand universe uses only 5% of Paradise gravity capability (12:3.8)
improvedDescription: The seven evolving superuniverses in association with the central and divine universe of Havona, representing the present organized and inhabited creation. The grand universe contains approximately seven trillion inhabitable worlds plus architectural spheres and the billion spheres of Havona. It is part of the larger master universe, which also includes the uninhabited universes of outer space. The grand universe currently uses only about five percent of Paradise's gravity capability.

name: grandchildren
originalDescription: Savage man loves his child, but civilized man loves also his grandchild. (66:7.4) (84:7.10)
improvedDescription: The children of one's children, representing the expanded love and planning capacity that distinguishes civilized humans from savage humans and animals. While savage humans and animals love only their immediate offspring, civilized humans extend their love and concern to their grandchildren and future generations.

name: Grandfanda
originalDescription: acting head of Corps of the Finality (112:7.13) chief of Supreme Council of Destiny (31:10.9-10,20) exclusive jurisdiction over Gravity Messengers (31:2.1) first mortal to attain Havona; space reports of glory inaugurated upon his arrival (7:5.8) (24:6.4-8) (119:0.2) from superuniverse #1 (24:6.8) Malvorian was Graduate Guide assigned to (24:6.4-6)
improvedDescription: The first mortal pilgrim from time and space to reach Havona and attain Paradise, serving as the acting head of the Corps of the Finality and chief of the Supreme Council of Destiny. He originated from planet 341 of system 84 in constellation 62 of local universe 1,131 in superuniverse number one. His arrival at Havona inaugurated the broadcast service of the universe of universes and was guided by Malvorian, the first Graduate Guide.

name: granite
originalDescription: graniet began forming 1.500 million years ago, in the volcanic age (57:7.5) The lava layers of the earth’s crust, when cooled, form granite. The average density of Urantia is a little more than five and one-half times that of water; the density of granite is less than three times that of water. The earth’s core is twelve times as dense as water. (58:5.5)
improvedDescription: A rock formed from cooled lava layers of the earth's crust, with a density less than three times that of water. Granite began forming during the volcanic age approximately 1.5 billion years ago and constitutes the lighter material that formed the earth's early crust.

name: grapes
originalDescription: in the lintel of the Capernaum synagogue there was the emblem of a pot of manna embellished with grape clusters (153:2.8) Jeremiah said: ‘In those days they shall no more say, the fathers have eaten sour grapes and the children’s teeth are set on edge. (145:2.5-6)
improvedDescription: Fruit that appeared as decorative grape clusters on the lintel of the Capernaum synagogue alongside a pot of manna. Grapes are also referenced in Jeremiah's prophecy about sour grapes and the establishment of a new covenant where individuals are personally responsible for their own spiritual condition.

name: grass
originalDescription: Said Jesus: “If God so clothes the grass of the field, which is alive today and tomorrow is cut down and cast into the fire, how much more shall he clothe you, the ambassadors of the heavenly kingdom’. (165:5.3)
improvedDescription: Plant life comprising seed-bearing grasses that were among the most evolutionarily important developments in the plant world, second only to the appearance of man himself. Jesus used grass as a teaching metaphor about divine providence and trust in God's care.

name: gratification
improvedDescription: The satisfaction or fulfillment obtained from achieving desires or meeting needs.

name: gravel
originalDescription: The deposits of the early Carboniferous age are from 500 to 2,000 feet thick, consisting of sandstone, shale, and limestone. The oldest strata yield the fossils of both land and marine animals and plants, along with much gravel and basin sediments. (59:5.10)
improvedDescription: Small rock fragments found in early Carboniferous geological deposits, appearing alongside basin sediments and fossils of both land and marine life in strata ranging from 500 to 2,000 feet thick.

name: graves
originalDescription: The very first land titles granted by tribes to individuals were graves—family burying grounds. (69:9.14)
improvedDescription: Family burying grounds that represented the very first land titles granted by tribes to individuals, marking the earliest form of private land ownership in human society.

name: gravita
originalDescription: gravita is also known as universe power (0:6.7) they are the material organizations in superuniverses and are dual in constitution (11:8.7) (42:5.6) Universe power includes all forms of energy which, while still responding to Paradise gravity, are directly responsive to linear gravity. This is the electronic level of energy-matter and all subsequent evolutions thereof. (0:6.7) gravita comprise 30 energy systems (15:8.2) (29:2.13-15) constitutes the Postgravity stages of Paradise gravity response (11:8.7) power centers and physical controllers partially control it (15:8.2) (29:2.13-15) it is working energy useful to superuniverses. First the space-force is changed into space-energy and then into gravity controlled energy, energy that can be channeled and controlled by the Universal Power Directors. (42:2.14-15)
improvedDescription: Dual-constitution material organizations in superuniverses that comprise 30 energy systems and represent the postgravity stage of Paradise gravity response. Also known as universe power, gravita includes all forms of energy that respond to Paradise gravity and are directly responsive to linear gravity, serving as working energy useful to superuniverses.

name: gravity
originalDescription: characteristics types of gravity
improvedDescription: The fundamental force that holds all creation together through four absolute circuits: personality gravity of the Universal Father, spiritual gravity of the Eternal Son, mind gravity of the Conjoint Actor, and material gravity of the Isle of Paradise. These universal presence circuits pervade all realms and are independent of time and space.

name: gravity energy
originalDescription: gravity energy is the second phase of transmutation of segregata or primordial force into universe power (42:2.12) This secondary or gravity energy is the product of the energy elaboration resulting from the pressure-presence and the tension-trends set up by the Associate Transcendental Master Force Organizers. (42:2.12) it is Paradise gravity-responding energy that carries the potential of universe power and becomes the active ancestor of all universe matter. It is one of the two forms of ultimata. (42:2.12-13) The Supreme Power Directors and the Supreme Center Supervisors function both as individuals and conjointly with regard to all cosmic phenomena below the levels of “gravity energy”. (29:2.11)
improvedDescription: The second phase of energy transmutation that carries the potential of universe power and becomes the active ancestor of all universe matter. This Paradise gravity-responding energy is produced through the work of Associate Transcendental Master Force Organizers and represents one of the two forms of ultimata.

name: Gravity Messengers
originalDescription: The Gravity Messengers are nearly infinite spiritual personalities that can defy time and space. They are highly personal beings, divine, intelligent, and touchingly understanding. Much of the inscrutable work of the far-flung universe is wrought by them (31:2.2) (108:4.5) they are under the exclusive jurisdiction of Grandfanda and finaliter corps (31:2.1) Gravity Messengers hail from Divinington, and they are modified and personalized Adjusters, but few in Uversa will undertake to explain the nature of one of these messengers. (31:2.2) Divinington is their home (13:1.6) (31:2.2) they appear in Paradise classification as single-origin beings and Supreme Spirits (30:1.57) There exists a liaison of tremendous power and import between the Evening Stars and the Gravity Messengers attached to the finaliter working groups. (37:2.11) Certain gravity traversers are personal beings, such as Gravity Messengers and Transcendental Recorders, but they are not available to the super- and the local universe administrators. (23:3.2) they become finaliters when they become chiefs with a permanent staff of 999 companions; they can even make use of unlimited reserves available (31:2.3) Gravity Messengers are able to move in very short order independently of time and space (23:2.23) (31:2.1) they are in charge of certain space messages (44:5.7) they are without a discoverable physical form (42:12.10)
improvedDescription: Highly personal, divine beings who are modified and personalized Thought Adjusters serving exclusively under the jurisdiction of Grandfanda and the finaliter corps. These superspirit personalities can transcend time and space, have no discoverable physical form, and serve as an invaluable messenger corps for the finaliters.

name: Great Beyond
originalDescription: From Oban he sent out teachers to the remote settlements to spread his new doctrines of one Deity and his concept of the hereafter, which he called the Great Beyond. (63:6.7)
improvedDescription: Onagar's concept of the hereafter taught to remote settlements as part of his religious doctrine of one Deity, representing one of humanity's earliest formalized beliefs about life after death.

name: Great Britain
improvedDescription: The largest island in the British Isles, located off the northwestern coast of continental Europe.

name: great commandment
originalDescription: Jesus stated that the greatest commandment is to love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and the second is to love your neighbor as yourself, with all the law and prophets depending on these two commandments. (174:4)
improvedDescription: Jesus stated that the greatest commandment is to love God with all your heart, soul, mind, and strength, and the second is to love your neighbor as yourself, with all the law and prophets depending on these two commandments.

name: Great Lakes
originalDescription: During the retreat of a glacier the North American system of Great Lakes was produced. Our geologists have very accurately deduced the various stages of this development and have correctly surmised that these bodies of water did, at different times, empty first into the Mississippi valley, then eastward into the Hudson valley, and finally by a northern route into the St. Lawrence. It is thirty-seven thousand years since the connected Great Lakes system began to empty out over the present Niagara route. (61:7.10)
improvedDescription: The North American system of Great Lakes was produced during the retreat of a glacier, with these bodies of water emptying first into the Mississippi valley, then eastward into the Hudson valley, and finally by a northern route into the St. Lawrence. The connected Great Lakes system began to empty out over the present Niagara route thirty-seven thousand years ago.

name: Great Mother cult
improvedDescription: A religious cult centered on the worship of a great mother figure or goddess.

name: Great Pyramid
improvedDescription: A monumental pyramid structure, likely referring to the Great Pyramid of Giza in Egypt.

name: Great River
originalDescription: Egyptians expected ka would be waiting on other side (111:0.6)
improvedDescription: In Egyptian belief, the Great River was the boundary that souls crossed after death, where the ka (spirit genius) was expected to be waiting on the other side to guide the deceased.

name: Great Sources and Centers
originalDescription: Refers to: (15:1.4) * Universal Father * Eternal Son * Infinite Spirit
improvedDescription: The three primary divine beings of Paradise: the Universal Father, Eternal Son, and Infinite Spirit, representing the fundamental sources and centers of all reality.

name: Great Spirit
originalDescription: Onamonalonton, a far-distant leader of the red man and the one who directed this race from the worship of many gods to the veneration of “The Great Spirit.” (45:4.5) (64:6.7)
improvedDescription: The supreme deity concept promoted by Onamonalonton, a leader who directed the red race from the worship of many gods to the veneration of "The Great Spirit."

name: great supper
originalDescription: the parable of the great supper of Jesus is about who will be worthy to eat the bread in the kingdom of God and sit at the table of the Father (167:2)
improvedDescription: A parable told by Jesus about who will be worthy to eat bread in the kingdom of God and sit at the table of the Father.

name: greater
originalDescription: Seek the greater thing, and the lesser will be found therein; ask for the heavenly, and the earthly shall be included. (165:5.3)
improvedDescription: A spiritual principle taught by Jesus: "Seek the greater thing, and the lesser will be found therein; ask for the heavenly, and the earthly shall be included."

name: Greater Road
originalDescription: Gradually the concept of God, as contrasted with the Absolute, began to appear in Buddhism. Its sources are back in the early days of this differentiation of the followers of the Lesser Road and the Greater Road. It was among the latter division of Buddhism that the dual conception of God and the Absolute finally matured. (94:12.2) before the collapse in India, the Chinese and north Indian groups of Gautama’s followers had begun the development of the Mahayana teaching of the “Great Road” to salvation in contrast with the purists of the south who held to the Hinayana, or “Lesser Road.” (94:9.4-5)
improvedDescription: The Mahayana division of Buddhism, also known as the "Great Road" to salvation, which developed among Chinese and north Indian followers of Gautama in contrast to the Hinayana or "Lesser Road" of the southern purists.

name: greatness
originalDescription: The more steadfastly we behold, and the more persistently we pursue, the concepts of divine goodness, the more certainly will we grow in greatness, in true magnitude of genuine survival character. (28:6.21-22) (56:10.12) (100:7.17) nobility itself is always an unconscious growth. (100:1.7) The highest moral choice is the choice of the highest possible value, and always—in any sphere, in all of them—this is to choose to do the will of God. If man thus chooses, he is great, though he be the humblest citizen (39:4.14) Greatness lies not so much in possessing strength as in making a wise and divine use of such strength. (48:7.27) Said Jesus: “Whosoever would become great in my Father’s kingdom shall become a minister to all; and whosoever would be first among you, let him become the server of his brethren” (56:10.14) (140:1.6) (171:0.6) (175:1.10) the manifestation of greatness on a world like Urantia is the exhibition of self-control. The great man is not he who “takes a city” or “overthrows a nation,” but rather “he who subdues his own tongue.” (28:6.20) When the spiritual tests of greatness are applied, the moral elements are not disregarded, but the quality of unselfishness revealed in disinterested labor for the welfare of one’s earthly fellows, particularly worthy beings in need and in distress, that is the real measure of planetary greatness. (28:6.20) (140:4.6) Spiritual greatness consists in an understanding love that is Godlike and not in an enjoyment of the exercise of material power for the exaltation of self. (158:6.3) Greatness is synonymous with divinity. (28:6.21) worldly greatness
improvedDescription: Greatness is synonymous with divinity and cannot be separated from goodness. True greatness lies not in possessing strength but in making wise and divine use of such strength, manifesting as self-control and unselfish service to others. The highest moral choice—choosing to do God's will—makes one great, regardless of their humble status.

name: Greece
originalDescription: Greek history cities Greek language Greek religion (98:1-2) | mystery cults; Platonism
improvedDescription: Greece was an ancient Mediterranean civilization that became a major center of culture, philosophy, art, and learning. The Greeks developed advanced philosophical thought and were among the world's greatest traders and colonizers, spreading their cultural influence throughout the Mediterranean region and beyond.

name: Greeks
originalDescription: The Greeks were not only great teachers and artists, they were also the world’s greatest traders and colonizers. (80:7.12-13) Greeks an Christianity mentioned Greeks intellectual progress and stagnation
improvedDescription: The Greeks were not only great teachers and artists but also the world's greatest traders and colonizers. They succeeded in planting numerous cultural outposts throughout the Mediterranean, and many advances of early Greek civilization persisted in later European peoples through their mixed descendants.

name: green race
originalDescription: The green race was one of the less able groups of primitive men, and they were greatly weakened by extensive migrations in different directions. (64:6.17) mortal stature tends to decrease from the red man down to the indigo race, although on Urantia unexpected strains of giantism appeared among the green and orange peoples. Many of their leaders being eight and nine feet in height. (51:4.2) (64:6.19) green race wiped out and absorbed remnants of orange race 100,000 years ago (64:6.11,13,18) (64:7.14,16) destruction surviving remnants Fantad
improvedDescription: The green race was one of the six original Sangik races and among the less able groups of primitive men. They were greatly weakened by extensive migrations and eventually split into three divisions—northern tribes were absorbed by yellow and blue races, eastern groups amalgamated with Indian peoples, and the southern nation entered Africa where they destroyed the orange race before being absorbed by the indigo race.

name: Greenland
originalDescription: As the Sangik migrations draw to a close the purer Andonites live in the extreme northern regions of Europe and in Iceland, Greenland, and northeastern North America. (64:7.16-18) (78:1.5) Eastern America and western Europe were connected by the continent of Greenland. (59:5.20) (61:1.12) geology
improvedDescription: Greenland is a large Arctic island that was connected to both North America and Europe during various geological periods. It served as a land bridge facilitating early human migrations and contains geological evidence of ancient life forms beneath its ice mantle. The purer Andonites lived in Greenland along with other extreme northern regions.

name: greensand marl
originalDescription: 85 million years ago, the deposits of chalk and green sandstone marl, which give this period its name, varied in thickness from 60 meters to 3,000 meters and can be observed in the eastern foothills of the Rocky Mountains. (60:3.9)
improvedDescription: Greensand marl refers to geological deposits of chalk and green sandstone that characterize a specific geological period. These sedimentations varied in thickness from 200 feet to 10,000 feet and can be observed in locations such as the eastern foothills of the Rocky Mountains and various European regions.

name: Gregorian calendar
originalDescription: The Gregorian calendar is the one used in the Urantia Papers. Although the exact years are known to the developers, they have not been used to conform to this calendar. (57:0.1) (126:3.1)
improvedDescription: The Gregorian calendar is the present leap-year calendar system of 365¼ days per year that is used as the time reference throughout The Urantia Papers. Although the revelators possess exact chronological records, they chose to conform to this twentieth-century calendar system for presenting historical facts and dates.

name: groups
originalDescription: Three persons are a minimum social group, but this is least of all the many reasons for believing in the inevitability of the Conjoint Actor. (10:2.3) (39:3.6) celestial involvement leadership | leadership Jesus and his disciples primitive groups what to do value of groups
improvedDescription: Groups represent associations of three or more individuals that constitute a social unit requiring leadership and coordination. Groups possess potential far greater than the simple sum of their individual members, with effective teamwork and wise leadership being essential for achieving common goals and maintaining social cooperation.

name: growth
originalDescription: Growth is not truly indicated by mere products but rather by progress. (100:1.3) The progression of eternity does not consist solely in spiritual development. Intellectual acquisition is also a part of universal education. (37:6.6) finite and absonite growth nature of growth growth drivers cosmic insights
improvedDescription: Growth is indicated by progress rather than mere products, and represents the unconscious development of personality, character, and spiritual capacity. True growth cannot be forced or manufactured but must emerge naturally from within, requiring favorable conditions such as freedom from external pressure and devotion to supreme values.

name: grudges
improvedDescription: Grudges refer to persistent feelings of resentment or ill will held against others. The ability to avoid holding grudges represents emotional maturity and spiritual growth, as demonstrated by apostle Thomas who was described as a good loser who did not hold grudges or nurse wounded feelings.

name: guardian angels
originalDescription: The teaching about guardian angels is not a myth; certain groups of human beings do actually have personal angels. (113:1.1) after subjects’ death assignment guardians of destiny Lucifer's rebellion technique of service
improvedDescription: Personal angels assigned to guide and protect certain groups of human beings throughout their earthly lives and beyond. Guardian angels are seraphim who minister to mortals spiritually, serve as custodians of the surviving soul during the period between death and resurrection, and accompany their mortal associates on the Paradise ascension journey.

name: guardians of destiny
originalDescription: after subjects’ death assignment destiny Notable assignees technique of service
improvedDescription: Personal seraphic guardians assigned to individual mortals who have achieved the third psychic circle, made the supreme decision to become Godlike, or been enrolled in the reserve corps of destiny. These angels serve as custodians of the mortal soul's survival values during the interval between death and morontia resurrection, and may accompany their human associates throughout the Paradise ascension journey.

name: guardians of health and life
improvedDescription: A specialized group within the Caligastia one hundred, the superhuman staff that served on Urantia before the planetary rebellion.

name: guests
improvedDescription: Individuals who are received and entertained by hosts, representing an important aspect of social relationships and hospitality customs.

name: guilt
originalDescription: origin dealing with guilt Old guilt tests Peter
improvedDescription: The consciousness of violating moral standards or ideals, which may arise from interrupted spiritual communion or lowering of one's moral ideals. While guilt can serve as a positive stimulus toward spiritual growth, it should not be confused with sin and can be overcome through faith and divine forgiveness.

name: Guites
originalDescription: About 2500 B.C. the Sumerians suffered severe reverses at the hands of the northern Suites and Guites. (78:8.10)
improvedDescription: A northern people who, along with the Suites, inflicted severe military defeats on the Sumerians around 2500 B.C., contributing to the fall of Sumerian civilization in Mesopotamia.

name: gulf
originalDescription: There exists a great cosmic gulf between matter and thought, and this gulf is immeasurably greater between material mind and spiritual love. (112:2.10) There exists a vast gulf between the human and the divine, between man and God. (110:4.5)
improvedDescription: A vast conceptual divide or separation, particularly referring to the great cosmic gulf between matter and thought, and the even greater gulf between material mind and spiritual love, as well as the vast separation between the human and divine natures.

name: Gulf of Mexico
originalDescription: The marine animals of those ages took temporary refuge in three favorable retreats: the present Gulf of Mexico region, the Ganges Bay of India, and the Sicilian Bay of the Mediterranean basin. (59:6.9)
improvedDescription: A large body of water that formed when Mexico emerged from the sea 310 million years ago and has maintained its identity ever since. During ancient times, it served as one of three favorable refuges where marine animals survived periods of oceanic life destruction.

name: Gulf Stream
originalDescription: The Gulf Stream coursed over the central portion of North America, being deflected eastward to bathe and warm the shores of Greenland, making that now ice-mantled continent a veritable tropic paradise. (59:1.17) The great ocean currents were in function in Miocene and affected climate much as they do today. (61:3.9)
improvedDescription: A major ocean current that in ancient times flowed over central North America before being deflected eastward to warm Greenland's shores, making that continent a tropical paradise. These great ocean currents have functioned throughout geological history, significantly affecting global climate patterns.

name: gulls
originalDescription: 30 million years ago, birds continued to develop on the planet with few major evolutionary changes, and most modern birds, including gulls, herons, flamingos, vultures, hawks, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches, already existed. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Seabirds that were among the modern bird species that had already evolved and existed 30 million years ago, during a period when the planet's bird life continued developing with few major evolutionary changes.

name: Guru Nanak
originalDescription: Guru Nanak or Nanak was the recent 15th century religious leader who founded Sikhism (92:5.8) Sikhism is a synthesis of Islam, Hinduism and Buddhism (92:5.15)
improvedDescription: Guru Nanak was a 15th century religious leader who founded Sikhism by synthesizing teachings from Islam, Hinduism, and Buddhism into one of the most advanced religions of Asia.

name: gyroscope
originalDescription: The gravity-resistant phenomenon of a gyroscope is a fair illustration of the effect of antigravity but of no value to illustrate the cause of antigravity. (9:3.3)
improvedDescription: A spinning device whose gravity-resistant phenomenon serves as a fair illustration of the effect of antigravity but provides no insight into the cause of antigravity.

name: habits
originalDescription: Habits which favor religious growth embrace cultivated sensitivity to divine values, recognition of religious living in others, reflective meditation on cosmic meanings, worshipful problem solving, sharing one’s spiritual life with one’s fellows, avoidance of selfishness, refusal to presume on divine mercy, living as in the presence of God. (100:1.8) It is true that the supremacy of a decision, its crisis relationship, has a great deal to do with its circle-making influence; nevertheless, numbers of decisions, frequent repetitions, persistent repetitions, are also essential to the habit-forming certainty of such reactions. (110:6.6) When these experiences are frequently repeated, they crystallize into habits, strength-giving and worshipful habits, and such habits eventually formulate themselves into a spiritual character, and such a character is finally recognized by one’s fellows as a mature personality. (160:3.2)
improvedDescription: Religious habits that favor spiritual growth include cultivated sensitivity to divine values, recognition of religious living in others, reflective meditation on cosmic meanings, worshipful problem solving, sharing one's spiritual life, avoiding selfishness, refusing to presume on divine mercy, and living as in God's presence. When spiritual experiences are frequently repeated, they crystallize into strength-giving habits that eventually form spiritual character recognized as mature personality.

name: Hades
originalDescription: The Greeks believed that weak men must have weak souls; so they invented Hades as a fit place for the reception of such anemic souls; these unrobust specimens were also supposed to have shorter shadows. (86:4.8) In John the Baptist's parable about the rich man and the poor man, the rich man was tormented in Hades. (169:3.2)
improvedDescription: In Greek belief, Hades was conceived as a place for weak souls of weak men, who were thought to have shorter shadows. In Jesus's parable, Hades is depicted as a place of torment where the rich man found himself after death.

name: Hagar
originalDescription: Hagar was a concubine of Abraham, not a wife (93:9.8)
improvedDescription: Hagar was a concubine of Abraham, not a wife, similar to Keturah in her status within Abraham's household.

name: Haggai
originalDescription: Haggai was a spiritual leader whom, unlike others, the tyranny of the Jewish priests failed to silence in Israel (97:10.3)
improvedDescription: Haggai was a spiritual leader and prophet who, unlike many others, was not silenced by the tyranny of the Jewish priesthood in Israel.

name: hail
originalDescription: Clouds, rain, and hail have all been feared and worshiped by numerous primitive tribes and by many of the early nature cults. (85:4.3)
improvedDescription: Hail, along with clouds and rain, has been feared and worshiped by numerous primitive tribes and early nature cults as manifestations of divine power.

name: hair
originalDescription: Hair in biology Hair as fetish Hair a measurement sample for falseness Events related with Jesus
improvedDescription: Hair has served various purposes throughout human history: as a biological covering for early mammals, as a powerful fetish in primitive societies, and as a symbol in religious practices. In spiritual teachings, even the hairs of one's head are said to be numbered by divine knowledge.

name: half-breeds
originalDescription: For the past centuries more racial hybridization has been taking place on our world than has occurred in thousands of years. The danger of gross disharmonies as a result of crossbreeding of human stocks has been greatly exaggerated. The chief troubles of “half-breeds” are due to social prejudices. (82:6.8)
improvedDescription: The term refers to people of mixed racial heritage, whose chief troubles stem from social prejudices rather than actual biological disharmonies, as the dangers of racial crossbreeding have been greatly exaggerated.

name: Hallel
originalDescription: Hallel is a Jewish prayer, a verbatim recitation from Psalms 113–118 which is recited by observant Jews on Jewish holidays as an act of praise and thanksgiving. (162:6.2)
improvedDescription: The Hallel is a Jewish prayer consisting of responsive reading of Psalms accompanied by waving of branches before the altar during worship services and holidays.

name: halo
originalDescription: The bodies of Adam and Eve gave forth a shimmer of light, but they always wore clothing in conformity with the custom of their associates. Though wearing very little during the day, at eventide they donned night wraps. The origin of the traditional halo encircling the heads of supposed pious and holy men dates back to the days of Adam and Eve. (74:6.5)
improvedDescription: A luminous radiance that traditionally encircles the heads of holy figures in art, originating from the actual light emanations of Adam and Eve's bodies. Their descendants portrayed spiritually advanced individuals with this glowing effect around their heads, establishing the halo as a religious symbol.

name: Hamathites
originalDescription: Hamathites were descendants of Canaan. They inhabited the Kingdom of Hamath, located in what is now western Syria and northern Lebanon. About seven hundred years B. C. , Sargon, king of Assyria, in subduing a revolt in central Palestine, carried away and into captivity over twenty-five thousand Jews of the northern kingdom of Israel and installed in their place an almost equal number of the descendants of the Cuthites, Sepharvites, and the Hamathites. Later on, Ashurbanipal sent still other colonies to dwell in Samaria. (143:4.1)
improvedDescription: Descendants of Canaan who inhabited the Kingdom of Hamath in western Syria and northern Lebanon. Around 700 B.C., they were among the peoples relocated by Assyrian rulers to replace Jews carried away from the northern kingdom of Israel.

name: hammers
originalDescription: Invention Hammers of fear and anguish
improvedDescription: Tools that represented a significant advance in human civilization, originally created by attaching hard flint to wooden handles. The term also metaphorically describes the divine forces of necessity and anguish that forge human character and spiritual development.

name: Hammurabi
originalDescription: Hammurabi, like other ancient chiefs, admitted to punishing some crimes with burning at the stake. (70:10.14)
improvedDescription: An ancient Mesopotamian ruler who established dominance after the fall of Sumerian civilization around 2500 B.C. Like other leaders of his era, he practiced capital punishment by burning for certain crimes, particularly those of a sexual nature.

name: Han Empire
originalDescription: In the times of Jesus there were only two great world powers—the Roman Empire in the West and the Han Empire in the East—and these were widely separated by the Parthian kingdom and other intervening lands of the Caspian and Turkestan regions. (134:5.1) the civilization of the sons of Han comes the nearest of all to presenting an unbroken picture of continual progression right on down to the times of the twentieth century. (79:8.16)
improvedDescription: The great Chinese empire that, along with Rome, was one of only two major world powers during Jesus' time. Chinese civilization under the Han represents the most continuous progression of any culture, spanning nearly forty thousand years with unbroken development through the twentieth century.

name: Hanavard
originalDescription: Hanavard is the 67th Primary Lanonandek in Nebadon; he is a member of the higher body of universal counselors and co-ordinators. He functions as acting chairman of Satania's executive council. Hanavard is the twelfth of this order to serve on Jerusem since the Lucifer rebellion. (45:3.8) (45:3.10)
improvedDescription: The 67th Primary Lanonandek Son in Nebadon who serves as a high counselor and acting chairman of Satania's executive council. He is the twelfth of his order to serve on Jerusem since the Lucifer rebellion.

name: handbreadth
originalDescription: Jesus once said about anxiety: “Which of you by anxiety can add a handbreadth to your height or a day to your life?” (165:5.2)
improvedDescription: A unit of measurement Jesus used when teaching about anxiety, asking his followers which of them could add even a handbreadth to their height or a day to their life through worry.

name: handicaps
originalDescription: how are compensated what cannot do in other universes
improvedDescription: Physical, mental, or environmental limitations that mortals face during their earthly existence. These deficiencies are fully compensated in the morontia career, where all deprivations are remedied and every being receives opportunity for complete spiritual development regardless of their earthly disadvantages.

name: hands
originalDescription: evolutionary advantage ancestral customs the Jewish ceremonial of washing hands Jesus healings
improvedDescription: The human appendages that provided evolutionary advantages to early primates and became central to many religious customs. Jesus often used his hands for healing and challenged traditional ceremonial hand-washing practices, teaching that spiritual cleanliness comes from the heart rather than external rituals.

name: Hannah
originalDescription: Hannah was the mother of Mary, the mother of Jesus. All Mary's family, including her mother, were always very skeptical about the divine mission of Jesus (122:3.3)
improvedDescription: The mother of Mary and grandmother of Jesus. Along with the rest of Mary's family, Hannah remained skeptical about Jesus' divine mission throughout his life, never fully accepting his spiritual calling.

name: Hap
originalDescription: Hap led the college of revealed religion in the Prince's staff. This group had made considerable progress in their attempt to substitute Creator fear for ghost fear before their labors were interrupted by the later confusion attendant upon the Lucifer's secession upheaval. (66:5.13) in rebellion Hap and the entire college of revealed religion remained loyal with Van and his noble band. (67:4.1) Hap presented the early races with a moral law. This code was known as “The Father’s Way” and consisted of seven commands (66:7.8-16)
improvedDescription: Hap was the head of the college of revealed religion on Prince Caligastia's staff who remained loyal to Van during the Lucifer rebellion. He presented early races with a moral code called "The Father's Way," consisting of seven commands that formed the law of Dalamatia for nearly 300,000 years.

name: happiness
originalDescription: achieving happiness nature of happiness Jesus happiness
improvedDescription: Happiness is a state of well-being that arises from the unification of physical, mental, and spiritual systems within the personality. True happiness is linked to spiritual progress, intelligent effort toward worthy goals, and the harmonious coordination of human will with divine spirit guidance.

name: happy hunting grounds
originalDescription: Andites referred by the term “happy hunting grounds” to a supposed place after death. Inferior captives of the blue race who had been declared unfit, after an elaborate ceremony, were drowned in the river and told that they were being sent to “happy hunting grounds”. (80:5.6)
improvedDescription: The "happy hunting grounds" was a term used by Andite invaders to describe a supposed afterlife destination when they ritually drowned inferior captives of the blue race who had been deemed unfit by tribal councils.

name: Haran
originalDescription: Haran was a place of passage on the way to Salem where Abraham's family tarried for a while (93:5.4)
improvedDescription: Haran was a place where Abraham's family tarried on their journey from Ur to Salem. Nahor, Abraham's brother, persuaded the family to stay there for an extended time, delaying their pilgrimage to meet Melchizedek.

name: harbors
originalDescription: harbors named in The Urantia Book: | Alexandria; Antioch; Athens; Bethsaida; Caesarea; Carthage; Charax; Corinth; Cyrene; Ephesus; Joppa; Lasea; Malta; Messina; Naples; Nicopolis; Paphos; Salamis; Syracuse; Tarentum; Troas * Alexandria * Antioch * Athens * Bethsaida (fishing harbor of Capernaum) * Caesarea * Carthage * Charax * Corinth * Cyrene * Ephesus * Joppa * Lasea * Malta * Messina * Naples * Nicopolis * Paphos * Salamis * Syracuse * Tarentum * Troas about harbors in eastern Mediterranean, we are said there were very few good harbors in Jesus time (121:2.2)
improvedDescription: Harbors were important seaports along ancient trade routes, particularly the few good harbors at the eastern end of the Mediterranean where caravan traffic from the Orient connected with maritime commerce to the western world.

name: harem
originalDescription: The purpose of a harem was to build up a strong and numerous body of blood kin for the support of the throne. (83:5.15)
improvedDescription: A harem was an institution designed to build up a strong and numerous body of blood relatives to support a ruler's throne, though the practice could create political complications when abandoned.

name: harm
improvedDescription: Harm refers to injury, damage, or negative consequences that can affect physical, mental, or spiritual well-being.

name: harmony
originalDescription: concord | order; spiritual unity music | music
improvedDescription: Harmony is the keynote of the central universe, representing spiritual unity, proper order, and melodious association. It encompasses both musical expression and the coordination of diverse personalities working together in spiritual unity while maintaining individual originality.

name: harmony supervisors
originalDescription: The Supervisors of Harmony are a corp of tertiary Supernaphim who are in charge in Havona of taking care that everything works smoothly and expeditiously. (26:3.4) Joys of Existence are reflectively attuned to the superaphic harmony supervisors above (28:5.16)
improvedDescription: Harmony Supervisors are tertiary supernaphim who coordinate activities in Havona to ensure everything moves along smoothly and expeditiously. They maintain system harmony and assist in preparing pilgrims of time for their Paradise achievements.

name: harmony workers
originalDescription: Harmony workers are a group of Celestial Artisans who deal with manipulating and organizing spiritual forces to make them visible to mortals. (44:7)
improvedDescription: Harmony workers are celestial artisans who manipulate and organize specialized spiritual forces and energies to make them visible and comprehensible to mortals, working in a unique field of spirit achievement beyond mortal understanding.

name: harp
originalDescription: Jesus was skillful at playing harp, he had an unusual liking for everything musical (123:6.5) (126:1.6) Jesus sold his harp to defray Jude’s school expenses (127:4.10)
improvedDescription: Jesus was skillful at playing harp and had an unusual liking for everything musical. He later sold his harp to defray Jude's school expenses.

name: harpoon
originalDescription: Andonites were the discoverers of stick and harpoon, and made effective use of them (63:5.6)
improvedDescription: The Andonites were the discoverers of the throwing stick and harpoon, making effective use of these tools as fearless and successful hunters.

name: harps of God
originalDescription: “harps of God” are a morontia devices used at Jerusem for reception of space communications (47:10.2) Adam and Eve, when on Earth, could communicate with each other and with their immediate children over a distance of about 50 miles. This thought exchange was effected by means of the delicate gas chambers located in close proximity to their brain structures. By this mechanism they could send and receive thought oscillations. But this power was instantly suspended upon the mind’s surrender to the discord and disruption of evil. (74:6.6)
improvedDescription: The "harps of God" are morontia devices used for reception of space communications on worlds like Jerusem, compensating for the inability to directly adjust immature morontia sensory mechanisms.

name: harvest
originalDescription: Jesus said: “You should no longer say it is such and such a time until the harvest. I tell you the fields are already white for the harvest.” (143:6.1) “The harvest is indeed plenteous, but the laborers are few” (150:4.1) (163:1.3)
improvedDescription: Jesus taught that "the harvest is indeed plenteous, but the laborers are few" and told his disciples that "the fields are already white for the harvest," referring to the readiness of people to receive the gospel message.

name: haste
originalDescription: Religionists seem to live in effective emancipation from harrying haste and the painful stress of the vicissitudes inherent in the temporal currents of time; they exhibit a stabilization of personality and a tranquillity of character not explained by the laws of physiology, psychology, and sociology. (102:2.3) Jesus was never in a hurry; his composure was sublime.  John was an eager and earnest worker, but Jesus was a calm and happy laborer; only a few times in his entire life was he ever in a hurry. (100:7.14) (136:0.1) (171:7.5) One of the great troubles with modern life is that man thinks he is too busy to find time for spiritual meditation and religious devotion. (195:6.7) Primitive man disliked hard work, and he would not hurry unless confronted by grave danger. The ancients were never rushed. (69:2.4)
improvedDescription: Jesus was never in a hurry and exhibited sublime composure, serving as an example of how religionists can live in effective emancipation from harrying haste and the painful stress of temporal life.

name: Hatita
originalDescription: Hatita was one of the towns visited in the mission of Perea, from January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: Hatita was one of the towns visited during the Perean mission conducted by the seventy messengers from January 3, 30 AD until Jesus's death.

name: hatred
originalDescription: Jesus said in his memorable speech: “Hate is the shadow of fear; revenge the mask of cowardice.” (145:3.4) causes of hate effects of hatred believers’ reaction to hatred
improvedDescription: Jesus taught that "hate is the shadow of fear; revenge the mask of cowardice," and believers are called to overcome hatred with love, transforming enemies through acts of mercy and forbearance.

name: Havona
originalDescription: Havona is the central universe that is at the center of all creation, a universe without beginning or end and perfect. At the center of Havona is the stationary Isle of Paradise, the geographic center of infinity and the dwelling place of God. (0:0.5) the awakening in Havona represents for us the celebration of the fourth mortal jubilee (27:7.8) When we mortals attain Havona, we are granted clearance for Ascendington, one of the Sacred Spheres surrounding Paradise, where we are most welcome, but we are not permitted to visit the other six sacred worlds.
