(13:2.6) The original Michael, the firstborn Creator Son, made the Havona passage of all ascending pilgrims as a bestowal in the times of Grandfanda, the first mortal to arrive in Havona (7:5.8-9) Havona circuits circuits of Havona, generally creation of Havona The Eternal Son's bestowal in Havona was transcendental, adding to the experience of all inhabitants and pilgrims. (7:5.5-7) function of Havona The omnipresence of God is discernably present only in Paradise and the central universe, with a wide range of manifestation through various channels. (3:1.6) individual worlds of Havona The Infinite Spirit ministers personally in Havona, serving as an inspiring pattern for all beings in the divine universe. (8:4.3) (16:2.3) Life in Havona (14:5) nature and constitution inhabitants of Havona perfection of Havona The sacred spheres of Paradise emit spiritual light to all realms of the universe, including Havona and the seven superuniverses. (13:0.3-4) Seven Spirits of Havona Circuits pervade; not part of original The Seven Spirits of the Circuits oversee the seven planetary circuits of Havona, each a creation within the pattern universe. (26:2.6) The Supreme Being functions as a spirit personality in Havona, while his other functions in the universe remain uncertain. (116:0.5)
improvedDescription: Havona is the central universe at the heart of all creation, consisting of one billion perfect worlds arranged in seven concentric circuits around Paradise. It serves as the eternal pattern universe and training ground for ascending mortals on their journey to Paradise.

name: Havona Centers
originalDescription: Personality registers in Uversa track Havona Centers. (30:2.94) Power centers in Havona were not required before the creation of superuniverses, but since then, one million have supervised a thousand worlds each, ensuring perfection of energy control unparalleled in the cosmos. (29:2.12) One million Havona Centers supervise 1000 worlds each, ensuring perfection of energy regulation in the divine universe. (29:2.12)
improvedDescription: One million Havona Centers function in the central universe, each supervising a thousand Havona worlds and ensuring perfection of energy control that exists nowhere else in the cosmos.

name: Havona graph method
originalDescription: The prodigious quantities of information assimilated by the Intelligence Co-ordinators are used on Paradise through the Havona graph method. (26:3.8) (27:6.4)
improvedDescription: The Havona graph method is a technique used by Intelligence Co-ordinators to automatically assimilate vast amounts of information, enabling them to absorb in one hour what would require a thousand years using mortal communication methods.

name: Havona natives
originalDescription: Havona natives harmonize viewpoints and comprehend the Supreme through interactions with pilgrims from Paradise and the superuniverses. (117:6.14) Through contact with ascenders, Havona natives and ascending mortals develop a deep and eternal friendship that compensates for spiritual impoverishment and overcomes experiential handicaps. (19:6.2) The personality of the Havona Natives registers with distinction. (30:1.21) (30:2.35) (30:2.124) Havona natives, projected in divine perfection, may evolve in status by fusing with Father fragments and qualifying for membership in the Mortal Corps of the Finality. (19:6.3) nature No new Havona natives are being created, leading to a gradual decrease in their numbers as they join the Paradise Corps of the Finality. (19:0.9) (19:6.4) Havona natives have optional present and future unrevealed destinies, with a unique progression to higher status within the central universe. (14:4.14) (19:6.3) The Seven Spirits of Havona Circuits are akin to Adjusters in their relationship with the native inhabitants, being impersonal and not permanently merging with their personalities. (17:5.5) The significance of lives possessed by Havona natives is unique to the central universe and satiates the perfect ideals of divine beauty and truth. (14:4.12-13) The Havona natives, although naturally brave and loyal, are utterly strangers to saving faith that mortal man climbs from animal status up to the portals of Paradise. (3:5.16-17) (19:6.2) The supreme goal of Havona natives is to enter the Mortal Corps of the Finality, fulfilling their divine perfection through experiential endowments and evolving status towards an unrevealed future eternity-destiny. (19:6.3) (31:1) Trinitization by united parents results in magnificent creature-trinitized sons held in reserve on Vicegerington for future universe ages. (22:7.9-13) (30:2.35) The unrevealed future destiny of Havona natives is a subject of speculation and mystery, as they may evolve in status and potentially be joined by new beings in the central universe. (19:6.3-4)
improvedDescription: The direct creation of the Paradise Trinity who inhabit the central universe and serve as eternal residents of the billion perfect spheres. These perfect beings gain experiential understanding through contact with ascending pilgrims from the evolutionary universes and may evolve in status by achieving fusion with Father fragments to qualify for membership in the Mortal Corps of the Finality.

name: Havona pilgrims
originalDescription: Achievement is the final test for advancing on the Havona circles. (26:5.5) Arriving on Havona, pilgrims possess only one perfection: perfection of purpose, as decreed by the Universal Father to be perfect like Him. (26:4.12-15) Ascendant mortals' personal education begins as they prepare for the long flight to Havona, where their spiritual training is threefold: intellectual, spiritual, and experiential. (30:4.26-30) Ascenders arrive on the pilot world of the 7th circuit, celebrating the 4th mortal jubilee of their journey towards perfection in the central universe. (14:3.4) (14:5.4) (26:4.12) (27:7.8) (106:2.4) Havona pilgrims benefit greatly from contact with creature-trinitized sons, aiding in their adjustment to various majestic beings on their journey to becoming Mortal Finaliters. (27:3.3) Ascendant pilgrims must accomplish a specific task on every world throughout their journey towards the Deities on Paradise. (14:3.4) (14:5.1) (26:3.2) (26:5.4) Havona Pilgrims will learn avout the Seven Spirits of Havona Circutis but do not hold personal communion with them (17:5.4) Grandfanda was the first mortal to attain Havona, journeying alongside the original Michael, the first-born Creator Son. (7:5.8) if defeated So perfect and so divine are the methods and means of selection in the universes of time that never in the records of Havona has an error occurred; no mistakes have ever been made; no ascendant soul has ever been prematurely admitted to the central universe. (14:2.9) After attaining the Infinite Spirit, examinations cease and advancement is based solely on spirituality, with failure not seen as defeat or disgrace in the eyes of the Gods. (26:8.3) After passing through the terminal rest of time, Havona pilgrims finally achieve residential status on Paradise. (26:7.4) There is no time limit on the progress of Havona pilgrims as they journey through the seven circuits of spiritualization towards divinity attainment. (26:3.2) (26:5.5) Havona pilgrims begin their journey to Paradise alongside the Graduate Guide and the Havona Servital. (26:7.4) The Graduate Guide stays with Havona pilgrims from arrival to Paradise culmination, witnessing their entire celestial journey. (24:6.3) (26:7.4) Upon arrival in Havona, the pilgrims send adoration to Creator Son for their safe journey and sonship career, before enjoying a leisurely period of observation and reuniting with fellow ascenders. (30:4.28) Havona pilgrims traverse achieved space without ensupernaphimation in their journey of ascent. (14:5.5) Comprehension leads to Paradise, belief to Havona, and cooperation with the Adjuster secures evolutionary survival. (26:4.15) Havona pilgrims progress inward from the seventh to the first circuit to work towards spiritual development for Paradise. (14:4.16)
improvedDescription: Ascending mortals who have successfully traversed the superuniverses and arrive on the seventh circuit of Havona with perfection of purpose. These pilgrims progress inward through the seven circuits of the central universe, accomplishing specific tasks on each world as they prepare for Paradise attainment and eventual induction into the Corps of the Finality.

name: Havona Servitals
originalDescription: One quarter of Havona Servitals who achieve divine embrace never return, their careers closed as newborn Graduate Guides spontaneously appear in the outer circuit of the Havona universe. (24:7.3-6) For every Havona Servital created, seven Universal Conciliators are brought into being, one in each superuniverse, reflecting the creative enactment of the Seven Master Spirits. (25:2.1-2) (25:3.15) 138 billion Havona Servitals serve on Uversa, embodying the spirit prerogatives of their assigned Master Spirit on the educational worlds of the superuniverses. (25:1.7) Havona Servitals assist ascendant creatures and eventually become Graduate Guides, showing extraordinary attachment and mutual devotion beyond human understanding. (24:7.1-7) (25:1.5-7) (26:7.4) The Havona Servitals, created by the Seven Master Spirits and the Seven Supreme Power Directors, reflect evolutionary potentialities of the time-space domains and are involved in phases of Supremacy. (24:7.8) (25:1.2) The Havona Servitals, fourth creatures, discern reality of spirit and matter and are semiphysical, with every fourth servital being more physical in type. (25:1.3-4) (38:7.6) (44:0.17) Havona Servitals help ascenders on superuniverse capitals and provide assistance on educational worlds surrounding the headquarters spheres of the seven superuniverses. (25:1.5,7) Havona Servitals showcase their unique personalities in personality registers. (30:1.78) (30:2.75) The Havona Servitals, created by the collaboration of the Seven Master Spirits and Seven Supreme Power Directors, are inherently involved in phases of Supremacy, reflecting evolutionary potentialities of the time-space domains and undergoing transformation in response to the will of the Infinite Spirit. (24:7.8) The servitals of Havona are honored for their sacred service in the central universe. (25:1.1) The prodigious number of servitals is constantly growing, appearing in groups of one thousand on the third moment following the assembly of the Master Spirits and the Supreme Power Directors, with every fourth creature being more physical in nature. (25:1.3) The Havona Servitals resemble individual Master Spirits and serve on educational worlds in the superuniverses, inspiring and ministering to ascending creatures of time. (25:1.7) The Havona Servitals stay with the same pilgrim throughout his ascent, providing pleasurable and highly profitable sojourn on the second circuit. (26:10.5)
improvedDescription: Unique beings created jointly by the Seven Master Spirits and Seven Supreme Power Directors who serve in both the central universe and superuniverses. These versatile beings exist in both spiritual and semiphysical forms, assist ascending pilgrims, and may eventually transform into Graduate Guides through divine embrace experiences.

name: Havoners
improvedDescription: The native inhabitants of the central universe, also known as Havona natives, who are the direct creation of the Paradise Trinity and live on the billion perfect spheres of Havona.

name: head-hunters
originalDescription: Sexual surrender to head-hunters or as temple servants provided a means of redemption for women in ancient times, rooted in the connection between primitive worship and human sacrifice. (89:7.4) Head-hunting was a method of capturing enemies' souls, believed by some to reside in the bodily fat or eyes. (86:5.15) Belief in skull fetishes led to the practice of head-hunting, with human body parts such as nails and umbilical cords considered powerful fetishes. (88:1.8) The custom of creating slaves out of murderers’ ghosts motivated men to become head-hunters. (87:2.8) Head-hunters, practicing skull worship, served as scapemen to redeem primitive man in debt to spirits for original sin. (89:0.1) Winners of athletic contests among head-hunters were awarded the first prize of choosing a bride, with marriage requiring possession of a head, which could be bought, or won through riddle contests. (82:3.5)
improvedDescription: Primitive peoples who practiced capturing enemies' skulls, believing this would capture their souls. This practice was connected to ancient beliefs about skull worship, ghost servitude, and spiritual redemption, with some groups requiring possession of a head as a prerequisite for marriage.

name: headaches
originalDescription: Medicine men trephined skulls to heal, eventually mastering surgery for various ailments, including headaches. (90:4.4)
improvedDescription: A physical ailment treated by ancient medicine men through skull trephining, which involved opening the skull to supposedly allow headache spirits to escape as part of early surgical practices.

name: healing
originalDescription: Bethsaida hospital provided material and spiritual treatment under Elman's leadership, with Jesus personally visiting and making a profound impact on each patient. (148:2.1) spiritual healing
improvedDescription: The restoration of health through both material and spiritual means, including Jesus' ministry which combined medical treatment with prayer and faith encouragement, though true healing requires spiritual power and living faith rather than mere material manifestations.

name: healing at sundown
originalDescription: At sundown, 683 were healed by the ruling of Jesus' Father, with celestial beings descending to make them whole in a thrilling spectacle. (145:3.10) The majority did not experience lasting spiritual benefits from the sundown healing demonstration. (145:3.14) Only seven of the 683 enrolled at school for evangelists were among the large number healed at the sundown scene. (148:1.4) Jesus became as much a physician as a preacher due to the unintended demonstration of physical healing at sundown. (145:3.13)
improvedDescription: A extraordinary healing event in Capernaum where 683 people were made whole through divine power after sunset on the Sabbath, creating a spectacular display of creative healing that attracted many but provided lasting spiritual benefit to only a small number.

name: health
originalDescription: Health arises from the integration of truth, beauty, and goodness in human experience, bringing about efficiency in living through unified energy, idea, and spirit systems. (2:7.11) The key to health, mental efficiency, and happiness is found in the unification of physical, mental, and spiritual systems through the dominance of love. (100:4.3) The first focus in all industry is on health, with certain aspects seen as communal priorities while individual and family health issues are considered personal matters; government intervention in personal health matters, including in medicine, is becoming less common. (72:7.2) “A merry heart does good like medicine, seek the Lord with all your heart and find joy in the morning.” (131:2.9) Lut's guardians of health promoted sanitation, primitive hygiene, and cooking to improve public health. (66:5.17-22) A soul dedicated to the will of the Father in heaven fosters spiritual growth, influenced by physical health, inherited temperament, and social environment, while maintaining a balanced triune harmony of development. (100:1.6) (110:6.4) Sickness and health are the result of material causes, not divine favor or punishment. (166:4.10) The coming of the Spirit of Truth purifies the human heart and leads to spiritual joy, which is a tonic for health. (194:3.19)
improvedDescription: The integration of physical, mental, and spiritual systems that arises from the harmonious blending of truth, beauty, and goodness in human experience, leading to efficiency in living and true happiness through unified development.

name: hearing
originalDescription: Throughout the settled eras, the physical evolution of man continues, with vision and hearing extended in light and life for a magnificent race. (55:6.3)
improvedDescription: One of the physical senses that continues to evolve and extend throughout the settled eras of planetary development, becoming enhanced along with vision as mortals progress toward the magnificent race achieved during the era of light and life.

name: hearsay
originalDescription: Speak only what you have heard from me, speak not hearsay. (138:8.4)
improvedDescription: Information that is secondhand or unverified, which Jesus instructed his disciples to avoid when teaching, emphasizing they should speak only what they had personally heard from him.

name: hearth
originalDescription: During the days of Melchizedek, the Andite family altar was central to religious practice in India, under the dominance of Aryan invaders. (94:1.1) The hearth, once a mere fireplace, became a sacred fetish spot, embodying the concept of the law of God. (88:2.3)
improvedDescription: The family fireplace that served as both a practical cooking area and a sacred altar in ancient religious practices, particularly among the Andites in India during Melchizedek's time, and later became a fetish object embodying divine presence.

name: hearts
originalDescription: The prophet warned that the natural human heart is deceitful above all things and oftentimes desperately wicked, originating evil thoughts and deeds within. (145:2.6) (153:3.5) The law written on hearts is a key aspect of the new covenant prophesied by Jeremiah and fulfilled in spiritual understanding. (145:2.5) “Let not your hearts be troubled, for I give you my peace and assure you of triumph through faith.” (180:3.4) (181:1.5) Ancients believed the soul resided in the heart, among various other body parts. (86:5.15)
improvedDescription: The center of human moral and spiritual nature, which according to Jesus is the source of both good and evil thoughts and deeds, and the place where God's law is written under the new covenant prophesied by Jeremiah.

name: hearts of counsel
originalDescription: The Hearts of Counsel, reflective of superaphic intelligence co-ordinators, provide the Divine Counselors with a ruling that incorporates the co-ordinated wisdom of the most competent minds. (28:5.15) The Hearts of Counsel selectively reflect the co-ordinated wisdom and advice of the most competent minds of the entire superuniverse, censored and revised in the light of high minds of Havona and Paradise. (28:5.15)
improvedDescription: A group of reflective seconaphim under the supervision of Divine Counselors who possess and coordinate the wisdom and advice of the most competent minds throughout the entire superuniverse, censored and revised in light of Havona and Paradise counsel.

name: heat
originalDescription: Heat disrupts matter and dissipates energy in the constant cosmic dance of creation and destruction. (15:8.10) (42:4.7) The internal temperature of suns, even your own, can reach an unbelievable 35,000,000 degrees due to intense X-ray bombardment, ultimately breaking down atoms and electrons. (41:7.1-13) (41:9.1) Heat is the measure of electronic activity, fundamental in the creative organization of ultimatons into electrons in the vast expanse of space. (42:4.5,7) Heat can overcome gravity stability in certain internal solar states, but cannot convert ultimatons back into energy. (42:4.8) Heat relieves pain, as early man discovered with sunlight, hot clay, and hot stones. (90:4.6) Light, heat, electricity, magnetism, chemism, energy, and matter are all manifestations of the same eternal energy, evolving and transforming throughout the infinite expanse of the cosmos. (42:4.1-2) The stars of space exist in a thousand different states, some suns shine without heat. (15:6.8)
improvedDescription: The measure of electronic activity and energy that, along with antigravity, disrupts matter and dissipates energy in cosmic processes, reaching temperatures of 35 million degrees in stellar interiors and playing a fundamental role in atomic construction and disintegration.

name: heathen
originalDescription: The heathen rage with narrow outlook, but Jesus's kingdom is founded on love and mercy. (155:1)
improvedDescription: Non-Jewish peoples who, according to the Psalmist quoted by Jesus, rage against God and his anointed, representing those with narrow outlook who oppose the kingdom founded on love and mercy.

name: heaven
originalDescription: “Deliver us from the curse of the ice and save us from our forest enemies, to be mercifully received into the Great Beyond - also referred to as Great Beyond.” (63:6.6-7) ancient conceptions of heaven For Jesus and for The Urantia Book
improvedDescription: The celestial realm conceived as the Great Beyond by early humans and later understood as the mansion worlds, system headquarters, and higher spiritual spheres where mortals progress after death, with the first heaven being the first mansion world and successive heavens extending through Jerusem, Edentia, Salvington, Uversa, Havona, and Paradise.

name: heavenly reproducers
originalDescription: The celestial artisans of the spirit-morontia world reproduce experiences unknown on Urantia with supreme value and eternal significance. (44:2) types
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans of the spirit-morontia worlds who reproduce experiences and realities of supreme value and eternal significance that are unknown on material worlds like Urantia.

name: historic pageanteers
originalDescription: The historic pageanteers are heavenly reproducers and celestial artisans who dramatically reproduce the crucial events of universe records and history. (44:2.6)
improvedDescription: A division of heavenly reproducers who dramatically reproduce and present the crucial events from universe records and history.

name: Hebrew language
originalDescription: The Hebrews adopted an alphabet from the Philistines around 900 B.C., leading to increased writing and a shift towards a unified creation story after the Babylonian captivity. (74:8.9) The Hebrew language, distinctly Andonite, was spoken by the Jewish descendants of Abraham, who carried the strain in their ancestry from the Andonite stock. (80:8.2) Jesus spoke Hebrew fluently, having mastered it as a young man in Nazareth. (123:2.14) (123:5.1,4) Moses' work is scarcely documented due to the absence of written Hebrew language during the exodus from Egypt, with records relying on oral traditions from over a millennium later. (96:5.2) The Hebrew language spoken by priests and rabbis in times of Jesus influenced the subsequent predominance of Greek in Jewish culture. (121:6.2)
improvedDescription: The sacred language of the Jewish people, distinctly Andonite in origin, which Jesus mastered as a young man and used to read scriptures in the synagogue, though the Hebrews adopted their alphabet from the Philistines around 900 B.C. and had no written form during Moses' time.

name: Hebrews
improvedDescription: An ancient Semitic people who became the foundational race of Judaism and later Christianity.

name: Hebrews, Book of
originalDescription: One of the writers of Hebrews recognized Melchizedek's divine mission, comparing him to Jesus as a priest forever. (93:9.11) The Book of Hebrews portrays the unadulterated religious teachings of Jesus, embodying the true essence of Christianity. (196:2.1)
improvedDescription: A New Testament book that recognized Melchizedek's divine mission and compared him to Jesus as a priest forever. The Book of Hebrews is noted as one of the few New Testament writings that portrays the unadulterated religious teachings of Jesus, embodying the true essence of Christianity rather than Paul's personal theological interpretations.

name: Hebron
originalDescription: Abraham King David In Jesus times
improvedDescription: An ancient city in Palestine that served as Abraham's military capital after his separation from Melchizedek, and later became King David's headquarters and the base of operations for John the Baptist's disciples under Abner. Jesus visited Hebron during his final tour through Palestine and his ministry in Judea.

name: Heidelberg race
originalDescription: The Heidelberg race, descendants of superior and prolific Andonites, spread across Europe from the north ice to the southern Alps and Mediterranean. (64:2.3)
improvedDescription: Descendants of superior and prolific Andonites who spread across Europe from the northern ice to the Alps and Mediterranean in the south.

name: height
originalDescription: Adam and Eve, members of the senior corps of Material Sons on Jerusem, were eight feet tall. (74:1.1) Anxiety cannot add handbreadth to your stature or a day to your life. (165:5.2) Unexpected strains of giantism among the green and orange peoples show variation at the expense of the original endowment, with mortal stature tending to decrease from the red man down to the indigo race. (51:4.2)
improvedDescription: A physical measurement of stature. Adam and Eve were over eight feet tall, representing the superior physical form of Material Sons. Among evolutionary races, height generally decreases from the red man down to the indigo race, though unexpected strains of giantism appeared among the green and orange peoples.

name: Heldua
originalDescription: Jesus visited Heldua during his week of teaching and preaching in Phoenicia. (156:3.1)
improvedDescription: A coastal city in Phoenicia where Jesus' evangelists taught during his week-long ministry in the region, with many gentiles being added to the kingdom.

name: helium
originalDescription: In solar energy reactions, hydrogen is converted into helium through complex nuclear-reaction chains involving carbon as an energy catalyst. (41:8.1)
improvedDescription: An element produced in stellar nuclear reactions where hydrogen is converted into helium through complex chains involving carbon as an energy catalyst.

name: hell
originalDescription: In Egyptian religion, souls armed with magic could make their way to Osiris' judgment hall, avoiding evil spirits, to determine their fate in bliss or hell. (95:2.9) Hell was invented for punishment of taboo violators who were seen as committing both crime and sin. (89:2.4) Jewish traditions of heaven and hell, influenced by Zoroastrianism, incorporated the concept of a day of judgment tied to the end of the world. (95:6.6) Primitives entertained no ideas of hell or future punishment, viewing the afterlife as similar to this one without misfortune, leading to a preference for being killed before old age. (86:4.7) The wicked, destroyed by fire under Mithraism, faced judgment for eternal life with Mithras. (98:5.4)
improvedDescription: A concept of future punishment that evolved from primitive taboo violations being seen as combined crime and sin. Various religions developed ideas of hell, from Egyptian judgment halls to Zoroastrian teachings that influenced Jewish traditions, though early humans originally had no concept of future punishment.

name: Hellenes
improvedDescription: The ancient Greek people, also known as Greeks, who developed one of the most influential civilizations in human history.

name: Hellenistic Empire
improvedDescription: The Greek empire and cultural sphere that emerged following Alexander the Great's conquests, spreading Greek culture and influence throughout the Mediterranean and Near East.

name: helplessness
originalDescription: Jesus' triumph over creature personality isolation, and his unity with the Father, offers believers deliverance from helplessness and fear. (184:4.6) Fortune's feelings of helplessness were transformed by Jesus into faith, courage, and devoted service to humanity, leading him to become a leader among Christians in Crete. (130:6) The man at the pool of Bethesda was a victim of his own helplessness, but when he finally took up his bed and walked, he found freedom. (147:3.5)
improvedDescription: A state of powerlessness and inability to act, which Jesus' teachings and example help believers overcome through faith and unity with the Father, transforming despair into courage and service.

name: hem
originalDescription: Veronica touched Jesus’ garment with faith, and was made whole. (152:0.2-3)
improvedDescription: The border or edge of Jesus' garment that a woman with a hemorrhage touched in faith, believing she would be healed, though Jesus clarified that her faith rather than the physical touch brought about her cure.

name: henotheism
originalDescription: Monotheism emerged as a reaction to the inconsistencies of polytheism, progressing from pantheon structures to henotheistic glorification of one god over others, ultimately leading to the exclusive worship of a singular, supreme deity. (104:2.1) The Hebrews passed through henotheism on their journey from polytheism to monotheism, acknowledging the existence of other gods but believing them to be subordinate to Yahweh. (96:1.2,14)
improvedDescription: A religious belief system that exalts one god above many others while still acknowledging their existence, representing an evolutionary stage between polytheism and monotheism that the Hebrews experienced in their spiritual development.

name: hens
improvedDescription: Female chickens, part of the domesticated poultry kept for eggs and meat.

name: Henselon
originalDescription: Except for Henselon, our universe has lost the largest number of Lanonandeks in rebellion due to their excessive personal liberty in choosing and planning. (35:9.8) Henselon is the neighboring universe to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton. (32:2.12)
improvedDescription: A neighboring local universe to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton, notable for being the only universe that has lost more Lanonandek Sons to rebellion than Nebadon.

name: heralds of the resurrection
improvedDescription: Messengers who proclaimed news of Jesus' resurrection, likely referring to members of David Zebedee's messenger corps who spread word of this pivotal event.

name: herders
originalDescription: In those days, herders like Abel would offer their flocks, while farmers like Cain would offer the fruits of the fields to the priesthood, sparking debate and envy between them. (76:2.2) The Andites were expert herders as they poured out of Mesopotamia, contributing to the advancement of surrounding peoples. (78:5.8) Friction between herders and agriculturists, rooted in their divergent viewpoints and reactions, persists due to their historical association with struggle and force versus patience and peace in the activities of peace. (68:5.11) (70:8.7) (81:1.4) The accidental domestication of animals allowed herders to control and harvest entire herds for food. (69:7.2) The herders were the first capitalists, relying on their herds as capital and living off the natural increase, eventually leading to the early origin of serfdom by forcing male prisoners to work the land, with Africans becoming the predominant slave race. (69:8.5) Herding reduced the effort needed to secure food, allowing early man more time for social culture and activities. (69:6.7) Farmers were looked down on by both hunters and herders in the past, despite the fact that working the soil is a blessing, not a curse. (81:1.4) Herders in the pastoral era lived off the interest of their flocks, freeing them from food slavery and allowing time for culture and progress. (68:5.7) The mother-family came to a speedy end under herder mores as power shifted to the father-family due to the control of the chief food supply by herding practices. (84:2.6) No herds were kept in the Garden of Eden, only birds and domesticated species, as instructed by Van. (73:4.1,3) Slavery was not prevalent among herders, who killed male captives in war and only enslaved women and children, as detailed in the Mosaic code. (69:8.4) The rise of animal husbandry in the pastoral era led to women being reduced to social slavery, as man's independence from woman grew and her status steadily declined, mirroring the treatment of animals in society. (68:5.8) (69:7.5)
improvedDescription: Pastoral peoples who domesticated and raised animals for livelihood, representing one of humanity's early economic and social developments that provided capital wealth through livestock and freed time for cultural advancement, though creating social tensions with agricultural communities.

name: heredity
originalDescription: Through divine heredity, beings can inherit attributes not visible in their Creator, such as the transcendent ability of reflectivity found in the Master Spirits and the Conjoint Actor. (17:3.3) about human heredity see genetics topic | genetics
improvedDescription: The transmission of characteristics from creators to their created beings, which can mysteriously manifest attributes in creatures that are not visible in their creators, as demonstrated in the reflectivity abilities of certain celestial beings.

name: hermit
originalDescription: Gautama missed a chance to lead a revival of the Salem gospel by not listening to the hermit Godad. (94:7.4)
improvedDescription: A solitary religious person, specifically referring to Godad, a hermit who preserved Melchizedek traditions and whose counsel Gautama failed to heed, missing an opportunity to revive the Salem gospel.

name: Herod Agrippa
originalDescription: Herod Agrippa feared James Zebedee more than any apostle, and had him executed (139:3.8)
improvedDescription: A ruler who executed the apostle James Zebedee with the sword, making James the first apostle to be martyred, and who particularly feared James above all the other apostles.

name: Herod Antipas
originalDescription: Herod Antipas was tetrarch of Perea and Galilee and played a prominent role in the deaths of John the Baptist and Jesus He had some animosity towards Pilate (185:3.8) Character People close to Herod position building zeal and residences Regarding John the Baptist concerning Jesus
improvedDescription: Herod Antipas was tetrarch of Galilee and Perea during Jesus' ministry, ruling from 4 B.C. to A.D. 39. He was responsible for the execution of John the Baptist and played a role in Jesus' trial, refusing to condemn him despite pressure from religious leaders. Known for his building projects including the reconstruction of Sepphoris and Tiberias, he was called "that fox" by Jesus due to his cunning and unjust treatment of Joseph's family regarding payment for construction work.

name: Herod Archelaus
originalDescription: Herod Archelaus was ethnarch of Judea, Samaria, and Idumea. He was the brother of Herod Antipas. He was deposed by Augustus in A.D. 6. (124:6.2) Joseph feared that Herod Archelaus would follow his father's threatening policy (123:0.5) Archelaus had his palace in Jericho (171:8.2) The parable of the minas is based on the experience of Archelaus and his futile attempt to gain the government of Judea (171:8.2)
improvedDescription: Herod Archelaus was ethnarch of Judea, Samaria, and Idumea and brother of Herod Antipas. Joseph feared that Archelaus would follow his father Herod the Great's threatening policies more than Antipas would in Galilee. He was deposed by Augustus in A.D. 6 and had his palace in Jericho, which served as the basis for Jesus' parable of the pounds about his futile attempt to gain rule over Judea.

name: Herod the Great
originalDescription: Herod the Great was king of Judea, Galilee, Samaria, and Idumea. He was the father of Herod Archelaus (123:0.5) He was the father of Herod Antipas; he built the port of Caesarea; he died 4 B.C. (121:2.11) He was a native of Idumea (121:2.9) The Maccabean palace was the usual residence of Herod the Great's family in Jerusalem (185:4.1) He ruled Judea under the distrust of the Jews, since Herod was not a Jew; in fact, although he professed his loyalty to the Hebrew ceremonial, he built many temples to foreign gods (121:2.9-10)
improvedDescription: Herod the Great was king of Judea, Galilee, Samaria, and Idumea, ruling until his death in 4 B.C. An Idumean by birth, he was father to Herod Antipas and Herod Archelaus. Despite professing loyalty to Hebrew ceremonial observances, he built temples for foreign gods, which made the Jews suspicious of his rule. He was a great builder who constructed the harbor at Caesarea and maintained the Maccabean palace in Jerusalem as his residence.

name: Herod the Idumean
improvedDescription: Herod the Idumean refers to Herod the Great, emphasizing his ethnic origin from Idumea rather than Jewish heritage, which made his rule over the Jewish people particularly controversial and suspect.

name: Herodians
originalDescription: The Herodians pushed for freedom from Roman rule through the restoration of the Herodian dynasty. (137:7.10) The council of religious leaders, including herodians, united in effort to discredit Jesus, avoiding public arrest due to his popularity. (174:2.1)
improvedDescription: The Herodians were a purely political party that advocated for emancipation from direct Roman rule through the restoration of the Herodian dynasty. They united with the Pharisees and Sadducees in efforts to discredit Jesus, despite their usual political differences.

name: Herodias
originalDescription: Herodias was the illegal wife of Herod Antipas; she intensely hated John the Baptist, and orchestrated a plan that ultimately caused the prophet's death. (135:12.2,5-6)
improvedDescription: Herodias was the unlawful wife of Herod Antipas who harbored intense and bitter hatred toward John the Baptist. She orchestrated John's execution by having her daughter dance for Herod at his birthday feast and request the Baptist's head as a reward, successfully manipulating the situation to achieve John's death.

name: Herod’s temple
improvedDescription: Herod's temple refers to the Jerusalem temple as rebuilt and expanded by Herod the Great, representing one of his major construction projects that enhanced the religious center of Judaism.

name: heroes
originalDescription: Help children choose their heroes wisely and guide them in selecting their lifework. (140:5.14) Man has been profoundly influenced by the character of his heroes, both earthly and divine. (92:7.12) A misguided youth, influenced by violent teachings, admired a criminal as a hero, but on the cross, he saw Jesus as the true hero who inspired courage, self-respect, and love. (187:4.5)
improvedDescription: Heroes are influential figures who profoundly shape human character and values. Parents should help children choose their heroes wisely, as people are deeply influenced by both earthly and divine heroes they choose to honor. Jesus represents the ideal hero who inspires courage, self-respect, and moral greatness.

name: herons
originalDescription: 30 million years ago, birds continued to develop on the planet with few major evolutionary changes, and most modern birds, including gulls, herons, flamingos, vultures, hawks, eagles, owls, quails, and ostriches, already existed. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Herons are wading birds that existed 30 million years ago during a period when bird life continued developing on the planet with few major evolutionary changes, alongside other modern birds like gulls, flamingos, and eagles.

name: Hertzian waves
originalDescription: Desde el punto de vista de la iluminación científica de Urantia del siglo XX, el décimo grupo de manifestación de energía ondulatoria son las llamadas ondas hertzianas (42:5.12)
improvedDescription: Hertzian waves are electromagnetic energies utilized on Urantia for broadcasting, representing the tenth group of wave-energy manifestations in the classification of energy forms.

name: Heshbon
originalDescription: Jesus passed through Heshbon when he brought his brother Simon to Jerusalem in A.D. 17. (128:3.2) Heshbon is one of the towns visited by Jesus and the twelve during the second tour of the Decapolis between August 18 and September 16, 29 (159:0.2) It was one of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve, and the seventy during the mission in Perea between January 3, 30 until Jesus' death (165:0.1) This was the last time Abner and Jesus saw each other, and knowing they would not see each other again, Jesus addressed him with emotional parting words (171:3.2)
improvedDescription: A town in Perea that Jesus visited multiple times during his ministry. Jesus passed through Heshbon while bringing his brother Simon to Jerusalem in A.D. 17, and later during his tours of the Decapolis and Perea with the apostles and seventy messengers. It was at Heshbon where Jesus had his final meeting with Abner, the former chief of John the Baptist's apostles.

name: Hestia
originalDescription: Greek virgin goddess of hearth; became Vesta in Rome (98:3.3)
improvedDescription: Greek virgin goddess of hearth; became Vesta in Rome

name: Hezekiah
originalDescription: Hezekiah was the thirteenth king of Judah. ​​The Hebrews worshipped snakes until the days of Hezekiah. (85:3.3)
improvedDescription: King of Judah in whose time the Hebrews ceased their traditional worship of serpents.

name: hidden things
originalDescription: Our Father is not hiding; he yearns for our association and works endlessly to reveal himself to us. (5:1.2.10) There is nothing hidden that shall not be known, all will be revealed in due time. (150:4.2) (151:3.1) (165:3.2-3)
improvedDescription: Jesus taught that nothing is hidden that shall not be revealed, and all secrets will ultimately be made known. Our Father is not hiding from us but yearns for our association and works endlessly to reveal himself to humanity.

name: hides
originalDescription: Early people wrote on hides, tree barks, clay tablets, stone slabs, and paperlike material made from wasps' nests, preserving knowledge for dissemination and conservation. (66:5.9)
improvedDescription: One of the early writing materials used by ancient peoples, made from hammered animal skins to create a form of parchment. Along with tree barks, clay tablets, and stone slabs, hides were used to preserve and disseminate knowledge in the educational system established at Dalamatia.

name: Hierapolis
originalDescription: Leah, prophetess of Hierapolis, followed in her parents' footsteps after Philip was crucified, becoming a fearless evangelist like her mother. (139:5.11-12)
improvedDescription: A city where Philip the apostle was crucified and buried. It was also the home of Philip's daughter Leah, who became a renowned prophetess and continued her parents' evangelistic work after their martyrdom.

name: hieroglyphics
originalDescription: Early writing evolved from message sticks, knotted cords, and picture writing to hieroglyphics and symbolic alphabets. (69:4.7)
improvedDescription: An advanced form of picture writing that developed from earlier message sticks and knotted cords, representing a stage in the evolution toward symbolic alphabets.

name: High Commissioners
originalDescription: High Commissioners accompany Paradise Sons on bestowal missions and are encountered on universe assemblies and missions of the Sons of God. (37:5.8) As race commissioners, the High Commissioners act as friends of the court, advocating for mercy, justice, and fair treatment for all mortal races on the planets they serve. (37:5.5-7) High Commissioners in group Universe Aids. (37:1.6) High Commissioners are recorded in the Uversa personality register. (30:2.145) Over 1.5 billion High Commissioners in Nebadon are ascendant beings of long experience and great service to their native realm. (37:5.6) Spirit-fused mortals may become High Commissioners serving the local universe in the ascension career beyond Adjuster fusion. (37:5.1-10) High Commissioners work with court advisor seraphim to ensure charges against mortals are stated justly and adjudicated mercifully. (39:1.7)
improvedDescription: Spirit-fused ascendant mortals who serve the local universe administration as advocates for mortal races. They begin as race commissioners on planets, interpreting viewpoints and seeking mercy and justice for human races, then advance to higher levels of function. Over 1.5 billion High Commissioners serve in Nebadon, acting as friends of the court in tribunals and accompanying Paradise Sons on missions.

name: High Son Assistants
originalDescription: High Son Assistants are touchingly affectionate, superbly loyal, exquisitely intelligent, supremely wise, and transcendently humble, imparting universal lore on their singular idea while humbly seeking knowledge on other subjects. (22:10.8) High Son Assistants, as personification of some single universal ideal, embody the divine wisdom of the Trinity in a way that surpasses all conceivable expressions and exemplifications. (22:10.2) High Son Assistants carry out routine major and minor sector work within the superuniverse governments. (18:4.4-5) The High Son Assistants impart inexpressible illumination to our deliberations, embodying unique and original personalizations of supreme concepts and stupendous ideals, making them invaluable assets to the superuniverse administrations. (22:10.2-7) High Son Assistants in Uversa personality register. (30:2.61) The High Son Assistants, like the Celestial Guardians, are not indwelt by Adjusters nor Spirit or Son fused, yet they are compensated by the Trinity embrace in their nonfused status as Trinitized Sons of Perfection. (22:9.5) High Son Assistants are not permanently assigned but serve in constant circulation throughout the superuniverse to further the eternal purposes of the Paradise Trinity. (22:10.7) In our superuniverse, there are approximately 8.75 million High Son Assistants who serve throughout the major and minor sectors, including on Uversa, and often accompany us on assignments to remote universes, circulating to further the eternal purposes of the Paradise Trinity. (22:10.7) The High Son Assistants, embraced by the Paradise Trinity in classes of seven thousand, serve as personal aids to the high sons of the governments of the Ancients of Days, exhibiting more distinguished performance than Celestial Guardians. (22:9.1) (22:10.1) The High Son Assistants serve as private secretaries to high superuniverse Sons and support various cosmic entities in their roles within the superuniverse service. (22:10.1) Twice-trinitized High Son Assistants are invaluable beings embodying supreme concepts and ideas, providing essential assistance for the administration of the superuniverses. (18:4.4) (22:1.12) (22:10)
improvedDescription: Twice-trinitized beings who are the personality embodiment of single supreme concepts or universal ideals. They serve as personal aids and private secretaries to high superuniverse Sons, functioning in the governments of the Ancients of Days. These touchingly affectionate and supremely wise beings provide invaluable assistance to superuniverse administrations, though they are humble seekers of knowledge outside their singular area of expertise.

name: high spirit personalities of circuit control (unrevealed)
originalDescription: Circuit supervisors, utilizing high spirit personalities of circuit control, direct the proper circuits for transmission of all spirit messages and transit of personalities throughout the universe. (24:1.12)
improvedDescription: Unrevealed spiritual beings who assist circuit supervisors in directing the proper circuits for transmission of spirit messages and transit of personalities throughout the universe. They work alongside staffs composed of personalities of the Infinite Spirit to maintain universal communication circuits.

name: Higher Personalities of the Infinite Spirit
originalDescription: In personality register, Higher Personalities of the Infinite Spirit include Solitary Messengers, Universe Circuit Supervisors, Census Directors, Personal Aids of the Infinite Spirit, Associate Inspectors, Assigned Sentinels, and Graduate Guides. (30:2.66-73) Master Spirit Number Three directs many high personalities of the Infinite Spirit, presiding over their assemblies and speaking for the Infinite Spirit in council. (16:3.6) orders Home
improvedDescription: Higher Personalities of the Infinite Spirit include seven orders: Solitary Messengers, Universe Circuit Supervisors, Census Directors, Personal Aids of the Infinite Spirit, Associate Inspectors, Assigned Sentinels, and Graduate Guides. Master Spirit Number Three directs many of these high personalities, presiding over their assemblies and speaking for the Infinite Spirit in council.

name: Hildana
originalDescription: Hildana is the name of an anonymous woman mentioned in the Gospel of John as being caught in adultery and who was to be stoned; her husband was a degenerate from Nazareth, a man known to Jesus who had caused her difficulties in her youth. (162:3)
improvedDescription: Hildana is the name of a woman of evil repute who was brought before Jesus by her accusers during his visit to Jerusalem, in an episode that became distorted in later records.

name: hills
improvedDescription: Hills are elevated landforms that are lower and less steep than mountains.

name: Himalayas
originalDescription: 290 million years ago, geology saw the birth of the mighty Himalayas, marking the beginning of significant crustal upheavals across the continents. (59:3.3) (60:1.12) (60:3.15) (61:1.13)
improvedDescription: The Himalayas are a mighty mountain range in Asia that began forming 290 million years ago, representing one of the greatest crustal upheavals in Earth's geological history. This mountain system contains extensive fossil beds and limestone deposits that have been elevated to heights of 16,000 feet in the Himalayas and 20,000 feet in Tibet.

name: Hindu Kush
originalDescription: The growth of commerce became the primary focus for superior tribes from the valley of the Nile to the Hindu Kush. (81:3.1)
improvedDescription: The Hindu Kush is a mountain range that marked one of the boundaries of the region where superior Andite tribes engaged in trade and commerce as their primary occupation.

name: Hinduism
originalDescription: Hinduism, the oldest and most cosmopolitan of the world’s religions, has been influenced by Jainism, Islam, and Christianity over the passing centuries in India. (94:4.1) Hinduism's survival is credited to its deep integration into the social fabric of India, displaying adaptability and tolerance towards other religions. (94:4.9) Hinduism is the most adaptive and amorphic religion, capable of almost unlimited change while maintaining tolerance. (94:4.8) Hinduism, the most ancient and advanced religion, has greatly influenced religious development in both Orient and Occident. (92:6.3,14-15) Hinduism impacted Buddhism and led to its eventual decline in India, where it became assimilated into Brahmanism before succumbing to Islam. (94:9.3) beliefs traditions Jesus and Hinduism
improvedDescription: Hinduism is described as the oldest and most cosmopolitan of the world's religions, having been influenced by various teachings including those of Melchizedek missionaries, Buddhism, Jainism, Islam, and Christianity. It is characterized as the most adaptive and tolerant religion, capable of unlimited change while maintaining its essential nature as an integral part of India's social fabric.

name: Hindus
improvedDescription: Hindus are the followers of Hinduism, known for their devotion to physical disciplines, reverence for certain animals like cattle, and their belief in concepts such as the atman that approximate an understanding of divine presence.

name: Hinnom, valley of
originalDescription: Judas hanged himself in the valley of Hinnom in despair and solitude. (186:1.7)
improvedDescription: The valley of Hinnom is a desolate valley outside Jerusalem where Judas Iscariot went in despair and took his own life by hanging himself from a tree on the steep rocks.

name: hippopotamuses
originalDescription: Mammalian life in Europe was dramatically altered by the arrival from Africa of lions, which virtually exterminated hippopotamuses. (64:4.7) Neanderthalers hunted hippopotamuses 800,000 years ago in Europe. (64:4.2)
improvedDescription: Hippopotamuses were large mammals that lived in Europe during prehistoric times and were hunted by Neanderthalers 800,000 years ago, but were later virtually exterminated by lions that arrived from Africa.

name: Hippos
originalDescription: It was one of the places visited during the period of preparation of the twelve, the first preaching tour to the Sea of ​​Galilee, June 23-July 7, 26, which the twelve made alone (138:9.3) It was also one of the places visited by Jesus, the twelve, and the 117 evangelists during the second preaching tour in Galilee, October 3, 28-December 30, 28 (149:0.1) It was one of the towns visited by the twelve, the twelve evangelists, and other evangelists during the second tour of the Decapolis, August 18-September 16, 29. Here Jesus preached a sermon on forgiveness in response to a disciple's question (159:0.2) (159:1.1)
improvedDescription: Hippos was a city in the Decapolis region where Jesus and his apostles conducted preaching tours on multiple occasions, including during their preparation period and second preaching tour in Galilee. It was at Hippos where Jesus taught a lesson on forgiveness in response to a disciple's question.

name: Hiram
originalDescription: Hiram was the king of Tyre in the time of David and Solomon. Jesus and his apostles taught and preached in Tyre from July 11 to July 24, A.D. 29, baptizing many in the kingdom near Hiram's tomb. (156:4.1)
improvedDescription: Hiram was the king of Tyre during the times of David and Solomon, whose tomb was located near where Jesus and his apostles taught and preached from July 11 to July 24, A.D. 29.

name: hirelings
originalDescription: Amidst the hostile and hypercritical atmosphere of Nazareth rejection, Jesus found himself surrounded by hired ruffians intent on causing trouble. (150:7.3) (150:8.11) (150:9.3) The hireling flees when danger arises, but the true shepherd stands firm and protects the flock. (165:2.8)
improvedDescription: Hired ruffians and rough men used by Jesus' enemies to cause trouble and harass him, particularly during his rejection at Nazareth. Jesus contrasted hirelings, who flee when danger comes, with true shepherds who protect their flock even unto death.

name: history
originalDescription: beginnings of the universe history of Urantia Sacred and profane history Value Orders of beings dedicated to history
improvedDescription: The record of events and experiences across time, encompassing both sacred and secular accounts. History includes the chronicles of universe development, planetary evolution, and mortal civilizations, though revelators note that finite origins alone cannot reveal eternal destinies.

name: Hittites
originalDescription: The ancient Hittites, with their pale skins and broad heads, were descendants of the Andonite stock, dispersed throughout central and southeastern Europe and reinforced by arrivals from Asia Minor. (80:8.2) The Hittites, Assyrians, and Philistines constantly raided tribes in Palestine, prompting Abraham and Lot to seek refuge in a hilly fastness near Salem. (93:5.6) The Hittites, better-organized and highly civilized, often defeated the Arabian Semites in the northern Promised Land. (96:2.2) Hittite teachers from Salem spread the cult to Adamson's descendants around the lake of Van, later reaching Europe, Asia, and even the British Isles. (93:7.2)
improvedDescription: An ancient people descended from Andonite stock, characterized by pale skins and broad heads, who occupied central and southeastern Europe and Asia Minor. They were better-organized and highly civilized than many contemporary groups, often defeating Arabian Semites in northern Palestine, and some became teachers of the Salem cult.

name: Holdant
originalDescription: Holdant is the guardian of the system, the watchman and controller of the incarnated spirits who are above men. He is a Tertiary Lanonandek, the 19th in that body, and is currently one of the seven Lanonandeks who form the provisional government of Satania following the rebellion of Lucifer. (45:3.5)
improvedDescription: A Tertiary Lanonandek, number 19 of that corps, who serves as the custodian of the Satania system and holder and controller of all interned spirits above the order of mortal existence.

name: holidays
originalDescription: Prayer keeps holy days from reverting to mere holidays by conserving and augmenting essential social, moral, and spiritual values, reflecting the mission of early evolutionary religion. (91:1.1)
improvedDescription: Days that would become mere secular celebrations without the safeguarding influence of prayer, which helps preserve the essential social, moral, and spiritual values that distinguish holy days from ordinary holidays.

name: Holocene
originalDescription: The Holocene, the postglacial period, began roughly 35,000 years ago, signifying the end of the great ice age and the beginning of the Adamic dispensation. (61:7.18)
improvedDescription: The postglacial period that began approximately 35,000 years ago, marking the end of the great ice age and roughly corresponding to the arrival of Adam and Eve and the beginning of the Adamic dispensation.

name: Holy Area
originalDescription: The Holy Area on upper Paradise is divided into seven concentric zones, each dedicated to the welfare and advancement of the personalities of different superuniverses and encompassing an expansive residential complex for billions of glorified individual working groups. (11:3.2-4) The residential area of 7 zones within the Holy Area is meticulously organized to accommodate a vast array of ascendant beings and celestial entities on their eternal journey towards Paradise. (11:3.3-4) Abundant souvenirs of our material days can be found in the Holy Land sectors and peripheral Paradise. (11:3.2)
improvedDescription: The outlying residential region of upper Paradise, divided into seven concentric zones that serve as the eternal dwelling places for Paradise Citizens, Havona natives, and ascending beings from the seven superuniverses. Each zone contains vast residential complexes organized into units, divisions, congregations, and assemblies to accommodate billions of glorified beings.

name: Holy City
improvedDescription: A sacred city, likely referring to Jerusalem in its religious significance as a center of worship and pilgrimage.

name: Holy Mother
originalDescription: Tibetans pray to angels, saints, a Holy Mother, and the gods, despite their complex and burdensome religious rituals. (94:10.2)
improvedDescription: A maternal religious figure venerated by Tibetan Buddhists alongside angels, saints, and gods as part of their complex ritual practices and hierarchical religious system.

name: holy of holies
originalDescription: Only high priests and Nazarites, like John, were permitted to enter the holy of holies in the Jerusalem temple, revered with the same respect and veneration as the sanctified Nazarites. (135:1.2) Young Jesus, after wondering what lay behind the veil of separation, spent most of his time at teaching conferences in the temple, seeking answers about the holy of holies. (125:2.5) (125:5.3)
improvedDescription: The most sacred inner chamber of the Jerusalem temple, accessible only to high priests and lifelong Nazarites like John the Baptist. Young Jesus spent considerable time at the temple wondering what lay behind the veil of separation that concealed this holy sanctuary.

name: Holy Spirit
originalDescription: The Holy Spirit is the spirit of the personal presence of the Divine Minister, the Mother Spirit of a local universe. The work of the Adjutant Mind-Spirits is prior to the work of the Holy Spirit and is preparatory to its arrival (36:5.16) John the Baptist revealed to his disciples that though he baptized with water, Jesus would baptize them with the Holy Spirit (135:7.2) (135:9.4,7) Jesus instructed the apostles concerning the nature of the Holy Spirit, but they failed to fully understand (138:5.2) The Master Spirit of Orvonton is likely to have participated in the bestowal of the Holy Spirit (16:4.13) Mortals can know the presence and be conscious of the presence of the Holy Spirit (9:2.5) The Holy Spirit “searches all things” and is able to communicate to other beings all that it knows about us (28:5.22) Spiritual reason, the intelligence of the soul, is the endowment of the Holy Spirit, the gift of the Creative Spirit to man (101:3.2) nature of the Holy Spirit Associated ceremonies
improvedDescription: The spirit presence of the Divine Minister, the Mother Spirit of a local universe, which serves as a spiritual circuit for divine ministry. The Holy Spirit prepares mortal minds for higher spiritual receptivity and works in coordination with the Spirit of Truth to guide and enlighten human souls toward spiritual growth and divine understanding.

name: holy water
originalDescription: The remnant of water fetish survives as a belief in holy water. (88:1.2) Birth rituals historically aimed to protect against ghostly interference and purify the mother, highlighting the perceived danger and uncleanliness of childbirth. (84:4.6) The practice of dipping fingers in holy water, used in Mithraic and Christian ritual, united followers of both religions in ancient Rome. (98:6.4) Holy water, once used by priests to wash feet, was believed to be the ultimate protection against ghosts. (87:6.12)
improvedDescription: Water believed to possess protective powers against ghosts and evil influences, used in various religious ceremonies. Holy water represents a survival of ancient water fetish beliefs and was commonly used for purification rituals, baptism, and spiritual protection in both ancient and modern religious practices.

name: home
originalDescription: The gospel of Jesus, based on the father-child relationship, can only be world-wide embraced when modern homes embody more love and wisdom, as seen in Jesus' home in Galilee and John Mark's home in Judea, where acceptance of the gospel leads to immediate improvement. (177:2.6) Evangelists were instructed to abide in the first worthy home chosen in a city during their sojourn, showing kindness and courtesy to their hosts. (163:4.6) (163:4.14) Few modern homes can compare to the love and wisdom found in Jesus' and John Mark's homes, which exemplify the ideal environment for nurturing children and embodying the gospel teachings of a father-child relationship. (177:2.6-7) The home is founded on full sex equality during the golden age. (52:2.7) The early home, founded on male sex restlessness and female mother love, was a place where woman cultivated the soil to maintain a settled residence. (68:2.6) Home is where woman is, rooted in devoted mother love and the need for settled residence. (68:2.6) history home in civilization value of the home
improvedDescription: The fundamental social institution founded on marriage and family relationships, serving as the basic unit of civilization and the primary environment for child-rearing and moral development. The home represents humanity's greatest evolutionary achievement in social organization, providing stability, love, and the foundation for spiritual and ethical growth.

name: home designers and builders
originalDescription: Divine builders and celestial artisans create real and beautiful spirit domiciles for individuals and working groups, allowing for abundant variety and individual expression in their construction. (44:3.2)
improvedDescription: Divine builders among the celestial artisans who construct and remodel morontia and spirit dwellings for individuals and working groups. These skilled beings create real and beautiful spiritual abodes that accommodate the needs of their inhabitants, allowing for abundant variety and individual expression in their construction.

name: homeless
originalDescription: To bring home the homeless and share bread with the hungry is the true fast the Lord desires. (147:8.3)
improvedDescription: Those without shelter or home, representing one of humanity's fundamental needs. True spiritual fasting involves caring for the homeless by bringing them into one's house, along with feeding the hungry and clothing the naked, as expressions of genuine religious devotion.

name: homesick Phoenician
originalDescription: Jesus' comforting words to the homesick Phoenician helped him find solace and hope amidst envy and discouragement. (133:6.2)
improvedDescription: A young Phoenician man whom Jesus encountered who was discouraged, envious, and longing for home. Jesus offered him comfort and quoted the Hebrew proverb about how a person's gifts make room for them and bring them before great men.

name: Honan
originalDescription: The advanced Chinese civilization in Honan was greatly influenced by the infiltration of half Andonite and half Andite Andites from the west, stimulating the rapid development of the latent tendencies of the superior Chinese stocks. (79:7.1,5) Fifteen thousand years ago, Andites infiltrated Honan from the west, blending with the Andonite settlers in the progressive Chinese settlements. (79:7.1) The hydraulic problems of agriculturists in Honan required group cooperation to solve, promoting peace among farming groups. (79:8.6)
improvedDescription: A region in ancient China where progressive settlements developed through the infiltration of Andite peoples from the west about fifteen thousand years ago. Honan became a center of advanced Chinese civilization, with agricultural developments that required cooperative solutions to hydraulic problems, promoting peace among farming groups.

name: Honen Shonin
originalDescription: Honen was a Japanese Buddhist leader who taught the dual concept of God and the Absolute through belief in Amida Buddha. He taught that the soul can attain Nirvana through sincere and pure faith. (94:12.2-3)
improvedDescription: A Japanese Buddhist leader who, along with Ryonin and Shinran, helped develop the concept of God within Buddhism through teachings about Amida Buddha. Honen taught that souls could attain salvation through sincere faith in the divine mercies and loving care of Amida.

name: honesty
originalDescription: Base your life on the highest truth of divine leading, coordinating truth, beauty, and goodness through wisdom, worship, faith, and love. (110:3.7) It is a mistake to always speak one's mind without spiritual discernment and discretion, as sincerity must be modulated for the intellectual and spiritual well-being of others. (180:5.5) (181:2.21) Early traders' honesty extended only to their own tribes, as they deemed it permissible to cheat strangers. (69:4.3) Face the facts of life honestly, as Jesus instructed, and be a real man determined to live courageously and intelligently. (133:4.11) (193:4.9) Man must grow up in a world of error and falsehood, guided by a love of truth and the willingness to follow it regardless of conclusions. (3:5.10) (103:7.5) The fruits of the spirit include loving service, unselfish devotion, courageous loyalty, sincere fairness, enlightened honesty, undying hope, confiding trust, merciful ministry, unfailing goodness, forgiving tolerance, and enduring peace. (193:2.2) Jesus adopted the title “the Son of Man” after finding the truth in the partially accredited Book of Enoch, embracing it regardless of its apparent source. (126:3.8) Property honesty originally rested on the belief that personal belongings were imbued with the owner's essence, eliminating the need for policing and promoting unity within the group. (69:9.9) Truth makes man free, as the bestowal Son lives and dies for the spiritual uplift of mortal races, establishing a new and living way of salvation and human liberty through sincere recognition of truth. (52:5.4) (101:6.10) (141:7.6) (143:1.4) (162:7.2) (194:2.2) (194:3.5) Truth must strike in heart with living roots, for those who hear the gospel superficially will not withstand tribulation and persecution. (151:2.2) (177:5.2)
improvedDescription: A fundamental virtue involving truthfulness, integrity, and sincerity in all dealings and relationships. True honesty requires spiritual discernment and discretion, as it must be balanced with wisdom and consideration for others' intellectual and spiritual capacity to receive truth.

name: honor
originalDescription: Honor, pride, ambition - futile without an audience to parade before, forming the origins of art, ceremonial, and sports. (68:2.10) To live unselfishly, one must confront the constant demand for honor from within. (3:5.13) Seek only what honestly belongs to you in matters of honor and recognition. (156:5.19) historically
improvedDescription: Honor is a human emotion tied to pride and ambition that requires social recognition to have meaning. It contributed to the early development of art, ceremonies, and competitive activities, as these emotions need an audience for expression and gratification.

name: hopes
originalDescription: Hope, the grandeur of trust, makes facing insecurities and uncertainties a necessary part of human existence. (3:5.8) Man earns status by his faith and hope, a reality of personal experience in contrast to the inherent goodness of the perfect beings of Havona. (3:5.16) Eye has not seen what God has prepared for those who survive the life in the flesh, as the ultraspiritual realities of Paradise are beyond mortal comprehension. (11:4.5) (24:6.2) (43:6.8) The spirit-led mortal's life is marked by the characteristics of the Spirit, for the fruits of the spirit encompass love, joy, peace, long-suffering, gentleness, goodness, faith, meekness, and temperance, revealing the eternal hope that transcends all fear through God's love. (34:6.13) Sometimes our fondest hopes must die to make way for new opportunities, leading us to learn and grow through disappointments. (48:6.36) Spirit-born individuals remain calm as their keenest hopes crash, knowing that such catastrophes are redirecting cataclysms that pave the way for more noble and enduring realities in a new level of universe attainment. (100:2.8) Hope springs eternal in the human breast, providing comfort to those who follow the will of God rather than seeking joy in the material world. (181:1.7) The fruit of the spirit includes undying hope, among other qualities, which believers must show forth in loving service to others. (193:2.2) Jesus repeatedly dashed hopes, but the apostles remained faithful except few who prepared to reject him. (139:0.1) (145:4.3) (153:1.3) (153:5.1) (157:4.1) (158:5.4) Entertaining false hopes led to racial disappointment and frustration, causing confusion among the Jewish leaders who failed to recognize the divine Son of Paradise when he came to them in mortal flesh. (97:8.4) (137:6.5) Serve with tireless affection, admix friendship with counsel, add love to philosophy, and be less critical to lessen disappointment. (192:2.10) Transformation of faith engenders hope, liberating one from the fear of existence and empowering them as a son of the Universal Father. (101:10.8)
improvedDescription: Hope represents the grandeur of trust and serves as a divine quality that sustains mortals through life's uncertainties and disappointments. It is one of the fruits of the spirit, providing eternal perspective that transcends fear through God's love, and enables spirit-born individuals to remain calm even when their fondest ambitions fail.

name: horde
originalDescription: In the most primitive society, the horde was everything and even children were its common property before evolving families and emerging clans and tribes took its place as the social unit. (70:3.1)
improvedDescription: In the most primitive society, the horde was the fundamental social unit where everything was held in common, including children as collective property. The horde was eventually displaced by evolving families in child-rearing and by emerging clans and tribes as the primary social organization.

name: Horeb
improvedDescription: Horeb is an alternative name for Mount Sinai, the sacred mountain where Moses received divine revelations and the law from God.

name: hormones
originalDescription: Chemical reactions resulting from hormones regulate countless vital processes in living organisms. (65:6.6) Spirit reception is influenced by the organization of certain glands comparable to the pituitary bodies, determining inherent imagination and spiritual receptivity. (49:5.19)
improvedDescription: Hormones are chemical substances that regulate countless vital processes in living organisms through complex reactions in the bloodstream. The organization of certain hormone-producing glands, comparable to the pituitary bodies, influences spiritual receptivity and inherent imagination in different mortal races.

name: hornets
originalDescription: Impatience is a spirit poison, and anger is like a stone hurled into a hornet’s nest. (48:7.20)
improvedDescription: Hornets are used metaphorically to illustrate that anger is like throwing a stone into a hornet's nest, creating unnecessary disturbance and danger, while impatience is described as a spirit poison.

name: horsepower
originalDescription: One drop of water containing 100 horsepower exerted over 2 years showcases the incredible energy potential within the atom. (41:7.13)
improvedDescription: Horsepower is a unit of energy measurement used to illustrate the incredible atomic energy potential within matter. A single drop of water contains enough energy equivalent to more than one hundred horsepower operating continuously for two years.

name: horses
originalDescription: evolution domestication value worship of horses
improvedDescription: Horses evolved over millions of years, reaching their modern form about 5 million years ago in North America before migrating worldwide and becoming extinct on their continent of origin. They were among the most important domesticated animals, serving humanity for food, transportation, agriculture, and warfare, and played a crucial role in the spread of civilization and military conquest.

name: Horus
originalDescription: Horus was the Egyptian god of the sky, son of Osiris and Isis. In his legendary battle with Set he loses an eye which is later restored by the god Thoth spitting into his wound, reproducing an ancient belief in the healing power of saliva. (95:2.6)
improvedDescription: Horus was the Egyptian sky god, son of Osiris and Isis, whose legendary battle with Set resulted in the loss of his eye. According to myth, the wise god Thoth restored Horus's eye by spitting on the wound and healing it, reflecting ancient beliefs in the healing power of saliva.

name: Hosea
originalDescription: Hosea is a Hebrew prophet who preached hope and forgiveness through repentance rather than sacrifices (97:4.5-6) The secret writings of Hosea and other prophets began the Old Testament (97:9.20) Jesus said that the Father had spoken in the past through prophets like Hosea (155:6.2)
improvedDescription: Hosea was a Hebrew prophet who followed Amos and preached a gospel of divine love, forgiveness, and mercy. He emphasized repentance over sacrifice and proclaimed that God would show loving-kindness to all people, contributing to the secret writings that became the foundation of the Old Testament.

name: hospitality
originalDescription: Guest friendship was a tradition of temporary hospitality, where departing guests were given a dish to introduce to future visitors, while paying their way with tales of their travels. (70:3.9) Minister your hospitality as one who entertains the children of the Most High, transforming daily toil into a fine art of serving God within each guest. (133:4.8)
improvedDescription: A tradition of welcoming and caring for guests, including the ancient custom of guest friendship where departing visitors received a broken dish piece for future introductions and paid their way with travel stories. Jesus taught that hospitality should be ministered as if entertaining the children of the Most High, elevating daily service into a fine art of serving God.

name: hostility
improvedDescription: A state of opposition, antagonism, or aggressive unfriendliness toward others.

name: hosts to celestial visitors
originalDescription: Morontia Companions entertain superhuman groups on the transition worlds. (48:3.10)
improvedDescription: Gracious Morontia Companions who are dedicated to entertaining superhuman groups of student visitors and other celestial beings who sojourn on the transition worlds.

name: house of Fad
originalDescription: Fad presided over the educational faculty at the library of Dalamatia, organizing and directing the dissemination and conservation of knowledge in the early ages. (66:5.9)
improvedDescription: The library of Dalamatia that contained more than two million separate records and was presided over by Fad, who organized the educational faculty for disseminating and conserving knowledge in the early ages.

name: houses
originalDescription: sayings of Jesus using examples about houses
improvedDescription: Physical dwellings that Jesus frequently used in his teachings and parables, and which served as centers for apostolic ministry, including house-to-house visitation to comfort the afflicted and teach the gospel.

name: housetops
originalDescription: “Proclaim the gospel of the kingdom from the housetops, for nothing hidden shall not be known.” (150:4.2)
improvedDescription: Flat rooftops of houses that Jesus referenced metaphorically when instructing his disciples to boldly proclaim the gospel of the kingdom, emphasizing that hidden truths would be revealed openly.

name: huddling proclivity
originalDescription: The unpredictability of ultimatons is influenced by their huddling proclivity, similar to the variability seen in individual atoms. (42:7.10)
improvedDescription: An unexplained tendency of ultimatons to cluster or group together, which contributes to the unpredictability of electronic behavior in atoms and affects the differential revolutionary velocities of these fundamental particles.

name: Hudson Bay
originalDescription: In northeastern Canada around Hudson Bay, extensive granite elevations contain stone from preoceanic ages, modified by intense metamorphic experiences. (57:8.12) A chance meeting on the shores of Hudson Bay led to the merging of an Indian tribe and an Eskimo group, marking the only contact between North American red men and another human stock for thousands of years. (64:7.19)
improvedDescription: A large bay in northeastern Canada surrounded by extensive granite elevations containing ancient preoceanic rocks that have undergone intense metamorphic experiences. It was also the site where an Indian tribe and Eskimo group met and intermarried about five thousand years ago.

name: Hudson River
originalDescription: The Great Lakes once emptied through the Hudson River during the retreat of the glacier. (61:7.10)
improvedDescription: A river in eastern North America that once served as an outlet for the Great Lakes during glacial retreat, and along whose west bank the Catskill Mountains stand as geological monuments from the Devonian period.

name: human nature
improvedDescription: The inherent characteristics, tendencies, and qualities that define mortal beings and their behavioral patterns.

name: human rights
originalDescription: Every human right is associated with a social duty, meant to ensure group protection and prevent a return to early antisocial conditions. (81:5.4,6) Granting rights to individuals inevitably curtails the liberties of all others, a concept enforced through primitive law and taboos.
