originalDescription: The human mind can conceptually approach the eternity-infinity mystery by envisioning an almost limitless ellipse produced by one absolute cause, guiding the universe's endless diversification toward an ultimate and infinite potential of destiny. (105:0.1) Infinity is both unity and diversity, a concept that becomes one word only due to man's distance from it. (115:3.4) Infinity, capable of self-limitation, holds finite possibility inherent within, expanding mortal comprehension throughout endless careers. (105:1.8) (105:5.4) (108:0.2) (115:2.4) (115:4.3-4) Infinity, existent in 7 Absolutes, encompasses total reality within the Universal Father, Eternal Son, Infinite Spirit, Isle of Paradise, Deity Absolute, Universal Absolute, and Unqualified Absolute, allowing for endless possibilities despite its eternal presence. (104:3.5-12) (115:2.1) God acted to break deadlock of unqualified unity inherent in existential infinity necessitates the eternal cycle of creation, consciousness, and divine action. (118:5.3) God’s infinity provides ultimate assurance for ongoing personal development in the universe, ensuring perpetual opportunities for growth and Deity connection. (106:7.6) It is inconceivable ever to realize the infinite reality integration, as there will always be unexplored possibilities within the three potential Absolutes that cannot be exhaustively experienced. (106:7.1) The Nebadon Melchizedeks denominated the phenomenon of energy transmutation as one of the seven “infinities of divinity,” implying a inherent power in energy catalyzers to segment, organize, or assemble energy in unit formation. (42:2.22) Numbers provide the conceptual basis for contemplating infinity, allowing the finite mind to understand that no matter how many are conceived, one more can always be added, showcasing the limitless nature of mathematical comprehension. (118:0.11) Potential infinity is inseparable from eternity, while actual infinity is partial and nonabsolute. (0:11.12) Infinity is a pre-existential and postexperiential unified reality beyond beginnings and destinies. (106:0.9) Segmentation is required for the human mind to comprehend the unity and diversity within the primordial stasis of infinity. (115:3.2-4) Time-space creatures cannot comprehend the infinity of the First Father's divine plans and purposes. (2:1.10) (116:2.3) The triunities are the functional balance wheel of infinity, unifying the uniqueness of the Seven Infinity Absolutes and enabling the Father-I AM to experience functional infinity unity. (104:4.46)
improvedDescription: Infinity is both unity and diversity, encompassing total reality within the seven Absolutes while remaining capable of self-limitation and reality expression at all levels of universe existence. It provides the ultimate assurance for endless personality development and contains inherent finite possibility, though its complete realization remains inconceivable to finite creatures.

name: Infinity (The)
originalDescription: The Infinity represents an eternal continuum of the I AM that can maintain a triune relationship with The Infinite One and The Infinitude, those theoretical existences of the I AM that gave rise to the first volition and the first repercussion. (105:2.2)
improvedDescription: The Infinity represents the eternal continuum of the I AM that maintains a triune relationship with The Infinite One and The Infinitude in the theoretical portrayal of reality's genesis.

name: influences (spiritual)
improvedDescription: Spiritual influences refer to the various divine and spiritual forces that affect and guide mortal beings, including the ministrations of angels, the Holy Spirit, and other spiritual agencies working for human uplift and spiritualization.

name: infrared rays
originalDescription: Infrared rays slow down electronic activity closer to appreciable heat. (42:5.11)
improvedDescription: Infrared rays are a form of energy manifestation characterized by the slowing down of electronic activity to a stage approaching appreciable heat.

name: infraultimatonic rays
originalDescription: The borderland revolutions of ultimatons begin to assume definite form as infraultimatonic rays, marking the first stage of emergent energy with detectable and measurable wavelike phenomena. (42:5.3)
improvedDescription: Infraultimatonic rays represent the first stage of emergent energy where the borderland revolutions of ultimatons begin to assume definite form, marking the initial phase where wavelike phenomena become detectable and measurable.

name: inheritance
originalDescription: about biologic inheritance, see link to eugenics | eugenics; genetics about inheritance of taxes, see link to taxation | taxation:inheritance tax Jesus urged the rich man to share his inheritance with his brother, emphasizing the spiritual value of wealth over material possessions. (165:4) Fathers like to leave inheritance for their sons, ensuring the continuation of family across generations. (142:7.12) Jesus’ advice to rich man: as a trustee of inherited wealth, consider its sources, disburse dishonest portions with justice and generosity, and use the remainder wisely for the benefit of succeeding generations. (132:5.3,15,23) As steward of inherited wealth, you have a moral obligation to transmit legitimate wealth to succeeding generations while ensuring fairness and justice in the distribution of resources. (69:9.3) (132:5.15) In early times, primitives reckoned inheritance in the female line due to the belief in blood as the creator of the child. (84:2.1) The prodigal son wasted his inheritance in riotous living, only to find himself in want and feeding swine in a distant land. (169:1.7)
improvedDescription: The passing of wealth, property, or possessions from one generation to another, which Jesus taught should be viewed as a stewardship responsibility rather than mere material accumulation. The Urantia Book discusses both biological inheritance and the moral obligations of wealth inheritance.

name: iniquitous one
improvedDescription: A being or personality that has embraced complete wickedness and defiance of divine reality, ultimately facing the loss of continued existence.

name: iniquity
originalDescription: defined as what Jesus said
improvedDescription: The willful, determined, and persistent transgression of divine law and the Father's will, representing an open and persistent defiance of recognized reality that borders on cosmic insanity. Iniquity is the most severe form of spiritual rebellion, beyond sin, error, or evil.

name: initiation ceremonies
improvedDescription: Ritual practices associated with secret societies and religious cults used to induct new members into their organizations.

name: initiative
originalDescription: Belief in charms destroyed; tradition stifles initiative and creativity, leading to slavery to the past. (66:6.2) (88:6.6) The level of freedom or initiative you possess is directly proportional to how much you are aligning with and doing “the Father's will” in human experience. (195:6.16) Industrialism should promote initiative and encourage individualism in a civilized manner, unlike the autocratic and cruel nature of militarism. (70:2.18) Jesus' disciples, equipped with spiritual weapons, are to conquer the world with unfailing forgiveness, matchless goodwill, and abounding love, overcoming evil with good and vanquishing hate with love. (194:3.11) As social classes solidify into caste, personal initiative diminishes, hindering individual development and stifling social cooperation in an evolving civilization. (70:8.13) Men should make the best of their lives, persistently striving towards the betterment of their earthly estate, and accepting their lot with cheerful ingenuity. (148:5.3) (149:5.3) Overdevelopment of institutions diminishes individual worth and initiative, highlighting the need for man to control rather than be controlled by them. (69:1.1) The interruption of the proclamation of individual initiative led to the continued slavery to tradition on Urantia, stifling creativity and preventing liberation from the dominance of fashion. (66:6.2) Life under ghost cults was perceived as a gamble, with one's future being dictated by spirit control rather than personal effort or talent. (87:5.8)
improvedDescription: The personal drive and creative power to act independently and originally. The Urantia Book teaches that initiative is directly proportional to one's spiritual alignment with the Father's will, and that excessive tradition or institutional control can stifle this essential quality for human progress.

name: injunctions
improvedDescription: Authoritative commands or directives, often of a spiritual or moral nature, similar to commandments.

name: injury
originalDescription: The intricate process of wound healing involves the activation of healing chemicals by injured cells, showcasing the intelligent planning behind the phenomenon of mortal existence. (65:4.3-6)
improvedDescription: Physical harm or damage to the body. The Urantia Book describes the intelligent design behind cellular healing processes, where injured cells release chemicals that stimulate neighboring healthy cells to begin healing and regeneration.

name: injured boy at Pella
originalDescription: Jesus ministered a lad who had injured himself in a fall (137:0.1) (137:1.1,4) Jesus believed that real harm could not befall him, as he trusted in his heavenly Father's overcare and had absolute confidence in the friendly nature of the entire universe. (133:1.4) Represent the Father's kingdom with trust in nonresistance and mercy, ministering to those in need and upholding social justice. (140:3.14) (140:8.4) “The wise man universalizes his heart, recompensing injury with kindness to win others' love.” (131:8.4) Jesus used material means in healing the injured boy at Pella to teach his followers not to despise alternative methods in curing illnesses. (164:3.15)
improvedDescription: A young boy whom Jesus ministered to after he was injured in a fall near Pella. Jesus delivered the boy safely to his parents, and this incident led to Andrew becoming Jesus' first apostle.

name: injustice
originalDescription: As ambassadors of my Father's kingdom, do not forcibly resist injustice; put your trust in the Father and be willing to suffer rather than seek vengeance. (140:3.14) (140:8.4) Faith maintains mysterious poise in face of injustice, embracing forgiveness, love, and forbearance. (101:3.9) (194:3.12) Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle advocated for virtue as knowledge, teaching that it is preferable to endure injustice than to perpetrate it, and that it is unjust to seek revenge. (98:2.6) The purpose of the morontia career is to eradicate unfairness, an animal vestigial trait, from mortal survivors. (48:5.8) Jesus detested unfairness and profiteering at the expense of the poor and unlearned, and he did not approve of refusing to employ force to protect the majority against unjust minorities who abuse their power. (173:1.11) Faithfulness, whether in little or much, is a reflection of one's character and integrity. (169:2.6) Sin is contemptible, evil is degrading, but embracing the truth brings happiness and peace. (131:3.3) Human minds on Mars must strive towards divine dignity, despite the imperfections of mortal intellect on Earth. (9:5.5-7) Rejoice in Him who has power to deliver us from all injustice, for soon the hidden plans of our enemies will be brought into the light. (165:3.3)
improvedDescription: Unfair treatment or violation of rights and moral principles. Jesus taught his followers not to forcibly resist injustice but to suffer it willingly rather than seek revenge, while still approving of society's need to maintain justice through proper governmental authority.

name: inner life
originalDescription: The advances of true civilization are all born in the inner life, as it is the only truly creative force. (111:4.3)
improvedDescription: The spiritual and mental realm within each individual where true creativity and civilization's advances originate. It represents the internal spiritual world as opposed to the external material world.

name: inns
originalDescription: The first inns were maintained by smiths, who were the first nonreligious group to enjoy special privileges but eventually became universally hated due to gross abuse of their privileges. (69:3.6)
improvedDescription: The first inns were maintained by smiths, who were the first nonreligious group to enjoy special privileges but eventually became universally hated due to gross abuse of their privileges. After being driven out of villages, smiths maintained these first public lodginghouses on the outskirts of settlements.

name: inquiring Greeks
originalDescription: Thirty inquiring Greeks had faithfully attended Jesus' teachings, resulting in their decision to join the kingdom after a conference at Nicodemus' home. (174:5.5) At Jesus' appearances to the inquiring Greeks, he proclaimed the gospel of the kingdom and urged them to share the truth of sonship with God through faith. (190:4) (193:0) Shortly after two o'clock on this Tuesday afternoon, Jesus, accompanied by eleven apostles, Joseph of Arimathea, the thirty Greeks, and certain other disciples, arrived at the temple and began the delivery of his last address, intended as his final appeal to the Jewish people and an indictment of his enemies, the scribes, Pharisees, Sadducees, and chief rulers of Israel; at last temple discourse, no one asked him a question. (175:0.1) Instructed by Rodan, the Greeks who met with Jesus had advanced knowledge of the kingdom and were considered for ordination by him. (177:3.5-6) Jesus' final message to the inquiring Greeks was one of forewarning, comfort, and encouragement. (182:2.6) Philip’s response to the inquiring Greeks at Jerusalem showcased his inability to adapt to new circumstances, leading him to seek guidance from Andrew before escorting them to Jesus. (139:5.9) The Greeks, encamped alongside eight sleeping apostles, were more vigilant and ready for trouble, as they streamed forth fully armed upon the sentinel's alarm. (183:0.2)
improvedDescription: A group of believing Greeks from Alexandria, Athens, and Rome who faithfully attended Jesus' teachings in the temple. Thirty of them elected to enter the kingdom after a conference at Nicodemus' home, and they were present during many significant events of Jesus' final days, including his last temple discourse and several morontia appearances.

name: insanity
originalDescription: One third believed, one third rejected, one third thought Jesus beside himself, all were astonished by his words. (137:8.18) Through Jesus's intervention, Amos’s transformation and restoration from insanity was miraculously achieved in front of a amazed crowd. (151:6.5) Jesus distinguished between insanity and demon possession, a distinction lost on many in his time due to confusion. (77:7.6-7) (141:4.8) (148:2.3) The continental nation's justice system condemns the incurably insane to death by lethal gas, a shocking practice compared to the pleasing methods of dealing with crime and insanity on Urantia. (72:10.1,3) Insanity is practically nonexistent in the advanced stages of a world settled in light and life. (55:5.2) The human self is not just a sum of conscious states, but relies on a consciousness sorter and associator for unity, or else there would be uncontrolled and random associations of mental madness. (133:7.9) insanity in the primitive man
improvedDescription: A mental condition distinguished by Jesus from demon possession, though this distinction was often confused in his time. Insanity is practically nonexistent in advanced worlds settled in light and life, and primitive peoples often regarded the insane as either divine or cursed beings.

name: insects
originalDescription: about the types named in The Urantia Book, see links below | butterflies; caterpillars; centipedes; cockroaches; crickets; dragon flies; gnats; hornets; locusts; scorpions; spiders Insects with extraordinary appearance quickly spread worldwide, including massive dragonflies and giant cockroaches. (59:5.7) Insects are born fully educated, while humans have the power to shape civilization through educational training. (81:6.24) Fossils of insects preserved in lithographic stone tell the ancient tale of a once submerged Europe during the time of the Andes Mountains' emergence. (60:2.6) Insects are nonprogressive species of early and lower animals in the evolutionary tree of life. (65:2.5) The insects evolved resting stages of suspended animation during times of climate change. (59:6.7) Misinterpretation of the golden rule led to the worship of insects and other animals. (85:3.4)
improvedDescription: Arthropod creatures that evolved during early geological periods, including massive dragonflies and giant cockroaches. Unlike humans, insects are born fully educated and equipped for life, representing nonprogressive species that developed resting stages to survive climate changes.

name: insecurity
originalDescription: Faith contemplation of the Father antidotes the feelings of insecurity arising from the fear of personality isolation in the universe. (143:7.3) Insecurity provides opportunity for hope, driving human existence to face recurrent uncertainties for the grandeur of trust. (3:5.8)
improvedDescription: Feelings of uncertainty and fear of personality isolation that can be antidoted through faith contemplation of the Father. Insecurity provides opportunities for hope and trust, driving human existence to face recurrent uncertainties for spiritual growth.

name: insideness
originalDescription: Religion, through a spiritual viewpoint, reveals the insideness of human experience, allowing for a transformation of the universe from material to spiritual within personality awareness. (103:6.3) (103:7.6)
improvedDescription: The spiritual viewpoint or inner awareness of human experience that allows personality to perceive the universe as spiritual in nature. This inward perspective contrasts with the outward material viewpoint, enabling the transformation of universal understanding from material to spiritual.

name: insight
originalDescription: Only man, with the capability to transcend the blind urge to live and reach the high plane of intelligent art, possesses the self-conscious insight into the meanings of meanings. (160:1.5) Enhancing universe insight by adjusting socialization techniques to grasp eternal goal-meanings in seemingly insignificant activities furthers the Paradise-ascension career. (43:8.10) Humor can help achieve insight by providing a safety valve for pressures of self-contemplation and by lessening the impact of unexpected facts or truths. (48:4.18) Insight can be exercised before experimentation, allowing for learning from looking as well as from leaping. (16:7.2-3) Only a poet can discern poetry in the commonplace prose of routine existence. (48:7.22) Spirit-indwelt man has the power of prevision into the future, visualizing forward-looking and progressive attitudes that are truly human. (12:5.10) Insight precedes foresight in the cosmic economy, with life coming before evaluation or interpretation in all concepts of selfhood. (112:2.6) Stars are best discerned from the lonely isolation of experiential depths, not from the illuminated and ecstatic mountain tops. (48:7.15) Personality's finite dimensions encompass cosmic length meaning, depth value, breadth insight. (112:1.10)
improvedDescription: The capacity for understanding meanings and grasping spiritual truths, often preceding direct experience. Only personalities possess insight in advance of experience, allowing them to learn from looking as well as from doing, and to discern deeper significance in seemingly ordinary circumstances.

name: insincerity
originalDescription: Morontia career eradicates insincerity, teaching mortal survivors to confront situations promptly in mansonia life. (48:5.8) Lucifer's insincerity evolved into deliberate sin as he deceived himself into believing his rebellion was for the greater good, ultimately rejecting mercy and descending into willful evil. (53:2.5) Human minds on Mars reflect the imperfection and insincerity of mortal thoughts. (9:5.5-7) Social architects, seraphim strive to eliminate artificiality and promote sincere social evolution among intelligent beings. (39:3.4)
improvedDescription: A character flaw that the morontia career works to permanently eradicate from mortal survivors. Insincerity can evolve into deliberate sin, as demonstrated by Lucifer's self-deception, and represents one of the animal vestigial traits that must be overcome in spiritual development.

name: inspiration
originalDescription: Jesus' life was inspirational, not exemplary, for all beings in the vast universe, guiding them towards spiritual enlightenment. (120:2.7) (129:4.7) (136:0.1) (140:10.3) (181:1.3) (194:2.8) (196:1.5)
improvedDescription: Jesus' life served as spiritual inspiration rather than a detailed example for all beings throughout the universe. His earth life was designed to inspire and guide all mortal beings on their Paradise ascension journey, providing a high spiritual ideal rather than a literal pattern to copy.

name: Inspired Trinity Spirits
originalDescription: Inspired Trinity Spirits, seemingly omnipresent, operate independently from planetary and universal evolution, with its existence revealed to be near at this very moment. (19:5.7) Nonpersonalized Adjusters are visible only to Personalized Adjusters, but Inspired Trinity Spirits can detect the presence of Adjusters through spiritual reactive phenomena. (107:4.4) function future
improvedDescription: One of the few wholly secret orders of beings created by the Paradise Trinity, capable of superconscious ministry throughout the universes. They appear to be almost omnipresent, operate independently of evolutionary schemes, and may function as teachers bridging gaps in spiritual enlightenment through techniques beyond conscious reception.

name: instability
originalDescription: Overmuch sympathy can degenerate into emotional instability, just like any virtue carried to extremes may become a vice. (149:4.3)
improvedDescription: A state of emotional imbalance that can result when virtues like sympathy are carried to extremes, demonstrating how any virtue taken too far may become a vice.

name: instigators of rest
originalDescription: Upon reaching the end of their journey on the first inner circle of Havona, ascending pilgrims encounter the primary order of supernaphim, known as the instigators of rest, who prepare them for the transition into eternal sleep before the final resurrection. (26:11.6) The primary supernaphim are the instigators of rest for ascending pilgrims nearing the end of their first-circle sojourn. (26:11.6) (27:0.10)
improvedDescription: Primary supernaphim who serve as Paradise inspectors on Havona's inner circuit, preparing ascending pilgrims for the final transition sleep that graduates them from time into eternity. They collaborate with complements of rest to ready pilgrims for their introduction to Paradise.

name: instincts
originalDescription: While the herd instinct plays a role in human society, it is not enough to explain the intricate social organization present today; rather, it is influenced by acquired sociability driven by food hunger, sex love, vanity, and fear, particularly ghost fear. (68:2.4) Inner spiritual progress is influenced by physical health, temperament, and social environment, but innate drives towards growth and self-realization function unless inhibited. (100:1.6) Man's technique of accumulating wealth may vary, but his disposition towards social distinction remains constant. (69:5.13) (84:5.13) Reduced land yield or increased population brings out the worst traits of human nature, while improving land yield and reducing population fosters the better side of human nature. (68:6.4) The training of intellect was ultimately annulled by the tendencies of instinct. (111:7.5)
improvedDescription: Innate drives and behavioral tendencies that influence human society and individual development, including the herd instinct, food hunger, sex urges, and self-preservation impulses. While instincts provide basic motivations, they can conflict with higher spiritual aspirations and intellectual training.

name: institutions
originalDescription: Civilized man's institutions are the accumulated mores of the past conserved by taboos and dignified by religion. (69:0.3) Man should control his institutions, as all human institutions minister to some social need, but their overdevelopment detracts from individual worth-whileness by overshadowing personality and diminishing initiative. (69:1.1) The basic types of institutions include those of self-maintenance, self-perpetuation, and self-gratification, each encompassing different aspects of human society and behavior. (69:1.3-5) | self-gratification; self-maintenance; self-perpetuation Through institutions, man secured civil freedom. (81:5.5) Man should control institutions, not be controlled by them, as they should serve social needs without overshadowing individual worth or stifling initiative. (69:1.1) Institutions arise from social groups and aim to save labor while enhancing group security. (69:0.2) Man should control institutions to ensure they minister to social needs without overshadowing individuals or diminishing initiative. (69:1.1) Man transcends his animal ancestors emotionally through appreciation of humor, art, and religion; socially, he exhibits superiority as a toolmaker, communicator, and primitive institution builder. (69)
improvedDescription: Social establishments that arise from human groups to serve collective needs and enhance security, categorized into three types: self-maintenance (industry, property), self-perpetuation (family, education), and self-gratification (customs, entertainment). While they provide social benefits, their overdevelopment can overshadow individual worth and diminish personal initiative.

name: instruction for teachers and believers
originalDescription: Jesus taught at Edrei: Always respect the personality of man and preach truth with spiritual power, not coercion or fear. (159:3)
improvedDescription: Jesus' principles for preaching truth and teaching the gospel, emphasizing respect for human personality and relying on spiritual power rather than coercion or fear.

name: insubordination
originalDescription: Civilization's protection of children from consequences of foolish conduct contributes to modern insubordination. (84:7.20)
improvedDescription: Modern tendency toward disobedience that results from civilization protecting children from the natural consequences of foolish conduct, unlike ancient times when children learned discipline through direct experience of failure or danger.

name: insult
originalDescription: Man's animal reaction to intellectual or spiritual superiority leads him to insult and physically assault what he cannot attain or achieve. (184:4.5) Despite continued insults, Jesus maintained his dignity and composure, showing his strength in the face of adversity. (186:2.5)
improvedDescription: An animal trait in humans that leads to verbal or physical assault against those who are spiritually or intellectually superior, representing a brutish reaction to what one cannot attain or achieve.

name: insurance
originalDescription: among primitives in a nearby planet in modern time
improvedDescription: A concept of protection against risk that evolved from primitive ghost cults and magical practices into modern economic and social systems, representing humanity's attempt to secure safety against disasters, accidents, and future uncertainties.

name: intellectualism
originalDescription: An impoverished spiritual environment jeopardizes higher religious growth by hindering the crucial link between religion and scientific knowledge, thus highlighting the paradoxical need for balanced intellectual development in faith. (102:3.1)
improvedDescription: The overdevelopment of intellectual factors in religion, which can handicap and embarrass spiritual growth despite the important role that intellectual elements play in religious experience.

name: intelligence
improvedDescription: The capacity for understanding, reasoning, and learning that contributes to cultural progress and enables effective problem-solving and adaptation.

name: intelligence co-ordinators (Seraphic Corps of Completion)
originalDescription: Broadcasters of superior seraphim associate with intelligence co-ordinators in the Seraphic Corps of Completion, facilitating the exchange of essential information through intricate ideographic techniques. (39:2.15)
improvedDescription: Glorified intelligence co-ordinators in the Seraphic Corps of Completion who work with seraphic broadcasters to facilitate the exchange of essential information through sophisticated ideographic techniques.

name: intelligence co-ordinators (tertiary supernaphim)
originalDescription: Broadcasters of superior seraphim associate with intelligence co-ordinators of tertiary supernaphim for reciprocal contact and dissemination of essential information. (39:2.15) The Hearts of Counsel reflective of the highest minds guide Divine Counselors in important decisions. (28:5.15) The tertiary supernaphim are living newspapers of Havona, intelligently coordinating and disseminating information to promote unity among pilgrims in the central universe. (26:3.8)
improvedDescription: Tertiary supernaphim who serve as "living newspapers" of Havona, promoting fraternal association between ascending and descending pilgrims by keeping them informed about universe affairs through advanced communication methods.

name: intelligence corps
originalDescription: Report to Gabriel for guidance in the realms, the superior seraphim of the Intelligence Corps are the mighty hosts of Nebadon. (39:2.3)
improvedDescription: Superior seraphim belonging to Gabriel's personal staff who gather information throughout the local universe for his guidance in the councils of Nebadon, reporting directly to Salvington on independent circuits.

name: intentions
improvedDescription: The purposes or aims that guide conscious decisions and actions, reflecting the inner motivations of individuals in their spiritual and moral development.

name: interbreeding
improvedDescription: The reproduction between different racial or biological groups, which can contribute to genetic diversity and evolutionary development within species populations.

name: intercessors
originalDescription: Greater enlightenment should deliver educated mortals from dependence on intermediaries in communion with Deity, as religious meanings progress in self-consciousness, transferring fear into love from parent-child relationships. (92:7.11) Mithras, a militant god originating in a rock, became an intercessor for humans among gods after slaying the sacred bull. (98:5.3) No mediators are necessary between man and the affectionate Father whose love is the eternal source of mercy. (2:6.6) (6:3.4) During dark centuries, religion reverted to secondhand status, overshadowed by church authority, giving rise to a spiritual menace in a galaxy of saints able to intercede with the divine on behalf of individuals. (195:4.2)
improvedDescription: Intermediaries who supposedly mediate between humans and divine beings, though true communion with the loving Father requires no such mediators according to advanced religious understanding.

name: interdependence
originalDescription: Interdependence unifies all forces and personalities in the vast expanse of the universe, intertwining Creator Sons with power centers and physical controllers, and linking human physical life with the leadings of purposive spirit. (116:6.5)
improvedDescription: The fundamental principle that all forces and personalities throughout the universe are interconnected and rely on cooperative relationships, from Creator Sons and power centers to human physical and spiritual development.

name: internationalism
originalDescription: Internationalism, while a step in the right direction, can never bring lasting peace as long as nations hold onto their sovereign powers. (134:5.10) The post-Adamic epoch is characterized by the dispensation of internationalism, as race blending leads to the decline of nationalism and the emergence of global brotherhood through representative government and a universal educational system. (52:3.10) Internationalism should be fostered through language, trade, sports, and religion. (81:6.19)
improvedDescription: A movement toward global cooperation and unity that represents progress beyond nationalism, though true lasting peace requires nations to surrender sovereign powers to a world government of mankind.

name: interpreters and translators
originalDescription: Morontia Companions are proficient interpreters and translators guiding you in learning new languages on the mansion worlds. (48:3.12-13)
improvedDescription: Morontia Companions who serve as skilled language teachers and communication facilitators, helping ascending mortals learn the tongues of Satania and Nebadon on the mansion worlds.

name: interpreters of cosmic citizenship
originalDescription: Seraphim administrators portray responsibilities of universe government to cosmic citizens. (39:4.7-9)
improvedDescription: Seraphic administrators who guide new citizens of system capitals in understanding their responsibilities of universe government and help quicken their appreciation of cosmic citizenship duties.

name: interpreters of ethics
originalDescription: The interpreters of ethics play a crucial role in aiding Paradise arrivals as they navigate an array of majestic beings from the attainment of residential status to induction into the Corps of Mortal Finaliters, many of whom have already been encountered on the circuits of Havona. (27:3.3) Primary supernaphim are the interpreters of ethics. (27:0.8) Unions of Souls in reflective synchrony with interpreters of ethics help guide intelligent beings towards ethical relationships. (28:5.13)
improvedDescription: Primary supernaphim who serve on Paradise to help ascending mortals adjust to the numerous groups of majestic beings during their transition from residential status to induction into the Corps of Mortal Finaliters. They work in reflective liaison with the Unions of Souls to provide guidance on ethical relationships and the Paradise ideal of proper adjustment to complex interpersonal problems.

name: interregnum of wisdom
originalDescription: The inexorable restoration of the imbalance between self-liberty and self-control during the interregnum of wisdom slows human cultural progress, serving as a vital brake on dangerous velocities of advancement. (118:8.6)
improvedDescription: A period characterized by the inexorable restoration of balance between self-liberty and self-control that occurs when human civilization advances too rapidly, causing material achievement to outrun the evolution of worship-wisdom. This phenomenon serves as a vital brake on dangerous velocities of progress and leads to the "dark ages" that witness society's recession from premature levels of attainment.

name: intersonship circuit
originalDescription: The intercommunication circuit between the Eternal Son and Paradise Sons is exclusive and instant, transcending time and space. (7:6.7) (15:9.7)
improvedDescription: A direct and exclusive communication channel between the Eternal Son and all Paradise Sons scattered throughout creation, based on their spiritual kinship. This circuit provides instantaneous communication that is independent of time though sometimes conditioned by space, and is entirely different from the universal circuit of spirit gravity.

name: intolerance
originalDescription: With revealed religion came autocratic and intolerant theologic egotism, overshadowing the democratic nature of primitive religion. (92:7.2-3) Intolerance caused summary dismissal at Urmia among the diverse faculty of religious teachers. (134:3.6) Intolerance covers secret doubts but courage comes from thoroughgoing honesty and true conviction. (146:3.2) The danger of religious intolerance is greatly multiplied by the institutionalization of religion. (99:6.3) Intolerance derives from the assumption of superiority among religions, leading to conflict instead of peace on earth. (134:4.3-4,8) In olden times, the fear-inspiring doctrine was the most terrible tyrant, enslaving men into bigotry, fanaticism, superstition, intolerance, and barbarous cruelties. (88:2.7) Ethical consciousness unmasks the immorality of human intolerance and condemns the sinfulness of fratricidal strife. (52:6.5) Intolerance is everlastingly inimical to human progress and must be mastered for ideal levels of state functioning, combatting it with the co-ordination of science, commerce, play, and religion. (71:3.2) Intolerance is a mental poison that greatly interferes with spiritual progress. (110:1.5) Intolerance is nonexistent on celestial worlds. (47:4.2) “Stumble not into entanglements of mortal suspicion and human intolerance; love all men as I have loved you, regardless of their beliefs.” (191:4.3)
improvedDescription: A mental poison and mask covering secret doubts about one's beliefs that greatly interferes with spiritual progress and human advancement. Religious intolerance arises from assumptions of superiority among faiths and is greatly multiplied by the institutionalization of religion, while being combated through the coordination of science, commerce, play, and religion.

name: intoxication
originalDescription: among primitives in current religions
improvedDescription: A state that primitive peoples believed rendered one divine, leading to the worship of plants that produced intoxicating substances. Ancient cultures viewed intoxication as a form of spirit possession, with shamans using drugs and intoxicants as fetishes to induce mystical states and impress tribesmen during religious ceremonies.

name: introspection
improvedDescription: The practice of examining one's own thoughts, feelings, and motivations as part of spiritual and psychological development.

name: intuition
improvedDescription: The immediate apprehension or understanding of knowledge without evident rational thought and inference, often associated with spiritual insight and the leadings of the adjutant mind-spirits.

name: invention
originalDescription: Invention drives cultural acceleration, benefiting science and enabling cultural advancement worldwide. (81:6.9) Economic freedom secured through capital and invention enhances societal cooperation. (81:5.5) The inestimable benefits of mechanical inventions liberate mankind from the necessity for unremitting toil, allowing civilization to flourish through leisure and innovation. (81:2.14) The post-Adamic dispensation is an age of great invention, energy control, and mechanical development. (52:3.6)
improvedDescription: The creation of new devices, processes, or methods that drive cultural acceleration and liberate humanity from unremitting toil. Invention benefits science most of all and, combined with capital, provides economic freedom while enabling civilization to flourish through the leisure time it creates for thinking, planning, and imagining better ways of doing things.

name: invisible
originalDescription: Observing effects on the visible reveals movement in the invisible. (115:6.7)
improvedDescription: That which cannot be seen but whose movement and trends can be detected by observing its effects on the visible, as demonstrated in the universe by detecting the movements of Supremacy through observing repercussions in the personalities and patterns of the grand universe.

name: ionization
originalDescription: Increased ionization of the upper atmosphere causes compass variation, which is greater during sunspot cycles. (58:2.9) Heat in the stratosphere ionizes oxygen, creating the realm of constant temperature within the forty-mile ascent. (58:2.6) Sunspots' ionizing influence produces aural displays in the atmosphere. (58:2.8) Ionization occurs when atoms lose their outer electrons through various means such as electrical, thermal, X-ray activities, or solvents. (42:3.8)
improvedDescription: The process by which atoms lose their outer electrons through electrical, thermal, X-ray activities, or solvents, creating charged particles. In Earth's atmosphere, ionization occurs in the upper levels due to solar radiation and sunspot activity, causing phenomena such as auroral displays and compass variation.

name: ionosphere
originalDescription: Storms in the ionosphere can disrupt long-distance radio broadcasts. (58:2.6,10)
improvedDescription: A region of the Earth's upper atmosphere that becomes ionized by intense heat and contains electrified conducting layers that enable long-distance radio transmission, though storms in these outer ionospheres can sometimes disrupt broadcasting.

name: Iowa
originalDescription: The central lobe of the advancing fifth glacier covered most of the state of Iowa in North America. (61:7.5)
improvedDescription: A North American state that was mostly covered by the central lobe of the advancing fifth glacier during the ice age.

name: Iran
originalDescription: historical facts beliefs in Iran
improvedDescription: A region in ancient times known as the Iranian plateau, which served as a corridor for Andite migrations and was home to various peoples including the followers of Zoroaster, who revived monotheistic teachings in Persia.

name: Ireland
originalDescription: 290,000,000 years ago, continent movement in Ireland contributed to the formation of the great Caledonian Mountains. (59:3.3)
improvedDescription: An island where early mountain movements 290 million years ago contributed to the formation of the great Caledonian Mountains, which extended through Scotland to Spitzbergen.

name: Iron Age
originalDescription: Metals were widely used in early industrial and trading cities during an era where distinct periods like the Stone, Bronze, and Iron Ages coexisted simultaneously in different regions, as evidenced by the discovery of a bronze culture in Turkestan dating before 9000 B.C. (81:3.4)
improvedDescription: A period characterized by the widespread use of iron tools and weapons, though distinct Stone, Bronze, and Iron Ages existed simultaneously in different regions rather than as sequential evolutionary stages.

name: Iron (city)
originalDescription: Jesus worked in mines at Iron, but also preached at the synagogue and healed a leper. (146:4) A man from Iron, ridiculed in the temple, caught Jesus' attention amidst the chaos of commerce and confusion. (173:1.6)
improvedDescription: A city in Galilee where Jesus worked in mines, taught at the synagogue, and encountered a simple-minded Galilean man who was being ridiculed by Judeans in the Jerusalem temple.

name: iron (metal)
originalDescription: Iron brought up in lava crumpled and twisted the oldest fossil-bearing stratum. (58:7.6) (58:7.10) Early mankind regarded fire as a sacred spirit or fetish, using iron pyrites and flints to produce sparks for fire while fearing and appeasing it through various rituals and taboos. (69:6.4) The exodus from the Euphrates valley enriched Egypt with skilled Andite artisans who improved metalworking with iron ore from Mount Sinai and the Black Sea. (80:6.3) Iron in red blood cells plays a vital role in oxygenating blood and removing carbon dioxide, showing how organisms adapt to their environment. (65:6.4) Iron settled towards the center of the planet as the earth's crust stabilized 850,000,000 years ago. (57:7.1) (57:8.16) Urantia’s density is about that of cooling stars which can be physically gaseous and tremendously dense at the same time. (41:4.3)
improvedDescription: A heavy metal that settled toward Earth's center during planetary formation and serves vital biological functions, including oxygen transport in red blood cells and various industrial applications in ancient civilizations.

name: ironies
originalDescription: Chinese state's development was delayed by thorough conquests, unlike the red man's, who suffered from excessive warfare. (79:8.1) The fatal weakness of Ikhnaton’s gospel was its greatest truth: that Aton created not only Egypt but the whole world, man, beasts, and foreign lands. (95:5.9) Attempting to clarify the complexities of how living beings control the universe's master circuits would only increase confusion, as they are both living and personal entities beyond human understanding. (29:3.8) Ignorance may be necessary for success in understanding the secrets of Father fragments and other unrevealed beings beyond our conceptual capacity. (13:1.6) (39:5.9) (138:7.1) Despite its regrettable nature, national egotism has played a crucial role in social survival throughout history, intertwining with the chosen people doctrine to forge tribal bonds and nations; however, true societal progress can only be achieved by conquering all forms of intolerance, which can be best addressed through the integration of science, commerce, recreation, and spirituality. (71:3.2) The discovery of pottery through observing burned clay huts led to the independent origin of basket weaving and other ancient arts. (81:2.18) Simon Zelotes was motivated to continue working by the belief that Jesus would soon return, which was reinforced by the misunderstanding of Jesus' words about John not dying before his return. (192:2.5-6) The submergence of Eden coincided with the accumulation of the violet race's reserves for world rehabilitation, suggesting a deliberate timing rather than divine punishment or human error. (73:7.2)
improvedDescription: Paradoxical situations or contradictory outcomes that reveal deeper truths about existence, such as how ignorance can be essential for success or how national egotism, though regrettable, has been necessary for social survival.

name: Iroquois
originalDescription: The Iroquois federation was the closest the American red men came to achieving a state, but they lacked essential elements of modern national life and ultimately did not survive. (71:1.3-12) The Iroquois' adherence to the mother-family structure prevented them from achieving statehood. (84:2.2) The Iroquois reformed funeral waste by conserving property, enabling them to become the most powerful among northern red men. (87:2.10)
improvedDescription: A federation of six Native American nations that represented the closest approach to statehood among the northern red men, but failed to survive due to their adherence to mother-family organization and lack of essential elements like private property inheritance and definite territory.

name: irrigation
originalDescription: For thousands of years, the Adamites labored along rivers of Mesopotamia perfecting irrigation, flood-control, and defenses, preserving traditions of Eden. (78:2.1) Caligastia 100 taught advanced methods of irrigation and material welfare to enhance the human species' development. (66:5.2) The Sumerians utilized irrigation canals for military defense, maintaining their unity and integrity against barbarian invaders. (78:8.5)
improvedDescription: Advanced agricultural techniques involving water management and flood control that were developed by the Adamites in Mesopotamia and taught by Caligastia's staff, and later used by the Sumerians as part of their military canal defense systems.

name: irritation
improvedDescription: A state of annoyance or agitation that can lead to negative emotional responses and complaining behavior.

name: Isaac (Nazareth moneylender)
originalDescription: Isaac was a wealthy Jew, a moneylender to the Gentiles, who offered to financially support Jesus' family if he decided to lead the nationalist patriots of Nazareth. (127:2.5)
improvedDescription: Isaac was a wealthy Jewish moneylender to the Gentiles in Nazareth who offered to financially support Jesus' family if Jesus would assume leadership of the local nationalist patriots.

name: Isaac (Old Testament)
originalDescription: After the covenant between Abraham and Melchizedek, Isaac was born as promised, and this made Abraham take his covenant very seriously (93:6.5) (93:9.8) The idea of ​​Abraham sacrificing Isaac was a very common idea at that time, which Abraham finally refused to commit (89:6.8) Abraham secured a wife for Isaac by sending servants to his brother Nahor's city to find one for him there (93:9.4) Isaac kept his father's teachings well and promoted the gospel of the Salem colony (93:9.5)
improvedDescription: Isaac was the son of Abraham, born in accordance with Melchizedek's promise. After Isaac's birth, Abraham took his covenant with Melchizedek very seriously and formalized it in writing. Isaac maintained his father's teachings and promoted the gospel of the Salem colony, though it was harder for the next generation to fully grasp the significance of Melchizedek's traditions.

name: Isador
originalDescription: Isador is the authentic writer of the Gospel of Matthew. Isador fled Jerusalem in 70 AD after the blockade by the armies of Titus. In 71 AD while living in Pella he wrote the gospel (121:8.5-7) (139:7.5)
improvedDescription: Isador was a disciple of the Apostle Matthew who wrote the Gospel of Matthew. He escaped from Jerusalem in 70 AD during the siege by Titus's armies and took refuge in Pella, where he wrote the gospel in 71 AD using Matthew's personal remembrances and notes, as well as portions of Mark's narrative.

name: Isaiah (first)
originalDescription: Isaiah the first appeared in the wake of the awakening of the Hebrew consciousness (97:5.1) The “birthday text” that Jesus chose as a rule to guide him in his studies at school was drawn from the writing of Isaiah, and is the text of Isaiah 61 verse 1 (97:5.3) (123:5.11) (126:4.2) (140:3.2) (150:8.9) (190:5.4) monotheism was not widely accepted until Isaiah (92:6.17) He preached about a beneficent, liberating king (97:8.3) Isaiah preached about the infinite wisdom and perfection of God (97:5.1-3) he prophesied that since Jerusalem was the city of Yahweh, it would never fall; But that did not happen, and Jeremiah did not hesitate to proclaim his downfall (97:9.22)
improvedDescription: Isaiah the first was a Hebrew prophet who appeared during the awakening of Hebrew consciousness. He preached about the infinite wisdom and perfection of God, proclaimed a beneficent king-deliverer, and wrote the famous text that became Jesus' 'birthday text' at school. His teachings represented a transition from the nationalistic Yahweh to a more universal concept of God, though he prophesied that Jerusalem would never fall.

name: Isaiah (second)
originalDescription: This second Isaiah did much to counteract the many racially selfish misconceptions about the mission of the promised Messiah (97:7.13-14) His description of the infinite attributes of the Universal Father was poetic. Never have more beautiful statements been made about the heavenly Father (97:7.9,13) He was the prophet of the Babylonian captivity (97:7.14) He spoke of salvation by sacrifice and redemption (97:8.3) He wrote chapters 40-55 in the Book of Isaiah (97:7.4) John the Baptist impressed by the writings of Isaiah (135:4.4) Jesus claimed that the Father had spoken through Isaiah (155:6.2)
improvedDescription: The second Isaiah was a prophet of the Babylonian captivity who wrote chapters 40-55 of the Book of Isaiah. He effectively countered many racially selfish misconceptions about the promised Messiah and made beautiful proclamations about the heavenly Father. He preached about salvation by sacrifice and redemption, and his writings were among the most sublime presentations of the spiritual concept of God prior to Michael's arrival on Urantia.

name: Ishtar
originalDescription: Mesopotamian Spirit of Sexual Fertility (95:1.5-7) In Mesopotamia it was customary for all young women to give themselves sexually to a stranger at least once as an act of devotion to Ishtar (95:1.5)
improvedDescription: Ishtar was the Mesopotamian goddess of sexual fertility and mother of gods. In ancient Mesopotamia, it was customary for all young women to submit at least once to the embrace of strangers as an act of devotion to Ishtar, as fertility was believed to depend on this sexual sacrifice.

name: Isis
originalDescription: Isis was the mother of Osiris in the Egyptian mystery cult (98:4.2-8) Egyptian agricultural workers worshipped Isis (95:5.12) Isis worship in Egypt was an ass equivalent to Ishtar in Mesopotamia (95:1.7)
improvedDescription: Isis was an Egyptian goddess in the mystery cult centered around her and her son Osiris. She was worshipped by Egyptian agricultural workers and represented the Egyptian equivalent of Ishtar in Mesopotamia. The worship of Isis included elaborate rituals built around the legend of death and resurrection.

name: Islam
originalDescription: In the sixth century after Christ, Mohammed founded a religion with sublime ideals superior to many creeds of his time. (92:5.3,14) Islam provides deliverance from the rigorous moral standards of Judaism and Christianity, offering salvation from sin and sanctity. (5:4.5) Islam's strength lies in the clear-cut presentation of Allah as the sole Deity, but its weakness is the use of military force for promulgation and the degradation of women. (95:7.6) Islam's strength lies in its clear-cut presentation of Allah as the only Deity, despite its weaknesses. (94:2.8) (95:7.6) Islam is a great international, interracial faith. (92:6.12,16) believes influence of Islam Influences in Islam
improvedDescription: Islam is the religion founded by Mohammed in the sixth century after Christ, featuring sublime ideals superior to many creeds of his time. Its strength lies in the clear-cut presentation of Allah as the one and only Deity, while its weaknesses include the association of military force with its promulgation and the degradation of women. Islam is one of the great international, interracial faiths that provides deliverance from the rigorous moral standards of Judaism and Christianity.

name: Isle of Paradise
improvedDescription: The Isle of Paradise is the eternal center of the universe and the dwelling place of the Universal Father, serving as the geographical heart of infinity and the focal point of all spiritual gravity.

name: Isle of Patmos
originalDescription: John was tactful and sagacious during his imprisonment and banishment to the Isle of Patmos, where he wrote the Book of Revelation. (139:4.13-14)
improvedDescription: A Greek island where the apostle John was imprisoned and banished for four years, during which he wrote the Book of Revelation.

name: isms
originalDescription: The vast gulf between human and divine communication led to the rise of many garbled isms. (110:4.5)
improvedDescription: Various garbled religious and philosophical movements that arise from misunderstood or distorted communications from Thought Adjusters to human minds.

name: isolation
originalDescription: Every impulse of every electron, thought, or spirit is an acting unit in the whole universe; no thing or being lives in isolation. (56:10.14) Prayer prevents isolation of personality while contributing greatly to the development of the religious sentiment. (91:2.7) Religion cures man's sense of idealistic isolation by enfranchising him as a son of God and citizen of a new universe. (101:10.7) Civilized society is the result of man's early efforts to overcome his dislike of isolation, despite the turmoil and conflicts that may arise within it. (68:2.1) The Spirit of Truth destroys feelings of orphanhood, fostering truth and liberating humanity's highest form of freedom. (194:2.2) effect examples Jesus
improvedDescription: The separation of individuals from social, spiritual, or cosmic connections, which can lead to various problems including personality stagnation, spiritual difficulties, and emotional distress, though it can also provide opportunities for communion with God and personal growth when used properly.

name: isolation worlds
improvedDescription: Worlds that have been quarantined or cut off from normal universe circuits, typically as a consequence of rebellion, providing unique opportunities for faith development.

name: Israel
originalDescription: The Children of Israel were named after the days of Abraham. (96:2.3) (136:1.1) Israel conspired with Egypt to refuse to pay tribute to Assyria, leading to their destruction. (97:9.21) Israel's tumultuous history under various dynasties culminated in the rule of city despots who exploited land trade, leading to a significant shift in power dynamics. (97:9.18) Israel's wars with Judah marked a tumultuous period of shifting powers and alliances, culminating in a decisive battle at Karkar against Assyrian forces. (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: The northern kingdom of the Hebrew people, named after the descendants of Abraham, which fell to Assyria after conspiring with Egypt and refusing to pay tribute.

name: Israelites
originalDescription: Only three or four tribes settled in Palestine, ultimately forming the Hebrew nation through intermarriage with the Canaanites. (97:9.1) The Israelite national consciousness began in Ephraim, while the Jewish consciousness originated in Judah, with the Judahites trying to discredit the Ephraimites. (97:9.2)
improvedDescription: The Hebrew people who formed the northern kingdom, consisting of only three or four tribes that settled in Palestine and intermarried with the Canaanites, with their national consciousness originating in Ephraim.

name: isthmuses
improvedDescription: Narrow strips of land connecting larger landmasses, serving as natural bridges for migration and trade routes.

name: Italy
originalDescription: Andites settled Italy and Spain then mingled in Switzerland near Carthage. (80:7.11) Jesus' trip to Rome influenced the religious leaders, instigated the spread of Christianity, and taught a lesson in mercy over self-defense. (132) (130:8.3-6) (133:0-2) The tribes in Italy dwelt in houses erected on piles over lakes, part of the expanding fringes of African, Aegean, and Danubian migrations. (80:8.3)
improvedDescription: A Mediterranean country settled by Andites and later visited by Jesus during his journey to Rome, where he influenced religious leaders and spread his teachings.

name: Jabbok
originalDescription: The Jabbok flows into the Jordan river, a site rich with historical significance and natural beauty. (124:6.6)
improvedDescription: A river flowing from the east into the Jordan River, historically significant as the region where Gideon fought against the invading Midianites.

name: Jacob (evangelist)
originalDescription: James is an unknown disciple of Jesus who led the group of younger evangelists (150:5.1)
improvedDescription: A disciple of Jesus who directed a group of twelve younger evangelists during the Master's teaching ministry.

name: Jacob (messenger)
originalDescription: James was David Zebedee's fastest and most trusted messenger, to whom Jesus entrusted his last farewell message to Abner. (182:2.5)
improvedDescription: Jacob was David Zebedee's fastest and most trusted messenger, to whom Jesus entrusted his last farewell message to Abner at Philadelphia on the night of his betrayal.

name: Jacob of Crete
originalDescription: James was a wealthy Jewish merchant from Crete. During a meeting with Jesus at Passover in 27 AD, they had a fruitful conversation about God the Father and the so-called “wrath of God.” (142:2)
improvedDescription: Jacob was a wealthy Jewish merchant from Crete who met privately with Jesus during the Passover festivities in Jerusalem, seeking to understand Jesus' teachings about God as a loving Father rather than a wrathful deity.

name: Jacob of Emmaus
originalDescription: James of Emmaus, along with his older brother Cleopas, were two shepherds, somewhat believers in Jesus, who were the fortunate witnesses of the seventh appearance of Jesus, while he was on the road to Emmaus. Curiously, the name of James does not appear in the gospels, although that of Cleopas does. (190:5)
improvedDescription: Jacob of Emmaus was a shepherd and the younger brother of Cleopas who, though not a believer in Jesus, was intrigued by the Master's teachings and witnessed Jesus' seventh morontia appearance on the road to Emmaus.

name: Jacob (Old Testament)
originalDescription: Jacob is a grandson of Abraham who had difficulty understanding the traditions of Salem (93:9.5) He slept on a rock because he venerated and anointed it (85:1.1)
improvedDescription: Jacob was the grandson of Abraham who had difficulty understanding the traditions of Salem and was known for sleeping on a stone which he venerated and anointed.

name: Jacob the stone mason (father)
originalDescription: James was a bricklayer and a business partner with Joseph, and they shared several houses in the village; he was next-door neighbor in Nazareth (124:2.4) (126:3.1) Young Jesus sought advice from James on what to do when a rabbi from Jerusalem came to Nazareth with an offer for the family (123:6.9) James must have died around A.D. 20 or earlier, and his house was taken over by his son James and Jesus' sister Miriam (128:7.12)
improvedDescription: Jacob the elder was a stone mason and business partner of Joseph who owned houses in partnership with him in Nazareth and served as a neighbor and adviser to the family until his death around 20 AD.

name: Jacob the stone mason (son)
originalDescription: James was the son of James the bricklayer, Joseph's business partner; he was Jesus' closest childhood friend and self-appointed defender, who kept bullies from taking advantage of Jesus' docility (123:1.4) (124:2.4) (133:1.5) He was a bricklayer like his father and was a business partner of Jesus' brothers (128:2.2) (128:5.8) He married Jesus' sister Miriam in November A.D. 20 (128:5.8) (128:7.10,12)
improvedDescription: Jacob was the son of Jacob the stone mason, Jesus' closest childhood friend and self-appointed defender who protected Jesus from bullies and later became a business partner of Jesus' brothers before marrying Jesus' sister Miriam in November 20 AD.

name: Jacob’s well
originalDescription: Jesus, as a young man, discussed tradition with his brother at Jacob’s well, where He also drank several times and spoke of living water. (124:6.1) (127:3.3) (143:5.2-3) (193:1)
improvedDescription: Jacob's well was a historic water source in Samaria where Jesus stopped during his travels and later had his famous conversation with Nalda the Samaritan woman about living water.

name: Jah
originalDescription: Jah is a Semitic name for the Deity, a shorter version of Yahweh to avoid pronouncing this name. (96:1.9)
improvedDescription: Jah is a Semitic name for the Deity, a shortened version of Yahweh used to avoid pronouncing the sacred name.

name: Jainism
originalDescription: Jainism arose as a significant religious movement in the sixth century B.C. (92:5.12) Ganid's abstract of the Suduanists' teachings, called today Jainism, emphasized righteousness, truth, self-conquest, and compassion towards others. (131:6) The influence of Jainism on Hinduism has shaped the oldest and most cosmopolitan of the world's religions. (94:4.1)
improvedDescription: Jainism arose as a significant religious movement in the sixth century BC, emphasizing righteousness, truth, self-conquest, and compassion, and has influenced Hinduism as one of the world's oldest and most cosmopolitan religions.

name: Jairus
originalDescription: Jairus was one of the leaders of the new synagogue in Capernaum (152:0.1) (153:1.1) Jesus healed Jairus's daughter from a coma, although the healing was always considered a resurrection by witnesses (152:0.1) (152:1.1-3) Jerusalem leaders worked to dissuade Jairus from allowing Jesus into the synagogue (153:1.7) He resigned as chief leader and openly aligned himself with Jesus (154:1.2)
improvedDescription: Jairus was a ruler of the synagogue in Capernaum whose daughter Jesus healed from a coma, though witnesses considered it a resurrection, and who later resigned his position and openly aligned himself with Jesus despite pressure from Jerusalem religious leaders.

name: James, Epistle of
improvedDescription: The Epistle of James is one of the few exceptions in the New Testament that describes the religious life of Jesus, offering a more authentic portrayal of the Master's teachings compared to other New Testament writings.

name: James Alpheus
improvedDescription: James Alpheus was a fisherman and farmer from Kheresa who was selected by James Zebedee to be one of the twelve apostles.

name: James (brother of Jesus)
originalDescription: James is one of Jesus' brothers. He was born on April 2, 3 B.C.E.; he was the second son of Joseph and Mary (123:1.5) James was one of the brothers with whom Jesus got along best (124:4.3) He was the one who accompanied Mary to Sepphoris when the accident occurred that cost Joseph his life (126:2.1) James greatly helped to dispel a problem that had arisen with the nationalistic Zealots of Nazareth with an emotional speech (127:2.8-9) Jesus took James to his first Passover in Jerusalem in A.D. 12. (127:3.1-6) Jesus made him the head of the family when James was eighteen (128:2.4-7) (128:5.1) (134:1.4) He and Jesus signed a contract on family obligations whereby Jesus gave him the repair shop in exchange for James taking charge of the household finances (128:7.13) (129:2.5) He married Esau, a young woman from Nazareth, in November A.D. 20, in a double wedding that also included Miriam (128:5.7) (128:7.10-11) Jesus gave James a house in Capernaum that John Zebedee had purchased there in Jesus' name (134:1.1) He worked in Zebedee's boat shop in Capernaum, taking the position that Jesus had left (134:1.1) (134:9.6) James, along with his brother Jude and John the Baptist, were the only four who heard the voice at Jesus' baptism (135:8.2,6) He accompanied his mother Mary to the wedding at Cana (137:4.4) He joined the apostles during the four months of preparation for preaching (137:7.1) He remained in Jerusalem after Jesus' crucifixion (190:1.10) James was present at the gathering of 120 disciples held at Pentecost following Jesus' death (193:6.1) (194:3.15) He was the only witness of Jesus' third morontia appearance; he was also present at the fourth appearance (190:2.2-6) he was the titular head of the Christian church in Jerusalem (163:2.7) (166:5.3) (194:4.12) James was supported by Peter in the early conflicts between Christians (166:5.5) The Epistle of James is one of the few exceptions in the New Testament that describes the religious life of Jesus (196:2.1) James' character
improvedDescription: James was Jesus' second-born brother, born April 2, 3 B.C. He became the acting head of the family at eighteen and later served as the titular head of the Christian church in Jerusalem after Jesus' crucifixion and resurrection. James was one of only four witnesses who heard the voice at Jesus' baptism and was present for Jesus' third morontia appearance.

name: James of Safed
originalDescription: James of Safed was a citizen of Tiberias who had a fourteen-year-old son who was afflicted with epilepsy, and who was actually possessed by a malevolent midwayer. Jesus healed the boy (158:4-5) James is the one who uttered to Jesus the famous phrase that says “Lord, I believe; help my unbelief” (158:5.2)
improvedDescription: James of Safed was a father from Tiberias whose fourteen-year-old epileptic son was healed by Jesus. He spoke the memorable words of faith mixed with doubt: "Lord, I believe. I pray you help my unbelief."

name: James Zebedee
originalDescription: James Zebedee, nicknamed “Son of Thunder” like his brother John, was married when he met Jesus and had four children; he was from Capernaum; he was 30 years old in A.D. 26; his parents were Zebedee and Salome (139:3.1) He was a fishing companion of Andrew, Peter, and his brother John (139:1.1) (139:3.1) He was baptized by John in A.D. 25. (135:8.1) Together with his brother John, they searched for Jesus during the forty days he was in the hills after the baptism (136:3.7) apostle Jesus' teachings death Attitude
improvedDescription: James Zebedee, nicknamed "Son of Thunder" along with his brother John, was one of Jesus' twelve apostles. He was thirty years old when called, married with four children, and worked as a fisherman in partnership with his brother John, Andrew, and Simon Peter. James was the first apostle to be martyred, put to death by Herod Agrippa.

name: Jamnia
originalDescription: Jesus passed through this town on the Mediterranean coast during his solo journey through Palestine in the year 25 AD. (134:7.4)
improvedDescription: Jamnia was a town on the Mediterranean coast that Jesus passed through during his solo journey through Palestine in A.D. 25.

name: Jansad
originalDescription: Jansad was the son of Evason, the second son of Adam, and therefore his grandson; he was Adam's successor as chief of the Adamite tribes. (76:3.3)
improvedDescription: Jansad was the eldest son of Eveson and grandson of Adam who became the successor of Adam as head of the Adamite tribes.

name: Japan
originalDescription: Andite migration to Japan greatly influenced the ancestors of the Turanians in central Asia and added desirable qualities to the later Chinese stocks. (78:5.6) Asoka established Buddhism in Japan, where it became the dominant religion in one generation. (94:9.1) (94:11.1) Japan hovers over precipice of watery grave due to continental pressure and ocean bottom depression. (58:5.8) The Japanese were driven off the Chinese mainland by powerful northern Chinese tribes in 12,000 B.C. (79:6.3) The library at Alexandria contained manuscripts from Greece, Rome, Palestine, Parthia, India, China, and even Japan. (130:3.4) Salem missionaries in Japan spread the teachings of the Salem cult throughout Asia and Europe. (93:7.2) Shinto is revered in Japan while India is divided among Hindu, Sikh, Mohammedan, and Jain beliefs. (92:6.15)
improvedDescription: Japan is an island nation in the Pacific where Buddhism became the dominant religion within one generation under Asoka's missionary efforts. The Japanese ancestors were driven off the Chinese mainland by northern Chinese tribes around 12,000 B.C., and Japan's eastern coastline hovers precariously over oceanic depths due to geological pressures.

name: Japhia
originalDescription: Jesus visited this town during his second public preaching tour in Galilee. (149:0.1)
improvedDescription: Japhia was a town that Jesus visited during his second public preaching tour of Galilee, which lasted from October 3 to December 30, A.D. 28.

name: Jaram the Hittite
originalDescription: Yaram the Hittite was a student of the school of Melchizedek who was sent by him to invite Abraham and his brother Nahor to Salem. (93:5.5)
improvedDescription: Jaram the Hittite was a student of Melchizedek who was sent to invite Abraham and his brother Nahor to come to Salem to hear the teachings about the eternal Creator.

name: Java
originalDescription: During the Andonic migrations, a continuous land path from England to Java connected Europe and Asia. (64:1.5) Asoka established Buddhism in Java, where it soon became the dominant religion in half the world. (94:9.1) 950,000 years ago, descendants of Andon and Fonta crossed the land bridge connecting Java to Asia. (64:1.6)
improvedDescription: Java is an island in Southeast Asia that was connected to the Asian mainland by a land bridge during the ice age, allowing early human migrations. During the Andonic migrations 950,000 years ago, descendants of Andon and Fonta crossed this land bridge and journeyed eastward to Java. Later, Asoka established Buddhism in Java, where it became the dominant religion.

name: Java man
originalDescription: The bones of the recently found Java man belonged to the descendants of Andon and Fonta, who migrated eastward 950,000 years ago. (64:1.6)
improvedDescription: Java man refers to the fossil remains found in Java belonging to descendants of Andon and Fonta who migrated eastward 950,000 years ago during their expansion across Europe and Asia.

name: jealousy
originalDescription: Judas's jealousy towards Jesus can turn love into hate. (177:4.11) God is jealous for man, never of him. (4:3.1) Jealousy is a mental poison that interferes with spiritual progress and greatly hinders the efforts of the Adjuster to exalt the material mind. (110:1.5) Older spouses among primitives delighted in new wives due to the olden taboos fostering polygyny and the need for younger help with childbearing and domestic work. (83:5.13) The Primates were jealous of Andon's tree house, leading to fear and isolation for the twins as they fled northward. (63:2.1) Sexual jealousy is not innate but rather a product of evolving societal norms and mores. (82:4.4) Rest in the citadel of spirit, unaffected by jealousy or earthly crashes, for spiritual sureties are impregnable. (100:2.7)
improvedDescription: Jealousy is a mental poison that interferes with spiritual progress and greatly hinders the efforts of the Adjuster to exalt the material mind. While God is jealous for man, never of him, human jealousy can turn genuine love into hate through disappointment and resentment. Modern sexual jealousy is not innate but rather a product of evolving social customs and mores.

name: Jebus
originalDescription: The city of Salem transformed into Jerusalem, known for its rich religious and historical significance, including the legacy of Melchizedek and the establishment of David's capital at Jebus. (93:2.4) (97:9.11,17) David solidified his political power by strategically marrying wives from Jebus and other influential figures. (97:9.10-11) David strategically formed a compact with the Jebusites to establish his capital at Jerusalem, a strong-walled city sitting between Judah and Israel. (97:9.11)
improvedDescription: Jebus was the ancient name for the city that became Jerusalem, originally the site of Salem where Melchizedek established his community. David made a strategic compact with the Jebusites and established his capital of the united kingdom at Jebus, a strong-walled city positioned between Judah and Israel.

name: Jebusites
originalDescription: David welcomed the non-Hebrew Palestinians, including Jebusites, into his new kingdom in Judah, where they shared the Hebrew concept of land ownership. (97:9.7)
improvedDescription: The Jebusites were a Canaanite people who inhabited the city of Jebus (later Jerusalem). David welcomed these non-Hebrew Palestinians into his new kingdom of Judah, where they shared the Hebrew concept of land ownership and held the ideologies of the desert clans.

name: Jehoash
originalDescription: Jehoash and Jeroboam brought new life by delivering Israel from its enemies, while the gangster-nobility in Samaria led to the secret writing of Elijah, Amos, and Hosea, marking the beginning of the Jewish and Christian Bibles. (97:9.20)
improvedDescription: Jehoash was a king of Israel who, along with his son Jeroboam, delivered Israel from its enemies and brought new life to the nation. Their reign preceded the period when a gangster-nobility ruled in Samaria, which led to the secret writing of Elijah, Amos, and Hosea.

name: Jehonadab
originalDescription: Jehonadab, son of Rechab, was a gypsy chieftain who helped Jehu destroy the Samaritan prophets of Baal; his nomadic life was opposed to the sedentary life of the cities and the purchase of land, which they considered sinful (97:9.19)
improvedDescription: Jehonadab, son of Rechab, was a gypsy chieftain who joined forces with the country landlord Jehu to destroy the prophets of Baal at Samaria. He opposed the sedentary life of the cities and considered the purchase of land to be sinful.

name: Jehovah
originalDescription: Jehovah is a term that has been used in recent times to designate the definitive concept of Yahweh, arising from the long experience of the Hebrews; the term was first used around 1500 A.D. (96:1.10) Originally Jehovah was the demonic spirit of Mount Horeb (94:12.1)
improvedDescription: Jehovah is a term used in recent times to designate the completed concept of Yahweh that evolved through the long Hebrew experience. The name Jehovah did not come into use until fifteen hundred years after the times of Jesus, around 1500 A.D.

name: Jehu
originalDescription: Jeshu is considered the tenth king of Israel although The Urantia Book calls him a landowner and not a king; he joined forces with Jonadab to destroy the prophets of Baal in Samaria (97:9.19)
improvedDescription: Jehu was a country landlord who joined forces with the gypsy chieftain Jehonadab to destroy the prophets of Baal at Samaria. He supported Elijah's reform defending the old land customs against the land-selling attitude of the Baalim and the attempt of cities to dominate the countryside.

name: jellyfish
originalDescription: 310 million years ago, there were many varieties of jellyfish, which have since become extinct, along with well-developed cephalopods and primitive ancestors of corals. (59:2.11) (65:2.5)
improvedDescription: Jellyfish were marine animals that existed in many varieties during ancient geological periods, particularly 310 million years ago. These early jellyfish species have since become extinct, though they were abundant during the time when lime-secreting algae were widespread and cephalopods were well developed.

name: Jephthah
originalDescription: Jephthah is a biblical figure, a judge of Israel, who offered his only daughter as a human sacrifice to fulfill a sacred promise; a clear example of how heartbreaking ancient religious customs could be (89:6.3)
improvedDescription: A biblical judge of Israel who made a tragic vow to sacrifice whatever first came out of his house to greet him in exchange for victory over his enemies. When his daughter and only child came to welcome him home, he fulfilled his promise and offered her as a human sacrifice, illustrating the heartbreaking conflicts between ancient religious customs and advancing civilization.

name: Jeramy
originalDescription: Jerami was a Greek Jewish proselyte from Nicopolis who met Jesus during the latter's Mediterranean voyage; he would later meet Paul, during the apostle's third missionary journey. (133:2.5)
improvedDescription: A Greek Jewish proselyte from Nicopolis who hosted Jesus and his companions during their Mediterranean voyage. Paul later spent a winter with Jeramy's son during his third missionary journey.

name: Jeremiah
originalDescription: Jeremiah, the fearless prophet, boldly proclaimed Yahweh as the God of all nations, ushering in a new era of universal monotheism. (97:6) Jeremiah fearlessly declared Yahweh as the universal God of all nations, transcending the confines of Hebrew identity. (97:6.2) Jeremiah counseled surrendering Jerusalem to Nebuchadnezzar, as he believed in the righteousness and power of the Lord. (97:6.4) In the filthy dungeon, Jeremiah sank in mire as the priests sought his death for warning his people. (153:2.3) Jeremiah boldly proclaimed the downfall of Jerusalem, despite Isaiah's reassurance that the city would never fall. (97:9.22) Jeremiah proclaimed an era of inner righteousness, but deliverance was deferred despite the cries of the prophets and priests. (97:8.3) The Prophet Jeremiah warned that the human heart is deceitful above all things, leading people astray into foolishness, lusts, pleasures, malice, envy, and hatred.
