(143:2.5) Jeremiah warned the Judahites of their impending doom as Nebuchadnezzar returned to rule over Babylon. (97:9.25) Jeremiah foresaw the law written in men’s hearts, a vision that the Jews could not fully embrace due to the fetters of tradition, leading to the transfer of religious evolution to European peoples. (121:7.5) Jesus quoted Jeremiah to encourage Ezra in his search for God, leading to the foundation of the first Christian church in Syracuse. (130:8.2) (145:2.5) (149:6.12) (153:2.2) (165:4.8)
improvedDescription: A Hebrew prophet who boldly proclaimed Yahweh as the universal God of all nations, not just the Hebrews. He fearlessly declared that righteousness comes from within the heart rather than external observances, counseled surrender to Babylon during Jerusalem's siege, and proclaimed the downfall of the city despite opposition from priests and rulers who cast him into a dungeon.

name: Jericho
originalDescription: ancient times Jesus times
improvedDescription: An ancient Palestinian city near the Jordan River crossing where Joshua and the Israelites first entered the promised land. Jesus visited Jericho multiple times during his ministry, healing Bartimeus the blind beggar and encountering Zaccheus the tax collector, who climbed a sycamore tree to see Jesus and welcomed him into his home.

name: Jeroboam
originalDescription: Jeroboam, son of Joash, was the thirteenth king of Israel; he freed Israel from its enemies (97:9.20)
improvedDescription: Son of Joash and thirteenth king of Israel who delivered Israel from its enemies. During his reign, a gangster-nobility ruled in Samaria, and the attempts to suppress freedom of speech led prophets like Elijah, Amos, and Hosea to begin their secret writings, marking the real beginning of the Jewish and Christian Bibles.

name: Jerusalem
originalDescription: ancient Jerusalem Jerusalem in Jesus life announcement of its destruction the heavenly Jerusalem highlights of the city
improvedDescription: The holy city and spiritual center of Judaism, originally called Salem in Melchizedek's time and later Jebus before becoming Jerusalem. Built on the site where David established his capital, it housed the temple that served as the focal point for Jewish worship worldwide. Jesus made his triumphal entry here, cleansed the temple, taught in its courts, and was crucified outside its walls. He prophesied its destruction, which occurred in 70 A.D. when Roman armies leveled the city.

name: Jerusalem temple
originalDescription: Jewish hearts around the world remained centered on the holy temple in Jerusalem, uniting them despite theological and philosophical differences. (121:6.8-9) The survival of Judaism depended on centralizing worship at the Jerusalem temple, which embodied monotheism and the potential to spread a universal concept of God. (121:2.6) temple services The irreverent temple trade Jesus relation taxes places in Jerusalem temple
improvedDescription: The central place of Jewish worship in Jerusalem, where animal sacrifices were offered and religious ceremonies conducted. Built by Herod, it featured multiple courts including areas for gentiles, women, priests, and the holy of holies. The temple became commercialized with money-changers and animal sellers, leading Jesus to cleanse it twice. Jesus taught regularly in its courts, particularly Solomon's Porch, and prophesied the temple's destruction.

name: Jerusalem temple guards
originalDescription: They are a type of soldiers who worked in the temple to maintain order Judas met with the captain of the temple guards to arrange for Jesus' capture (182:2.13) (183:2.1) The group of temple guards who arrested Jesus had a dispute with the Roman soldiers over where to take Jesus, whether to Annas' or Caiaphas' house (183:5.1) This group that captured Jesus and other servants engaged in mocking, spitting, and beating Jesus (184:4.1) Pilate agreed to send ten soldiers and join ten temple guards to guard Jesus' tomb (188:2.3) They fled when they saw the stone of Jesus' tomb move by itself (189:2.4) They were bribed to say that the disciples had taken Jesus' body (189:2.4) The captain of the temple guards was the one who removed the stones Jesus' burial cloths and threw them over a nearby cliff (190:1.2)
improvedDescription: Jewish soldiers responsible for maintaining order at the Jerusalem temple. Led by a captain, they collaborated with Roman soldiers to arrest Jesus in Gethsemane, participated in mocking and beating him during his trial, and later fled in terror when they witnessed the stone rolling away from Jesus' tomb. They were subsequently bribed to claim that disciples had stolen Jesus' body.

name: Jerusem
originalDescription: Jerusem, the headquarters of Satania, consists of seven transition worlds encircled by seven satellites, including the seven mansion worlds of morontia detention. (15:7.5) (15:14.6) (45:0.1) The headquarters world of system #24 in constellation Norlatiadek is called Jerusem. (15:14.6) It is the first place we will be taken to after crossing the threshold of death. location How it is envisioned councils on Jerusem inhabitants of Jerusem Jerusem organization physical aspects broadcasts economy suffrage transitional culture worlds
improvedDescription: The headquarters world of the local system of Satania, consisting of a central sphere surrounded by seven major transition worlds and forty-nine subsatellites. Located about one hundred times the size of Urantia, Jerusem serves as the administrative center where ascending mortals from the mansion worlds gather before proceeding to the constellation capital of Edentia. It features various residential circles for different orders of beings, magnificent architecture, and serves as the first major destination for mortals after death.

name: Jesus
originalDescription: physical description names or titles attitudes of Jesus attitudes toward Jesus events in Jesus’ life Jesus teachings | gospel:explained; kingdom of heaven; religion:explained; truth miracles and healings | healing; miracles personality and nature purpose of his life | Michael of Nebadon:bestowals value of Jesus’ life | Paradise Sons of God:bestowals Jesus and Sanhedrin second coming skills of Jesus spiritual accomplishments writings what Jesus asks of us
improvedDescription: The Creator Son Michael of Nebadon incarnated in human form on Urantia as Joshua ben Joseph of Nazareth. Born to Mary and Joseph around 7 B.C., he lived a complete human life while gradually revealing his divine nature and mission. He established the gospel of the kingdom of heaven, demonstrating the loving character of the Paradise Father through his teachings, healings, and perfect human life. After his crucifixion and resurrection, he ascended to assume full sovereignty over his local universe of Nebadon.

name: Jethro
originalDescription: Jethro, Moses’ father-in-law, played a crucial role in combining the teachings of Melchizedek with the traditions of Egypt and Palestine to guide the creation of the improved religion of the Israelites. (96:5.3)
improvedDescription: Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, played a crucial role in combining the teachings of Melchizedek with the traditions of Egypt and Palestine to guide the creation of the improved religion of the Israelites.

name: Jewelry
improvedDescription: Decorative objects worn for personal adornment, including various forms of ornamental accessories.

name: Jewish priesthood
improvedDescription: The religious leadership class within ancient Jewish society, responsible for temple worship, ritual ceremonies, and religious instruction.

name: Jewish religion
improvedDescription: The religious system and practices of the Jewish people, based on monotheistic worship and adherence to divine law.

name: Jewish tutor
originalDescription: Jesus was known as the “Jewish tutor” in Corinth (129:3.2)
improvedDescription: Jesus was known as the "Jewish tutor" in Corinth during his travels through the Roman world.

name: Jews
originalDescription: the Jewish religion the Messiah Jesus and the Jews chosen people, but spiritually daily life political matters Jews, opposed to Gentiles persecutions against the Jews
improvedDescription: The Jewish people, descended from ancient Hebrew tribes, who developed a distinctive monotheistic religion and maintained their identity despite centuries of foreign rule and dispersion throughout the Roman Empire. They were characterized by their strong religious traditions, adherence to divine law, and expectation of a coming Messiah.

name: Jews (ancient Hebrews)
originalDescription: Abraham’s offspring formed nucleus of the later Jewish people, but not all Hebrews were his descendants. (96:2.3) The ancient Hebrews adopted aliens with fraternal kindness, sealing the ceremony through the touching and drinking of wine. (70:3.8) racial influences beliefs and attitudes history Scriptures
improvedDescription: The ancient Hebrew people who formed the nucleus of the later Jewish nation. While Abraham's offspring were central to their formation, the Hebrews also incorporated many fellow nomads and adopted aliens, creating a diverse tribal confederation united by religious beliefs and shared traditions.

name: Jezebel
originalDescription: Jewish queen of Phoenician origin. Jesus heard of the Queen of Jews from Phoenicia and the deeds of Ahab, Jezebel, and Jehu while passing through historic locations on his journey. (124:6.3)
improvedDescription: Jewish queen of Phoenician origin whose deeds, along with those of Ahab and Jehu, were recounted to Jesus during his journey through historic locations in Palestine.

name: Jezreel
originalDescription: Jesus passed through this town with his parents on his journey to Jerusalem in A.D. 7 for his first Passover celebration there (124:6.1,3) Jesus and the twelve apostles visited Jezreel during the second public preaching tour of Galilee, in the months of October-December, A.D. 28 (149:0.1)
improvedDescription: A town in Palestine where Jesus and his family stopped during their journey to Jerusalem for the Passover celebration in A.D. 7, and later visited by Jesus and the twelve apostles during their second public preaching tour of Galilee in A.D. 28.

name: Joab
originalDescription: The young evangelist Joab, ringleader of the plot to make Jesus king, was met with resistance and desertion as the crowds grew hungry and Jesus secluded himself. (152:2.5) (153:5.2) (152:2.5) (153:5.2)
improvedDescription: A young evangelist who served as the ringleader of a plot to make Jesus king during the feeding of the five thousand, but faced resistance and desertion when the crowds grew hungry and Jesus withdrew from public view.

name: Joachim
originalDescription: Mary's father Joachim was always skeptical about Jesus' divine mission. (122:3.3)
improvedDescription: Mary's father who was always skeptical about Jesus' divine mission, along with the rest of Mary's family except her sister Salome.

name: Joanna
originalDescription: Joanna arranged a meaningful meeting in Herod's new palace, focusing on women's roles in religious life. (150:3.1) At Jesus' appearances, he instructed the women believers to proclaim the good news of the liberty of mankind through the gospel of sonship with God in the kingdom of heaven. (190:3.1) (193:0) Joanna, along with five other women, discovered the empty tomb of Jesus early on Sunday morning. (189:4.2-14) Joanna, treasurer of the women's corps and wife of Chuza, steward of Herod, was among the ten women appointed by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom, establishing their organization with Susanna as chief and treasurer. (150:1.1-2) (154:0.2)
improvedDescription: Wife of Chuza, steward of Herod Antipas, and treasurer of the women's corps that ministered to Jesus. She was one of the ten women appointed by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom and was among the five women who discovered Jesus' empty tomb on resurrection morning.

name: Joash
originalDescription: Boy king Joash crusaded for Yahweh, ending a reign of terror in Jerusalem. (97:9.21)
improvedDescription: Boy king who led a monotheistic revolt and crusaded for Yahweh for thirty-five years, ending a reign of terror in Jerusalem that included a temple of Baal alongside the temple of Yahweh.

name: Job (book)
originalDescription: The discourse on Job taught John the true meaning of suffering and the divine nature of compassion. (148:6) Job's suffering led to his salvation through a vision of God, demonstrating that human suffering is not always punishment for sin. (148:6.3) Job's longing for a human God reflected his desire for a divine being who could understand and comfort those who suffer unjustly. (148:6.7) The Jews struggled with the Egyptian concept of divine rewards for righteousness, leading to the protest in the drama of Job and the worldly wise reaction in Ecclesiastes due to the devastating pressure and coercion of secular history. (97:8.2) The Psalms found on stone in Babylon were from the descendants of Salem missionaries who penned Job, reflecting the Salem school teachings at Kish. (95:1.10) The Book of Job, written over 300 years by Mesopotamian religious teachers, presents a variegated picture of Deity that preserves the idea of a real God from the neighborhood of Ur of Chaldea in the dark days of Palestine. (96:7.5)
improvedDescription: An Old Testament book that demonstrates human suffering is not always punishment for sin, as Job achieved salvation through a vision of God despite his innocent suffering. The book was compiled by Mesopotamian religious teachers over 300 years and preserves the Salem school teachings, presenting a variegated picture of Deity from the neighborhood of Ur of Chaldea.

name: Jogbehah
originalDescription: One of the towns visited during the Perea mission (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the towns visited during the Perea mission led by Abner and the seventy messengers of the kingdom.

name: Johab
originalDescription: Johab was the groom at the wedding in Cana, and husband of Naomi, the bride. (137:3.6)
improvedDescription: Son of Nathan and groom at the wedding in Cana where Jesus performed his first public miracle, marrying Naomi in a celebration that Jesus and his first disciples attended.

name: John Hyrcanus
originalDescription: John Hyrcanus destroyed the Samaritan temple on Mount Gerizim, further fueling the religious enmity between the Jews and the Samaritans. (143:4.2)
improvedDescription: Maccabean leader who destroyed the Samaritan temple on Mount Gerizim, further intensifying the religious enmity between the Jews and the Samaritans.

name: John Mark
originalDescription: The Mark lad, their boy of all chores, held custody of the three days' supply of food Philip provided for Jesus and the twelve, as the crowd's provisions dwindled and rumors swirled about Jesus being crowned king. (152:2.5) John Mark was present during Jesus' miracle of feeding 5,000 people. Andrew looked for the boy and saw that the boy only had five loaves and two fish left in his basket. (152:2.7) John Mark was the son of Elijah and Mary Mark and faithfully followed Jesus during his final fateful hours, witnessing everything from his parents' home to the hour of the crucifixion. (182:0.1) the last days with Jesus Gospel of Mark
improvedDescription: Son of Elijah and Mary Mark who served as the boy of all chores for Jesus and the twelve apostles. He faithfully followed Jesus during his final hours, witnessed the crucifixion, and later wrote the Gospel of Mark based on his own memories and Peter's recollections.

name: John the Baptist
originalDescription: birth education teachings Jesus and John personal characteristics death after death and current status
improvedDescription: Born March 25, 7 B.C., John was the son of Zacharias and Elizabeth and the forerunner of Jesus' mission. He preached repentance and baptism in preparation for the coming kingdom, baptized Jesus in the Jordan River, and was later executed by Herod Antipas on January 10, A.D. 28.

name: John the Revelator
improvedDescription: John Zebedee in his later role as the author of the Book of Revelation, one of the original twelve apostles who became known for his mystical writings about the end times.

name: John Zebedee
originalDescription: John Zebedee, the youngest apostle from Capernaum, was unmarried at the age of twenty-four in A.D. 26 and played a significant role in caring for Jesus' family both before and after becoming an apostle. (139:4.1) (181:2.2) John Zebedee purchased a house in Capernaum for Jesus with money entrusted to him by his friend. (129:2.4,10) (134:1.1) life as apostle only apostle present at all important events Book of Revelations written by John First Epistle Gospel of John visions of John
improvedDescription: John Zebedee was the youngest of the twelve apostles at age 24, unmarried and living with his parents in Bethsaida. A fisherman working with his brother James in partnership with Andrew and Peter, John served as Jesus' personal agent in dealing with the Master's family affairs and continued to care for Mary, Jesus' mother, throughout her life. Known for his dependability and devotion, he was one of the three apostles chosen to be Jesus' closest companions and was present at all the important events of Jesus' ministry.

name: Jonah
originalDescription: Jesus' discourse on the importance of seeking truth and living in accordance with the will of God brought great enlightenment to Gadiah during their time in Joppa. (130:1)
improvedDescription: Jonah is a figure from Hebrew scripture whose story Jesus used to illustrate profound spiritual truths about God's love for all people, including those considered outsiders or heathens, regardless of whether Jonah actually existed as a historical person.

name: Joppa
originalDescription: Jesus visited Joppa several times during his journeys throughout Palestine. (123:0.4) (128:3.2) (130:0.2) (130:1) (134:7.4) (152:7.3)
improvedDescription: Joppa was an important port city on the Mediterranean coast where Jesus and his traveling companions stopped several times during their journeys. It was here that Jesus met and befriended Gadiah, a Philistine interpreter, during his Mediterranean travels with Gonod and Ganid.

name: Jordan River
originalDescription: there was a famous ford in the Jordan River. See link below | Bethany beyond Jordan Jesus' baptism in A.D. 26 completed the plan set forth for John's arrival in Pella, solidifying his mission. (135:8) Jesus and the twelve apostles left Magadan Park for Caesarea-Philippi, nestled in a region of wondrous beauty with the Jordan River pouring forth from an underground cave. (157:3.1) Jesus journeyed through the tropical Jordan valley, marveling at its glistening waters and lush fields. (124:6.5)
improvedDescription: The Jordan River was a significant waterway in Palestine where John the Baptist conducted his ministry near Pella and where Jesus was baptized. The river flows through the tropical Jordan valley with winding, glistening waters that flow toward the Dead Sea, and it emerges from an underground cave near Caesarea-Philippi.

name: Jose
originalDescription: Jesus, influenced by the renowned liberal Nazareth teacher Jose, made significant modifications to religious practices in his community. (124:4.8)
improvedDescription: Jose was a renowned liberal teacher and rabbi in Nazareth who influenced the synagogue's progressive interpretation of religious practices. Under his influence, Jesus was able to modify certain family religious customs and practices in his community.

name: Joseph (brother of Jesus)
originalDescription: The apostles stayed with Joseph as Jesus showed them the importance of living by his teachings rather than relying on written words. (137:2.9) At Jesus' 4th appearance to mortal recognition, he vanished from their sight as they moved to embrace him. (190:2.6) Joseph, born March 16, A.D. 1, was the fourth member of the Nazareth family. (123:4.9) Joseph, unlike his brothers, defended himself when assailed by playmates, showing a departure from the family's teachings of nonresistance. (127:4.5) Joseph, a faithful plodding worker, was not too smart compared to his siblings James, Simon, and Jude. (127:4.7) Jesus prepared for his withdrawal by installing Joseph as head of the family after James had gained experience and before his own marriage, ensuring a smooth transition. (128:2.7) (128:7.11) Joseph struggled with friction with Jesus but excelled in getting along with his other siblings. (124:4.3) Joseph graduated from synagogue school this year and prepared to begin work at the small bench in the home carpenter shop, with prospects to successfully fight off poverty. (127:6.11) This year, Jesus and Joseph traveled to Jerusalem for Passover, with Jesus ensuring to share important teachings with Joseph along the journey. (128:1.14) In A.D. 24, Joseph and his family moved into the old Nazareth home after Mary and Ruth went to live in Capernaum. (134:1.6) Despite their lingering faith, Jesus' family members let pride cloud their judgment and hinder their spiritual beliefs, with only Ruth unwavering in her belief in his divine mission. (154:6.1) Joseph's tentative belief in Jesus as the long-expected deliverer grew as his family discussed the wonderful events that had taken place in Nazareth. (137:3.2)
improvedDescription: Joseph was the fourth child in Jesus' family, born March 16, A.D. 1. Unlike his brothers, he was more inclined to defend himself physically when attacked by playmates, departing from the family's teachings of nonresistance. Though a faithful worker, he was less spiritually minded than his siblings and served as head of the family household after Jesus' departure.

name: Joseph (father of Jesus)
originalDescription: The human father of Jesus Joseph died September 25, 8 A.D. while working on Herod's building in Sepphoris, leaving Jesus to care for his family. (126:2) (126:5.11) (185:4.1) attitude background and heritage Joseph and Jesus work
improvedDescription: Joseph was the human father of Jesus, a skilled carpenter and later a prosperous contractor from Nazareth. A Hebrew of the Hebrews with mixed ancestry, he was mild-mannered, conscientious, and faithful to Jewish religious practices. He died from injuries sustained in a construction accident at Sepphoris on September 25, 8 A.D., when Jesus was fourteen years old, leaving Jesus to become the head of the family.

name: Joseph of Arimathea
originalDescription: Shortly after two o’clock, Jesus, accompanied by Joseph of Arimathea, arrived at the temple to deliver his final address. (175:0.1) Joseph of Arimathea believed Jesus would rise from the tomb, despite Nicodemus' doubts. (188:1.2,8) Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea boldly claimed the body of Jesus from Pilate, acknowledging their faith even when most of the disciples had fled in fear. (142:6.9) (187:6.2) (188:0.3) Despite spending June near Jerusalem, with no public teaching, Jesus attracted visitors like Joseph of Arimathea, who came to see Him in Gethsemane but turned back in fear. (142:8.4) Rebecca, the daughter of Joseph of Arimathea, was among the women selected and commissioned by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom. (150:1.1) Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus embalmed Jesus' body with myrrh and aloes before placing it in the tomb. (188:1.4) Former Sanhedrin members Joseph and Nicodemus knowingly defied the law to bury Jesus in Joseph's new family tomb, firmly believing in his resurrection despite doubts. (188:1.2) Joseph of Arimathea, Nicodemus, John, and a Roman centurion helped bear the body of Jesus to the tomb at half past four o'clock. (188:1.3) Jesus' fifth appearance was at the home of Joseph of Arimathea, where he bestowed a message of unity and ministering to others before vanishing from sight. (190:3.1) Jesus met with Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea in the home of Nicodemus during the feast of dedication. (164:2.1) Jesus was buried in a tomb of Joseph | Jesus:events in life
improvedDescription: Joseph of Arimathea was a wealthy member of the Sanhedrin who became a secret believer in Jesus. Along with Nicodemus, he boldly claimed Jesus' body from Pilate after the crucifixion and provided his own new family tomb for Jesus' burial. He believed Jesus would rise from the dead and later hosted one of Jesus' post-resurrection appearances at his home.

name: Joseph of Tyre
originalDescription: Jesus stayed with Joseph of Tyre from July 11 to July 24, teaching and preaching in the city and baptizing many believers into the kingdom. (156:4.1-2) (156:6.1)
improvedDescription: Joseph of Tyre was a Jewish believer who lived three or four miles south of Tyre, near the ancient tomb of King Hiram. He hosted Jesus and the apostles at his home for two weeks in July, serving as their headquarters while they taught and preached throughout the busy seaport city of Tyre.

name: Joseph (Old Testament)
originalDescription: great-grandson of Abraham; civil administrator in Egypt Joseph's steadfast belief in Melchizedek set him apart as a dreamer among his brothers and ultimately shaped his path as a civil administrator in Egypt, as he labored for the advancement of the kingdom of heaven. (93:9.5,8) Political circumstances in Egypt allowed for the promotion of Salem teachings by Joseph, who exerted great influence in a highly ethical but not overly spiritual society. (95:3.5) From the hills of Simeon, Jesus viewed Dothan, where Joseph was sold into Egyptian slavery, and recounted the historic events of his people. (126:1.2)
improvedDescription: Joseph was the great-grandson of Abraham and a firm believer in Melchizedek's teachings, which his brothers considered him a dreamer for accepting. Rather than accepting military command of Egyptian armies, he chose to serve as a civil administrator in Egypt, believing he could better advance the kingdom of heaven through peaceful governance and ethical leadership.

name: Joshua ben Joseph
improvedDescription: The Hebrew name of Jesus, meaning "Yahweh is salvation," which was his given name at birth.

name: Joshua (Jesus)
originalDescription: Gabriel instructed Mary to name her son Joshua, who would inaugurate the kingdom of heaven on earth. (122:3.1) (122:8.2)
improvedDescription: The name given to Jesus by divine instruction through Gabriel, who told Mary to call her son Joshua, announcing that he would inaugurate the kingdom of heaven on earth. On the eighth day after his birth, the child was circumcised and formally named Joshua (Jesus).

name: Joshua (Old Testament)
originalDescription: Jesus recalled how Joshua and the Israelites came up to Jerusalem through Jericho. (135:6.1) (172:1.3) Jesus quoted Joshua, saying “Choose you this day whom you will serve; many stand at the parting of the ways.” (153:2.5) Despite the belief that only a few will enter the promised land, all who seek the kingdom of heaven shall find salvation. (166:3.2) Upon Moses turning over command of the Hebrews to Joshua, he had already built a self-sustaining nation of pastoral warriors from collateral descendants of Abraham and other related tribes. (96:5.9) Joshua preached a stern gospel to a disbelieving people, emphasizing Yahweh as a holy, jealous, and unforgiving God of power, judgment, and justice. (96:6.1,3) John warned the Pharisees and Sadducees about their false sense of security by the Jordan, declaring that God can raise up worthy children for Abraham from the twelve stones set up by Joshua. (135:6.7)
improvedDescription: The Hebrew leader who succeeded Moses and led the Israelites into the promised land, known for his military conquests including Jericho. After Moses' death, Joshua found it necessary to preach a stern gospel emphasizing Yahweh as a holy, jealous God of power, judgment, and justice, though the common people gradually reverted to older desert concepts of deity.

name: Joshua the teacher
originalDescription: The mission of Joshua the teacher concluded that chapter in Jesus' life as the Son of Man. (133:9.6)
improvedDescription: A reference to Jesus during his teaching ministry phase, representing a chapter in his life as the Son of Man that concluded his work as an educator and spiritual instructor.

name: Josiah (blind beggar)
originalDescription: Josiah, a recipient of Jesus's miraculous ministry, became a lifelong preacher of the gospel. (164:5.6) Josiah's healing on the Sabbath day sparked a heated debate among the Sanhedrin, who ultimately failed to ensnare him in their traps, leading Josiah to boldly proclaim Jesus as a prophet before them. (164:4) Cast out of synagogue, Josiah boldly proclaimed the truth and was rewarded with the revelation of the Son of God by Jesus. (164:4.11) (164:5.4-5) Josiah, the blind beggar, was healed by Jesus, challenging the religious leaders and teaching the apostles to not neglect material means in healing. (164:3) Josiah, neither dumb nor lacking in humor, responded to the court officer: “Whether this man is a sinner, I know not; but one thing I do know—that, whereas I was blind, now I see.” (164:4.9) Josiah's parents, poor and fear-ridden souls, stood silent before the Sanhedrin, unable to explain how their blind son could now see, directing the court to question him instead. (164:4.7)
improvedDescription: A man born blind who was healed by Jesus on the Sabbath, sparking controversy with the Sanhedrin. Despite being cast out of the synagogue for boldly proclaiming Jesus as a prophet, Josiah became a lifelong preacher of the gospel and accompanied Jesus to Pella, proving to be one of the fruitful recipients of the Master's miraculous ministry.

name: Josiah (disciple of Abner)
originalDescription: Josiah baptized the Pharisee who professed faith in the gospel of the kingdom. (174:4.4)
improvedDescription: One of the disciples of Abner who baptized a Pharisee lawyer that professed faith in the gospel of the kingdom after being moved by Jesus' teaching.

name: Josiah (king)
originalDescription: Under Josiah's rule, the destruction of the corrupt Jerusalem politicians marked the turning point for Judah's recovery from the grip of corruption and idolatry. (97:9.23) Josiah, king, remembered the Egyptians defeated at Megiddo while looking out from the hill of Simeon towards Nazareth, Taanach, Dothan, Ebal, and Gerizim. (126:1.2) Josiah's tragic end came when he intercepted Necho’s army, leading to Judah falling under tribute to Egypt and the return of the Baal political party to power in Jerusalem, marking the start of Egyptian bondage. (97:9.24)
improvedDescription: A righteous king of Judah who destroyed the corrupt ring of politicians in Jerusalem and attempted to restore proper worship. His reign marked a temporary recovery from corruption and idolatry, but his tragic death while intercepting Pharaoh Necho's army led to Judah falling under Egyptian tribute and the return of Baal worship.

name: Jotapata
originalDescription: Jesus’ discourse on prayer at Jotapata emphasized the importance of sincerity, spiritual progress, and the transformative power of worship. (146:2) Mary Magdalene and Rebecca were ordained as effective teachers of the gospel at Jotapata, laboring faithfully and effectively for the upliftment of women alongside ten other evangelists. (150:2.3)
improvedDescription: A small town in Galilee where Jesus and his apostles conducted an important teaching mission. Here Jesus gave a significant discourse on prayer, thanksgiving, and worship, and Mary Magdalene and Rebecca were ordained as effective teachers of the gospel, becoming part of the twelve women evangelists.

name: Jove
originalDescription: As man advances in culture, the great Jove persists only as an exclamation, subordinated to a supreme deity. (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: The Roman supreme deity who, as human culture advances and monotheism develops, persists only as an exclamation while being subordinated to a higher concept of supreme deity.

name: joy
originalDescription: Your joy is born of trust in the divine word, grounding your sonship in faith and freeing you from fear. (143:2.7) Real joy cannot be achieved in solitude; sharing life with others is essential. (111:4.7) True joy, like divine indwelling, cannot be measured or analyzed through materialistic techniques. (196:3.18) The fruit of the Spirit is joy, a characteristic exhibited by spirit-led mortals who are guided by divinely illuminated truth and love. (34:6.13) (56:10.20) The highest joy on Paradise is worship, utilizing the arts of all beings in the universe to intensify self-expression and convey appreciation. (27:7.5) Inherent joy in freewill existence is promoted by beings attuned to superaphic harmony, seeking to upstep pleasure reactions and develop a superhumor among mortals and angels. (28:5.16) Mostly material reactions to environment shape transient human emotions of joy and sorrow, not the mission of the Adjuster. (108:5.6) The Havona Servitals and Graduate Guides find joy in transcendent devotion to duty, eclipsing all other emotions as spiritual beings. (25:1.6) The secoraphic Joys of Existence promote joy among angelic hosts and will creatures, demonstrating inherent joy in freewill existence. (28:5.16) Do not seek transient joy; seek the sureties of divine sonship for supreme joy in the spirit. (149:5.4) The Spirit of Truth gives profound joy, security, and confidence to believers praying together, compelling them to proclaim the gospel and Christ's resurrection. (194:0.1) Spiritual growth yields lasting joy through the dominance of love. (100:4.3) To share the Master's joy is to share his love and service, transforming the old world into something new. (180:1.5)
improvedDescription: A spiritual fruit that emerges from trust in the divine word and faith in God's indwelling spirit, transcending material circumstances. True joy cannot be experienced in isolation but requires sharing life with others, and represents one of the characteristics exhibited by spirit-led mortals guided by divine truth and love.

name: Joys of Existence
originalDescription: Collaborating with reversion directors, the Joys of Existence promote joy among angelic hosts and lower will creatures, seeking to demonstrate inherent joy in freewill existence. (28:5.16) Promoting reactions of joy is the principal activity of the Joy of Existence beings, who seek to upstep pleasure reactions and develop a superhumor among mortals and angels. (28:5.16) The Joy of Existence is reflected in the superaphic harmony supervisors, promoting joy among angels and will creatures while demonstrating inherent joy in freewill existence. (28:5.16) Reflective associates of Divine Counselors promote joy among angelic hosts and lower will creatures, attuned to superaphic harmony supervisors above. (28:5.16)
improvedDescription: A specialized order of celestial beings who serve as reflective associates of Divine Counselors, promoting reactions of joy among angelic hosts and lower will creatures. They collaborate with reversion directors and function as joy clearinghouses, seeking to demonstrate that there is inherent joy in freewill existence independent of all external influences.

name: jubilee of jubilees
originalDescription: At the jubilee of jubilees, the Universe Spirit publicly acknowledges subordination to the Creator Son, establishing fidelity and obedience before all. (33:3.5)
improvedDescription: The momentous occasion when the Universe Spirit publicly acknowledges subordination to the Creator Son before assembled hosts, pledging fidelity and obedience. This event occurs at the enthronement of the Creator Son as a Master Son following completion of his seventh and final creature bestowal.

name: jubilees
originalDescription: After ascending through the stages of spiritual realization, the seventh jubilee marks entrance into the eternal service of the Finality Corps. (27:7.8) The jubilee celebration marks the settling of a local universe in light and life. (17:3.11)
improvedDescription: Significant spiritual milestones marking major transitions in the ascendant career. The seventh jubilee celebrates entrance into the mortal finaliter corps and the beginning of eternity service, while jubilee celebrations also mark the settling of local universes in light and life.

name: Judah
improvedDescription: The southern Jewish kingdom and clan from which Jewish consciousness originated, as distinguished from the northern Israelite kingdom of Ephraim. David established his kingdom of Judah at Hebron and later united it with Israel under his rule from Jerusalem.

name: Judaism
originalDescription: Judaism is one of the most advanced religions of ancient times, greatly influencing religious development in Orient and Occident. (92:6.4,14) The Hebrew religion is one of the great international, interracial faiths alongside Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam. (92:6.4,16) beliefs strengths and weaknesses eastern/western Judaism differences feasts rituals Influence of Judaism on other religions Influence of other religions on Judaism Jesus and Judaism oral law
improvedDescription: The Hebrew religion that evolved as one of the great international faiths alongside Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam. Judaism preserved moral values and the concept of one universal God, though it was characterized by rigid traditionalism and ceremonial law that sometimes hindered spiritual progress and philosophical development.

name: Judas Alpheus
improvedDescription: One of the Alpheus twins who served among Jesus' twelve apostles, known for his quiet and contemplative nature in contrast to his more outspoken brother James Alpheus.

name: Judas Iscariot
originalDescription: Judas Iscariot was the twelfth apostle. He was chosen by Nathaniel, and later confirmed in this election by Jesus. He was born in Kerioth, a small town in southern Judea. When he was a lad, his parents moved to Jericho, where he lived and had been employed in his father’s various business enterprises until he became interested in the preaching and work of John the Baptist. (139:6.2) (139:12.1-2) Judas’s parents were Sadducees, and when their son joined John’s disciples, they disowned him. (138:2.9) (139:12.1) When Nathaniel met Judas at Tarichea, he was seeking employment with a fish-drying enterprise at the lower end of the Sea of Galilee. He was thirty years of age in A.D. 26 and unmarried when he joined the apostles. He was probably the best-educated man among the twelve and the only Judean in the Master’s apostolic family. He was cultured, educated, and a good thinker, but he was not truly honest. (138:2.9) (139:12.1-2) Judas was accepted by Jesus in the group of apostles along with Thomas, in Tarichea. When Jesus accepted Judas, he told him: “Judas, we are all of one flesh, and as I receive you into our midst, I pray that you will always be loyal to your Galilean brethren. Follow me.”. Judas was the only Judean. All the rest were from Galilee. (138:5) His work as treasurer His criticism his relationship with Jesus the betrayal causes of downfall (157:7.2-4) (193:4) his end
improvedDescription: The twelfth apostle chosen by Nathaniel, born in Kerioth in southern Judea and the only Judean among the twelve. He served as treasurer of the apostolic group and was well-educated and cultured, but became increasingly isolated and resentful, ultimately betraying Jesus for thirty pieces of silver before taking his own life.

name: Judas Maccabee
originalDescription: Jesus rejected the disappointment of reign like Judas Maccabee, choosing loyalty to his Father's will above all. (136:8.8) The Jewish people were vibrant with expectation due to Judas Maccabee's heroic exploits, hoping for the appearance of the Messiah. (121:2.7) During the times of Judas Maccabee, Jews were removed from Perea, leaving the region equally gentile and Jewish. (165:0.3) After meeting John and learning about the history of Israel, Jesus expressed a keen interest in the meaning of Sabbath rites and commemorations, including the festive illumination marking the temple's dedication after Judas Maccabee restored the Mosaic services. (123:3.5)
improvedDescription: A Jewish military leader whose heroic exploits in restoring Mosaic temple services and delivering the Jews from foreign oppression created vibrant expectations among the Jewish people for the appearance of an even greater deliverer, the long-expected Messiah.

name: Jude
originalDescription: Jude was the seventh son of Joseph and Mary, one of Jesus' younger brothers, twelve years younger than Jesus. (124:3.4) attitude life facts
improvedDescription: The seventh child and youngest brother of Jesus, born when Jesus was twelve years old. Known for his fiery temperament and patriotic sentiments, Jude was impulsive and often got into trouble, but he eventually became a faithful supporter of the family and later believed in Jesus' mission.

name: Judea
originalDescription: Caesarea, the Roman capital of Judea, was where Annas desired charges be formulated against Jesus for Pilate. (184:3.17) The fall of Assyria and ascendancy of Egypt brought deliverance to Judah, as corrupt politicians were overthrown by the country folk under King Josiah. (97:9.23) Jewish consciousness originated in the southern clan of Judah while Israelitish consciousness began in the hill country of Ephraim. (97:9.2) Judah was enslaved by Egypt after Josiah's defeat in battle against Necho's army, with Baal worship and the Baalim politicians gaining control in Jerusalem. (97:9.17,24) Judahites perpetually tarnished Ephraimites to establish Jewish superiority. (97:9.2) Judas Iscariot was the only apostle from Judea among Jesus' twelve. (138:2.9) (139:12.1-2) Despite Jesus' explanation, Judeans remain less willing to believe the gospel preached by the seventy. (166:2.2) Pilate's deep-seated hatred of the Jews led to fatal blunders in governing Judea, allowing the Jews to easily manipulate him due to his fear and lack of moral courage. (185:1.2) David established the kingdom of Judah in Hebron, a non-Hebrew city with a diverse population of nomads devoted to the Hebrew idea of land ownership. (97:9.7,11) Despite ups and downs, wars between Israel and Judah ultimately led to the downfall of both kingdoms. (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: The southern region of Palestine that served as the Roman province governed by Pilate, with Caesarea as its capital. This was the homeland of the Jewish religious establishment and the location of Jerusalem, making it a center of orthodox Jewish tradition and resistance to new religious teachings.

name: Judge-Arbiters
originalDescription: The Spirit-Advocate represents and protects the rights of all personalities in matters before conciliating commissions. (25:2.7)
improvedDescription: An order of celestial beings who serve as judicial officials on conciliating commissions, appointing Spirit-Advocates to represent and safeguard the rights of all personalities in adjudication matters.

name: judges
originalDescription: Jesus’ address to judges on behalf of the poor man emphasized the importance of impartiality, fairness, and integrity in the pursuit of justice. (132:4.8) Jesus wisely defended Jude before the military magistrate, warning him of potential trouble. (128:6.7) Micah denounced rulers who judge for reward, priests who teach for hire, and prophets who divine for money, envisioning a future of freedom from superstition and priestcraft. (97:5.5)
improvedDescription: Judicial officials responsible for administering justice and maintaining law and order. Jesus emphasized that judges must demonstrate impartiality, fairness, and integrity in their pursuit of justice, noting that a nation's endurance depends upon the integrity of its courts.

name: Judges (Old Testament)
originalDescription: The Hebrew tribal sheiks led the people in a backward drift of the concept of Deity, away from the all-wise, beneficent, and almighty God of Moses to the older desert idea of Yahweh. (96:6.1)
improvedDescription: The tribal leaders and sheiks who ruled Israel after Moses' death, under whose successive leadership the Hebrew concept of Yahweh deteriorated from Moses' elevated understanding of an all-wise, beneficent, and almighty God back to the older desert conception of deity.

name: judgment
originalDescription: by man day of judgment divine judgment Universal Censors
improvedDescription: The divine process of evaluation and decision-making regarding individual survival and spiritual progression. Judgment is always a group function rather than a personal act, administered by collective bodies such as the Paradise Trinity, with mercy tempering justice in all divine verdicts.

name: Julias
originalDescription: Herod maintained residence at Julias in Perea, where Joanna led the Sabbath services in Herod's palace banquet room. (135:12.1) (150:3.1)
improvedDescription: One of Herod's residences in Perea where he maintained court, and where Joanna conducted Sabbath services for Jesus' apostolic party in the palace banquet room.

name: Juno
originalDescription: In Rome, Ganid studied and explored the city's points of interest, including the Capitolium dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. (132:0.2)
improvedDescription: A Roman goddess honored alongside Jupiter and Minerva in the magnificent temple on the Capitolium in Rome, which Jesus often visited and contemplated during his stay in the city.

name: Jupiter (god)
originalDescription: While at Rome, Ganid regularly visited the Capitolium, a magnificent temple dedicated to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva. (132:0.2) Labor distinguishes man from the beast, but Jupiter was a reveler. (69:2.5) Romans invoked Jupiter by throwing a stone in the air, as symbols of worship and reverence for stones were common among ancient and modern cultures alike. (85:1.3) Zeus was renamed Jupiter when he was transplanted from Greece to Rome, along with many other Olympian gods. (98:3.3)
improvedDescription: The chief Roman god, equivalent to the Greek Zeus, honored in a magnificent temple on Rome's Capitolium. Jupiter was characterized as a reveler in contrast to the Hebrew ideal of divine labor and industry.

name: Jupiter (planet)
originalDescription: Jupiter, largely gaseous since its formation, remains a brilliant secondary sun in the solar system. (57:5.10) Jupiter might capture outer planets, becoming a sun and forming a double star system. (15:5.8) A moon of Jupiter is nearing tidal disruption, like shooting stars and Saturn's rings which are fragments of disrupted bodies. (57:6.5) Jupiter and Saturn, derived from highly heated sun material, shone as secondary suns after forming from the central portions of a massive extrusion of matter disgorgued from the sun. (41:10.1) (57:5.9-10) Jupiter's powerful gravity pull early captured most of the material stolen from Angona, evident in the retrograde motion of certain satellites. (57:5.9) Jupiter and Saturn were, for a short period after formation, secondary suns, emitting brilliant light and enormous heat as they retained highly heated sun material within their gaseous composition. (57:5.10)
improvedDescription: The largest planet in the solar system, formed from the central portions of superheated solar material and remaining largely gaseous since its formation. Jupiter briefly functioned as a secondary sun and captured most of the material stolen from the Angona system, as evidenced by the retrograde motion of some of its satellites.

name: Jurassic
originalDescription: 120,000,000 years ago marked the beginning and eventual decline of the dinosaurs during the later reptilian age from 125 to 100 million years ago. (60:2)
improvedDescription: A geological period beginning 120,000,000 years ago that marked the evolution and eventual decline of the dinosaurs during the later reptilian age, when land-animal life reached its greatest development in terms of size.

name: jurors
originalDescription: Members of the superuniverse assembly serve as judges in the Uversa courts, presided over by celestial beings representing wisdom, counsel, and authority. (15:12.1) Those without Name and Number serve as supreme jurors on Orvonton, rendering verdicts with evolved spiritual mentality on Uversa and its extension tribunals. (15:12.1) (22:4.3)
improvedDescription: Celestial beings who serve in the superuniverse courts of Uversa, particularly Those without Name and Number who function as supreme jurors of Orvonton, chosen for their evolved spiritual mentality and experience in the ascendant career to render verdicts in cosmic adjudications.

name: just man
originalDescription: The prayer of a just man, one who had discharged ritual obligations, was highly esteemed. (89:8.7) The just man shall live by faith in the Lord, acknowledging Him in all ways, for God will judge every work, whether good or evil. (131:2.8)
improvedDescription: A person who has fulfilled all ritual obligations and spiritual duties, making their prayers highly esteemed by God. In ancient contexts, this referred to someone who had satisfied all accounts with the spirits and discharged every religious requirement.

name: Justa
originalDescription: Justa, daughter of Bernice of Sidon, hosted Jesus and his followers, who were amazed by the gentiles' eagerness to hear and believe the gospel. (156:2.2) (156:3.2)
improvedDescription: The daughter of Bernice of Sidon who, along with her mother, hosted Jesus and the twenty-four evangelists in their home north of Sidon. Jesus taught the evangelists each morning at Justa's house during their ministry to the gentiles.

name: justice
originalDescription: human justice divine justice | conciliating commissions; judgment:divine judgment Jesus justice
improvedDescription: A divine attribute and universal principle that ensures fair judgment and righteous administration. Justice is always a group function rather than a personal act, administered by tribunals and courts throughout the universe hierarchy to maintain cosmic order and fairness.

name: justice guides
originalDescription: Seraphim administrators, tasked with defending cases of doubtful survival, serve as justice guides in presenting evidence for adjudication in tribunals. (39:4.4) Justice guides ensure that unerring justice is administered with generous mercy, especially in minor misunderstandings brought before the courts. (39:4.5) Justice guides may become Voices of Mercy, ensuring unerring justice is delivered with generous mercy in fairness to all creatures, transitioning from lower realms to higher spheres and Salvington. (39:4.5) During the Lucifer rebellion in Satania, few justice guides were lost, while over a quarter of other administrator seraphim and lower orders of seraphic ministers were misguided by the allure of unrestrained personal freedom. (39:4.6)
improvedDescription: Seraphim who prepare and present evidence concerning the eternal welfare of mortals and angels in universe tribunals. They ensure that unerring justice is administered with generous mercy, defending cases of doubtful survival and preparing statements for higher courts.

name: Justus (believer)
originalDescription: Simon Peter eloquently recounted the touching farewell and ascension of their Master at the last meeting of the eleven apostles, leading to the decision to choose a successor to Judas, with Justus suggested to fill his position. (193:6.2)
improvedDescription: One of two men suggested to replace Judas Iscariot as an apostle, along with Matthias. He was considered by the apostles during their final meeting after Jesus' ascension.

name: Justus (Jewish merchant of Corinth)
originalDescription: Justus, the devout merchant that hosted Jesus, witnessed his compassionate and transformative encounter with two struggling women, leading to one finding hope in eternal survival and the other embracing a new life in the Christian faith. (133:3.5-10)
improvedDescription: A devout Jewish merchant in Corinth who lived near the synagogue and frequently hosted Jesus and Ganid. His home later became a place where the Apostle Paul stayed and heard stories of Jesus' earlier visits.

name: ka and ba
originalDescription: Egyptian and African teaching of spirit and soul (111:0.4-6)
improvedDescription: Egyptian concepts representing two aspects of human nature - the ka being a protective guardian spirit assigned at birth that guides the soul through life and into the afterlife, and the ba representing the soul itself.

name: Kaaba stone
originalDescription: The Kaaba stone is a black stone fetish at Mecca (88:1.2) (95:7.5)
improvedDescription: A black stone fetish housed in a temple at Mecca that became a point of common reverence among Arabian tribes. This sacred stone served as a unifying factor that later contributed to the establishment of the Islamic religion.

name: Kanata
originalDescription: Aden went right on with his efforts and raised up a considerable company of believers at Kanata (159:2.4)
improvedDescription: A location where Aden, despite being forbidden by the apostle John, successfully established a considerable company of believers before continuing his ministry into Mesopotamia.

name: kangaroos
originalDescription: A small reptilian, egg-laying type of mammal flourished, and the ancestors of the later kangaroos roamed Australia (61:1.9) while their peculiar forms were perpetuated in the leaping kangaroos (65:2.10)
improvedDescription: Marsupial mammals whose ancestors roamed Australia 45 million years ago, evolving from small reptilian, egg-laying mammals. Their distinctive leaping form perpetuated the peculiar characteristics of their reptilian ancestors.

name: Kansas
originalDescription: The central ice sheet extended south as far as Kansas; the eastern and western ice centers were not then so extensive
improvedDescription: A U.S. state that marked the southernmost extent of the central North American ice sheet during the first glacier's advance approximately 2 million years ago.

name: Kansu
originalDescription: The Andites were traversing the pass of Ti Tao and spreading out over the upper valley of the Yellow River among the Chinese settlements of Kansu (79:7.1)
improvedDescription: A region in the upper valley of the Yellow River in ancient China where Chinese settlements received Andite migrants approximately 15,000 years ago.

name: Karahta
originalDescription: Jesus journeyed on north; and passing east of the Waters of Merom, he went by Karahta to Dan, or Caesarea-Philippi
improvedDescription: A place in northern Palestine that Jesus passed through on his final tour as a private individual, located east of the Waters of Merom on the route to Dan.

name: Karbar
originalDescription: King Ahab of Ephraim gathered ten other groups and resisted King Shalmaneser III at Karkar (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: The site of a battle where King Ahab of Ephraim and ten allied groups fought against Assyrian King Shalmaneser III, resulting in a draw that stopped the Assyrian advance but decimated the allies.

name: karma
originalDescription: Every act shall receive its reward (131:3.5) men reap what they sow (2:3.2) (131:2.8-9) (131:9.4) (148:6.3) (192:2.8) The karma principle of causality continuity is, again, very close to the truth of the repercussional synthesis of all time-space actions in the Deity presence of the Supreme (94:3.5)
improvedDescription: The universal principle that every act receives its reward and people reap what they sow, reflecting the truth of repercussional synthesis of all time-space actions in the presence of the Supreme.

name: Karuska
originalDescription: Karuska, a patient at Bethsaida hospital from Sidon, hosted Jesus and his associates at her home where a Syrian woman sought help for her afflicted daughter. (156:0.1) (156:1.1-2)
improvedDescription: A wealthy woman from Sidon who had been a patient at Bethsaida hospital and hosted Jesus and his associates at her home during their visit to the region.

name: Kashmir
originalDescription: Asoka established Buddhism in Kashmir, where it prospered and became the dominant religion in half the world. (94:9.1)
improvedDescription: A region where Buddhism was established by Emperor Asoka and became the dominant religion, contributing to Buddhism's spread across half the known world.

name: Kateri
originalDescription: The Kateri people of southern India, like other tribes in both northern and southern India, still worship stones as sacred objects. (85:1.1)
improvedDescription: A people of southern India who continue the ancient practice of stone worship, venerating stones as sacred objects.

name: Katro
originalDescription: Moses inherited a compelling tradition of higher truths from his illustrious ancestor Melchizedek, who lived with the family of Katro for over thirty years. (93:3.5)
improvedDescription: The head of a family with whom Melchizedek lived for over thirty years, whose descendants preserved Melchizedek's higher truths down to the time of Moses.

name: Keilah
originalDescription: David defended the Canaanite city of Keilah, explaining Saul's defeat at Gibeon due to attacking a city with a peace treaty with the Ephraimites. (97:9.13)
improvedDescription: A Canaanite city that David defended against the Philistines, demonstrating his policy of protecting and allying with Canaanite peoples.

name: Kenan
originalDescription: Kenan, grandson of Seth, initiated foreign missionary service, continuing the spiritual work of his father and grandfather. (76:3.4)
improvedDescription: Kenan, grandson of Seth, initiated foreign missionary service to surrounding tribes, continuing the spiritual work of his father and grandfather in improving the religious status of Adam's people.

name: Kenites
originalDescription: The Salem religion, revered by the Kenites and other Canaanite tribes, paved the way for the earth bestowal of a Son of the one God. (96:0.2-3) (96:1.12) (131:2.1) Moses, educated as an El Shaddaist, became an El Elyonist through his father-in-law, blending traditions of Melchizedek to create an expanded concept of Deity for the Hebrews. (96:4.2) (96:5.3) Kenites, Calebites, Jebusites, and other Canaanites—nomadic Palestinians—joined David's new kingdom of Judah in Hebron. (97:9.7) The progeny of Abraham through Isaac, as intermarried with the Kenites, were the only line to maintain a clear concept of the Salem teachings, which Machiventa Melchizedek continued to take a great interest in. (93:10.3)
improvedDescription: A nomadic Canaanite tribe that preserved the Salem religion of Melchizedek and maintained belief in El Elyon. The Kenites influenced Hebrew religious development through intermarriage with Abraham's descendants and their preservation of Melchizedek's teachings, which Moses later incorporated into Israelite worship.

name: Kentucky
originalDescription: The eruption of the great North American volcano in eastern Kentucky, 330,000,000 years ago, was one of the world's most significant geological events. (59:2.5) (59:4.6)
improvedDescription: The eruption of the great North American volcano in eastern Kentucky 330,000,000 years ago was one of the world's most significant geological events, covering 500 square miles with volcanic ash 15-20 feet deep.

name: Kerioth
originalDescription: Judas Iscariot, the twelfth apostle, born in Kerioth, was chosen by Nathaniel before being disowned by his Sadducee parents for joining John the Baptist's disciples. (139:12.1)
improvedDescription: A small town in southern Judea, the birthplace of Judas Iscariot, the twelfth apostle who was chosen by Nathaniel.

name: Keturah
originalDescription: The narrative of Keturah as a concubine of Abraham was intentionally altered in the Hebrew records during the Babylonian captivity. (93:9.8)
improvedDescription: A concubine of Abraham whose status in Hebrew records was deliberately altered during the Babylonian captivity, when she was incorrectly portrayed as Abraham's wife rather than his concubine.

name: keys of the kingdom
originalDescription: Jesus gave his chosen apostles and their successors authority over things temporal, entrusting them with the affairs of the kingdom of heaven. (140:2.2-3) (157:4.5-6) The keys of the kingdom of heaven are sincerity, more sincerity, and more sincerity; all men possess these keys and can advance in spirit status through decisions, more decisions, and more decisions, always choosing to do the will of God to achieve the highest moral value. (39:4.14)
improvedDescription: Jesus gave his apostles authority over temporal affairs of the kingdom, while the spiritual keys of the kingdom are sincerity and moral decisions to do God's will. All people possess these keys and can advance spiritually through choosing the highest possible values.

name: Kheresa
originalDescription: Alpheus twins were from Kheresa, a town in the coast of Galilee Sea (138:2.6-7) (138:4.1) (139:10.1) about the lunatic of Kheresa, see link to Amos | Amos (Kheresa lunatic)
improvedDescription: A fishing village on the shore of the Sea of Galilee, home to the twin apostles James and Judas Alpheus. Jesus and his apostles frequently visited Kheresa during their ministry, using it as a base for their travels in the region.

name: Kidron
originalDescription: Jesus and his apostles lived in tents near the brook Kidron, spending time in the shaded park of Gethsemane without teaching publicly, while receiving visitors and occasionally venturing into Jerusalem. (142:8.4)
improvedDescription: A brook near Jerusalem that flows past the Mount of Olives and Gethsemane. Jesus and his apostles often crossed the Kidron valley when traveling between Jerusalem and their camp at Gethsemane.

name: kindred spirits
originalDescription: The cosmic mind connects kindred spirits through astonishingly similar and cooperative human and superhuman minds. (16:6.3) Kindred spirits are spiritually minded persons of like longings, drawn together by a direct attractiveness of spirit nature. (7:1.6)
improvedDescription: Spiritually minded persons of similar tastes and longings who are drawn together by direct spiritual attraction. The cosmic mind explains the kinship and fraternal cooperation between such kindred minds, both human and superhuman.

name: king-making episode
originalDescription: Despite the overwhelming desertion of Jesus' followers following the king-making episode, about five hundred remained steadfast in their following of him. (152:3) (152:5.2)
improvedDescription: The attempt by the multitude to proclaim Jesus king after the feeding of the five thousand, which led to widespread desertion when Jesus rejected earthly kingship. Only about five hundred of the original five thousand remained faithful followers after this episode.

name: king making war
originalDescription: The parable of the king making war reminds us to carefully assess the cost before embarking on any endeavor. (171:2.3)
improvedDescription: The parable of the king making war reminds us to carefully assess the cost before embarking on any endeavor. Used by Jesus to teach the necessity of counting the cost of discipleship before making commitments.

name: king of the Jews
originalDescription: Jesus accused of referring to himself as the king of the Jews, Pilate questioned him and found his kingdom to be spiritual and not of this world. (185:2.13) (185:3.1-4) (185:5.9) (185:6.2) (185:7.5) (185:8.1) (186:2.8) (187:3.3-4) The chief priests' clamoring for the pardon of a murderer angered Pilate more when they preferred him over Jesus, figuratively called the king of the Jews, inciting the Jews' resentment and hatred. (185:5.6) The sign on Jesus' cross read “Jesus of Nazareth - the King of the Jews,” causing outrage among the Jewish authorities. (187:1.2-3) (187:2.5)
improvedDescription: A title applied to Jesus during his trial before Pilate, referring to accusations that he claimed temporal kingship. Jesus clarified to Pilate that his kingdom was spiritual, not of this world, and that he was indeed a king in the sense of ruling over the family of faith sons of his heavenly Father. The inscription "Jesus of Nazareth—the King of the Jews" was placed on his cross, causing outrage among Jewish authorities.

name: kingdom
originalDescription: about the believers in the kingdom, see link below | faith sons of God A kingdom divided against itself cannot stand, for if Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? (153:4.3) about the kingdom of God, see link to kingdom of heaven | kingdom of heaven
improvedDescription: A concept central to Jesus' teachings, referring to spiritual realities rather than earthly dominions. Jesus taught that a kingdom divided against itself cannot stand, using this principle to refute accusations that he cast out devils by Beelzebub's power.

name: kingdom of heaven
originalDescription: description entrance establishment | gospel:proclaiming Jews other uses of the term
improvedDescription: The central theme of Jesus' teaching, representing the spiritual brotherhood of God-knowing individuals and the realization of divine sonship. Jesus taught that the kingdom of heaven is not a temporal or political realm, but rather the reign of God's spirit in human hearts, achieved through faith, spiritual rebirth, and doing the Father's will.

name: kings
originalDescription: The aristocracy exerted a restraining influence upon unscrupulous kings seeking power through poison and diabolical magic. (70:6.6) Kings, believed sacred and often kept in seclusion, were revered as possessing supernatural royal blood extending back to ancient times, their touch thought to cure disease and their very presence inspiring fear and special court speech. (70:6.4-5) War chiefs increasingly ruled during peacetimes and ensured quick transitions to new wars, showing they were not fond of peace. (70:5.6) Early kings were greatly restricted by mores, the unwritten laws of tradition and public opinion, until they evolved to enforce legislative enactments reflecting the crystallization of public opinion. (70:12.3-4) Hereditary kingship, stemming from belief in divine origins, prevented succession anarchy and evolved from patriarchal authority to be regarded as supernatural and fetishized by the people. (70:6.2-4) (85:6.5) The prestige of kings and queens throughout the universe realms stems from their knowledge of the Material Sons and Daughters who serve as actual rulers, or directors, of settled worlds in light and life. (55:7.2) Moneylenders, craving power and treasure, established themselves as kings through debtor armies and enslavement at an interest rate of one hundred percent per year. (69:5.8) Throughout history, kings were permitted to marry close kin to maintain the purity of royal blood. (82:5.4,7) Despite their power, kings were always restrained by the ever-present fear of assassination, as medicine men, witch doctors, priests, aristocracy, and rebellions were all checks on their authority. (70:6.6) War, not debate, has always been the true arbiter of power, as history shows that a single strong leader can triumph over divided clan heads. (70:5.5) Primitives worshiped kings as superhuman beings inspired by the gods. (85:6.2)
improvedDescription: Temporal rulers who evolved from war chiefs and patriarchal authority, becoming hereditary positions believed to possess divine origins. Throughout history, kings were both revered as superhuman beings and constrained by various forces including mores, aristocracy, priests, and the constant fear of assassination. The concept of kingship gained universal prestige from knowledge of the Material Sons and Daughters who serve as actual planetary rulers.

name: Kirmeth of Bagdad
originalDescription: Kirmeth of Bagdad, the trance prophet and self-deceived pretender, caused a disturbance at the camp before Jesus allowed him to preach freely for a few days. (148:8.3)
improvedDescription: A trance prophet from Baghdad who arrived at the Bethsaida encampment with peculiar visions and fantastic dreams. Though he created considerable disturbance and his teachings were unsound according to the gospel of the kingdom, Jesus allowed him freedom to preach for several days before he departed with only a few followers.

name: Kish
originalDescription: Sargon, the priest of Kish, ended the weak rule of city priests by proclaiming himself king and initiating the conquest of Mesopotamia, leading to the formation of a confederation of Sargon in Mesopotamia city-states. (78:8.8-9) The Salem school at Kish made early progress until Nabodad's attack on temple harlotry led to their failure and defeat, with their spiritual teachings later being incorporated into the Old Testament Psalms and the Book of Job. (95:1.6,10)
improvedDescription: An ancient Mesopotamian city-state where Sargon served as priest before proclaiming himself king and conquering Mesopotamia. Kish was also the location of a Salem school that made early progress in spreading Melchizedek's teachings until it failed after attempting social reforms against temple practices.

name: kiss
originalDescription: Early Christians greeted with a holy kiss, embodying a fellowship of worship, sharing, and hopeful anticipation of Jesus' return. (194:4.7) Judas betrayed Jesus with a kiss, sealing his deceitful act with false words of respect and loyalty. (183:3.5) The ancient origin of social kissing lies in the ceremony of saliva exchange during adoption rituals. (70:3.7)
improvedDescription: A form of greeting and betrayal with both social and spiritual significance. Early Christians greeted each other with a holy kiss as part of their fellowship, while Judas used a kiss to betray Jesus to his captors. The practice of social kissing originated from ancient adoption ceremonies involving saliva exchange.

name: knocking on wood
originalDescription: Despite the continuation of primitive ritual, modern man no longer openly attempts to coerce spirits, instead showing a disposition to bargain with Deity through actions like knocking on wood and crossing fingers. (87:6.17)
improvedDescription: A superstitious practice representing modern man's continued disposition to bargain with Deity, despite no longer openly attempting to coerce spirits as primitive peoples did.

name: knotted cords
originalDescription: Jesus exposed and denounced belief in spells, ordeals, bewitching, cursing, signs, mandrakes, and knotted cords. (150:3.12)
improvedDescription: A form of superstitious belief involving the use of tied cords for magical purposes. Jesus exposed and denounced belief in knotted cords along with other forms of ignorant and enslaving superstition.

name: knowledge
originalDescription: A little knowledge is potentially evil, as it is a dangerous thing; truth, on the other hand, is beautiful and symmetrical, leading the wise man to universalize his heart. (2:7.4) (131:8.4) (195:6.3) Human culture and civilization are built through the cumulative communication of knowledge and wisdom across generations, manifesting in activities such as art, science, religion, and philosophy. (160:2.3) Knowledge is a torch passed on through generations, allowing man to stand on the shoulders of his predecessors and contribute to the sum of culture and knowledge. (81:6.23) In days of barbarism, knowledge was dangerous to possess due to the fear of being accused of sorcery and executed. (88:6.6) The Census Directors are a special creation, existing in unknown numbers, able to maintain synchrony with reflectivity technique and awareness of will births, except on Paradise, where knowledge is inherent. (24:2.2) (30:3.5) The quest for knowledge and wisdom is fueled by effort, leading to the transformation of civilization through experience and education. (50:5.7) The custodians of knowledge are eternal seekers, always open to acquiring more. (27:5) (44:3.6) (102:2.4) Evolutionary knowledge, the accumulation of protoplasmic memory material, is the most primitive form of creature consciousness, while wisdom, derived from protoplasmic memory and enabled by the spirits of the Father and Sons, sets human mind apart from mere animal knowledge. (101:6.4) Belief outweighs pure knowledge in shaping behavior and personal achievements, as factual knowledge only holds influence when emotionally engaged, particularly through the powerful unifying force of religion. (99:4.5) All finite knowledge and creature understanding are relative, gleaned from even high sources, only relatively complete, locally accurate, and personally true. (2:7.1) The goal of the master philosophers of Paradise is to lead native and ascendant minds in the exhilarating pursuit of attaining truth through solving universe problems. (27:6.2) Find Jesus' ideals, strive for his life purpose, and share his religious faith for the greatest knowledge. (196:1.3) Invention precedes the acceleration of cultural development worldwide. (81:6.9) Jesus accumulated knowledge as a child, then classified and correlated it as a youth, and now organizes it for his teaching and ministry. (127:6.14) In knowledge alone there can never be absolute certainty, only increasing probability of approximation. (102:2.4) Science's discoveries are not truly real in human consciousness until their facts are unraveled and correlated into meaning. (102:2.5) Material knowledge allows for understanding ideas and ideals, combining personal truth with practicality in everyday life. (111:6.7) In the postMagisterial era, physical sciences have reached their height of development as society begins to return to simplified forms of living, leading to an age of flourishing art, music, and higher learning. (52:4.8) (55:4.8) Acquiring knowledge is not about possessing it for oneself, but about sharing it with others in order to safeguard it with wisdom and socialize it with love. (25:4.12) (48:7.28) Recognition is the intellectual process of fitting sensory impressions into memory patterns, which leads to organized principles. (111:4.1) Knowledge is the realm of the fact-discerning mind, while truth resides in the spiritually conscious intellect. (130:4.10)
improvedDescription: Knowledge encompasses the accumulation of facts and information, which becomes valuable through experience and wisdom. While a little knowledge can be dangerous without proper understanding, the pursuit of knowledge through education and learning forms the foundation of civilization and culture. Knowledge must be shared and socialized with love to safeguard it with wisdom, as it represents the material or fact-discerning domain of mind that prepares individuals for spiritual truth and service to others.

name: Kopet Dagh
originalDescription: Adamson's civilization was situated near the Kopet Dagh, the cradle of Occidental and Indian civilizations. (77:5.10) (78:1.3)
improvedDescription: A mountain range near the southern shore of the Caspian Sea where Adamson's civilization was centered. This region served as the secondary headquarters of the violet race and was the cradle of Occidental and Indian civilizations, with various Adamsonite cultures arising in the fertile foothills over different periods.

name: Koran
improvedDescription: The sacred text of Islam, containing the religious teachings and revelations central to the Islamic faith.

name: Korea
originalDescription: Asoka established Buddhism in Korea, where it soon became the dominant religion, spreading its message of salvation and enlightenment throughout eastern Asia. (94:9.1) (94:11.1)
improvedDescription: A nation in eastern Asia where Buddhism was established by Asoka's missionaries and became the dominant religion. Buddhism later amalgamated with local religious customs and spread throughout the region along with China and Japan.

name: Kung Fu-tze
improvedDescription: The Chinese name for Confucius, the ancient Chinese philosopher and teacher who founded Confucianism, one of the major philosophical and ethical systems that influenced Chinese culture and society.

name: Kyrios
originalDescription: Kyrios is a Semite appellation of Deity, among other names such as Adonai and The Lord of Hosts. (96:1.9)
improvedDescription: A Semitic name for Deity meaning "Lord," used among various appellations by the Semites who preferred not to speak the actual name of their God. It was one of several titles including Adonai, The Lord of Hosts, and The Almighty.

name: labor
originalDescription: The ascendant life finds balance in an equal division between work and play, embracing joyful mirth and spiritual laughter as universal expressions. (48:4.1) The early division of labor among Andonites greatly advanced civilization, leading to the coordination of specialists in society. (63:4.2) (81:6.29) The ideal society compels all able-bodied persons to engage in compulsory employment to eliminate idleness and poverty, promoting self-sustainability and productivity. (69:8.11) (71:3.7-8) (71:4.8) Compulsory labor in this continental nation is both honorable and mandatory for all able-bodied citizens over eighteen. (72:5.11) (72:6.4,6) The industrial courts in the continental nation are functioning satisfactorily, recognizing legal compensation divided into rates of interest on capital, skill salary, and fair wages for labor, with any excess earnings distributed among all three divisions. (72:5.4-8) Devise techniques for locating suitable employment to ensure effective co-ordination of specialists in an expanding and diversifying society. (81:6.29-33) Labor, the efforts of design, distinguishes man from the beast and is man's paramount blessing; the Hebrews were the first to put a premium on industry. (69:2.5) Elevate the drudgery of daily toil to the high levels of a fine art, ministering as for God in all earthly labor. (133:4.8) (181:2.19) (192:2.13) “He who does not work shall not eat” is a paramount blessing that distinguishes man from the beast. (69:2.5) (140:8.2) Labor, the Hebrews were first to put premium on, distinguishes man from the beast and is man's paramount blessing. (69:2.5) Idleness is destructive of self-respect; therefore, take care not to wound the self-respect of timid and fearful souls by indulging in sarcasm or cynicism towards them. (159:3.4) Jesus was painstaking about the essentials of any undertaking, taking great satisfaction in completing commendable handiwork at the boatshop. (134:9.7) Jesus, very regular in his working hours, had arranged to advise his brothers on baptism the following day after close communion with the Father in heaven. (135:8.2) Labor is ennobling, but the threat of standardized industrial slavery leads to personality stagnation. (70:2.17) Labor, man's paramount blessing, distinguishes him from instinctive beasts, and ennoble physical labor, and Creator God ordained work as a sacred duty. (69:2.5) In spiritual worlds, all service is sacred and exhilarating; there is no such thing as menial labor, as even the servitals are not considered servants in a menial sense. (25:1.1) The work of your status sphere is of utmost importance; self-importance exhausts, not the effort to achieve. (48:6.37) Primitive man initially disliked labor but recognized the advantages of organization and specialization, leading to the division of labor and the pursuit of less resistance for efficiency. (69:2.3-4) (102:2.7) Be compassionate and proactive in helping others find meaningful work, without causing harm to their self-esteem or fear. (159:3.4) Labor should alternate with leisure, just as worship should be balanced by service and religion by humor. (143:7.3) The specialization of labor, driven by adapting to pressure and pursuing paths of lessened resistance, brought immediate savings of time and materials. (69:2.3) (69:3) Specialization of labor on morontia worlds enhances vocational enhancement and mutual ministry among evolving spirits.
