(96:1.11) The evolution of religious concepts, from Mount Horeb to Mount Sinai, shaped the perception of deity for the Hebrew Semites and enlightened Christians alike. (94:12.1) (96:1.3,11) The Ten Commandments were promulgated at Mount Sinai by Moses in the name of Yahweh, the god of the Hebrews. (96:4.4)
improvedDescription: Mount Sinai was an intermittently active volcano up to about 2000 B.C. whose eruptions awed the Bedouins and inspired Hebrew worship of Yahweh. It was where Moses promulgated the Ten Commandments and where the Israelites received iron ores that enhanced Egyptian metalworking skills.

name: Mount Tabor
originalDescription: In late June, 3 A.D., Jesus and father Joseph reached the summit of Mount Tabor, marveling at the breathtaking view on a clear day. (124:1.6)
improvedDescription: Mount Tabor is part of a mountain range east of Nazareth, which nine-year-old Jesus climbed with his father Joseph in late June 3 A.D., marveling at the superb view from its summit on a clear day.

name: mountains
originalDescription: origin mountains in faith mountains in the heavens To see a list of mountains named in The Urantia Book see links below | Adirondack Mountains; Alps; Andes mountains; Apennines; Appalachian Mountains; Black Forest mountains; Brenner Pass; Caledonian mountains; Carpathians; Catskill Mountains; Dakota Black Hills; Eastern Ghats; Himalayas; Kopet Dagh; Longs Peak; Moab, hills of; Mount Ararat; Mount Carmel; Mount Gerizim; Mount Hermon; Mount Hood; Mount Lebanon; Mount Olivet; Mount Olympus; Mount Rainier; Mount Royal; Mount Sartaba; Mount Shasta; Mount Sinai; Mount Tabor; Pikes Peak; Pyrenees; Rocky Mountains; Siwalik Hills; Ural mountains; Vosges mountains; Western Ghats
improvedDescription: Mountains are geological formations that result from the action of rain, gravity, and erosion rather than from planetary cooling and contraction. They form when lighter land areas filled with erosion deposits are subjected to crustal pressure, causing them to crumple, fold, and rise upward.

name: mourning
originalDescription: Survivors disguised themselves in mourning costumes to deceive ghosts and show respect for the dead. (87:1.5) Jesus found flute players at Jairus's house playing for the mourners, who did not believe his words about the damsel not being dead. (152:1.1) Long and frequent periods of mourning inactivity were a great obstacle to civilization’s advancement, wasted on rituals rooted in fear, not sorrow. (87:2.4-5) Jesus resented forced outward exhibitions of mourning, as he was perturbed by the contention between true love and false show of affection. (168:0.12) (168:1.4) Those who mourn with tenderness shall find comfort and true happiness. (140:3.8) (140:5.16) Professional mourners were hired for funeral occasions in ancient times, indicating that mourning was a ritual rooted in fear rather than genuine sorrow. (87:2.5) Prolonged mourning for ancient widows was a feared and tragic fate, often leading to blame, death, or suicide due to unbearable social restrictions and disapproval of remarriage. (82:3.12) Self-torture was a common form of mourning, believed to please ghosts lurking before departure to deadland. (87:2.4)
improvedDescription: Mourning in ancient times was largely a ritual rooted in fear of ghosts rather than genuine sorrow, involving practices like self-torture, disguised clothing, and professional mourners. Jesus distinguished between sincere tenderhearted mourning, which he blessed, and forced outward exhibitions of mourning, which he resented.

name: mouth
originalDescription: Choose now between the will of the Father and the self-chosen ways of darkness, for from the abundance in your hearts, your mouths will speak. (153:4.4) What proceeds out of the mouth spiritually defiles, not what enters it. (153:3.5)
improvedDescription: The mouth represents the expression of what originates in the heart, with Jesus teaching that spiritual defilement comes not from what enters the mouth but from the evil thoughts, words, and deeds that proceed out of it.

name: multilingualism
improvedDescription: The ability to speak or use multiple languages, though specific details about multilingualism are not provided in the available context.

name: multitude
improvedDescription: A large gathering or crowd of people.

name: music
originalDescription: among primitives evolution of music celestial music
improvedDescription: A universal language of sound and rhythm that has evolved from primitive monotony to higher forms of harmony and melody. Music serves as the speech of Havona and represents spiritual expression, with celestial music being far superior to earthly compositions.

name: musk oxen
originalDescription: Musk oxen were among the few animal species that survived the rigorous glacial period in North America. (61:5.7) (61:7.13)
improvedDescription: Large Arctic mammals that were among the few animal species to survive the rigorous glacial periods in North America by following the advancing and retreating ice sheets.

name: mussels
originalDescription: Mussels are part of the ancient lineage of bivalve gastropods that have persisted through millions of years. (59:2.12)
improvedDescription: Bivalve gastropods that are part of an ancient lineage of shell animals that have persisted through millions of years with little change from their ancient forms.

name: mustard seed
originalDescription: The kingdom of heaven is like a mustard seed, constantly growing and adapting to the needs of each generation. (140:8.27) (178:1.15) The parable of the mustard seed teaches growth and transformation in the kingdom of heaven. (151:4.2)
improvedDescription: A tiny seed used by Jesus in parables to illustrate the kingdom of heaven, representing how something small can grow into something magnificent and how the gospel unfolds in new manifestations across generations.

name: myrrh
originalDescription: Jesus embalmed with myrrh and aloes by Joseph and Nicodemus in a small tomb chamber. (188:1.4)
improvedDescription: An aromatic resin used in the embalming process, notably used by Joseph and Nicodemus to prepare Jesus' body for burial in the tomb.

name: mysteries
originalDescription: Mysteries and power evoke deep religious emotions, shaping the evolution of beliefs from fear to reverence and compassion. (92:1.4) Conciliating commissions evolve into explainers of mysterious phenomena, transitioning from arbitrators to interpretative teachers in the Superuniverse Major Sectors. (25:3.12) To understand the nature and origin of Adjusters, we must gather information from 1001 different sources and correlate it to gain informative knowledge. (107:3.8) The mind reaches out to discover the hidden mysteries. (42:12.3) particular mysteries purpose of divine mysteries
improvedDescription: Phenomena that are not fully understood or explained, ranging from cosmic forces and divine purposes to the nature of personality and spiritual communion. Mysteries stimulate religious feelings and serve as subjects for philosophical exploration.

name: mystery cults
originalDescription: Augustus tried to destroy the imported cults and mysteries that had conquered the Roman state. (98:3.6) Christianity provided comfort and liberation in Rome, amid the struggle between Stoicism and the mystery cults. (195:3.3) Mystery cults influenced Christianity through the Roman version of the miraculous birth of Mithras. (98:7.7) Mithras replaced Phrygian and Egyptian mysteries as the greatest of all mystery cults, spreading throughout the Roman Empire. (98:5.1) The most popular mystery cults were Cybele and Attis, Osiris and Isis, and Mithras. (98:4.2-5) The mystery cults were personal, fraternal, and united diverse individuals across cultures and nations. (121:5.9) The mystery cults offered hopes of personal salvation and provided diversion and excitement, satisfying the spiritual hunger of the people but ultimately failing to satisfy their longing for true righteousness and redemption. (98:3.9) (121:5.6-16) The mystery cults from Egypt and the Levant provided promises of salvation and hope for immortality to the majority in the Greco-Roman world who had lost their traditional religions and were unable to understand Greek philosophy. (98:4.1) The Orphic brotherhood stood out as the best among the mystery cults, offering moral guidance and promises of salvation that resonated with many, despite the ignorance and disinterest of the average men of that time in Greek philosophy and abstract deities. (98:2.10) The mystery cults were centered around a god's life, death, and rebirth, as seen in Mithraism and Christianity. (121:5.8) The mystery cults plunged Greece into intellectual stagnation, moral depravity, and spiritual poverty, pushing aside the Greek philosophy of self-realization and abstract Deity in favor of promises of salvation from a personal God. (98:2.10-11) (98:3.5) Mystery cults paved the way for Jesus and Christianity, offering individual salvation and preparing the spiritually hungry for superior teachings. (121:5.6,12) Some mystery cults had gruesome secret rites and revolting rituals. (121:5.10)
improvedDescription: Personal and fraternal religious movements from Egypt and the Levant that flourished in the Greco-Roman world, offering individual salvation through elaborate initiation ceremonies and secret rituals centered around mythical stories of gods' death and rebirth.

name: Mystery Monitors
improvedDescription: Another name for Thought Adjusters, referring to their mysterious nature and function as divine fragments that monitor and guide mortal minds.

name: mysticism
originalDescription: Do not be passive mystics, but instead be valiant defenders of righteousness and aggressive in spreading the gospel of the kingdom. (178:1.14) During the European “dark ages”, the church's decline led to a shift towards monasticism and legalism, while Christian mysticism embraced a spiritually vibrant but unreal and pantheistic worldview. (195:4.1) The factors which induce mystic state are physical fatigue, fasting, psychic dissociation, profound aesthetic experiences, vivid sex impulses, fear, anxiety, rage, and wild dancing. (100:5.10) The habitual practice of religious daydreaming poses great danger, as mysticism may become a technique of reality avoidance, albeit it has sometimes been a means of genuine spiritual communion. (100:5.8) (102:2.8) Mystics experience diffusion of consciousness with vivid islands of focal attention, leading to passive intellect and gravitation towards the subconscious rather than the superconscious, often resulting in abnormal mental manifestations. (100:5.9) True values of creature experience are concealed in depths beyond the single-eyed vision of material scientists and spiritual mystics. (130:4.4) The practical validations of mysticism are evident in the enhanced physical health, mental efficiency, social integration, spiritual living, love for truth, beauty, and goodness, preservation of values, and heightened spiritual insight it brings to individuals. (91:7.5-12) The practice of mysticism, cultivating the consciousness of God's presence, is praiseworthy, but when it leads to social isolation and fanaticism, it becomes reprehensible. (91:7.1) The cultivation of consciousness through mysticism can lead to divine inspiration or fanaticism, depending on the source of the revelation. (91:7.1-4) (100:5.6) (110:4.1-6) (159:3.6) Mysticism is praiseworthy in cultivating the consciousness of God's presence, yet reprehensible when leading to isolation or fanaticism. (91:7.1)
improvedDescription: The practice of cultivating consciousness of God's presence through meditation and spiritual communion. While praiseworthy when leading to enhanced spiritual insight and service, it becomes dangerous when used to escape reality or when resulting in fanaticism and social isolation.

name: myths
originalDescription: Ancients always sought supernatural explanations, leading to the birth of modern science as a result of the fearless search for true causes. (81:2.9) The myth of Eve's creation from Adam's rib combines elements of the Adamic arrival with ancient celestial surgery and the arrival of the Planetary Prince over 450,000 years ago. (74:8.3) The garbled legends of Prince's staff fueled the creation of numerous myths, intertwining with traditions of Adam and Eve's planetary installation. (66:2.8) (66:4.1) (67:4.3-4) (77:2.3) Human leaders are often enshrouded in myths of supernatural origins and miraculous careers, as men have an instinctive longing for help from above and beyond. (92:5.5) Noah and the flood story was influenced by the Babylonian tale and connected with the sacred Mount Ararat in Mesopotamia. (77:4.12) (78:7.3-5) Oriental minds delighted in fairy stories about the star of Bethlehem, weaving beautiful myths about the adoring Magi and the newborn babe. (122:8.7) Early magic and mythology paved the way for superior religion by acknowledging the existence of supermaterial beings, ultimately leading to the unveiling of Jesus' saving gospel and the eradication of superstitious beliefs. (103:9.4) Religious tradition is an imperfectly preserved record altered by the mythmaking nature of primitive man. (4:5.1) The myth of the creation of Urantia in six days was based on the story of Adam and Eve spending just six days in the Garden, a legend developed over thousands of years after the actual events took place. (74:8) The story of Jonah and the whale was important to Gadiah as a lesson on the folly of trying to escape duty, with Jesus using it to teach the importance of seeking truth and righteousness. (130:1.2)
improvedDescription: Traditional stories and legends that often provide supernatural explanations for natural phenomena or historical events. The Urantia Book describes how myths developed from garbled accounts of actual events, such as the arrival of celestial beings, and how ancient peoples created mythological explanations when they lacked understanding of natural causes.

name: Nabal
originalDescription: David strategically married women from powerful families to strengthen his political position, including the widow of Nabal the rich Edomite. (97:9.10)
improvedDescription: A rich Edomite whose widow David married as part of his strategy to strengthen his political position by forming alliances with powerful families.

name: Nabodad
originalDescription: Nabodad, leader of the school at Kish, attempted to eradicate temple harlotry but ultimately led to the downfall of the Salem missionaries' teachings. (95:1.6)
improvedDescription: Leader of the school at Kish who attempted to reform temple practices by attacking prevalent temple harlotry, but his failure led to the downfall of the Salem missionaries' more important spiritual and philosophical teachings.

name: Nabon
originalDescription: Nabon, a Greek Jew and Mithraic high priest, was profoundly impacted by Jesus' teachings and ultimately became an early convert to the gospel of the kingdom. (132:3.1,11)
improvedDescription: A Greek Jew who served as a high priest of the Mithraic mystery cult in Rome. Through his discussions with Jesus about truth and faith, he was profoundly influenced and became one of the early converts to the gospel of the kingdom.

name: Naboths
originalDescription: Elijah confronted the Yahweh-Baal controversy by turning Ahab's murder of the Naboths into a moral issue and launching a campaign against the Baalites. (97:3.6) (97:9.19)
improvedDescription: A family murdered by King Ahab in an intrigue to seize their land, which prompted the prophet Elijah to make their deaths a moral issue and launch his vigorous campaign against the Baalites.

name: Nabu
originalDescription: Nabu was one of the chief Mesopotamian deities exalted by the Salem teachers alongside Bel and Ea. (95:1.4)
improvedDescription: One of the chief Mesopotamian deities that the Salem teachers incorporated into their reduced pantheon, eventually becoming part of the Babylonian triad alongside Bel and Ea.

name: Nahor (evangelist)
originalDescription: Nahor blundered at Nazareth rejection, jeopardizing Jesus' peaceful resolution with the violent crowd. (150:9.3)
improvedDescription: One of the younger evangelists who, along with Simon Zelotes, made a tactical blunder at Nazareth by gathering Jesus' supporters in a belligerent stance against his enemies, which escalated the hostile crowd's anger.

name: Nahor (Old Testament)
originalDescription: Abraham's lukewarm brother Nahor delayed the family's journey to Salem and built up his own city-state instead. (93:5.4-5) (93:9.4) Moses gathered descendants of Abraham, Nahor, and Lot into a self-sustaining nation of pastoral warriors led by Joshua. (96:5.9)
improvedDescription: Abraham's brother who was a lukewarm convert to the Salem religion and persuaded the family to delay their journey to Salem by staying in Haran. He later built up a strong city-state bearing his name rather than fully embracing Melchizedek's teachings.

name: Nahor (rabbi)
originalDescription: Nahor, a Jerusalem rabbi, advised Joseph and Mary to allow Jesus to come to Jerusalem for education, but Jesus decided to stay with his parents after consulting with them, a neighbor, and ultimately, his Father in heaven. (123:6.8-9)
improvedDescription: A Jerusalem rabbi and teacher at an academy who came to Nazareth to observe Jesus and proposed taking him to Jerusalem for education. After Jesus consulted with his parents and prayed to his heavenly Father, he decided to remain at home with his family.

name: nails
originalDescription: Thomas would not believe until seeing marks of truth in the nail marks of the risen Master's hands. (191:5.4) Jesus was bound and nails used in crucifixion, making his suffering excruciatingly visible and audible. (187:2.1)
improvedDescription: The metal fasteners used to secure Jesus to the cross during his crucifixion, piercing his hands to the crossbeam and his feet to the upright timber. Thomas later referenced the nail marks as evidence he would need to believe in Jesus' resurrection.

name: Nain
originalDescription: The hill of Nain stood tall near Jesus' home in Nazareth, providing a scenic backdrop for his frequent strolls in the countryside. (122:6.1) Joseph, father of Jesus, worked at Nain and various other towns in the surrounding areas, where Jesus gained practical knowledge and insight about mankind. (123:3.8) The widow's son at Nain was raised from the dead, causing an emotional frenzy and miracle belief among the people of Galilee. (146:6)
improvedDescription: Nain was a hill town in Galilee, about the same height as Mount Tabor, where Joseph did carpentry work and Jesus gained practical knowledge of mankind. It was the site where Jesus raised the widow's son from the dead, causing great excitement among the miracle-seeking people of Galilee.

name: Nalda
originalDescription: Nalda was the woman Jesus met at the well of Sychar (143:5) Jesus' first pronouncement about his divine nature was to Nalda (143:5.7-8) She was at Jesus' seventeenth appearance (193:1)
improvedDescription: Nalda was the Samaritan woman Jesus met at Jacob's well in Sychar, to whom he made his first direct pronouncement of his divine nature and sonship on earth. She was present at Jesus' seventeenth morontia appearance to about seventy-five Samaritan believers near the same well.

name: Nambia
originalDescription: Nambia is the first-born Life Carrier of Nebadon, part of the efficient, self-governing corps of one hundred million Life Carriers directed by Gabriel and the Father Melchizedek in the universe of Nebadon. (36:1.2)
improvedDescription: Nambia is the original and first-born Life Carrier of Nebadon, serving as one of the life-determining trio along with Gabriel and the Father Melchizedek who direct the corps of one hundred million Life Carriers in the universe.

name: names
originalDescription: After fusion, mortals receive their real universe names. (47:8.5) (108:3.3) Hereditary kingship and aristocracy were established to prevent anarchy through name ownership. (70:6.3) Human subjects are often known by numbers of their Adjusters until receiving real universe names after Adjuster fusion, signaled by the bestowal of the new name by the destiny guardian. (108:3.3) Parental love and pride in children's success transcends biological instincts, evolving into a new realm of name pride and family pride. (84:7.10,15) Primitive man believed that names were sacred entities that could be pawned, like today's signed notes. (88:5.5) Translating the names of celestial beings into human language proves to be a challenging task for revelators. (18:4.3)
improvedDescription: Names hold spiritual significance in the universe, with mortals receiving their real universe names after Adjuster fusion. Primitive cultures regarded names as sacred entities that could be pawned like property, and translating celestial being names into human language presents challenges for revelators.

name: Nanak, Guru
improvedDescription: Guru Nanak was a religious teacher and founder of Sikhism in India.

name: Naomi
originalDescription: Naomi was the bride at wedding at Cana where Jesus made the first real, although unintentional, miracle (137:3.6)
improvedDescription: Naomi was the bride at the wedding in Cana where Jesus performed his first miracle, turning water into wine, marking the beginning of his public ministry and miraculous demonstrations.

name: Naples
originalDescription: In Naples, a beggar lacked the ability to respond to spirit leading. (130:8.4) Claudus preached in Rome, Naples, and Spain after being inspired by Jesus in Malta. (130:8.1) Jesus’ stop at Naples provided an opportunity for Ganid to learn about compassion and discernment in helping those in need. (130:8.4-5)
improvedDescription: Naples was a major Italian port city where Jesus, Gonod, and Ganid stayed during their Mediterranean journey. Here Jesus taught Ganid about discernment in helping others and encountered a street beggar who lacked the capacity for spiritual response.

name: Nasanta
originalDescription: Nasanta, daughter of Elman, was chosen by Jesus to be one of the ten women in the ministering work of the kingdom. (150:1.1) At Jesus’ 16th appearance, he instructed the believers to proclaim the gospel of the kingdom and serve their fellow men with love and faith. (193:0)
improvedDescription: Nasanta, daughter of Elman the Syrian physician, was one of the ten women commissioned by Jesus for the ministering work of the kingdom, marking a revolutionary step in his ministry.

name: Nathan of Busiris
originalDescription: Nathan of Busiris, who had carried news of crucifixion to Alexandria, was present when Jesus appeared before believers in morontia form at about half past eight on the evening of Tuesday, April 18. (191:6.1)
improvedDescription: Nathan of Busiris was the fifth messenger in the Jerusalem-Alexandria relay who carried news of Jesus' crucifixion to the believers in Alexandria, where Jesus appeared in morontia form during his report.

name: Nathan of Caesarea
originalDescription: Nathan of Caesarea, a Greek Jew, was encouraged by John to write the Gospel of John. (121:8.10) (139:4.4,15)
improvedDescription: Nathan of Caesarea was a Greek Jew who wrote the Gospel of John under the direction and inspiration of the apostle John when John was ninety-nine years old at Ephesus.

name: Nathan of Cana
originalDescription: Nathan of Cana was the father of the groom of the wedding at Cana, where Jesus made the first real, although unintentional, miracle (137:3.6) Despite the father of the bridegroom providing plenty of wine, a shortage occurred during the marriage feast, prompting Mary to confidently seek help from Jesus. (137:4.7)
improvedDescription: Nathan of Cana was the father of the bridegroom Johab at the wedding feast in Cana where Jesus performed his first miracle. Despite providing ample wine for the celebration, a shortage occurred during the festivities, prompting Mary to confidently seek Jesus' help.

name: Nathan of Nazareth
originalDescription: Nathan of Nazareth, the potter near the flowing spring, inspired young Jesus to dream of becoming a potter himself. (123:5.15) (124:1.3)
improvedDescription: Nathan of Nazareth was a potter who worked near a flowing spring and became a friend to young Jesus and the neighbor boy Jacob. His skillful work on the potter's wheel inspired both boys to dream of becoming potters, and he encouraged their creativity by giving them clay to play with.

name: Nathaniel (Apostle)
originalDescription: Nathaniel of Cana, the only support of his infirm parents, joined Jesus and his disciples on their journey to Galilee at the age of 25 in A.D. 26. (137:2.3) (139:6.2) Nathaniel, chosen as apostle, joined Jesus after being convinced by Philip. (137:2) Nathaniel, the apostolic philosopher and dreamer, was the odd genius among the twelve, offering profound philosophy alongside rare humor and becoming the group's controlling influence throughout the tragic day. (139:6.4) (147:4.10) (191:0.7) Judas Iscariot, selected by Nathaniel, was the only Judean among Jesus' apostles and had a troubled past with his Sadducee parents disowning him. (138:2.9) (139:12.2) Nathaniel watched over apostles' families diligently, ensuring their needs were met and providing funds as required. (138:10.5) (139:6.7) Nathaniel, well-educated and the youngest of seven, was the only support for his aged parents in Cana and had aspirations of becoming a merchant. (139:6.2) Nathaniel, chosen as the last apostle by Jesus, was introduced by his business partner Philip to the Master while on their way to see John the Baptist. (139:6.1) Nathaniel was a personal worker, conducting classes for special groups of inquirers alongside Philip, Thomas, and Simon. (141:3.2) During a week of illness, Nathaniel and James Zebedee suffered from a painful digestive disturbance, prompting Jesus to minister to his afflicted apostles instead of using instant healing. (154:2.4) Nathaniel, who attended session of Sanhedrin, met Jesus after he spoke about the Father and faced accusations of blasphemy from the Pharisees. (164:5.3) Nathaniel asked Jesus about the place of justice and mercy in the teachings of the kingdom, seeking clarification on the laws of Moses and the rigorous requirements of apostleship. (140:6.9) (146:2.1) (147:4.1) (148:5.1) (156:5.3) (159:4.1) (164:3.2) (167:7.1) (176:0.2) (181:2.23) Nathaniel delivered a powerful speech on “Supreme Desire” to a small group, highlighting the importance of wholehearted love for the Master. (177:3.2) Nathaniel taught the art of teaching in a daily company training session led by Jesus and other apostles. (163:0.2) Nathaniel taught that ability and faithfulness are key factors in determining life's opportunities and rewards, as illustrated in the parables of talents and pounds. (171:8.9-13) Nathaniel, by calling attention to Jesus’ teachings regarding nonresistance, influenced the apostles to refrain from using their swords in defense of Jesus. (182:2.3) (183:4.2) Throughout the tragic day of resurrection Sunday, Nathaniel's philosophic counsel proved to be the controlling influence among the apostles. (191:0.5,7) During discussions with Rodan, Nathaniel and Thomas delved into harmonizing Greek philosophy with Jesus' teachings. (160:0.1) (161:0.1-2) Nathaniel understood the strategic purpose behind Jesus' entry into Jerusalem, showing poise and confidence in the Master's wisdom and foresight. (172:5.7) Jesus’ admonitions to Nathaniel: “Judge not by appearances; remain firm in your faith when all appears to vanish; be true to your commission as an ambassador of the kingdom.” (174:0.2) (181:2.21-24) (192:2.10) Jesus’ talk with Nathaniel about Scriptures enlightened and shocked him to the core. (159:4) attitude after Jesus
improvedDescription: Nathaniel of Cana was the sixth and last apostle chosen by Jesus, brought to the Master by his friend Philip. At age 25, he was the youngest of seven children and the only support for his aged parents. Known as the "apostolic philosopher and dreamer," he was well-educated, honest, and sincere, though sometimes proud and prejudiced. He served as the group's controlling influence during crises and was responsible for overseeing the welfare of the apostles' families.

name: Nathaniel the Pharisee
originalDescription: Nathaniel the Pharisee invited Jesus to breakfast, but Jesus rebuked the Pharisees for their self-righteousness and refusal to acknowledge the truth. (166:1)
improvedDescription: Nathaniel the Pharisee was a wealthy resident of Ragaba who invited Jesus and about twenty fellow Pharisees to a Sabbath breakfast, serving as host with Jesus as the guest of honor.

name: National Council of Defense
originalDescription: The National Council of Defense, empowered to assess states for money, men, and materials in times of war, operates under strict financial limitations set by the federal government in the main continental nation of a nearby inhabited planet. (72:7.7,14) (72:11.1)
improvedDescription: The National Council of Defense is a governing body of twenty-five members on a nearby inhabited planet, empowered to assess states for money, men, and materials during wartime. Led by a president and presided over by the chief of staff of coordinated military affairs, it also commissions graduates of federal military schools as "guardians of civilization."

name: nationalism
originalDescription: The age of Planetary Prince culminates in intense nationalism, but racial struggles and tribal wars continue with diminishing frequency and severity. (52:2.5) Nationalism's delusion of national sovereignty is the chief barrier to world peace, Urantia's lasting peace hinges on surrendering sovereign powers to mankind government. (134:5.10) (134:6.6-7) (195:8.10) National egotism, rooted in the chosen people doctrine, has been essential to social survival; yet, true progress only comes when intolerance is overcome through the integration of science, commerce, play, and religion. (71:3.2) The hero-venerating nationalism of the 20th century echoes primitive ancestor worship and persists in various radical and nationalistic secularisms across the Occident. (92:6.20) Ikhnaton’s gospel fatally lacked appeal due to its internationalist, non-nationalistic Deity concepts, hindering its effectiveness as a nation builder. (95:5.9) Moses wisely encouraged nationalism by nationalizing his religious teachings and proclaiming Yahweh as the Lord God of Israel. (96:4.6) Rome overcame nationalism with imperial universalism, paving the way for diverse nations to embrace one religion. (195:3.2) As race blending increases, nationalism wanes and the era of international brotherhood begins. (52:3.10) War, by fostering and solidifying nationalism, has had social value in promoting discipline, cooperation, fortitude, and courage in past civilizations, but as civilization advances, it must be abandoned for more modern methods of cultural exchange and progress. (70:2.3-9)
improvedDescription: Nationalism is described as both essential to social survival and the chief barrier to world peace. While it has fostered discipline, cooperation, and nation-building throughout history, it represents a delusional virus of national sovereignty that prevents lasting peace until nations surrender their sovereign powers to a unified mankind government.

name: nations
originalDescription: As sovereignty consolidates into larger nations, minor wars diminish, but conflicts escalate as powerful nations brush against each other, signaling the potential for global conflict. (134:5.7) The failure to increase in population prevents the full realization of national destiny, but excessive growth leads to lower standards of living or territorial expansion through conquest. (81:6.11) The Spirit of Truth, a universal world influence, is designed to unify mankind by lessening self-assertiveness in individuals, groups, nations, and races, thus preventing destructive wars. (194:3.18)
improvedDescription: Nations represent consolidated sovereignty that reduces minor wars between smaller groups but increases the potential for greater conflicts as larger, more powerful nations come into contact with each other. The consolidation of sovereignty into fewer, stronger nations sets the stage for major world-wide conflicts.

name: natural events
improvedDescription: Natural events refer to the ordinary occurrences and phenomena that take place according to the established laws and patterns of the physical universe, as distinguished from supernatural or miraculous interventions.

name: natural law
originalDescription: Jesus settled on a consistent policy of living according to natural laws, even though his Personalized Adjuster warned that these laws could be greatly accelerated in certain circumstances. (136:6.2) (136:7) (136:9.9) Man reaps the harvest of efforts to comply with natural laws. (166:4.8) The predictability of physical phenomena decreases as one moves farther from Paradise, encountering forces beyond our understanding despite our grasp of universal laws. (15:8.8-9) Sin affects more than just the individual; its consequences ripple across all levels of existence, but only fully manifest in actions, not just thoughts or intents. (67:7.4)
improvedDescription: Natural laws are the established principles governing the physical universe that Jesus chose to follow consistently during his earthly ministry. These laws provide predictable patterns for physical phenomena, though their predictability decreases with distance from Paradise, and they can be accelerated under certain circumstances.

name: natural philosophy
originalDescription: Natural philosophy tends to dogmatize, just like religion. (42:9)
improvedDescription: Natural philosophy, like religion, tends toward dogmatism in its approach to understanding the physical world and natural phenomena.

name: natural resources
originalDescription: Natural resources were administered as social possessions, community property. (55:3.7) Early wars were fought over natural resources, such as flint, salt, and metals, leading to the first formal tribal treaty focused on a salt deposit, which fostered intertribal communication and unity. (69:4.6) All natural wealth on the continent is held as a social trust by the federal government, with income utilized for social purposes, such as old-age pensions and education. (72:6.7) (72:7.13) The extent of material civilization is largely determined by available natural resources and inherent genius. (78:2.4) (81:6.3) Share discoveries with the largest number of fellows for the benefit of all, without selfishly claiming all advantages. (132:5.16)
improvedDescription: Natural resources are materials and energy sources found in nature that societies can utilize for various purposes. Throughout history, early wars were fought over natural resources like flint, salt, and metals, with the first formal tribal treaty concerning a salt deposit fostering intertribal communication. The extent of material civilization is largely determined by available natural resources and the inherent genius of a people.

name: natural rights
improvedDescription: Natural rights refer to the concept that individuals possess inherent rights that come from their very existence as human beings, rather than being granted by governments or societies.

name: natural selection
originalDescription: Variety is indispensable to natural selection's wide opportunity for differential survival of superior strains. (64:6.31)
improvedDescription: Natural selection is the evolutionary process by which organisms with favorable traits survive and reproduce more successfully than those without them. Variety among organisms is indispensable to provide wide opportunity for the differential survival of superior strains.

name: naturalism
originalDescription: When philosophy leans heavily toward the world of matter, it tends to become rationalistic or naturalistic. (103:6.14)
improvedDescription: Naturalism is a philosophical approach that emphasizes the material world and natural causes. When philosophy leans heavily toward the world of matter, it tends to become rationalistic or naturalistic in its perspective.

name: nature
originalDescription: definition nature for primitives worship of nature natural law God and nature
improvedDescription: Nature is described as the physical habit of God in a limited sense, representing the material manifestation of divine laws as they operate throughout the universe. While nature exhibits divine perfection from Paradise, it is also modified and sometimes marred by the evolutionary processes, imperfections, and rebellions of creatures in time and space. Nature provides no inherent rights to humans and should not be worshiped, though it can serve as an analogy for spiritual truths and inspire contemplation of the Creator.

name: navels
originalDescription: Many shrewd shamans induced autohypnosis by staring at their navels, impressing and mystifying tribesmen with their mysterious conduct and supernatural feats. (90:1.4)
improvedDescription: Many shrewd shamans induced autohypnosis by prolonged staring at their navels, using this technique to impress and mystify tribesmen with their mysterious conduct and seemingly supernatural feats.

name: Nazareth
originalDescription: Nazareth, a liberal center of Jewish thought and culture, was where Jesus received his education and moral training, embracing a diverse community and challenging traditional beliefs. (123:5.7-12) (124:4.8) The climate of Nazareth, ranging from 50° to 90° F, was mild compared to the extremes found throughout Palestine. (124:1.9) The people of Nazareth, contaminated by low moral standards of Sepphoris, were resentful of Jesus' fame and actions elsewhere, leading to unfavorable opinions towards him. (150:7.2) Nazareth, a major caravan way station, was largely gentile and a crossroads of travel from three continents. (121:2.2) (123:5.6-7) In Nazareth, pupils studied Hebrew scriptures from a complete copy at the synagogue under the chazan's guidance until age twelve. (123:5.3) Jesus began his formal education at the synagogue school in Nazareth, with a view from the hill above guiding his young mind towards a deeper understanding of the world around him and his place within it. (123:5) At the spring in Nazareth, young Jesus charmed the town, learning skills and dreaming of a future as a potter with his friend Jacob. (123:5.15) From the hill of Simeon behind Nazareth, once a high place of Baal, Jesus beheld a landscape rich with historic and biblical significance, reflecting on the tales of triumph and tragedy that shaped his people. (126:1.2) “Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?” was a question asked due to the city's unique characteristics and reputation, but ultimately proved wrong by the virtues and qualities of Jesus. (123:5.7) (125:4.3) (137:2.2,6) (139:6.3) (187:2.5) Jesus
improvedDescription: Nazareth was a liberal center of Jewish thought and culture where Jesus received his education and moral training. Located at a major caravan crossroads with a largely gentile population, it was known for liberal interpretation of Jewish law, leading to the common saying "Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?" The town had a mild climate and served as Jesus's home base, though its people later became resentful of his fame and refused to accept his teachings.

name: Nazarites
originalDescription: Abner was the former head of the Nazarite school at Engedi (165:0.1) The Nazarites in Engedi, ascetic and eccentric, had mixed reactions to Jesus, with many becoming believers but the majority refusing to accept him. (142:8.1) The Nazarites, along with other groups, believed in the coming Messiah, but Jesus and his disciples distanced themselves from these beliefs. (137:7.12) John took the Nazarite vow at Engedi, the southern headquarters of the Nazarite brotherhood, before completing the offerings in Jerusalem. (135:1.1) (165:0.1) John the Baptist joined the Nazarites at the age of fourteen and lived a sanctified life with vows of abstinence. (135:1) John, like Samson and Samuel before him, was held in highest regard as a sanctified Nazarite. (135:1.2) Embarrassed by his lack of obedience to the Nazarite vow, John cleansed himself in the Nazarite corner of the women's court in the Jerusalem temple after his father's death. (135:2.1) Simon Peter took charge of a noisy meeting, reminding them of the Nazarite parable of the rich man and the beggar preached by John the Baptist. (169:3.1) John, a Nazarite, was allowed to enter the holy of holies in the temple, following in the footsteps of Samson and the prophet Samuel. (135:1.2) John followed in the footsteps of renowned Nazarites Samson and prophet Samuel, revered as sanctified and holy figures by the Jews. (135:1.2) At the age of fourteen, John took the formal Nazarite vow, abstaining from intoxicants, growing his hair, and avoiding contact with the dead, even in his own family. (135:1.1-2) (135:2.1)
improvedDescription: The Nazarites were a religious brotherhood with headquarters at Engedi, known for their ascetic lifestyle and strict vows of abstinence from intoxicants, growing their hair, and avoiding contact with the dead. John the Baptist was a lifelong Nazarite who took formal vows at age fourteen, following in the tradition of Samson and Samuel. While some Nazarites became believers in Jesus, the majority of this eccentric group refused to accept him as a teacher from heaven.

name: Neanderthalers
originalDescription: Neanderthal descendants of Andon in Europe quickly absorbed invading blue race. (64:7.9) The Adamites ruthlessly exterminated Neanderthal strains to unite with higher types of the blue race, producing superior Cro-Magnons. (80:1.7) The blue race was greatly retarded by absorption of Neanderthal, but they were otherwise the most aggressive, adventurous, and exploratory of all evolutionary peoples in Eurasia. (78:1.8) (79:5.1) The yellow race destroyed or drove out Neanderthalers from Asia, allowing the red man to reign supreme for almost one hundred thousand years. (79:5.2) For 500,000 years, Neanderthalers dominated the world as excellent fighters, traveling extensively to spread from India to France, China, and northern Africa. (64:4.1) Neanderthalers feared darkness so greatly that they resorted to human sacrifices to bring back the moon's light. (64:4.13) Neanderthal peoples greatly improved work in flint by utilizing reindeer horns and bones for tools. (64:4.3) The early Neanderthalers in eastern Asia were contaminated with debased animal strains, eventually pushed out by the arrival of later migrating yellow tribes. (79:5.2) The Neanderthal races retrogressed and had no religion, only superstition, as they drifted on for almost a quarter of a million years during the spiritually dark ages. (64:4.11-13)
improvedDescription: The Neanderthalers were descendants of Andon who dominated the world for almost half a million years, spreading from northwest India to France, China, and northern Africa. They were excellent fighters and travelers but had little culture beyond improved flint work and lived in fear of darkness, practicing human sacrifice to bring back the moon's light. These peoples gradually retrogressed spiritually and were eventually absorbed or displaced by the evolutionary races of color.

name: Nebadon
originalDescription: Urantia is one of many inhabited planets in Nebadon, a local universe within the superuniverse of Orvonton, which is part of the greater creation of divine perfection. (0:0.5) (15:1.5) (15:14.6) Salvington, the capital of Nebadon, is surrounded by ten university clusters of forty-nine spheres each, where man is spiritualized. (15:7.7) celestial personalities in Nebadon courts of Nebadon language of Nebadon physical aspects suffered 3 rebellions by System Sovereigns
improvedDescription: Nebadon is the local universe in which Urantia is located, consisting of one hundred constellations with headquarters at Salvington. Created by Michael of Nebadon, it belongs to the superuniverse of Orvonton and contains over three million inhabited worlds. The universe has experienced three rebellions by System Sovereigns and is still considered a young creation not yet settled in light and life.

name: Nebadon Corps of Completion
improvedDescription: A celestial order mentioned in connection with Jesus's soul-identity and the future destiny of finaliters, though specific details about their function and composition are not provided.

name: Nebadon Corps of Perfection
originalDescription: Spirit-fused ascenders who attain the Nebadon Corps of Perfection may become Universe Aids and interpret the viewpoints of evolving creatures to celestial authorities. (37:5.4) Members of the Nebadon Corps of Perfection acquire authoritative wisdom about Nebadon from witnessing the ascending processional of planetary ages on ten million inhabited worlds. (40:10.6)
improvedDescription: Spirit-fused ascenders who attain the Nebadon Corps of Perfection may become Universe Aids and interpret the viewpoints of evolving creatures to celestial authorities. Members acquire authoritative wisdom about Nebadon from witnessing the ascending processional of planetary ages on ten million inhabited worlds.

name: Nebadon Corps of the Finality
originalDescription: The soul-identity of Jesus, built up through divine guidance and perfect alignment with the Father's will, may have been placed in the custody of the Paradise Father for eventual leadership of the Nebadon Corps of the Finality. (188:3.8)
improvedDescription: A celestial corps that Jesus's soul-identity, built up through divine guidance and perfect alignment with the Father's will, may have been destined to lead in connection with their undisclosed destiny in outer space universes.

name: Nebadon Power Centers
improvedDescription: A local universe order of power centers operating within Nebadon, likely involved in energy control and distribution functions similar to other power centers in the grand universe.

name: Nebadon Revelatory Corps
originalDescription: The Nebadon Revelatory Corps, directed by Mantutia Melchizedek, created Part II of the Urantia Papers in 1934. (56:10.22-23)
improvedDescription: The Nebadon Revelatory Corps, directed by Mantutia Melchizedek, created Part II of the Urantia Papers in 1934. This commission of twelve Nebadon personalities sponsored various presentations and authored narratives for the revelation.

name: Nebuchadnezzar
originalDescription: Nebuchadnezzar overthrew Necho, destroyed Jerusalem, and carried the population into captivity, marking the end of Judah and the Yahweh-Baal struggle. (97:9.25-26) Jeremiah counseled surrendering Jerusalem to Nebuchadnezzar, the king of Babylon, during the siege. (97:6.4)
improvedDescription: The Babylonian king who overthrew Necho, destroyed Jerusalem, and carried the Jewish population into captivity, marking the end of Judah and the Yahweh-Baal struggle. Jeremiah counseled surrendering Jerusalem to Nebuchadnezzar during the siege.

name: nebulae
originalDescription: The critical centrifugal breakup stage marked the beginning of the great breakup in Andronover ages ago. (57:3.5) Some nebulae can produce up to one hundred million suns, varying greatly in size and progeny. (15:4.5) Observation of star clouds, spiral forms, and concentric contraction rings in the grand universe of Orvonton reveals the vast and intricate structure of the superuniverse. (15:3) (15:4.4-9) (15:5.3-5) Paradise force organizers are responsible for initiating the spiral and other nebulae, the mother wheels of the direct-origin suns and their varied systems in outer space. (12:4.6) (15:4.4) (29:4.3) (41:1.1) (57:1) The life cycle of nebulae begins as circular and gaseous entities, evolving into spiral forms before giving birth to suns and planetary systems. (57:2-4) The origin of Urantia is in the force-charge of space in the superuniverse of Orvonton. (57:1.1) The power centers and physical controllers direct the evolution of spiral and other nebulae in the spheres of space, initiated by Paradise force organizers. (41:1.1) Superuniverse boundaries never divide a nebular family or cross a local universe in the organized post-Havona creation. (12:1.12)
improvedDescription: Cosmic formations that serve as the mother wheels of suns and planetary systems, initiated by Paradise force organizers and existing in various forms including spiral, concentric, and other configurations. They vary greatly in size and can produce up to one hundred million suns, playing a crucial role in the material evolution of the universes.

name: necessities (economic)
originalDescription: Early civilization was forged upon the anvils of necessity and fear, as revealed religion slowly functioned to substitute Creator fear for creature fear. (66:5.13) Primitive society evolved through cycles of isolation, fear, and reluctant cooperation, founded on the reciprocity of necessity and enhanced safety. (68:1.3) Economic necessities tie man up with reality, while personal religious experience brings him face to face with eternal cosmic citizenship. (99:7.5)
improvedDescription: The fundamental material requirements that tie humanity to reality and drive social development. Early civilization was forged upon the anvils of necessity and fear, while primitive society evolved through cycles founded on the reciprocity of necessity for survival and cooperation.

name: Necho
originalDescription: Necho wiped out King Josiah, leading Judah into Egyptian bondage under the Baal political party's control of courts and priesthood, promoting Baal worship as an economic and social system. (97:9.24) Judah fell under Babylon's rule after Necho was overthrown by Nebuchadnezzar, leading to a decade of grace followed by rebellion. (97:9.25)
improvedDescription: The Egyptian pharaoh who defeated and killed King Josiah, leading Judah into Egyptian bondage under the Baal political party's control. Necho was later overthrown by Nebuchadnezzar, causing Judah to fall under Babylonian rule.

name: necklaces
improvedDescription: Personal ornaments worn around the neck, part of humanity's early development of decorative accessories and social status symbols.

name: necromancy
originalDescription: Necromancers were among the practitioners of the black art classified as witchcraft or shamancraft over time. (90:2.2)
improvedDescription: Necromancy is a form of black art practiced by those who claimed supernatural contact with the dead, later classified as witchcraft or shamancraft alongside other occult practices.

name: needle
originalDescription: It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for the rich to enter the kingdom of God. (163:3.1)
improvedDescription: A needle is referenced in Jesus's teaching that it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for the self-satisfied rich to enter the kingdom of heaven.

name: needs
originalDescription: Father's knowledge of our needs surpasses our own understanding. (3:3.2) (140:6.11) Society's overgrowth of supposed needs has burdened modern civilization with an overload of luxury and excessive desires. (68:2.5)
improvedDescription: Human needs encompass both genuine requirements that the Father knows before we ask, and the excessive desires that burden modern civilization with luxury and materialism beyond basic necessities.

name: negativism
originalDescription: The major error of modern religions is negativism, as moral worth stems from growth and positive action, not mere repression. (140:4.7) Children are often trained into negativity, but their natural psychology is positive and forward-thinking. (103:2.5-6)
improvedDescription: Negativism is a major error of modern religions that emphasizes repression rather than positive growth, while children naturally possess positive moral impulses that move toward righteousness and social ministry.

name: Negroids
originalDescription: The Negroid race, characterized by a mix of orange, green, and indigo, is most prevalent in Africa, India, and Indonesia. (81:4.13)
improvedDescription: The Negroid race is a secondary Sangik type originally composed of the orange, green, and indigo races, found primarily in Africa, India, and Indonesia.

name: neighbor
originalDescription: Love your neighbor as yourself, for in doing so, you reflect the love of the Lord. (131:2.11,13) (159:5.4,9) (170:3.6) (180:1.1) Jesus taught that our neighbor is not just those close to us, but includes all of humanity, even those we consider our enemies. (103:5.2) (140:8.11) (164:1.2)
improvedDescription: A neighbor, in Jesus's expanded teaching, includes all of humanity rather than just those close to us, with the commandment to love one's neighbor as oneself representing the highest ethics.

name: neighboring planet
originalDescription: A very superior civilization is evolving on an isolated continental nation, self-sustaining and able to live indefinitely without importing from neighboring planets. (72:1-11) No Paradise Sons missions on neighboring planet, yet Urantians should not be divided by cultural differences. (72:12.4) With permission from Lanaforge and approval from the Most Highs of Edentia, I can describe the social, moral, and political life of an advanced human race on a neighboring planet in the Satania system. (72:0.1) Other nations should take note of the advanced culture and government of this neighboring planet, and consider the potential benefits of promoting mutual exchange and education for the advancement of civilization. (72:12) Neighboring planet's Planetary Prince and Material Son defaulted, leading to isolation and lack of a Magisterial Son, following in the footsteps of Urantia's fate. (72:0.3) The pouring out of the Spirit of Truth on Urantia has significantly advanced its readiness for planetary government and world-wide peace under law. (72:12.5)
improvedDescription: A neighboring planet in the Satania system displays an advanced civilization on one continental nation, though it suffered defaults by both its Planetary Prince and Material Son like Urantia, and has never received a Magisterial Son mission.

name: Nepal
originalDescription: Gautama born in the sixth century before Christ in Nepal. (94:7.1)
improvedDescription: Nepal is the north Indian province where Gautama Siddhartha was born in the sixth century before Christ, in a small mountain valley in the southern Himalayas.

name: Nephilim
originalDescription: The Nephilim (Nodites) in Old Testament reference to Nodites were the “mighty men of old” and “men of renown” born from sons of the gods and daughters of men during the postrebellion era on Urantia. (77:2.3)
improvedDescription: The Nephilim were the Nodites of the postrebellion era, regarded as the "mighty men of old" and "men of renown" born from the union of Caligastia's staff with mortal women.

name: Neptune, god
originalDescription: Neptune, Roman god of the sea and earthquakes, was worshiped in the temple of Alexandria alongside other chief attractions. (130:3.4)
improvedDescription: Neptune was the Roman god of the sea who had a temple in Alexandria, one of the city's chief attractions alongside the library and other cultural landmarks.

name: Neptune
originalDescription: The temple of Neptune stood amidst the city's chief attractions in Alexandria, a city of one million people, where Jesus and Ganid explored the vast library. (130:3.4)
improvedDescription: The temple of Neptune was one of the chief attractions in Alexandria, a cosmopolitan city of one million people where Jesus and Ganid explored during their Mediterranean journey.

name: Nereids
originalDescription: Evolving monotheists keep subordinate gods as spirits, demons, fates, Nereids, fairies, brownies, dwarfs, banshees, and the evil eye. (96:1.14)
improvedDescription: Sea nymphs from Greek mythology that evolving monotheists kept as subordinate spiritual beings along with other lesser deities like spirits, demons, fates, and fairies.

name: Nerites
originalDescription: The final Andite eruption from the Caspian region, the Nerites, marked the end of the Mesopotamian descendants' dominance during the nomads' rampage from 2500 to 2000 B.C. (78:8.11)
improvedDescription: The final Andite group to emerge from the Caspian region, representing the last eruption of Mesopotamian descendants of blended Andonite and Andite races during the nomadic rampage from 2500 to 2000 B.C.

name: net cast into sea
originalDescription: The “parable of net cast into sea” illustrates the idea of gathering and sorting good from bad in the kingdom of heaven. (151:4.6)
improvedDescription: A parable told by Jesus comparing the kingdom of heaven to a fishing net that gathers all kinds of fish, which are later sorted with the good kept and the bad discarded.

name: nether Paradise
improvedDescription: A region or aspect of Paradise, the central universe location and dwelling place of the Universal Father.

name: neutrons
originalDescription: Atomic stability depends on the number of neutrons in the nucleus, while chemical behavior relies on the activity of revolving electrons. (42:7.6) Neutrons and protons in the atom's nucleus are held together by the reciprocating mesotron, preventing disruption from the protons' electric charge. (42:8.3)
improvedDescription: Electrically inactive particles in the atomic nucleus that determine atomic stability, held together with protons by the reciprocating function of mesotrons.

name: new commandment
improvedDescription: A divine directive or teaching given by Jesus, representing an elevated spiritual principle for human conduct.

name: New Guinea
originalDescription: The New Guinea group believes in their descent from the parrot, embracing the ancient understanding of human evolution. (74:8.5)
improvedDescription: An island region where primitive tribes believed they descended from parrots, illustrating ancient peoples' totemic understanding of human evolution.

name: New Jerusalem
originalDescription: Many of Jesus’ followers believed Messiah would appear to establish the New Jerusalem, connecting it with the end of the world and the new heavens and earth. (176:1.6) John the Revelator witnessed the new Jerusalem descending from God, as the culmination of the Teacher Sons' mission. (52:7.11)
improvedDescription: The prophetic holy city that many believed the Messiah would establish as the world's capital, and which John the Revelator saw descending from God as the culmination of the Teacher Sons' mission.

name: New Red Sandstone
originalDescription: New Red Sandstone can be found over Europe, including Germany and Russia, and in England belonging to this epoch, with deposits all over Africa and Australia, and Carrara marble derived from modified limestone in the southern Alps. (60:1.4)
improvedDescription: Geological deposits from a specific epoch found across Europe, Africa, and Australia, with notable formations in Germany, Russia, England, and the southern Alps where modified limestone became Carrara marble.

name: New Stone Age
originalDescription: In Europe, the New Stone Age of 3000 B.C. overlapped the Bronze Age, with sun worship and agriculture playing significant roles in the building of sun temples and the persistence of ancient superstitions in Brittany. (80:9.13-14)
improvedDescription: A prehistoric period in Europe around 3000 B.C. that overlapped with the Bronze Age, characterized by sun worship, agriculture, and the construction of circular, roofless sun temples, with superstitions from this era persisting in regions like Brittany.

name: New Testament
originalDescription: The New Testament writers' honest accounts of Jesus, shaped by Paul's theology, have changed history for 2000 years. (121:8.11) The gospels' portrayal of Jesus was shaped by Paul's theology, influencing the history of Urantia for centuries. (121:8.11) Due to Jesus' deliberate avoidance of leaving written records, the writing of the Gospels was significantly delayed as seen in the New Testament. (121:0.1) A reformation in the Christian church may one day return to the unadulterated teachings of Jesus, as the New Testament is mostly devoted to Paul's beliefs rather than the religion of Jesus. (196:2.1) The symbolism of the New Testament was intended for backward ages, but subsequent generations should understand the great struggle Jesus faced on Mount Hermon. (134:8.6) The New Testament, written in Greek, originated from the eyewitness accounts of Mark, Matthew, Luke, and John. (121:8.2-11) (195:2.7) Abner, head of Philadelphia church, remains unmentioned in New Testament due to ongoing feud with Jerusalem church. (166:5.3) authors gospels, books, and epistles named in The Urantia Book
improvedDescription: The collection of Christian scriptures written in Greek, primarily based on the gospels of Mark, Matthew, Luke, and John, which present honest but imperfect accounts of Jesus' life as influenced by Paul's theology, changing the course of Urantian history for nearly two thousand years.

name: new things
originalDescription: Ancient civilizations viewed new practices such as writing, printing, and pictures with disfavor due to their perceived evil magical nature. (88:5.4) The Master's love creates a new world in the old one, through sharing his joy and service. (180:1.5) We should not embrace new things solely because they are novel, nor should we despise things simply because they are old. (81:6.39) (147:7.3)
improvedDescription: Novel practices or ideas that should be evaluated on their merit rather than embraced simply because they are new or rejected merely because they are old, as ancient civilizations often viewed new practices like writing and printing with suspicion due to their supposed magical nature.

name: New Zealand
originalDescription: Men in New Zealand tribe adjusted Christianity to allow for loose sex under the guise of a divine revelation from Gabriel. (92:2.5)
improvedDescription: A location where a tribe's priests nominally accepted Christianity but claimed to receive revelations from Gabriel allowing them to maintain their traditional loose sexual practices and other customs, illustrating how races superficially adopt new religions while adapting them to their existing beliefs.

name: Newfoundland
originalDescription: Newfoundland is part of an ancient and primitive fossil-bearing stone layer that extends from Pennsylvania to Mexico, passing through several regions of Canada. (58:7.4)
improvedDescription: Part of an ancient geological formation of primitive fossil-bearing stone that extends across eastern North America, from Pennsylvania through various Canadian regions to Mexico.

name: newlyweds
originalDescription: The origin of pranks played upon newlyweds dates back to ancient beliefs in avoiding the envy of spirits by appearing miserable and ill at ease. (83:4.7)
improvedDescription: Newly married couples who are traditionally subjected to teasing and pranks, customs that originated from ancient beliefs about appearing miserable to avoid arousing the envy of jealous spirits.

name: Niagara Falls
originalDescription: The Great Lakes system emptied through Niagara Falls 37,000 years ago, following a path from the Mississippi valley to the Hudson valley and finally to the St. Lawrence. (61:7.10)
improvedDescription: A waterfall flowing over Niagara limestone that became the outlet for the Great Lakes system 37,000 years ago, after the lakes had previously emptied through different routes including the Mississippi and Hudson valleys.

name: Nicaea
originalDescription: Athanasius' defense at the Council of Nicaea preserved the true nature of Jesus from being obscured by compromises. (195:0.18)
improvedDescription: The location of a crucial church council where Athanasius, a Greek from Egypt, courageously defended the true nature of Jesus against the teachings of Arius, preserving essential Christian doctrine from dangerous compromise.

name: Nicodemus
originalDescription: Jesus of Nazareth's strong and forceful personality appealed not only to spiritually minded women, but also to educated and intellectual Nicodemus, a wealthy elderly member of the Sanhedrin, and a hardy Roman soldier who declared, “Truly, this was a Son of God.” (141:3.5) (142:6.1) Jesus’ visit with Nicodemus revealed the necessity of being born of the spirit to enter the kingdom of God, challenging Nicodemus to submit his will to the divine Father like a child. (142:6) Nicodemus, a member of the Jewish Sanhedrin, feared to be seen openly with Jesus, so he arranged to see him privately at night in Flavius's home. (142:6.1) Nicodemus came to see Jesus in Gethsemane but turned back in fear with Joseph of Arimathea. (142:8.4) Nicodemus' home became a secret meeting place for Jesus' followers, including prominent Jewish leaders, who made a pact to openly acknowledge their allegiance to the Master, even in the face of his arrest. (164:2.1) (174:5.5) (175:4.3) (183:4.4) (186:3.3) (189:4.2) after the crucifixion
improvedDescription: A wealthy, elderly member of the Jewish Sanhedrin who secretly met with Jesus at night to avoid public detection, later became a bold follower who helped claim Jesus' body for burial, and whose home served as a meeting place for Jerusalem believers.

name: Nicopolis
originalDescription: Nicopolis, founded by Augustus to commemorate Actium, was where the Apostle Paul spent the winter during his third missionary journey, lodging in the home of Jeramy, a Greek proselyte of the Jewish faith. (133:2.5)
improvedDescription: A Greek city founded by Augustus around 31 B.C. as the "city of victory" to commemorate the battle of Actium, where Jesus and his companions lodged with a Greek proselyte named Jeramy, and where the Apostle Paul later spent a winter during his third missionary journey.

name: night police
originalDescription: Secret societies functioned as night police and contributed to the building up of social castes through their mysterious initiations and rituals. (70:7.15)
improvedDescription: Secret societies functioned as night police and contributed to the building up of social castes through their mysterious initiations and rituals.

name: Nile god
originalDescription: The legendary Nile god inspired the refined rituals of the worship of Isis and Osiris, surpassing those of the Phrygian cult in sophistication and significance. (98:4.8) The Nile god bestows a protecting spirit called the ka, depicted in the birth of Amenhotep III at a temple in Luxor. (111:0.5)
improvedDescription: The legendary Nile god of ancient Egypt was a deity who died and was resurrected, inspiring the refined rituals of Isis and Osiris worship. The Nile god was believed to bestow a protecting spirit called the ka upon favored individuals at birth.

name: Nile valley
originalDescription: Abraham and Lot journeyed to the Nile valley to obtain food supplies during a drought in Palestine, where Abraham found a distant relative on the Egyptian throne and led successful military expeditions before living at court with his wife, Sarah. (93:5.7) Adamites and Andites journeyed to the Nile valley, contributing to the civilization that thrived and advanced in the Mediterranean islands and beyond. (78:3.2) (78:5.5) (78:6.8) (79:1.3) Andites enjoyed sheltered position of the Nile valley, advancing culture and craftsmanship in Egypt. (80:6) Apostle Simon worked his way up the Nile valley, preaching the gospel of Jesus and baptizing believers until he died and was buried in the heart of Africa. (139:11.11) Internal warfare in the Nile valley abruptly ended a brilliant epoch of culture, leading to the conquest of the country by inferior tribes and a decline in social progress lasting over five hundred years. (80:6.5) Saharan immigration into the Nile valley by the Nodite-Andonite Syrians enriched the region's culture for thousands of years. (80:1.2-3) (80:2.2) Semites enslaved in the Nile valley endured bitter toil as laborers. (96:2.2) The green men prevailed in the struggles of green and orange races in the lower Nile valley, leading to the extinction of the orange race almost one hundred thousand years ago. (64:6.13) The western end of the cradle of civilization was in the Nile valley, where climate played a decisive factor in its establishment. (81:1.1)
improvedDescription: The valley of the Nile in Egypt served as a major center of ancient civilization, receiving migrations of Adamites and Andites who contributed to its cultural advancement. It was the destination for various peoples including Abraham and Lot during drought, and later became the successor to Mesopotamia as headquarters of the most advanced earthly civilization.

name: Nineveh
originalDescription: The profound truth of the narrative is the love of God for Nineveh and the so-called heathen. (159:4.4)
improvedDescription: An ancient city referenced in the story of Jonah, representing God's love for all peoples including the so-called heathen.

name: Nirvana
originalDescription: Amidists believe in the teaching of ultimate existence by Amida Buddha, seeking salvation through faith in his divine mercies. (94:12.3) Religion is the enlistment of the totality of selfhood in loyal service to loving God and serving man, not a technique for attaining static and blissful peace of mind. (100:3.1) Nirvana is a state of supreme enlightenment and bliss, representing release from the material world. (94:8.16)
improvedDescription: In Buddhist philosophy, a state of supreme enlightenment and supernal bliss representing freedom from material desires and deliverance from reincarnation. Amidist believers teach that souls may enjoy Paradise before entering Nirvana, the ultimate existence.

name: nitrogen
originalDescription: The ancient planetary atmosphere lacked free nitrogen and oxygen, evolving to contain water vapor, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride alongside volcanic gases and meteoric combustion products. (57:7.6)
improvedDescription: A chemical element that was absent as free nitrogen in the ancient planetary atmosphere, which instead contained water vapor, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride.

name: Noah (Garden dweller)
originalDescription: Noah, son of the architect and builder of the Garden of Eden, was part of the reception committee that welcomed Adam and Eve as rulers of the world. (74:2.5)
improvedDescription: Son of the architect and builder of the Garden of Eden who served on the reception committee that welcomed Adam and Eve as rulers of the world.

name: Noah (wine maker of Aram)
originalDescription: Noah, the wine maker of Aram, advocated for houses to be built like boats to survive the impending flood. (78:7.5) Noah, the wine maker of Aram, was reminiscent of the Mithraic flood legend. (98:5.3) The story of Noah, the ark, and the universal flood was woven into Jewish tradition, influenced by the Babylonian story of the flood, with Mount Ararat being the sacred mountain of northern Mesopotamia. (77:4.12) (78:7.3-5)
improvedDescription: A wine maker from Aram who kept records of annual river floods and advocated building houses like boats to survive flooding. He became the basis for later flood legends, including the Biblical story of Noah and the ark.

name: Nod (land of)
originalDescription: Cain departed for the land of Nod and became a great leader, promoting peace between Nodites and Adamites throughout his lifetime, marrying his cousin Remona, and seeing his son Enoch lead the Elamite Nodites. (76:2.9) Cain found wife in Nod, pre-Adamites recognized in early teachings by Moses. (74:8.8) (148:4.7) The Elamite Nodite settlement was the origin of the Nodites, descendants of the rebellious planetary staff led by Nod, who were doomed to mortal existence. (67:4.2) (77:4.5)
improvedDescription: The dwelling place of the Nodites, descendants of the rebellious planetary staff, located east of the second Eden. Cain departed to this land after leaving Eden, where he married his cousin Remona and became a leader promoting peace between Nodites and Adamites.

name: Nod (Prince’s corporeal staff)
originalDescription: The entire council lost in rebellion, with only forty out of the one hundred saved in the end. (67:4.1) Nod, former chairman of the council on industry and trade, led the commission in fostering industry and promoting trade among the peace groups, contributing to the elevation of living standards and expanding trade in improved salt. (66:5.11) (73:1.3) The Nodites rebelled against Prince and followed their chosen leader, Nod, discovering their mortal status and eventually becoming known as the Nodites in the land of Nod. (67:4.2)
improvedDescription: Former chairman of the commission on industry and trade who led the sixty members of the planetary staff into rebellion against Prince Caligastia. After the fall of Dalamatia, Nod and his followers became known as the Nodites and settled in the land of Nod.

name: Nodites
originalDescription: The Nodites, descendants of rebel members of the Prince's staff, were the eighth race to appear on Urantia. (73:1.3) (77:2.8) The Nodites, descendants of 60 rebel members of Prince's staff and 44 Andonic counterparts, displayed superior physical, intellectual, and spiritual qualities, representing the eighth race on Urantia. (77:2.4,8) Adam impregnated 1,682 Nodites in the second garden to establish the Andite race for the world's benefit. (76:4.8) The Nodites built the tower of Babel as a monumental memorial to their racial greatness, after founding the city of Dilmun to preserve their unity and culture. (77:3.1-2) The centers of civilization established by the Nodites following the Bablot conflict greatly influenced the development of the Assyrian stock, the Elamites, and the Sumerian peoples. (77:4) The white race has inherited a significant portion of the original Andonite and Nodite strains, mixed with the Sangik races. (80:0.2) Descendants of rebels on Prince's staff, the Nodites were once superhuman but degraded to mortal beings, persisting in the traditions of Dalamatia and the culture of the Planetary Prince despite the post-Adamic era's lack of progress on Urantia. (67:4.2) (73:1) Descendants of the Vanite division of the Nodites settled Crete around 12,000 B.C., known for their advanced skills in various trades and agriculture. (80:7.2) The dispersion and decline of the Nodites following the tower of Babel conflict hindered their ability to establish a great pre-Adamic civilization, but their mixed descendants continued to contribute to the development of society. (77:4.1) The Elamite Nodites advocated for culture and commerce, while the Syrian Nodites were nationalistic memorialists, contributing to the development of different civilizations. (77:4.3-4) The progeny of the rebels of the Prince’s staff, followers of Nod, were far superior to Andonites and Sangiks in physical, intellectual, and spiritual qualities. (77:2.4) In retaliation for Eve's default, the Garden dwellers destroyed the nearby Nodite settlement, sparking bitter warfare between the two groups. (75:5.3,9) The Nodites, descendants of Caligastia's staff, gave valuable cooperation to the Edenites, ultimately leading to the undoing of the Edenic regime. (75:2.5) (75:3.5) (77:4.1) Remnants of the ancient Nodite culture in Mesopotamia never entirely lost their traditions, despite becoming admixed with the Adamites. (78:1.4) Some Nodites later joined Van and his loyal followers, mingled with the Amadonites, and became the “mighty men of old.” (73:1.6) The Nodites were long-headed, while the Andonites were broad-headed. (81:4.2-3) After Adam vacated the first garden, it was occupied by the Nodites who opposed cooperation with the Adamites for almost four thousand years until the sinking of the Edenic peninsula. (73:7.1) The blending of pre-Sumerian Nodites with Adamites gave rise to the Sumerians, whose ancient kings had remarkably long reigns, hinting at a connection to the Nodite rulers of Dalamatia. (77:2.10) (77:4.6) Priests in the 1st and 2nd gardens were given preference for animal sacrifices over offerings from the fields, causing strife between Cain and Abel. (76:2.2-3) The Nodites, referred to as Nephilim in the Old Testament, were considered mighty men of old and men of renown due to their union with mortal women. (77:2.3) The Nodites regarded man as soul and body, inspiring diverse beliefs in human composition among ancient cultures. (86:5.17) After Dalamatia was destroyed, the Nodites split into three major groups: one stayed near the Persian Gulf, another migrated to Elam, and the third settled in northeastern Syria. (73:1.5) The Nodites, still an able people in the times of Adam, were once dispersed due to internal conflict over the tower of Babel, ultimately hindering their ability to establish a great pre-Adamic civilization. (77:4.1) The Nodites took the tree of life after default, but it did them no good. (73:6.7) (75:3.5) Nodites named in The Urantia Book practices
improvedDescription: The Nodites were descendants of the rebel members of Prince Caligastia's staff who participated in the planetary rebellion. Named after their leader Nod, they constituted the eighth race to appear on Urantia. Despite being degraded from superhuman to mortal status after the rebellion, the Nodites displayed superior physical, intellectual, and spiritual qualities compared to other contemporary races. They established the cities of Dalamatia and later Dilmun, built the tower of Babel, and eventually dispersed into various groups that influenced the development of many ancient civilizations including the Sumerians, Assyrians, and Elamites.

name: Nog
originalDescription: When the first capital of the world was engulfed, it harbored renegades who had converted the Father's temple into a shrine dedicated to Nog, the false god of light and fire. (67:5.5)
improvedDescription: Nog was a false god of light and fire worshipped by the lowest types of Sangik races who had converted the Father's temple in Dalamatia into a shrine dedicated to this deity before the city was engulfed.

name: noise
originalDescription: Bells, shouting, and drums still drive ghosts away, as dogs and roosters warn of their approach. (87:6.11)
improvedDescription: Noise was believed by ancient peoples to disturb and frighten away ghosts and spirits. Methods such as shouting, ringing bells, and beating drums were used to drive spirits away from the living, practices that continue in modern funeral wakes.

name: nomadism
originalDescription: The progressive decline of nomadism has mirrored the evolution of human society from hunting to agriculture. (68:5.12)
improvedDescription: Nomadism represents the wandering lifestyle that has progressively declined as human society evolved from hunting through herding to agricultural stages, with each advancement resulting in more settled, home-based living.

name: nonbreathers
improvedDescription: Nonbreathers are a type of mortal beings who do not require atmospheric respiration, representing one of the planetary variations in mortal life forms found throughout the universes.

name: nonconformists
originalDescription: Civilization imposes heavy penalties on dissenters and nonconformists as it strives to create a better world for future generations. (81:5.3)
improvedDescription: Nonconformists are individuals who dissent from established social norms and practices. Civilization imposes heavy penalties on such dissenters as it strives to maintain order and create a better world for future generations.

name: nonexistence
