improvedDescription: Nonexistence refers to the state of complete cessation of being that awaits those personalities who definitively reject eternal survival and choose against participation in the divine plan of spiritual progression.

name: nonreligionists
improvedDescription: Nonreligionists are individuals who do not embrace or practice religious beliefs, representing those who remain outside organized spiritual movements and faith traditions.

name: nonresistance
originalDescription: The accusation that enfeebled specimens would soon perish was typical of the difficulties faced by Philip when confronted by skeptics of their message. (143:1.2) Lao-tse's teaching of nonresistance influenced the pacifism of the Chinese people. (94:6.4,7) True religion opposes violence and promotes nonviolence as the proper attitude, advocating for peaceful evolution over violent revolution in society's adaptation to new economic and cultural conditions, while staying away from direct involvement in political and economic matters. (99:0.2) (99:2.5) (143:1.7) Understanding the Master's teaching of nonresistance requires perceiving the living flexibility and cosmic adaptability of divine truth to each individual son of God, and embracing the aggressive pursuit of righteous spirit values while rejecting selfish reactions to the universe. (180:5.9) Jesus taught to conquer through sacrifice of pride and selfishness, showing mercy for spiritual deliverance from anger and revenge, and to resist not injury to personal dignity. (141:3.8) The peaceful violet race's retention of Edenic traditions led to their delay in territorial conquests and the unique approach of sending out teachers instead of making war, ultimately influencing surrounding peoples biologically and culturally. (78:3.1) (78:4.5) Jesus
improvedDescription: Nonresistance is the spiritual philosophy taught by Jesus advocating the conquest of evil through positive spiritual means rather than physical retaliation. This teaching emphasizes active love, mercy, and the aggressive pursuit of righteous spiritual values while refusing selfish reactions to personal injury or injustice. Jesus demonstrated that true nonresistance involves dynamic spiritual response rather than passive submission to evil.

name: nonsurvivors
originalDescription: If mortal man fails to survive natural death, the spirit content of any value is imperishable, and the surviving Adjuster conserves those realities born of love and nurtured in service. (16:9.3) (47:3.4) (196:3.35) Those who reject the eternal journey are moving counter to the stream of universe events. (117:4.11) When nonsurvivors reject the ascendant life and choose against the Havona adventure, death results in their probationary careers ending, leaving them as if they had never been. (47:2.7) The Supreme's divinity expression is delayed by the choices of nonsurvivors in the grand universe. (117:4.2,11-13) The eternal survival of souls is never hindered by neglect from the Adjuster; they faithfully fulfill their duties even after the mortals fail to achieve it. (40:4.1) The final divergence of personality from the trend of the universe leads to intellectual isolation and spiritual cessation, highlighting the misadaptation of self-conscious life and the existence of a purposeful universe guided by a divine Creator. (130:4.8) Guardian angels respond to the dispensational roll call for sleeping survivors, while Adjusters make no answer for nonsurvivors who have ceased to exist. (49:6.7) (113:6.8) As nonsurvivors, can you allow yourself to pass into the realm of the unrealized when before you lies the enchanting vista of the universe career? (117:4.13) The personalities of nonsurvivors are absorbed into the Supreme, never to appear again as an individual creature. (2:3.4) (16:9.3) (117:4.2,4) The cosmic self-destruction is inevitable if evolving personalities are to have true freedom in exercising finite will. (118:7.7) Full adjudication of universe status follows identification with evil before penalty is executed. (54:3.2) By embracing God, man moves towards eternal reality; by withdrawing, he approaches nonreality and eventual cessation of existence. (117:4.14)
improvedDescription: Nonsurvivors are mortal beings who choose to reject eternal life and spiritual progression after death. Though their personalities cease to exist as individuals, any spiritual values from their earthly experience are preserved by their Thought Adjusters and absorbed into the Supreme Being. Their choice against survival delays the Supreme's full expression in the grand universe, as each personality decision affects the cosmic evolution of deity.

name: nonviolence
improvedDescription: The concept of avoiding or rejecting violence as a means of resolving conflicts or achieving goals.

name: Norana
originalDescription: Norana's great faith in Jesus led to her daughter being healed of her affliction, demonstrating the power of belief in the teachings of the gospel. (156:1)
improvedDescription: A Syrian woman whose great faith in Jesus led to her twelve-year-old daughter being healed of a grievous nervous disorder characterized by convulsions and other distressing manifestations.

name: Nordan the Kenite
originalDescription: Nordan the Kenite received advanced teachings on the superuniverse and Havona from his mentor Melchizedek. (93:3.4)
improvedDescription: A brilliant disciple of Melchizedek who, along with his band of earnest students, received advanced teachings about the truths of the superuniverse and even of Havona.

name: Nordic race
improvedDescription: A division of the white race characterized by northern European ancestry and physical traits.

name: Norlatiadek
originalDescription: In Norlatiadek, the 10 Constellation Centers are stationed at the center of the enormous stellar system. (41:1.4) Norlatiadek, a constellation currently administered by twelve Vorondadek Sons as requested during times of stress. (35:6.5) Since Christ's triumph, all Norlatiadek is being cleansed of sin and rebels. (43:4.9) Norlatiadek is constellation #70 in local universe of Nebadon. (15:14.6) Edentia is headquarters of Norlatiadek, with seventy satellites for ascenders' socialization and training after Jerusem regime. (15:7.6) Norlatiadek's enormous stellar system is the physical core of its constellation, where ten Supreme Power Centers of the fifth order are assigned. (41:1.4) The constellation government of Norlatiadek has expanded to include twelve Sons of the Vorondadek order, with no change in rulers since the time of the Lucifer rebellion. (43:5.1-15) Urantia, once among the least of all creation, now traverses the outer fringe of Nebadon, elevated to a position of honor by Michael's bestowal. (41:10.5) In Norlatiadek, our constellation, ten Supreme Power Centers are positioned at the heart of the vast stellar system, outside of the headquarters sphere. (41:1.4) In Norlatiadek, the ten Supreme Power Centers of the fifth order are stationed at the physical core of the constellation, not on the headquarters sphere. (41:1.4) Every one hundred years, Satania elects ten members to the Norlatiadek legislature, chosen by the Jerusem council of one thousand and graduates of the Melchizedek College of Administration, who represent the system in the constellation legislature. (45:3.22) (45:7.4)
improvedDescription: Constellation number 70 in the local universe of Nebadon, with headquarters on Edentia and consisting of one hundred local systems including Satania. Currently administered by twelve Vorondadek Sons during the ongoing effects of the Lucifer rebellion, with ten Supreme Power Centers stationed at the center of its enormous stellar system.

name: north
originalDescription: North is the absolute direction established by the axis of Paradise, due to dimensional differences and greater force-energy out-pressure. (11:2.3)
improvedDescription: The absolute direction established by the axis of Paradise, due to dimensional differences and greater force-energy out-pressure at the north end of the Isle.

name: North America
originalDescription: North America stood as one of the two extensive and fertile open hunting areas during the opening of the Andite era. (81:6.4) The configuration of continents and oceans strongly influences peace or war, offering North America a unique opportunity for peaceful, continuous development. (81:6.5) The future of North America hinges on the quality of racial factors allowed to enter its population. (80:9.16) geology humans
improvedDescription: A continent that served as one of two extensive fertile hunting areas during the Andite era, overspread by the Amerinds. Its favorable geographic configuration, protected by vast oceans, provided unique opportunities for continuous peaceful development, with its future depending on the quality of racial factors entering its population.

name: North Pole
originalDescription: The vast ice sheets at the North Pole began forming 100,000 years ago, preventing another glacial age from occurring. (61:7.11)
improvedDescription: The northern polar region where vast ice sheets began forming 100,000 years ago during the retreat of the last glacier, with ice accumulation centers moving northward and preventing future glacial ages as long as polar regions remain ice-covered.

name: North Sea
originalDescription: Andon and Fonta's descendants established settlements along warm North Sea waters before the ice sheet reached France and the British Isles. (63:5.2)
improvedDescription: A body of water whose then-warm shores provided settlement locations for the descendants of Andon and Fonta, who established over one thousand separate settlements along great rivers leading to these waters before ice sheets reached France and the British Isles.

name: northern lights
originalDescription: The Urantia upper air belts, concerned with auroral phenomena, reflect light-energy similar to gases in the Jerusem upper ionosphere. (46:1.6)
improvedDescription: Auroral phenomena on Urantia produced by gases in the upper air belts that reflect light-energy, similar to gases in Jerusem's upper ionosphere, though the causes differ between the two worlds.

name: Norway
originalDescription: Norway's coastwise climate varied greatly between times of glacial inaction and icebergs thundering down Norwegian fiords into the North Sea. (61:5.8)
improvedDescription: A Scandinavian country whose coastwise climate varied greatly between times of glacial inaction and periods when enormous icebergs thundered down Norwegian fiords into the North Sea.

name: nostalgia
originalDescription: On Salvington, mortals experience nostalgia as they stand on the brink of their spirit adventure in the vast superuniverse of Orvonton, guided by Universe Orientators. (39:1.10)
improvedDescription: A human emotion experienced by ascending mortals on Salvington as they stand on the brink of their spirit adventure in the vast superuniverse of Orvonton, looking back at familiar realms of achievement while facing the challenging mystery ahead.

name: numbers
originalDescription: Friday has long been an unlucky day, with 13 considered evil and 3, 4, and 7 as lucky numbers. (88:1.6)
improvedDescription: Certain numbers held as fetishes by primitive peoples, with Friday being an unlucky day, 13 considered evil, and 3, 4, and 7 regarded as lucky numbers derived from early revelations and recognition of natural patterns.

name: numerology
originalDescription: Belief in magic numbers founded the science of mathematics, leading to a scientific age born from the evolutionary religious tree branch of magic. (88:6.5) The belief in magic numbers and omens of good or bad luck is a pure unfounded superstition. (150:3.8)
improvedDescription: The belief in magic numbers that founded the science of mathematics and led to scientific development, though the belief in magic numbers and omens of good or bad luck is pure unfounded superstition.

name: nutrition
improvedDescription: The science and practice of nourishing the body through proper food intake and dietary management.

name: oaks
originalDescription: Oaks were among the many present-day trees that first appeared during a period of great plant-life evolution, dominated by angiosperms, which were second in evolutionary importance only to the appearance of man himself. (60:3.19)
improvedDescription: Trees that first appeared during a period of great plant-life evolution dominated by angiosperms, which were second in evolutionary importance only to the appearance of man himself.

name: oaths
originalDescription: Adam and Eve remained loyal to Michael during the Lucifer rebellion, taking oaths of allegiance to the Most Highs and to Michael. (74:1.4) (74:2.6) (75:4.2) by Creator Sons not to assume full sovereignty until after 7 In completing the seven creature bestowals, a Creator Michael fulfills his sacred oath not to assume supreme sovereignty until certified by the superuniverse rulers, ultimately becoming a supreme universe sovereign. (21:3.12) (21:4.5) In fulfillment of their oath to David and each other, the messengers went forth as the first heralds of the resurrected Jesus. (190:1.5-6) Visitors were freely admitted to the Garden, but to sojourn one had to be adopted and make a declaration of loyalty to join the Garden community. (74:7.11) The finality oath of eternal import and Trinity oath of eternity mark the culmination of the Paradise supernaphim's journey into the Mortal Corps of the Finality. (27:7.9) (30:4.31) (31:0.12) (31:1.3) (31:3.4) (31:4.1) (31:8.4) (117:7.7) Swearing oaths on sacred books or objects of veneration is simply a form of fetishism. (88:2.8) Oaths in Dalamatia aimed to ensure truthful testimony through self-cursing and loyalty to one's group. (70:11.3) Ascendant graduates receive group pledges from the Perfections of Days. (18:4.7) primitive oaths
improvedDescription: Sacred pledges or vows of allegiance, ranging from primitive self-cursing oaths in Dalamatia to the Trinity oath of eternity taken by finaliters, including the oaths of loyalty taken by Adam and Eve to Michael and the Most Highs.

name: oats
originalDescription: After years of discipline and training, young men were granted a brief freedom before marriage, only to later abide by tribal taboos, likened to the foolish notion of sowing wild oats. (70:7.12)
improvedDescription: A grain crop referenced in the ancient custom of granting young men brief freedom before marriage, which continued as the modern notion of "sowing wild oats."

name: Obadiah
originalDescription: Obadiah fearlessly attacked and confirmed Isaiah's gospel by denouncing the sacrificial system. (97:5.4)
improvedDescription: A Hebrew prophet who followed Isaiah and, along with Micah, confirmed and embellished Isaiah's gospel while boldly denouncing the priest-ridden ritual and sacrificial system of the Hebrews.

name: Oban
originalDescription: Onagar's headquarters were in Oban on the northern shores of the ancient Mediterranean, where he sent out the world's first missionaries to spread his doctrines of one Deity and the Great Beyond. (63:6.7)
improvedDescription: Onagar's headquarters settlement on the northern shores of the ancient Mediterranean in the region of the present Caspian Sea, from which he sent out the world's first missionaries to spread his doctrines of one Deity and the Great Beyond.

name: obligations
improvedDescription: Duties or responsibilities that individuals are bound to fulfill.

name: observer
originalDescription: The observer transcends the thing observed in the evaluation of reality. (112:2.12)
improvedDescription: The observer transcends the thing observed in the evaluation of reality, representing a fundamental principle that evaluation demands some degree of transcendence of what is being evaluated.

name: observers (universe)
originalDescription: Observers commission and represent local systems within the universe. (33:6.4)
improvedDescription: Observers are commissioned by executive decree of a System Sovereign to represent their system to other systems and at the constellation capital, functioning only within the confines of the local universe.

name: oceans
originalDescription: Throughout this epoch, the oceans became too salty, causing minimal life to survive in the excessively salty seas. (59:3.10) The ocean bottoms sank under the weight of accumulating meteors from the Pacific Ocean, causing the continental land masses to rise. (57:8.20-21) During the Pleistocene, ocean currents shifted and climate changed, leading to the formation of glaciers and the shaping of our planet's landscape for the last 15 million years. (61:3.9) (61:5.2) There are no great oceans on Jerusem or Edentia, only sparkling lakes and interconnecting streams. (43:1.1) (46:2.2) Not all inhabited planets have atmospheric oceans suited to respiratory exchange of gases, such as present on Urantia. (49:2.24) Originally fresh, the ocean attained requisite saltiness over time. (57:8.3,25) North America's protection by oceans facilitated its peaceful and continuous development. (81:6.5) The ideal conditions for life implantation on a planet with marine origins are provided by shallow inland seas where sunlight can penetrate more than 600 feet. (58:1.7) The weight of the oceans, in relation to land elevations, plays a crucial role in maintaining the balance between continents and sea bottoms. (58:5.5-8) One billion years ago, a world-wide ocean covered the entire planet of Urantia, not yet salty but a fresh-water covering for the world. (57:8) for oceans named in The Urantia Book see links below | Arabian Sea; Arctic Ocean; Atlantic Ocean; Bay of Bengal; China Seas; Gulf of Mexico; Gulf Stream; Indian Ocean; Pacific Ocean; Persian Gulf
improvedDescription: Large bodies of water that have played crucial roles in planetary development, originally formed as fresh water but becoming increasingly salty over time through geological processes. These vast waters support marine life and influence climate patterns through their currents and seasonal interactions with atmospheric systems.

name: octopuses
originalDescription: Octopuses are descendant of cephalopods that have survived and evolved alongside corals, sponges, and other ancient sea creatures. (59:2.11)
improvedDescription: Descendants of ancient cephalopods that have survived from early evolutionary periods alongside corals, sponges, and other primitive sea creatures, representing one of the stationary types that failed to progress evolutionarily.

name: Og
originalDescription: Og was an Amorite king of Bashan. The brutal and effective Hebrew raid upon Og was a step towards enslavement and the merciless treatment of war captives, contrasting with the more compassionate practice of adopting superior captives seen among contemporary tribes. (69:8.3)
improvedDescription: An Amorite king of Bashan who was defeated in a brutal Hebrew raid that exemplified the merciless treatment of war captives, contrasting with the more compassionate practices of adopting superior captives practiced by contemporary tribes.

name: Ohio
originalDescription: Ohio was covered by the eastern sheet of the sixth glacier 150,000 years ago. (61:7.9)
improvedDescription: A region that was covered by the eastern ice sheet during the sixth and final glacier period 150,000 years ago.

name: Ohio River
originalDescription: 260 million years ago, the Ohio River banks near Louisville, Kentucky displayed thick coral deposits, reflecting clear and shallow inland seas during the Devonian period. (59:4.6) (61:7.2)
improvedDescription: A river whose banks near Louisville, Kentucky display thick coral deposits from 260 million years ago, indicating the presence of clear and shallow inland seas during the Devonian period.

name: oil (petroleum)
originalDescription: Oil and gas deposits are found in sedimentations from previous land submergence, along with zinc and lead, representing a variety of sedimentary rocks including chalk, sandstone, and limestone. (59:3.4) (60:3.9) Oil is often found in coal beds. (59:5.16)
improvedDescription: Fossil fuel deposits formed from enormous collections of vegetable and animal matter carried down during previous land submergences, often found in sedimentary rocks including chalk, sandstone, limestone, and coal beds.

name: oil (vegetable)
originalDescription: Early medicine for treating wounds, including oil and wine, was passed down from the Greeks and Egyptians, who acquired their medical knowledge from the Euphrates valley. (90:4.9) The Good Samaritan poured oil onto wounds, showing mercy and compassion. (164:1.3) Jesus' apostles would not agree to anoint with certain forms of oil, despite the push from John's followers. (150:0.2)
improvedDescription: Plant-derived oils used in ancient medicine for treating wounds, often combined with wine, representing medical knowledge passed down from early civilizations including the Greeks, Egyptians, and Sumerians.

name: ointment
originalDescription: The former brothel keeper anointed Jesus' feet with perfumed lotion in a heartfelt display of gratitude and devotion at Simon's house. (147:5.3) Jesus’ body was embalmed with myrrh and aloes by Joseph and Nicodemus in the tomb, while the women waited nearby to properly prepare him for burial. (188:1.4,7) (189:4.3,5) Judas resented Mary’s waste of incense at Jesus' feet, leading to a lifetime of accumulated hate and revenge. (139:12.10) Mary of Bethany poured spikenard on Jesus' head and feet. (172:1.5-8)
improvedDescription: Ointments, perfumed anointing lotions, and precious embalming substances were used in various significant events during Jesus' ministry. A reformed woman anointed Jesus' feet with perfumed lotion at Simon's house in gratitude, Mary of Bethany poured costly spikenard on Jesus' head and feet, and women prepared spices and ointments for Jesus' burial preparation.

name: Okhban
originalDescription: Okhban, the great Egyptian prophet, was ultimately murdered by the people who once followed his teachings. (95:3.5)
improvedDescription: Okhban was one of four great prophets who arose among the Egyptians over six thousand years, but was ultimately murdered by the people who had once followed his teachings.

name: Old Testament
originalDescription: origin editing inconsistencies relics of barbarism Jesus Books named
improvedDescription: The collection of Hebrew sacred writings that Jesus recognized as containing valuable religious wisdom and spiritual truth, while also acknowledging their human origin and the need to discern the better teachings within them. Jesus taught that these scriptures represent the views and enlightenment of their times, containing both truth and human error.

name: old things
originalDescription: The old things are passing away, behold all things are becoming new in the eternal abode of God and his Son, where self-mastery is the measure of man's moral nature and spiritual development. (27:1.5) (100:7.18) (143:2.3) Old things should not be despised merely because they are old; the timeless truths must abide alongside the new and true. (81:6.39) (147:7.3) (195:9.1) Ancient traditions are revered as sacred, causing religion to hold onto the old ways despite advances in technology and society. (92:2.2)
improvedDescription: The former ways and ancient traditions that are passing away as spiritual transformation occurs. Jesus taught that in the Father's kingdom, believers become new creatures and old things pass away, while emphasizing that ancient truths should not be despised merely for being old if they remain valid.

name: Oligocene
originalDescription: The Oligocene era, from 35 to 25 million years ago, was characterized by the rapid evolution of placental mammals and the establishment of modern land flora. (61:2)
improvedDescription: A geological period from 35 to 25 million years ago characterized by the rapid evolution of placental mammals and the development of more progressive forms of mammalian life.

name: olive press
originalDescription: At the apostles' discussion at the olive press, Nathaniel and James reminded the group of Jesus' teachings on nonresistance, leading to their decision to scatter and await further information. (183:4) Alone on the olive press near the entrance to Gethsemane, Jesus awaited his apprehenders in solitude. (183:0.3,5)
improvedDescription: The location near the entrance to Gethsemane where Jesus sat alone awaiting his arrest, and where the apostles gathered to deliberate their response to the Master's capture.

name: Olivet
improvedDescription: A location referenced in The Urantia Book, likely referring to the Mount of Olives area near Jerusalem.

name: Olympian religion
improvedDescription: The religious system associated with ancient Greek worship of the Olympian gods.

name: omens
originalDescription: The spirits' intentions were deciphered through omens and signs, shaping a philosophy of duty, right, and truth in ancient rituals to appease and influence them. (87:5.9-14) Medicine men put great trust in signs, omens, and the communication with spirits of the dead. (90:2.5) The belief in magic numbers and omens is pure unfounded superstition. (150:3.8)
improvedDescription: Signs and portents used by ancient peoples to interpret the intentions and will of spirits, forming part of primitive religious practices. Medicine men and shamans placed great trust in omens, though The Urantia Book characterizes belief in omens as pure superstition.

name: omniaphim
originalDescription: Michael, accompanied by an omniaphim, embarked on a Melchizedek bestowal mission that left the inhabitants of Salvington eagerly awaiting news. (119:1.3,5) Omniaphim are registered in the registers of personalities in the universe. (30:1.84) (30:2.86) Omniaphim serving as Technical Advisers may become Paradise Companions, but they are not of permanent status and typically return to their previous duties after completing their ministry. (25:4.6) (25:8.1,8) Omniaphim, exclusive servants and messengers of the Seven Supreme Executives, are not directly involved in the ascent of mortals. (28:0.3) (28:2.1) The omniaphim serves as the personal representative of the Assigned Sentinel at the system headquarters. (55:4.16) Omniaphim are exclusive servants of the Seven Supreme Executives, created by Infinite Spirit, residing in Uversa, but not directly involved in mortal progression. (26:1.13) (28:2.1)
improvedDescription: A celestial order created by the Infinite Spirit in liaison with the Seven Supreme Executives, serving as their exclusive servants and messengers. They are assigned to grand universe service, maintain headquarters in Orvonton, and serve as personal representatives of Assigned Sentinels at system headquarters.

name: omnificence
originalDescription: Advocating the omnificence of primal Deity disenfranchises all other personalities in the universe. (118:6.2) The freewill actions of spirits do not always reflect the perfection and immutability of the Eternal Son, who maintains absolute control over all spirit realities. (7:0.5) Recognizing Deity omnipotence brings security and assurance in the journey to Paradise, while accepting the fallacy of omnificence leads to the colossal error of pantheism. (118:6.8) God's actions may seem cruel or indifferent due to our limited perspective, but it is important to understand that God does not personally do everything, as omnificence is not a characteristic of the Universal Father. (3:2.10) (118:6) God's love does not entail predestined decisions; man has relative freedom of choice. (118:10.1) Jews saw God in almost everything that happened, but it is our mission to help men solve their spiritual problems and not ascribe all misunderstandings to the Father. (148:5.4) (156:5.4)
improvedDescription: The belief that God personally does all that is done in the universe, which would disenfranchise all other personalities and lead to pantheism. The Urantia Book teaches that God is omnipotent but not omnificent—he does not personally do everything that occurs.

name: omnipotence
originalDescription: Omnipotence and compossibility dictate that even God cannot create square circles or produce inherently good evil, as divinity inherently includes the concept of what can and cannot be done. (118:5) God is truly omnipotent, but not omnificent; only the Father-I AM possesses finality of volition. (118:6) God's omnipotence is perfectly coordinated with his nature, will, and law, limiting his power only in spiritual manifestation. (3:2.11-15) (4:4.3-4) Omnipotence does not allow for the doing of the nondoable or ungodlike actions, reflecting the innate compossibility in divine power. (3:3.5) (118:5.1) The Seven Master Spirits collectively disclose omnipotence, omniscience, and omnipresence, each individually endowed with supreme-ultimate attributes of the Third Source and Center, yet limited to their respective superuniverses. (16:2.4) God's volitional absoluteness is demonstrated through his infinite will and eternal nature, as he chooses only that which is infinitely perfect despite the possibility of self-limitation. (4:4.4) Transcendental reality encompasses omnipotence, omniscience, and omnipresence. (105:7.13) When God decrees a thing, that thing is like the Most High of heaven and earth, supreme and compassionate. (131:1.2)
improvedDescription: The divine attribute of unlimited power held by Deity, though constrained by God's nature, will, and law. True omnipotence does not include the power to do ungodlike things or logical contradictions, and God's omnipotence is perfectly coordinated with his other divine attributes.

name: Omnipotent
originalDescription: God the Ultimate manifests as the Omnipotent in controlling universes of time under the overcontrol of the Ultimate. (118:2.4)
improvedDescription: A transcendental manifestation of God the Ultimate as the overcontroller of time and space, exercising administrative functions beyond those of the Almighty Supreme.

name: omnipresence
originalDescription: The Universal Father's omnipresence allows him to be in two places, or even numberless places, simultaneously. (3:1.1) The omnipresence of the Mother Spirits in their own universes allows for independent and diffused presence throughout the entirety of the local universe, transcending space but not time. (34:3.3,8) (41:0.1) (45:1.8) (116:5.13) The omnipresence of God, alone in two places, in countless places, at the same time, is a fundamental aspect of His infinite nature, transcending spatial barriers and revealing His divine presence throughout all creation. (3:1) (131:1.6) The omnipresence of the Infinite Spirit is a universal and divine attribute present throughout all the universe of universes. (8:5.1) (9:1.5) The Seven Master Spirits collectively disclose the attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and omnipresence through their supreme-ultimate endowment from the Third Source and Center. (16:2.4) The concept of omnipresence is a fundamental aspect of the transcendental reality. (105:7.13) The Supreme may be personally present on Uversa, directing the administration of time creations, while maintaining omnipresence in the universe. (117:7.16)
improvedDescription: The ability of the Universal Father to be everywhere present simultaneously, existing in numberless places at the same time. This divine attribute allows God to transcend spatial limitations while maintaining personal presence throughout all creation.

name: omniscience
originalDescription: In the grand universe, Creator will and creature will operate within established limits and possibilities, without abridging sovereignty or invalidating authenticity, suggesting foreknowledge of those who may elect to contribute to reality. (118:7) Supreme directors of the grand universe maintain sacred secrecy to honor the privacy and sanctity of all beings, whether lowly or high. (18:1.5) The Solitary Messengers investigate undiscovered energy systems of space, knowing of them prior to experience but never revealing such information to others. (23:2.20) (23:2.20) (25:4.16) God's foreknowledge of sin does not diminish human freedom, yet we are unsure if He chooses to foreknow it; however, what is definite is that God is never caught off guard. (3:3.4) God's foreknowledge of sin does not limit free will, guaranteeing God's unwavering omniscience. (3:3.4) God's all-encompassing knowledge is derived from Adjusters, personality circuits, divine Sons, and the Infinite Spirit, making his consciousness universal and his power to know all things unlimited. (3:3.3) God's omniscience surpasses our comprehension, but we can grasp the avenue through which the Universal Father intimately knows all beings in all creation. (32:4.8) God's preknowledge is existential, as the infinite Deities are compensated for lack of experience, knowing all even before they experience all. (25:4.16) (108:0.2) God's understanding, infinite and unsearchable, remains a mystery to his lowly creatures. (2:1.1) Jesus self-limited his divinity consciousness to interact with humanity in a way they could comprehend and accept. (146:5.2) (161:3.2-3) (192:1.7) The Holy Spirit on your world knows mortal man fully, reflecting with the divine Spirit in making knowledge immediately available. (28:5.22) Reflectivity is omniscience within the experiential finite, potentially connecting us to the consciousness of the Supreme Being. (9:7.5) The Seven Master Spirits collectively disclose the attributes of omnipotence, omniscience, and omnipresence. (16:2.4) Foreknowledge and omniscience do not diminish the freedom of choice within established limits by the Master Architects. (118:7.1) Transcendental reality encompasses omnipotence, omniscience, and omnipresence. (105:7.13)
improvedDescription: The divine attribute of unlimited knowledge possessed by God, who knows all things universally and perfectly. God's omniscience encompasses awareness of all thoughts and events throughout creation, though this foreknowledge does not abridge creature free will.

name: Omri
originalDescription: King Omri attempted to buy Shemer's estate amidst wars, civil strife, and resistance against Assyrian forces, ultimately leading to the decimation of allies at the battle of Karkar. (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: A king of Israel who attempted to purchase Shemer's estate during a period of civil wars and resistance against Assyrian forces under Shalmaneser III.

name: Onagar
originalDescription: Onagar, the mastermind of the pre-Planetary Prince age, led his people in worship of the “Breath Giver” and established a high spiritual civilization on Earth. (45:4.3) (63:6) Onagar, born 983,323 years ago, was a masterful spiritual leader who organized primitive peoples into a high spiritual civilization. (63:6.8) Born 983,323 years ago, Onagar lived to the age of sixty-nine and instituted an efficient tribal government that paved the way for a high spiritual civilization on Earth. (63:6.8) Onagar was one of 24 counselors in the pre-Planetary Prince age, directing his fellows in the worship of “The Breath Giver.” (45:4.3) The prayer of Onagar reveals the essential significance of food in the lives of primitive humans. (63:6.5-6)
improvedDescription: The first great spiritual leader of primitive humanity, born 983,323 years ago. He organized early tribes into the first high spiritual civilization on Earth and led them in worship of "The Breath Giver," establishing efficient tribal government that lasted until the arrival of the Planetary Prince.

name: Onamonalonton
originalDescription: Onamonalonton, the far-distant leader and spiritual deliverer of the red man, lived 65,000 years ago and brought temporary peace among American red men and revived their worship of the “Great Spirit,” but his teachings were eventually forgotten, leading to renewed internecine wars and the loss of a potential great civilization. (45:4.5) (64:6.7-8) Amerinds looked for return of Hesunanin Onamonalonton, a leader born of virgins and expected back miraculously by their group, echoing the concept across various races. (92:5.6) Onamonalonton, descendant of Blackfoot Indians, brought peace and revived worship among American red men, living to be ninety-six years old. (64:6.7) The Onamonalonton center in California was the oldest Amerindian culture, which vanished by 35,000 B.C, before the later civilizations in Mexico, Central America, and the mountains of South America were founded by red and yellow races with diverse influences. (79:5.8) Onamonalonton was one of 24 counselors who guided the red man to worship “The Great Spirit” from many gods. (45:4.5) Some tribes upheld the teaching of Onamonalonton by following the unanimous rule of a council of seven, including women rulers in some tribes. (70:5.4)
improvedDescription: A spiritual leader of the American red men who lived 65,000 years ago and brought temporary peace among the tribes while reviving their worship of "The Great Spirit." He lived to be ninety-six years old and established his headquarters among the redwood trees of California.

name: one-brained mortals
improvedDescription: A classification of mortal beings with a single brain structure, representing one of the planetary types of evolutionary mortals described in the cosmic scheme of intelligent life.

name: One God
originalDescription: Ikhnaton's book “The One God” was a profound testament to his wise leadership and devotion to the supreme creator. (95:5.7)
improvedDescription: A book of thirty-one chapters written by Ikhnaton promoting monotheistic worship, which was later destroyed by Egyptian priests when they returned to power after his reign.

name: One High in Authority
improvedDescription: A celestial being of high administrative rank and authority.

name: one hundred councils of supreme sanction
originalDescription: The presidents of the one hundred councils of supreme sanction constitute Gabriel's working cabinet on Salvington. (33:8.4-5)
improvedDescription: High advisory councils situated on Salvington whose presidents constitute Gabriel's working cabinet. These councils function as universe superlegislatures but operate without authority of enactment or power of execution, referring their findings to judicial bodies or legislative assemblies.

name: One Truth
originalDescription: Singlangton proclaimed God to the yellow race as the worship of the “One Truth,” leading them towards spiritual advancement and cultural development. (64:6.15) (79:6.10) (79:8.4)
improvedDescription: The spiritual concept proclaimed by Singlangton to the yellow race about one hundred thousand years ago, representing the worship of truth as the supreme Deity. This progressive religion provided spiritual stimulus that advanced Chinese civilization, though later became overshadowed by reverence for established traditions.

name: ONE UNCAUSED
improvedDescription: A designation referring to the absolute, self-existent nature of the ultimate source of all existence.

name: One without Name and Number
improvedDescription: A celestial being belonging to the class of Those without Name and Number.

name: ooze
originalDescription: Man's ascent from seaweed to lordship is a romantic tale of biologic struggle and mind survival. Man’s primordial ancestors were literally the slime and ooze of the ocean bed in the sluggish and warm-water bays and lagoons of the vast shore lines of the ancient inland seas (65:2.1)
improvedDescription: The primordial slime and ooze of ocean beds in ancient inland seas from which human life originally evolved. Man's primordial ancestors were literally this organic matter found in sluggish, warm-water bays and lagoons where the Life Carriers established life on Urantia.

name: Ophel
originalDescription: In Jerusalem, Ophel mount played a significant role in the sacred rituals of the last day of the feast, with priests and pilgrims engaging in symbolic ceremonies and offerings. (162:4.4)
improvedDescription: A location in Jerusalem through which religious processions passed during feast day ceremonies. Priests carrying golden water pitchers would march through Ophel to the fountain gate near Siloam as part of sacred temple rituals.

name: opiate
originalDescription: The religion of Jesus is not an opiate, but a powerful force urging humanity forward towards spiritual growth and enlightenment. (194:3.3-4)
improvedDescription: A deadening drug or influence that provides escape from reality. Jesus refused such substances and taught that true religion is not an opiate to the people but rather a powerful spiritual force that leads humanity upward and forward in growth and enlightenment.

name: opinions
improvedDescription: Personal viewpoints or judgments formed about matters, often distinguished from established facts or revealed truth.

name: opium
originalDescription: Opium was used by the Sumerians as medicine, along with other ancient remedies that lost their power once known. (90:4.9)
improvedDescription: A narcotic substance used medicinally by ancient peoples, particularly the Sumerians. Along with other secret remedies, opium lost its perceived power once its properties became widely known and understood.

name: opossums
originalDescription: 45 million years ago, opossums were among the small, primitive mammals flourishing in the evolving mammalian life on elevated continental backbones. (61:1.9)
improvedDescription: Small, primitive mammals that flourished 45 million years ago during the rapid evolution of mammalian life on elevated continental backbones.

name: opportunities
originalDescription: Seeking God with a whole heart will lead to deliverance from captivity and new opportunities for a better life. (130:1.2) The government's prime mission is to define rights, regulate class differences, and enforce equality of opportunity under the rules of law. (81:5.6) Hasten off to where great things wait to be done, for the big and real things of this world are on your side, and your potential for achievement lies within you. (130:6.3) Only untrustworthiness stands between us and the sanctity of service. (28:6.16) Society should allow each individual the opportunity for self-realization and self-respect, ultimately leading to a culturally rich and evolutionarily advanced society with minimal government intervention. (71:3.9) From the death of fondest hopes arise new opportunities for growth and blessings, as noted by angels who have experienced disappointments themselves. (48:6.36) Fusion with a fragment of the Universal Father opens the career of universal service to Adjuster-fused mortals, leading to ultimate glory and divine validation of eventual Paradise attainment. (40:7.5)
improvedDescription: Chances for growth, service, and achievement that arise through faithful living and alignment with divine will. Seeking God with a whole heart leads to deliverance from captivity and new opportunities for better living, while disappointments often become the foundation for greater blessings and renewed service.

name: oppression
originalDescription: Jesus denounced oppression and exploitation of the weak and less fortunate, advocating for fairness and compassion in all dealings. (140:8.21) (147:8.4) (163:2.11) (173:1.11) Man learned industry through oppression, culminating in the slaves sharing the blessings of a higher society they helped create. (69:8.8) Only love, brotherhood, can prevent idealism from being extinguished by the oppressive forces of humanity. (71:4.17)
improvedDescription: The unfair exploitation and cruel treatment of the weak by the strong, which Jesus consistently condemned as incompatible with the ideals of the kingdom of heaven. While historical oppression like slavery taught industry and contributed to social development, only love and brotherhood can prevent the strong from oppressing the weak.

name: optimism
originalDescription: A certain amount of optimism, along with stoicism, is beneficial in navigating life on earth, but true peace comes from aligning one's will with the divine Father's purpose as exemplified by Jesus. (181:1.8) Embrace the art of defeat gracefully, always fearless of disappointment, and never hide failure under beaming optimism. (160:4.13) Faith, being positive and living, always triumphs over doubt, illustrating that the positive holds the advantage over the negative in all aspects of life. (102:6.7) Secular social and political optimism is an illusion; without God, freedom, liberty, property, and wealth will not lead to peace. (195:8.12) Jesus’ optimism
improvedDescription: A positive outlook that, while serviceable for earthly living, differs from the divine peace that comes from knowing and doing God's will. Jesus displayed a controlled, divine optimism based on unwavering trust in the Father, but warned against blind, unreasoning optimism that hides failure under deceptive cheerfulness.

name: oracles
originalDescription: Through omens, oracles, divination, soothsaying, magic, ordeals, and astrology, the intentions of spirits were believed to be revealed, all part of a scheme to placate and satisfy them through disguised bribery. (87:5.9) The Greeks believed in oracular advice, the Chinese used magic as protection against demons, shamanism flourished in India, and it still openly persists in central Asia. (90:2.8) In crisis, Latin peoples consulted oracles for optimism, embodying their deeply rooted religious practices. (98:3.4)
improvedDescription: Prophetic revelations or divine messages believed to reveal the intentions of spirits, used alongside omens and divination in ancient religious practices. The Greeks believed in oracular advice, while Latin peoples consulted oracles during crises as part of their deeply rooted religious traditions.

name: oral law
improvedDescription: Traditional Jewish legal interpretations and teachings passed down verbally alongside the written Torah, forming part of rabbinic Judaism's comprehensive legal system.

name: orange race
originalDescription: The orange race, with their custom of naming children after ancestors, believed in transmigration and reincarnation, rooted in observations of hereditary resemblance and influenced by teachings of Adam. (86:4.6) Porshunta's wise leadership during their time at Armageddon brought a great revival of higher living to the orange race before their cultural and spiritual decline. (64:6.12) The orange race thrived in Central and South America, blending seamlessly with the red and yellow races to create enduring civilizations. (79:5.8) In southern Europe in 15,000 B.C., a mixed race of Andonite and Sangik peoples, including orange, green, and indigo, inhabited the region. (78:3.5) India absorbed much of the orange race, contributing to its complex mixture of races. (78:1.9) (79:2.2) The indigo in Sahara carried extensive strains of the extinct orange race during the last great struggle between the orange and green men in Egypt. (64:6.13) (78:1.10) (80:1.4) The orange race had a peculiar urge to build but was not progressive in their ways. (64:6.10) Porshunta's wise leadership sparked a great revival of higher living for the orange race 300,000 years ago at Armageddon. (64:6.12) The orange race, with unexpected strains of giantism, was evenly matched with the green race, both carrying the legacy of their giant ancestors. (51:4.2) (64:6.19) The orange race is typically subdued by the red race, leading to servitude or extermination. (51:4.6) extermination
improvedDescription: One of the six colored races characterized by their peculiar urge to build massive structures, including vast mounds of stone. They believed in transmigration and reincarnation, practiced naming children after ancestors, and experienced a great cultural revival under Porshunta's leadership 300,000 years ago at Armageddon before eventually being absorbed by other races.

name: oratory
originalDescription: Celestial artisans preserve thought for reproduction in the harmonious and euphonic orations of the spirit world, a rare treat compared to the crude and stumbling oratory of Urantia. (44:4.7-8) He told the speaker at the forum, “Your eloquence and logic are pleasing, but true teaching comes from knowing God as your spiritual Father.” (132:4.7)
improvedDescription: The art of public speaking, which in the spirit world represents one of the rare treats with harmony of music and euphony of expression that inspires beyond description. Celestial artisans preserve thought for reproduction in the harmonious orations of higher worlds, far superior to the crude and stumbling speeches of earthly speakers.

name: ordeals
originalDescription: The ordeals were believed to interpret spirit messages in a scheme to placate the spirits through disguised bribery. (87:5.9) The early crime detectors and officers of the law believed that ghosts administered justice through medicine men and priests, conducting ordeals of poison, fire, and pain as methods of detecting crime. (70:10.5-7) Jesus denounced belief in spells, ordeals, bewitching, cursing, signs, mandrakes, knotted cords, and all other forms of ignorant and enslaving superstition. (150:3.12)
improvedDescription: Primitive methods of detecting crime or determining guilt through trials of poison, fire, and pain, believed to be administered by ghosts through medicine men and priests. These crude techniques of arbitration were practiced by early tribes as a form of divine justice, though Jesus denounced such superstitious practices.

name: order
originalDescription: Harmony is the keynote of the central universe, with detectable order prevailing on Paradise, guiding pilgrims towards the spiritual heights of spontaneous worship. (27:4.2) Maintain temporal order in fellowship, as Jesus taught, to ensure fairness and justice in the community. (159:1.3,6) The kingdom is a realm of order, providing authority of leadership when two or more creatures act in cooperation. (181:2.16) Social groups may maintain discipline, unlike the kingdoms of this world which often rely on physical force for order. (178:1.2)
improvedDescription: The harmonious arrangement and proper conduct that prevails throughout creation, from Paradise to earthly kingdoms. Jesus taught that maintaining temporal order in spiritual communities requires group wisdom and definite leadership, distinguishing between the spiritual methods of the kingdom of heaven and the physical force sometimes employed by material governments.

name: Ordovician
originalDescription: The Ordovician era, from 350 to 300 million years ago, was a time of gradual land fluctuations and multiple major floods, characterized by the evolution of various marine organisms. (59:2)
improvedDescription: The Ordovician era was a geological period characterized by gradual land fluctuations and multiple major floods, during which various marine organisms evolved.

name: organisms
originalDescription: Organisms are inherently active participants in the dynamic relationship between selfhood and environment. (112:1.13)
improvedDescription: Organisms are inherently active participants in the dynamic relationship between selfhood and environment, functioning as active agents rather than passive mechanisms.

name: organs
originalDescription: The ancients believed the soul resided in various physical organs and elements. (111:0.3)
improvedDescription: The ancients believed the soul resided in various physical organs including the eye, liver, kidney, heart, and later the brain.

name: orientation
originalDescription: The sense of orientation in living beings is fixed by directional control creatures in Nebadon, guided by universe currents and assisted by the spirit of quick understanding. (34:4.12-13)
improvedDescription: The sense of orientation in living beings is maintained by four directional control creatures at Salvington who operate over universe currents, working with microscopic direction cells in animal organisms that point north and south.

name: origin
originalDescription: The significance of origin is the paramount question regarding all relationships, ethics, and advancement in the cosmic realm. (28:6.2)
improvedDescription: The significance of origin is the paramount question regarding all relationships, ethics, and advancement in the cosmic realm, serving as the basis for the relational reaction of the Gods.

name: Original
originalDescription: Originality causes and balances the metamorphosis of reality's motions, from potentials to actuals. (115:3.12) The Original allows man to coexist as actual, potential, and eternal beings. (115:3.15) The Original, the unqualified concept of the First Source and Center, is the source manifestation of I AM from which all reality originates. (115:3.6,11)
improvedDescription: The Original is the unqualified concept of the First Source and Center, that source manifestation of the I AM from which all reality originates, making possible the coexistence of actual, potential, and eternal aspects of existence.

name: Original Mother Son
improvedDescription: A designation related to the Eternal Son in their original relationship with the Universal Father.

name: original sin doctrine
originalDescription: The concept of original sin accounted for death and weakened the grip of magic on ancient peoples. (88:4.7) Adam's sin cursed the human race, contrary to the Jewish belief in the Shekinah and the Messiah's role in restoring divine favor. (136:1.4) (186:5.7) The crass injustice of damning a soul because of ancestors' sin goes against the teaching that each individual's relationship with God is personal and not based on the sins of others. (145:2.7-8) (186:5.7) (188:4.3) Philo's teachings influenced Paul, but he diverged on the doctrine of hereditary guilt, innate evil, and redemption from original sin. (121:6.5) The doctrine of total depravity hindered religion's ability to uplift society and inspire, until Jesus proclaimed all men as children of God, reaffirming their dignity. (99:5.5) Mankind, by nature evil but not necessarily sinful, requires the new birth to be delivered from evil and enter the kingdom of heaven, rejecting the erroneous teaching of descending from godly perfection towards oblivion. (148:4.7-10) The fall of man did not occur with Adam and Eve, as the human race has evolved and progressed despite their mistakes and ultimate failure in carrying out the divine plan. (75:8) (86:3.3) Paul partly incorporated Mithraic elements into his theory of original sin, distinct from both Hebrew theology and Philo's teachings. (121:6.5) The doctrine of original sin necessitates a onetime golden age hypothesis, perpetuated by Christian teachers. (74:8.13) PRIMITIVE man, in debt to spirits, developed the doctrine of sin and salvation, seeing the soul as born under forfeit and in need of redemption. (89:0.1) (89:4.6) Jesus' gospel redeems man from the superstition of being a child of the devil, elevating him to a faith son of God, providing a timeless solution to spiritual difficulties. (194:2.1)
improvedDescription: The doctrine of original sin teaches that Adam's transgression cursed the human race with inherited guilt, but The Urantia Book rejects this concept, teaching instead that guilt is purely a matter of personal sin and that mankind has no racial guilt before God.

name: originality
originalDescription: Society should foster originality to cultivate individualism and encourage initiative in a more civilized manner than militarism. (70:2.18) Jesus exhorted his hearers to strive for originality while shunning eccentricity, and to embrace sympathy without sentimentality and piety without sanctimoniousness. (149:4.4)
improvedDescription: Society should foster originality to cultivate individualism and encourage initiative in a more civilized manner than militarism, while individuals should strive for originality while avoiding eccentricity.

name: Orlandof
originalDescription: Orlandof, the prince and teacher of the blue race 200,000 years ago, led a revival of learning and religion, guiding many tribes back to worshiping the true God as the “Supreme Chief.” (45:4.6) (64:6.23) Orlandof, one of 24 counselors, is a prince of the blue men and their leader in recognizing the divinity of “The Supreme Chief.” (45:4.6)
improvedDescription: Orlandof was a prince and teacher of the blue race who lived about 200,000 years ago and led a revival of learning and religion, guiding many tribes back to worshiping the true God as the "Supreme Chief."

name: ornamentation
originalDescription: Saliva, hair, and nails were viewed as potent fetishes, but mankind's first necklace was a preserved umbilical cord set with pearls. (88:1.8) The early industrial and trading cities saw the widespread use of metals, with gold being the first metal sought by man and initially used as an ornament. (81:3.4-5) Early Andonites engraved animal gods on objects as symbols of creative might and sustaining power, evolving a form of animal worship due to their fear of the elements and constant urge of hunger. (63:6.3)
improvedDescription: Decorative items and artistic embellishments used by early humans, including the first necklaces made from preserved umbilical cords set with pearls, gold ornaments as humanity's earliest use of metal, and animal god engravings on various objects by the Andonites.

name: orphans
originalDescription: Orphans were adopted into families of Prince’s corporeal staff, who raised and trained them to become emissaries of the Prince to their tribes. (66:7.5)
improvedDescription: Children adopted into the fifty pattern homes of Dalamatia by Prince's corporeal staff, who were raised and trained for three years before being commissioned as emissaries to their respective tribes.

name: Orphic brotherhood
originalDescription: The Orphic brotherhood stood out as the best of mystery cults with its moral preachments and promises of salvation amidst a landscape of orgiastic folly and the exiling of philosophers. (98:2.10)
improvedDescription: The best of the mystery cults in the Mediterranean region, distinguished by its moral preachments and promises of salvation, offering a respectable alternative to the orgiastic follies of other mystery religions.

name: Orvonon
originalDescription: Orvonon brought spiritual awakening to the isolated indigo race in Africa, who, though later forgetting his message, maintained a desire to worship the Unknown. (64:6.26) Orvonon, who led the indigo races and served “The God of Gods,” now is one of the special 24 counselors of Jerusem designated by Jesus after his depart from our world. (45:4.10)
improvedDescription: A spiritual leader who brought enlightenment to the isolated indigo race in Africa, teaching them about "The God of Gods" and now serving as one of the twenty-four special counselors of Jerusem designated by Jesus.

name: Orvonton (Paradise satellite)
originalDescription: Each executive and facility of Orvonton, the seventh Paradise satellite, is dedicated to the efficient governance of the seventh superuniverse. (17:1.5)
improvedDescription: The seventh Paradise satellite of the Spirit, named Orvonton, whose executive facilities are dedicated to the efficient governance and administration of the seventh superuniverse.

name: Orvonton (superuniverse)
originalDescription: Our world, Urantia, is part of superuniverse number 7 in the grand universe, known as Orvonton, from which the Urantia Book revelatory commission hails. (0:0.5) (12:1.12) (15:1.5) (15:14.7) (30:4.34) Our astronomers have identified eight of the ten major divisions of Orvonton, with the remaining two requiring a distant external view for recognition within the seventh galaxy. (15:3.4) Uversa, capital of Orvonton, is the spiritual and administrative headquarters for approximately one trillion inhabited or inhabitable worlds, including Earth (Urantia) in Nebadon. (0:0.5) (15:7.10-11) Limited sun-forming nebulae are active in Orvonton, with Andromeda being one of them, although it is located in an uninhabited region. (15:4.7) The double star explosion in Orvonton, whose intense light reached Urantia in A.D. 1572, was the most recent major cosmic eruption. (41:3.5) Nebadon is on the periphery of Orvonton, while Urantia is well out towards the borderland of the local universe which is traversing the same periphery. (15:1.6) Nebadon, part of the seventh superuniverse Orvonton, follows a counterclockwise path around Paradise, currently journeying on the northward trajectory towards untold cosmic horizons. (15:1.5) In Orvonton, spheres travel in a vast elongated plane that is far broader than it is thick or long. (15:3.2) Time on Orvonton is standardized, with a day equal to almost 30 Earth days and a year equal to 3000 Earth days. (15:7.2) what comprises the superuniverse evolutionary development
improvedDescription: The seventh superuniverse of the grand universe, containing approximately one trillion inhabited or inhabitable worlds including Urantia. Orvonton encompasses the local universe of Nebadon and is characterized by its lavish bestowal of merciful ministry, representing a unique demonstration of love and mercy in cosmic evolution.

name: Osiris
originalDescription: Osiris, son of Isis in Egyptian mystery cult, was not recognized by Jeremiah as a deity compared to Yahweh, God of all nations and peoples. (97:6.2) (98:4.2-8) Isis was the consort of Osiris, miraculously resurrected from a cruel death at the hands of Set. (95:5.12) Departed souls passed through the judgment hall of Osiris to be weighed in the balances for admission to the realms of bliss or consignment to hell. (95:2.9)
improvedDescription: Egyptian deity in mystery cult worship, depicted as the son of Isis who experienced death and resurrection. Souls of the departed were believed to pass through Osiris's judgment hall to be weighed in balances for admission to realms of bliss or consignment to hell.

name: ostriches
originalDescription: Ostriches are descended from enormous ancient reptiles, making their evolution one of the greatest leaps in prehuman history. (65:2.9)
improvedDescription: Modern birds descended from enormous ancient reptiles, representing one of the greatest evolutionary leaps in prehuman history. Ancient ostrich-like birds grew to ten feet in height and laid eggs nine by thirteen inches, serving as ancestors to later gigantic passenger birds.

name: otters
originalDescription: Otters are among the thriving and evolving members of the weasel family. (61:3.13)
improvedDescription: Members of the weasel family that thrived and developed throughout the northern latitudes alongside martens, raccoons, and other small carnivorous mammals.

name: oudah
originalDescription: The oudah beliefs of African Pygmies encompassed their practices of prayer, alongside the mana practices of Melanesians and manitou superstitions of North American Indians. (91:0.5)
improvedDescription: Religious beliefs and prayer practices of African Pygmies, representing an early evolutionary stage of spiritual development alongside the mana practices of Melanesians and manitou superstitions of North American Indians.

name: outer space
improvedDescription: The regions beyond the seven superuniverses where future universes are expected to develop and evolve.

name: outerspacers
originalDescription: The incoming spiritual aristocracy of the successive outer universes may become future citizens of Havona. (19:6.7) The ultimate destiny of outer-spacers may involve approaching Havona through the seven superuniverses, contributing to the maintenance of experiential equilibrium while welcoming Paradise pilgrims. (40:10.8) (55:12.2) (56:7.9)
improvedDescription: The future inhabitants of the outer space universes who may eventually become citizens of Havona and contribute to the maintenance of experiential equilibrium in the perfected superuniverses.

name: overcontrol
originalDescription: The variational and unpredictable phenomena encountered when exploring beyond are indicative of the universal overcontrol of all things by experiential Deities and Absolutes. (15:8.9) The integrated functioning of the Life Carriers, physical controllers, and spirit adjutants conditions the course of evolutionary processes, making evolution purposeful and never accidental. (65) Universal stability is achieved through balanced energies, cooperative minds, coordinated morontias, spirit overcontrol, and personality unification, all proportional to divinity. (12:6)
improvedDescription: The universal oversight and balanced coordination of all creation by the experiential Deities and Absolutes, manifested through integrated energies, minds, and spirit influence.

name: overplanning
originalDescription: Overplanning sometimes defeats its own high purpose. (160:4.11)
improvedDescription: Excessive planning for the future that sometimes defeats its own high purpose by becoming an end in itself rather than a means to achieve worthy goals.

name: overpopulation
improvedDescription: The condition of having too many inhabitants relative to available resources or living space.

name: overprotection
originalDescription: John Mark was fortunate to have not been overprotected, allowing him to develop a strong and dependable character rooted in love and wisdom. (177:2.2-4)
improvedDescription: Excessive parental protection that injuriously exalts a child's concept of self-importance and prevents the development of independence, wisdom, and dependable character.

name: overrevelation
improvedDescription: The provision of too much revealed truth beyond what recipients can properly understand or effectively utilize.

name: oversoul of creation
improvedDescription: A designation referring to the Supreme Being as the experiential soul of all creation.

name: overspecialization
originalDescription: The danger of monotony threatens world-wide civilization without effective coordination in human relations. (81:6.36) Jesus deplored overspecialization and emphasized the importance of possessing well-balanced characters, warning against becoming narrow-minded and circumscribed in life's activities. (149:4.3,6) (155:1.4)
improvedDescription: Excessive focus on a narrow field that breeds monotony, narrow-mindedness, and circumscribed thinking, threatening both individual development and civilizational progress.

name: Ovid
originalDescription: Ovid, a Phoenician teacher, converted Terah's family to the Salem religion in Ur, leading them on a journey to Salem, delayed by their halfhearted faith. (93:5.4)
improvedDescription: A Phoenician teacher who preached the Salem doctrines in Ur and converted Terah's family, including Abraham, to the Salem religion.

name: owls
originalDescription: The diverse bird life included owls among the existing modern species. (61:2.12)
improvedDescription: Nocturnal birds of prey that were among the diverse modern bird species existing during the early development of avian life on the planet.

name: oxen
originalDescription: By 5000 B.C., Mesopotamian, Turkestan, and Chinese farmers had begun to domesticate various animals, including the ox, camel, horse, and yak. (81:2.12) The long struggle to substitute oxen for men has ultimately led to the liberation of man power for more valuable tasks in society. (81:6.21) Oxen were used for burden bearing in ancient times alongside other domesticated animals that have since disappeared. (66:5.5)
improvedDescription: Large domesticated cattle used as beasts of burden and for agricultural work, first domesticated by Mesopotamian, Turkestan, and Chinese farmers around 5000 B.C. alongside other animals like camels, horses, and yaks.

name: oxygen
originalDescription: Seaweed and other plants generated the first free atmospheric oxygen, vital for life on Earth. (57:7.9) The primitive planetary atmosphere, lacking free oxygen and nitrogen, is evolving with water vapor, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride, influenced by volcanic gases and meteoric bombardment. (57:7.6,9) On morontia worlds like Edentia, the atmosphere is a unique blend of the usual three-gas mixture plus a morontia gas for the respiration of morontia creatures, free from storms, hurricanes, summer, and winter, allowing for embellished outdoor settings. (43:1.3) The sum of parts in the universe is often greater than their predictable consequences; mathematics and material science alone could not have predicted the creation of liquid water from two hydrogen atoms and one oxygen atom. (12:9.3)
improvedDescription: A vital atmospheric gas first generated by seaweed and other plant life, essential for respiration and life on Earth, initially absent from the primitive planetary atmosphere which contained mainly water vapor, carbon monoxide, carbon dioxide, and hydrogen chloride.

name: oysters
originalDescription: Oysters, mussels, clams, and scallops have evolved over millions of years, surviving the long struggle of marine life development. (59:2.12) (60:2.8) (65:2.5)
improvedDescription: Bivalve mollusks that evolved millions of years ago and survived the long struggle of marine life development, continuing to exist much as they did in ancient seas alongside mussels, clams, and scallops.

name: ozone
originalDescription: The relatively thin and seemingly insignificant ozone layer ten miles above the earth's surface is a crucial protective layer that shields inhabitants from dangerous ultraviolet radiation and allows for the essential influx of health-giving ultraviolet rays. (58:2.2-4)
improvedDescription: A thin atmospheric layer about ten miles above Earth's surface that protects inhabitants from dangerous ultraviolet radiation while allowing beneficial ultraviolet rays to reach the surface, essential for vitamin production.

name: Pacific Ocean
originalDescription: Approximately 950 million years ago, the Pacific Ocean was originally not salty, serving as a fresh-water covering for the world before becoming the world's largest body of water. (57:8.3-5) The Pacific Ocean, formed from the condensation of the earth's hydrosphere, covers 9/10 of the earth's surface due to accumulated heavy materials from meteors falling into the sea. (57:8.20) The geology of Pacific Ocean islands reveals rich fossil beds from ancient marine life, including beautiful ammonites found globally. (60:1.12) The island peoples of the Pacific were improved by the Andites during the dispersal of the mixed descendants of Adam. (79:1.6)
improvedDescription: The world's largest body of water, originally formed as a fresh-water ocean covering the entire planet before becoming salty, and later covering nine-tenths of Earth's surface after meteoric accumulation on the ocean floor.

name: pacifism
improvedDescription: The doctrine or practice of rejecting violence and warfare as means of resolving conflicts.

name: pack
originalDescription: Carry the pack the second mile to challenge the unrighteous. (159:5.15)
improvedDescription: A burden or load to be carried, referenced in Jesus' teaching about going beyond the minimum required service by willingly carrying someone's pack a second mile to challenge unrighteousness.

name: paddle
originalDescription: Jesus assisted in building a steering paddle for the vessel in Caesarea. (130:2.1,4)
improvedDescription: A steering implement for boats, which Jesus helped construct during his stay in Caesarea when the vessel's original steering paddle needed replacement.

name: paganism
originalDescription: Paganism combines Hellenic and Latin mythology with patriotism and tradition in its cults. (121:5.3) Jesus understood that the thoughtless pagan was not ready to receive the wisdom and higher learning he had to offer. (132:7.1-2)
improvedDescription: Religious practices combining Hellenic and Latin mythology with patriotism and tradition, representing spiritual systems that Jesus recognized required readiness and spiritual hunger before higher teachings could be effectively received.

name: pain
originalDescription: The mores and folkways developed unconsciously out of a shared desire to avoid pain and humiliation while pursuing pleasure and power. (68:4.2) In a world of inevitability, pleasure and happiness can only be truly appreciated in the face of pain and suffering. (3:5.14) Andonites not so sensitive to pain, they were imitative and lacked a developed sense of humor like early human beings. (63:4.2) Pain and suffering, essential to progressive evolution, inhibit activity and prompt biologic adaptations as anxiety reverts to the natural state of our savage ancestors. (86:2.1) Heat, through methods like sunlight and hot stones, was used to relieve pain and treat ailments, as early man believed the spirit causing sickness could be eliminated by sweating. (90:4.6) Life Carriers’ healing technique affords relief from pain and better control over cell proliferation. (65:4.6) No pain on Paradise, only eternal joy and light in the presence of God and his Son. (27:1.5)
improvedDescription: Pain is a universal human experience that serves essential evolutionary purposes. It drives the development of social mores and institutions as groups seek to avoid suffering while pursuing pleasure and power. Pain and suffering are necessary for progressive evolution, prompting biological adaptations and behavioral changes. While early humans were less sensitive to pain than later mortals, the experience remains fundamental to mortal existence until reaching Paradise, where eternal joy replaces all pain and sorrow.

name: Palatine Hill
originalDescription: Jesus and Ganid, his student, regularly explored Rome during his stay, spending time at the forum, Capitolium, and Palatine Hill where the Roman emperor’s residence was located. (132:0.2)
improvedDescription: One of the seven hills of Rome where the emperor's residence, the temple of Apollo, and the Greek and Latin libraries were located. Jesus regularly visited Palatine Hill during his stay in Rome, along with the forum and Capitolium, as part of his exploration of the city.

name: palavers
originalDescription: Seldom useful “palavers” cannot effectively run peace or war, as history has shown that a strong, one-man army prevails over armies led by clan heads in the race for power. (70:5.5)
improvedDescription: Primitive debate councils or discussion groups that were seldom effective for conducting affairs of war or peace. History demonstrated that armies led by groups of clan heads could not compete with those commanded by a single strong leader.

name: Paleozoic
originalDescription: The Paleozoic era spanned 250 million years as the marine-life era from 400 to 150 million years ago. (59:0.4) (59:6.11)
improvedDescription: The Paleozoic era, also known as the marine-life era, lasted 250 million years and ended with the Permian period. This era represents one quarter of planetary history and was characterized by the dominance of marine life forms.

name: Palestine
originalDescription: In ancient Palestine, Aramaic, Hebrew, and Greek were the prevalent languages spoken by different social classes. (121:6.2) The climate of Palestine was diverse, ranging from frigid mountains to torrid valleys, with unexpected sandstorms like the one that caused Jesus's fall down the stone stairs. (123:4.5) (124:1.9-10) (163:5.2) Palestine stood as the crossroads of three continents, welcoming travelers and traders from Babylonia, Assyria, Egypt, Syria, Greece, Parthia, and Rome throughout history. (121:2.2) The central location of Palestine determined the missions of Melchizedek and Michael. (93:5.2) The northern kingdom of Ephraim vanished after a three-year siege and dispersion caused by a conspiracy between the king of Israel, Egypt, and Assyria, leading to the concentration of land and a monotheistic revolt in Jerusalem. (97:9.21) The Arabian Semites attempted to claim the Promised Land, described as flowing with milk and honey, but were repeatedly driven out by the more advanced northern Semites and Hittites. (96:2.2) Saharans emigrated to Palestine and Arabia, forming different tribes that spread to different regions. (80:2.2) Jesus chose to be born in Palestine for his mission. (134:0.1-2) During Jesus' time, Palestine enjoyed peace, prosperity, and extensive trade due to its control by the Romans. (121:1.9)
improvedDescription: Palestine was the crossroads of three continents where multiple ancient civilizations met through trade and travel routes. During Jesus's time, it enjoyed peace and prosperity under Roman rule, with three prevalent languages: Aramaic among common people, Hebrew among priests, and Greek among educated classes. The region's central location and diverse climate, ranging from frigid mountains to torrid valleys, made it an ideal setting for both Melchizedek's and Michael's earthly missions.

name: Palisades of the Hudson
originalDescription: The Palisades of the Hudson River were formed by the extrusion of basalt lava between these Triassic strata. Volcanic action was extensive in different parts of the world. (60:1.3)
improvedDescription: A geological formation along the Hudson River created by the extrusion of basalt lava between Triassic rock layers during a period of extensive worldwide volcanic activity.

name: Palm Sunday
originalDescription: Palm Sunday commemorates for Christianity the day of April 2, 30 AD, when Jesus entered Jerusalem riding on a donkey, being acclaimed by the crowd with palm and olive branches. (172:3)
improvedDescription: The Christian commemoration of Jesus's triumphal entry into Jerusalem, when he was acclaimed by crowds waving palm branches. This event occurred on a Sunday when Jesus chose to perform his mightiest earthly work in nearby Bethany, the village that had accepted and believed in him.

name: palms
originalDescription: Palms were used by Jewish people for expressing joy in the Easter festival. When Jesus and his followers descended Olivet, visitors waved branches of palm, expressing joy and fellowship after the Master's unexpected arrival. (172:3.11)
improvedDescription: Palm trees that appeared during the Cretaceous period alongside other angiosperms and became abundant across Europe and North America. Palm branches were also used by crowds to express joy and fellowship when greeting Jesus during his approach to Jerusalem.

name: Palonia
originalDescription: The first system rebellion in Nebadon took place in Palonia, where the Savior Sovereign was ardently loved and widely honored, bringing order and forgiveness to the confused and demoralized local system. (119:2.5-6) In system 11 of constellation 37, a rebellion erupted after a Lanonandek Son's misunderstanding led to dissatisfaction and a disastrous act against the sovereignty of the Creator Son, despite being adjudicated by higher authorities in the universe of Nebadon. (119:2.1)
improvedDescription: A local system in Nebadon where the first system rebellion occurred under System Sovereign Lutentia. Michael served there for over seventeen years as a temporary ruler known as the Savior Sovereign, bringing order and justice to the confused system while offering mercy even to the rebellious predecessor.

name: Panama Isthmus
originalDescription: Twice during the Cenozoic age, the Panama Isthmus rose or fell, shaping the world's landscape and connecting oceans. (61:0.2) (61:1.12)
improvedDescription: The Isthmus of Panama, which rose and fell twice during the Cenozoic age, alternately connecting and separating the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans and significantly affecting global geography and ocean circulation patterns.

name: Pandora
originalDescription: Throughout history, narratives have blamed women like Pandora for bringing evil upon man, perpetuating a universal distrust of women and complicating understanding between the sexes. (84:4.4)
