(12:7.2,7) The length of an individual's life serves as their yardstick for measuring time, influencing their perspective on the duration of evolution in comparison to those on Paradise where time is nonexistent. (65:8.3) Insight into the wholeness of past-present-future events reveals the panoramic perspective of time's eternal continuum. (118:1.8) Only one thing is really worth while, the lesser matters of life should give way before the greater things of the heavenly kingdom. (162:8.3) All intellects, whether partial, incomplete, or evolving, rely on erroneous postulates to form rational thought within the frame of their universe. (115:1.1) Human dignity and nobility through defending family from danger. (100:4.5) In social reconstruction, a cosmic perspective provides the stabilizing loyalty and steadying goal needed by mortal man. (99:7.2) A one-eyed person, and likewise single-eyed material scientists and spiritual mystics, cannot fully grasp the depth of universe reality or the true values of creature experience. (130:4.4) The present is exasperatingly trivial when divorced from the past and future, yet it is through mutual affection and association that we find enhanced defense against all evil. (160:2.9) Religion offers a stabilizing loyalty and far-reaching perspective above temporal goals in social reconstruction. (99:7.2) Rest and relaxation shrink the size of problems, a valuable lesson taught by Jesus during a memorable mountain retreat. (143:3.3) Perspective reveals evil against the backdrop of ultimate good, highlighting the beauties of spiritual truth over the phenomenon of evil. (195:5.12-13) All levels of creation are unified in the divine oneness overspreading universal phenomena, revealing the single purpose of the Architects of the Master Universe. (56:0.2) Values and facts must remain in proper perspective in religion, avoiding disproportionate extremes to grasp the central truth of Jesus' teachings. (143:6.3) The vastness of God’s plan makes it impossible to see much in one life, but all of our mortal struggling is part of an immense and eternal project overseen by the First Great Source and Center. (32:5.1-2) Look beyond mortal uncertainty to the eternal righteousness shining through the clouds of opportunity. (108:6.8)
improvedDescription: A cosmic viewpoint that enables understanding of reality by considering origin, history, and destiny together rather than viewing events in isolation. True perspective reveals the eternal context of temporal experiences and provides the broader understanding necessary for spiritual growth and wisdom.

name: perspiration
originalDescription: As Jesus bid farewell to his apostles and faced great anguish, perspiration rolled off his face in great drops, accepting the natural events to take their course. (182:3.7)
improvedDescription: The physical manifestation of Jesus's great anguish in Gethsemane, where perspiration rolled off his face in great drops as he faced his impending crucifixion and accepted that natural events would take their course.

name: persuasion
originalDescription: Use persuasion, not compulsion, to influence minds and uphold the principle of treating others as you would want to be treated. (178:1.12)
improvedDescription: The preferred method for influencing minds and establishing truth, which Jesus taught should be used instead of compulsion or force. True persuasion respects human free will and follows the principle of treating others as you would want to be treated.

name: Peru
originalDescription: Traces of Andite blood reached Peru, impacting the evolutionary products of the Sangiks in the Western Hemisphere. (79:5.9)
improvedDescription: A South American region where traces of Andite blood reached the indigenous populations, representing one of the few places in the Western Hemisphere where contact with the more advanced racial strains occurred before the end of the first millennium after Christ.

name: pervaded space
improvedDescription: The regions of space that are occupied by and subject to the presence and influence of various cosmic forces and energies, in contrast to unpervaded or empty space.

name: pessimism
originalDescription: Pessimism proves universe not wholly material, as consciousness of values contradicts materialism. (195:7.8) The pursuit of mere knowledge leads to pessimism and human despair without wisdom and spiritual insight. (195:6.3)
improvedDescription: A negative outlook that paradoxically proves the universe is not wholly material, since consciousness of values contradicts pure materialism. Pessimism often results from the pursuit of mere knowledge without the wisdom and spiritual insight that provide proper understanding.

name: pet evil
originalDescription: Almost every human being has a pet evil that must be relinquished to enter the kingdom of heaven, as seen in Matadormus's eventual obedience and role as treasurer of the Jerusalem church. (163:2.7)
improvedDescription: A cherished personal failing or attachment that nearly every human being must relinquish as part of entering the kingdom of heaven. This represents the individual sacrifice or surrender required for spiritual advancement, as illustrated by Matadormus's attachment to his wealth.

name: Peter
improvedDescription: Peter was one of Jesus' twelve apostles, also known as Simon Peter, who played a prominent role in the early Christian movement.

name: petition
originalDescription: The inherent privilege of petition allows citizens to be heard in representative government and progress to the actual management of the government. (71:2.15-16)
improvedDescription: The inherent privilege of petition allows citizens to be heard in representative government and must progress to actual participation in the management of government.

name: petrified forests
improvedDescription: Petrified forests formed when ancient land depressions were filled with erosion deposits and later elevated by underground lava flows during geological epochs.

name: phallic cult
originalDescription: The phallic cult emerged as a defense against the evil eye believed in by the Jews and addressed in a chapter of the Koran, viewing reproductive organs as the only fetish capable of warding off its influence. (87:5.5)
improvedDescription: The phallic cult emerged as a defense against the evil eye, with reproductive organs viewed as the only fetish capable of warding off its harmful influence.

name: Pharisee and publican
originalDescription: The parable of the Pharisee and publican teaches that true happiness comes from humility and seeking spiritual wealth, not self-righteousness or material possessions. (140:5.7) (167:5.1-2)
improvedDescription: The parable of the Pharisee and publican teaches that true happiness comes from humility and seeking spiritual wealth, contrasting self-righteousness with genuine repentance and faith.

name: Pharisees
originalDescription: attitudes Jesus and Pharisees teachings practices
improvedDescription: A Jewish religious sect known for strict observance of ritual law, tithing, and purification practices, who often opposed Jesus' teachings due to their adherence to tradition and ceremonial requirements.

name: Pharos, lighthouse of
originalDescription: The lighthouse of Pharos in Alexandria, a wonder of the world, showed Ganid the way to salvation. (130:3.2)
improvedDescription: The lighthouse of Pharos in Alexandria was one of the seven wonders of the world and served as a metaphor for spiritual guidance when Jesus told Ganid he would be like this lighthouse, showing others the way to salvation.

name: Phasaelis
originalDescription: Jesus preached to Greek, Roman, and Syrian gentiles in Phasaelis, gaining valuable experience in reaching exclusively non-Jewish populations. (143:1.1) (143:3.8)
improvedDescription: A Greek city where Jesus and the apostles gained valuable experience preaching to predominantly gentile populations including Greeks, Romans, and Syrians.

name: Phenix
originalDescription: Jesus asked the youth familiar with the mountains the way to Phenix, offering guidance and assistance in return. (130:6.2)
improvedDescription: A destination in the mountains that Jesus asked directions to while encountering a troubled youth, using the inquiry as an opportunity to offer spiritual guidance.

name: Philadelphia
originalDescription: Jesus and Simon met a merchant from Damascus in Philadelphia, forming a strong bond that led to an invitation to visit the man's headquarters in Jerusalem. (128:3.3) Philadelphia was the largest congregation of believers with a feud with Jerusalem. (166:5) Jesus arrived in Philadelphia with over 600 followers, while Simon Peter and Andrew returned to Pella to instruct the multitude. (167:0.1) Abner's teachings in the Philadelphia synagogue attracted a diverse group of Jews and gentiles, rich and poor, leading many to embrace the teachings of the seventy and enter the kingdom of heaven. (166:5.1) Philadelphia, center of the Abnerian kingdom of heaven, faithfully held to Jesus' teachings better than the Jerusalem brethren. (130:2.3) (166:5.6) (171:1.6) The unfriendly Pharisee, chief ruler of the synagogue in Philadelphia, was deposed for criticizing Jesus on the Sabbath. (167:3.2-4) Lazarus, treasurer of the church at Philadelphia, escaped persecution and found a home alongside Abner and David in Philadelphia. (168:5.3) (171:1.5) (172:2.4) The synagogue in Philadelphia, never subject to Jerusalem, remained open to the teachings of Jesus and his associates. (166:5.1) David oversaw large interests of the kingdom in Philadelphia, after becoming the financial overseer and settling there with Abner and Lazarus. (171:1.5) Jesus' 10th appearance at Philadelphia brought peace and a message of love and unity to his followers. (191:4) David Zebedee left Bethany with Martha and Mary for Philadelphia after marrying Ruth, Jesus' youngest sister, joining Lazarus's sisters and their waiting family of Jesus in about one week. (190:1.9-10) Nathaniel lived in Philadelphia for over a year before going on to preach his understanding of the gospel beyond Mesopotamia. (193:6.4)
improvedDescription: A city in Perea where Abner led the largest group of believers who embraced Jesus' teachings, serving as the center of the Abnerian version of the kingdom of heaven and maintaining closer adherence to Jesus' original teachings than other early Christian communities.

name: philanthropy
improvedDescription: The practice of charitable giving and benevolent acts toward humanity, aimed at promoting the welfare of others through generous donations or selfless service.

name: Philip (Apostle)
originalDescription: Philip, the apostolic steward, was known for his methodical reliability and lack of imagination, but his unwavering faith and effective personal work ultimately led to his martyrdom. (138:10.4) (139:5) life after Jesus accomplishments personal characteristics
improvedDescription: Philip was one of the twelve apostles, serving as the group's steward responsible for provisions and food. Known for his methodical reliability but lacking imagination, he was a successful personal worker who effectively used the invitation "Come and see" to bring others to Jesus. Despite his literal-mindedness and tendency to ask seemingly foolish questions, Philip remained loyal and was eventually martyred for his faith.

name: Philip (Tetrarch)
originalDescription: Philip (Tetrarch), brother of Herod Antipas, sent officials to investigate rumors about Jesus being proclaimed the king of the Jews in his brother's domain. (153:1.1) Caesarea Philippi, capital of Tetrarch Philip's domain, nestled in a region of wondrous beauty. (157:3.1) Philip, the brother of Herod, reluctantly believed in Jesus and allowed him to minister in his territories, but the unexpected boldness of Jesus in Jerusalem caught his enemies off guard, leading them to falsely believe he had secured Roman protection through Philip. (156:6.6) (162:1.7)
improvedDescription: Philip the Tetrarch was the brother of Herod Antipas who ruled over territories including Caesarea Philippi. Unlike his brother, Philip became a reluctant believer in Jesus and allowed him to minister freely in his domain, even sending word that Jesus was welcome to live and work there.

name: Philistines
originalDescription: Abraham made converts among the Philistines, becoming a revered spiritual leader in Palestine and leaving a legacy of faith in God. (93:9.4) Abraham and Lot chose a hilly fastness near Salem to defend against constantly raiding Philistines. (93:5.6) The Philistines' victory over Saul at Gilboa diminished Yahweh's standing among the Canaanite gods. (97:9.6) During their stay in Joppa, Jesus met Gadiah, a Philistine interpreter who worked for Simon the tanner, bringing great and liberating enlightenment through their discussions on good and evil. (130:1.1,5) The Hebrews learned the alphabet from Philistines, leading to a delay in the development of their written language until about 900 B.C. (74:8.9) David built up a divine kingdom through defeating the Philistines and assuming collection of caravan tariffs. (97:9.11-15) The Hebrews learned the alphabet from the Philistine political refugees from Crete, resulting in various creation stories circulating before turning to the Mesopotamian version post-Babylonian captivity. (74:8.9)
improvedDescription: The Philistines were a people who frequently raided Palestine and engaged in conflicts with the Hebrews. Abraham made converts among them and was held in reverence as a spiritual leader. The Hebrews learned the alphabet from Philistine political refugees from Crete, and David later defeated them to establish his kingdom and gain control of caravan trade routes.

name: Philo of Alexandria
originalDescription: Philo of Alexandria, a wealthy Jew, restored the concept of the Trinity and possibly glimpsed Thought Adjusters; his teachings on the atonement doctrine influenced Paul but were ultimately eliminated due to inconsistencies in combining Greek, Roman, and Hebrew theology. (121:6.5-6) Philo of Alexandria possessed a copy of the Book of Wisdom, embodying the teachings that earthly riches are fleeting, fear should be avoided, and one should focus on the acts of God over the words of men. (95:4.3) Throughout various regions, including among older Jewish teachers, Plato, Philo of Alexandria, and the Essenes, there was a tolerated belief in reincarnation. (164:3.4) accomplishments
improvedDescription: Philo of Alexandria was a wealthy Jewish philosopher who attempted to harmonize Greek philosophy with Hebrew theology. His teachings influenced Paul's Christian theology, particularly in restoring concepts of the Trinity, though Paul wisely eliminated many of Philo's inconsistencies. Philo was considered one of the greatest teachers of his era, ranking alongside figures like Moses and Buddha in his influence on ethical and religious thought.

name: philosopher in Ephesus
originalDescription: The philosopher in Ephesus engaged in profitable discussions with Jesus about the concept of the soul, defining it as the self-reflective, truth-discerning, and spirit-perceiving part of man that elevates humans above the animal world. (133:6.4-7)
improvedDescription: A learned Greek philosopher in Ephesus who engaged in profitable discussions with Jesus about the nature of the soul. Through their conversations, Jesus explained that the soul is the self-reflective, truth-discerning, and spirit-perceiving part of man that elevates humans above the animal world and represents the potential survival value of human experience.

name: philosopher’s stone
originalDescription: Belief in philosopher's stone led to mastery of metals, a cornerstone of scientific advancement rooted in ancient mythology. (88:6.5)
improvedDescription: A legendary substance sought by ancient alchemists that was believed to have the power to transmute base metals into gold. The belief in the philosopher's stone, though mythical, led to the mastery of metals and contributed to scientific advancement.

name: philosophy
originalDescription: philosophy and religion named philosophies purpose strengths
improvedDescription: Philosophy is the search for wisdom and the systematic study of fundamental questions about existence, knowledge, values, and reality. It serves as a bridge between science and religion, helping to coordinate ideas and unify human understanding. True philosophy seeks to integrate the insights of material science, moral reasoning, and spiritual experience into a coherent worldview.

name: Phoenicians
originalDescription: Apostles won souls among the gentiles of Phoenicia, greatly cheered by their warm reception of Jesus' teachings. (155:4.1-2) (156:2.3) Beth-Marion, a Phoenician woman, became so fanatical that she almost drowned trying to walk on water and was sent away by her friends. (148:8.4) Phoenicians believed snakes to be the mouthpiece of evil spirits, leading to the reverence of reptiles in various cultures. (88:1.5) The Phoenicians employed crucifixion as punishment, a method later adopted by the Greeks and Romans, but not used on Roman citizens. (187:1.5) Jesus’ 18th appearance to believers at Tyre brought a message of salvation and the requirement to bear the fruits of the divine spirit. (193:2) The Phoenicians were known to have offered their first-born as sacrifice, a practice rooted in the ancient tradition of dedicating the first fruits to the spirits. (89:6.7) The influential Jews, Babylonians, Phoenicians, and Carthaginians were all part of an older Semitic race, strategically positioned for trade. (121:2.1) (156:3.2) The Phoenicians were a superior and well-organized group of mixed Semites, blending hereditary factors from almost all world races, ruling the western section of Palestine. (96:2.1) The Phoenicians considered swine sacred to them, while cows were revered by the Hindus. (89:1.5)
improvedDescription: The Phoenicians were a superior and well-organized group of mixed Semites who controlled the western Mediterranean coast of Palestine. They were skilled traders and seafarers who developed the alphabet system that the Hebrews later adopted. The Phoenicians practiced crucifixion as a form of execution and were among the peoples who received Jesus's teachings favorably during his ministry in their coastal regions.

name: photographers
originalDescription: Morontia recorders are like group photographers of transition worlds, preserving vital scenes and associations for future generations. (44:4.12)
improvedDescription: A term used metaphorically to describe morontia recorders who function like group photographers of the transition worlds, preserving vital scenes and associations of progressive epochs in the archives of morontia halls of records.

name: Phrygian mystery cults
originalDescription: The Phrygian cult of Cybele and Attis conducted imposing but degrading ceremonies, culminating in the bloody Black Friday festival to honor the death and resurrection of Attis. (98:4.3,7)
improvedDescription: The Phrygian cult of Cybele and Attis conducted imposing but degrading ceremonies, culminating in the bloody Black Friday festival to honor the death and resurrection of Attis.

name: phyla
originalDescription: Only fourteen phyla have appeared on Urantia, with fishes being the most recent, and no new classes have emerged post birds and mammals. (65:2.11)
improvedDescription: Only fourteen phyla have appeared on Urantia, with fishes being the most recent, and no new classes have emerged since birds and mammals.

name: phylacteries
originalDescription: Self-centered rulers make broad their phylacteries to seek honor from men while secretly exploiting widows and profiting from the temple. (175:1.9)
improvedDescription: Religious accessories worn by Jewish leaders that were broadened to display piety and seek honor from men, while the wearers secretly exploited widows and profited from the temple.

name: physical controllers
improvedDescription: Celestial beings who manipulate and control physical energies and forces throughout the universe.

name: physical energy
improvedDescription: The material force that underlies all physical phenomena and manifestations in the universe.

name: physical-energy manipulators
originalDescription: Physical-energy manipulators, celestial artisans, assist Morontia Power Supervisors in manipulating and controlling various phases of physical energy, knowledgeable about the three basic currents and thirty energy segregations of the superuniverses. (44:5.2)
improvedDescription: Physical-energy manipulators are celestial artisans who assist Morontia Power Supervisors in manipulating and controlling various phases of physical energy, knowledgeable about the three basic currents and thirty energy segregations of the superuniverses.

name: physical matter
improvedDescription: The tangible, material substance that constitutes the physical universe and all its manifestations.

name: physical nature
improvedDescription: The material or bodily aspects of existence, as distinguished from spiritual or mental aspects.

name: physicians
originalDescription: Elman, the Syrian physician, supervised Bethsaida hospital for four months, treating the sick with material methods and spiritual practices, with Jesus visiting them three times a week. (148:0.1) (148:2) Luke, the gentile convert of Paul, wrote a different Gospel about the life of Jesus, portraying him as “the friend of publicans and sinners.” (121:8.8) Physicians should refrain from despising material means of healing and consider alternative methods in curing human diseases. (164:3.15) Veronica suffered many things from many physicians, but found healing and wholeness in touching Jesus' garment. (152:0.2)
improvedDescription: Medical practitioners who treat the sick, including Elman the Syrian physician who supervised the Bethsaida hospital and Luke who wrote a Gospel about Jesus.

name: physics
originalDescription: Despite the power of mathematics and material science, they could not have predicted the miraculous creation of a water molecule. (12:9.3) The laws of physics may be immutable and unresponsive to training, but the endowment of mind allows for adaptation and learning beyond the limitations of chemistry. (65:6.8) Truth, beauty, and goodness are not inherent in physics or chemistry, so a machine cannot know or value them. (195:6.11)
improvedDescription: The science of matter and energy, whose laws are immutable but cannot predict emergent qualities or explain the concepts of truth, beauty, and goodness that transcend material phenomena.

name: piety
originalDescription: The ideal life is one of loving service, not mock piety or self-torture, in cooperation with the Adjuster. (110:3.4) The Son of Man embodied pious humility without sanctimoniousness, striking a perfect balance in his well-poised personality. (100:7.4) (149:4.4)
improvedDescription: True piety is characterized by genuine spiritual devotion without sanctimoniousness or self-torture. Jesus exemplified pious humility perfectly balanced with practical loving service, demonstrating that cooperation with the divine spirit requires sincere dedication rather than mock religiosity.

name: pigeons
originalDescription: Pigeons are descendants of enormous ancient reptiles, making their evolution a significant leap in prehuman history. (65:2.9) Joseph's fear of seeking work led to their small savings rapidly disappearing, prompting him to offer 2 pigeons for temple sacrifice. (122:10.2)
improvedDescription: Pigeons are descendants of enormous ancient reptiles, representing one of the greatest evolutionary leaps in prehuman history when reptiles became birds. Joseph offered two young pigeons as a temple sacrifice for Mary's purification, following the prescribed offering for the poor.

name: Pikes Peak
originalDescription: Pikes Peak stands as a lasting symbol of the ever-changing landscape in the North American Rocky Mountains. (60:4.4)
improvedDescription: Pikes Peak is an outstanding example of North American Rocky Mountain geological activity, representing remnants of original mountain ranges that were re-elevated after being leveled by erosion. The peak held its head above water during several prehistoric inundations, extending over multiple generations of mountain formation.

name: Pilate, Pontius
improvedDescription: Pontius Pilate was the Roman governor of Judea who presided over the trial and crucifixion of Jesus.

name: pilgrim guardians
originalDescription: Pilgrim Guardians are not assigned to specific duties in their association with the morontia progressors. These companions are responsible for the whole of the morontia career and are therefore the co-ordinators of the work of all other morontia and transition ministers. (48:3.7)
improvedDescription: Pilgrim Guardians are morontia beings responsible for the entire morontia career of ascending mortals, serving as coordinators of all other morontia and transition ministers rather than being assigned to specific duties.

name: pilgrim helpers
originalDescription: Pilgrim helpers on the seventh circle of Havona worlds teach ascenders Trinity, Father-Son partnership, and Infinite Spirit in intricate detail. (26:5.2) Pilgrim helpers on the seventh circuit of Havona serve ascending mortals by imparting essential lessons on the Trinity, Father-Son partnership, and the Infinite Spirit. (26:5)
improvedDescription: Pilgrim helpers are secondary supernaphim who function on the seventh circle of Havona worlds, teaching ascending mortals about the Paradise Trinity, the Father-Son partnership, and the Infinite Spirit through comprehensive educational programs. They are beings of quick understanding and broad sympathy who welcome space ascenders to the central universe.

name: pilgrim receivers and free associators
originalDescription: Pilgrim Receivers and Free Associators are the social companions of new mansion world arrivals, greeting them upon awakening and accompanying them throughout their journey in the morontia life. (48:3.8-9)
improvedDescription: Pilgrim Receivers and Free Associators are morontia companions who welcome new arrivals upon awakening on the mansion worlds and serve as social companions throughout the morontia journey. They remain with ascending mortals from initial resurrection until departure from the local universe as first-stage spirits.

name: pilgrimages
originalDescription: The making of pilgrimages to sacred shrines is an ancient ritual that has evolved into modern types of religious worship. (90:5.3)
improvedDescription: Pilgrimages to sacred shrines are ancient religious rituals that evolved into elaborate ceremonies of purification and sanctification, eventually developing into modern forms of religious worship and social ceremonials.

name: pilot
originalDescription: Mind is your ship, the Adjuster is our pilot, the human will is captain. (111:1.9)
improvedDescription: In the spiritual metaphor of human existence, the mind is the ship, the Thought Adjuster serves as the pilot, and the human will acts as captain. The divine pilot guides the ascending soul toward eternal survival when trusted by the mortal vessel's master.

name: pilot light
originalDescription: The pilot light of the Adjuster's spirit luminosity is a universal beacon of divine presence. (107:4.5)
improvedDescription: The pilot light refers to the characteristic spirit luminosity that accompanies the divine presence of Thought Adjusters. This Paradise luminosity is known by different names throughout the universes and represents the divine light that illuminates every person.

name: Pindar
originalDescription: Pindar and other Greek poets aimed to reform Greek religion, but ultimately fell short in developing a sustainable method for preserving moral principles. (98:2.5)
improvedDescription: A Greek poet of the fifth and sixth centuries who, along with other poets, attempted to reform Greek religion by elevating its ideals, but failed to develop a technique for fostering and conserving supreme values.

name: pines
originalDescription: During the Jurasic age, the pine forests began to replace fern forests as part of an eventful and active biological transition towards modern tree varieties. (60:2.7) (60:4.5)
improvedDescription: Coniferous trees that became more like present-day varieties during the Jurassic age, largely replacing fern forests as part of an eventful biological transition toward modern tree varieties.

name: pint
originalDescription: Inherent capacities cannot be exceeded, a pint can never hold a quart, just like the spirit concept cannot be forced into a material memory mold. (48:7.5)
improvedDescription: A unit of measurement used metaphorically to illustrate that inherent capacities cannot be exceeded, just as a spirit concept cannot be mechanically forced into a material memory mold.

name: pirates
originalDescription: During Jesus' time, the Mediterranean seas were free of pirates, paving the way for a flourishing era of trade and travel. (121:1.7)
improvedDescription: Maritime raiders who were cleared from the Mediterranean seas by Jesus' time, contributing to a great era of trade and travel throughout the unified Roman Empire.

name: Pisidia
originalDescription: Luke, the physician of Antioch in Pisidia, wrote the Gospel in A.D. 82, portraying Jesus as the friend of sinners. (121:8.8)
improvedDescription: A region in Asia Minor where Antioch was located, the home of Luke the physician who wrote his Gospel in A.D. 82 after learning about Jesus from Paul and others.

name: Pitcairn experiment
originalDescription: The successful blending of white and Polynesian races in the Pitcairn experiment demonstrated the potential for creating new and biologically effective characteristics. (82:6.9)
improvedDescription: A successful racial blending of white men and Polynesian women that demonstrated the potential for creating new and biologically effective characteristics when good racial strains intermix.

name: pituitary glands
originalDescription: Imagination and spiritual receptivity are influenced by the organization of certain glands comparable to the pituitary bodies, as described in the spirit-reception series. (49:5.19)
improvedDescription: Endocrine glands that vary in number among different mortal races across worlds, with their chemical organization significantly influencing inherent imagination and spiritual receptivity.

name: pity
improvedDescription: An emotional response involving compassion for another's suffering or misfortune.

name: placation
originalDescription: The evolution of religious observances progressed from ghost placation to spirit coercion and supplication, culminating in sacrifice, atonement, and redemption. (87:2.1) (89:4.4) (90:0.1)
improvedDescription: An early religious practice of appeasing ghosts and spirits to avoid their displeasure, which evolved from defensive worship techniques into more complex forms of religious ritual including coercion, propitiation, and eventually sacrifice and redemption.

name: Planetary Adams
improvedDescription: Material Sons who serve as biological uplifters on evolutionary worlds, working to improve the genetic heritage of mortal races.

name: planetary commission of seventy
originalDescription: The Edentia group approved the planetary life-initiation plans proposed by the advisory counselors of the Life Carriers. (57:8.9)
improvedDescription: A commission of seventy beings on Edentia that approved the planetary life-initiation plans for Urantia proposed by the Life Carriers.

name: planetary council on art and science
improvedDescription: A specialized council that was part of the Caligastia one hundred, dedicated to advancing artistic and scientific development on evolutionary worlds.

name: planetary custodians
originalDescription: Seventy guardians hold supreme morontia authority over the morontia spheres, guiding ascending creatures through their transformative journeys without the need for unconsciousness. (48:2.18) Planetary Custodians are registered in Uversa personality register. (30:2.111) The Planetary Custodians, as the local council of supreme morontia authority, grant morontia forms to all ascending creatures on the spheres, enabling them to progress through the phases of morontia life without surrendering consciousness. (48:2.18)
improvedDescription: Seventy guardians on each morontia world who constitute the local council of supreme morontia authority, granting material forms to ascending creatures and authorizing transitions between morontia phases without loss of consciousness.

name: planetary epochs
originalDescription: First – pre-Planetary Prince era Second – post-Planetary Prince era Third – post-Adamic era Fourth – post-Magisterial Son era Fifth – post-Bestowal Son era Sixth – post-Teacher Son era dispensations epochs of planetary development immediate future light and life
improvedDescription: Seven successive ages of human development on evolutionary worlds, determined by the arrival of divine Sons: pre-Planetary Prince, post-Planetary Prince, post-Adamic, post-Magisterial Son, post-Bestowal Son, post-Teacher Son, and the era of light and life.

name: planetary helper seraphim
originalDescription: About one third of the planetary helper seraphim were lost in the Lucifer rebellion. (53:7.7) The fifth order of seraphim, the planetary helpers, play a crucial role in guiding the Adamic mission on Earth, exemplified by Solonia's reprimand of Adam and Eve for their deviation from the divine plan. (51:3.5) Planetary helper seraphim, numbering around 100,000, accompany Planetary Adams on evolutionary worlds, aiding in physical and intellectual upliftment. (39:5) (51:3.5) Upon the collapse of the Adamic regime, the majority of Urantia's planetary helpers were removed, leading to greater responsibility for the administrators, transition ministers, and guardians of destiny in the seraphic supervision of the planet. (39:5.2) Planetary helper seraphim reside in Jerusem circles alongside other orders of angels, with their records preserved in triplicate in the temple of records. (46:5.22) Solonia is chief of Urantia’s planetary helper seraphim, assisting in the physical and intellectual upliftment of the material races on the evolutionary worlds. (39:5) (51:3.5) types
improvedDescription: Seraphim assigned to accompany Planetary Adams on evolutionary worlds, typically numbering about 100,000, who assist in the physical and intellectual upliftment of material races and maintain headquarters on system capitals.

name: planetary physical types
improvedDescription: Classifications of mortals based on their physical characteristics and environmental adaptations across different planetary conditions and atmospheres.

name: Planetary Prince of Urantia
originalDescription: Caligastia was deposed as Planetary Prince of Urantia almost half a million years after the appearance of human will. (66) (179:2.3) (189:3.3) Immanuel’s commission that Michael return as Planetary Prince of Urantia, to graciously achieve what he repeatedly declined to do by power and might during the Lucifer rebellion. (120:2.2-3) The Planetary Prince's late arrival on Urantia was a unique variation from the usual procedure, occurring almost five hundred thousand years after the appearance of the six Sangik races. (65:4.8-9) Machiventa Melchizedek, as vicegerent Planetary Prince of Urantia, holds eternal authority to act on behalf of Michael. (45:4.16) (55:7.3) (93:10.5-6) (114:1.2-4) Michael is the proclaimed vicegerent Planetary Prince of Urantia, with authority to act in behalf of Michael, the actual Planetary Prince. (45:4.16) (114:0.8) (114:1.1) (136:3.1) (176:4.4)
improvedDescription: Originally Caligastia, who was deposed after joining the Lucifer rebellion; now Michael of Nebadon holds this title, with Machiventa Melchizedek serving as vicegerent Planetary Prince.

name: Planetary Princes
originalDescription: All Planetary Princes seek counsel from a varied staff, including Melchizedeks and four and twenty counselors. (50:2-4) Planetary Princes, assigned to all planets inhabited by mortals, govern diverse creatures in a vast and wonderful creation. (32:2.7) (50:0.1) Planetary Princes, belonging to the order of Lanonandek Sons, are specialized in service and commonly regarded as a distinct group among Local Universe Sons of God. (20:1.10) (50:0.1) Planetary Princes are commissioned by System Sovereigns upon Life Carrier report that will is functioning, serving as rulers with almost complete personal grasp and acting as custodians of legislative mandates and judicial verdicts in the local affairs of inhabited worlds. (35:9.5) (50:0.1-2) (51:2.1) (52:1.8) (52:3.1) The controllers and transformers collaborate with angels and other celestial beings to render Planetary Princes visible to mortals for communication and guidance. (50:2.7) During their early rule, Planetary Princes organize assistants and form a supreme council, governing autocratically. (50:2.3) The staff of a planetary prince typically consists of 1000 personalities, including beings from the Infinite Spirit and higher evolved mortals, with the potential to expand to 100,000 or more upon request to System Sovereigns. (50:2.4) Upon the inauguration of the era of light and life, the Planetary Princes are elevated to Planetary Sovereigns in recognition of their service and leadership through the epochs of planetary progress. (50:5.11) (51:7.2) (52:7.10) (55:1.1) (55:7.1) The Planetary Princes, invisible to mortals, serve as intermediary beings between the divine and the material worlds, providing a crucial link in the chain of evolving personalities. (45:5.3) (50:4.9) The Planetary Prince and his assistant brethren offer the closest personal connection to the Eternal Son for the beings of time and space. (50:1.1) No high trust deemed more sacred than that reposed in a Planetary Prince for the welfare and guidance of evolving mortals on a newly inhabited world. (67:1.3) The successful Planetary Princes, permanently attached to planets of original assignment, independently govern their realms to foster planetary civilization despite the coming and going of Paradise Sons and their dispensations. (50:5.1) In the ascension career, all three orders face the same intellectual and spiritual challenges with equal treatment under system administration and constellation overcontrol, where even the regimes of Planetary Princes are identical. (49:5.18) There are 10,234,601 secondary Lanonandeks in Nebadon, assigned as Planetary Princes. (35:8.5) The prince's corporeal staff organizes planetary schools at simple headquarters for the cream of evolutionary races to teach better ways to their people. (50:4) The Planetary Prince and his assistant brethren embody the closest earthly connection to the Eternal Son and Mother Spirit. (50:1.1) Planetary Princes are under Gabriel's jurisdiction and follow System Sovereign mandates. (50:2.1) Sympathetic rulership for planetary princes increases the risk of being led astray by the exaltation of their own minds. (50:1.2) During the time of Jesus' tomb, a personality likely sat in the seat of Caligastia in the system council of Planetary Princes on Jerusem. (188:3.14) Planetary princes' tribunals exhibit a highly fatherly and discretionary attitude in adjudicating matters across their domains. (50:2.5) The united administrations with Planetary Adams create a new and effective order of world administration before the first Magisterial Son concludes his mission. (51:7.2) arrival mission rebellion by Planetary Princes in Nebadon
improvedDescription: Secondary Lanonandek Sons who serve as rulers of evolutionary worlds, commissioned by System Sovereigns to guide primitive mortals through early civilization and prepare planets for subsequent divine Sons.

name: planetary sentinels
originalDescription: Midwayers patrol and guard the invisible spirit realm of the planet as planetary sentinels. (77:8.7)
improvedDescription: Midwayers who serve as guardians and observers of the invisible spirit realm of planets, patrolling and monitoring supernatural phenomena and communications.

name: Planetary Sovereigns
originalDescription: When a system achieves system light and life, Planetary Sovereigns join the system conclave, forming a self-governing administrative body subject to the Constellation Fathers' veto, with a system legislative assembly on the headquarters world and courts on each planet's capital. (55:8.1) Upon the inauguration of the era of light and life, the Planetary Princes are elevated to the position of Planetary Sovereigns, marking the culmination of their service and the beginning of a new order of world administration. (50:5.11) (51:7.2) (52:7.10) (55:1.1) (55:7.1) The Material Son and Daughter are the visible rulers, while the Prince-Sovereign is only seen in the morontia temple, making them the true heads of the planetary regime. (55:7.2)
improvedDescription: The elevated status of Planetary Princes upon the inauguration of the era of light and life, marking their advancement to supreme planetary authority after successfully guiding their worlds through all evolutionary epochs.

name: planets
originalDescription: All creatures inhabit material abodes, including the divine beings of Paradise. (11:0.1) (12:8.1) (14:2.1) (14:4.11) All mortal-inhabited planets are evolutionarily settled and stabilized in time and space as part of their spiritual progression. (32:3.2) (49:0.1) Ideal planets for intelligent life are cold accretion planets in good relationship to a sun. (15:6.14) Each inhabited evolutionary planet is ruled over by a Planetary Prince within a system governed by a System Sovereign. (15:2.3) (32:2.7) (50:0.2) If physical catastrophe dooms a planet, the dematerialization technique is used to evacuate the salvable population. (51:2.3) Powerful transformers insulate planets from energy streams, maintaining universal balance and adjusting energy levels as needed."(29:4.24) Evolutionary planets are the initial worlds of ascending mortal career, where you are joined with your divine Thought Adjuster on Urantia, your starting point, in a temporary union, with the reward of eternal unity awaiting those who sincerely run the race of time. (112:0.1) Although all planets in a local system exhibit physical similarities, each has its own unique array of plant and animal life due to the decisions made by the Life Carriers, whose actions are always guided by law and order. (49:1.3-4) God calls all planets by name, for he knows all things and his divine consciousness encompasses every aspect of creation. (1:5.3) (3:3.1-2) Governmental techniques of a universe cannot retard development. (55:11.6) Havona's immense planets are uniquely balanced and utilize antigravity for material and spiritual harmony. (14:3.6) The epoch of the planets extends from the appearance of the morontia temple to the settling of the system in light and life. (55:7) All mortal-inhabited worlds are evolutionary in origin and nature, serving as training schools for the ascendant life stage just ahead, categorized into local systems with limited evolutionary planets, where only mortals of survival status reside, following a strict decree of the Ancients of Days. (49) Mortals transported by seraphim never return to their native planets during the same dispensation. (39:4.15) (40:9.5) On each inhabited planet, only one bestowal Son will serve in the course of history. (20:5.4) Only planets in the main circuits of the superuniverse are assured of continuous survival, settled worlds destined to last through all future ages. (55:0.3) System Centers ensure efficient distribution of power to inhabited planets through the perfect coordination of material energies and regulation of physical power. (29:2.18) about series one, two, and three of planets see link about mortals | mortals:planetary types origins suitability for life
improvedDescription: Material spheres that orbit suns and serve as dwelling places for diverse orders of intelligent life throughout the universe. All mortal-inhabited worlds are evolutionary in origin and serve as training schools for ascending beings on their journey toward Paradise. Each inhabited planet is presided over by a Planetary Prince and governed within a local system under a System Sovereign, with ideal planets being cold accretion worlds in proper relationship to their sun.

name: plans
originalDescription: Man's evolving morontia soul permeated by truth, beauty, and goodness becomes indestructible if valued as God-consciousness, for what is begun in time will be finished in eternity - if worth finishing. (111:3.7) Learning self-government on the mansion worlds includes cooperating and planning with other, wiser beings to benefit all concerned and appreciate cosmic morality. (39:4.10) about God’s plan see link on divine plan | divine plan; will of God Validated plans of action are derived from positive decisions, while ideas and stereotypes serve as theoretical and accepted plans, respectively, in the formulation of a personal philosophy of religion. (101:7.1) Skill, not just ability, is key to the satisfaction of living, as overplanning can defeat its own purpose. (160:4.11) Suffer less by accepting your lot and making fewer personal plans concerning others. (48:6.36) Jesus' desire to see his family before leaving was thwarted by a conspiracy of events. (154:6.10)
improvedDescription: Theoretical or validated schemes for action that guide personal and cosmic development. While positive decisions represent validated plans of action, mere ideas serve as theoretical plans, and stereotypes function as unvalidated accepted plans. True satisfaction comes from skillful execution rather than overplanning, and spiritual growth involves learning to make fewer personal plans concerning others while faithfully performing one's duties.

name: plants
originalDescription: Association with plants instills patience, quiet, and peace, fostering an advanced culture of peace-loving agriculturalists. (68:5.9,11) The evolution of spore-bearing plants into seeds was a crucial advancement in plant development, increasing their reproductive potential and versatility. (59:5.22) (65:6.3) The appearance of apparently useless forms in plant evolution is a necessary step destined to pass in the grand scheme of planetary life metamorphosis. (36:2.17) Flowering plants suddenly emerged during the Cretaceous period, marking a significant evolution in the plant world. (60:3) Lower forms of plant life are wholly responsive to physical, chemical, and electrical environment, while higher forms demonstrate increasingly adjustive, creative, co-ordinative, and dominative abilities through the influence of the adjutant spirits. (65:6.7) The most significant evolutionary advancement in plants was the development of chlorophyll-making ability, followed by the transformation of the spore into the complex seed. (65:6.3) Throughout history, there was a superstition that women were better plant growers because they were seen as mothers. (84:3.6) Some tribes believed in nature spirits within charms made from plants once considered fetishes, which were also taboo as food, such as the apple for the Levantine peoples. (88:1.3) On morontia worlds, pure energy growth distinguishes their unique and varied vegetable life. (43:6.6) The closing epochs of the Permian era witnessed the near extinction of spore-bearing plants. (60:0.2) Plants were feared, then worshiped for their intoxicating liquors, believed to render one divine, even in modern times known as “spirits”. (85:2.1-2) a list of plants named in The Urantia Book is in the links | algae, seaweed; aloes; angiosperms; anise; apple; bacteria; barley; beeches; birches; breadfruit trees; cereals; citron; cocoa; conifers; cumin; ferns; fig tree; fungi; grapes; grass; Hydra; magnolias; maples; mint; mustard seed; myrrh; oaks; oats; palms; pines; redwoods; rice; seaweed; seeds; slime; stone lilies; sycamores; tulip trees; viruses; Volvox; walnuts; weeds; wheat
improvedDescription: Living organisms that played a crucial role in planetary evolution and human civilization development. The most significant evolutionary advances included the development of chlorophyll-making ability and the evolution from spores to complex seeds. Association with plants through agriculture fostered peace-loving cultures, instilling patience, quiet, and peace in contrast to the militant nature of hunting and herding societies.

name: plasm
improvedDescription: The fundamental life substance that contains the full potential for all future evolutionary developments and variations on inhabited worlds.

name: platinum
originalDescription: The Andonites excelled in hunting and tool-making, seeking out platinum much like modern humans search for gold and diamonds. (63:5.6)
improvedDescription: A precious metal that the early Andonites actively sought and valued, traveling great distances to find it much as modern humans journey to find gold and diamonds.

name: Plato
originalDescription: Plato, along with Socrates and Aristotle, taught that virtue is knowledge and that it is better to suffer injustice than to be guilty of it. (98:2.6) Plato, like Confucius, was not a religious teacher, but a philosopher. (92:5.12)
improvedDescription: Greek philosopher who, along with Socrates and Aristotle, taught that virtue is knowledge and that it is better to suffer injustice than to be guilty of it. Like Confucius, Plato was a philosopher rather than a religious teacher, though his idealistic concepts significantly influenced later religious thought.

name: Platonism
originalDescription: Four great philosophies derived from Platonism dominated the gentile world. (121:4.1) The Fourth Book of Maccabees exemplifies the great inroad of Stoicism and the penetration of Platonic philosophy among Hellenized Jewish beliefs. (121:6.3) Christianity incorporated Babylonian and Persian ideas and Plato's theories, making the teachings more acceptable to diverse audiences. (146:1.3) The Platonist emphasis on the eternal order of heaven mirrors Confucianism's focus on the Way of Heaven over the potential God concept. (94:6.10) Hellenized Jewish beliefs, affected by Platonic philosophy and Stoic doctrines, were harmonized by Philo of Alexandria to form the foundation of Christianity. (121:6.3) Jesus' comments on Plato's teachings sought to lay a more trustworthy foundation for understanding reality in the universe. (130:4.1) (133:5.2) Jesus recommended using nature parables to teach truths, alluding to the natural as an unreal shadow of spirit realities, influenced by Greek idealism. (151:3.3) (170:5.2) Lingering belief in reincarnation was tolerated by older Jewish teachers, Plato, Philo, and many Essenes, who believed in expiating sins from past lives. (164:3.4)
improvedDescription: Greek philosophical system that profoundly influenced the gentile world and early Christianity through its emphasis on ideal spiritual patterns underlying material reality. Platonic concepts of the eternal order and the material world as shadows of spiritual realities were incorporated into Christian doctrine, making the teachings more acceptable to diverse audiences throughout the Mediterranean world.

name: play
improvedDescription: A form of recreation and spiritual recharging that continues throughout the ascending career from mortal worlds to Paradise. The principles of play provide philosophical soundness and serve as a basis for humor, diversion, and the recalling of past experiences that enrich present existence.

name: play builders
originalDescription: Celestial artisans construct divine edifices for the play builders, providing a suitable setting for the training of ascendant beings on the morontia worlds. (44:3.4)
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans who construct enormous edifices used during periods of rest and recreation on the morontia worlds, providing suitable settings for the training and spiritual recharging of ascendant beings.

name: pleasures
originalDescription: Maturity substitutes higher meanings for personal pleasure, elevating loyalties to the utmost concepts of life situations and cosmic relations, while evil arises from making personal evaluations absolutes. (100:1.1) In the morontia world, there are countless spiritual realities that surpass and enhance the material pleasures familiar to mortals. (44:3.1) Recalling past experiences provides pleasures, allowing for the enjoyment of celestial humor and spiritual amusement throughout our ascending lives. (48:4.10,20) The desirability of pleasure (the satisfaction of happiness) requires man to navigate a world filled with the potential for pain and suffering. (3:5.14) physical pleasures
improvedDescription: Physical and spiritual satisfactions that serve legitimate purposes when properly balanced but become destructive when elevated to absolute values. While mortals are entitled to physical pleasures and human affections, spiritual maturity involves substituting higher meanings for personal pleasure and recognizing that lasting satisfaction comes from divine rather than material realities.

name: Pleistocene
originalDescription: The Pleistocene ice age occurred from 2 million to 100,000 years ago, shaping the landscape and climate of northern regions. (61:5-7)
improvedDescription: The Pleistocene ice age occurred from 2 million to 100,000 years ago, shaping the landscape and climate of northern regions.

name: Pliocene
originalDescription: The Pliocene marked a mammal migration era, spanning 10 to 1 million years ago, during which significant geological changes occurred on a global scale, shaping the land and facilitating the exchange of animal life between continents. (61:4)
improvedDescription: The Pliocene era, spanning 10 to 1 million years ago, was marked by preglacial land elevation in North America, Europe, and Asia, with significant topographical changes including the birth of mountain ranges, altered stream courses, and volcanic activity worldwide.

name: plough
originalDescription: To be ordained, one must forsake all; as with the plough, one cannot turn back once committed. (163:2.3)
improvedDescription: A farming implement used metaphorically by Jesus to illustrate the commitment required for discipleship, teaching that one cannot turn back once they have put their hand to the plough and committed to serving the kingdom.

name: plumbing
originalDescription: Highly skilled Andite emigrants to Crete used plumbing approximately 12,000 B.C. (80:7.2)
improvedDescription: An advanced technology used by skilled Andite emigrants who migrated to Crete around 12,000 B.C., demonstrating their superior craftsmanship alongside textiles, metals, pottery, and stone building.

name: poetry
originalDescription: Only a poet can discern poetry in the commonplace prose of routine existence. (48:7.22) Poetry is mortal morontia, serving as man's attempt to transcend material constraints and embrace spiritual truths. (195:7.15) Without incantations, poetry would not have originated in religion, handicapping the cultural development of society. (92:3.6) Rhythmic poetry can relieve profound philosophy with restfulness, balancing the strain of living and feelings of insecurity through faith contemplation and contemplation of the Father. (143:7.3)
improvedDescription: A form of artistic expression that serves as mortal morontia, representing humanity's attempt to transcend material realities and embrace spiritual values. Poetry can discern beauty in commonplace existence and provides rhythmic relief to profound philosophy, balancing the strain of living through its connection to spiritual contemplation.

name: poets
originalDescription: Celestial artisans, rhythm recorders, transcend earthly poets, capturing beauty and harmony in rhythmic form. (44:4.11)
improvedDescription: Celestial artisans known as rhythm recorders who transcend earthly poets, creating rhythmic forms that capture beauty and harmony while increasing efficiency and pleasure for morontia and spirit beings.

name: pointing bow
originalDescription: Primitives thought much sickness was caused by the influence of enemies through bewitchment, the evil eye, and the magic pointing bow. (90:3.7)
improvedDescription: A magical weapon believed by primitives to cause sickness and death through bewitchment, along with the evil eye, making it dangerous and ill-mannered to point at someone.

name: poise
originalDescription: Believers, emancipated from haste and painful stress, display a tranquil stability and a unique originality in their lives infused with the presence of the Eternal. (102:2.3) Faith maintains mysterious poise in the face of physical suffering and injustice. (101:3.8-9)
improvedDescription: A quality of tranquil stability and mysterious composure displayed by believers, characterized by emancipation from stress and the ability to maintain tranquility despite physical suffering, injustice, and life's vicissitudes.

name: poisons
originalDescription: Poisons and intoxicants were revered as fetishes, believed to be possessed. (88:1.9) In ancient times, crime was detected in ordeals of poison, fire, and pain, as medicine men and priests acted as the first crime detectors and officers of the law through these crude techniques of arbitration. (70:10.5-6) Mental poisons hinder spiritual progress just as physical poisons impede the efforts of the Adjuster. (110:1.5) Deliberately polluting the physical body with poisons hinders the efforts of the Adjuster to elevate the material mind and spiritual progress of the soul. (110:1.5) Poisoned weapons were used very early in history, alongside various mutilations and demands. (70:1.18) Unscrupulous rulers gained great power through the discovery and use of poison, but their rule was always subject to the threat of assassination and rebellion. (70:6.6) Modern physicians still employ the wisdom of the serpent as a symbol, inherited from ancient snake cults whose members became genuine venom addicts. (85:3.3)
improvedDescription: Substances that were revered as fetishes in ancient times and used in primitive ordeals for crime detection, early warfare with poisoned weapons, and court intrigue. Both physical and mental poisons hinder the Thought Adjuster's efforts to elevate the material mind and impede spiritual progress of the soul.

name: polarity
originalDescription: Ministering spirits form complemental relationships to function in pairs for synchronized action in specific circuits. (26:1.16)
improvedDescription: A spiritual characteristic that requires certain ministering spirits to work in complemental pairs to function effectively, enabling them to synchronize with specific spiritual circuits and perform coordinated actions.

name: Pole Star
originalDescription: The sloping entrance of the great pyramid pointed to the Pole Star, guiding the soul of the king to the eternal constellations of the fixed stars. (95:2.7)
improvedDescription: The North Star that served as a celestial guide in ancient Egyptian beliefs, where the sloping entrance passage of the great pyramid pointed directly toward it so that the soul of the dead king could travel straight to the stationary constellations of the fixed stars.

name: poles, magnetic
improvedDescription: The magnetic poles of a planet or celestial body, relating to the planet's magnetic field properties.

name: poles of space communication
originalDescription: The personal abodes of the Ancients of Days are the point of spiritual polarity within their administrative sectors on Uversa. (18:3.6) On Urantia, the archangels' circuit operated for the first time, signaling the resurrection of the dead and the departure of seraphim for the mansion worlds. (62:7.2) (189:3.2) Spiritual reflection of the characteristics of the Trinity is achieved through the unique nature of the seven Reflective Spirits assigned to each superuniverse, each reflecting a different combination of divinity characteristics. (17:3.1) Reports from space are received at noon at the designated headquarters, while seraphic transports depart from there at midnight for interplanetary transfers. (39:5.13-15)
improvedDescription: Designated planetary locations that serve as communication centers for celestial beings and interplanetary transport, functioning as the spiritual polarity points where space reports are received at noon and seraphic transports depart at midnight.

name: police
originalDescription: Shamans, as the first crime detectors and officers of the law, administered justice through ordeals of poison, fire, and pain based on ancient beliefs in ghosts. (70:10.5) Secret societies, acting as night police and practicing lynch law, played a key role in shaping social hierarchies through secretive rituals and control over information. (70:7.15-16) Secret societies enforced oaths, promoted trust, and cultivated secrecy, while also intimidating and regulating the masses, functioning as vigilante groups, spies in times of conflict, and secret police in times of peace, ultimately keeping tyrannical kings in check. (70:7.16) A united few police can restrain a mob, demonstrating the power of organized cooperation over individual strength. (68:1.4) State police forces in continental nations recruit unmarried men aged 25 to 50 and are funded through bachelor taxes, with no municipally appointed peace officers. (72:7.5) Police regulations ensure freedom from violence for society's internal peace and preservation. (70:9.5) (81:5.5) Lasting peace on Urantia requires intelligent surrender of sovereign powers to a mankind government, as only world-wide confederations can effectively prevent minor wars and control smaller nations. (134:5.10) Religion, as the moral police force of all time, fostered civilization, promoted art and knowledge, and drove humanity forward to higher levels of reason and wisdom. (92:3.9)
improvedDescription: Law enforcement organizations that evolved from primitive shamans and secret societies into organized state forces responsible for maintaining internal peace, preventing violence, and preserving social order through organized cooperation and regulation.

name: politics
improvedDescription: The activities, processes, and structures related to governance and the exercise of authority in organized societies.

name: polyandry
improvedDescription: A form of marriage in which one woman has multiple husbands simultaneously, practiced among some primitive societies.

name: polygamy
improvedDescription: A marriage system in which one person has multiple spouses simultaneously, including both polygyny and polyandry.

name: polygyny
improvedDescription: A form of marriage in which one man has multiple wives simultaneously, commonly practiced among primitive peoples.

name: Polynesia
originalDescription: Biologically modified Andite sailors from Japan reached South America via the Pacific, establishing the ancestry of the Incas through intermarriage with Andean natives. (78:5.7) In the days of Andites, the islands of Polynesia were more numerous and larger than they are now, as evidenced by a fleet of 132 sailors who traveled from Japan to South America, intermarrying with Andean natives and laying the foundation for the Inca rulers. (78:5.7) Polynesia's racial composition includes a mix of green, indigo, yellow, and red blood, with influences from Andonites and Japanese ancestors as well. (79:6.3)
improvedDescription: A Pacific Ocean region consisting of numerous islands that were more extensive in ancient times, inhabited by racially diverse peoples including descendants of Andite sailors who traveled from Japan and established flourishing centers of civilization.

name: polytheism
originalDescription: The commandment in Dalamatia against polytheism: “You shall not fear nor serve any God but the Father of all.” (66:7.9) Polytheism is monotheism in the making, leading humanity towards a lasting peace and profound assurance through the concept of a transcendent and divine deity. (5:4.2,9)
improvedDescription: The worship of multiple gods, which represents an evolutionary stage in religious development that serves as a transitional step toward monotheism and the eventual recognition of one universal Father.

name: Pontius Pilate
originalDescription: Pilate, hoping to buy time and mend his strained relationship with Herod, sent Jesus to appear before him in Galilee amidst their animosity. (185:3.8) Pontius Pilate was greatly disliked by the Jews for using temple funds to build an aqueduct, sparking numerous riots and bloodshed. (185:1.5) The court reconvened at 5:30, presenting charges to Pilate claiming blasphemy held no weight, with Jesus guarded by a Roman soldier and temple guards as Annas emphasized the futility to his associates, while Judas remained silent. (184:5.1) Jews’ lack of respect for Pilate's authority led to his disfavor among them, as seen in his hesitant response to their demands and his ultimate surrender to their protests. (185:1.3-4) (185:2.1-5) In his last appeal to the mob, Pilate sought pity for Jesus, but the unfeeling hearts of the crowd remained determined to crucify him. (185:6) personal characteristics political position Jesus and Pilate tragic end
improvedDescription: Roman procurator who governed Judea, Samaria, and Idumea for ten years under Tiberius Caesar. Known as a moral coward and weak administrator who feared the Jewish leaders due to past confrontations over religious matters. He presided over Jesus' trial and reluctantly ordered his crucifixion despite finding no fault in him, later falling into disfavor and committing suicide.

name: pool of Bethesda
originalDescription: Healing at the pool of Bethesda moved many to believe in the gospel of the kingdom. (147:3)
improvedDescription: A natural hot spring in Jerusalem with reddish-tinged water surrounded by five porches where many sick people gathered seeking healing. The pool's periodic bubbling, caused by underground gas accumulations, was believed to have supernatural healing properties.

name: poor
originalDescription: The poor are always with us, so we must minister to them whenever it seems good to us. (172:1.6) The gospel is preached to all, regardless of status, as seen in the greater numbers of the poor accepting it readily. (143:1.5) (166:4.3) Jesus blessed the sincere and pious poor, condemning the wanton and irreligious rich. (196:2.8) Minister to and share with the poor to let the oppressed go free and break every yoke, as Jesus taught. (147:8.3) (167:1.5) (192:2.2)
improvedDescription: Those lacking material wealth who Jesus blessed because they were usually sincere and pious, and who readily accepted the gospel of the kingdom. Jesus taught that followers should minister to and share with the poor, noting that 'the poor are always with us.'

name: poor in spirit
improvedDescription: A spiritual condition of humility and recognition of one's need for God, representing those who are spiritually receptive and open to divine guidance rather than self-reliant or proud.

name: poor man falsely accused
originalDescription: Jesus interceded before the Roman magistrate on behalf of a poor man falsely accused, which led to the accused's release. (132:4.8)
improvedDescription: An unnamed individual whom Jesus defended before a Roman magistrate, making a powerful speech about justice and the sacred duty to protect even the humblest citizens. The man was subsequently released due to Jesus' intervention.

name: populace
originalDescription: A well-trained police force can effectively control a larger group of people through organized cooperation. (68:1.4) Beware of the fickleness of the populace, as they can quickly shift their admiration from a hero to a prisoner in the eyes of Jesus. (185:5.5) The populace clamored for the release of Barabbas, choosing a murderer over Jesus. (185:5.4-11) The populace craved promises of salvation and immortality, forsaking Greek philosophy for the comfort of a personal God and mystery cults. (98:2.10) (98:4.1) The populace discerned the insincerity of Jewish rulers, leading to fear and collaboration against Jesus. (173:2.8) The shallow welcome of the festive multitude did not indicate a genuine belief, as they were easily led and unthinking. (172:3.15) (185:6.1) In modern times, institutional religion must realize that true religion is the only power to bridge the gap between social groups, as the lower orders are no longer as ignorant or politically helpless. (99:1.5) The free proletariat were seen as worthless by the upper classes, only useful for breeding. (121:3.5) The Sanhedrin feared the open revolt of the populace, as they greatly dreaded the multitudes' approval of Jesus. (172:3.12-14) (172:4.1) (173:1.9) (173:2.1) (173:4.5) (174:2.1) Jesus
improvedDescription: The common people or masses, often described as easily led and unthinking. In Jerusalem, they initially welcomed Jesus but later, influenced by religious leaders, clamored for his crucifixion and the release of Barabbas during his trial before Pilate.

name: population
originalDescription: Recognizing the necessity for producing large numbers of mortals, nations should strive for optimum population stabilization to enhance culture and prevent war. (81:6.12) 500 million primitive human beings populated Earth at the arrival of Caligastia, the Planetary Prince, about five hundred thousand years ago. (66:0.2) In the era of light and life, the population remains stationary, with reproduction regulated for planetary requirements and genetic quality, ensuring the continued evolution of a magnificent race. (55:6.3) The population fluctuates in direct relation to land resources and in reverse proportion to the prevailing standard of living in a determining law governing human society. (68:6.3) overpopulation
improvedDescription: The number of inhabitants in a given area, governed by the law that it varies directly with land resources and inversely with standard of living. Optimum population stabilization enhances culture and prevents war, while overpopulation can lead to social problems and conflict.

name: pork
improvedDescription: Meat from swine, which was considered unclean and forbidden under Jewish dietary laws, making it a significant cultural and religious issue in Jesus' time.

name: Porogia
originalDescription: Porogia is an evolutionary and progressive system located immediately neighboring Satania in the constellation of Norlatiadek. (41:2.1)
improvedDescription: One of the local systems neighboring Satania in the constellation of Norlatiadek. Like other systems in the constellation, Porogia is evolutionary and progressive in nature.

name: Porphyreon
originalDescription: Town through which Jesus passed with the twelve during their flight north on May 22 and August 4, 29 (156:3.1)
improvedDescription: A coastal town where Jesus and the apostles stayed during their ministry in the Phoenician region. The gentile population there received them well, with many accepting the gospel message during their week of teaching.

name: porpoises
originalDescription: Placental mammals that returned to sea became the modern whales, dolphins, porpoises, seals, and sea lions. (61:2.11)
improvedDescription: Marine mammals that evolved from placental mammals that returned to the sea, along with whales, dolphins, seals, and sea lions.

name: Porshunta
originalDescription: Porshunta, the master mind of the extinct orange race, led a great revival of higher living at Armageddon as the oracle of “The Great Teacher.” (45:4.7) (64:6.12) Porshunta is one of 24 counselors for “The Great Teacher” worshiped by the extinct orange race she leads as their oracle. (45:4.7)
improvedDescription: Oracle and master mind of the extinct orange race who led a great revival of higher living at Armageddon some three hundred thousand years ago and serves as one of 24 counselors in the worship of "The Great Teacher."

name: Portalon
originalDescription: Portalon is a neighboring universe to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton. (32:2.12)
improvedDescription: A neighboring universe to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton.

name: porters
originalDescription: The ruler of the blue race once had 100,000 men in his colony of burden bearers. (81:2.12)
improvedDescription: Human burden bearers used for carrying loads, with one ruler of the blue race having had a colony of one hundred thousand such workers.

name: position
improvedDescription: A location, status, or role held by an individual or entity.

name: positive
originalDescription: Faith, being positive and living, always triumphs over doubt and fear, as the positive has the advantage over the negative. (102:6.7) Jesus’ religion was a positive, action-oriented, and loving practice based on the teachings of the gospel of the kingdom. (159:5)
improvedDescription: A constructive and affirmative approach to life and faith that always triumphs over doubt, fear, and negative thinking through spiritual certainty and action-oriented love.

name: possession
improvedDescription: The state of being controlled or owned by another entity, whether material ownership or spiritual domination.

name: possessions
originalDescription: Disciples are required to renounce all they have and dedicate themselves completely to be worthy of following Jesus. (171:2.4) The possessions held in common by the early church led to disaster. (194:4.2,7) A man’s life consists not in abundance of material possessions, but in the joys of spiritual realities and eternal life. (165:4.1) The ancient belief that possessions are a spiritual handicap influenced European philosophy for centuries. (89:3.2) God's gifts of eternal reality are bestowed upon those who consecrate their will to doing the Father's will, becoming more than they are as they approach God through love. (117:4.14)
improvedDescription: Material goods and property owned by individuals, which disciples must renounce to follow Jesus, as true life consists not in abundance of things but in spiritual realities and eternal values.

name: post-Adamic era
improvedDescription: The planetary epoch that follows the arrival and ministry of the Adamic Sons, representing an advanced stage of planetary spiritual and biological development.

name: post-Bestowal Son era
improvedDescription: The planetary epoch that follows the completion of a Creator Son's bestowal mission, marking an advanced stage of spiritual enlightenment and planetary progress.

name: post-Magisterial Son era
improvedDescription: A planetary epoch that follows the dispensation of a Magisterial Son, representing an advanced stage in a world's spiritual and administrative development.

name: post-Planetary Prince era
improvedDescription: A planetary epoch that follows the dispensation of a Planetary Prince, marking the transition to a more advanced stage of world administration and development.

name: post-Teacher Son era
improvedDescription: A planetary epoch that follows the dispensation of Trinity Teacher Sons, representing the final stage of a world's evolutionary development before the age of light and life.

name: postulates
improvedDescription: Basic assumptions or principles taken as given without proof, serving as foundational premises for theoretical frameworks or logical reasoning.

name: Potential
originalDescription: The Potential is that which is becoming and will be, while the Actual is that which was and is. (115:3.11)
improvedDescription: That which is becoming and will be, representing the unrealized possibilities inherent in existence, contrasted with the Actual which was and is.

name: potentials
originalDescription: The Father, Son, and Spirit are eternal, while all other Deity personalities have an origin but are eternal in destiny, with the Paradise Trinity as the only Deity reality embracing infinity. (0:7.5) (0:12.2) The potential for personality is always supreme over actuality in the evolving cosmos. (102:5.1) The completed Trinity Absolute will achieve final expression, consummating the absolute actualization of all potentials in the creations of infinity. (106:6.3) The transmutation of static potentials into actuals is driven by the influence of transcendental agencies and the actions of the Paradise Trinity, ultimately leading to the continual cosmic evolution of the grand universe. (118:4.3) The potential of personality is the unfolding of the purposive mandates of Deity, always supreme over the actual in the evolving cosmos. (102:5.1)
improvedDescription: Unrealized possibilities that exist within the Deity Absolutes and cosmic reality, which become actualized through the influence of transcendental agencies and the actions of the Paradise Trinity. In evolving universes, potential is always supreme over the actual, representing what is to be as the unfolding of Deity's purposive mandates.

name: potholes
originalDescription: Glaciers alone leave distinctive evidence such as potholes in connection with no other terrestrial phenomenon. (61:7.1)
improvedDescription: Distinctive geological features created by glacial action, representing one of the characteristic pieces of evidence left by ice movement that distinguishes glacial phenomena from other terrestrial activities.

name: potters
originalDescription: The first group specialists were potters who made plain or fancy pottery, while women or men in different tribes specialized in sewing and weaving. (69:3.10) Before Jesus was eight years old, he was known to all in Nazareth, especially the mothers and young women, who often saw him at the spring near his home, one of the town's social hubs. (123:5.15)
improvedDescription: Craftspeople who specialized in making pottery, representing some of the first industrial specialists in human society. Women typically made plain pottery while men created fancy pottery, though these roles varied among different tribes.

name: pottery
originalDescription: Highly skilled Cretan Andites made pottery ca. 12,000 B.C. (80:7.2) The Danubians were Andonites who made pottery, tilled the land, and preferred living in valleys, but their tribes deteriorated as they moved away from their cultural center. (80:8.4) Early commerce in pottery helped drive the growth of urban civilizations across the ancient world. (79:1.4) (80:1.2) The garden Adamites excelled in pottery making, contributing to the foundations of modern art, science, and literature. (76:3.8) The origination of the idea for pottery arose from the accidental occurrences of daily life among early peoples, leading to the revival of the art during Adam’s time. (81:2.18-20) The Sumerians appeared suddenly in Mesopotamia with a full-grown culture including pottery and writing systems that had their origin two hundred thousand years ago. (77:4.7) (78:8.2) Pottery and decorative arts were greatly advanced, taught by Prince’s staff in Dalamatia. (66:5.24)
improvedDescription: Clay vessels and decorative objects that were among humanity's earliest manufactured goods, first introduced by the Prince's staff about 500,000 years ago and later revived during Adam's time. Pottery making became a significant cultural indicator and trade commodity, spreading across civilizations and helping drive urban development.

name: poultry
originalDescription: The cock's crow marked Peter's denial of Jesus, a moment etched in Peter's memory forever. (181:2.29) (184:2.8) Roosters atop weather vanes serve as ghost-cult symbols, warding off spirits with their crowing. (87:6.11) Peter realized his denial of Jesus when he heard a rooster crow in the courtyard of Annas' palace outside Jerusalem.
