improvedDescription: Urban drainage systems that were flushed by tides in ancient cities like Caesarea, where Jesus and his companions stayed and Ganid learned about the city's water management techniques.

name: sex
originalDescription: Proper sex relations have always involved the element of choice and co-operation by woman, giving intelligent women considerable influence, despite societal constraints. (84:4.3) The mansion worlds offer ample opportunities for compensating experiential deprivations, except for those concerning the discipline of sex relationships. (45:6.3) Life on nonbreather worlds differs radically from Urantia, especially in terms of living functions, including reproduction. (49:3.4) Free love has never been in good standing above the scale of rank savagery (82:2.3) Women have higher sex standards as society evolves, due to suffering more consequences of sex mores transgression, while men's standards improve slowly out of fairness demanded by civilization, with nature making only women suffer childbirth pangs. (84:5.2) Individuals always rebel against society's regulations on sex, causing an ongoing struggle between personal desires and social expectations in the realms of marriage. (82:3.2) Children in the probation nursery of Satania are cared for by morontia personalities, where offspring of surviving mortals who perished before acquiring spiritual status are reassembled, ensuring their repersonalization on the finaliters' world without sex differentiation or mortal reproduction. (45:6.7) The regulation of sex in relation to marriage reflects cultural advancement and genetic heritage. (82:1.3-5) Reflective worship and prayer of thanksgiving offer a healthier spiritual attitude compared to mystic experiences induced by sex impulses. (100:5.10) Sex jealousy is not innate; it is a product of evolving mores that stem from guarding property and protecting lineage. (82:4.4) The social group is entitled to regulate sex, as part of the government's mission to enforce equality and protect individual and group rights. (81:5.6) historically sex urge
improvedDescription: The biological and social aspects of gender relations that have always involved choice and cooperation, serving as both a civilizing force that compels altruistic behavior and a source of ongoing social regulation throughout human evolution.

name: sexes
originalDescription: Men and women are complementary though often personally antagonistic, yet they need each other throughout their mortal, morontial, and spiritual journeys. (84:6.6-7) The differences between the sexes multiply versatility and overcome limitations, benefiting mankind individually and collectively. (84:6.5) Gender is not a factor when Adjusters volunteer for assignment to spiritualize and eternalize mortal personalities. (108:1.7) Throughout history, man has selfishly chosen the more agreeable work, while woman has always been the burden bearer, carrying out real production. (69:3.3) (84:3.6,7,10) The sexes struggle to fully comprehend each other, with deep-rooted historical distrust and incomprehension between man and woman, perpetuated through tribal and racial traditions and narratives that vilify women. (84:4.4) (84:6.3,7) Men and women are mutually dependent on co-operation throughout their morontial, spiritual, and mortal careers. (84:6.6) The partnership of male and female in marriage is essential for the foundation and maintenance of the home, reflecting the inherent differences and complementarity between the sexes. (84:6) Pastoral men shamefully mistreated women, reducing them to social slavery as the spread of animal husbandry changed the dynamics of gender roles, leading to a decline in women's status and dignity throughout the pastoral era of human existence. (68:5.8) (69:7.5) The innate biologic fact that men and women positively will not live without each other forever safeguards marriage and the resultant family. (83:0.2) The presence of a helpless baby differentiated early male-female activities and shaped the foundation of the home as woman maintained a settled residence for cultivation. (68:2.6) The respective personality trends of men and women continue to aid and complement each other throughout their immortal journey towards higher spiritual realms. (84:6.6) The personality gulf between men and women is inherent, yet the sex urge ensures their unity for species reproduction, despite their essentially different viewpoints and life reactions. (82:1.1) (84:5.13) (84:6.3,6) Women's natural love for babies led to their specialization in childcare, while men focused on hunting and fighting. (69:3.2) equality of men and women
improvedDescription: The complementary male and female variations of humanity that, despite their differences in viewpoint and personality trends, remain mutually dependent throughout their mortal, morontial, and spiritual careers, with their cooperation essential for civilization's continuation and reproduction of the species.

name: Shabattum
originalDescription: The Jews retained Mesopotamian taboos regarding the Sabbath, carrying them back to Palestine from Babylon. (95:1.2)
improvedDescription: The Babylonian name for the seventh day that was considered taboo-ridden and unlucky in ancient Mesopotamia, with its associated prohibitions later carried back to Palestine by the Jews and incorporated into their Sabbath observances.

name: shadows
originalDescription: Seek the true realities of the spirit and cease to be attracted by unreal and material shadows, for they shall not lead you astray. (174:0.2) The shadow, like the breath, was both feared and revered by early man, who also held their names in high regard for magical purposes and believed in the power of reflection and images to affect the soul. (86:5.14) (88:5.5) The shadow will always follow the substance, so devote yourselves to the work of the heavenly kingdom. (165:5.3) Shadows should be interpreted in terms of substance, not relied upon to fully comprehend actuality. (1:6.1) The shadow of matter, cast by mind in the presence of spirit, evolves into a reflection of divine enlightenment, underscoring the interconnected reality of mind, matter, and spirit in the journey towards divinity. (12:8.15-16) Building shadows once “walled” into new buildings substituted entombing living persons in walls. (89:6.5) Falsehood is the shadow of a hair’s turning for untrue purpose, a perversion of truth. (48:6.33) Weak men had shorter shadows for the Greeks, while others believed in various destinies for ghost souls. (86:4.8)
improvedDescription: Primitive humans feared and revered shadows as proof of a double self or soul, while philosophically they represent the material reflection cast by divine reality, serving as imperfect representations that should be interpreted in terms of their true spiritual substance.

name: shale
originalDescription: Pressure and folding has transformed shale into slate, altering the original character of the deposits in various regions worldwide. (59:1.16) Shale alternates with coal, stone, and conglomerate in coal-bearing strata, which vary in thickness from forty to fifty feet in central and eastern United States, but can reach 18,000 feet in some parts of North America and Europe. (59:5.15) Shale depositions in early Carboniferous age show similarities between Europe and North America, indicating an arid climate with erosion from violent cloudbursts. (59:1.12) (59:5.10) (60:1.1) (60:3.9) Shale and slate of dark colors in transition rock deposits testify to the existence of plant life ancestors during the Carboniferous age, with some containing organic carbon. (58:7.10) Limestone changed into shale over 300 million years ago during a period of land submergence. (59:3.1)
improvedDescription: A sedimentary rock formed from deposits in deeper, quieter waters that has been transformed by pressure and folding into slate in many regions, commonly found alternating with coal and limestone in various geological formations.

name: Shalmaneser III
originalDescription: Shalmaneser III's decision to control the Mediterranean coast altered the course of history, leading to wars and alliances that were left unrecorded in the Old Testament. (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: An Assyrian king whose decision to control the Mediterranean coast led to the battle of Karkar, where King Ahab of Ephraim and allied groups fought him to a draw, though this significant conflict went unrecorded in the Old Testament.

name: shamans
originalDescription: The medicine men and priests were always a powerful check on kings, restraining them even in the face of poison and assassination. (70:6.6) The majority of shamans believed in their spirit possession, leading to powerful shamanesses who communicated with the dead through cataleptic trances and professional dancing. (90:1.3) Enchanters' practices, derived from ancient superstitions, were classified as shamancraft along with witchcraft. (90:2.2) (150:3.11) Fetish men, directed by spirit ghosts, enforce taboos with such fear that even violations can result in death. (89:1.2) Shamans were believed to be the first police officers, as ghosts administered justice through them using crude techniques of arbitration such as ordeals of poison, fire, and pain. (70:10.5) The shamans were the pioneer professional class, exempted from physical work and differentiated based on religion, competing with smiths who were feared for their skills in working with metals and associated with beliefs in white and black magic. (69:3.5) Epileptics were often shamans, believed to be possessed by spirits. (88:1.9) Despite often being of low-grade mind and morals, shamans became wealthy by suppressing rivals and rising to positions of influence and power. (90:2.12) Shamans once stood between man and God, but evolution of religion led to the realization of an all-powerful love. (90:0.3) The shaman, as the ranking medicine man and ceremonial fetishman, held a central role in the practices of evolutionary religion, often surpassing the war chief and even functioning as a priest-king in some tribes. (90:1.1) Many shamans in history resorted to fraud and trickery to enhance their power and authority. (88:1.10) (90:1.4) Successful shamans were revered for their weather control abilities, while those who failed without alibi were either demoted or killed. (90:1.5) (90:2.6) Some early communities ruled by medicine men also served as chiefs and priests, their royal insignias often originating from priestly attire. (70:5.8) The early specialists in industry were the flint flakers and stone masons, followed by smiths, leading to group specialization with whole families and clans dedicating themselves to specific labor - such as the superstitious exaltation of a family of expert swordmakers, which led to the origin of one of the earliest castes of priests. (69:3.9) Shamans, terribly expensive, but worth all they cost, have played a crucial role in the development and preservation of civilization, despite the shortcomings of religion. (92:3.7-9) Religion evolved to require shamans and priests as mediators between ordinary mortals and the unresponsive spirit world. (90:0.2) Ancient shamans performed magic rituals unclothed, using wands, incantations, and “medicine” to conjure mystery, a tradition carried on by modern voodoo doctors. (88:6.1) Women, as custodians of home fires, were chosen as shamans and practiced magic through rituals and incantations. (69:6.5) (88:6.1)
improvedDescription: The earliest professional class of medicine men and spiritual intermediaries who served as priests, healers, and ceremonial leaders in primitive societies, often wielding significant political power despite frequently resorting to fraud and trickery to maintain their authority.

name: Shamash
originalDescription: Shamash was one of the chief Mesopotamian deities exalted by the Salem teachers alongside Bel and Ea. (95:1.4)
improvedDescription: One of the chief Mesopotamian deities that the Salem teachers elevated alongside Bel and Ea, later incorporated into the Babylonian triad as part of their effort to reduce the number of gods in ancient Mesopotamian religion.

name: shame
originalDescription: Do not be ashamed of Jesus and his words in this sinful generation; take up your responsibilities daily and follow him to gain eternal life. (158:7.5) Judas, influenced by the ridicule of his friends, was ashamed of Jesus and ultimately betrayed him. (172:5.13) Religion should be motivated by the revelation of God's fatherhood and the enhancement of human brotherhood, not by fear and shame as unworthy motivations for religious living. (140:4.7)
improvedDescription: A feeling of humiliation or distress caused by consciousness of wrong or foolish behavior. Religion warns against shame as an unworthy motivation for religious living, emphasizing that faith should be motivated by God's love rather than fear and shame.

name: Shang-ti
originalDescription: Shang-ti, the Taoist God of Heaven, was the focal point of Singlangton's teachings, leading the Chinese people towards a monotheistic belief in the Spirit of Heaven. (94:5.3) The dissemination of knowledge through developments in writing and schools was hindered by the cumbersome ideographic writing system, leading to a numerical limit on the learned classes, yet vestiges of a real concept of God were preserved in the imperial worship of Shang-ti. (79:8.7)
improvedDescription: The Taoist God of Heaven who was the focal point of Singlangton's teachings, leading the Chinese people toward monotheistic belief in the Spirit of Heaven. Imperial worship of Shang-ti preserved vestiges of a real concept of God even as the Chinese ideographic writing system limited the spread of religious knowledge.

name: sharing
originalDescription: The divine pattern of co-ordinate participation in creation is the master design for all Sons and Daughters of God, fostering a magnificent adventure of experiential perfection through sharing with the time-space Creators. (54:2.1-2) Sharing is Godlike - divine, as it embodies the self-distribution and selflessness of divine personality and characterizes the willingness to share life and reality with others, mirroring the example set by the Universal Father, Eternal Son, and Conjoint Actor. (10:1.3) (111:5.1) The understanding of the eternal purpose of God was clear: the will of the Absolute Deity always benefits, never destroys, and it is more blessed to give than to receive. (94:6.6) (103:2.9) Judas did not confide, isolating himself and fostering destructive tendencies that led to his downfall. (193:4.2-3) True knowledge is possessed only by sharing, safeguarded by wisdom, and socialized by love. (25:4.12) (48:7.28) Sharing one’s spiritual life with others favors spiritual growth and contributes to the economy of religious growth. (100:1.8) Sharing life and all that relates to it with a partner is Godlike. (133:2.2)
improvedDescription: A divine pattern of participation and distribution that characterizes God's nature, seen in the Father's sharing with the Son and Spirit, and exemplified by creatures who willingly share life, knowledge, and spiritual experiences with others. Knowledge is possessed only by sharing, safeguarded by wisdom, and socialized by love.

name: sharks
improvedDescription: Large predatory fish that are survivors of ancient teeth-bearing varieties from earlier geological epochs, some of which reached twenty-five to thirty feet in length. The shell-feeding sharks were highly evolved and dominated the oceans for more than five million years.

name: Shawnee Tenskwatawa
improvedDescription: A Shawnee religious leader and prophet, also known as Tenskwatawa, who played a significant role in Native American religious and cultural movements.

name: Shechem
originalDescription: Jesus passed through Shechem on his final tour through Palestine. (134:7.5) (186:3.2-3)
improvedDescription: An ancient Palestinian city that Jesus passed through during his final tour as a private individual through Palestine. It was also one of the destinations where David's messengers carried news of Jesus' crucifixion.

name: sheep
originalDescription: In the arithmetic of shearing, one must consider the practical limitations of applying mathematical logic to real-life situations. (133:5.5) Sheep were domesticated by 5000 B.C. along with other animals by ancient farmers of Mesopotamia, Turkestan, and China. (81:2.12) Egyptian tribes worshiped various nature gods, including the ram. (95:2.3) It is lawful to exhibit loving-kindness on the Sabbath day, even to men. (148:7.2) The Nodites imported sheep to Syria, along with other domesticated animals and improved metalworking techniques. (80:1.2) The parable of the lost sheep illustrates the Father's proactive search for repentant sinners, bringing great joy in heaven. (159:1.2) (159:5.16) The shepherd is smitten and the sheep scatter, as warned by Jesus, causing distress and shock among his followers. (181:2.27) (183:4.6) We are the sheep of God's pasture, led by the Good Shepherd, who restores our souls and guides us in paths of righteousness. (131:2.5,7) (131:4.4) Jesus
improvedDescription: Domesticated animals that were among the first livestock raised by ancient farmers around 5000 B.C. Used frequently by Jesus in parables, particularly the lost sheep parable illustrating God's active search for repentant sinners, and in his teaching about being the Good Shepherd who knows and cares for his flock.

name: shekels
originalDescription: Every first-born son must be redeemed for five shekels according to Mosaic law. (122:9.1) Peter caught a fish with a shekel in its mouth, fulfilling Jesus's half-humorous remark. (157:1.4) Jews licensed to exchange silver shekels profaned temple by banking and commerce within. (173:1.3) (173:2.3) (186:1.2) one-half shekel
improvedDescription: Ancient Jewish silver coins used for temple taxes and religious obligations. Every first-born son was redeemed for five shekels according to Mosaic law, and the temple tax was one-half shekel. Money-changers in the temple exchanged various currencies for the required Jewish shekels.

name: Shekinah
originalDescription: The Jews believed in the Shekinah as a symbol of Divine Presence, longing for its restoration by the Messiah to redeem man from inherent evil nature. (136:1.4)
improvedDescription: A Jewish symbol of the Divine Presence that was believed to have once resided in the temple. The Jews hoped that the coming Messiah would restore the Shekinah and redeem humanity from its inherent evil nature.

name: shell
originalDescription: The shell is of no worth after the chick is hatched; forms are valueless when lessons are learned. (48:6.32)
improvedDescription: Used metaphorically to represent forms or structures that serve a purpose but become valueless once their function is fulfilled. Just as a shell protects the developing chick but has no worth after hatching, forms are valuable only until the lessons they contain are learned.

name: Shema
originalDescription: The congregation took upon themselves the yoke of the kingdom of heaven and the commandments by reciting the Shema, the Jewish creed of faith. (150:8.4)
improvedDescription: The Jewish creed of faith consisting of numerous passages from the law, recited to indicate that worshipers took upon themselves the yoke of the kingdom of heaven and the commandments as applied to day and night.

name: Shemer
originalDescription: King Omri attempted to buy Shemer's estate, amidst wars and power struggles in Israel and Judah, before the Assyrian threat changed the course of history. (97:9.18)
improvedDescription: A landowner whose estate King Omri attempted to purchase during a period of civil wars and political instability in Israel, before the Assyrian threat under Shalmaneser III changed the region's history.

name: Shensi
originalDescription: From Honan to Shensi, the arrival of Andite immigrants stimulated the rapid development of advanced Chinese civilization through metalworking and manufacture. (79:7.5) The hydraulic problems of agriculturists in Shensi led to group co-operation and the promotion of peace among farming communities. (79:8.6)
improvedDescription: A Chinese province where Andite immigrants stimulated rapid development of advanced civilization through metalworking and manufacture, and where hydraulic problems in agriculture led to group cooperation and peaceful relations among farming communities.

name: Sheol
originalDescription: Various cultures had diverse beliefs about the afterlife, with the Hebrews viewing Sheol as the destination for the deceased soul. (86:4.8)
improvedDescription: In Hebrew belief, the destination for the deceased soul, conceived as a place where a phantom replica of the individual went down and could not return to the land of the living.

name: shepherd
originalDescription: The 23rd Psalm was whispered to the shepherd boy in the night season. (48:6.20) (131:2.7) (165:2.6) God feeds his flock like a shepherd, renewing their strength and carrying them in his bosom. (97:7.8) Jesus told Peter to be a good and true shepherd to the flock, not betray their confidence, and be on guard at all times. (192:2.3-4) John the Baptist's life as a shepherd provided him with time for deep contemplation, leading him to believe in an imminent new age marked by the kingdom of heaven. (135:3) The Lord is my shepherd reflects the heritage of shepherds in the mores of the Old Testament era. (84:3.2) The good shepherd joyfully seeks and saves the one lost sheep, showing more joy in heaven over one sinner who repents than over ninety-nine righteous persons who need no repentance. (159:1.2) The sheep know the true shepherd's voice, for they will not follow a stranger. (165:2.4) The shepherd was smitten and the sheep scattered, as Jesus had forewarned. (181:2.27) (183:4.6) The Father, mindful of lost ones, goes out with his Sons, the true shepherds, to seek the straying sheep and confused souls, welcoming them back with complete restoration. (169:1.15) A true shepherd leads their flock to green pastures and still waters, feeding them with good food. (165:2.6)
improvedDescription: A pastoral figure used extensively as a metaphor for divine care and leadership, with God portrayed as the shepherd who feeds his flock, leads them to green pastures and still waters, and seeks out the lost sheep with loving dedication.

name: Shiloh
originalDescription: Yahweh's temple at Shiloh discredited, worship shifted to Jebus in the northern kingdom, as Judah fell under tribute to the Pharaohs. (97:9.17)
improvedDescription: A location where Yahweh's temple was situated but later became discredited, leading to the centralization of worship at Jebus in the northern kingdom while Judah fell under tribute to the Pharaohs.

name: Shinran
originalDescription: Shinran taught faith in the God concept of Amida Buddha. (94:12.2-3)
improvedDescription: A Japanese Buddhist teacher who, along with Ryonin and Honen Shonin, helped develop the mature God concept in Buddhism that culminated in belief in Amida Buddha.

name: Shinto
originalDescription: Shinto affected Buddhism in Japan. (94:9.3) Ganid's abstract of Shinto reveals a belief system influenced by ancient Melchizedek teachings and a divine power that seeks to reveal itself through nature and righteous men. (131:7) Shinto, a revered proto-Taoism in Japan, influences religio-philosophic thought in the lands of the yellow and brown races. (92:6.9,15) (94:5.6) The worship of the state in the imperial family, found in Shinto, is considered a reversion to the early Roman emperor worship and ancestor veneration in various radical and nationalistic secularisms globally. (92:6.20)
improvedDescription: A Japanese religion that affected Buddhism and contains remnants of earlier Melchizedek teachings, representing a form of proto-Taoism that influenced religio-philosophic thought and included worship of the state through the imperial family.

name: ship
originalDescription: Mind is our ship, the Adjuster is our pilot, the human will is captain. (111:1.9)
improvedDescription: A metaphor for the human spiritual journey where mind serves as the vessel, the Thought Adjuster as the pilot, and human will as the captain navigating toward eternal survival.

name: Shittim
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the cities and towns in Perea where Jesus, the twelve apostles, and the seventy messengers conducted their final ministry mission from January to March in A.D. 30.

name: shock
originalDescription: The Melchizedek teachers encountered a Greek society tainted by primitive beliefs and rituals, including lower classes transforming executions into elaborate ceremonies. (98:1.1) Some enjoyed crucifixion for the shock, witnessing Jesus being led to Golgotha by soldiers. (187:0.4)
improvedDescription: A psychological reaction mentioned in contexts of spectacle or trauma. In ancient Greece, lower classes made ceremonial entertainment from executions of condemned criminals. During Jesus' crucifixion, curious onlookers followed the procession to Golgotha seeking the shock of witnessing the event.

name: shooting stars
improvedDescription: Meteoric phenomena visible in the night sky, appearing as brief streaks of light as small celestial objects burn up in Earth's atmosphere.

name: short cuts
originalDescription: There is never any gain from attempting short cuts to perfection on the way to Paradise. (75:8.5) There are no short cuts to Paradise; you must master each lesson on the path to ascent. (48:5.7)
improvedDescription: Attempted bypasses of the established divine plan for spiritual progression. There are no shortcuts, royal roads, or easy paths to Paradise; spiritual growth requires mastering each lesson and sphere in the proper sequence on the ascension journey.

name: short space rays
improvedDescription: A type of space radiation with shorter wavelength characteristics, part of the broader spectrum of cosmic energy phenomena.

name: showbread
originalDescription: King David ate showbread when hungry, just as Jesus and his associates ate grain on the Sabbath day. (147:6.4)
improvedDescription: Sacred bread placed in the temple that was lawfully eaten only by priests. Jesus referenced David eating showbread when hungry to defend his apostles' action of eating grain on the Sabbath, arguing that human needs take precedence over rigid ceremonial law.

name: shrewd steward
originalDescription: The parable of the shrewd steward teaches the importance of wisely preparing for the future in both earthly and heavenly matters. (169:2)
improvedDescription: The central figure in Jesus' parable teaching that the children of the world are often wiser in their generation than the children of the kingdom, emphasizing the importance of wise preparation for both earthly and spiritual matters.

name: shrimps
originalDescription: Shrimps, crabs, and lobsters are crustaceans that have remained relatively unchanged since ancient times, unlike many other marine species. (59:1.18)
improvedDescription: Marine crustaceans that, along with crabs and lobsters, have remained virtually unchanged since ancient geological periods. They represent early life forms that have survived to the present time practically unaltered.

name: shrines
improvedDescription: Sacred places or structures dedicated to religious worship or commemoration, typically smaller and more intimate than temples.

name: shrub of Edentia
improvedDescription: A plant species native to Edentia, the constellation headquarters world, possibly related to or similar in function to the tree of life.

name: Shunem
originalDescription: Jesus preached about salvation and the works of Elisha in the ancient village of Shunem. (124:6.3) (150:5.1)
improvedDescription: An ancient village where Jesus heard stories of the beautiful maiden who once lived there and the wonderful works performed by the prophet Elisha. Jesus later taught about salvation to a group of evangelists and women in this historic location.

name: shunning
originalDescription: Feeling, not thinking, guides the primitive mind in shunning, honoring, and worshiping. (85:7.2)
improvedDescription: The primitive emotional response of avoiding or rejecting something based on feeling rather than rational thought, which along with fearing, honoring, and worshiping, guided early evolutionary development.

name: Siam
originalDescription: Asoka established Buddhism in Siam, spreading it far and wide through his missionary efforts. (94:9.1)
improvedDescription: A region in eastern Asia where Buddhism became established through the missionary efforts of Asoka, who built a great Indian empire by spreading Buddhist teachings to the farthest frontiers of the known world.

name: Siberia
originalDescription: Andites arrived in Europe from Siberia in seven major invasions, the last arrivals coming on horseback in three great waves. (80:4.1-2) Siberia was connected to Alaska through the Bering Strait land bridge 130,000,000 years ago, leading to revolutionary yet gradual changes in marine life along the Californian Pacific coast. (60:1.13) The red race left an imprint in Siberia before migrating to North America. (64:6.5) One hundred thousand years ago, the Andonite Siberians assimilated much red stock, leaving a genetic imprint on the yellow race. (79:5.6) The yellow race drove Andonites into Siberia, after pushing them north from eastern Asia and west into Turkestan. (79:6.1)
improvedDescription: A vast northern region of Asia that served as a corridor for human migration, where Andonites were driven by expanding Chinese populations and where the red race left genetic imprints before migrating to North America. The region was connected to Alaska by the Bering Strait land bridge and experienced significant glacial activity during ice ages.

name: Sibmah
originalDescription: One of the towns visited by Jesus, the twelve and the seventy, during the Perea mission between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: One of the cities and towns in Perea where the seventy messengers, along with Jesus and the twelve apostles, conducted their final ministry mission from January 3, A.D. 30 until Jesus' departure for Jerusalem.

name: Sicilian land bridge
originalDescription: The Sicilian land bridge connected Europe to Africa, allowing Andonites to migrate across. (64:1.5) During the 4th glacier, the Sicilian land bridge allowed for the migration of new mammalian species into Europe, leading to a significant shift in the wildlife population. (64:4.7) The Sicilian land bridge and Gibraltar isthmus protected Mediterranean in early days of violet race. (80:1.1) submergence
improvedDescription: An ancient land connection between Europe and Africa that allowed early human migration and the movement of mammalian species, including straight-tusked elephants and African lions into Europe. The bridge eventually submerged, creating the unified Mediterranean Sea and connecting it with the Atlantic Ocean.

name: Sicily
originalDescription: Marine life evolved in the bay of Sicily as a refuge for new species born to adversity. (59:6.9) Thomas preached in Sicily, Cyprus, Crete, and North Africa, spreading the glad tidings of the kingdom before his martyrdom in Malta. (139:8.13)
improvedDescription: An island in the Mediterranean that served as both a refuge for marine life during oceanic cooling periods and a destination for Thomas's missionary work, where he preached the gospel before his eventual martyrdom in Malta.

name: sick
originalDescription: The apostles and women’s corps visited every house to minister to the sick in Jericho and surrounding cities, spreading the teachings of Jesus. (141:8.1) (142:8.1) (146:3.9) (151:0.2) (163:7.3) For long ages, primitive tribes greatly feared and shamefully neglected the sick until the evolution of shamancraft brought about a great advance in humanitarianism. (90:4.3) Ministry must alleviate or cure sickness in body and mind. (163:4.15) The gospel was very comforting to the sick in Jericho, bringing healing and hope to the afflicted as the apostles quietly carried out their work. (141:8.2) Minister to all who may be sick in mind or body, and proclaim the kingdom of heaven is at hand. (163:1.4) (190:3.1) Do not despise material healing methods for curing sickness; Jesus used them to teach the importance of not neglecting such means in the healing process. (164:3.15) Refrain from teaching the expectation of miracles while ministering to the sick. (163:4.3)
improvedDescription: Those afflicted with physical or mental ailments who became a focus of Jesus' ministry and apostolic work. The apostles were instructed to minister to the sick while preaching the kingdom, visiting homes to comfort the afflicted and provide healing, though they were cautioned against teaching expectations of miraculous cures.

name: Siddhartha
improvedDescription: The given name of Gautama Buddha, the founder of Buddhism who became known as the enlightened one.

name: Siddim
originalDescription: After Abraham's victory at the battle of Siddim, he consolidated power and influence by forming a confederation and establishing a powerful state in Palestine, showcasing his diplomatic prowess and leadership skills. (93:5.14) (93:9.9)
improvedDescription: The location of a significant battle where Abraham achieved victory, after which he became leader of a confederation of eleven tribes and established a powerful state in Palestine, paying tithes to Melchizedek and consolidating his influence in the region.

name: Sidon
originalDescription: On Friday afternoon, June 10, Jesus and his associates arrived in Sidon, home of Karuska and Norana, where they rested for almost two and a half weeks before continuing their journey. (156:0.1) Jesus’ teaching in Sidon emphasized spiritual progression and the importance of embracing the greater realities of the kingdom, urging his disciples to go forward in righteousness and strive for divine sonship in the fellowship of believers. (156:2) If the mighty works done in Chorazin, Bethsaida-Julias, and Capernaum had been done in Tyre and Sidon, the people of these heathen cities would have repented in sackcloth and ashes long ago. (163:6.5)
improvedDescription: A Phoenician coastal city where Jesus and his associates stayed for almost two and a half weeks during their northern journey, resting at the home of a former Bethsaida hospital patient. Jesus used the city as an example of a place that would have repented if it had witnessed the mighty works performed in Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum.

name: Sierras
originalDescription: The Sierras were formed during the modern mountain-building stage of geologic history, with their gold-bearing quartz strata being the product of lava flows from this epoch. (60:3.3) (61:3.3) (61:4.2)
improvedDescription: The Sierras are a mountain range on the Pacific coast that began forming during the modern mountain-building stage of geologic history. Their gold-bearing quartz strata are the product of lava flows from this epoch, and they have been continuously rising for millions of years.

name: sifting
originalDescription: Amos warned of Yahweh's unchanging justice and vowed to sift the house of Israel like wheat among all nations. (97:4.3) The sifting of the kingdom should not distress you, for it is unavoidable and necessary for the new glory of the spiritual teachings of heaven. (153:5.3)
improvedDescription: A metaphorical process of separation and testing. Amos warned that Yahweh would sift the house of Israel like wheat among all nations, while Jesus taught that the sifting of the kingdom, though distressing, was necessary for the spiritual teachings of heaven to shine forth in new glory.

name: Significances of Origins
originalDescription: These angels of seven reflective types are collectively assigned to Mighty Messengers, Those High in Authority, and Those without Name and Number. (28:6.1) The Significances of Origins teach ascending creatures to apply spirit ethics and the Memories of Mercy guide them to true mercy. (28:6.8) The living reference genealogies of reflective seconaphim provide vital insights into the significance of origins for beings across the superuniverses. (28:6.2-4)
improvedDescription: A type of tertiary seconaphim who serve as living ready-reference genealogies of the vast hosts of beings throughout the seven superuniverses. They provide up-to-date estimates of ancestral factors and current status of any individual, teaching ascending creatures about spirit ethics and the paramount importance of origin in cosmic relationships.

name: signs
originalDescription: Spirit messages, interpreted through omens and signs, revealed the will of the spirits in a cult of bribery. (87:5.9) Civilized races cursed with belief in signs, dodging ill luck through divination, dreams, omens, and superstitious remnants. (87:5.14) (150:3.12) Jesus' resurrection after 3 days was the only sign he gave to show he is the Son of God. (173:5.4) Medicine men put great trust in signs, omens, stars, and spirits of the dead. (90:2.5) No sign shall be given to this faithless and sign-seeking generation, other than that which they already have, and that which they shall see when the Son of Man departs. (153:4.5) (157:2.1) Pharisees requested Jesus to give a predetermined sign but he refused, saying no sign shall be given except for what they already have and what they will see when the Son of Man departs. (153:4.5)
improvedDescription: Omens, tokens, or supernatural manifestations sought to reveal divine will or predict the future. Ancient peoples interpreted spirit messages through signs, while Jesus refused to give predetermined signs to faithless generations, stating that only one sign would be given - his resurrection after three days.

name: Sikhism
originalDescription: In the fifteenth century, Guru Nanak synthesized Islam, Buddhism, and Hinduism into Sikhism, an advanced religion of Asia. (92:5.15) (92:7.2) Sikhism is the most recent religion. (92:6.13)
improvedDescription: A religion founded by Guru Nanak in the fifteenth century that synthesized teachings from Islam, Hinduism, and Buddhism. Described as one of the most advanced religions of Asia and the most recent of the major world religions.

name: Silas
originalDescription: Silas joined Paul in Corinth for the final months of his preaching there. (133:3.3)
improvedDescription: A companion who joined Paul and Timothy in Corinth during the final months of Paul's preaching ministry there, where they encountered many who had been taught by Jesus during his earlier visit.

name: silence
originalDescription: After prayer, remain in silent receptivity to allow the indwelling spirit to speak to the listening soul. (146:2.17) Jesus refuted false accusations by his calm and majestic silence. (184:3.8)
improvedDescription: A state of receptivity recommended by Jesus for prayer and worship. After prayer, believers should remain in silent receptivity to allow the indwelling spirit to speak to the listening soul, while Jesus himself demonstrated the power of calm and majestic silence when facing false accusations.

name: Siloam (pool)
originalDescription: At half past four o'clock, 2000 believers were baptized in Siloam pool by the apostles. (194:1.4) Abraham, a young and influential Pharisee, was baptized in Siloam by Abner, sparking astonishment and intrigue in Jerusalem and causing division among the spying Pharisees. (148:8.1) The golden pitcher was filled at the pool of Siloam for the morning ceremony during the feast. (162:4.4) Josiah washed clay off in the pool of Siloam to receive his sight from Jesus. (164:3.8,12) The blind man went to Siloam, a semi-sacred place, for healing despite his slight faith, guided by the belief in the efficacy of spittle and the need to wash away the clay. (164:3.14)
improvedDescription: A pool in Jerusalem used for ceremonial purposes and healing. Jesus directed the blind man Josiah to wash clay from his eyes there to receive sight, and over 2,000 believers were baptized there by the apostles. The pool was also used in temple ceremonies, with priests filling golden pitchers from it during feast days.

name: Siloam (tower)
originalDescription: The tower of Siloam fell on 18 innocent men, showing that tragedy can strike anyone regardless of their actions. (166:4.4)
improvedDescription: A tower in Jerusalem that collapsed and killed eighteen men. Jesus used this tragedy as an example to teach that such accidents do not indicate greater sin in the victims, but rather show that innocent people can suffer from the accidents of time.

name: Silurian
originalDescription: The ancient Silurian seas teemed with lime-shelled life and gradually built up thick layers of limestone, covering Europe and North America from 300 to 275 million years ago. (59:3)
improvedDescription: An ancient geologic period from 300 million years ago when vast seas covered most of Europe and North America. These Silurian seas teemed with lime-shelled life that fell to the sea bottom and gradually built up thick layers of limestone, creating the first widespread limestone deposits.

name: Simeon (reputed holy man)
originalDescription: On the hill in Nazareth, once a “high place of Baal,” now stood the tomb of Simeon, a reputed holy man of Israel, where Jesus pondered the historic and traditional events of his father Joseph's people. (126:1.2) (150:7.1) (158:1.4)
improvedDescription: A reputed holy man of Israel whose tomb stood on a hill in Nazareth where Jesus often went to meditate. This hill had previously been a high place of Baal, and from this location Jesus would contemplate the historic and traditional events of his people.

name: Simeon (singer)
originalDescription: At the presentation of Jesus, Simeon the singer and Anna the poetess recognized him as the promised Messiah. (122:9.2-27)
improvedDescription: A Judean singer who, along with Anna the poetess, lingered in the temple courts and recognized the infant Jesus as the promised Messiah at his presentation. Simeon sang a hymn of redemption written by Anna, proclaiming Jesus as the salvation prepared for all peoples.

name: simians
originalDescription: The simian tribes of modern times and the present-day human species are descendants of lemurs in the almost paradisiacal Mesopotamian or Persian peninsula. (62:1.3) Man did not descend from the retarded mid-mammals, but rather from a superior strain of the same mid-mammal tribe. (61:6.1) (62:1.1) (62:3.11-13) Some Andonites mated with their retarded cousins of the simian tribes, implying a quality of mind far above their later descendants. (63:1.4) Man is not descended from the simians. (62:1.1) (62:3.11-13)
improvedDescription: Modern apes and monkeys that descended from inferior mid-mammal stock alongside humans, but from separate lineages. While humans descended from superior mid-mammal strains, simians originated from retarded couples of the same tribe who survived in hiding and became the ancestors of modern chimpanzees, gorillas, and other apes.

name: Simon (assistant of David Zebedee)
originalDescription: Simon, assistant of David Zebedee, experienced a miraculous draught of fishes and decided to follow Jesus. (145:1.2)
improvedDescription: An assistant of David Zebedee who experienced a miraculous catch of fish when Jesus taught from their boat. Amazed by the miracle, Simon fell at Jesus' knees declaring himself a sinful man, and from that day forsook his nets to follow Jesus.

name: Simon (boat)
originalDescription: Jesus taught from Simon the boat, built by his own hands, as the people crowded near the water's edge. (145:1.1) The old fishing boat of Andrew and Peter, kept at Jesus' disposal, became the platform for his teachings as he shared the parable of the sower with the multitudes gathered by the seaside. (151:1.1)
improvedDescription: A fishing boat named after Simon Peter that had been built by Jesus' own hands. Jesus used this boat as a platform for teaching the multitudes gathered by the seaside, including when he delivered the parable of the sower.

name: Simon (brother of Jesus)
originalDescription: Simon, born April 14, A.D. 2, was the third brother of Jesus. (123:6.7) Simon brought Jude home after he ran away, and Jesus spent time with each family member, even taking Jude to become a fisherman. (128:7.8) In A.D. 16, Simon graduated from school and diversified his trade to work with Jacob the stone mason, alongside Jesus and other brothers. (128:2.2) Jesus took Simon to Jerusalem for Passover, and on their journey they encountered people from distant lands, sparking Simon's curiosity and strengthening their bond. (128:3.1-8) Simon and Jude finally married in March, A.D. 24, eagerly awaiting Jesus' blessing. (134:1.4) James and Simon successfully followed Jesus' plan of placating bellicose playmates through persuasion and nonresistance, unlike Joseph and Jude who often defended themselves when attacked. (127:4.5) Simon started school, while James took charge of teaching his sisters as Ruth was taken in hand by Miriam and Martha. (127:1.5) Simon, a well-meaning but overly dreamy boy, caused Jesus and Mary considerable anxiety. (127:4.7) Simon graduated from school this year and now works alongside Jacob the stone mason, diversifying their trades to take on contracts for putting up entire buildings. (128:2.2) At Jesus' 4th appearance, Simon and his family saw the Master vanish before their eyes. (190:2.6)
improvedDescription: The third son of Joseph and Mary, born April 14, A.D. 2. Simon was a well-meaning but dreamy boy who graduated from school and worked as a stone mason with Jacob. He traveled with Jesus to Jerusalem for Passover, married his longtime sweetheart in A.D. 24, and witnessed Jesus' fourth resurrection appearance before his family.

name: Simon (brother of Jesus’ mother)
originalDescription: Simon, Mary's brother and officer of Zealots, caused an estrangement between himself and Jesus due to his involvement in the nationalist cause. (127:2.4,7)
improvedDescription: Mary's brother who became an officer in the Galilean division of the Zealots. His involvement in the nationalist cause created an estrangement between himself and Jesus, who refused to join the revolutionary movement despite family pressure.

name: Simon (father of Lazarus)
originalDescription: Jesus presided over the Passover supper in Bethany, where Simon had been laid to rest with his fathers by A.D. 12. (127:3.4) Simon, father of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus, welcomed the Nazareth family into his home on the eastern slopes of Olivet, sparking a lifelong friendship with Jesus. (124:6.9) Simon of Bethany purchased the paschal lamb and invited five Nazareth families for Passover, where Jesus caused a stir with his insightful remarks. (125:2.1-2) Simon, leading resident of Bethany, comforted sorrow-stricken sisters and was highly respected by friends from near-by hamlets and Jerusalem. (168:0.4) Simon watched young Jesus in the temple and marveled at his wisdom and humility as he challenged the teachings of the wise men with thought-provoking questions. (125:5.1,8-9)
improvedDescription: The father of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus who lived in Bethany and welcomed Jesus' family during their visits to Jerusalem. Simon was a well-to-do and honorable Jew who served as the leading resident of Bethany and hosted Passover celebrations, developing a lifelong friendship with Jesus.

name: Simon of Bethany
originalDescription: Simon of Bethany gave a public banquet for Jesus and Lazarus in defiance of the Sanhedrin, with Martha serving and Mary among the onlookers, as Jesus spoke of breaking down walls of prejudice with the Father's love. (172:1.2-3) Jesus beckoned to his apostles to leave the temple and go to Bethany for their evening meal, setting up camp in Gethsemane on ground belonging to Simon of Bethany. (173:5.5) Jesus said good-bye to Lazarus, Simon, and the women's corps at a meeting at Simon's home before heading to Philadelphia. (174:0.1) Jesus stayed with Simon of Bethany, the leading citizen of the village. (172:0.1) (172:2.1) Judas deposited funds with Simon of Bethany for safekeeping. (172:2.3) Simon of Bethany, who lived near Lazarus, hosted Jesus and John at his home in Bethany while they journeyed to Jerusalem, avoiding public teaching along the way. (152:7.1)
improvedDescription: A leading citizen of Bethany who became Jesus' host during his final visits to Jerusalem. Simon gave a public banquet for Jesus and Lazarus in defiance of the Sanhedrin, provided lodging for Jesus and his apostles, and owned the ground in Gethsemane where they established their camp.

name: Simon of Bethphage
originalDescription: Thomas brooded at Simon of Bethphage's home, isolated and withdrawn from his fellow apostles, refusing to believe in the resurrection of Jesus despite their persistent efforts. (189:4.1) (191:0.13) (191:5.1)
improvedDescription: A believer in Bethphage who provided shelter to the apostle Thomas. Thomas stayed at Simon's home for a week after Jesus' crucifixion, brooding in solitude and refusing to believe in the resurrection until his fellow apostles finally brought him back to rejoin them.

name: Simon of Cyrene
originalDescription: Simon of Cyrene, compelled to carry Jesus' crossbeam, became a valiant believer. (187:1.9-10) Simon of Cyrene, whose son Rufus was aided by Jesus, unknowingly carried Jesus' cross. (130:6.6)
improvedDescription: Simon of Cyrene was compelled by Roman soldiers to carry Jesus' cross after the Master collapsed under its weight. A Passover visitor from northern Africa, Simon became a valiant believer after witnessing the crucifixion and led his family into the kingdom upon returning home, though he never knew that Jesus was the same man who had once befriended his injured son Rufus.

name: Simon Peter
originalDescription: Simon's name changed from Simon to Peter by Jesus, signifying a new beginning and transformation in his role as a disciple. (137:1.3) (139:2.2) family Jesus personal traits and attitudes position and achievements writings work after Jesus end
improvedDescription: Simon Peter, originally a fisherman from Bethsaida, was the first apostle chosen by Jesus and became the recognized spokesman and leader of the twelve. Despite his impulsive nature and tendency to speak before thinking, Peter's wholehearted belief in Jesus' divinity and his passionate loyalty made him the chief preacher among the apostles and the real founder of the early Christian church.

name: Simon (Sanhedrin member)
originalDescription: Simon, a Sanhedrin member, publicly espoused Jesus' teachings, causing new agitation for his apprehension. (142:8.5)
improvedDescription: Simon was a Sanhedrin member who publicly espoused Jesus' teachings, causing renewed agitation among the Jewish rulers for Jesus' arrest and prompting the Master to withdraw into Samaria and the Decapolis.

name: Simon the Pharisee
originalDescription: During his visit, Jesus forgave the repentant woman much to the surprise of Simon the Pharisee. (147:5)
improvedDescription: Simon the Pharisee was an influential Jerusalem Pharisee and halfhearted believer who invited Jesus and his personal associates to his home for a social meal, during which Jesus forgave a repentant woman much to Simon's surprise.

name: Simon the tanner
originalDescription: Simon the tanner, once a wealthy leather merchant, embraced Christianity after the profound influence of Jesus and Gadiah in Joppa. (130:1.1,3)
improvedDescription: Simon the tanner was a wealthy leather merchant in Joppa who embraced Christianity after being influenced by the profound impact Jesus had on his interpreter Gadiah, a Philistine who worked for Simon and became a devoted follower of the Master.

name: Simon Zelotes
originalDescription: Simon Zelotes witnessed the Samaritan among the lepers and learned a lesson about gratitude and faith from Jesus. (166:2.3-6) At Jesus’ appearances, Simon Zelotes returned to his brethren in Galilee amidst the scenes of their earlier associations, having experienced the risen Master's profound teachings of love, service, and divine commission. (191:2.5) (192:1-4) (193:0.3,5) Simon Zelotes and Judas Iscariot boldly claimed they could heal James of Safed’s son in the name of Jesus, only to be met with skepticism and mockery from the scribes and disappointment from the believers. (158:4.6) The call of Jesus brought together sinners and publicans in joyous fellowship at Matthew's banquet. (138:3) Simon Zelotes distributed 100 swords to Jesus and his followers, except for Nathaniel who refused to arm himself, before the night of the Master's betrayal in the garden. (171:4.1) (182:2.3) (183:2.2) Simon Zelotes, a former fiery agitator and high officer of the Zealots, was selected by Peter to join Jesus' apostles after abandoning his merchant career in Capernaum. (138:2.8) (139:11.1) Simon Zelotes organized the apostles' recreation activities efficiently, managing their diversions and relaxation. (138:10.9) (139:11.2) Simon Zelotes was in retirement before going forth to preach the gospel of the risen Lord, unlike Thomas, the Alpheus twins, and Nathaniel who opposed the shift in the burden of their public message. (192:4.7) (193:6.4) Jesus’ personal admonitions to Simon Zelotes were focused on rising above disappointment, seeking spiritual realities, and being steadfast in faith. (174:0.2) (181:2.7-11) Simon Zelotes stayed active in his work, fueled by the belief that Jesus would imminently return. (192:2.5-6) Simon Zelotes was a personal worker among the disciples. (141:3.2) Simon Zelotes preached the gospel in Alexandria, journeyed along the Nile to the heart of Africa, and died there after a lifetime of spreading the teachings of Jesus. (139:11.11) Simon Zelotes learned from Jesus the key to persuasion: lead men into the kingdom and the truths of the kingdom will dispel error. (141:6.2) (149:5.1) (169:2.1) (193:3.2) Simon Zelotes' tactical blunder at Nazareth led to Jesus being almost pushed off a cliff by a violent mob. (150:9.3) After handing his business to a nephew, Simon Zelotes followed Jesus. (138:3.3) Simon Zelotes wished to deal roughly with Kirmeth, but Jesus intervened, allowing the self-deceived prophet freedom before he returned to Bagdad. (148:8.3) personal traits and attitudes
improvedDescription: Simon Zelotes was the eleventh apostle, a former fiery Zealot agitator and merchant from Capernaum who was chosen by Peter to join Jesus' followers. Despite his patriotic nationalism and struggles to reconcile his political views with spiritual teachings, he became an efficient organizer of the apostles' recreational activities and later preached the gospel throughout Africa until his death.

name: sin
originalDescription: definition God and sin among primitives dealing with sin effect of sin
improvedDescription: Sin is the conscious, knowing, and deliberate transgression of divine law and the Father's will. It represents purposeful resistance to divine reality and cosmic truth, distinguished from error (misconception) and evil (maladjustment to reality) by its willful nature and personal moral guilt.

name: Sin (god)
originalDescription: Sin was one of the chief Mesopotamian deities and part of the Babylonian triad alongside Bel and Anu. (95:1.4)
improvedDescription: Sin was one of the chief Mesopotamian deities and a member of the Babylonian triad alongside Bel and Anu, representing one of the seven principal gods that Salem teachers recognized in their efforts to reduce the pantheon of Mesopotamian religion.

name: sincerity
originalDescription: Sincerity becomes sublime when ascenders reach Havona, their purpose perfected through faith and trust tested by the universal scheme of survival. (26:4.13) Even in material-mindedness, Philip was sincere, leading him to fulfill his great mission on earth with spiritual vision and faith. (181:2.20) Eve's sincere project to modify the divine plan was deemed evil for straying from the right path. (75:4.6) The key of the kingdom is sincerity, the highest moral choice is to do the will of God, and to come into the kingdom with genuine trustfulness like an unspoiled child. (39:4.14) (170:2.21) Sincerity is most serviceable when wedded to discretion in the work of the kingdom. (181:2.21) Sincerity in prayer is the assurance of its being heard, related to the prophet's exhortation to make religion an affair of the heart and to relate to God as individuals, as warned about the deceitfulness of the natural human heart. (145:2.6) Evil is a transgression of law, not a violation of the rules of conduct pertaining to life, which is the law, as falsehood is a premeditated perversion of truth. (48:6.33) Jesus' sincere proclamation of saving truth sometimes causes pain. (100:7.2)
improvedDescription: Sincerity is one of the keys to the kingdom of heaven and becomes sublime when perfected through faith and spiritual testing. It represents genuine trustfulness and honest intention, most serviceable in the work of the kingdom when combined with discretion and spiritual wisdom.

name: singers
originalDescription: Singers are heavenly reproducers and celestial artisans, harmonists reiterating the harmonies of the past and interpreting present melodies, operating on the morontia level. (44:2.3)
improvedDescription: Singers are celestial artisans and heavenly reproducers who serve as harmonists, reiterating the specific harmonies of the past and interpreting present melodies on the morontia level as part of the spiritual ministry in the universe.

name: Singlangton
originalDescription: Singlangton, a Chinese spiritual leader 100,000 years ago, influenced the cultural development of the Chinese people through worship of the One Truth. (79:6.10) Singlangton was the first of the yellow men to teach and lead his people in the worship of “One Truth,” one of 24 counselors. (45:4.8) Singlangton, the first yellow man to teach his people the worship of “One Truth” thousands of years ago. (45:4.8) (64:6.15) The teachings of Singlangton on the concept of Shang-ti continued to influence the Chinese people, leading to a shift from the pursuit of new truths to the preservation of established beliefs. (79:8.4) (94:5.3)
improvedDescription: Singlangton was a Chinese spiritual leader who lived approximately 100,000 years ago and was the first of the yellow men to teach and lead his people in the worship of "One Truth." His teachings on monotheism kept the Chinese among the more spiritual peoples of earth and influenced their cultural development, with the concept later evolving into Shang-ti, the God of Heaven.

name: single-origin beings
originalDescription: Single-origin beings are descendants of the Paradise Deities or creations of beings with direct or indirect descent from them. (30:1.55) In Paradise classification, single-origin beings are those directly or indirectly descended from the Paradise Deities. (30:1.55-91) orders
improvedDescription: Single-origin beings are those of origin in any one of the Paradise Deities or otherwise created by any one being of direct or indirect descent from the Paradise Deities. This classification includes the Supreme Spirits, Ascending Orders, and the Family of the Infinite Spirit, encompassing numerous orders of celestial beings.

name: Sinkiang
originalDescription: The Andite culture, dominant in 15,000 B.C., spread new advances through Eurasia and North Africa from Mesopotamia to Sinkiang, blending with the Andite-yellow inhabitants and contributing to the improvement of the northern division of the yellow race in China. (78:5.2,6) (78:6.4) Aridity forced Andites to invent new methods in adapting to natural circumstances. (81:6.3) The Tarim River in Sinkiang was an ancient gateway for the Andite tribes infiltrating eastward into the northern lands of the yellow men and the highland regions of Tibet. (79:1.1-2) Twenty thousand years ago, migration from Sinkiang and Tibet to China brought superior blended peoples to strengthen primitive centers of culture along the Yellow River and Yangtze. (79:6.5)
improvedDescription: Sinkiang (Eastern Turkestan) was an ancient gateway through which Andite peoples from Mesopotamia penetrated the mountains to reach the northern lands of the yellow men. For almost fifteen thousand years, centers of mixed Andite culture persisted in the Tarim River basin of Sinkiang, serving as the easternmost outpost of true Andite civilization.

name: sinners
originalDescription: The rebellion has ended and the unworthy traitors will “become as though they had not been” after the executionary broadcast on Uversa. (53:9.7) Sinners who commit sin are bond servants of sin and are not likely to abide forever in the master's house, but those who are disciples and do the will of the Father will be made free and remain in the Father's house as His sons. (162:7.2) God goes in search of sinners, rejoicing in their repentance and welcoming them back into His fold with open arms. (159:1.2) (159:5.16) God loves sinners unconditionally and seeks their redemption through mercy and forgiveness. (2:5.4) (2:6.8) Jesus loved sinners and came to call them, seeking to save those who are lost. (100:7.11) (137:8.16) (138:3.6) (138:6.4) (157:6.9) Joy in heaven over one sinner who repents is greater than over ninety-nine righteous persons. (159:1.2) (167:7.5) (169:1.2,4) There is no peace for the wicked, but God offers forgiveness and salvation to those who repent and turn to Him for healing and protection. (131:2.10) Every sinner is given the opportunity to accept salvation and change their ways, as mercy allows for sufficient time to make a deliberate and fully chosen attitude towards their sinful acts and thoughts. (54:5.2-3) An ideal society based on brotherhood and the Golden Rule cannot be achieved if the wicked are allowed to exploit those striving for truth, beauty, and goodness. (71:4.16)
improvedDescription: Sinners are those who commit sin and become bond servants of sin, yet God loves sinners unconditionally and actively seeks their redemption. Jesus came specifically to call sinners to repentance, and there is great joy in heaven over one sinner who repents, with divine mercy providing sufficient time for each wrongdoer to choose their eternal destiny.

name: Sisera
originalDescription: Deborah and Barak defeated Sisera at Taanach, a historic event Jesus considered while overlooking the tomb of Simeon on the hill of Nazareth. (126:1.2)
improvedDescription: Sisera was a military commander who was defeated by Deborah and Barak at Taanach, a historic battle that Jesus reflected upon while overlooking the surrounding countryside from the hill of Nazareth.

name: Siva
originalDescription: Siva is the Hindu lord of life, death, fertility, and destruction, while Vishnu is believed to periodically incarnate in human form. (94:4.5) Siva is a member of the Hindu trinitarian concept that includes Brahma and Vishnu, brought to India by Sethite priests and developed further by native intellectual evolution. (104:1.5)
improvedDescription: Siva is one of the principal deities in the Hindu trinitarian concept, alongside Brahma and Vishnu. Known as the lord of life and death, god of fertility, and master of destruction, Siva's worship arose in the first millennium after Christ and represents one aspect of the later Indian development of Trinity concepts.

name: Siwalik Hills
originalDescription: Fossils in the Siwalik Hills of northern India are the closest to transition man. (64:3.4)
improvedDescription: The Siwalik Hills of northern India contain fossils that approach nearer to transition types between man and prehuman groups than any other location on earth.

name: skeletal structures
originalDescription: Skeletal structures are more dependable than skull dimensions in deciphering human racial origins due to the distinct types that still persist and help identify the ancestry of present-day races. (81:4.3,14) Originally, there were five distinct types of skeletal structure in the development of the Urantia races, but as human remains are unearthed, it becomes impossible to clearly distinguish the five original types. (81:4.3-14)
improvedDescription: Skeletal structures are more dependable than skull dimensions in deciphering racial origins and help identify the ancestry of present-day races. Originally, there were five distinct types of skeletal structure in Urantia's racial development, though extensive intermingling has now reduced human remains to approximately three main classifications: Caucasoid, Mongoloid, and Negroid.

name: skepticism (philosophy)
originalDescription: Skepticism asserts that knowledge is fallacious and conviction is impossible. (121:4.5) Philosophy, leaning heavily on metaphysics, unfailingly becomes skeptical and confused. (103:6.14)
improvedDescription: Skepticism is a philosophical position asserting that knowledge is fallacious and that conviction and assurance are impossible. It represents a purely negative attitude that becomes prevalent when philosophy leans too heavily upon metaphysics, resulting in confusion and doubt.

name: Skepticism (religion)
originalDescription: Occidental religion languished until the days of the Skeptics, Cynics, Epicureans, and Stoics, and the great contest between Mithraism and Christianity. (98:6.2)
improvedDescription: Skepticism was one of the philosophical schools that emerged during a period when Occidental religion languished, along with the Cynics, Epicureans, and Stoics, preceding the great contest between Mithraism and Christianity.

name: skies
originalDescription: Do not mistake God as distant in the skies, for the divine presence dwells within you through the indwelling Mystery Monitor. (5:2.3)
improvedDescription: The heavens above, which represent the misconception of God as distant rather than dwelling within through the indwelling Mystery Monitor.

name: skulls
originalDescription: Skulls became potent fetishes, believed to hold powerful spiritual significance. (88:1.8) Ancient medicine men performed trephining surgeries to release headache spirits, evolving from spiritual to physical healing practices. (90:4.4) The skull types found in the graves of ancient Mesopotamia evidence the thorough blending of races by 6000 B.C. (78:8.1) (80:1.2) (80:2.1-2) (80:7.2) (80:8.2,5) (80:9.5-10) (81:4.2-14)
improvedDescription: Bony head structures that became powerful fetishes in primitive cultures and served as key evidence for racial identification and blending patterns throughout human history. Ancient medicine men performed trephining surgeries on skulls to release headache spirits, while archaeologists use skull types to trace the mixing of different racial groups across civilizations.

name: slate
originalDescription: The dark slate in transition rock layers indicates the presence of organic carbon, connecting to the ancestral plant life of the Carboniferous age. (58:7.10) Shale changed into slate under pressure in many regions throughout all continents except central Asia. (59:1.16)
improvedDescription: A metamorphic rock formed when shale is transformed under pressure, found in transition rock layers containing organic carbon that indicates ancestral plant life from the Carboniferous age.

name: slavery
originalDescription: in primitive times modern times abolition of slavery benefits of slavery detriment of slavery
improvedDescription: An institution that compelled backward peoples to work, serving as an indispensable bridge from chaos to civilized society while providing the foundation for primitive government and social organization. Though oppressive, slavery enabled cultural advancement and eventually evolved into modified forms of industrial servitude in modern times.

name: slaves
originalDescription: The herders, as the first capitalists, kept their capital in herds and were hesitant to trust slaves or women, later turning to male prisoners as serfs, leading to Africans becoming the great slave race. (69:8.5) Jesus' interactions with individuals across all social classes left a lasting impact, inspiring Claudius to free his slaves and leading a senator to advocate for a shift in governmental policy. (132:4.5) Slaves struggle to transform master-fear into God-love, hindering the progress of religious meanings and self-consciousness in individuals. (92:7.11) The temple head tax was required from all except women, slaves, and minors, who were exempted. (173:1.3) Jesus rescued a slave girl from a drunken assailant in Fair Havens, leaving Ganid with a lasting desire to challenge the injustices of the caste system in India. (130:5.4) Half of the Roman population were slaves, with many rising among the free and tradesmen, while the majority were inferior. (121:3.6) (133:0.2) Despite his initial expectations, Judas was horrified to receive only thirty pieces of silver as payment for betraying Jesus, a sum equivalent to the cost of a slave in Judea. (186:1.2) Slaves were killed to serve their masters in ghostland, a belief that led to gruesome customs like spearing a slave to accompany a deceased chief's journey to the afterlife. (87:2.8) Slavery under Roman law allowed masters unqualified power over their slaves, even those of superior peoples, which influenced the early Christian church largely made up of lower class individuals, including slaves. (121:3.7) Skilled slaves often earned wages, saved up to buy their freedom, and subsequently rose to prominence in society, a phenomenon that the early Christian church accepted and even encouraged. (121:3.8-9) Many wealthy ancient men distributed their fortunes out of fear of being killed by those who coveted their treasures, often sacrificing scores of slaves to show disdain for wealth. (69:5.14) The early war was caused by the need for recruits for the labor ranks, including slaves. (70:1.11) Women were the first slaves, a result of man's decreased dependence on them for family and sex. (69:8.1)
improvedDescription: Individuals held in bondage, beginning with women as the first slaves due to decreased male dependence, and later including war captives forced into agricultural labor. Many superior slaves earned wages, purchased freedom, and rose to prominent positions in society, influencing the early Christian church's tolerance of this modified form of servitude.

name: sleep
originalDescription: Adjusters may detach during sleep, allowing for experiences beyond the first heaven of the mansion worlds. (48:6.23) Supreme and self-acting Adjusters sometimes effect spiritual transformations during sleep. (109:5.1) (110:5.2-4) It was custom to speak of death as sleep, but Jesus clarified: “Lazarus is dead.” (167:4.6) The ancients believed that souls left the body during sleep, with dreams being the experiences of the absent soul. (86:5.6,10) Young Jesus' disturbed, revolting dreams greatly upset his sleep and left his heart torn. (125:2.4) Early ownership of sleeping space evolved into the tribal chiefs assigning homesites, eventually leading to ownership being conferred by a fire site and established title to the adjacent land. (69:9.11)
improvedDescription: A natural state of rest during which Adjusters may detach and attempt spiritual transformations, while ancient peoples believed the soul temporarily left the body, experiencing dreams as the absent soul's adventures.

name: sleeping survivors
originalDescription: When repersonalized on the mansion worlds, sleeping survivors become Spirit-fused mortals through the individualization of the spirit of the Divine Minister, making them equals in mind and spirit to their contemporaries. (40:9.3) (49:6.6) Magisterial Sons preside over the awakening of sleeping survivors and execute the mandates of dispensation terminators. (20:2.5) (20:3.1) Sleeping survivors, mortals without personal guardians, must rest in unconscious sleep until the judgment day of a new epoch arrives, unable to go directly to mansion worlds. (30:4.11) The sleeping survivors of Adamic dispensation were raised for the inauguration of the general resurrection on Urantia by Gabriel and the archangels. (189:3.1-3) personality, mind transcripts, and memory held by Adjusters; mind-matrix and potential of identity in soul held by The reassembling of creature personality for resurrection of sleeping survivors involves the spirit-mind trust of the Adjuster and the morontia-soul trust of the seraphic destiny guardians. (47:3.3-4) On archangels' worlds, personality records are classified and preserved between mortal death and repersonalization. (37:3.8) Survivors are repersonalized at the end of a dispensation associated with the advent of a divine Son of planetary service. (49:6.3-7) (112:5.10,13) Rest in unconscious sleep until new dispensation, waiting for the Son of God to call the rolls and adjudicate the realm. (30:4.11-12) Group guardians facilitate the resurrection by gathering sleeping survivors in the mansion spheres, as recorded in the dispensational roll call. (113:6.7-8)
improvedDescription: Mortals without personal guardians who must rest in unconscious sleep until judgment day of a new dispensation, when they are repersonalized on mansion worlds through the reunion of their morontia soul and Adjuster elements.

name: slime
originalDescription: Man's ascent from slime to earthly lordship is a tale of biologic struggle and mind survival. (65:2.1) 450,000,000 years ago, the transition from vegetable to animal life occurred gradually, with slime molds persisting as a reminder of the many transitional stages between early primitive forms. (58:6.1)
improvedDescription: Primitive organic matter from ocean beds that represents humanity's earliest ancestral forms and the transitional stages between vegetable and animal life 450 million years ago.

name: slogans
originalDescription: United Midwayers persistently work for the progress of their native planet, as suggested by their motto: “What the United Midwayers undertake, the United Midwayers do.” (77:9.3) Religion must have up-to-date slogans to call modern man to spiritual battle effectively. (195:6.10)
improvedDescription: Motivational phrases or mottos, exemplified by the United Midwayers' determination expressed in their saying and the need for religion to develop modern calls to spiritual battle.

name: sloth
improvedDescription: A state of laziness or reluctance to work, representing one of the challenges that slavery helped overcome in primitive societies by compelling backward peoples to engage in productive labor.

name: slothful servant
originalDescription: In the parable of the slothful servant, the lord will cast out those unfaithful who mistreat others and do not prepare for his unexpected return, while those who endure trials will be exalted in the affairs of the kingdom. (165:6.2-3)
improvedDescription: In Jesus's parable of the slothful servant, the lord will cast out those unfaithful servants who mistreat others and do not prepare for his unexpected return, while those who endure trials will be exalted in the affairs of the kingdom.

name: sloths
originalDescription: Asiatic sloths, armadillos, antelopes, and bears entered North America, while North American camels went to China (61:4.3) (61:4.4) (61:5.7) (61:7.15)
improvedDescription: Asiatic sloths entered North America during ancient continental connections, while ground sloths as large as elephants lived in North America during the ice age. South American sloths, armadillos, and anteaters evolved as distinctive species on that continent.

name: smiths
improvedDescription: Skilled craftsmen who work with metals, forging tools, weapons, and other implements.

name: smoke signals
originalDescription: From smoke signals to advanced communication technology, the early message system evolved through various stages. (69:4.7)
improvedDescription: An early form of long-distance communication that evolved from primitive message systems, eventually leading to more advanced technologies like telegraph, telephone, and wireless communication.

name: snails
originalDescription: Snails crawled out of the briny waters onto land in ancient times, surviving through millions of years alongside bivalves, starfish, and other early animal types. (59:2.12) (59:5.6) (65:2.5)
improvedDescription: Ancient gastropods that crawled from the briny seas onto land millions of years ago, surviving as one of the early animal types alongside bivalves, starfish, and other primitive creatures.

name: snakes
originalDescription: The worship of serpents has ancient roots in multiple cultures, including the Hebrews, Hindus, and Chinese. (85:3.3) Indiscriminate kindness may lead to social evils; be wise as serpents and harmless as doves. (140:8.13) (140:9.3) (163:1.3) (178:1.7) The snakes became fetish animals, leading to taboos on eating their flesh. (88:1.5) Childless wives were feared to transform into snakes in the spirit world due to the belief that barrenness was solely the wife's fault, causing many unsatisfactory marriages among primitive peoples. (83:7.2) Snakes, descended from nonprogressive reptiles, are one of the surviving branches of the saurian family after the disappearance of the dinosaurs. (61:2.6) (65:2.10) The fatality of snake bites was attributed to the sorcerer's belief in the power of magic. (88:4.8) If a child asks for a watersnake, a wise father would give what is needed, not what is foolishly requested. (144:2.4) The Phoenicians and Jews considered the snake to be the mouthpiece of evil spirits, a belief shared by many throughout history. (88:1.5) The religious leaders were condemned as offspring of vipers by John, and now face inevitable judgment. (175:1.21) Snake charming shamans, once venom addicts in love cults, inspire the worship of serpents worldwide. (85:3.3) The wisdom of the serpent, a symbol of Greek medicine, is still employed as an emblem by modern physicians. (85:3.3)
improvedDescription: Reptiles descended from the saurian family that survived the extinction of dinosaurs and became objects of worship in many cultures. Jesus taught his followers to be "wise as serpents but harmless as doves," and snakes were considered sacred by various peoples including Hebrews, Hindus, and Chinese.

name: snares
originalDescription: Primitive hunters used ingenious snares and traps to catch game, while more advanced hunters became expert trappers and fishermen. (68:5.5)
improvedDescription: Ingenious traps used by primitive hunters and blue men to catch game and fish, representing early technological advances in hunting techniques.

name: sneezing
originalDescription: Sneezing was seen by savages as the soul's failed escape, thwarted by the vigilant body, and later became intertwined with religious expressions like “God bless you!” (86:5.9)
improvedDescription: Primitive peoples viewed sneezing as the soul's failed attempt to escape from the body, later leading to religious expressions like "God bless you!" to accompany sneezing.

name: snow
originalDescription: Increased snowfall in highlands caused floods in Euphrates valley, forcing inhabitants to flee to eastern highlands. (78:7.2) No two snowflakes are ever alike, just like children and personalities - always unique within their forms. (111:4.6) Elevated northern highlands and shifting ocean currents caused glaciers to form on vast areas of North America and northern Europe.
