(136:4.6-13) Through Jesus as the new and living way, mortal man can discover the path to eternal realities and ascend from humanity to divinity. (101:3.3) (101:6.17) (117:3.3) (129:4.7) (136:6.11) (141:7.4) (180:3.7) (182:1.20) (196:2.4) Let the wicked forsake his evil way and return to the Lord for abundant pardon. (97:7.7) (131:2.11) At the parting of ways, Jesus boldly chose to assume the offensive in the midst of open warfare. (153:1.2-3) (153:2.5) (153:4.4) By the Spirit's transformation, you are freed from the old way of bondage and empowered to live according to the perfect will of God. (143:2.4) Do not attack old ways, but proclaim new truth in a spirit of peace and love, allowing the Spirit of Truth to work. (178:1.16) The Sons of God, through their bestowals and survival plans, embody the injunction to “Be you perfect, even as your Father in Havona is perfect,” becoming for all creatures “the way, the truth, and the life.” (7:5.1) Having accepted the assignment on the way of life everlasting, fear not, for the Spirit of Truth will always speak, saying “This is the way”. (34:7.8) Isaiah preached the eternal nature of God, His unchanging perfection of reliability, and His promise to guide them saying, “this is the way, walk in it.” (97:5.2) Judas, a successful treasurer and devoted apostle, exemplified the saying “there is a way that seems right but ends in death.” (139:12.5) The way to eternal life is narrow, yet wide enough for all who sincerely seek entrance through the Son. (166:3.3,6)
improvedDescription: A spiritual path or method of living, often referring to God's way versus human ways. Jesus declared himself to be 'the way, the truth, and the life,' establishing a new and living way for mortals to find the Father and achieve spiritual growth. The concept encompasses both the path of divine will and the means by which creatures can ascend from humanity to divinity.

name: Way of Heaven
originalDescription: Confucius taught that morality is based on the Way of Heaven, viewing earthly ways as reflections of the eternal order in the cosmos. (94:6.10)
improvedDescription: A Confucian concept teaching that earthly morality reflects the eternal cosmic order. According to Confucius, temporal civilization mirrors the heavenly pattern, with earthly ways being distorted shadows of the divine order in the cosmos.

name: weak
originalDescription: Jesus said about weakness: “Did John or my chosen apostles resemble weaklings enslaved by fear, preaching the gospel to all, rich and poor, Jew and gentile alike?” (143:1.5) God gives power to the faint, renewing their strength and allowing them to soar like eagles. (97:7.8) The weak have always contended for equal rights, demanding the state to compel the strong to supply their needs and address their deficiencies resulting from indifference and laziness. (70:9.16) The weak indulge in resolutions, but the strong act; life is but a day's work, the act is ours, the consequences God's. (48:7.13) Indwelt by the spirit of the living God, mortal man finds divine mercy and tenderness towards the weak, a trait beyond doubt of the Creator Son in his eternal mission. (26:11.5) Minister to the weak, poor, and young with love and compassion, as Jesus commanded. (192:2.2) National survival demands military preparedness, but only love and brotherhood can prevent the strong from oppressing the weak. (71:4.17) The weak should not be allowed to take advantage of “golden rulers” who strive for a society based on truth, beauty, and goodness. (71:4.16)
improvedDescription: Those lacking strength, courage, or resources, often contrasted with the strong in social and spiritual contexts. According to Jesus, the weak should not be dismissed, as his gospel was not intended only for weaklings, and God gives power to the faint while showing divine mercy and tenderness toward them.

name: wealth
originalDescription: acquiring dangers historically Jesus persons possessing wealth significance of wealth
improvedDescription: Material riches and possessions that can be accumulated through various means including inheritance, trade, genius, or labor. Jesus taught that wealth itself is not sinful, but the love of riches can become spiritually dangerous, emphasizing that material prosperity should be held as a trust and used for the benefit of others.

name: weaning
originalDescription: Cooking food reduces infant mortality and facilitates early weaning, knowledge lost until the twentieth century. (66:5.18) When Eve stopped nursing her children at one year old, she fed them nuts, fruits, and juices instead of animal milk. (74:6.3) Mary did not wean Jesus until they arrived safely in Alexandria, where the family settled and Joseph's career as a contractor began. (123:0.1) Maternal affection proved too strong for infanticide after weaning. (68:6.9) The earlier weaning of babies, thanks to the provision of cow and goat milk, led to a reduction in infant mortality and allowed mothers to bear more children. (84:3.9)
improvedDescription: The process of transitioning infants from breast milk to other foods. Early weaning was facilitated by cooking food and the availability of animal milk, which reduced infant mortality and allowed mothers to bear more children, with some ancient practices involving weaning at one year versus the traditional four to five years.

name: weapons
originalDescription: The term “seventy-seven” was derived from Lamech's exultation over Tubal-Cain's metal weapons. (159:1.7) Old men and cripples were tasked with making tools and weapons before being reassigned to building irrigation works due to age and disease. (69:3.4)
improvedDescription: Tools of warfare and combat, including metal implements that represented technological advancement. The term "seventy-seven" originated from Lamech's boasting about his son Tubal-Cain's superior metal weapons, and weapon-making was traditionally assigned to older men and those unable to perform other labor.

name: weasels
originalDescription: Weasels are part of the thriving group of mammals that evolved from a now-extinct carnivorous creature with characteristics of both cats and seals. (61:2.7) (61:3.13)
improvedDescription: Small carnivorous mammals that evolved from an extinct ancestor resembling a cross between a cat and a seal. Weasels, along with martens, otters, and raccoons, thrived and developed throughout the northern latitudes as part of the diversifying mammalian fauna.

name: weaving
originalDescription: Around 10,000 B.C., highly skilled Andites from a brilliant tribe migrated to Crete, being driven off the mainland by their larger and inferior fellows, they were masters of weaving, among other crafts. (80:7.2) The sudden appearance of the Sumerians in Mesopotamia, marked by their advanced culture including weaving, remains a mystery to investigators. (77:4.7) Caligastia 100 taught weaving, well digging, spring control, and irrigation to advance the material welfare of humans. (66:5.2) Jesus, an expert weaver by age ten, befriended a potter in Nazareth as a child. (123:5.15) Weaving was a cultural achievement practiced in Eden, the second garden where Adam and Eve were invested with kingly robes. (74:2.7) (76:3.8)
improvedDescription: The craft of creating textiles by interlacing threads or fibers. This skill was taught by the Caligastia one hundred and practiced in advanced civilizations including Eden, with Jesus becoming an expert weaver by age ten and skilled Andite migrants bringing weaving expertise to various regions.

name: weddings
originalDescription: The wedding at Cana was a moment of anticipation and belief, leading to an unexpected manifestation of divine power through the creation of wine. (137:3.6) (137:4) Marriage among ancients was associated with charms and superstitions, including human sacrifices and magical rites to ensure fecundity and resist ghosts. (83:4.4-9) Ancient weddings held under trees were rooted in tree worship and beliefs in supernatural forces. (85:2.3) The wedding ceremony was a public affair in ancient times, reflecting the importance of children's status and the community's involvement in marriage. (83:4.1-3) James and Miriam's double wedding brought joy and support to their family, even amidst uncertainty about Jesus's future plans. (128:7.10-11) Jesus attended Martha's wedding in October, then returned for the double wedding of Simon and Jude over two years later. (129:1.6) (134:1.4) The origin of wedding presents dates back to the tradition of giving presents to brides in exchange for sex services before marriage. (82:3.10) The parable of the wedding feast teaches the importance of accepting the king's invitation to the celebration. (173:5)
improvedDescription: Marriage ceremonies that evolved from simple community affairs to elaborate celebrations involving ritual, magic, and public witness. Ancient weddings included various customs for ensuring fertility and warding off evil spirits, with Jesus attending several weddings including the famous one at Cana where he performed his first miracle.

name: Wednesday
originalDescription: It was a Jewish custom to celebrate weddings on Wednesday in Cana, where Jesus led the preliminary wedding procession. (137:4.1) Jesus established the mid-week holiday for rest and recreation for his apostles, urging them to take time for reflection and growth. (138:6.2) (141:3.1) (177:0.1)
improvedDescription: The fourth day of the week, traditionally used for Jewish wedding celebrations and designated by Jesus as a mid-week holiday for rest and recreation for his apostles. This weekly day of relaxation allowed time for reflection and spiritual growth away from regular ministry duties.

name: weeds
originalDescription: The parable of wheat and weeds illustrates how good and evil coexist in the kingdom of heaven until the final harvest. (151:4.1)
improvedDescription: Unwanted plants that grow among crops, used by Jesus in a parable to illustrate how good and evil coexist in the kingdom of heaven. In the parable, weeds sown by an enemy among wheat represent how righteousness and wickedness must grow together until the final harvest and judgment.

name: week
originalDescription: The seven-day week was introduced by the Dalamatia teachers, stemming from the fact that seven was one fourth of twenty-eight, a reminder of spiritual significance with no natural origin. (66:7.17) The sacred 7-day week's incidental origin was based on Adam and Eve's 6-day inspection of the Garden of Urantia. (74:8.1)
improvedDescription: A seven-day period of time measurement introduced by the Dalamatia teachers, derived from one-fourth of the 28-day lunar month. The weekly cycle gained sacred significance when Adam and Eve spent six days surveying the Garden of Eden, establishing the tradition of the seventh day for worship.

name: weights
originalDescription: The first weights were grains of wheat and other cereals, while the first medium of exchange was a fish or a goat, later transitioning to the cow as a unit of barter. (69:4.5) One Jerusem gradant is equal to 5 Urantia ounces. (46:1.2) The weight disparity between electrons and atoms is immense, with an electron weighing over two thousand times less than a proton despite being almost as small. (42:6.7-8)
improvedDescription: The first weights were grains of wheat and other cereals, while the first medium of exchange was a fish or a goat, later transitioning to the cow as a unit of barter. On Jerusem, one gradant equals almost exactly ten Urantia ounces. The weight disparity between subatomic particles is immense, with an electron weighing over two thousand times less than a proton despite being almost as small.

name: welfare (government)
improvedDescription: Government-administered welfare programs designed to provide social support and assistance to citizens in need.

name: well-doing
originalDescription: Be not weary in the great work of teaching mortal man he is a son of God, for the invitation-call is always - Come to me all you who labor and are heavy laden. (163:6.7) (174:0.2) (192:2.9)
improvedDescription: The practice of doing good works and maintaining righteous conduct. Jesus encouraged his followers to be not weary in well-doing, emphasizing perseverance in service to others and faithfulness to spiritual duties even in difficult circumstances.

name: wells
originalDescription: Among the first private possessions were water holes and wells, guarded by the fetish practice before laws evolved to protect belongings. (69:9.12) Caligastia 100 taught well digging to promote material welfare and advancement in human society. (66:5.2) Nalda, woman at the well, received the living water from Jesus and became a daughter of the Most High. (143:5)
improvedDescription: Water sources that were among the first private possessions, initially guarded by fetish practices before laws evolved to protect belongings. The Caligastia 100 taught well digging to promote material welfare and human advancement. Jesus met the Samaritan woman Nalda at Jacob's well, where he offered her living water.

name: werewolves
originalDescription: Ancient beliefs in souls entering animals culminated in the werewolf concept of animal identification, allowing a person to transform into a wolf or other creature for nocturnal exploits. (86:5.12)
improvedDescription: Ancient beliefs in souls entering animals culminated in the werewolf concept of animal identification, allowing a person to transform into a wolf or other creature for nocturnal exploits.

name: West Indies
originalDescription: The West Indies is a region where high elevations of land rose, the youngest mountains being in the Rocky Mountain system. (59:1.5) (60:4.3) (61:1.13)
improvedDescription: A region where high elevations of land rose during various geological periods, with the entire Caribbean region being highly elevated. The West Indies contains some of the mid-age mountains and chalk deposits from ancient periods.

name: Western civilization
originalDescription: Christianity suffers as part of the guilt-ridden society of Western civilization, tainted by science without idealism, politics without principles, and wealth without work. (195:10.20)
improvedDescription: Christianity suffers as part of the guilt-ridden society of Western civilization, which staggers under the burden of tolerating science without idealism, politics without principles, wealth without work, and industry without morality.

name: Western Ghats
originalDescription: Dravidian settlements along seacoast of Western Ghats had maritime relationships with Sumeria. (79:3.6)
improvedDescription: A mountain range in India where Dravidian settlements along the seacoast maintained maritime relationships with Sumeria, owing their prominence to these sea-based trade connections.

name: whales
originalDescription: Placental mammals that returned to sea evolved into modern whales and seals. (49:2.17) (61:2.11) (65:2.12)
improvedDescription: Marine mammals that evolved from placental mammals that returned to the sea. These creatures, along with dolphins, porpoises, seals, and sea lions, represent a whole tribe of mammals that deserted the land to take up permanent residence in the oceans.

name: wheat
originalDescription: Amos said God would sift Israel like wheat, symbolizing the unchanging justice of Yahweh. (97:4.3) The apostles rubbed wheat on Sabbath, causing the spies to accuse Jesus of breaking the law. (147:6.4) The first weights were grains of wheat and other cereals, with fish or a goat as the first medium of exchange; later, the cow became a unit of barter. (69:4.5) God, like nature, permits good and evil to coexist until the end of life, just as wheat grows together with weeds. (130:1.6) The descendants of Adam traded improved wheat in the highlands of Mesopotamia, where they mingled with the descendants of Adamson. (81:1.7) The parable of the grain of wheat teaches that selflessly laying down one's life can lead to a more abundant existence on earth and in heaven. (174:5.8) The parable of wheat and weeds teaches that good and evil will coexist until the final judgment. (151:4.1)
improvedDescription: A grain crop that served as one of the first weights in ancient commerce and was cultivated by Adam's descendants in Mesopotamia. Jesus used wheat in parables to teach about spiritual growth, sacrifice, and the coexistence of good and evil until final judgment. The apostles' act of rubbing wheat on the Sabbath sparked controversy about religious law observance.

name: wheels
originalDescription: Prince’s staff first taught use of the wheel for the facilitation of traction. (66:5.5) By 5000 B.C., vehicles equipped with wheels became a common feature in the spread of cultural civilization through trade channels. (81:3.6)
improvedDescription: Prince's staff first taught the use of the wheel for facilitating traction, and by 5000 B.C., wheeled vehicles had become common in spreading cultural civilization through trade channels.

name: whirlwind
originalDescription: The Father does not reveal himself in the whirlwind, but speaks within the human heart as a still, small voice, guiding with love and mercy. (148:6.10) Though we may weep in the darkness, joy will come in the morning for those who fear and seek the Lord sincerely and truthfully. (131:2.9)
improvedDescription: While ancient scriptures spoke of God revealing himself in the whirlwind, The Urantia Book teaches that the Father does not reveal himself in such dramatic ways, but rather speaks within the human heart as a still, small voice.

name: white race
originalDescription: composition divisions of white race history the real “white race” in normal planets
improvedDescription: The modern white races are descendants of the blue man as modified by mixture with yellow and red peoples, and later greatly enhanced by assimilating much of the violet Adamic race. These races are predominantly descended from ancient blue men but admixed with all other races, with no pure racial divisions remaining in the modern world.

name: white smiths
improvedDescription: Skilled craftsmen who worked with metals other than iron, in contrast to blacksmiths who worked primarily with iron and steel.

name: whole
improvedDescription: The unified totality or complete entity, often contrasted with its constituent parts in discussions of cosmic relationships and universal organization.

name: wickedness
improvedDescription: Deliberate evil behavior or moral corruption that violates divine principles and spiritual values.

name: widows
originalDescription: Ancients customarily committed suicide on husbands' graves in order to appease ghosts and follow cultural norms of the time. (87:2.7) Hypocritical rulers publicly seek recognition, while secretly laying hold to widows' houses for profit. (175:1.9) In Rome, Jesus and Ganid comforted a widow and her five children after the accidental death of her husband, helping secure a job for the eldest son to support the family. (132:6.2) When Jesus arrived in Nain, he raised the only son of the widow of Nain from the dead, much to the amazement of the villagers. (146:6.2) The rich widow of Tyre sought healing for her many infirmities, offering money in a futile attempt to purchase the power of God. (149:1.9) Under certain mores, widowhood was greatly feared, with widows facing blame, death, or social restriction. (82:3.12) As evening fell and the crowds dispersed, Jesus and his followers witnessed a poor widow's profound act of generosity, with her tiny offering of “widow's mites” surpassing all the wealthy contributions. (172:4.2)
improvedDescription: Women who had lost their husbands faced varied circumstances throughout history, from ancient customs requiring suicide on husbands' graves to social restrictions and poverty. Jesus showed compassion to widows, raising the son of the widow of Nain and commending the poor widow's sacrificial offering in the temple.

name: wilderness of Judea
originalDescription: John the Baptist tended sheep in the wilderness of Judea, where he fraternized with ascetic herdsmen and the Nazarite brotherhood, supported by sheep raising and generous gifts. (135:2.3)
improvedDescription: The region where John the Baptist tended sheep along tributaries leading to the Dead Sea at Engedi, home to Nazarites and ascetic herdsmen who supported themselves through sheep raising and gifts from wealthy Jews.

name: will
improvedDescription: The faculty of conscious choice and decision-making that characterizes personality, ranging from mortal free will to the absolute volition of the Universal Father.

name: will of God
originalDescription: what is doing the will of God Jesus limitations to God’s will misconceptions
improvedDescription: The divine purpose and plan of the Universal Father, which mortals are called to discover and follow. Doing God's will involves progressive spiritual communion, choosing the highest values, and seeking to become perfect as the Father is perfect. It represents the way of God and partnership with divine choice in all circumstances.

name: wind
originalDescription: The ancients believed winds were produced by birds, fearing and worshiping all winged creatures in accordance with the golden rule. (85:3.4) Different regions regard wind as either good or evil, just as diverse cultures worship various natural phenomena. (85:4.2) Those born of spirit are like the wind, unseen but their effects are felt. (142:6.5) Those who seek the Lord with all their heart shall find salvation in abundance. (131:2.9)
improvedDescription: A natural phenomenon that ancient peoples believed was produced by the wings of birds, leading them to fear and worship winged creatures. Different cultures regarded wind as either beneficial or malignant, with some regions viewing it as divine while others saw it as demonic. Jesus used wind as a metaphor for those born of the spirit, whose effects are visible though the spirit itself remains unseen.

name: wine
originalDescription: The cup of wine, emblem of divine Spirit of Truth, symbolizes the bestowal and ministry of the new dispensation of grace and truth as Jesus blesses it for his apostles. (179:5.1-3) Jesus refused the drugged wine offered to lessen his suffering during crucifixion, choosing to retain his human consciousness until the very end in order to conquer death by voluntary submission to the full human experience. (187:2.3) Wine replaced blood in adoption ceremonies, symbolized by touching cups and sealed by drinking the beverage. (70:3.8) Jesus moistened his parched lips with wine on the cross. (187:3.5) Jesus turned water into wine at the wedding feast in Cana, surprising even himself with the manifestation of his Messianic power. (137:4.10-13) (138:8.5) Melchizedek offered bread and wine as a sacrament of a substitute for the older sacrifice of flesh and blood. (93:4.14) The new gospel of the kingdom must not conform to the old, for new wine must be put into fresh wine skins. (140:6.2) (147:7.2-3) The sacrament of cakes and wine in the Mother of God cult replaced older human sacrifices and has roots in Hebrew Passover ceremonies, influencing the Christian sacrament. (89:9.2) The wine served at Passover was considered by Jesus as “the water of life” and “the fruit of the vine.” (127:6.7) (179:1.1) (179:2.2) Wounds treated with oil and wine were an early medicine for the sick, dating back to ancient civilizations like the Greeks and Sumerians. (90:4.9) (164:1.3)
improvedDescription: A fermented beverage that held deep religious significance in various cultures and ceremonies. Jesus transformed it into a symbol of divine ministry when he blessed the cup at the Last Supper, declaring it the emblem of the new dispensation of grace and truth. Wine featured prominently in adoption ceremonies, healing practices, and religious sacraments, including Melchizedek's substitution of bread and wine for blood sacrifice, and Jesus's miraculous transformation of water into wine at Cana.

name: wings
originalDescription: The belief that angels have wings originated from visualizations of transport seraphim in transition for interplanetary transit. (39:5.11-14)
improvedDescription: The erroneous belief that angels possess wings originated from human observations of transport seraphim being prepared for interplanetary travel. What appeared to be wings were actually energy insulators or friction shields that extended from head to foot, serving as protective barriers during transit through universe energy currents.

name: Wisconsin
originalDescription: Ridges of ancient, fossil-bearing stone extend from Pennsylvania to Mexico, but none showcase earth's history quite like Wisconsin. (58:7.4) (58:7.7)
improvedDescription: A North American state notable for containing ancient fossil-bearing stone layers that showcase Earth's geological history. The St. Croix valley of Wisconsin graphically displays one hundred and twenty-seven successive lava flows with water submergence and rock deposition, with about sixty-five to seventy stratified records of past ages currently exposed to view.

name: wisdom
originalDescription: In the vast Paradise domain, the masters of philosophy conduct elaborate courses in the 70 divisions of wisdom to co-ordinate experiences and compose knowledge in uniform agreement. (27:6.3) In the ideal state, education is a lifelong pursuit where citizens gradually experience ascending levels of wisdom, from knowledge of things to cosmic insight and God-consciousness. (71:7.2,5-13) Ask God for wisdom and spiritual strength to guide you through difficulties, as you courageously tackle problems on the journey towards divine perfection. (91:6.5) (91:9.7) The woman, despite being spiritually distant from God, is on a progressive path towards an eternal goal, unobstructed by worldly wisdom and spiritual pride. (147:5.8) definition human wisdom sources of wisdom
improvedDescription: The highest achievement of intellectual and spiritual development, representing the coordination of knowledge, experience, and divine insight. True wisdom encompasses the consciousness of meaning elevated by cosmic mind and activated by the adjutant spirit of wisdom. It progresses through ascending levels from knowledge of things to God-consciousness, ultimately leading to worship and spiritual understanding.

name: Wisdom, Book of
originalDescription: Amenemope's wisdom was copied into the Book of Proverbs. (95:4.5) The concept of El Shaddai, influenced by the Book of Wisdom, was a complex combination of several teachings including Amenemope, Ikhnaton, and Melchizedek, ultimately merging with Yahweh beliefs. (96:1.5) Philo possessed a copy of the Book of Wisdom, teaching that riches are fleeting, earthly things are temporary, to turn from human words to divine acts, and that man plans while God decides. (95:4.3)
improvedDescription: An ancient wisdom text by Amenemope that significantly influenced Hebrew scripture, with chapters from Proverbs taken almost verbatim from this work. The book taught that earthly riches are fleeting and emphasized turning from human words to divine acts, with the philosophy that while humans plan, God ultimately decides outcomes.

name: Wisdom of Solomon
originalDescription: The Wisdom of Solomon reflects the influence of both Platonic philosophy and Stoic doctrines among Hellenized Jewish beliefs. (121:6.3) Fate and chance dictate outcomes, as seen in the Wisdom of Solomon's statement that “the race is not to the swift”. (86:1.6)
improvedDescription: A Hellenistic Jewish text that exemplified the influence of both Platonic philosophy and Stoic doctrines on Jewish religious thought. The work reflects themes of fate and chance governing human affairs, including the famous passage that 'the race is not to the swift' but to those whom fate and chance favor.

name: wise men (Magi)
originalDescription: Certain wise men on earth were informed of Michael's impending arrival by seraphim, just as the wise men of Jesus' birth were notified. (119:7.6) The wise men from Mesopotamia found baby Jesus and left gifts with Mary. (122:8.6) The interview with Herod left him unsatisfied, as the wise men could not provide him with the whereabouts of the newborn “king of the Jews.” (122:10.1) Wise men arrived in Bethlehem guided by a legend of a star that never shone, seeking the “light of life” born in the manger, unaware of the angelic anthems sung at the noontide birth of Jesus. (122:8.5-7)
improvedDescription: Chaldean priests from Mesopotamia, led by Ardnon, who were informed by seraphim of Michael's birth and traveled to Bethlehem to find the infant Jesus. These wise men, seeking the prophesied 'light of life,' found the three-week-old babe and left gifts with Mary. Their visit was the only supernatural event associated with Jesus's birth, though later legend created the appealing myth of the star of Bethlehem.

name: wish fulfillment
originalDescription: The apostles, interpreting the Master's words to justify their intense longings, believed Jesus would return to establish the kingdom after a brief visit with his Father. (181:0.1) Moral values do not grow out of wish fulfillment. (100:3.2) Wisdom comes from facing facts and adjusting them to ideals, not from preferring optimistic illusions to reality. (160:1.7) (160:4.13-14)
improvedDescription: The tendency to interpret events and teachings according to one's intense desires rather than objective reality. The apostles exemplified this when they interpreted Jesus's promise to return as meaning he would establish an earthly kingdom after a brief visit with the Father. True moral values and wisdom cannot grow from such wishful thinking but require honest acknowledgment of facts and adjustment to ideals.

name: witchcraft
originalDescription: The practice of witchcraft involved magic performed by irregular spirits. (90:2.2-3) Ancient people attributed deaths to witchcraft, leading to the execution of accused witches. (90:3.7) Shamans, the original aristocracy exempt from tribal restrictions, dressed well and had multiple wives, rose to power by suppressing rivals through accusations of witchcraft, dominating chiefs and kings. (90:2.12) Divination, sorcery, and witchcraft are pure superstitions, grounded in ignorant minds and unfounded beliefs. (150:3.8-11) In ancient times, progress meant death as a sorcerer, yet the slow advance of mores and epochal revelation can uplift and modify natural religion. (92:3.5) During his stay at Endor, Jesus recounted the tale of King Saul and the witch of Endor to his apostles, assuring them that the mischievous midwayers posing as spirits of the dead would soon be restrained. (146:7.1) Private magic, used to bring evil on enemies, was dispensed by witches and sorcerers, while public magic aimed for the good of the whole tribe. (88:6.2) (91:1.2) Wizards' powers were believed to be attained by consuming human flesh in a solemn ceremony of revenge, where an enemy's ghost could be destroyed or fused with that of the eater. (89:5.5)
improvedDescription: The practice of magic performed by irregular, unrecognized spirits, distinguished from shamancraft which involved miracles by recognized tribal gods. Ancient peoples attributed many deaths and illnesses to witchcraft, leading to the execution of accused witches. Practitioners included sorcerers, wizards, and witches who were believed to dispense private magic for personal gain, while shamans performed public magic for tribal welfare.

name: withered hand
originalDescription: The man with the withered hand was healed by Jesus on the Sabbath day, demonstrating the importance of doing good to others. (148:7)
improvedDescription: The withered hand refers to a physical condition that was healed by Jesus on the Sabbath day, demonstrating the importance of doing good to others.

name: witnesses
originalDescription: Jewish law required two witnesses to agree before laying charges, preventing Judas from testifying against Jesus, disconcerting even the false witnesses. (184:3.6)
improvedDescription: Jewish law required at least two witnesses to agree before charges could be laid against a prisoner. This legal requirement prevented Judas from testifying against Jesus as a traitor, and the contradictory testimony of false witnesses disconcerted even the perjurers themselves.

name: wives
originalDescription: Throughout history, wives have always been subject to more restrictive taboos than husbands, rooted in the concept of marital fidelity and the protection of lineage and inheritance. (82:2.5) (82:4.4) Leaving wives and family for the sake of the kingdom is necessary for true discipleship. (150:4.3) (163:3.4) (167:2.2) (171:2.2) A wife's qualifications were based on her ability to work hard and bear children, with added value if she had borne a child before marriage. (82:3.7) Even in primitive societies, husbands could escape punishment for killing their wives if they had paid for them. (70:10.11)
improvedDescription: Throughout history, wives have been subject to more restrictive taboos than husbands, rooted in concepts of marital fidelity and the protection of lineage and inheritance. In primitive societies, a wife's value was based on her ability to work hard and bear children, and husbands could escape punishment for killing their wives if they had paid for them.

name: wizards
originalDescription: The practitioners of the black art called sorcerers, magicians, wizards, witches, enchanters, necromancers, conjurers, and soothsayers were once known by various titles including priests, seers, shamans, or medicine men, when practicing what was then termed white art. (90:2.2)
improvedDescription: Wizards were practitioners of the black art along with sorcerers, magicians, witches, enchanters, necromancers, conjurers, and soothsayers. When practicing what was considered white art, these same individuals were known by titles such as priests, seers, shamans, or medicine men.

name: Wolvering
originalDescription: Wolvering is a neighboring universe to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton. (32:2.12)
improvedDescription: Wolvering is one of the seven nearest neighboring universes to Nebadon in the superuniverse circuit of Orvonton.

name: wolves
originalDescription: Wolves were everywhere and hunted by Neanderthalers 800,000 years ago in Europe. (64:4.2)
improvedDescription: Wolves were abundant throughout Europe 800,000 years ago and were actively hunted by the Neanderthalers. They belonged to the dog family along with foxes and thrived in northern latitudes during this period.

name: women
originalDescription: among primitives Adam and Eve among Jews among early Christians equality with men nature of women
improvedDescription: Women throughout history have been subject to more restrictive taboos and social limitations than men, often treated as property rather than equals. Jesus revolutionized women's status by recognizing them as spiritual equals, commissioning female evangelists, and teaching that in the kingdom of heaven there is no distinction between male and female as sons and daughters of God.

name: women’s corps
originalDescription: The women's corps ministered to the sick and taught the gospel during house-to-house visitation in Capernaum while Jesus recovered from rejection in Nazareth. (151:0.2) Fifty women were trained for home visitation and ministering work in the women's corps, which included Perpetua, Simon Peter's wife, as its leader alongside Abner, and other prominent women such as the wives of Philip and Matthew and the mother of James and John. (163:7.3) Abner in charge of the women’s corps led them in labor and ministry in the cities of Perea, including Perpetua, Simon Peter’s wife, and the wives of Philip and Matthew, and the mother of James and John. (163:7.3) The women’s corps preached in brothels, exemplifying their usefulness and vindicating their selection at Magdala. (150:2.2) The women's corps in Perea, that became numbering sixty-two, took over ministration to the sick during a period of spiritual growth and absence of miracle working, paving the way for wide acceptance of Jesus' teachings among the better classes of citizens. (165:0.2) The original women’s corps joyfully joined the festive crowd's procession into Jerusalem. (172:3.8) The women's corps believed Jesus resurrected despite initial doubt from the apostles and other men. (190:1.2) Philip's wife, a fearless and efficient member of the women’s corps, bravely continued her evangelistic work with her husband, even when faced with persecution and ultimately sacrificing her life for her faith. (139:5.11) (163:7.3) Jesus' morontia appearance to the women's corps at Joseph of Arimathea's home brought a message of universal fellowship and a call to spread the gospel of sonship with God. (190:3.1) Mary Magdalene was the chief spokesman for the women’s corps, boldly proclaiming the Master's resurrection with great circumspection and devotion. (190:0.5) Mary Magdalene and the other women evangelists faithfully and effectively labored to enlighten and uplift their downtrodden sisters, none deserted during the tragic episode of Jesus' life on earth. (150:2.3) (153:5.2) Perpetua led an expanded group of women in ministering and evangelizing in Perea, alongside other dedicated members of the women's corps. (163:7.3)
improvedDescription: The women's corps was a group of female evangelists commissioned by Jesus to teach the gospel and minister to the sick. Originally consisting of ten women selected by Jesus, it later expanded to include twelve and eventually sixty-two women who worked alongside the male apostles and evangelists, with Mary Magdalene serving as their chief spokesman.

name: women’s gallery
improvedDescription: The women's gallery was a separate section in Jewish synagogues and the Jerusalem temple where women were confined during religious services, reflecting the restrictive social customs that prevented women from participating alongside men in worship.

name: wood
originalDescription: Early commerce in wood accelerated urban life and trade in central Asia. (79:1.4) The ancient practice of rapping on wood perpetuates customs of tree worship. (85:2.5) Jesus, a skilled carpenter and boatbuilder from Nazareth, revolutionized boatmaking techniques in Galilee with his innovative designs. (126:1.6) (129:1.2-3) Jesus wrote on smooth white boards, reflecting the improved economic condition of his family in Nazareth. (126:1.6) (187:1.2)
improvedDescription: Wood was an important material in ancient commerce and craftsmanship. Jesus, as a skilled carpenter and boatbuilder, revolutionized boatmaking techniques in Galilee with innovative wood-working methods, and the improved economic conditions of his family were reflected in their use of smooth white wooden boards for writing.

name: woolly mammoths
originalDescription: Woolly mammoths roamed from Mexico to Canada, while mastodons preferred the sheltered fringes of the forest regions until both were rapidly reduced by the advancing ice age. (61:5.7) (61:7.13-14)
improvedDescription: Large, prehistoric elephants covered in thick fur that roamed from Mexico to Canada during the ice age. They preferred open prairies and migrated with the advancing and retreating glaciers before becoming extinct in North America.

name: word of God
originalDescription: The Word of God, bestowed in flesh, is divine sustenance for the soul. (153:3.2) Divine truth can be discerned regardless of its human origin; seek the word of God beyond old records and in the spiritual simplicity of a sincere child. (155:6.12) The divine Sons are the “Word of God,” while the children of the Spirit are the “Act of God.” (10:3.3) The Eternal Son is the perfect expression of the Word of God, residing at the center of all creation. (6:0.1) (6:1.3) (6:2.2) (6:8.3) (14:6.15) (20:5.1) The word of God, revealed in the Son as the bread of life, is the united life of the Father and the Son in one gift. (179:5.3) Belief in the gospel is belief in Him who sent me, the light of the world, offering salvation and eternal life through truth and mercy. (174:5.7) Paul of Tarsus never imagined his letters would be seen as the word of God when he wrote them. (98:7.9) Take a stand against traditional scriptures regarded as the word of God and embrace a spirit of adventurous and progressive faith. (155:6.2) about Jesus being the “word of God”, see the link about Jesus | Jesus:personality and nature
improvedDescription: The divine expression of God's truth and nature, primarily manifested through the Eternal Son as the perfect Word of the Father. In spiritual context, it represents divine sustenance for the soul that can be discerned regardless of human origin or traditional religious records.

name: words
improvedDescription: Verbal expressions or language used for communication.

name: workmanship
originalDescription: Jesus, a painstaking workman, wasted little time on trifles, focusing instead on the essentials of his craft. (134:9.7)
improvedDescription: Jesus was a painstaking workman who took great care with his handiwork, focusing on the essentials of any undertaking rather than wasting time on trifles, and experiencing satisfaction in completing commendable work.

name: works
originalDescription: Works of self-righteousness cannot buy the favor of God, for salvation is a free gift based on faith, not outward deeds. (167:5.1) Salvation is not achieved by blaming evil influences, but by acknowledging and overcoming one's own natural tendencies. (143:2.5)
improvedDescription: Self-righteous deeds or outward actions that cannot earn God's favor or salvation. Salvation is a free gift based on faith, not on works or blaming external influences for one's failings.

name: world (earth)
improvedDescription: The planet Earth, also known as Urantia in cosmic terminology.

name: world government
improvedDescription: A unified planetary governmental system.

name: world of the cross
originalDescription: When the bestowal Sons enter the portals of death, they reappear on the third day, but Urantia, known as 'the world of the cross,' does not always impose a tragic end like that of Jesus of Nazareth. (20:6.6) (57:8.6) (119:8.8) (188:4.1)
improvedDescription: Urantia's designation throughout the local universe due to the tragic crucifixion of Jesus, making it locally known as the world where a Creator Son met an unusually cruel death, unlike the more considerate reception given to bestowal Sons on most other worlds.

name: world of the Father
originalDescription: The seven circling worlds around the Father's world serve as detention spheres for interned groups of the Lucifer rebellion in Satania, while Edentia, the constellation capital, lacks similar prison worlds. (45:1.10) The silent sphere of transitional culture world #7, Jerusem satellite, is the World of the Father, where loyal Panoptians serve as caretakers and builders under the guidance of Michael. (45:1.9) (53:7.1) Ellanora of Panoptia led her people to remain loyal to Michael, serving as caretakers under the Melchizedeks on the Father's sphere and its detention worlds, guided by the personal orders of Michael for their future use on the seventh Jerusem transition world. (53:7.1) Salvation was offered to rebels by Michael, but only the angels and lower orders accepted, now laboring with the Panoptians on the Father's world. (53:9.1)
improvedDescription: The silent seventh transitional culture world orbiting Jerusem, featuring a great temple of light where all system beings are welcomed as worshipers. It currently serves as headquarters for detention spheres housing Lucifer rebellion internees and is maintained by loyal Panoptians under Michael's guidance.

name: world of the Sons
originalDescription: The transitional culture world #5 is the headquarters of the divine Sons of all orders, including the creature-trinitized sons, with the surrounding seven worlds devoted to specific groupings of these sons. (45:1.7)
improvedDescription: The fifth transitional culture world orbiting Jerusem that serves as headquarters for divine Sons of all orders, including creature-trinitized sons, with seven surrounding worlds devoted to specific groupings of these divinely related sons.

name: world of the Spirit
originalDescription: Transitional culture world #6 serves as the system rendezvous for high personalities of the Infinite Spirit, with no representation of the Spirit present. (45:1.8)
improvedDescription: Transitional culture world number 6 that serves as the system rendezvous for high personalities of the Infinite Spirit, with no representation of the Spirit present.

name: World War I
originalDescription: The unfortunate re-establishment of submerged political groups during World War I hindered the progression of political sovereignty from smaller to larger organizations. (134:5.9)
improvedDescription: A major conflict that unfortunately re-established submerged political sovereignty of numerous small groups in Europe, hindering the normal progression of political sovereignty from smaller to larger organizations.

name: worlds
improvedDescription: Celestial spheres and planetary bodies throughout the universe, including both inhabited and architectural worlds.

name: worm of the dust
originalDescription: Man, originally a worm of the dust, becomes divine in destiny when indwelt by the spirit of the Father in heaven. (149:6.9)
improvedDescription: A humble description of man's mortal nature and origin, contrasted with his divine destiny when indwelt by the Father's spirit.

name: worship
originalDescription: Worship, contemplation of the spiritual, must alternate with service, contact with material reality, for a balanced and fulfilling life. (143:7.3) Worship encompasses more than just moral conduct and social service, as the adoration of the abstract beautiful and exaltation of nature are not forms of true worship of God. (5:5.4) The quality of spiritual worship defies estimation like spiritual joy, love, and moral values cannot be quantified. (196:3.18) Religion is the consciousness of universe fellowship through divine worship, transcending science, society, philosophy, and morality. (5:5.2) Adoration and worship of the Universal Father is conveyed to Father by Adjusters through the personality circuit. (5:3.2) (5:6.11) Worshipping the Eternal Son and Infinite Spirit is also worshipping the Universal Father, as they are all equally divine and eternal. (6:2.3) The Father in heaven always accepts sincere worship, regardless of how primitive or crude the concept of Deity may be. (142:3.3,8) The defense of outgrown systems of worship hinders the growth and evolution of spiritual practices, impeding the advancement of true faith. (195:10.8) True worship is found in giving thanks to the Lord, as Jesus exhorted his followers to do. (146:2.15) The depth of worship is determined by the quality of creature perception, reflecting differing concepts of the purpose of religion. (5:4.8) (27:7.1) The evolution of religion does not depend on revelation as the human mind, guided by universal spirit bestowal, is fully capable of developing worship. (86:0.1) The worship of wealth-power results in value distortion. (70:2.13) The value of beauty in worship was emphasized by Jesus, who taught that simplicity and nature-like surroundings elicit true spiritual communion. (167:6.5-6) In enlightened and reflective worship, wisdom is transcended and ultimately leads to the finality of thought. (112:2.11-13) definition primitive worship Jesus teachings practice of worship on Paradise
improvedDescription: The highest privilege and first duty of all created intelligences, being the conscious and joyous recognition of the intimate personal relationships between Creators and creatures. Worship is the act of acknowledging divine truth and reality, ranging from primitive fear-based reverence to the supreme spiritual communion experienced by Paradise beings.

name: worship builders
originalDescription: The worship builders are divine builders and celestial artisans, constructing exquisite temples of worship in the spirit and morontia realms. (44:3.5)
improvedDescription: Divine builders and celestial artisans who construct exquisite temples of worship in the spirit and morontia realms.

name: wounds
originalDescription: The treatment of wounds with oil and wine was an ancient and effective medical practice, lost when the secrets of healing remedies became known. (90:4.9) (164:1.3)
improvedDescription: Physical injuries that were effectively treated in ancient times with oil and wine, a medical practice that lost its effectiveness when the secrets of healing remedies became known.

name: wrath
improvedDescription: Divine or human anger, often associated with justice and judgment in religious contexts.

name: writing
originalDescription: alphabets Early writing methods Jesus
improvedDescription: The art of recording thoughts and language in permanent form, evolving from primitive methods like message sticks and picture writing to alphabetic systems. The Dalamatians developed the first alphabet of twenty-five characters, while modern writing originated from early trade records.

name: Wyoming
originalDescription: Fresh-water fossils from the Morrison beds of Colorado, Wyoming, and Montana reveal a past era of abundant inland lakes. (60:2.5)
improvedDescription: A western American state containing Morrison beds with abundant fresh-water fossils that reveal a past era of inland lakes during the Mesozoic period.

name: X rays
originalDescription: The suns of space are not very dense. Too great a density would hold back the light by opacity until the pressure of light energy reached the point of explosion. Five meters of surface area with the density of Urantia would effectively prevent the escape of all X-rays and all light energies until the pressure would cause an explosion (41:5.1) X rays enable light to escape suns by penetrating all space and assisting in maintaining ultimatonic associations of energy. (41:9.1) The sun's interior energizes electrons, creating a vast X-ray generator that propels these powerful emanations out into space. (41:5.3-5) X rays are the great levelers of atomic existence in the intense temperatures found within suns. (41:7.1) The mutant traits in the first Nodite generation were caused by changes in the germ plasm, similar to how scientists modify life plasm of plants and animals with X rays. (77:2.5) X rays activate preatomic matter to become slightly gravity-responsive. (42:6.2) The equilibrium between gravity and heat in the sun determines the stability of its outer layers, with the pressure of X rays supporting its continued existence. (41:9.4) X rays are produced by the electromagnetic commotion upon an electron being suddenly stopped. (42:5.8) X rays are part of the ten octaves of wave energy above visible sunlight in the superuniverse of Orvonton. (42:5.1)
improvedDescription: High-energy electromagnetic radiation produced when electrons are suddenly stopped, forming part of ten octaves of wave energy above visible light. X rays serve as great levelers of atomic existence within stars, enable light to escape from suns by penetrating space, and activate preatomic matter to become slightly gravity-responsive.

name: Xenophanes
originalDescription: Xenophanes embraced a pantheistic deity concept in his teaching of one God. (98:2.6)
improvedDescription: Greek philosopher who taught the concept of one God, though his deity concept was too pantheistic to represent a personal Father to mortal humanity.

name: Yahweh
originalDescription: An anthropomorphic Yahweh holds greater religious value than a remote Absolute in Buddhism or Brahmanism, unifying philosophy and harmonizing cosmology but not vital to religious development. (94:11.12) Yahweh, believed to have created both good and evil, represents the evolving understanding of God in the Jewish scriptures recounted by Jesus. (142:3.9) influence in several peoples Evolution of the concept
improvedDescription: The evolving Hebrew concept of God that began as a tribal deity of Mount Horeb and gradually developed into the supreme concept of a universal Creator Father. This progressive transformation from a jealous, anthropomorphic god to a loving, merciful Father represents one of the greatest achievements in religious evolution, influencing nearly half the world's population.

name: Yahwehites
originalDescription: The clash between Yahwehites and Baalites was a socioeconomic battle over land ownership, which evolved into a religious struggle led by Elijah towards monotheism. (97:3) The Yahwehites believed land was an inalienable gift from God, not to be sold or mortgaged. (97:3.2) The Yahwehites, southern or wandering Arabian tribes, viewed land as a divine gift to their clan, insisting it could not be bought or sold. (97:3.2)
improvedDescription: Southern Arabian tribes who believed in Yahweh and viewed land as an inalienable gift from God that could not be sold or mortgaged. Their conflict with the Baalites was primarily a socioeconomic clash over land ownership ideologies rather than religious differences.

name: yaks
originalDescription: The yak was domesticate by 5000 B.C., alongside other beasts of burden such as the ox, camel, and horse, utilized by ancient Mesopotamian, Turkestan, and Chinese farmers. (81:2.12)
improvedDescription: Large, long-haired bovine animals domesticated by 5000 B.C. and used as beasts of burden alongside oxen, camels, and horses by ancient Mesopotamian, Turkestan, and Chinese farmers.

name: yang
improvedDescription: One half of the Chinese philosophical concept of complementary forces in nature, representing the active, masculine, light principle that balances with yin.

name: Yangtze River
originalDescription: Chinese culture along the Yangtze River dates back to 18,000 B.C. (79:6.5) (79:8.5)
improvedDescription: Major river in China where advanced Chinese culture and learning centers developed, with ancestors of the Chinese establishing strong cultural foundations along its banks by 20,000 years ago. Cultural union between Yangtze and Yellow River centers was achieved between 4000 and 500 B.C.

name: yawn
originalDescription: Belief in malignant ghosts entering the mouth during a yawn fueled the fear in early ancestor worship practices. (87:3.2)
improvedDescription: A natural bodily function that early ancestor worshipers feared because they believed malignant ghosts could enter their bodies through their open mouths during yawning.

name: yearning
originalDescription: Guilty of chronic yearning, the followers of Jesus were urged to take the kingdom by spiritual assault rather than simply regretting the past, whining over the present, and vainly hoping for the future. (155:1.3)
improvedDescription: A chronic attitude of longing that Jesus criticized in his followers, urging them instead to take the kingdom of heaven by spiritual assault rather than regretting the past, whining about the present, and vainly hoping for the future.

name: years
originalDescription: The Universal Father's plans are steadfast and immutable, with a thousand years in his sight like yesterday in the past. (2:2.2)
improvedDescription: Units of time that represent but a moment in God's eternal perspective, as a thousand years in the Universal Father's sight are like yesterday when it is past.

name: yellow race
originalDescription: The yellow race consolidated central Asia in 15,000 B.C. (78:1.7) (78:3.8) The yellow race drifted into great darkness following the Caligastia apostasy, but experienced a brilliant age under Singlangton's leadership proclaiming the worship of the “One Truth.” (64:6.15) The expanding yellow race drove the Andonites from Asia, pushing them north into Siberia and west into Turkestan. (78:1.5) (79:6.1) The red race was ultimately driven out of Asia into North America by their yellow brethren, who proved to be socially and collectively superior in fostering racial civilization. (64:6.4,14) (64:7.4) (78:1.5-7) The yellow race entered China in 300,000 B.C., driving off debased Neanderthalers contaminated with animal strains. (79:5.2-3) The genetics of the yellow race were strengthened by superior red and Andonic strains, allowing them to largely escape admixture with debased stocks unlike their blue cousins in Europe. (79:5.1-2) (79:6.7) The ancient yellow race's superiority was due to genetic, social, spiritual, and geographic factors. (79:6.6-12) (79:7.3,5) (79:8.15) For over 200,000 years, the yellow race fought the red race in Asia, marking an epic chapter in Urantia's history. (79:5.5) In Central and South America, the red and yellow races laid the foundations for enduring civilizations with a mix of yellow, orange, and blue influences. (79:5.8) The yellow race in India has a complex history of mixture with other races, resulting in a diverse population with varying influences. (79:2.2) The yellow race migrated to Asia after the red man, following the primary Sangik peoples who avoided the tropics. (64:7.3-4) The Mongoloid type includes the original yellow race, as well as the Chinese, Amerinds, Malayan, and other Indonesian peoples, modified by secondary Sangik and Andonic mixture. (81:4.12) The Pacific islands were initially occupied by a mix of races, including Andonites and later the yellow and red stock, with the ancestors of the Japanese people not leaving the mainland until 12,000 B.C. (79:6.3) The yellow race's survival and peaceful nature can be attributed to a small but potent Adamic legacy, leading to advancements in culture, metallurgy, and civilization in eastern Asia. (64:6.16) (78:5.6) (78:6.4) (79:6.7) (79:7) (80:1.5) The yellow race tended to be somewhat broad-headed, as were the blue men when mixed with Andonite stock. (81:4.2-3) The modern white race incorporates surviving strains of the Adamic, Sangik, Andonite, red, yellow, and blue races, resulting in a highly blended and diverse population. (80:0.2) (80:3.2) (80:9.2,5,10) The yellow race suffered from absorption of the green race, but the northern Chinese were strengthened by Andonic and Andite blood, while the southern Chinese were weakened by inferior peoples from India. (79:6.7) The yellow race usually enslaves the green race, as seen in the interactions between the different primitive races. (51:4.6)
improvedDescription: One of the six original Sangik races of humans on Urantia, characterized by their peaceful nature, agricultural settlements, and fraternal spirit. The yellow race consolidated control of central Asia around 15,000 B.C. and was the first Sangik people to abandon hunting for settled agriculture. They experienced spiritual leadership under Singlangton about 100,000 years ago, who proclaimed the worship of the "One Truth." The yellow race successfully drove the red race from Asia into North America through superior social organization and collective cooperation, though they fought the red race for over 200,000 years in an epic struggle. They largely avoided mixture with debased human stocks and received beneficial infusions of Andite blood, particularly in northern China, contributing to their advanced civilization along the Yellow River.

name: Yellow River
originalDescription: Andite nomads north of the Yellow River left traces of their blood among Turanian and Tibetan peoples. (79:1.8) Chinese culture in 18,000 B.C. flourished along the Yellow River, ultimately leading to the political reunification of the yellow race and a peaceful societal existence for thousands of years. (79:6.5) (79:8.5) Drought drove Andites to valleys of the Nile, Euphrates, Indus, and Yellow rivers, producing a new class of traders. (79:1.3) Andonite and Andite settlers advanced the progressive settlements along the Yellow River, surpassing those along the Yangtze. (79:7.1-2)
improvedDescription: A major river in China where advanced Chinese settlements flourished, particularly along its upper valley near Kansu. About 15,000 years ago, Andites traversed the pass of Ti Tao and spread throughout the Yellow River valley, bringing superior blood strains to the Chinese settlements. The Yellow River centers of culture were consistently more progressive than the southern Yangtze settlements, achieving political reunification of the yellow race between 4000 and 500 B.C.

name: yin and yang
originalDescription: Chinese teaching of soul and spirit distinguishes between the yang and the yin aspects of a human being, reflecting their recognition of the two components. (111:0.4)
improvedDescription: Chinese philosophical concepts representing the dual aspects of human nature - the soul and the spirit. The Chinese recognized these two factors within a human being, distinguishing between the material and spiritual components of personality, similar to how other cultures identified dual human nature through concepts like the Hindu atman or the Egyptian ka and ba.

name: Yoga
originalDescription: Gautama formulated Buddhism after six years of futile practice of Yoga, deterring seeking salvation through physical affliction and urging his followers to spread his gospel globally. (94:7.2)
improvedDescription: A spiritual practice that Gautama (Buddha) pursued futilely for six years before formulating Buddhism. After finding yoga ineffective for achieving enlightenment, Gautama developed his Buddhist philosophy and discouraged seeking salvation through physical affliction and personal pain.

name: yoke
originalDescription: Take upon you the divine yoke and experience the peace of God. (144:8.8) Jesus, at the age of fourteen, became an expert yoke maker while honing his skills as a carpenter and cabinetmaker. (124:1.7) (126:1.1) Take the yoke of Jesus and learn of him; find true spiritual rest. (163:6.7) The Master invites those who labor to take up his yoke, for it is easy and the burden is light, offering spiritual rest and strength to all who come willingly. (141:3.7) (159:3.7)
improvedDescription: Both a literal wooden implement that Jesus became expert at crafting as a young carpenter, and a spiritual metaphor used by Jesus to describe discipleship. Jesus invited followers to "take my yoke upon you and learn of me," promising that his spiritual yoke is easy and the burden light, offering rest and strength to those who willingly accept the responsibilities of the kingdom.

name: young man who was afraid
originalDescription: Rising from his despair, the once fearful young man became a leader of Christians in Crete through the guidance and encouragement of Jesus. (130:6)
improvedDescription: A fearful and downcast youth whom Jesus encountered in the mountains, struggling with feelings of helplessness and inferiority after losing his father at age twelve. Through Jesus' guidance and encouragement, this young man overcame his despair and later became a leader of Christians in Crete.

name: Zaccheus
originalDescription: Zaccheus, the chief publican in Jericho, eagerly climbed a sycamore tree to catch a glimpse of Jesus, who then surprised him by choosing to stay at his house for the night. (171:6.1) Jesus, upon visiting Zaccheus, taught and brought salvation to his home, declaring him a son of Abraham. (171:6) (171:8.1)
improvedDescription: The wealthy chief publican (tax collector) of Jericho who climbed a sycamore tree to see Jesus as he passed through the city. Jesus surprised Zaccheus by choosing to stay at his house, where he taught the gospel of the kingdom to Zaccheus and his family, declaring him a son of Abraham and bringing salvation to his household.

name: Zacharias
originalDescription: John the Baptist's father, Zacharias, was part of the Jewish priesthood and his mother, Elizabeth, was related to Mary, the mother of Jesus, receiving a divine visitation foretelling their son's mission as a forerunner to the Messiah. (122:2) John the Baptist's education was carefully overseen by his fairly well educated priest father, Zacharias. (122:2.1) (135:0.4) Zacharias and Elizabeth barely sustain their livelihood with their small farm, supplemented by Zacharias' temple allowance. (135:0.5) Despite his initial skepticism, Zacharias eventually became fully convinced of the prospective motherhood of Elizabeth, believing in the destiny of their son. (122:2.5) Zacharias believed in Gabriel's visitation only after a dream, leading to the birth of John the Baptist. (135:0.1) Joseph conferred with Zacharias in Jerusalem about Jesus becoming the Jewish deliverer. (122:8.4) Zacharias, Elizabeth, and Mary believed that Jesus would become the Messiah, prompting Joseph to remain in Bethlehem for Jesus to grow up as David's successor. (122:8.4) Zacharias prearranged with Simeon and Anna to identify Jesus in the procession of first-born children at the temple. (122:9.2-3) Zacharias sent wise men to Bethlehem to find the “light of life” as a babe named Jesus. (122:8.6) Zacharias, warned by a believer in the coming Messiah, saved Joseph from Herod’s massacre by sending a messenger the night before Jesus and his family fled to Egypt. (122:10.4) Zacharias died in July, A.D. 12, leading John to struggle with the Nazarite vow and ultimately seek purification in Jerusalem, before making plans for the future and eventually settling in the Engedi colony. (135:2)
improvedDescription: John the Baptist's father, a Jewish priest who was married to Elizabeth, a relative of Mary. Initially skeptical of Gabriel's announcement about their son's destiny, Zacharias became convinced through a dream and helped arrange for wise men to visit the infant Jesus in Bethlehem. He provided education for John and warned Joseph of Herod's massacre, enabling the holy family's escape to Egypt.

name: Zadoc
originalDescription: Joseph was accounted as of the “house of David” because Zadoc adopted his paternal ancestor six generations prior, linking him to the lineage of King David. (122:4.3)
improvedDescription: A direct descendant of King David who adopted Joseph's orphaned paternal ancestor six generations before Jesus' birth. This adoption linked Joseph to the Davidic lineage, accounting for why Joseph was considered to be of the "house of David" despite not being a direct blood descendant of the king.

name: Zaphon
originalDescription: It was one of the towns visited during the Perea mission of the seventy, between January 3, 30 until the death of Jesus (159:0.2) (165:0.1)
improvedDescription: A city in the Decapolis region visited during Jesus' ministry tours. It was included in both the four-week Decapolis mission of the twelve apostles and evangelists, and later in the three-month Perean mission of the seventy messengers that concluded Jesus' public ministry before his final journey to Jerusalem.

name: zeal
improvedDescription: Intense enthusiasm and passionate devotion to a cause or belief.

name: Zealots
originalDescription: The Zealots, a strong nationalist party opposed to Roman taxes, advocated for rebellion against Roman rule in Judea. (127:2.1) (137:7.9) Jesus refused to join the Zealots, despite the pressure from his family and peers in Nazareth. (127:2.1-10) (136:9.7) Jesus strived in vain for Jude's immediate hearing or release before Passover, as he was detained until the following day due to the “holy convocation.” (128:6.6) Jesus' uncle Simon, officer of the Galilean division, had joined the Zealots causing an estrangement between him and Jesus. (127:2.4) Simon Zelotes was a high officer in the Zealots before becoming an apostle with Jesus. (138:2.8) (139:11.1)
improvedDescription: A strong Jewish nationalist party that advocated rebellion against Roman rule in Judea, opposing Roman taxes and seeking political revolution to escape Roman bondage. Unlike the Pharisees, the Zealots were not willing to await the coming of the Messiah and believed any methods were justified in their struggle for liberation.

name: Zebedee
originalDescription: Zebedee and his family celebrated Passover with Annas in Jerusalem. (129:2.8) After Jesus departed, John consulted Zebedee about Jesus' money, and they decided to invest in a house in Capernaum, holding the title in trust for Jesus. (129:2.4) The Zebedee family attended Jesus' evening conferences, where he gave advanced instruction on various topics, showcasing his insightful ideas and ideals. (129:1.10) Zebedee, father-partner of Jesus, owned boatbuilding shops on the lake near Capernaum, where Jesus lived and worked alongside him. (129:1.4) Zebedee's home served as headquarters for the kingdom, where Jesus outlined plans for proclaiming the kingdom and emphasized avoiding clashes with civil authorities, as his disciples lived to maintain contact with him. (138:5.4) (154:7.2) In Capernaum, Jesus made his home with the Zebedees, bringing a sense of joy and light-heartedness to those who knew him. (134:2.5) (137:3.4) Jesus, the master craftsman, revolutionized boatmaking by partnering with Zebedee for over a year to create new designs and methods in Capernaum. (129:1.2-4,11) (134:9.6) (137:6.5) Zebedee alone was entrusted with the secret of Jesus' mysterious Mediterranean journey. (129:2.10) (129:3.3-4) Zebedee and Andrew were once partners in the fish-drying business at Bethsaida. (139:1.1) Zebedee purchased the last of Joseph's entire estate by acquiring his equity in a Capernaum property. (127:6.10) Salome, wife of Zebedee, loved Jesus as much as her own sons, James, John, and David, while her daughters saw him as their elder brother. (129:1.5)
improvedDescription: A moderately well-to-do boatbuilder who owned shops on the lake near Capernaum and became Jesus' business partner and host. Zebedee was the only person entrusted with the secret of Jesus' mysterious Mediterranean journey, and his home served as headquarters for the kingdom during Jesus' ministry.

name: Zebedee, David
improvedDescription: The youngest son of Zebedee and Salome, who loved Jesus as an elder brother and often went fishing with him.

name: Zebedee, James
improvedDescription: The eldest son of Zebedee and Salome, who became one of Jesus' twelve apostles and was a fisherman by trade.

name: Zebedee, John
improvedDescription: The son of Zebedee who managed Jesus' financial affairs and later became one of the twelve apostles.

name: Zebedee, Salome
improvedDescription: Wife of Zebedee and relative of Annas, the former high priest, who loved Jesus as much as her own sons and whose daughters regarded him as their elder brother.

name: Zebulun
originalDescription: The special instruction given by Jesus during their stay at Zebulun focused on clarifying personal religious experiences and social religious obligations. (146:3.10) (156:6.2)
improvedDescription: A village along the Nazareth-Mount Lebanon trail where Jesus and his followers stopped during their travels, holding meetings and providing instruction on the mutual obligations of the kingdom and the differences between personal religious experience and social religious obligations.

name: Zechariah
originalDescription: Zechariah was slain between the sanctuary and the altar, a symbol of the accountability for rejecting and destroying messengers of heaven throughout history. (175:1.22) Book of Zechariah
improvedDescription: A prophet who was slain between the sanctuary and the altar, representing the historical pattern of rejecting and destroying heavenly messengers. Jesus referenced his prophecy about the Messiah coming humbly, riding upon an ass, as guidance for his entry into Jerusalem.

name: Zeus
originalDescription: Zeus, head of the Greek pantheon, was an imposed man-god on a Mediterranean world dominated by mother cults, hindering the Greeks' development towards true monotheism. (98:1.3-4) Zeus' transformation into Jupiter symbolized the extensive cultural importation from Greece to Rome, greatly influencing Roman religion. (98:3.3)
improvedDescription: The head of the Greek pantheon, a man-god imposed by the Greeks on the Mediterranean world that was previously dominated by mother cults. Zeus later became Jupiter in Roman religion through extensive cultural importation from Greece.

name: zinc
originalDescription: Zinc is found in the deposits of this age alongside gas, oil, and lead, all formed from ancient collections of organic material and sedimentation in sluggish bodies of water. (59:3.4) (59:5.9)
improvedDescription: Zinc is a mineral found in ancient geological deposits alongside gas, oil, and lead, formed from organic material and sedimentation in sluggish bodies of water.

name: Zone of Infinity
originalDescription: Beneath the Trinity in nether Paradise lies the mysterious Zone of Infinity surrounded by an unnamed area, with specific focus on space potency and force-energy. (11:5.2-4)
improvedDescription: The Zone of Infinity is a mysterious, unknown and unrevealed region located directly beneath the Trinity in the central portion of nether Paradise.

name: Zophar
originalDescription: Zophar, in seeking hidden purpose in Job's miseries, failed to offer comfort in his words of advice. (148:6.8,9)
improvedDescription: Zophar was one of Job's three friends who offered uncomforting counsel, suggesting there might be hidden purpose in Job's miseries and reiterating melancholy advice during Job's trials.

name: Zoroaster
originalDescription: Zoroaster utilized the flame as a symbol of the pure and wise Spirit, not teaching worship but inspiring reverence. (95:6.4) Zoroaster's religion in Persia was influenced by Salem missionaries and their teachings of the one God. (131:5.1) Zoroaster, influenced by dual spiritism, still exalted one eternal Deity and the triumph of light over darkness. (92:5.12) Zoroaster emerged as an outstanding religious teacher in 6th century B.C. Iran, reviving the monotheistic teachings of the Salem cult amidst a period of moral renaissance. (95:6.1) (121:6.4)
improvedDescription: Zoroaster was an outstanding 6th century BC religious teacher in Iran who revived monotheistic teachings influenced by Salem missionaries, using flame as a symbol of the pure and wise Spirit rather than promoting fire worship.

name: Zoroastrianism
originalDescription: Zoroastrianism had a significant influence on Christianity through interactions with Mithraism, shaping the theologic and philosophic elements of Christian teachings. (98:7.6) Zoroastrianism militantly opposed Mithraism, which later influenced Christianity through the Mithraic cult. (98:5.2) Zoroaster's religion, influenced by Melchizedek missionaries, contained the most Salem teachings aside from Judaism. (131:5.1) Zoroastrianism is a religion of action, based on work rather than prayers and rituals, centered around a god of supreme wisdom and civilization, fighting against evil and inaction. (95:6.3) The Zoroastrians practiced a religion of morals, while Hindus focused on metaphysics and Confucianists emphasized ethics, but Jesus exemplified a religion of service that unifies the good, beautiful, and true in all human experience. (5:4.7) The Rimmonites embraced beliefs of the Babylonian god Ramman, leading Jesus and the twenty-four to clarify the differences between older teachings and the new gospel of the kingdom. (146:1.1-3) Zoroastrianism, spread by the sword, symbolized the pure and wise Spirit through fire, despite Zoroaster's original teachings. (95:6.4) From the records of this religion Ganid’s abstract of Zoroastrianism includes teachings on the all-wise, good, and glorious One God, as well as the importance of living in consonance with truth and seeking union with righteousness. (131:5)
improvedDescription: Zoroastrianism was a religion of action founded by Zoroaster in Persia, emphasizing work over rituals and centered on a god of supreme wisdom who battles evil, darkness, and inaction.

name: Zulus
originalDescription: The Zulus' religion of ghost fear and sacrifice is an example of the social evolution of man's worship impulse in the twentieth century. (92:6.1)
improvedDescription: The Zulus are an African people who, as of the twentieth century, were evolving a religion centered on ghost fear and sacrifice as part of their religious development.
